papers.title: Disentangled Anatomy-Disease Diffusion (DADD) for Controllable Ulcerative Colitis Progression Synthesis
papers.summary: Disentangled Anatomy-Disease Diffusion (DADD) for Controllable Ulcerative Colitis Progression Synthesis attacks the failure mode of longitudinal UC endoscopy synthesis where severity edits and patient identity are entangled, so a model either preserves the source lesion or destroys anatomy when asked to change Mayo Endoscopic Score (MES). It challenges the nearby assumption behind IP-Adapter-style reference conditioning and ordinal diffusion that a single image embedding can safely carry anatomy while a separate disease label steers pathology; in practice, CLIP tokens leak source disease, creating semantic conflict under counterfactual progression. The pivot is from “generate from noise with a severity label” toward “edit a specific patient across ordinal disease states while explicitly stripping disease from the reference image.” Mechanically, DADD conditions latent diffusion on two complementary streams: a pretrained image encoder for anatomy and an ordinal embedder for MES, then inserts a Feature Purifier that uses cross-attention plus a learned gate to erase disease-correlated channels from the image tokens. Those purified anatomy tokens and disease tokens are not concatenated but split into a Triple-Pathway Cross-Attention block with resolution-dependent routing, exploiting the U-Net hierarchy so coarse layers preserve structure and fine layers inject inflammatory texture. A further unusual move is Delta Steering, a training-free signed ordinal difference vector that replaces classifier-free guidance with single-pass, magnitude-proportional control at inference. On LIMUC (10,284 images, MES 0–3), DADD-H best preserves severity fidelity and downstream classification utility, while also running about 2× faster than CFG baselines; its main limitation is sensitivity to steering scale, pointing toward per-class steering schedules and broader longitudinal medical synthesis.
papers.abstract: Synthesizing longitudinal medical images at controllable disease stages while preserving patient-specific anatomy is hindered by the entanglement of pathological textures and structural features. We address this challenge for ulcerative colitis (UC) endoscopy, where severity follows a continuous ordinal progression along the Mayo Endoscopic Score (MES). Our framework, Disentangled Anatomy-Disease Diffusion (DADD), conditions a latent diffusion model on two complementary embeddings: a pretrained image encoder for patient anatomy and a separately trained ordinal embedder for cumulative disease severity. Since image embeddings inevitably capture disease information, we introduce a Feature Purifier, a cross-attention-based erasure mechanism that identifies and suppresses disease-correlated channels, yielding purified anatomical representations. These cleaned anatomy tokens and target disease tokens are injected into the denoising network via a Triple-Pathway Cross-Attention mechanism with resolution-dependent routing gates. This architecture leverages the U-Net hierarchy, in which different network depths encode global structure versus fine-grained pathological texture. Furthermore, we introduce Delta Steering, a training-free directional signal derived from the ordinal embeddings that enables explicit, single-pass control over disease transitions at inference without requiring additional forward passes. Validated on the LIMUC dataset, our approach produces high-fidelity images across all severity levels and effectively rebalances skewed class distributions, enhancing performance for downstream classification tasks. The dataset is available at zenodo.org/records/5827695 and the code base at github.com/umutdundar99/progressive-stable-diffusion

papers.title: DP-SfM: Dual-Pixel Structure-from-Motion without Scale Ambiguity
papers.summary: DP-SfM: Dual-Pixel Structure-from-Motion without Scale Ambiguity attacks the long-standing bottleneck that SfM+MVS reconstructs geometry only up to an arbitrary global scale unless a known-size reference object, external sensor, or prior calibration is inserted into the scene. The paper pivots away from marker-based photogrammetry and learning-based metric depth surrogates toward a sensor-native cue: dual-pixel defocus blur. Its core mechanical shift is to couple two ambiguities that normally remain separate—SfM’s scale ambiguity and DP imaging’s focus-distance ambiguity—through the thin-lens relation between signed blur size and inverse depth. Rather than estimating scale from a single calibration object, the method extracts patch-wise blur sizes from the left/right DP subaperture images, then solves a linear regression system for the global scene scale and per-view focus distances from the affine relation between blur and up-to-scale MVS depths. The second stage is unusual: it refines the estimate by intensity-based optimization that reblurs DP patches with cross-view PSF kernels and searches over candidate scales to maximize left-right alignment, making the final estimate sensitive to actual image evidence rather than only patchwise blur regressors. Evaluated on six real scenes across DSLR lenses and a Pixel 4, including multi-aperture captures, it recovers absolute scale reasonably well and outperforms metric monocular depth baselines, but it depends on sufficient defocus and stable SfM. This points toward future geometry systems that fuse commodity sensor physics with multi-view reconstruction to recover metric 3D without manual calibration.
papers.abstract: Multi-view 3D reconstruction, namely, structure-from-motion followed by multi-view stereo, is a fundamental component of 3D computer vision. In general, multi-view 3D reconstruction suffers from an unknown scale ambiguity unless a reference object of known size is present in the scene. In this article, we show that multi-view images captured using a dual-pixel (DP) sensor can automatically resolve the scale ambiguity, without requiring a reference object or prior calibration. Specifically, the defocus blur observed in DP images provides sufficient information to determine the absolute scale when paired with depth maps (up to scale) recovered from multi-view 3D reconstruction. Based on this observation, we develop a simple yet effective linear method to estimate the absolute scale, followed by the intensity-based optimization stage that aligns the left and right DP images by shifting them back toward each other using cross-view blur kernels. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach across diverse scenes captured with different cameras and lenses. Code and data are available at https://github.com/lilika-makabe/dp-sfm-tpami.git

papers.title: High-Fidelity Mobile Avatars with Pruned Local Blendshapes
papers.summary: High-Fidelity Mobile Avatars with Pruned Local Blendshapes attacks the bottleneck that high-quality Gaussian human avatars are still too expensive for mobile deployment because pose-dependent appearance is usually decoded with heavy global computation or distilled from pretrained teacher models, both of which either slow training or wash out details. The paper pivots away from the assumption that a single body-wide pose feature can linearly explain all Gaussian attribute changes toward a locality hypothesis: nearby Gaussians on human bodies form correlated clusters whose covariance is better captured by part-wise bases. The mechanical shift is two-stage: first partition the template into 256 body parts, predict a small local pose feature per part with lightweight MLPs, and linearly combine it with local blendshapes to produce rotation, scale, color, and sampled position correctives; then prune blendshapes by measuring per-Gaussian variance over training poses/expressions and deleting corrective parameters for low-changing Gaussians, followed by finetuning. Unlike nearby work such as TaoAvatar, SqueezeMe, or mmlphuman, it is end-to-end, does not distill from a pretrained large model, and explicitly treats blendshape sparsity as the optimization target. Trained on multi-view datasets including AvatarRex, Talking-Body4D, ActorsHQ, and DREAMS-Avatar, and deployed in Rust/WebGPU, it reaches 120 FPS at 2K on mobile with about a 19.4 MB model and improves detail over global-feature baselines while pruning 90% of blendshape parameters. The limitation is that loose garments can exceed the compact basis, suggesting future work on adaptive per-region capacity and more mature cross-device graphics runtimes.
papers.abstract: We propose a method to reconstruct high-fidelity human avatars from multi-view video that can run on mobile devices. Many works can model high-quality Gaussian-based full-body avatars from multi-view video. However, these methods require heavy computation to obtain pose-dependent appearance, making deployment on mobile devices very difficult. Recent methods distill from pretrained models and model pose-dependent nonlinear Gaussian attributes by linearly combining global pose features with blendshapes. Although they can run on mobile devices, they suffer some loss of detail. We observe that nearby Gaussians are often highly correlated within a local region of the body, and can be linearly modeled with less error. Therefore, we use local linear blendshapes in small body parts to capture global nonlinear changes of Gaussian attributes. To further reduce computation and model size, we propose to remove blendshapes for Gaussians whose attributes change little, yielding a minimal blendshape representation. Our method is an end-to-end training method without a pretrained model. To make it run on multiple devices, we implement our method using WebGPU. Experiments show that our method can render high-quality human avatars with better details, and can reach 120 FPS at 2K resolution on mobile devices.

papers.title: Decouple and Cache: KV Cache Construction for Streaming Video Understanding
papers.summary: Decouple and Cache: KV Cache Construction for Streaming Video Understanding attacks the bottleneck that streaming VideoVLLMs face when unbounded video arrives faster than memory and cache quality can scale: uniform KV updates both evict old context and silently contaminate newly built caches with residual history, while growing position IDs push RoPE-based models beyond training length and into position overflow. The paper pivots away from the common assumption that streaming success is mostly a cache-reuse or retrieval problem and toward the more specific question of how KV caches should be constructed in the first place. Its mechanical shift is DSCache, a training-free adaptation layer for pretrained offline MLLMs that splits streaming memory into a cumulative past KV cache and a separate on-demand instant cache built only from a FIFO feature buffer, so recent inputs are encoded without interference from the accumulated past. It further replaces standard position-aware cache storage with position-agnostic encoding, storing keys before positional transformation and reassigning positions at use time; for RoPE models the authors prove this is equivalent when relative distances are preserved, enabling eviction, partitioning, and reuse without positional discontinuity. Evaluated on StreamingBench, OVO-Bench, and RVS-Ego/Movie with Llava-OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL, DSCache gives about 2.5% average accuracy gains over prior streaming methods, with especially strong improvements on recency-sensitive tasks, though it trades some long-range reasoning for better current-frame fidelity. This suggests a future direction of streaming AI centered on cache-construction rules and position management rather than ever larger context windows.
papers.abstract: Streaming video understanding requires processing unbounded video streams with limited memory and computation, posing two key challenges. First, continuously constructing new and evicting old key-value(KV) caches is required for unbounded streams. Secondly, due to the high cost of collecting and training on unbounded streams, models must learn from short sequences while generalizing to long streams. Existing streaming VideoVLLMs fail to scale to unbounded video streams or focus on cache reuse strategies, leaving the impact of cache construction underexplored. In this paper, we propose Decoupled Streaming Cache(DSCache), a training-free cache construction mechanism that adapts pretrained offline models to streaming settings. DSCache maintains a cumulative past KV cache while constructing a separate instant cache on-demand, decoupled from past caches to preserve the informativeness of recent inputs. To enable position extrapolation beyond the training length, DSCache further incorporates a position-agnostic encoding strategy, ensuring KV caches to support unseen positions and preventing position overflow. Experiments on Streaming Video QA benchmarks demonstrate DSCache's state-of-the-art performance, with an average 2.5% accuracy gains over prior methods.

papers.title: BadmintonGRF: A Multimodal Dataset and Benchmark for Markerless Ground Reaction Force Estimation in Badminton
papers.summary: BadmintonGRF: A Multimodal Dataset and Benchmark for Markerless Ground Reaction Force Estimation in Badminton attacks the bottleneck that public sports datasets rarely pair laboratory-grade ground reaction force (GRF) with high-frame-rate multi-view video for non-periodic court play, leaving markerless load estimation trained mostly on periodic gait or scripted jumps and thus poorly matched to badminton’s abrupt landings, lunges, and fatigue shifts. The paper pivots from badminton as a semantic video-understanding problem toward badminton as an impact-centric biomechanics benchmark, replacing loose “video-to-force” claims with audited pose-to-GRF supervision on real training drills. The mechanical shift is a tiered, reproducible sensing stack: eight synchronized RGB cameras at ~120 FPS, four Kistler force plates, and Vicon C3D, aligned in software without hardware genlock via human-verified events, automated QA, per-camera time offsets, and uncertainty metadata. Tier 1 releases privacy-preserving pose, time-aligned GRF, and deterministic LOSO splits; Tier 2 holds raw RGB and mocap under controlled access. The public benchmark contains 17,425 impact segments from 10 subjects and deduplicates to 1,732 unique impacts; the task regresses body-weight-normalized vertical Fz from 2D pose plus motion features. Reference baselines show a cross-subject ceiling near r²≈0.40, with subject shift dominating residual sync noise; PatchTST and ST-GCN+Transformer lead different metrics, while late fusion can raise r² yet worsen peak/timing errors. This points toward subject-aware, uncertainty-aware, and fatigue-conditioned pose-to-force models that treat metadata and alignment as first-class control signals.
papers.abstract: Multimodal resources for non-periodic court sports with laboratory-grade sensing remain scarce: few publicly pair instrumented ground reaction force (GRF) with high-frame-rate multi-view video, limiting markerless load estimation in realistic training settings. BadmintonGRF records eight synchronized RGB views at ~120 FPS, four Kistler force plates, and Vicon motion capture (C3D) without hardware genlock across modalities; alignment combines human-verified events, automated quality assurance, and per-camera time offsets with uncertainty metadata. Tier 1 distributes pose, time-aligned GRF, metadata, and splits under CC BY-NC 4.0, enabling the primary benchmark without raw RGB or C3D; we report a Tier 1 task that maps 2D pose to GRF. Tier 2 provides raw RGB and C3D under controlled access for studies that require appearance or full kinematics. The public release contains 17,425 impact-segment archives in the 10-subject benchmark tree (156 instrumented trials; raw multi-view RGB alone exceeds 1 TB); benchmark loader gates retain 12,867 view-specific instances and 1,732 unique impacts after multi-view deduplication. We are not aware of prior public badminton corpora that combine this sensing layout with audited video--GRF alignment for impact-centric GRF estimation. We distribute preprocessing code, leave-one-subject-out splits, ten reference baselines, and optional late fusion (one deterministic test-time pass per instance; no test-time augmentation), with a within-trial diagnostic in the supplementary material.

papers.title: Chart-FR1: Visual Focus-Driven Fine-Grained Reasoning on Dense Charts
papers.summary: Chart-FR1: Visual Focus-Driven Fine-Grained Reasoning on Dense Charts attacks the failure mode where MLLMs collapse on high-information-density charts because global visual embeddings miss tiny but decisive cues, dense legends/OCR become noise, and standard RL imposes a fixed KL penalty that blocks the longer reasoning chains needed when more evidence appears. The paper pivots from “read the whole chart, then reason” and from sparse-answer-only optimization toward evidence-anchored, progressively focused reasoning over local regions and text. Its mechanical shift is a two-stage focused training loop on Qwen2.5-VL-7B: first, Focus-CoT bootstraps a cold-start set by reconstructing candidate CoTs with teacher-guided <focus> tags that explicitly bind each reasoning step to <ocr> and <box> evidence; then Focus-GRPO replaces vanilla GRPO with a multi-term reward that combines relaxed-accuracy, an information-efficiency penalty that suppresses redundant OCR/region mentions, and an adaptive KL coefficient that loosens or tightens exploration based on how much focused information has been discovered. Unlike nearby chart-reasoning work that mainly adds better prompts, code execution, or static RL, Chart-FR1 turns “what to look at next” into the learning signal itself and adds HID-Chart, a new benchmark with an information-density metric to separate cluttered from merely difficult charts. On five chart benchmarks and HID-Chart, it reports consistent gains over strong open and closed MLLMs, with especially large improvements in dense-chart regimes, suggesting a future where multimodal reasoning is controlled by density-aware focus policies rather than uniform decoding depth.
papers.abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown considerable potential in chart understanding and reasoning tasks. However, they still struggle with high information density (HID) charts characterized by multiple subplots, legends, and dense annotations due to three major challenges: (1) limited fine-grained perception results in the omission of critical visual cues; (2) redundant or noisy visual information undermines the performance of multimodal reasoning; (3) lack of adaptive deep reasoning relative to the amount of visual information. To tackle these challenges, we present a novel focus-driven fine-grained chart reasoning model, Chart-FR1, to improve perception, focusing efficiency, and adaptive deep reasoning on HID charts. Specifically, we propose Focus-CoT, a visual focusing chain-of-thought that enhances fine-grained perception by explicitly linking reasoning steps to key visual cues, such as local image regions and OCR signals. Building on this, we introduce Focus-GRPO, a focus-driven reinforcement learning algorithm with an information-efficiency reward that compresses redundant visual information for efficient focusing, and an adaptive KL penalty mechanism that enables flexible control over reasoning depth as more visual cues are discovered. Furthermore, to fill the gap in benchmarks for HID charts, we build HID-Chart, a challenging benchmark with an information-density metric designed to evaluate fine-grained chart reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments on multiple chart benchmarks demonstrate that Chart-FR1 outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs in chart understanding and reasoning. Code is available at https://github.com/phkhub/Chart-FR1.

papers.title: AFFormer: Adaptive Feature Fusion Transformer for V2X Cooperative Perception under Channel Impairments
papers.summary: AFFormer: Adaptive Feature Fusion Transformer for V2X Cooperative Perception under Channel Impairments attacks a concrete deployment bottleneck in cooperative LiDAR 3D detection: intermediate BEV features shared over V2X links are often corrupted by noise, fading, and interference, so fusion modules trained under the usual “perfect packet arrival” assumption amplify misalignment, confidence drift, and false/missed detections instead of rescuing occluded objects. The paper pivots from bandwidth-centric fusion and ideal-channel benchmarking toward corruption-aware, reliability-weighted feature recovery, where the bottleneck becomes how to preserve useful content inside degraded transmitted tensors rather than how to simply merge more agents. The mechanical shift is AFFormer’s three-stage Transformer fusion loop: Multi-Agent and Temporal Aggregation models cross-agent and history cues with cascaded inter-agent and temporal attention; Dual Spatial Attention replaces full 2D self-attention with separate width/height branches to capture anisotropic structure at far lower latency; and Uncertainty-Guided Fusion converts channel-wise entropy into spatial importance maps that downweight ambiguous regions while preserving confident evidence. A teacher-student distillation regime further aligns the student’s fused features and detection heads to clean early-collaboration supervision, giving the model a privileged training signal without extra inference cost. Evaluated on V2XSet and DAIR-V2X under ideal and simulated 802.11p-like impairments, AFFormer consistently outperforms prior cooperative and corruption-aware baselines, especially at low SNR, while keeping a competitive efficiency-accuracy trade-off. This suggests a future direction of cooperative perception that is channel-aware, uncertainty-calibrated, and temporally resilient, with extensions toward delay/packet-loss robustness and scalable heterogeneous fusion.
papers.abstract: Accurate 3D object detection is essential for ensuring the safety of autonomous vehicles. Cooperative perception, which leverages vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication to share perceptual data, enhances detection but is vulnerable to channel impairments, such as noise, fading, and interference. To strengthen the reliability of intelligent transportation systems, this work improves the robustness of V2X cooperative perception under communication conditions that reflect common channel impairments. This paper proposes an Adaptive Feature Fusion Transformer (AFFormer), a Transformer-based framework that mitigates the adverse effects of corrupted features by modeling temporal, inter-agent, and spatial correlations. AFFormer introduces three key modules: Multi-Agent and Temporal Aggregation for context-aware fusion across agents and over time, Dual Spatial Attention for efficient modeling of spatial dependencies, and Uncertainty-Guided Fusion for entropy-driven refinement of fused features. A teacher-student knowledge distillation strategy further enhances robustness by aligning fused features with reliable early-collaboration supervision. AFFormer is validated on the V2XSet and DAIR-V2X datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing methods under both ideal and impaired communication conditions, demonstrating improved robustness to communication-induced feature degradation while maintaining a competitive efficiency-accuracy trade-off.

papers.title: Divide and Conquer: Decoupled Representation Alignment for Multimodal World Models
papers.summary: Divide and Conquer: Decoupled Representation Alignment for Multimodal World Models tackles the bottleneck that multimodal video world models can predict RGB, depth, and masks jointly yet fail to absorb the strong priors already encoded in existing foundation models, especially when naïvely aligning several experts at once causes feature conflict and training collapse. The paper pivots from the usual “single expert, single modality” REPA recipe toward a multi-expert world-model training regime in which each modality is treated as a distinct semantic space with its own foundation-model teacher. The core mechanical shift is M^2-REPA: intermediate diffusion features are first split by lightweight modality-specific decoupling MLPs into separate subspaces, then each subspace is aligned to its corresponding frozen expert backbone—DINOv2 for RGB, DepthAnythingV2 for depth, and SAM2 for masks—using a cosine-similarity alignment loss, while a CKA-based decoupling regularizer suppresses redundancy across modality branches and enforces complementarity. Unlike direct feature injection or naïve multi-teacher REPA, this is a joint optimization scheme that explicitly prevents expert interference while still borrowing priors at training time with no inference overhead. Evaluated on RealEstate10K and Minecraft under camera-pose and action-conditioned autoregressive rollouts, across both UViT and DiT backbones and up to 200 frames, the method improves visual fidelity, geometric accuracy, mask mIoU, and long-horizon consistency. The result suggests a research direction where world models become modular latent routers over heterogeneous pretrained experts rather than monolithic generators.
papers.abstract: Emerging multi-modal world models attempt to jointly generate videos across diverse modalities (e.g., RGB, depth, and mask), yet they fail to fully exploit the rich priors of existing foundation models. We propose $M^2$-REPA, the first representation alignment method tailored for multi-modal video generation. Our key insight is that foundation models trained on different modality spaces naturally capture distinct domain-specific priors, acting as complementary "experts." Specifically, we first decouple modality-specific features from the diffusion model's intermediate representations, then align each with its corresponding expert foundation model. To this end, we design two synergistic objectives: a multi-modal representation alignment loss that enforces feature-to-expert matching, and a modality-specific decoupling regularization that encourages complementarity across different modalities. This design enables joint optimization, fully exploiting priors from multiple foundation models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms baselines in visual quality and long-term consistency.

papers.title: Behavior-Grounded Lane Representation Learning for Multi-Task Traffic Digital Twins
papers.summary: Behavior-Grounded Lane Representation Learning for Multi-Task Traffic Digital Twins argues that traffic digital twins break down when lanes are treated as static polylines, because operational meaning—edge vs interior, merge support, rank within a group, and anomalous behavior under live flow—does not live in geometry alone. The paper pivots from map-centric, descriptive digital-twin workflows and VLM-style scene narration toward infrastructure-centric representation learning, where the lane becomes the semantic unit. Its core mechanical shift is GeoLaneRep: three parallel encoders jointly process lane centerline geometry, assigned vehicle trajectory windows, and compact operational descriptors, then fuse them into a shared embedding with cross-lane attention that models relative rank, speed, and density inside each lane group. Training combines structurally mined cross-camera InfoNCE alignment, auxiliary role supervision for lateral rank/edge flags/group size, and synthetic temporal anomaly detection; geometry dropout further forces viewpoint transfer by preventing overreliance on static shape. On 16 roadside cameras, 132 lanes, and 104,415 trajectories, this yields zero-shot cross-camera matching with 0.004 lateral-rank error and perfect edge-role F1, plus AUROC 0.991 for window-level anomaly detection. Unusually, the same behavior-grounded embedding also conditions a FiLM-modulated diffusion model to synthesize lane geometries with targeted operational specifications, reaching 87.9% overall accuracy. The main limitation is that merge lanes and geometry generation remain weaker than the encoder, suggesting a future path toward corridor-level, multi-lane, and richer multi-view or V2X-supervised constructive digital twins.
papers.abstract: Traffic digital twins are powerful tools for advanced traffic management, and most systems are built on static geometric representations. However, these representations fail to capture the dynamic functional semantics required for behavior-aware reasoning, such as how a lane operates under complex traffic conditions. To address this gap, we introduce GeoLaneRep, a behavior-grounded lane representation learning framework for traffic digital twins. GeoLaneRep jointly encodes static lane geometry, observed vehicle trajectories, and operational descriptors into a shared, cross-camera semantic embedding. The encoder is trained with a joint objective combining contrastive cross-camera alignment, auxiliary role supervision, and temporal anomaly detection. Across 16 roadside cameras and 132 lanes, the learned embeddings achieve a $0.004$ lateral-rank error and an edge-role F1 of $1.000$ in zero-shot cross-camera matching, and an AUROC of $0.991$ for window-level anomaly detection. We further show that the same behavioral embeddings can condition a diffusion-based generator to synthesize lane geometries that satisfy targeted operational specifications, with $87.9\%$ overall specification accuracy across 38 lane groups. GeoLaneRep thus provides a semantic interface between roadside observations and downstream digital twin tasks, supporting cross-camera transfer, behavior-aware monitoring, and goal-directed lane synthesis. The framework is openly available at https://github.com/raynbowy23/GeoLaneRep.

papers.title: SurgCheck: Do Vision-Language Models Really Look at Images in Surgical VQA?
papers.summary: SurgCheck: Do Vision-Language Models Really Look at Images in Surgical VQA? attacks the hidden failure mode that high surgical VQA scores may come from question priors rather than visual grounding, especially when entity names like instruments or anatomy implicitly narrow the answer space. It challenges the old assumption that benchmark accuracy on existing surgical datasets reflects faithful image understanding, and pivots the field away from more training-time bias mitigation toward evaluation-time diagnosis of shortcut reliance. The mechanical shift is a paired-question benchmark built from Endoscapes-SG201 scene graphs: each of 1,933 laparoscopic frames is paired with an original question containing explicit entity names and a less-biased counterfactual that removes those names while preserving the same image and answer. To keep the counterfactual well-defined, SurgCheck injects one of four grounding cues—bounding box, arrow, spatial position, or periphrasis—creating a spectrum from explicit visual localization to contextual reference. The performance gap between original and less-biased questions becomes the diagnostic signal, and open-ended outputs are scored with an LLM-as-a-judge protocol using GPT-5-mini. Evaluating five models, including Qwen2.5-VL, LLaVA-OneVision, PaliGemma2, PitVQA-Net, and EndoChat, the paper finds consistent degradation on less-biased questions in both zero-shot and fine-tuned settings; action and target prediction are most shortcut-driven, while Anatomy-hop and Triplet rely more on actual spatial reasoning. This suggests a research direction where surgical multimodal systems are judged by counterfactual robustness and grounding faithfulness, not only by nominal accuracy.
papers.abstract: Purpose: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promising performance in surgical visual question answering (VQA). However, existing surgical VQA datasets often contain linguistic shortcuts, where question phrasing implicitly constrains the answer space. It remains unclear whether reported performance reflects visual understanding or reliance on such linguistic shortcuts. Methods: We introduce SurgCheck, a diagnostic benchmark for quantifying linguistic shortcut reliance in surgical VQA. SurgCheck employs a paired-question design in which each surgical frame is associated with an original question containing entity names and a less-biased counterpart that removes these names while preserving identical visual content and ground-truth answers. The resulting performance gap provides a diagnostic signal of shortcut reliance. To ensure that the less-biased question remains well-defined even without entity names, four grounding cues are incorporated: bounding box, arrow, spatial position, and periphrasis. We evaluate both general-purpose and surgical-specific VLMs under zero-shot and fine-tuned settings on SurgCheck. To evaluate open-ended zero-shot responses, we introduce an LLM-as-a-judge evaluation protocol. Results: Using SurgCheck, we observe consistent performance degradation on less-biased questions across five VLMs, despite identical visual inputs. Text-only ablation reveals minimal performance drops for action and target prediction, indicating that action and target prediction is largely driven by linguistic shortcuts rather than visual reasoning. Conclusion: SurgCheck provides a controlled diagnostic framework that exposes failure modes masked by linguistic bias in existing surgical VQA benchmarks. Our findings demonstrate that strong benchmark performance does not necessarily imply faithful visual understanding, underscoring the need for bias-aware evaluation in surgical VQA.

papers.title: EAPFusion: Intrinsic Evolving Auxiliary Prior Guidance for Infrared and Visible Image Fusion
papers.summary: EAPFusion: Intrinsic Evolving Auxiliary Prior Guidance for Infrared and Visible Image Fusion attacks a specific bottleneck in infrared-visible image fusion: fixed fusion weights and coarse external semantic teachers often fail at inference-time scene adaptivity and create granularity mismatch, where high-level auxiliary semantics disturb shallow texture/detail layers and either suppress thermal targets or wash out visible structure. The paper pivots away from the recent trend of injecting frozen detector/CLIP/SAM priors into fusion networks and toward self-generated, self-updating priors that are internal to the fusion model itself. The mechanical shift is an Adaptive Prior Generator that keeps a compact per-scale prior set, aligns and cross-attends current features into candidate priors, then performs a gated evolutionary update so priors move progressively from texture to semantics across scales; those evolved priors are then converted by the Prior-Driven Dynamic Convolution Block into instance-adaptive expert kernels with dense plus Top-K routing, replacing static filters with position-dependent convolution at test time. A second unusual piece, the Shuffle Channel Fusion Block, interleaves IR/VIS channels and applies local channel-wise mixing rather than one-shot concatenation, preserving cross-modal complementarity without over-homogenizing features. Trained on MSRS and evaluated cross-dataset on M3FD and TNO, EAPFusion reports SOTA fusion metrics and stronger DeepLabV3+ segmentation downstream, especially on safety-critical classes. Its main limitation is remaining sensitivity to domain shift, pointing future work toward more robust intrinsic prior evolution and tighter adaptive interaction mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Infrared-visible image fusion aims to create an information-rich fused image by integrating the complementary thermal saliency from infrared sensing and fine textures from visible imaging. Such accurate fusion is essential for real-world perception applications in complex scenes, including nighttime autonomous driving, search and rescue, and surveillance, and can further benefit downstream tasks such as semantic segmentation. However, most existing fusion methods rely upon static trained weights that cannot adapt to scene-specific content at inference time, and often suffer from a granularity mismatch when coarse auxiliary semantics are injected, which makes it difficult to simultaneously highlight targets and preserve details. In this work, we propose EAPFusion to address these issues by using self-evolving intrinsic priors instead of relying on external auxiliary models. Concretely, EAPFusion maintains a compact set of intrinsic priors and progressively updates them across scales. These evolved priors are utilized to dynamically generate convolutional kernels, shifting the paradigm from fixed, pre-trained filters to instance-adaptive parameters via prior-conditioned dynamic convolution. Furthermore, we design a channel-level fusion module that shuffles and interleaves infrared and visible channels, applying local channel mixing to boost cross-modal complementarity. Experiments on different datasets, including cross-dataset evaluation and semantic segmentation, show that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art quantitative and qualitative fusion results, and consistently boosts downstream performance. Code is coming soon.

papers.title: SimPB++: Simultaneously Detecting 2D and 3D Objects from Multiple Cameras
papers.summary: SimPB++: Simultaneously Detecting 2D and 3D Objects from Multiple Cameras attacks the bottleneck that multi-camera autonomous-driving perception still treats perspective 2D boxes as a disposable pre-step for BEV 3D detection, which leaves cross-view objects fragmented, interaction one-shot, and 3D labels expensive. The paper pivots from two-detector pipelines and “2D priors for 3D” toward a single end-to-end query-based system that predicts 2D and 3D together, with the mechanical shift being a hybrid decoder that interleaves multi-view 2D decoding and BEV 3D decoding inside each layer. Its core novelty is a cyclic 3D-2D-3D refinement loop: Dynamic Query Allocation projects 3D anchors into camera-specific 2D query groups, Query-group Attention keeps communication local within each camera, and Adaptive Query Aggregation fuses the resulting 2D evidence back into updated 3D queries, replacing one-time query initialization with repeated bidirectional correction. Compared with nearby work like MV2D or Far3D, SimPB++ explicitly learns association in the reverse direction, from 3D anchors to 2D observations, which reduces redundant cross-camera detections and improves coherence. It further adds Crop-and-Scale for distant objects, Propagating Denoising for coupled 2D/3D noisy queries, and a training-only auxiliary RoI-plus-depth branch; importantly, it supports mixed 2D-only and fully labeled supervision. On nuScenes it reaches state-of-the-art on both tasks, and on Argoverse2 it substantially strengthens long-range detection up to 150 m, pointing toward unified, label-efficient, temporally extensible perception systems.
papers.abstract: Simultaneous perception of 2D objects in perspective view and 3D objects in Bird's Eye View (BEV) is challenging for multi-camera autonomous driving. Existing two-stage pipelines use 2D results only as a one-time cue for 3D detection. We propose SimPB++, which simultaneously detects 2D objects in perspective and 3D objects in BEV from multiple cameras. It unifies both tasks into an end-to-end model with a hybrid decoder architecture, coupling multi-view 2D and 3D decoders interactively. Two novel modules enable deep interaction: Dynamic Query Allocation adaptively assigns 2D queries to 3D candidates, and Adaptive Query Aggregation refines 3D representations using multi-view 2D features, forming a cyclic 3D-2D-3D refinement. For multi-view 2D detection, we use Query-group Attention for intra-group communication. We also design a Crop-and-Scale strategy for long-range perception and a Propagating Denoising strategy with an auxiliary RoI detector. SimPB++ supports mixed supervision with 2D-only and fully annotated data, reducing reliance on expensive 3D labels. Experiments show state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes for both tasks and strong long-range detection (up to 150m) on Argoverse2.

papers.title: CADFS: A Big CAD Program Dataset and Framework for Computer-Aided Design with Large Language Models
papers.summary: CADFS: A Big CAD Program Dataset and Framework for Computer-Aided Design with Large Language Models attacks the bottleneck that generative CAD systems can only produce sketch-extrude histories because prior datasets collapse design intent into simplified token sequences or Python wrappers that cannot faithfully reference geometry created earlier in the construction tree. The paper challenges the assumption that CAD generation should be learned from lossy, artificially compact histories and pivots from tokenized sequence prediction toward native, engineer-authored program generation in FeatureScript, Onshape’s executable parametric language. The mechanical shift is a data-centric pipeline that reconstructs clean, executable FeatureScript from Onshape’s internal representation by resolving implicit parameters, normalizing units and identifiers, removing dead code, and validating compilability, then pairing each model with multimodal annotations and rendered views. This yields 451k real-world CAD models spanning 15 operations, including fillet, chamfer, revolve, sweep, loft, shell, holes, patterns, and booleans, which is unusual because it preserves full geometric and parametric fidelity rather than approximating to a limited syntax. A Qwen2-VL-2B model fine-tuned on this representation, with explicit bounding-box scale prompts for image reconstruction, reaches state-of-the-art text-conditioned CAD generation and multi-view reconstruction on both the legacy DeepCAD split and the new 15-operation test set, producing more diverse and feature-rich outputs; the main tradeoff is some invalidity and residual failures on refinement ops and image-derived references. The work points toward scalable, code-native CAD agents that learn real modeling workflows, not just geometric shells.
papers.abstract: We introduce CADFS, a data-centric framework that enables large vision-language models to generate complex CAD design histories. Existing generative CAD systems are restricted to sketch-extrude operations due to simplified representations and limited datasets. We address this by introducing a FeatureScript-based representation and constructing a dataset of 450k real-world CAD models spanning 15 modeling operations. We obtain the dataset via a new pipeline that reconstructs clean, executable FeatureScript programs and provides multimodal annotations. Fine-tuning a VLM on this representation yields state-of-the-art results in text-conditioned CAD generation and image-based reconstruction, producing more accurate, diverse, and feature-rich designs than prior frameworks. Ablations show that each individual component of our framework, i.e., the FeatureScript representation, the extended operation set, and representation-aligned textual descriptions, significantly improves performance. Our framework substantially broadens the complexity and realism achievable in generative CAD. The CADFS framework and the new dataset are available at https://voyleg.github.io/cadfs/.

papers.title: Exploring Data-Free LoRA Transferability for Video Diffusion Models
papers.summary: Exploring Data-Free LoRA Transferability for Video Diffusion Models attacks a practical failure mode in modern video generation ecosystems: LoRAs trained on a base text-to-video model break when reused on step-distilled or causal-distilled descendants, causing style degradation, duplicate limbs, ghosting, and structural collapse. The paper challenges the tacit assumption that LoRA updates can be ported by direct reuse or uniform subspace projection across closely related diffusion checkpoints; instead it pivots from model-space compatibility toward singular-subspace routing compatibility. The mechanical shift is Cluster-Aware Spectral Arbitration (CASA), a data-free transfer rule built from a first weight-space analysis of video diffusion fine-tuning: SVD reveals spectral rigidity, but adaptation mainly rotates coherent singular clusters rather than reshaping singular values. CASA constructs a top-k spectral graph capturing 90% of energy, derives cluster-level routing matrices C = UᵀΔV for both the source LoRA and distilled-model drift, then separates dominant routing regions from non-dominant ones using FFT-induced routing density. In weakly coupled regions it restores LoRA by compensating the distilled drift, while in high-density clusters it arbitrates over-activation by retaining only safe magnitudes and preserving the target manifold. Unlike ProLoRA-style uniform alignment, CASA is explicitly interference-aware and cluster-local. Experiments on Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B/14B and HunyuanVideo-1.5 with multiple public LoRAs show better style fidelity and stable or improved quality without extra training or user data, suggesting a future where adaptation is treated as constrained recovery of functional routing pathways rather than generic parameter transfer.
papers.abstract: Video diffusion models leveraging step distillation or causal distillation have achieved remarkable performance. However, adapting existing LoRAs to these variants remains a critical challenge due to weight space mismatches. We observe that direct application leads to style degradation and structural collapse, yet the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. To fill this gap, we delve into the weight space and identify that the incompatibility stems from spectral interference within shared functional clusters defined over singular subspaces. Specifically, our analysis reveals that while both paradigms respect spectral rigidity, they establish conflicting routing pathways that clash through constructive overload or destructive cancellation. To address this issue, we propose Cluster-Aware Spectral Arbitration (CASA), a data-free framework that dynamically arbitrates between safeguarding the target's manifold and restoring LoRA alignment based on spectral density. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CASA effectively mitigates artifacts and revives LoRA functionality. Our code is available at https://github.com/Noahwangyuchen/CASA

papers.title: ProtoFair: Fair Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning via Pseudo-Counterfactual Pairs
papers.summary: ProtoFair: Fair Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning via Pseudo-Counterfactual Pairs attacks the bottleneck that SSL encoders can learn high-quality visual features while silently encoding demographic signal, causing equalized-odds violations downstream on face attributes even when no target labels are used. Rather than following the dominant fairness workflow of redesigning the SSL objective, adding adversarial heads, or requiring supervised positives, it pivots from loss replacement toward a plug-in regularizer that leaves SimCLR/SupCon intact and only asks for sensitive-attribute annotations. The mechanical shift is to repurpose unsupervised prototype clustering as a proxy for semantic content: a separate cluster head maps features into a prototype space, prototypes are initialized by K-means after warmup and tracked by EMA, and samples with the same cluster assignment but different sensitive groups are treated as pseudo-counterfactual positives. ProtoFair then pulls these cross-group, content-matched pairs together while excluding same-cluster/same-group pairs from the numerator, and extends the signal with a FIFO cross-batch queue so rare pairs can be discovered beyond a mini-batch. This is unusual because clustering, usually used to improve representation quality, becomes the fairness mechanism itself and is detached to avoid degenerate cluster collapse. On CelebA and UTKFace with ResNet-18 and linear evaluation, ProtoFair consistently lowers equalized odds across imbalance regimes while keeping competitive accuracy, with especially strong gains under demographic skew. The paper points toward modular fairness controls that can ride on future SSL backbones without retraining the whole objective.
papers.abstract: Self-supervised learning methods learn high-quality visual representations, yet recent studies show that these representations often capture demographic biases present in the training data. Existing fairness-aware methods address this by redesigning the self-supervised objective itself, limiting portability across the rapidly evolving landscape of self-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks. We propose ProtoFair, a fairness-aware contrastive loss designed to work alongside existing SSL objectives without modifying them. ProtoFair leverages unsupervised prototype clustering to identify pseudo-counterfactual pairs: samples sharing the same cluster assignment but belonging to different sensitive groups. By pulling these content-matched, cross-group samples together in the embedding space, ProtoFair encourages the encoder to learn representations that are invariant to the sensitive attribute. The method requires only sensitive attribute annotations, no target labels, and integrates seamlessly with both SimCLR and SupCon. Experiments on CelebA and UTKFace demonstrate consistent fairness improvements while maintaining competitive accuracy.

papers.title: From Concept to Capability: Evaluating 3D Gaussian Splatting for Synthetic Scene Editing in Autonomous Driving
papers.summary: From Concept to Capability: Evaluating 3D Gaussian Splatting for Synthetic Scene Editing in Autonomous Driving attacks the bottleneck that field-collected driving data cannot cover rare, high-severity safety scenarios, while the common belief that photorealistic reconstruction alone is sufficient for verification and validation ignores whether edited scenes remain faithful from unseen viewpoints. The paper pivots from treating 3DGS as a promising scene-editing concept toward making it an industrially checkable capability for ADS pipelines, especially for vehicles and pedestrians, the highest-exposure object classes. The mechanical shift is a Dynamic Fidelity Evaluation framework built in CARLA: train 3DGS reconstructions on an original trajectory using multimodal RGB-LiDAR data, then render controlled novel viewpoints at increasing lateral, longitudinal, and vertical offsets and compare them against simulator ground truth. Rather than relying only on PSNR/SSIM, the evaluation adds YOLOv10 and DETR precision/recall plus semantic mIoU and Dice, with explicit pass/fail criteria tied to safety use cases and a <10% degradation threshold. Among DeformGS, PVG, StreetGS, and OmniRe, OmniRe is the strongest overall, but all methods degrade as viewpoint distance grows, with pedestrian fidelity collapsing fastest laterally because deformable humans demand denser coverage than rigid vehicles. The unusual contribution is the industrial framing: viewpoint-aware validity tags, cross-dataset agent injection, and training-free edits such as object removal, translation, and cut-in or jaywalker synthesis. The paper points toward safety-calibrated, object-class-specific synthetic scene generation where reconstruction is accepted only within quantified visibility bounds.
papers.abstract: The perception of an Autonomous Driving System (ADS) critically depends on relevant, comprehensive, and diverse datasets to ensure its safety while operating in the environment. Field data collection lacks completeness with respect to the list of rare but still possible safety-related scenarios needed for the development, verification, and validation of the ADS. 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has shown promising capabilities for the reconstruction and editing of scenes based on data collected by cameras and LiDAR sensors. However, the industrial fidelity evaluation of reconstructions is underexplored, which is crucial when employing such methods in safety-related systems, especially for ADS. This becomes more challenging as ADS operates in a dynamic, uncontrolled environment with limited viewpoints and often partially occluded objects. This paper addresses this gap by proposing and implementing a framework (Fig. 1) to systematically analyze the capabilities and limitations of 3DGS for use in the reconstruction of safety-related scenes. It focuses on the quality of reconstruction for vehicles and pedestrians, which are the two most critical object classes for ADS. Our findings provide industry insights into the fidelity degradation of reconstructions from multiple novel viewpoints, both lateral and longitudinal, enabling the integration of these methods into real-world industrial AD software development and testing pipelines.

papers.title: Cross-Language Learning within Arabic Script for Low-Resource HTR
papers.summary: Cross-Language Learning within Arabic Script for Low-Resource HTR tackles the failure mode where handwritten text recognition collapses when labeled lines are scarce, especially for cursive Arabic-script languages whose letters differ mainly by dots and diacritics. Rather than assuming that each language must be learned in isolation or that multilingual transfer helps uniformly, the paper pivots from single-script scaling toward script-family joint training under strict low-resource control. The mechanical shift is simple but revealing: one CRNN+CTC recognizer is trained on the union of Arabic (KHATT), Urdu (NUST-UHWR/UNHD), and Persian (PHTD) line images, while the target language is throttled to K∈{100,500,1000} labeled lines, the character vocabulary is unified, transcriptions are reversed for right-to-left alignment, and decoding uses greedy CTC without an external language model. What distinguishes the work is the character-level transfer audit: it computes per-character ΔCER to show that gains are not global but concentrated on shared characters, especially rare and visually confusable ones, while language-specific symbols can remain flat or even worsen under joint optimization. In controlled line-level experiments, multi-script training consistently beats single-script baselines, yielding new low-resource state of the art, including CER 9.99 on PHTD and 17.20→14.45 on UNHD. The result suggests a future direction where low-resource HTR is driven by overlap-aware transfer, diversity-sensitive data selection, and selective sharing rather than blanket multilingual pooling.
papers.abstract: Handwritten Text Recognition (HTR) under limited labeled data remains a challenging problem, particularly for Arabic-script languages. Although modern sequence-based recognizers perform well in high-resource settings, their accuracy degrades sharply as training data becomes scarce. Arabic-script languages share a common writing system with substantial character overlap, motivating cross-script training as a strategy to mitigate data scarcity. We performed experiments on Arabic, Urdu, and Persian scripts and achieved improvements over single-script baselines (new SotA especially for low-resource settings). A key finding of our experiments is that cross-script transfer is largely driven by script-level overlap rather than uniform accuracy improvements. Through a statistical character-level analysis we show that gains are structurally concentrated on characters shared across scripts, while language-specific characters exhibit limited or negative transfer. These findings provide insight into transfer dynamics in low-resource script families. Detailed results include: We conduct a controlled line-level study of cross-script joint training for Arabic-script HTR under low-resource regimes (number of samples K \in 100, 500, 1000 labeled lines) on Arabic (KHATT), Urdu (NUST-UHWR), and Persian (PHTD). A CRNN model is trained on the union of multiple related Arabic-script datasets and evaluated on individual target languages. On Persian (PHTD), joint training achieves a Character Error Rate (CER) of 9.99, surpassing previously reported results despite not using the full available training data. On an Urdu dataset (UNHD), joint training reduces CER from 17.20 to 14.45. Code and data splits are released to ensure reproducibility.1

papers.title: SignMAE: Segmentation-Driven Self-Supervised Learning for Sign Language Recognition
papers.summary: SignMAE: Segmentation-Driven Self-Supervised Learning for Sign Language Recognition attacks a specific bottleneck in isolated sign language recognition: subtle handshape and motion differences are drowned out when encoders are pretrained on generic action-video objectives that mask random tubes or treat the whole frame as equally informative. The paper pivots from action-centric pretraining toward sign-centric representation learning, replacing random spatio-temporal masking with segmentation-guided, hand-arm-aware masking and reconstruction. Its core mechanical shift is to use off-the-shelf human parsing and keypoint detection to localize signer crops, discard frames where both hands hang naturally or are absent, and then pretrain modality-specific ViTs on only the linguistically salient tokens: a global video stream with tube masking, a hand-guided video stream with directional spatial masking over active one- or two-handed regions plus temporal masking, and a keypoint-heatmap stream masked only over hand regions. Unlike nearby masked-video work, the decoder is not asked to reconstruct whole frames; it reconstructs just the masked hand/arm or hand tokens, forcing the model to infer fine-grained hand configuration from partial context. The downstream system then freezes these specialized encoders and fuses them with lightweight cross-attention rather than joint end-to-end multimodal training. Evaluated on WLASL, NMFs-CSL, and Slovo, SignMAE reaches state-of-the-art top-1 accuracies while using fewer frames and fewer modalities than heavier baselines, suggesting a research direction toward region-aware self-supervision, modality-tailored masking policies, and efficient fine-grained video understanding for gesture-centric languages.
papers.abstract: Subtle hand differences make sign language recognition challenging, yet many existing methods rely on encoders pretrained on generic action datasets that poorly capture such fine-grained cues. We propose a self-supervised pretraining method for sign language recognition that uses segmentation-based masking to adapt to the presence and motion of key body parts, rather than treating hand poses as static visual tokens. The resulting mask-and-reconstruct objective improves fine-grained sign representation learning. On WLASL, NMFs-CSL, and Slovo, our encoder achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving per-instance and per-class Top-1 accuracy while using fewer input frames and modalities than comparable encoders.

papers.title: From Spherical to Gaussian: A Comparative Analysis of Point Cloud Cropping Strategies in Large-Scale 3D Environments
papers.summary: From Spherical to Gaussian: A Comparative Analysis of Point Cloud Cropping Strategies in Large-Scale 3D Environments attacks the bottleneck that billion-point scenes still cannot fit modern 3D networks even after downsampling, forcing semantic segmentation pipelines to chunk space into subclouds and, under the usual spherical crop, throw away surrounding geometry at the crop boundary. The paper implicitly challenges the old assumption that crop shape is a neutral preprocessing detail or that gains mainly come from new backbones; instead it pivots from architecture-centric scaling and room-splitting toward input-side context preservation. The mechanical shift is to replace the hard, uniform cutoff of spherical crops with distance-based probabilistic selection functions—exponential, Gaussian, and linear—chosen to keep similar point counts while enlarging the spatial extent seen by the model. Using occupancy-grid center selection for fair comparison and averaging fused subcloud predictions back into the full scene, the authors test OA-CNNs and Point Transformer v3 from scratch on SemanticBridge, S3DIS full areas, and Paris-Lille-3D. The unusual contribution is that cropping itself becomes the control lever: a lightweight, fast alternative to changing the network, yet capable of changing learned point distributions. Results show the largest gains in large outdoor scenes, with Gaussian cropping reaching new SOTA on SemanticBridge and linear cropping lifting OA-CNNs on Paris-Lille-3D, while indoor S3DIS benefits are smaller and PTv3 is less robust to non-uniform crops. This points toward future work on geometry-aware resampling, uncertainty-driven center selection, and treating crop design as a first-class part of 3D perception systems.
papers.abstract: Large-scale 3D point clouds can consist of billions of points. Even after downsampling, these point clouds are too large for modern 3D neural networks. In order to develop a semantic understanding of the scene, the point clouds are divided into smaller subclouds that can be processed. Typically, this division is done using spherical crops, resulting in a loss of surrounding geometric context. To address this issue, we propose alternative methods that produce subclouds with larger crop sizes while maintaining a similar number of points. Specifically, we compare exponential, Gaussian, and linear cropping methods with the spherical method. We evaluated two 3D deep learning model architectures using multiple indoor and outdoor environment datasets. Our results demonstrate that altering the cropping strategy can enhance model performance, especially for large-scale outdoor scenes, yielding new state-of-the-art results. Code is available at https://github.com/mvg-inatech/point_cloud_cropping

papers.title: Ultrasound Vision-Language Alignment via Contrastive Learning
papers.summary: Ultrasound Vision-Language Alignment via Contrastive Learning tackles the bottleneck that ultrasound foundation models, though strong on structured prediction, remain vision-only and therefore brittle for zero-shot and few-shot transfer when task-specific labels are scarce. The paper pivots from scaling image encoders alone toward clinical image-text alignment, replacing a purely discriminative ultrasound workflow with a CLIP-style dual-encoder trained by symmetric InfoNCE in a shared 256-dimensional space. EchoCare-CLIP is trained on a curated 16K-pair multi-organ corpus spanning breast, liver, lung, and thyroid, where 78%+ of captions come from expert reports and the rest are synthesized through a three-tier template pipeline plus optional Qwen-based rewriting. The mechanical shift is not just adding text, but testing whether caption supervision, encoder choice, and fine-tuning depth preserve transfer geometry: CLIP-text versus BioClinicalBERT, template versus LLM captions, projection-head-only versus partial/full unfreezing. Compared with nearby medical VLM work, this study is unusual in showing that lexical diversity is not a proxy for better supervision: template captions often match or outperform LLM-generated ones. On BUSI and AULI, the best models improve alignment to 0.682 and reach zero-shot accuracies of 0.709 and 0.626, yet full end-to-end fine-tuning overfits and hurts transfer. The result points toward future ultrasound research centered on carefully balanced domain adaptation, verified caption quality, and retrieval/classification objectives that jointly optimize semantics and generalization.
papers.abstract: Ultrasound foundation models have achieved strong performance on structured prediction tasks but remain exclusively vision-based, limiting zero-shot and few-shot transfer to novel tasks where task-specific annotation is scarce. We address this gap with EchoCare-CLIP, a CLIP-style dual-encoder contrastive framework that aligns ultrasound images with clinical text in a shared embedding space. We curate a multi-organ corpus of over 16K image-text pairs spanning breast, liver, lung, and thyroid, with over 78% of captions derived from expert-annotated reports, and complement the remainder with a three-tier template-based and LLM-based caption generation pipeline. We evaluate model configurations spanning two text encoder families (CLIP, BioClinicalBERT) and two caption strategies (template-based, LLM-generated) against OpenAI CLIP and BiomedCLIP baselines. Our trained models consistently improve cross-modal alignment over baselines, with the best configuration achieving a paired alignment score of 0.682. However, stronger alignment does not guarantee better downstream performance: CLIP-based variants with partial fine-tuning achieve the strongest zero-shot classification on external held-out datasets (0.709 on BUSI; 0.626 on AULI), while full end-to-end fine-tuning degrades transfer due to overfitting. On linear probing and few-shot adaptation, model rankings are dataset-dependent, reflecting a trade-off between domain adaptation and representational generalizability. We further show that template-based captions match or outperform LLM-generated captions, suggesting lexical diversity is not a proxy for caption quality. Taken together, our results demonstrate that ultrasound vision-language alignment is achievable from public data alone, but robust clinical transfer requires careful balancing of domain adaptation, encoder capacity, and caption supervision quality.

papers.title: From Where Things Are to What They Are For: Benchmarking Spatial-Functional Intelligence in Multimodal LLMs
papers.summary: From Where Things Are to What They Are For: Benchmarking Spatial-Functional Intelligence in Multimodal LLMs attacks a blind spot in today’s video MLLM evaluation: models are often scored on object recognition, geometry, or single-frame spatial relations, yet real agentic intelligence requires maintaining a coherent cognitive map and reasoning about affordances, control, and tool use across time. The paper pivots from “where is it?” benchmarking toward “what is it for?” cognition, replacing perception-only QA with SFI-Bench, a video benchmark of 1,555 expert-annotated questions from 134 egocentric indoor scans in ARKitScenes and ScanNet++. The mechanical shift is in how tasks are constructed and what they demand: metadata is first extracted with Gemini-2.5-Pro, merged across passes, then turned into rigorously filtered questions spanning structured spatial reasoning and functional reasoning, including global/conditional counting, cross-view multi-hop path reasoning, layout inference, functional association, operation planning, and knowledge-grounded troubleshooting. Unlike nearby spatial benchmarks, SFI-Bench forces models to stitch multi-view temporal evidence, preserve spatial memory, infer object affordances, and sometimes retrieve manuals via web search. Experiments across Gemini, GPT-5, Qwen3-VL, InternVL, and LLaVA variants show a consistent bottleneck in compositional counting and functional grounding; stronger reasoning helps, but longer chains often drift semantically, and web search only helps when reasoning capacity is already high. The paper points toward multimodal agents that fuse visual evidence, memory, and external knowledge into actionable spatial-functional cognition.
papers.abstract: Human-level agentic intelligence extends beyond low-level geometric perception, evolving from recognizing where things are to understanding what they are for. While existing benchmarks effectively evaluate the geometric perception capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs), they fall short of probing the higher-order cognitive abilities required for grounded intelligence. To address this gap, we introduce the Spatial-Functional Intelligence Benchmark (SFI-Bench), a video-based benchmark with over 1,500 expert-annotated questions derived from diverse egocentric indoor video scans. SFI-Bench systematically evaluates two complementary dimensions of advanced reasoning: (1) Structured Spatial Reasoning, which requires understanding complex layouts and forming coherent spatial representations, and (2) Functional Reasoning, which involves inferring object affordances and their context-dependent utility. The benchmark includes tasks such as conditional counting, multi-hop relational reasoning, functional pairing, and knowledge-grounded troubleshooting, directly challenging models to integrate perception, memory, and inference. Our experiments reveal that current MLLMs consistently struggle to combine spatial memory with functional reasoning and external knowledge, highlighting a critical bottleneck in achieving grounded intelligence. SFI-Bench therefore provides a diagnostic tool for measuring progress toward more cognitively capable and truly grounded multimodal agents.

papers.title: Video Generation with Predictive Latents
papers.summary: Video Generation with Predictive Latents attacks a specific bottleneck in video VAEs: better reconstruction does not automatically yield better generative latents, because standard training optimizes pixel fidelity while leaving the latent space insufficiently “diffusable” for downstream diffusion. The paper pivots from the prevailing reconstruction-first tokenizer workflow toward predictive world modeling, asking what kind of latent structure makes video generation easier rather than merely sharper. Its mechanical shift is a predictive reconstruction objective: during training it randomly drops future frame groups, encodes only the observed past, then pads the missing latent slots and forces the decoder to both reconstruct the seen frames and predict the withheld future. This is paired with a motion-aware loss on temporal differences to suppress the copy-shortcut that would otherwise let the model ignore dynamics and memorize static content, plus a decoder-only fine-tuning stage that removes the train-test gap without changing the encoder. Unlike nearby video-VAE work that mainly inflates image VAEs or tunes compression ratios, PV-VAE explicitly shapes latents toward temporal coherence and motion priors, with no extra hyperparameters. Evaluated on UCF101, RealEstate10K, and Kinetics-400, and plugged into a Latte latent diffusion pipeline, it delivers 52% faster convergence and a 34.42 FVD gain over Wan2.2 VAE on UCF101, while also improving optical flow, next-frame prediction, and point tracking features. The result suggests a future where video tokenizers are trained as predictive latent world models, not passive compressors.
papers.abstract: Video Variational Autoencoder (VAE) enables latent video generative modeling by mapping the visual world into compact spatiotemporal latent spaces, improving training efficiency and stability. While existing video VAEs achieve commendable reconstruction quality, continued optimization of reconstruction does not necessarily translate into improved generative performance. How to enhance the diffusability of video latents remains a critical and unresolved challenge. In this work, inspired by principles of predictive world modeling, we investigate the potential of predictive learning to improve the video generative modeling. To this end, we introduce a simple and effective predictive reconstruction objective that unifies predictive learning with video reconstruction. Specifically, we randomly discard future frames and encode only partial past observations, while training the decoder to reconstruct the observed frames and predict future ones simultaneously. This design encourages the latent space to encode temporally predictive structures and build a more coherent understanding of video dynamics, thereby improving generation quality. Our model, termed Predictive Video VAE (PV-VAE), achieves superior performance on video generation, with 52% faster convergence and a 34.42 FVD improvement over the Wan2.2 VAE on UCF101. Furthermore, comprehensive analyses demonstrate that PV-VAE not only exhibits favorable scalability, with generative performance improving alongside VAE training, but also yields consistent gains in downstream video understanding, underscoring a latent space that effectively captures temporal coherence and motion priors.

papers.title: FLoRA: Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Flood Area Segmentation via Cross-Modal Multi-Task Distillation Network
papers.summary: FLoRA: Fusion-Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Flood Area Segmentation via Cross-Modal Multi-Task Distillation Network attacks a specific disaster-response bottleneck: SAR is dependable under clouds and night, but its backscatter is semantically opaque, while optical imagery is interpretable but often unavailable exactly when floods occur. The paper challenges the common workflow that splits SAR-to-optical translation and flood segmentation into separate models, or fuses modalities at raw-pixel/feature concatenation level, implying that visual realism and hydrologic correctness can be optimized independently. FLoRA pivots from isolated translation or segmentation toward a single teacher-guided latent-fusion regime where an optical prior stream, driven by RGB and NDVI cues, distills semantic structure into SAR representations. The mechanical shift is a fusion-latent space built with multiscale windowed cross-attention, FiLM conditioning, and confidence-gated residuals that prevent overcorrection, followed by gradient-decoupled dual decoders: one reconstructs high-fidelity RGB, the other predicts flood-water masks. Unlike nearby GAN or U-Net baselines, it couples reconstruction losses (Charbonnier, SSIM, FFT magnitude, edge) with hydrology-aware Dice/BCE/edge alignment and feature-level manifold distillation. Evaluated on SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS winter tiles, FLoRA reports stronger PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS and Dice/IoU than fusion baselines, including 33.61 PSNR and 0.71 IoU on SEN1FLOODS11. The result points toward physically consistent, semantically anchored remote-sensing models that can degrade gracefully to SAR-only inference while still benefiting from optical teacher priors during training.
papers.abstract: Accurate flood water mapping is critical for disaster management, yet current methods struggle to fully exploit the potential of spaceborne imagery. Optical data offers high interpretability but is limited by environmental conditions, whereas SAR provides reliable all-weather coverage with reduced visual interpretability. FLoRA (Fusion Latent for Optical Reconstruction and Area Segmentation) is a cross-modal multi-task framework that jointly reconstructs high-fidelity optical imagery and segments flood water regions from Sentinel 1 SAR by fusing the complementary strengths of optical and SAR data. During training, a lightweight optical teacher (driven by RGB and NDVI priors) provides pyramidal features that guide SAR representations into a fusion latent space via multiscale windowed cross attention and FiLM conditioning, with gated residuals preventing overcorrection. This design enables multi-task learning across two complementary objectives: (a) SAR-to-optical translation for fine-grained RGB reconstruction and (b) flood water region segmentation for hydrologic interpretation. The dual decoders are optimized using Charbonnier SSIM for structural fidelity, edge FFT magnitude losses for spectral realism, and Dice BCE hydrology-aware edge alignment for precise flood water delineation. A feature distillation constraint further aligns fused SAR features with the optical teacher's manifold. Evaluations on SEN1FLOODS11, DEEPFLOOD, and SEN12MS demonstrate that FLoRA surpasses fusion baselines in PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS, demonstrating that multi-modal fusion within a teacher-guided latent space yields semantically faithful and physically consistent flood-water intelligence from spaceborne observations.

papers.title: SpecEdit: Training-Free Acceleration for Diffusion based Image Editing via Semantic Locking
papers.summary: SpecEdit: Training-Free Acceleration for Diffusion based Image Editing via Semantic Locking attacks the bottleneck that instruction-guided diffusion editors still denoise every spatial token at full resolution for dozens of steps, making real-time editing expensive even when only a tiny region actually changes. It challenges the old spatial-acceleration assumption that edges, channel variance, or uniformly partial upsampling are good proxies for where compute should go; in editing, those low-level cues often highlight static structure instead of semantically modified content, causing redundant refinement and structural drift. The paper pivots from heuristic, geometry-first token selection toward semantics-aware verification, using a draft-and-verify loop inspired by speculative decoding: a 16× downsampled draft is fully denoised first, then token-level perceptual discrepancies between the draft and the original image identify edit-relevant regions, while a sparse uniform token set preserves global geometry. The mechanical shift is that only verified tokens are expanded and updated with high-resolution Transformer attention/MLP computation; untouched tokens remain coarse until final reconstruction, yielding a mixed-resolution latent without any training. This is unusual relative to nearby work because it is explicitly training-free, editing-specific, and orthogonal to step distillation and caching. On Qwen-Image-Edit and FLUX.1-Kontext-dev over GEdit-Bench and ImgEdit-Bench, it reaches up to 10× and 7× acceleration while maintaining strong semantic consistency, and up to 13× when combined with existing accelerators. The result points toward semantics-driven spatial compute, where future diffusion systems allocate resolution by verified change rather than saliency.
papers.abstract: Diffusion-based image editing offers strong semantic controllability, but remains computationally expensive due to iterative high-resolution denoising over all spatial tokens. Dynamic-resolution sampling reduces this cost by performing early steps at reduced resolution. However, existing approaches prioritize upsampling using low-level heuristics such as edge detection or channel variance, which are weakly aligned with editing semantics and may lead to structural inconsistency. Moreover, spatial regions are often upsampled without verifying whether semantic modification is actually required, resulting in redundant high-resolution computation and accumulated errors. Therefore, we propose SpecEdit, a training-free dynamic-resolution framework tailored for diffusion-based image editing. SpecEdit follows a draft-and-verify scheme: a low-resolution draft first estimates the semantic outcome, after which token-level discrepancies are used to identify edit-relevant tokens for high-resolution denoising, while the remaining tokens stay at a coarse resolution. Experiments on Qwen-Image-Edit and FLUX.1-Kontext-dev demonstrate up to 10x and 7x acceleration, while maintaining strong quality. SpecEdit is complementary to step distillation and other acceleration techniques, achieving up to 13x speedup when combined with existing methods. Our code is in supplementary material and will be released on GitHub.

papers.title: Cross-Polarization Fusion of VV AND VH SAR Observations for Improved Flood Mapping
papers.summary: Cross-Polarization Fusion of VV AND VH SAR Observations for Improved Flood Mapping attacks the failure mode of SAR flood segmentation in vegetated, mixed land-cover, and other heterogeneous scenes where open water, flooded vegetation, surface roughness, and soil moisture produce confusable backscatter, making single-polarization mapping brittle. It pivots from the usual assumption that one Sentinel-1 channel or simple early concatenation is enough toward explicitly modeling complementary VV surface-scattering cues and VH volume-scattering cues as a joint segmentation signal.
