The mechanical shift is a Cross-Polarization Fusion (CPF) module inserted at the input stage of standard encoder-decoder backbones: VV and VH are first processed by separate lightweight convolutional stems, augmented with ratio features (VV/VH and log(VV/VH)), then fused through bidirectional spatial attention and channel attention so each polarization selectively attends to the other before 3×3 projection. Unlike nearby flood-mapping work that changes the backbone or reports aggregate gains from generic multi-polarization input, this paper keeps architecture, optimizer, data splits, and training identical across VV-only, VH-only, addition fusion, concat fusion, and CPF, isolating fusion as the causal lever. On the DeepFlood dataset with Sentinel-1 dual-polarized imagery from Hurricane Florence and an unseen Hurricane Matthew test event, CPF improves IoU/F1 over both U-Net and a convolutional autoencoder, especially along vegetated flood boundaries, yielding sharper masks and better robustness under cross-event shift. The result points toward future flood AI that treats polarization interaction, uncertainty, and physically informed attention as first-class design variables rather than auxiliary inputs.
papers.abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery is widely used for flood monitoring due to its all-weather and day-night imaging capability. However, flood mapping using single-polarization SAR data remains challenging in complex environments where surface and volume scattering coexist. In this paper, we investigate the effectiveness of cross-polarization fusion of VV and VH SAR observations for improved flood mapping. A deep learning-based segmentation framework is employed to jointly exploit complementary information from VV and VH polarizations. To ensure a fair evaluation, three configurations are compared under identical training conditions: VV only, VH only, and fused VV-VH input. Performance is assessed using standard flood mapping metrics, including Intersection over Union (IoU) and F1-score, along with qualitative visual analysis. Experimental results demonstrate that VV-VH fusion consistently outperforms single-polarization models, particularly in vegetated and heterogeneous flood regions, leading to more accurate flood boundary delineation. The findings highlight the importance of cross-polarization SAR fusion for enhancing the reliability of SAR-based flood mapping in disaster monitoring applications.

papers.title: Heterogeneous Model Fusion for Privacy-Aware Multi-Camera Surveillance via Synthetic Domain Adaptation
papers.summary: Heterogeneous Model Fusion for Privacy-Aware Multi-Camera Surveillance via Synthetic Domain Adaptation attacks the deployment bottleneck in smart-city surveillance where cameras live in different environments, labels are partially overlapping, and raw imagery cannot be centralized because of privacy and regulation. It challenges the old assumption that multi-source domain-adaptive detection can rely on shared access to source/target data, homogeneous detector heads, and large target datasets for style transfer. The paper pivots from centralized adaptation and homogeneous federated averaging toward privacy-preserving federated multi-camera learning with synthetic target-domain augmentation and heterogeneous model fusion. The mechanical shift is a three-stage HeroCrystal pipeline: first, a one-shot target-aware diffusion module learns a target camera’s visual style from a single reference image via an instance prompt [V] plus a prior-preserving generic prompt, then composes class-controlled prompts to synthesize rare objects in target style and auto-labels them with an off-the-shelf detector; second, clients train source-only Probabilistic Faster R-CNN detectors with uncertainty-aware box regression and a dynamic contrastive objective that reverses the usual Moon assumption when the global model is still weak, interpolating between global-positive/local-negative and the opposite; third, the server fuses only backbones and distills client heads using an inconsistent categories integration algorithm that filters pseudo-labels by category availability and cross-head IoU consistency to reconcile label-space and architecture heterogeneity. Unlike nearby FL or DA work, it jointly handles privacy, long-tail categories, and heterogeneous heads. On Sim10k/KITTI/Foggy→Cityscapes and Cityscapes/KITTI→BDD100K, it reports +2.1 mAP over prior privacy-preserving baselines and 33.4 mAP SOTA, pointing toward synthetic, federated, privacy-critical surveillance systems.
papers.abstract: We propose HeroCrystal, a novel privacy-preserving framework for multi-camera domain-adaptive object detection, addressing challenges such as data privacy, class imbalance, and heterogeneous architectures. Our framework consists of three key stages. In the Generated Stage, we introduce a one-shot, target-aware diffusion-based generation module that learns visual style from a single target-domain image while leveraging prompt-based control to synthesize specific object instances. Unlike conventional style transfer-based methods that require large target datasets and ignore semantic-level discrepancies, our approach enables privacy-preserving augmentation to reduce ethical concerns, and introduces controllable rare object generation to mitigate long-tailed category degradation. In the Federated Stage, we employ probabilistic Faster R-CNN on the client side to improve localization accuracy, and a dynamic model contrastive strategy to suppress domain-specific bias. The server side performs model fusion across heterogeneous architectures without accessing raw data. Finally, in the Distilled Stage, we propose an inconsistent categories integration algorithm to resolve label inconsistency and architecture heterogeneity across clients. Extensive experiments on multiple cross-domain detection benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing multi-source domain adaptation and federated learning baselines under multi-class, privacy-preserving settings. Our method improves mAP by +2.1% over prior privacy-preserving approaches and achieves a new state-of-the-art mAP of 33.4%, highlighting the effectiveness of HeroCrystal in enabling practical multi-camera AI surveillance systems.

papers.title: RAFNet: Region-Aware Fusion Network for Pansharpening
papers.summary: RAFNet: Region-Aware Fusion Network for Pansharpening attacks the core pansharpening bottleneck of reconstructing HRMS imagery from LRMS+PAN when standard deep models either blur high-frequency details with static convolutions or pay quadratic cost for global attention that ignores the regional sparsity of remote-sensing scenes. It implicitly challenges the assumption that pansharpening should be handled by uniform kernels or dense token mixing over the whole image, and pivots from global, spatially invariant fusion toward region-aware, frequency-decoupled processing. The mechanical shift is a two-part coupling: Spatial Adaptive Refinement first applies DWT to split LL/LH/HL/HH bands, then uses differentiable K-means clustering on the low-frequency structure to build region-specific adaptive convolution kernels via low-rank kernel generation; Clustered Frequency Aggregation then routes attention through those semantic clusters, using dense query-key interactions only inside a cluster and cluster-mean keys for inter-cluster relations, which replaces quadratic attention with sparse region-aware aggregation. This is unusual relative to nearby pansharpening Transformers because the clustering mask is not just a heuristic window but a learned routing prior shared across convolution and attention, embedded in a progressive multi-scale wavelet pyramid. Evaluated on Wald-protocol reduced- and full-resolution benchmarks for WorldView-3 and GaoFen-2, RAFNet reports state-of-the-art PSNR/SSIM/SAM/ERGAS and HQNR/Dλ/Ds, while also cutting attention redundancy and FLOPs; the main tradeoff is sensitivity to cluster count, with performance peaking near K=64 at inference. This points toward future remote-sensing fusion models that explicitly exploit semantic geometry, sparse routing, and frequency-aware control rather than ever-denser global attention.
papers.abstract: Pansharpening aims to generate high-resolution multispectral (HRMS) images by fusing low-resolution multispectral (LRMS) and high-resolution panchromatic (PAN) images. Although deep learning has advanced this field, mainstream frequency-based methods relying on standard scaled dot-product attention suffer from quadratic computational complexity and fail to exploit the inherent regional sparsity of remote sensing imagery. Furthermore, existing spatial enhancement strategies typically employ static convolution kernels, which struggle to adapt to the complex frequency and regional variations of PAN and MS images. To address these bottlenecks, we propose a Region-Aware Fusion (RAFNet) Network that synergistically models spatial and frequency information. Specifically, we design a Spatial Adaptive Refinement (SAR) module that leverages the discrete wavelet transform (DWT) for directional frequency separation and K-means clustering for regional partitioning, which enables the dynamic construction of region-specific adaptive convolution kernels, achieving spatially and frequency-adaptive feature enhancement. Moreover, we introduce a Clustered Frequency Aggregation (CFA) module based on a sparse attention mechanism guided by the semantic clusters, which executes a region-aware sparse attention strategy that drastically reduces computational redundancy while ensuring high-quality frequency feature extraction. In addition we integrated these modules into a progressive, multi-level spatial-frequency network architecture to facilitate robust interaction and accurate image reconstruction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed RAFNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art pansharpening methods in both reduced- and full-resolution assessments. The code is available at https://github.com/PatrickNod/RAFNet.

papers.title: SlimDiffSR: Toward Lightweight and Efficient Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Diffusion Model Distillation
papers.summary: SlimDiffSR: Toward Lightweight and Efficient Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution via Diffusion Model Distillation attacks the deployment bottleneck of diffusion-based remote sensing SR, where iterative sampling and large latent-diffusion backbones deliver strong perceptual quality but are too slow and heavy for Earth-observation workloads. It challenges the assumption behind recent single-step diffusion SR that one fixed timestep is enough for all degradations, and pivots from “compress a generic diffusion model” toward “build an uncertainty-adaptive teacher, then distill it into a remote-sensing-specific student.” The mechanical shift is two-stage: first, an uncertainty-guided timestep assignment maps pixelwise reconstruction difficulty to a continuous noise level and inverted timestep, so harder regions receive stronger generative guidance in the teacher; second, the teacher is pruned and redesigned with semantic-aware removal of deep UNet blocks, a lightweight TAESD-style VAE, fused convolutions that bypass the 64→4→320 information bottleneck, and RS-tailored operators—frequency-separable convolution for sparse high-frequency detail, direction-separable convolution for roads/rivers/building edges, and a query-driven global aggregation module that approximates attention with linear-cost learnable queries. Distillation goes beyond MSE by adding MMD to align feature distributions, not just pointwise activations. On AID, NWPU-RESISC45, DOTA, DIOR, and real Sentinel-2/NAIP pairs, SlimDiffSR reaches up to 200× faster inference and 20× fewer parameters than multistep diffusion baselines while remaining competitive in perceptual quality, pointing toward adaptive, domain-structured, distillation-centric diffusion systems for large-scale remote sensing deployment.
papers.abstract: Diffusion models have recently achieved remarkable performance in image super-resolution (SR), but their high computational cost limits practical deployment in remote sensing applications. To address this issue, we propose SlimDiffSR, a lightweight and efficient diffusion-based framework for real-world remote sensing image super-resolution. Unlike existing single-step diffusion methods that rely on fixed timesteps, we first introduce an uncertainty-guided timestep assignment strategy to construct a stronger single-step teacher model, where reconstruction difficulty is explicitly linked to diffusion timesteps, enabling adaptive generative strength. Building upon this teacher, we further present a structured pruning strategy tailored to remote sensing imagery, which systematically removes redundant semantic modules and replaces standard operations with lightweight designs, including frequency-separable convolution, direction-separable convolution, and a query-driven global aggregation module. These components explicitly exploit the unique characteristics of remote sensing data, such as sparse high-frequency details, strong directional patterns, and long-range spatial dependencies. To enhance knowledge transfer, we incorporate Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) into the distillation process to align feature distributions between the teacher and student models. Extensive experiments on multiple remote sensing benchmarks demonstrate that SlimDiffSR achieves a favorable balance between efficiency and reconstruction quality. In particular, it attains up to $200\times$ inference acceleration and a $20\times$ reduction in model parameters compared with multi-step diffusion models, while achieving competitive perceptual quality and clearly outperforming existing lightweight diffusion baselines in efficiency. The code is available at: https://github.com/wwangcece/SlimDiffSR.

papers.title: Super-resolution of airborne laser scanning point clouds for forest inventory
papers.summary: Super-resolution of airborne laser scanning point clouds for forest inventory attacks the bottleneck that airborne laser scanning (ALS) gives wide-area coverage but leaves tree stems under-sampled, noisy, and fragmented, so stem localization and diameter-at-breast-height estimation must usually fall back on indirect allometric regressions or crown-based heuristics. The paper pivots from “predict forest attributes from sparse ALS points” toward “reconstruct measurement-ready tree geometry,” challenging the assumption that generic point-cloud upsampling methods for synthetic objects or urban scenes transfer to forests. The mechanical shift is 3DFSR, an octree-based voxel CNN with a U-Net backbone that converts coordinate regression into voxel occupancy prediction, simultaneously denoising and upsampling by deciding which octree nodes are empty/occupied and then refining geometry with displacement heads; training combines structure loss with regression loss and works on 10 m cubes. Unlike nearby upsampling work, it is explicitly forest-specific, scene-level, and validated as an inventory preprocessor rather than only by geometric fidelity. Evaluated on temperate forests in the U.S. and boreal forests in Germany, using TLS/MLS as references, it beats interpolation, PU-Net, and GradPU, reaching 0.249 m Chamfer Distance and 2.711 m Hausdorff Distance. More importantly, the enhanced point clouds let TLS/MLS stem detectors and circle-fitting DBH pipelines run directly, lifting stem-detection F1 from 0.71 to 0.97 and DBH RMSE from 13.45 cm to 6.43 cm, while also supporting stem reconstruction and cross-platform generalization without transfer learning. This suggests a direction where forest LiDAR research moves from indirect estimation toward sensor-agnostic geometric restoration for explicit, fine-grained inventory.
papers.abstract: Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) can collect point clouds across large areas, enabling large-scale forest inventory. However, ALS point clouds are sparse and noisy, resulting in inaccurate individual-tree-level forest inventory, such as stem localization and tree size estimation. To overcome this problem, we propose a deep learning model, 3D Forest Super Resolution (3DFSR), to simultaneously improve point density and reduce noise for ALS forest point cloud. 3DFSR is a voxel-based CNN with a U-Net architecture. The proposed 3DFSR is evaluated on ALS point clouds collected in both temperate forests in the U.S. and boreal forests in Germany. Experimental results demonstrate that 3DFSR can generate finer point clouds of tree structure than other state-of-the-art point cloud super-resolution algorithms, achieving 0.249 m Chamfer Distance and 2.711 m Hausdorff Distance. Furthermore, to verify the effectiveness of 3DFSR point clouds in forest inventory, we conduct stem detection, DBH measurements, and stem reconstruction on both original ALS point clouds and 3DFSR enhanced point clouds. We find that stem detection and reconstruction algorithms developed for TLS/MLS point clouds can directly work on our 3DFSR point clouds, and DBH can be derived with circle-fitting method. F1 score of stem detection is improved from 0.71 on original ALS point clouds to 0.97 on 3DFSR point clouds; DBH estimation improves from 13.45 cm RMSE using allometric equations to 6.43 cm using circle fitting; comparing to stems reconstruction from MLS point clouds, stem reconstructed from 3DFSR point clouds has 0.170 m of Chamfer Distance and 0.377 m of Hausdorff Distance, and 0.95 R2 volume estimation. Finally, we find that the proposed 3DFSR is applicable to process point densities from 10 to 1700 points/m2; it also can be generalized across data collected from different LiDAR platforms without transfer learning.

papers.title: Metric Unreliability in Multimodal Machine Unlearning: A Systematic Analysis and Principled Unified Score
papers.summary: Metric Unreliability in Multimodal Machine Unlearning: A Systematic Analysis and Principled Unified Score attacks the evaluation bottleneck in VLM unlearning: under GDPR-style erasure, methods are being compared with metrics that do not agree, so “best” unlearning depends on whether one looks at forget accuracy, retain accuracy, membership inference, activation distance, or JS divergence. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that these metrics are interchangeable proxies for a single forgetting quality and pivots from method design toward metric reliability as the central research object. The mechanical shift is a systematic Kendall’s τ study over 36 unlearned LLaVA-1.5-7B checkpoints across MLLMU-Bench, UnLOK-VQA, and MMUBench, with replication on BLIP-2 OPT-2.7B, showing two opposing metric clusters, {FA, RA, MIA} versus {AD, JS}, and lower agreement in multimodal VQA than unimodal CIFAR-10, implying that dual image-text pathways amplify inconsistency. It then replaces equal-weight leaderboard aggregation with a data-calibrated Unified Quality Score (UQS), whose weights come from each metric’s Spearman correlation with an oracle retrained only on the retain set; RA is most reliable, FA is negatively correlated, and UQS stays rank-stable under 100 random perturbations. Unusually, the paper also exposes a missing evaluation axis, knowledge recoverability, via rephrased, indirect, and negation probes that recover supposedly forgotten facts even when FA is zero. The result is a clearer direction for future work: scalable recoverability-aware unlearning evaluation and context-calibrated scoring, not metric-agnostic benchmark chasing.
papers.abstract: Machine unlearning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) is required for compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), yet current evaluation practices are inconsistent. We present the first systematic study of metric reliability in multimodal unlearning. Five standard metrics, Forget Accuracy (FA), Retain Accuracy (RA), Membership Inference Attack (MIA), Activation Distance (AD), and JS divergence (JS), yield conflicting method rankings across three VQA benchmarks (MLLMU-Bench, UnLOK-VQA, MMUBench). Kendall tau analysis over 36 unlearned LLaVA-1.5-7B models reveals two opposing clusters, {FA, RA, MIA} and {AD, JS}, with tau_FA_AD = -0.26, reproduced on BLIP-2 OPT-2.7B. Agreement is lower in multimodal VQA (average tau = 0.086) than in unimodal classification (average tau = 0.158; difference = 0.072), indicating that dual image-and-text pathways amplify inconsistency. We introduce the Unified Quality Score (UQS), a composite metric with weights derived from each metric's Spearman correlation with the oracle distance d(M_hat, M_star), where M_star is the oracle model retrained only on the retain set. RA shows the strongest reliability (rho = 0.484, p = 0.003), while FA is negatively correlated (rho = -0.418, p = 0.011). UQS yields stable rankings under 100 random weight perturbations (tau = 0.647 +- 0.262). We release the benchmark, 36 checkpoints, and an interactive leaderboard. Code and pre-computed results are available at https://github.com/neurips26/UnifiedUnl.

papers.title: MultiSense-Pneumo: A Multimodal Learning Framework for Pneumonia Screening in Resource-Constrained Settings
papers.summary: MultiSense-Pneumo: A Multimodal Learning Framework for Pneumonia Screening in Resource-Constrained Settings attacks the bottleneck that frontline pneumonia assessment in low-resource clinics is rarely image-only: symptoms, cough, speech, and radiography arrive unevenly, yet most prior computational work still assumes a unimodal chest-X-ray workflow. The paper pivots from radiograph-centric classification toward a screening-and-triage stack that mirrors community-health reasoning and can run fully offline on laptop-class hardware. The mechanical shift is not end-to-end multimodal representation learning, but a modular evidence-aggregation pipeline in which each channel is converted into a normalized risk signal and then fused transparently. Structured symptom answers are handled by deterministic guideline-inspired triage with hard escalation rules for severe dyspnea, confusion, chest indrawing, or inability to feed; cough audio from Coswara is turned into MFCC/statistical features and scored by LightGBM with imbalance weighting; spoken language is transcribed by Whisper Small and reduced to a keyword-based clinical concern score; and chest radiographs use a ResNet-18 backbone with domain-adversarial training, synthetic blur/noise/contrast perturbations, and gradient reversal to reduce acquisition-shift sensitivity. The late-fusion operator weights imaging most heavily while preserving missing-modality flexibility and interpretability, and MedGemma is used only for offline narrative reporting. Experiments show the radiograph branch is robust under synthetic domain shifts, while the acoustic branch suffers poor minority-class recall, underscoring that weak signals help mainly as complementary context. The paper points toward deployable, auditable, multimodal screening systems that prioritize robustness, modularity, and partial-information inference over monolithic prediction.
papers.abstract: Pneumonia remains a leading global cause of morbidity and mortality, particularly in low resource settings where access to imaging, laboratory testing, and specialist care is limited. Clinical assessment relies on heterogeneous evidence, including symptoms, respiratory patterns, and chest imaging, making screening inherently multimodal. However, many existing computational approaches remain unimodal and focus primarily on radiographs. In this work, we present MultiSense-Pneumo, a multimodal framework for pneumonia oriented screening and triage support that integrates structured symptom descriptors, cough audio, spoken language, and chest radiographs. The system combines deterministic symptom triage, LightGBM based acoustic classification, domain adversarial radiograph analysis using ResNet 18, transformer based speech recognition, and an interpretable multimodal fusion operator. Each modality is transformed into a normalized risk signal and aggregated into a unified screening estimate, enabling transparent and modular decision support. MultiSense-Pneumo is designed for real world deployment under modest computational constraints and can operate fully offline on standard laptop class hardware, making it suitable for community health workers, rural clinics, and emergency response settings. Experimental results demonstrate robustness of the radiograph pathway under domain shifts, while highlighting limitations in minority class recall for acoustic signals. MultiSense-Pneumo is intended as a research prototype for screening and triage support rather than a clinically validated diagnostic system.

papers.title: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement: Methods and Results
papers.summary: NTIRE 2026 Challenge on Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement: Methods and Results frames low-light image enhancement as an on-device bottleneck rather than a pure restoration benchmark: the core problem is to recover brightness, suppress noise/artifacts, and preserve color in smartphone night scenes while staying under a 1 MB model budget. This implicitly challenges the older assumption that LLIE progress should be measured mainly by ever-larger models and single distortion metrics; the pivot is from scaling-heavy enhancement toward deployment-constrained, multi-metric perceptual restoration. The mechanical shift is visible in the submitted methods: teams replace conventional full-size U-Nets with compressed encoder–decoder backbones, depthwise separable and partial convolutions, structural reparameterization, and tiny Retinex/HVI/YUV/LAB branches; many add illumination-conditioned normalization, grayscale-guided cross-attention, multi-exposure fusion, or frozen classical preprocessors such as CLAHE and CPGA before learned refinement. The challenge uses a new smartphone dataset from Huawei and Apple, spanning dim, extremely low-light, backlit, uneven, indoor, and outdoor night scenes at 3024×4032 resolution, with 349 training scenes, 49 validation scenes, and 102 hidden test scenes. Evaluation aggregates SSIM, LPIPS, DISTS, LIQE, MUSIQ, and Q-Align, then breaks ties by parameter count, emphasizing perceptual realism and efficiency together. The reported outcome is broad but consistent: substantial gains over prior efficient LLIE baselines, with the strongest systems pairing compact priors with metric-aware training. This points toward a future LLIE agenda centered on multi-objective, hardware-aware enhancement systems rather than isolated quality maximization.
papers.abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive review of the NITRE 2026 Efficient Low Light Image Enhancement (E-LLIE) Challenge, highlighting the proposed solutions and final outcomes. This challenge focuses on mobile image enhancement under low-light conditions, aiming to design lightweight networks that improve enhancement quality while ensuring practical deployability under limited computational resources. A total of 207 participants registered, 27 teams submitted valid entries, and 17 teams ultimately provided valid factsheet. Based on these submissions, this paper provides a systematic evaluation of recent methods for E-LLIE, offering a comprehensive overview of state-of-the-art progress and demonstrating significant improvements in both performance and efficiency.

papers.title: InfiltrNet: Dual-Branch CNN-Transformer Architecture for Brain Tumor Infiltration Risk Prediction
papers.summary: InfiltrNet: Dual-Branch CNN-Transformer Architecture for Brain Tumor Infiltration Risk Prediction attacks a clinically important gap in glioma modeling: standard BraTS-style deep networks segment the visible enhancing core and edema, but do not estimate how far malignant cells likely extend into apparently normal peritumoral tissue, which is the real bottleneck for surgical margin and radiation-field planning. The paper pivots from “what tumor is visible” toward “where infiltration risk is highest,” replacing single-target tumor segmentation with a three-zone risk-map prediction problem over the surrounding brain. The mechanical shift is twofold: first, it converts ordinary BraTS masks into reproducible infiltration supervision via Euclidean distance transforms, labeling high-, medium-, and low-risk zones by distance from the tumor boundary; second, it fuses a local-detail CNN encoder with a global-context Swin Transformer encoder through bidirectional cross-attention at every decoder stage, then sharpens boundary transitions with Dice-CrossEntropy plus a boundary-aware loss and auxiliary decoder heads. Unlike nearby work that leans on monolithic U-Nets, ViTs, or biophysical invasion simulators, InfiltrNet is an end-to-end volumetric predictor trained on multimodal MRI from BraTS 2020 and BraTS 2025 using only standard annotations. It reports the best mean Dice and lowest HD95 among five baselines, with the largest gains in the clinically sensitive transition zones, while GradCAM++ and occlusion maps show attention concentrated on peritumoral tissue. This points toward future MRI systems that learn invasion-aware, boundary-sensitive, clinically actionable risk geometry rather than mere tumor masks.
papers.abstract: Gliomas are aggressive brain tumors that infiltrate surrounding tissue beyond the visible tumor margins observed on Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI). Predicting the spatial extent of this infiltration is essential for surgical planning and radiation therapy, yet existing deep learning approaches focus on segmenting the visible tumor rather than estimating infiltration risk in the surrounding tissue. This paper presents InfiltrNet, a novel dual-branch architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) encoder with a Swin Transformer encoder through cross-attention fusion modules to predict three-zone infiltration risk maps from multimodal MRI. A label generation strategy based on distance transforms is proposed to derive reproducible infiltration risk zones from standard Brain Tumor Segmentation (BraTS) annotations. InfiltrNet is trained with a combined Dice-CrossEntropy and boundary-aware loss augmented by auxiliary supervision heads at intermediate decoder levels. Extensive experiments on BraTS 2020 and BraTS 2025 demonstrate that InfiltrNet outperforms five established baselines. Explainability analysis using GradCAM++ and Occlusion sensitivity confirms that the model attends to clinically relevant peritumoral regions.

papers.title: Fine-Tuning Impairs the Balancedness of Foundation Models in Long-tailed Personalized Federated Learning
papers.summary: Fine-Tuning Impairs the Balancedness of Foundation Models in Long-tailed Personalized Federated Learning attacks a specific failure mode in vision-language PFL: when CLIP-style foundation models are fine-tuned on federated long-tailed, non-IID clients, the global model loses its zero-shot class balance, becoming head-class biased and sometimes worse than the untouched zero-shot baseline, while existing personalization schemes then transmit that bias into local models through parameter or feature fusion. The paper pivots from the common assumption that any PEFT adaptation is benign toward the idea that in long-tailed PFL the bottleneck is preserving balanced knowledge before personalization, not merely fitting local data. The mechanical shift is FedPuReL, which replaces unconstrained local optimization with gradient purification and replaces feature/parameter fusion with output-space residual learning: clients compute a zero-shot-aligned TKL divergence to build an alignment gradient, then project task gradients away from conflict directions so updates cannot drift from the pretrained balance anchor; afterward, each client learns an additive residual branch atop a frozen global PEFT backbone, with the final personalized logit equal to global logit plus client-specific correction. This is unusual relative to nearby prompt/LoRA/adaptor PFL work because it treats zero-shot balance as a trainable constraint and makes personalization explicitly corrective rather than fused. Across ImageNet-LT, Places-LT, CIFAR-100-LT, and several fine-grained LT datasets with 20 clients and varying imbalance/heterogeneity, FedPuReL improves both global and personalized accuracy, especially on few-shot classes, while maintaining balancedness near the zero-shot level and reducing client drift, suggesting future federated foundation-model research should optimize for balance-preserving adaptation under label skew rather than only personalization or robustness.
papers.abstract: Personalized federated learning (PFL) with foundation models has emerged as a promising paradigm enabling clients to adapt to heterogeneous data distributions. However, real-world scenarios often face the co-occurrence of non-IID data and long-tailed class distributions, presenting unique challenges that remain underexplored in PFL. In this paper, we investigate this long-tailed personalized federated learning and observe that current methods suffer from two limitations: (i) fine-tuning degrades performance below zero-shot baselines due to the erosion of inherent class balance in foundation models; (ii) conventional personalization techniques further transfer this bias to local models through parameter or feature-level fusion. To address these challenges, we propose Federated Learning via Gradient Purification and Residual Learning (FedPuReL), which preserves balanced knowledge in the global model while enabling unbiased personalization. Specifically, we purify local gradients using zero-shot predictions to maintain a class-balanced global model, and model personalization as residual correction atop the frozen global model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FedPuReL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior performance on both global and personalized models across diverse long-tailed scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/shihaohou/FedPuReL.

papers.title: SpectraDINO: Bridging the Spectral Gap in Vision Foundation Models via Lightweight Adapters
papers.summary: SpectraDINO: Bridging the Spectral Gap in Vision Foundation Models via Lightweight Adapters attacks the practical failure mode that RGB-pretrained vision foundation models become brittle when deployed on NIR, SWIR, and LWIR sensors, where the same object induces radically different statistics and appearance cues. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that a single RGB backbone can be transferred to infrared by simple fine-tuning or by training separate modality-specific models, and pivots from full retraining toward a parameter-efficient shared-backbone design for spectral generalization. The core mechanical shift is a frozen DINOv2 ViT teacher that anchors semantics while a spectral student reuses the same transformer weights across RGB, NIR, SWIR, and LWIR, with only modality-specific input stems, learned modality embeddings, and lightweight per-block bottleneck adapters being trainable. Alignment is not just global: the multi-stage curriculum combines cosine CLS distillation, symmetric cross-modal InfoNCE, patch-token correspondence, and a queue-based neighborhood-structure-preservation KL loss that preserves local manifold geometry; Stage II warms up the teacher queue before Stage III selectively unfreezes the top half of the backbone to avoid collapse. Trained on paired RGB-IR data from MS2, RASMD, and LWIR collections and evaluated on detection and segmentation benchmarks including FLIR, LLVIP, MFNet, SemanticRT, FMB, RASMD, and MCubeS, SpectraDINO reaches state-of-the-art on most tasks, with especially large gains on SWIR, where it nearly doubles the prior best mAP. The result points toward unified, adapter-driven multispectral foundation models that can be reused across sensor regimes without rebuilding architectures per band.
papers.abstract: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) pretrained on large-scale RGB data have demonstrated remarkable representation quality, yet their applicability to multispectral imaging spanning Near-Infrared (NIR), Short-Wave Infrared (SWIR), and Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) remains largely unexplored. These spectral modalities offer complementary sensing capabilities critical for robust perception in adverse conditions, but present a fundamental domain gap relative to RGB-centric pretrained models. We present SpectraDINO, a multispectral VFM that bridges this spectral gap by extending DINOv2 ViT backbones to beyond-visible modalities through lightweight, per-modality bottleneck adapters, while preserving the rich representations of the frozen RGB backbone. We introduce a multi-stage teacher-student training protocol in which a frozen DINOv2 teacher guides a spectral student via cosine distillation, symmetric contrastive loss, patch-level alignment, and a novel neighborhood-structure-preservation loss. This staged curriculum enables strong cross-modal alignment without catastrophic forgetting of RGB priors. We evaluate SpectraDINO on multispectral object detection and semantic segmentation across challenging NIR, SWIR, and LWIR benchmarks using widely adopted fusion strategies. SpectraDINO achieves state-of-the-art performance across most benchmarks, validating its effectiveness as a general-purpose backbone for spectral generalization. The code and weights for model variants are available at https://github.com/Yonsei-STL/SpectraDINO.

papers.title: WindowQuant: Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization based on Window-Level Similarity for VLMs Inference Optimization
papers.summary: WindowQuant: Mixed-Precision KV Cache Quantization based on Window-Level Similarity for VLMs Inference Optimization attacks the long-video bottleneck where visual token sequences explode KV-cache size, making VLM inference latency and GPU memory scale intolerably with video length. It challenges the older assumption that KV compression should be decided at token granularity and then left to generic mixed-precision kernels; that workflow is both search-expensive and hardware-inefficient because irregular bit-widths fragment memory access. The paper pivots from token-level “importance” toward window-level semantic relevance, treating many video windows as text-irrelevant and therefore safe to compress aggressively. The mechanical shift is a two-stage system: first, window-level quantization search computes cosine similarity between each visual token window and the text prompt, then assigns FP16, INT4, or INT2 using layer-adaptive thresholds derived from hidden-state similarity, while always preserving the first window in FP16; second, window-level KV cache computation reorders windows so same-bit groups are contiguous before quantization, enabling group-wise RTN quantization plus fused dequantize-matmul operators that reduce SRAM/global-memory traffic. Compared with nearby KV methods like token-level mixed precision, WindowQuant is distinctive in making prompt-conditioned window similarity the control signal and physical cache layout part of the method, not just an implementation detail. Across LLaVA-OneVision-Qwen2, Qwen2-VL, and InternVL2 on NExT-QA, EgoSchema, IntentQA, MVBench, and MileBench, it preserves or improves accuracy while cutting memory and decoding latency, pointing toward semantics-aware, layout-aware multimodal inference compression.
papers.abstract: Recently, video language models (VLMs) have been applied in various fields. However, the visual token sequence of the VLM is too long, which may cause intolerant inference latency and GPU memory usage. Existing methods propose mixed-precision quantization to the key-value (KV) cache in VLMs based on token granularity, which is time-consuming in the search process and hardware inefficient during computation. This paper introduces a novel approach called WindowQuant, which employs window-adaptive mixed-precision quantization to optimize the KV cache. WindowQuant consists of two modules: window-level quantization search and window-level KV cache computation. Window-level quantization search quickly determines the optimal bit-width configuration of the KV cache windows based on the similarity scores between the corresponding visual token windows and the text prompt, maintaining the model accuracy. Furthermore, window-level KV cache computation reorders the KV cache windows before quantization, avoiding the hardware inefficiency caused by mixed-precision quantization in inference computation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that WindowQuant outperforms state-of-the-art VLM models and KV cache quantization methods on various datasets.

papers.title: EdgeLPR: On the Deep Neural Network trade-off between Precision and Performance in LiDAR Place Recognition
papers.summary: EdgeLPR: On the Deep Neural Network trade-off between Precision and Performance in LiDAR Place Recognition attacks the deployment bottleneck in long-term autonomous navigation: place-recognition networks are accurate on paper but too memory- and compute-hungry for edge robots that must run loop closure alongside mapping and other perception modules. The paper implicitly challenges the assumption that LiDAR localization should be optimized mainly by scaling up point-cloud models or adding aggregation-heavy descriptor heads; instead, it pivots from raw 3D processing and architecture-centric benchmarking toward edge-aware co-design of representation, backbone, and numeric precision. The mechanical shift is to project point clouds into 256×256 Bird’s Eye View images, feed them to lightweight image backbones, and force all models to emit comparable global descriptors through adaptive pooling plus a single linear projection, so the benchmark isolates backbone behavior rather than retrieval-head engineering. Unlike nearby work that compares heterogeneous pipelines, EdgeLPR evaluates MobileNetV3, ShuffleNetV2, and ResNet18 under FP32, FP16, and static post-training INT8 quantization on KITTI and NCLT, with Recall@K, F1, AUC, and latency/throughput measured under intrasequence and long-term seasonal shift. The main result is that FP16 is effectively “free” relative to FP32, while INT8 is architecture-dependent: MobileNetV3 is most fragile, ResNet18 most quantization-robust but too large, and ShuffleNetV2 gives the best memory-performance balance. This suggests a future research path toward use-case-aware quantization, descriptor compression, and deployment-driven neural design for robotic place recognition.
papers.abstract: Place recognition is essential for long-term autonomous navigation, enabling loop closure and consistent mapping. Although deep learning has improved performance, deploying such models on resource-constrained platforms remains challenging. This work explores efficient LiDAR-based place recognition for EdgeAI by leveraging Bird's Eye View representations to enable lightweight image-based networks. We benchmark representative architectures without aggregation heads using a unified descriptor scheme based on global pooling and linear projection, and evaluate performance under FP32, FP16, and INT8 quantization. Experiments reveal trade-offs between accuracy, robustness, and efficiency: FP16 matches FP32 with lower cost, while INT8 introduces architecture-dependent degradation. Overall, the presented results are a strong basis for future research on 'use-case'-aware quantisation of Neural Networks for Edge deployment.

papers.title: Rethinking Electro-Optical Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Retrieval: A Controlled Comparison with Generalist VFM
papers.summary: Rethinking Electro-Optical Vision Foundation Models for Remote Sensing Retrieval: A Controlled Comparison with Generalist VFM attacks the assumption that pretraining on remote-sensing imagery automatically yields better retrieval representations than strong natural-image foundation models. The bottleneck is not annotation scarcity itself but whether EO-specific pretraining actually shapes a more transferable embedding space under retrieval, especially when scenes, sensors, regions, and seasons shift. The paper pivots from the usual “EO model beats generalist model” benchmark narrative toward a controlled, protocol-matched comparison: representative EO-specific VFMs (MAE-RVSA, SatMAE, SatMAE++, SS-CMIR, Scale-MAE, Mask-VLM, CSMAE-SESD, ReJEPA) are evaluated against generalists (MAE, DINOv2, DINOv3, plus DINOv3-sat) on the same BEN-14K, FMoW-RGB, and FMoW-Sentinel datasets, with the same feature extraction, retrieval protocol, and F1 metric. The mechanical shift is the explicit split between in-domain retrieval and cross-scene leave-one-dataset-out transfer, making generalization failure visible rather than hidden by favorable train/test alignment. Unlike nearby EO work that tunes architectures or self-supervised objectives, this paper is a controlled negative result study: EO-specific models can lead in-domain, but often collapse cross-scene, while generalist VFMs remain competitive and sometimes superior. DINOv3 in particular outperforms many EO models, and DINOv3-sat does not consistently beat natural-image DINOv3, implying that satellite-only pretraining is insufficient. The paper points toward EO foundation models that explicitly encode physical, spatial, spectral, and geographic structure rather than merely swapping the pretraining corpus.
papers.abstract: Vision foundation models have attracted significant attention for their ability to leverage large-scale unlabeled visual data. This advantage is particularly important in remote sensing, where data acquisition is costly and annotation often requires expert knowledge. Recent electro-optical vision foundation models aim to learn domain-specific representations from remote sensing imagery, but it remains unclear whether they are more effective than strong generalist vision foundation models under retrieval-based evaluation. In this study, we conduct a controlled comparison between representative EO-specific and generalist vision foundation models for remote sensing image retrieval. Using the same datasets, retrieval protocol, and evaluation metric, we evaluate both in-domain performance and cross-scene generalization. Our results show that strong generalist vision foundation models are competitive with, and in some cases outperform, existing EO-specific models. Moreover, EO-specific models often suffer from substantial degradation under cross-scene evaluation, while generalist models show more stable transfer. These findings suggest that EO pretraining alone does not guarantee stronger retrieval-oriented remote sensing representations. We discuss the limitations of current EO-specific pretraining strategies and highlight the need for future EO vision foundation models to better exploit the physical, spatial, spectral, and geographic characteristics of remote sensing imagery.

papers.title: Beyond Known Objects: A Novel Framework for Open-Set Object Detection using Negative-Aware Norm
papers.summary: Beyond Known Objects: A Novel Framework for Open-Set Object Detection using Negative-Aware Norm tackles the OSOD bottleneck in autonomous driving: current detectors can classify known classes well but miss debris, barriers, and other unseen hazards, while many recent open-set methods pay for unknown-object recall with expensive retraining, pseudo-labeling loops, and detector surgery. The paper pivots away from the assumption that objectness must be learned by full detector re-optimization, and toward the claim that a pretrained off-the-shelf D-DETR already contains usable open-world cues in its hidden states. The mechanical shift is NAN-SPOT: a training-light objectness module that reads the last decoder output, computes Negative-Aware Norm (NAN) from feature sparsity plus norm, and fuses NAN with class confidence and simple box geometry features to score whether a proposal is any valid object, known or unknown. Unlike confidence-only schemes that suppress unknowns into background, NAN is shown to lift unknown scores but also spuriously fire on tiny edge fragments, so the final estimator corrects those failure modes rather than retraining the detector backbone. Training uses only minutes and a few hundred COCO-OOD images, with threshold calibration done in a pretest split to avoid leakage. The paper also introduces COCO-Open, expanding unknown annotations from 433 to 1853 for more exhaustive OSOD evaluation. Results claim better unknown recall and lower WI/AOSE than heavier baselines, while preserving known-class mAP, pointing future open-world perception toward hidden-representation mining and lightweight post-hoc objectness control rather than end-to-end retraining.
papers.abstract: Open-Set Object Detection (OSOD) is crucial for autonomous driving, where perception systems must recognize and localize both known and previously unseen objects in complex, dynamic environments. While recent approaches deliver promising results, they often require retraining the detector extensively to learn objectness, which describes the likelihood that a bounding box tightly encloses a valid object, regardless of whether its category was learned during training. Deviating from existing work, we hypothesize that standard off-the-shelf detectors may already contain helpful cues for objectness, owing to their training on numerous and diverse known categories. Building on this idea, we propose NAN-SPOT, a training-light framework that does not require to retrain the base object detector and estimates objectness by leveraging a hidden layer metric called Negative-Aware Norm (NAN), requiring only minutes of training on just hundreds of images. To support comprehensive evaluation, we introduce COCO-Open, an expanded version of the existing COCO-Mixed dataset, increasing unknown object annotations from 433 to 1853, making it the most exhaustively labeled dataset for OSOD to the best of our knowledge. Experimental results demonstrate that NAN-SPOT achieves even better performance on unknown object detection than methods requiring heavy training, without compromising performance on known objects. This efficiency and robustness make NAN-SPOT a promising step towards open-world perception in autonomous driving.

papers.title: LabBuilder: Protocol-Grounded 3D Layout Generation for Interactable and Safe Laboratory
papers.summary: LabBuilder: Protocol-Grounded 3D Layout Generation for Interactable and Safe Laboratory attacks the bottleneck in automated-lab deployment: simulator scenes must be not only photorealistic but protocol-executable, chemically safe, and robot-reachable, whereas prior 3D scene generators for household interiors mostly optimize visual plausibility and leave workflow validity to post hoc checks. It pivots from “generate a nice room from text” toward “compile an experimental request into a verified laboratory protocol and then synthesize a layout that can actually run it.” The mechanical shift is three-stage and unusually lab-specific: LabForge builds an asset knowledge base of 176 annotated laboratory entities plus a chemistry knowledge base distilled from CHEMTRANS/OPENEXP, then uses retrieval-augmented LLM synthesis plus schema and constraint validation to turn free-form descriptions into structured, asset-grounded protocols; LabGen factorizes scene creation into room-level zoning and desktop-level organization, then performs iterative constraint-aware optimization with a multi-objective reward over geometric validity and protocol-grounded chemical safety, mixing FastRepair with LLMAdjust and ending with navigation-aware refinement via A* reachability on inflated obstacles; LabTouchstone unifies evaluation over geometry, feasibility, chemical safety, semantic plausibility, and point-goal navigation in Isaac Sim. On 30 curated reactions, spanning substitution, redox, cyclization, and protection/deprotection, it reports near-zero collisions/boundary violations, strong asset availability and navigation success, and better safety than Holodeck and SceneWeaver. The main limitation is that it still stops short of equipment-spec validation and manipulation-level execution, pointing toward specification-aware, regulation-aware, sim-to-real laboratory generation.
papers.abstract: Automated laboratories hold the promise of accelerating scientific discovery, yet their deployment is bottlenecked by the difficulty of designing safe and executable environments. While simulator-based design offers scalability, existing 3D scene generation methods are primarily tailored for household settings, optimizing for visual plausibility while neglecting the rigorous functional semantics and safety constraints essential for scientific experimentation. We present LabBuilder, an end-to-end system that generates and verifies 3D laboratory layouts from concise textual specifications. It operates through three tightly coupled components: LabForge first curates a meta-dataset of annotated assets and chemical knowledge, translating natural language specifications into structured protocols; building on these protocols, LabGen synthesizes laboratory layouts via an iterative, constraint-aware optimization strategy; finally, LabTouchstone evaluates the resulting layouts as a unified benchmark. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LabBuilder significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, producing laboratory environments that are not only realistic but also functionally valid and safe for complex experimental workflows.

papers.title: A Hybrid Approach for Closing the Sim2real Appearance Gap in Game Engine Synthetic Datasets
papers.summary: A Hybrid Approach for Closing the Sim2real Appearance Gap in Game Engine Synthetic Datasets attacks the bottleneck that even ray-traced game-engine imagery still looks synthetic enough to hurt real-world generalization, especially when CV models are trained on Unity- or RAGE-generated data and expected to deploy on KITTI/Cityscapes-like scenes. The paper challenges the common assumption that photorealism enhancement is mostly a zero-shot diffusion problem: instead of betting solely on stronger image editing, it pivots from “maximize visual transformation” toward “match the target real-world distribution while preserving annotation geometry.” The core mechanical shift is a two-stage hybrid loop: first FLUX.2-4B Klein performs RGB-only diffusion-based enhancement, emphasizing lighting, materials, and object structure without extra control maps; then REGEN applies image-to-image translation to pull the result toward the statistics and characteristics of a chosen real-world domain. That combination is unusual because it explicitly separates geometric/material upgrading from distribution matching, rather than treating them as one generative task. Experiments on Virtual KITTI 2 and a GTA-V/Roboflow aerial dataset, evaluated with CMMD plus Mask2Former and YOLO26m for semantic preservation, show REGEN outperforms FLUX alone in realism, but FLUX+REGEN performs best overall and remains semantically consistent; on VKITTI2 it even slightly improves mIoU. The main limitation is lack of temporal consistency and real-time use, pointing toward future hybrid synthetic-data pipelines that preserve labels while closing sim2real gaps more deliberately.
papers.abstract: Video game engines have been an important source for generating large volumes of visual synthetic datasets for training and evaluating computer vision algorithms that are to be deployed in the real world. While the visual fidelity of modern game engines has been significantly improved with technologies such as ray-tracing, a notable sim2real appearance gap between the synthetic and the real-world images still remains, which limits the utilization of synthetic datasets in real-world applications. In this letter, we investigate the ability of a state-of-the-art image generation and editing diffusion model (FLUX.2-4B Klein) to enhance the photorealism of synthetic datasets and compare its performance against a traditional image-to-image translation model (REGEN). Furthermore, we propose a hybrid approach that combines the strong geometry and material transformations of diffusion-based methods with the distribution-matching capabilities of image-to-image translation techniques. Through experiments, it is demonstrated that REGEN outperforms FLUX.2-4B Klein and that by combining both FLUX.2-4B Klein and REGEN models, better visual realism can be achieved compared to using each model individually, while maintaining semantic consistency. The code is available at: https://github.com/stefanos50/Hybrid-Sim2Real

papers.title: Momentum-Anchored Multi-Scale Fusion Model for Long-Tailed Chest X-Ray Classification
papers.summary: Momentum-Anchored Multi-Scale Fusion Model for Long-Tailed Chest X-Ray Classification attacks the failure mode where ChestX-ray14 training is dominated by majority diagnoses, so gradient updates progressively overwrite subtle minority-pathology cues and produce feature drift under severe label skew plus large lesion-scale variability. It challenges the usual belief that class imbalance can be handled mainly by loss reweighting, augmentation, or a single static backbone, and also moves beyond the common self-supervised use of uniform EMA momentum by turning momentum into a supervised anchoring device. The pivot is from correcting the statistical imbalance after the fact toward stabilizing representation dynamics during optimization: the model forks an EfficientNetV2-S final expansion block into a slowly evolving EMA branch, updated with selective momentum, so high-capacity features are “slow-locked” against noisy majority-class gradients. The mechanical shift is then completed by a parallel multi-scale fusion module with 1×1, 3×3, and 5×5 convolutions, fused with the momentum stream before global average pooling, so temporal stability and spatial diversity interact at every location rather than only at the classifier head. This is unusual because it couples temporal anchoring with scale fusion in a lightweight dual-path medical classifier instead of introducing heavier attention or graph machinery. On 112,120 frontal radiographs, trained in a two-stage BCE/Asymmetric Loss protocol, it reaches 0.8682 mean AUC and wins 13 of 14 classes, with notable gains on rare Hernia and Pneumonia, though Infiltration remains a weakness. The paper points toward medical vision models that explicitly control feature drift under long-tailed optimization rather than merely reweighting it away.
papers.abstract: Chest X-ray classification suffers from severe class imbalance where gradient updates bias toward majority classes, causing feature drift and poor performance on rare but critical pathologies. We propose a Momentum-Anchored Multi-Scale Fusion Network that uses exponential moving averages (EMA) as a temporal anchoring mechanism to stabilize feature representations under long-tailed distributions. Our approach applies selective momentum updates to the final expansion block of an EfficientNet backbone, creating a slowly-evolving reference branch that resists gradient-induced drift while preserving discriminative patterns for minority classes. Combined with multi-scale spatial fusion ($1\times 1$, $3 \times 3$, $5 \times 5$ convolutions), this anchoring strategy maintains representational stability throughout training. On ChestX-ray14, our method achieves 0.8682 average AUC, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches and showing particular improvements on rare pathologies like Hernia (0.9470) and Pneumonia (0.8165). The results demonstrate that momentum anchoring effectively counters feature instability in long-tailed medical image classification.

papers.title: Open-access model for detecting openly dumped dispersed municipal solid waste from crowdsourced UAV imagery in Sub-Saharan Africa
papers.summary: Open-access model for detecting openly dumped dispersed municipal solid waste from crowdsourced UAV imagery in Sub-Saharan Africa attacks the bottleneck that informal, small-scale dumping is spatially fragmented, visually ambiguous, and largely invisible to satellite-only monitoring and labor-intensive field surveys. It challenges the old workflow in which waste mapping depended on site-specific digitization, manual inspection, or large-dumpsite benchmarks, and pivots from broad, coarse remote sensing toward centimeter-scale UAV imagery, open-data reuse, and cross-region generalization. The mechanical shift is a fine-tuned YOLO11x-cls classifier trained on 5,800 manually annotated 5×5 m tiles drawn from OpenAerialMap across 29 regions in 10 countries, with strong augmentation and direct inference on new UAV mosaics without retraining; predictions are then aggregated into the Openly dumped Dispersed MSW Contamination (ODDMSWC) index to compare regions and relate waste prevalence to population density, infrastructure deficit, and development measures. What differentiates the work is its operational, open-access orientation: it is meant for municipalities and local mapping communities, not just benchmark competition, and it explicitly converts crowdsourced drone imagery into a ready-to-use monitoring tool. The model reaches 92.87% accuracy and F1 scores around 0.93, reveals hotspots often aligned with waterways, and shows stronger links to population density and infrastructure deficits than to broad SHDI. Its main limitation is tile-level ambiguity under occlusion or context-dependent objects, pointing future research toward context-aware models, pixel-level segmentation, and hybrid UAV-satellite pipelines for scalable, locally actionable waste intelligence.
papers.abstract: Managing municipal solid waste in rapidly urbanizing Sub-Saharan Africa remains challenging due to dispersed informal dumping and limited high-resolution datasets for spatial monitoring. We present an open-access deep learning model for automated detection of openly dumped dispersed solid waste via crowdsourced UAV imagery, trained and evaluated across 29 regions in 10 countries, encompassing diverse environmental contexts. A deep learning model trained on manually annotated image tiles achieved excellent performance in detecting openly dumped dispersed solid waste across all study regions. Predicted distributions reveal heterogeneous accumulation patterns, ranging from localized hotspots - often along waterways, where waste can exacerbate flood and public health risks - to more dispersed litter across urban areas. Waste accumulation is most strongly associated with population density and indicators of lack of local infrastructure access, whereas its relationship with broader measures of regional development is weaker, highlighting the importance of fine-scale data for understanding localized waste dynamics. By releasing the model, this study provides a ready-to-use tool for UAV imagery collected by municipalities and local mapping communities, enabling openly dumped dispersed solid waste monitoring without extensive technical expertise. This approach empowers local practitioners to convert UAV imagery into actionable insights, supporting targeted interventions and improved municipal solid waste management across Sub-Saharan Africa.

papers.title: Improving Imbalanced Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Diagnosis via CBAM-Enhanced CNN Backbones
papers.summary: Improving Imbalanced Multi-Label Chest X-Ray Diagnosis via CBAM-Enhanced CNN Backbones attacks a practical failure mode in automated radiology: Chest X-ray models must predict 14 co-existing thoracic findings from weak image-level labels, yet performance is skewed by extreme long-tail imbalance, especially the dominance of “No Finding” and the poor detection of rare but clinically important diseases. The paper implicitly challenges the current pivot toward heavier transformer backbones and global end-to-end redesigns; instead, it moves from “replace the CNN” toward “repair the CNN” with lightweight, stage-selective attention and an imbalance-aware training curriculum. The mechanical shift is not a new detector head but a CBAM integration strategy that inserts channel-and-spatial attention into deeper CNN stages only—after dense blocks 3 and 4 in DenseNet121, and after VGG16 blocks 3, 4, and 5—so low-level texture extraction is preserved while high-level semantic refinement is amplified without pixel-level localization labels. Unusually, the paper treats module placement and training protocol as the main controllable levers, ablation-testing full vs selective attention placement and one-stage BCE/Focal training against a two-stage abnormal-first regimen followed by full-data fine-tuning. On ChestXray14’s 112,120 frontal images, the approach reaches mean AUC 0.8681 with DenseNet121 and 0.8695 with VGG16, outperforming prior baselines and lifting rare-class performance such as Hernia to 0.9660. This points toward future medical AI that is less architecture-hungry and more about curriculum, selective refinement, and clinically grounded long-tail robustness.
papers.abstract: Chest radiography is a widely used imaging modality for thoracic disease diagnosis, yet its conventional interpretation remains time-consuming and heavily dependent on expert knowledge. While deep learning has improved diagnostic efficiency through automated feature extraction, challenges such as class imbalance and the localization of multiple co-existing pathologies remain unsolved. In this paper, inspired by the strength of Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) in feature refinement and the capability of CNN blocks in feature extraction, we propose a strategy to integrate CBAM into traditional CNN blocks to enhance performance in multi-label classification tasks. Our method achieves a mean AUC of 0.8695 on ChestXray14 dataset, outperforming several state-of-the-art baselines.Our source code is available at: https://github.com/NNNguyenDuyyy/FETC_CBAM_Enhanced_CNN.git

papers.title: Channel-Level Relation to Attentive Aggregation with Neighborhood-Homogeneity Constraint for Point Cloud Analysis
papers.summary: Channel-Level Relation to Attentive Aggregation with Neighborhood-Homogeneity Constraint for Point Cloud Analysis attacks a specific failure mode in modern multi-scale point-cloud backbones: as neighborhoods are repeatedly aggregated, point-level attention and global channel reweighting collapse sub-structure information, so deeper layers blur noisy distractors with genuinely discriminative local geometry, especially in ambiguous indoor scenes and real-world cluttered objects. The paper implicitly challenges the old assumption that neighborhood quality can be judged sufficiently by either point-wise affinity or single-channel response statistics; instead, it pivots from coarse-grained spatial/channel scoring toward a fine-grained channel-level relational metric that tracks how each feature channel evolves across sequential aggregation stages. The mechanical shift is PointCRA: it computes channel-wise similarity from temporal trend variation of feature responses across stacked modules, groups channels to control overhead, then performs a three-level closed-loop calibration from channel to point to neighborhood, where neighborhood homogeneity modulates the exponent used to sharpen or soften weights depending on whether the region is smooth or boundary-like. Unlike nearby attention-based methods, the weighting is not learned as an opaque MLP/softmax but calibrated with explicit trend distance, homogeneity constraints, and a dedicated orthogonality-style loss that pushes channels to become complementary rather than saturated. Evaluated as a lightweight plug-in on DeLA-V1/V2, PointNext, and PointMetaBase across S3DIS, ScanObjectNN, and ShapeNetPart, it yields consistent gains and reaches 77.5% mIoU on S3DIS, 90.4% OA on ScanObjectNN, and 87.4% instance mIoU on ShapeNetPart. This points toward interpretable, constraint-driven neighborhood aggregation as a future axis for 3D perception.
papers.abstract: In 3D point cloud understanding, the core challenge lies in accurately capturing discriminative features within complex neighborhoods, which directly affects the execution precision of downstream tasks such as embodied AI and autonomous driving. Existing methods explore feature correlation discrimination but are limited to point-level spatial distribution or channel responses, enabling only coarse-grained level evaluation. For modern multi-scale point cloud networks, such coarse-grained metrics inevitably incur significant information loss in deeper layers. To address this issue, we propose a novel network equipped with a channel-level metric-based enhancement mechanism, termed the PointCRA network. Our core idea is to introduce temporal trend variation as a new evaluation dimension to avoid the information loss caused by weight dimension collapse in existing spatial and channel attention mechanisms. On this basis, we construct a multi-level calibration framework guided by neighborhood homogeneity for weight calibration, and design a dedicated loss function to enhance channel discriminability. The module effectively leverages the intrinsic feature priors of deep networks to adaptively correct the feature aggregation process, offering strong interpretability with low parameter overhead. Furthermore, our proposed method exhibits strong transferability, interpretability, and parameter efficiency. We validate the proposed method effectiveness on diverse datasets and benchmark models, and further demonstrate its rationality through extensive analytical experiments. Our PointCRA achieves 77.5% mIoU on the S3DIS dataset, 90.4% OA on the ScanObjectNN dataset, and 87.4% instance mIoU on the ShapeNetPart dataset. The code and pretrained weights are publicly available on GitHub:

papers.title: Graph-Augmented Topological Internalization with Dual-Stream Classifiers for Medical Report Generation
papers.summary: Graph-Augmented Topological Internalization with Dual-Stream Classifiers for Medical Report Generation attacks a specific failure mode in chest X-ray report generation: mainstream prompt-driven MRG systems predict each pathology as an independent label, so they miss disease co-occurrence priors, overfit long-tailed negatives, and hallucinate unsupported findings when subtle or concurrent lesions are present. The paper pivots away from retrieval-heavy or isolated multi-label classification pipelines toward an explicitly parameterized topology-first diagnosis front end, then into text generation. The mechanical shift is the Topological Knowledge Internalization (TKI) module: a GCN builds a pruned, normalized disease co-occurrence graph over 18 nodes (14 chest abnormalities plus 4 anatomical attributes) and turns it into a dynamic classifier weight matrix via weight tying, so the topology becomes part of the model’s parameters rather than an external knowledge source. A dual-stream classifier then separates concerns: the main branch emits discrete diagnostic prompts under topological constraints, while the auxiliary branch uses truncated asymmetric loss and adaptive thresholding to suppress easy negatives, noisy labels, and imbalanced omissions. Diagnosis-Guided Spatial Attention (DGSA) closes the loop by using diagnostic semantics to reweight visual features and reduce hallucination. On MIMIC-CXR, GDMRG lifts clinical recall and F1, with especially strong gains on complex co-occurrences, while retaining natural-language fluency at some BLEU cost; it also transfers zero-shot to IU X-Ray. This suggests a future MRG direction where explicit disease topology, calibrated classification, and diagnosis-to-grounding feedback are learned as a single interpretable control system.
papers.abstract: Automated medical report generation, MRG, holds substantial value for alleviating radiologist workload and enhancing diagnostic efficiency. However, mainstream approaches typically treat diverse chest abnormalities as isolated classification targets. This paradigm often overlooks inherent disease co-occurrences and struggles to translate medical topological structures into explicit data correlations, constraining the model's reasoning capacity on complex or subtle lesions. To address this, we propose a Graph-Augmented Dual-Stream Medical Report Generation with Topological Internalization, GDMRG. Our framework introduces a Topological Knowledge Internalization module, TKI, which leverages a Graph Convolutional Network, GCN, to generate an explicit parameterized weight matrix based on global disease co-occurrence priors. This facilitates efficient topological knowledge injection without relying on external retrieval mechanisms. Building upon this, we construct a dual-stream classification system: the main branch generates discrete diagnostic prompts under topological constraints, while the auxiliary branch employs an asymmetric optimization strategy to dynamically calibrate decision boundaries for highly imbalanced samples. Concurrently, to establish a logical closed loop between diagnosis and visual grounding, we design a diagnostic-driven Diagnosis-Guided Spatial Attention, DGSA, that utilizes high-dimensional clinical semantics to recalibrate the visual encoder, mitigating feature hallucinations. Comprehensive experiments on the MIMIC-CXR dataset demonstrate that GDMRG achieves competitive clinical efficacy, CE, while maintaining natural language fluency. Furthermore, our model exhibits robust zero-shot generalization on the IU X-Ray dataset. In summary, this work presents an integrated and interpretable paradigm for medical report generation.

papers.title: Enhancing Multimodal In-Context Learning via Inductive-Deductive Reasoning
papers.summary: Enhancing Multimodal In-Context Learning via Inductive-Deductive Reasoning attacks a specific failure mode of vision-language in-context learning: VLMs can sometimes guess the right answer from demonstrations while never extracting the shared rule, and this inductive correctness gap becomes worse when multi-image prompts are cluttered by redundant patches and a first-image attention bias. The paper pivots from treating multimodal ICL as mostly a retrieval or prompt-formatting problem toward treating it as a structured rule-discovery problem that must be mechanically enforced at both the visual and reasoning levels. Its core shift is MMInduction: a principled inductive-deductive loop that first compresses each image with similarity-guided token pruning, then rebalances cross-image attention with a dynamic visual attention module, and finally forces an inductive chain-of-thought that explicitly analyzes each case, judges whether a retrieved example is helpful or noise, extracts a generalizable rule, and applies that rule to the query. Unlike nearby work that only modulates attention or prunes tokens, MMInduction couples these controls with supervised distillation from Gemini 3.1-pro and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for task accuracy, helpful-case identification, and faithful citation. On Qwen2-VL, Qwen2.5-VL, and Qwen3-VL backbones, across eight benchmarks spanning VQAv2, TextVQA, MMIQ, MME-Reasoning, MDK12, CSVQA, Harmeme, and MAMI, it consistently improves performance and reverses the negative-shot trend on reasoning-heavy tasks. The limitation is that very long multi-shot contexts can still overrun generation stability, suggesting future multimodal ICL will need scalable inductive memory, noise-aware retrieval, and stronger structured reasoning controllers.
papers.abstract: In-context learning (ICL) allows large models to adapt to tasks using a few examples, yet its extension to vision-language models (VLMs) remains fragile. Our analysis reveals that the fundamental limitation lies in an inductive gap, models often produce correct answers from flawed reasoning, while struggling to extract consistent rules across demonstrations. This gap is further exacerbated by two visual-level obstacles: an overwhelming proportion of redundant visual tokens that obscure textual cues, and a skewed attention distribution that favors the initial image at the expense of subsequent context. To address these issues, we introduce a framework that restructures multimodal ICL as a principled inductive-deductive process. The framework incorporates a similarity-based visual token compression module to filter out redundant patches, a dynamic attention rebalancing mechanism to distribute focus equitably across all images, and a chain-of-thought paradigm that explicitly guides the model to analyze individual examples, derive a generalizable rule, and then apply it to the query. An auxiliary learning pipeline combines supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards to reinforce faithful citation and noise filtering. Evaluations across eight benchmarks covering visual perception, logical reasoning, STEM problems, and sarcasm detection demonstrate consistent and significant improvements over standard ICL baselines for multiple open-source VLMs, highlighting the potential of equipping models with genuine inductive capabilities in multimodal settings.

papers.title: UnGAP: Uncertainty-Guided Affine Prompting for Real-Time Crack Segmentation
papers.summary: UnGAP: Uncertainty-Guided Affine Prompting for Real-Time Crack Segmentation attacks a concrete failure mode in structural-health-monitoring segmentation: cracks appear under blur, glare, occlusion, and texture clutter, where conventional uncertainty-aware models either sample multiple blurred masks or predict uncertainty as a passive post-hoc map that never improves the features. The paper pivots from treating aleatoric uncertainty as an endpoint toward using it as a control signal inside the network, arguing that crack detection is one of the few dense-prediction settings where pixel-wise heteroscedastic modeling is more faithful than global object-level semantics because the signal is local gradients and irregular boundaries. The mechanical shift is a closed loop: a heteroscedastic module trained with β-NLL estimates pixel-wise log-variance without letting high-variance regions collapse the gradient, then the Uncertainty-Prompted Feature Modulator converts that variance into pixel-wise affine scale-and-shift parameters that actively recondition decoder features in ambiguous regions; a lightweight boundary-aware head adds explicit edge supervision to sharpen topology. Unlike probabilistic U-Nets, Bayesian dropout, or diffusion-style stochastic segmentation, UnGAP does not inject randomness or require repeated inference; it uses the model’s own uncertainty to rectify features in one pass. Tested on Crack500, CrackTree200, CFD, CrackSeg9K, and CrackVision12K with Xception/ResNet-101 encoders and 400×400 real-time training, it reports consistently higher precision/recall/F1, especially on complex backgrounds, while preserving speed. This points toward uncertainty-as-actuation for dense vision, where uncertainty maps become trainable prompts rather than diagnostic artifacts.
papers.abstract: Real-time crack segmentation is vital for structural health monitoring but is plagued by aleatoric uncertainties arising from varying lighting, blur, and texture ambiguity. Current uncertainty-aware approaches typically treat uncertainty estimation as a passive endpoint for post-hoc analysis, failing to close the loop by feeding this information back to refine feature representations.
