We contend that independent pixel-wise heteroscedastic modeling is uniquely suited for crack segmentation, as cracks are defined by fine-grained local gradients rather than the global semantic coherence relied upon in general object segmentation. However, this approach suffers from a structural optimization pathology: high predicted variance attenuates loss gradients, effectively causing the model to ignore difficult samples and under-fit complex boundaries. To address these challenges, we propose UnGAP, a novel framework that establishes a closed-loop mechanism between uncertainty estimation and feature learning. Central to our approach is the Uncertainty-Prompted Feature Modulator (UPFM), which treats aleatoric uncertainty as an active visual prompt rather than a mere output. UPFM dynamically calibrates feature distributions through pixel-wise affine transformations. Crucially, this mechanism mitigates the heteroscedastic pathology by transforming high variance, which would otherwise indicate gradient suppression, into a constructive signal for stronger feature rectification in ambiguous regions. Additionally, a boundary-aware detection head is introduced to further constrain prediction precision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UnGAP balances superior segmentation accuracy with real-time inference speed, effectively validating the benefit of transforming uncertainty from a passive metric into an active calibration tool.

papers.title: FEAT: Fashion Editing and Try-On from Any Design
papers.summary: FEAT: Fashion Editing and Try-On from Any Design attacks a specific bottleneck in fashion generation: existing multimodal editing and virtual try-on systems still assume the design source is a garment image, so they cannot faithfully transfer creative cues from artwork, abstract imagery, or natural photos, and they usually break down once the outfit includes accessories rather than only clothing. The paper pivots from garment-only, pairwise-trained fashion synthesis toward “any-design” compositional control, where the mechanical shift is to separate what in the source should be borrowed from how it should look. Its Disentangled Dual Injection (DDI) replaces monolithic image prompting with block-wise, selective routing of content and style: a content-subtractive proxy embedding suppresses structural leakage by subtracting a blurred-lightness proxy in CLIP space, while style and content features are injected into different U-Net blocks with independent scales. For try-on, Orthogonal-Guided Noise Fusion (OGNF) is a training-free region control lever that uses orthogonal projection to remove residual garment latents and then applies three-region noise handling so the replaced clothing, preserved body/face/background, and new garment synthesis are treated differently. This is unusual because it extends to accessories, uses no pairwise retraining for VTON, and even generalizes to game and animated characters. Evaluated on VITON-HD and DressCode with sketches, GPT-4V-generated text, and WikiArt/Midjourney prompts under SDXL, FEAT claims state-of-the-art flexibility, prompt consistency, and realism, pointing toward data-independent, region-aware diffusion editing for full-outfit design.
papers.abstract: Fashion design aims to express a designer's creative intent and to depict how garments interact with the human body. Recent methods condition on multimodal inputs to support garment editing and virtual try-on. However, existing methods still (i) confine design to garment-related images, excluding creative design sources such as artwork, abstract imagery, and natural photographs, and (ii) cannot support complete outfits, including accessories. We present FEAT (Fashion Editing And Try-On from Any Design), a method that enables editing and try-on across garments and accessories using diverse design sources. To achieve this, we introduce Disentangled Dual Injection (DDI). It takes both apparel and non-apparel design sources and selectively injects design cues via content and style disentanglement. Furthermore, we propose Orthogonal-Guided Noise Fusion (OGNF), a training-free mechanism that removes residual garments via orthogonal projection and applies region-specific noise strategies to enable virtual try-on for both garments and accessories. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FEAT achieves state-of-the-art performance in design flexibility, prompt consistency, and visual realism.

papers.title: DirectEdit: Step-Level Accurate Inversion for Flow-Based Image Editing
papers.summary: DirectEdit: Step-Level Accurate Inversion for Flow-Based Image Editing attacks the brittle bottleneck in training-free rectified-flow editing: standard inversion adds stepwise approximation error because the reconstruction path is propagated from mismatched noisy latents, so “source features” injected into editing are already drifted, causing drift accumulation, background corruption, and undercut reconstruction fidelity. The paper pivots away from the dominant strategy of fixing the inversion trajectory itself and toward directly aligning the forward reconstruction path to the actual inversion path, treating inversion as the pivot and enforcing step-level equality with stored latent residuals, so the velocity field is queried on latents that are noise-aligned with the true inverse trajectory. This mechanical shift eliminates reconstruction error without extra NFEs and is unusual compared with RF-inversion, higher-order solvers, fixed-point correction, or inversion-free interpolation schemes. DirectEdit then adds a preservation layer: attention-value feature injection from the reconstruction branch into the edit branch for a limited timestep window, plus multi-branch mask-guided noise blending generated by an MLLM and SAM to distinguish local, background, global, and other edits. Evaluated on PIE-Bench with FLUX.1-dev and SD3.5-medium at 30 steps, it reports superior structure retention, background fidelity, and CLIP alignment versus RFEdit, FlowEdit, DNAEdit, FTEdit, and related baselines, while the main limitation remains poor handling of resizing, spatial relocation, and complex reasoning. This suggests a future direction of trajectory-consistent, region-aware editing where fidelity comes from exact latent alignment rather than post hoc correction.
papers.abstract: With recent advancements in large-scale pre-trained text-to-image (T2I) models, training-free image editing methods have demonstrated remarkable success. Typically, these methods involve adding noise to a clean image via an inversion process, followed by separate denoising steps for the reconstruction and editing paths during the forward process. However, since the reconstruction path is approximated using noisy latents from mismatched timesteps, existing methods inevitably suffer from accumulated drift, which fundamentally limits reconstruction fidelity. To address this challenge, we systematically analyze the inversion process within the flow transformer and propose DirectEdit, a simple yet effective editing method that eliminates the inherent reconstruction error without introducing additional neural function evaluations (NFEs). Unlike most prior works that attempt to rectify the inversion path, DirectEdit focuses on directly aligning the forward paths, enabling precise reconstruction and reliable feature sharing. Furthermore, we introduce a preservation mechanism based on attention feature injection and multi-branch mask-guided noise blending, which effectively balances fidelity and editability. Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios demonstrate that DirectEdit achieves efficient and accurate image editing, delivering superior performance that outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Code and examples are available at https://desongyang.github.io/Directedit.

papers.title: Multi-Rater Calibrated Segmentation Models
papers.summary: Multi-Rater Calibrated Segmentation Models attacks a clinical bottleneck in medical image segmentation: modern networks can be accurate yet badly miscalibrated, and the usual response—collapsing multiple expert masks into a single “ground truth”—throws away the very ambiguity that should shape confidence. The paper explicitly challenges the assumption that inter-rater variability is mere noise, and pivots from consensus fusion toward learning the ordinal structure of disagreement. The mechanical shift is to sum K binary annotations into an ordinal rater-consensus target, then train a standard segmentation backbone (e.g., U-Net/encoder-decoder with ResNet-FPN) to predict K+1 ordered consensus levels with a hybrid Ranked Probability Score (RPS) ordinal loss plus BCE. At inference, foreground probability is recovered by aggregating the probability mass over majority-foreground consensus levels, so calibration is tied to empirical annotator agreement rather than post-hoc scaling. This is unusual relative to nearby work because it does not add a specialized uncertainty head, Bayesian machinery, or heavy label-fusion pre-processing; instead it turns annotation disagreement itself into the supervision signal and evaluates it with a multi-rater extension of ECE (MR-ECE). Across CoCaHis, REFUGE optic disc/cup, and LongCIU chest CT, the method consistently lowers MR-ECE while maintaining or matching AUC, especially in highly ambiguous regions like optic cup borders and GGO boundaries. The main implication is a plug-and-play route to reliability-aware segmentation, and the future direction is broader ordinal calibration for more complex multi-class, multi-annotator medical imaging.
papers.abstract: Objective: Accurate probability estimates are essential for the safe deployment of medical image segmentation models in clinical decision-making. However, modern deep segmentation networks are often poorly calibrated, a problem exacerbated when multiple expert annotations exhibit substantial disagreement. While inter-rater variability is typically treated as noise, it provides valuable information about intrinsic annotation ambiguity that must be reflected in model confidence. Methods: We improve the probabilistic calibration of medical image segmentation models by reformulating multi-rater supervision as an ordinal learning problem. Voxel-wise annotator agreement is treated as an ordered target, linking predictive confidence to the empirical variability in training data. This formulation allows the use of ordinal-aware scoring rules, such as the Ranked Probability Score ordinal loss, combined with a standard binary objective to preserve discriminative performance. Results: We evaluated the proposed approach across four public segmentation benchmarks spanning ophthalmology, histopathology, and thoracic imaging. Calibration was assessed using a multi-rater extension of expected calibration error. Results consistently show that ordinal-aware training yields substantially improved calibration with respect to inter-rater agreement without degrading segmentation accuracy. Conclusions: Treating multi-rater annotations as ordered information provides a principled and architecture-agnostic route to more reliable probabilistic segmentation models.

papers.title: Mixture Prototype Flow Matching for Open-Set Supervised Anomaly Detection
papers.summary: Mixture Prototype Flow Matching for Open-Set Supervised Anomaly Detection attacks the OSAD bottleneck where limited anomaly labels must generalize to unseen defect types, yet prior prototype-based detectors compress normality into a unimodal Gaussian and therefore blur rare-but-valid normal modes into false positives and weak boundaries. It pivots from single-centroid or single-velocity flow/diffusion formulations toward a structured Gaussian-mixture prototype space, treating normality as semantically separable modes rather than one blob. The mechanical shift is to learn a continuous transport from normal feature distributions into a K-component GMM prior with flow matching, but unlike standard flow matching it regresses a mixture-valued velocity field, where each component represents a distinct normal mode; K-means++ seeds the prototype means, and a Mutual Information Maximization Regularizer (MIMR) prevents prototype collapse by pushing confident yet balanced assignments while widening normal-anomaly separation. A further differentiator is the closed-form reverse-time mixture transition, which preserves mixture closure and enables stable step-wise inference without numerical ODE integration, alongside global, local, residual, and normal scoring heads that fuse likelihood, patch saliency, and prototype residuals. Evaluated on nine industrial and medical benchmarks under one-shot and ten-shot, general and hard OSAD settings, the method reports state-of-the-art AUC and especially strong gains on complex multimodal textures and subtle defects. The paper points toward future anomaly detection systems that model open-set normality as transport between structured latent manifolds rather than as a single density peak.
papers.abstract: Open-set supervised anomaly detection (OSAD) aims to identify unseen anomalies using limited anomalous supervision. However, existing prototype-based methods typically model normal data via a unimodal Gaussian prior, failing to capture inherent multi-modality and resulting in blurred decision boundaries. To address this, we propose Mixture Prototype Flow Matching (MPFM), a framework that learns a continuous transformation from normal feature distributions to a structured Gaussian mixture prototype space. Departing from traditional flow-based approaches that rely on a single velocity vector, MPFM explicitly models the velocity field as a Gaussian mixture prior where each component corresponds to a distinct normal class. This design facilitates mode-aware and semantically coherent distribution transport. Furthermore, we introduce a Mutual Information Maximization Regularizer (MIMR) to prevent prototype collapse and maximize normal-anomaly separability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MPFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks under both single- and multi-anomaly settings.

papers.title: Anomaly-Preference Image Generation
papers.summary: Anomaly-Preference Image Generation attacks the bottleneck in few-shot industrial defect synthesis: existing diffusion generators either chase fidelity but collapse diversity through overfitting and semantic misalignment, or preserve the base prior but push inference cost and latency upward. The paper pivots from hand-crafted anomaly heuristics, dual-stream fine-tuning, and human-ranked preference optimization toward an annotation-free preference-learning view of anomaly synthesis, where real anomalies themselves become the positive reference. The mechanical shift is Anomaly Preference Optimization (APO): instead of training a reward model or collecting preference pairs, it derives a Bradley–Terry-style preference signal from denoising-trajectory deviations between the adapted policy and the frozen reference diffusion model on the same real anomaly, so “better” means lower noise-prediction error than the base model at each timestep. This is paired with Time-Aware Capacity Allocation, a LoRA rank schedule that keeps adaptation minimal in high-noise steps to preserve structural diversity while expanding capacity in low-noise steps to capture fine-grained defect texture, plus a hierarchical inference sampler that separately tunes semantic coherence and anomaly injection strength. On Stable Diffusion v1-4 over MVTec AD, using one-third of anomalies as references, the method reports state-of-the-art realism/diversity, stronger downstream U-Net detection and ResNet-18 classification, and usable pixel-level localization from latent deviation maps. The implication is a broader move toward preference-aligned, time-structured generative control for synthetic rare-event data under extreme annotation scarcity.
papers.abstract: Synthesizing realistic and diverse anomalous samples from limited data is vital for robust model generalization. However, existing methods struggle to reconcile fidelity and diversity, often hampered by distribution misalignment and overfitting, respectively.To mitigate this, we introduce Anomaly Preference Optimization,a novel paradigm that reformulates anomaly generation as a preference learning problem.Central to our approach is an implicit preference alignment mechanism that leverages real anomalies as positive references, deriving optimization signals directly from denoising trajectory deviations without requiring costly human annotation. Furthermore, we propose a Time-Aware Capacity Allocation module that dynamically distributes model capacity along the diffusion timeline,prioritizing structural diversity during highnoise phases while enhancing fine-grained fidelity in low-noise stages. During inference, a hierarchical sampling strategy modulates the coherencealignment trade-off, enabling precise control over generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that significantly outperforms existing baselines,achieving state-of-the-art performance in both realism and diversity.

papers.title: M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse: Lightweight State-Space MoE with a Cross-Scale Gating Bridge for Brain Tumor Segmentation
papers.summary: M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse: Lightweight State-Space MoE with a Cross-Scale Gating Bridge for Brain Tumor Segmentation attacks a specific bottleneck in 3D BraTS-style MRI segmentation: many models become brittle and compute-heavy because they over-invest in large input cubes and imbalanced encoders/decoders that try to reconstruct appearance rather than preserve the tiny, contrast-sensitive enhancing tumor. The paper pivots from the familiar “wider decoder plus bigger volume” scaling story toward capacity placement where it matters: long-range context, purified skip transfer, and site-adaptive routing. Its mechanical shift is a three-part replacement of brute-force depth expansion: a grouped state-space mixer propagates volumetric context with linear complexity; a cross-scale dual-stage gating bridge first spatially denoises each skip and then realigns channels so the decoder receives consistent evidence; and a sample-level mixture-of-experts is confined to high-semantic, low-resolution stages to absorb cross-site and cross-protocol shifts without inflating early layers. Unlike nearby lightweight U-Nets that simply prune channels or bolt on a single attention block, M4Fuse explicitly couples skip purification with selective expert activation, and ablations show that once skips are cleaned, widening the decoder has diminishing or even negative returns—the paper calls this a decoder marginal utility principle. On BraTS2019 and BraTS2021, using 4 MRI modalities and even a reduced 64×128×128 input, the base 1.11M-parameter model slightly beats stronger lightweight baselines while cutting parameters by 62.63% versus SuperLightUnet and improving average performance by 0.09%, pointing future research toward controllable routing and state-space designs for medically fragile fine structures.
papers.abstract: Encoder-decoder imbalance and the reliance on large input volumes make many 3D brain tumor segmentation models both compute-heavy and brittle. We present M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse, a lightweight network that prioritizes discriminative brain tumor cues over exhaustive appearance reconstruction. Our method balances encoder and decoder capacity and replaces depth expansion with a synergistic design: it propagates long-range context with linear complexity via a grouped state space mixer, denoises and aligns skip features using a cross-scale dual-stage gating bridge, and absorbs cross-site acquisition shifts with a sample-level mixture-of-experts. On the BraTS2019 and BraTS2021 benchmarks, M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse outperforms other lightweight excellent methods in both parameter count and performance. Even at a challenging input resolution of \(64\times128\times128\) (half that of existing excellent models), M\textsuperscript{4}Fuse reduces parameters by 62.63\% and improves average performance by 0.09\%. Ablations of key components validate the method's exceptional parameter-to-accuracy efficiency and robustness across diverse data centers.

papers.title: ExpoCM: Exposure-Aware One-Step Generative Single-Image HDR Reconstruction
papers.summary: ExpoCM: Exposure-Aware One-Step Generative Single-Image HDR Reconstruction attacks the core bottleneck in single-image HDR recovery: the LDR input is spatially heterogeneous, with saturated highlights that destroy detail and dark regions that amplify noise, yet most prior methods either regress HDR directly or apply diffusion uniformly, assuming one global degradation process. The paper pivots away from iterative diffusion sampling and toward a distillation-free, one-step generative formulation built on a Probability Flow ODE and Consistency Models, where the mechanical shift is not merely faster inference but an exposure-conditioned trajectory design. ExpoCM first builds a soft exposure mask from luminance percentiles to partition each image into over-exposed, under-exposed, and well-exposed regions, then replaces the single trajectory with three region-specific flows: saturated pixels are hallucinated from noise, dark pixels are guided by a low-pass filtered version of the input to suppress amplified noise while preserving coarse structure, and reliable regions follow a standard conditional path. These flows are spatially blended into an Exposure-Aware Consistency Trajectory, letting one U-Net reconstruct HDR in a single pass without distillation from a multistep teacher. A second lever, the Exposure-guided Luminance-Chromaticity loss in CIE L*a*b* space, adaptively weights luminance and chroma to fight brightness bias and color drift. On HDR-REAL, HDR-EYE, and AIM2025, the method reports state-of-the-art fidelity and perceptual accuracy, with over 400× speedup over DDPM and 20× over DDIM, pointing toward spatially adaptive generative restoration as a practical alternative to uniform diffusion.
papers.abstract: Single-image HDR reconstruction aims to recover high dynamic range radiance from a single low dynamic range (LDR) input, but remains highly ill-posed due to detail saturation in over-exposed regions and noise amplification in under-exposed areas. While recent diffusion-based approaches offer powerful generative priors, they often overlook the exposure-dependent nature of the degradation and incur substantial computational costs from iterative sampling. To address these challenges, we propose ExpoCM, a novel one-step generative HDR reconstruction framework that reformulates HDR reconstruction as a Probability Flow ODE (PF-ODE) and constructs exposure-aware consistency trajectories via exposure-dependent perturbations. Specifically, a soft exposure mask is first constructed to separate the LDR image into over-, under-, and well-exposed regions. Based on this partition, region-conditioned consistency trajectories are designed to hallucinate saturated details, suppress noise in dark regions, and preserve reliable structures within a single, distillation-free inference step. To further enhance perceptual quality, we introduce an Exposure-guided Luminance-Chromaticity Loss in the CIE~$\text{L}^*\text{a}^*\text{b}^*$ space, which assigns exposure-aware weights to luminance and chromaticity components, effectively mitigating brightness bias and color drift. Extensive experiments on the HDR-REAL, HDR-EYE, and AIM2025 benchmarks demonstrate that ExpoCM achieves state-of-the-art fidelity and perceptual accuracy, while enabling over 400$\times$ and 20$\times$ faster inference compared to DDPM (1000 steps) and DDIM (50 steps), respectively.

papers.title: Multispectral Blind Image Super-Resolution for Standing Dead Tree Segmentation
papers.summary: Multispectral Blind Image Super-Resolution for Standing Dead Tree Segmentation attacks a remote-sensing bottleneck: dead-tree mapping needs sharper aerial detail, but high-quality multispectral imagery is scarce, expensive, and mismatched across sensors, while annotation is limited. The paper challenges the usual super-resolution assumption that low-resolution inputs are synthetic downsamplings of paired high-resolution targets; instead it pivots from paired reconstruction toward fully blind, unpaired image-to-image translation for real-world sensor degradation. The mechanical shift is an Attention-Guided Domain Adaptation Network (ADA-Net) generator that learns low-resolution-to-high-resolution mapping with residual blocks, residual self-attention, and 2-D convolutional Q/K/V projections, trained by a composite adversarial objective plus spatial contrastive loss, frequency-domain patch contrastive loss on FFT features, and identity regularizers to preserve already high-quality inputs. This is unusual relative to nearby CUT/CycleGAN-style translation because it explicitly models multispectral 4-band data, mixes spatial and frequency constraints, and treats super-resolution as a generic restorer for blur, saturation, noise, and low contrast rather than a narrow upsampler. The experiment uses unpaired 0.5 m Finnish low-resolution scenes and 0.25 m Polish high-resolution scenes, evaluated by downstream TreeMort-1T-UNet segmentation. ADA-Net yields 54% Dice without any high-resolution annotations and 63.86% Dice when paired with real HR data, while also exposing the tradeoff between detail recovery and GAN hallucination. The paper points toward task-driven, sensor-realistic super-resolution benchmarks for forest monitoring and multimodal environmental mapping.
papers.abstract: Mapping standing dead trees is crucial for acquiring information on the effects of climate change on forests and forest biodiversity. However, leveraging high-quality aerial imagery for dead tree segmentation poses challenges due to limitations in sensor availability and the scarcity of annotated data. In this study, we propose a generic blind super-resolution framework that incorporates Attention-Guided Domain Adaptation Networks (ADA-Nets) to learn the mapping from low-resolution to high-resolution multispectral image domains. Our approach operates solely on unpaired samples, mimicking real-world conditions, i.e., low-resolution images are not synthetically obtained by downsampling the high-resolution images. Moreover, the proposed method serves as a general-purpose restorer addressing several image degradation types, including saturation, noise, and low contrast that typically occur in low-resolution images acquired by low-end sensors. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to perform real-world and generic super-resolution for multispectral data in the scope of standing dead tree segmentation. Experimental evaluations demonstrate segmentation performances of 54% and 64% in Dice scores. Notably, the first result is obtained without using any high-resolution annotations; the segmentation network is trained on super-resolved low-resolution images, while evaluation is performed on the high-resolution data. We publicly share the aerial multispectral dataset with manually annotated labels at https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/meteahishali/aerial-imagery-for-dead-tree-segmentation-poland.

papers.title: MooD: An Efficient VA-Driven Affective Image Editing Framework via Fine-Grained Semantic Control
papers.summary: MooD: An Efficient VA-Driven Affective Image Editing Framework via Fine-Grained Semantic Control attacks the bottleneck that affective image editing has been trapped between two bad choices: discrete emotion labels that flatten subtle feeling changes, and diffusion/agentic pipelines that are too slow for practical interactive use. The paper pivots from prompt-heavy, category-driven AIE toward direct control in continuous valence-arousal space, arguing that emotion should be edited as a smooth trajectory rather than a fixed class. Its mechanical shift is a lightweight dual-channel framework: a VA-aware retrieval step first finds a reference image that is both emotionally close to the target VA point and semantically compatible with the source image, then MooD combines an image adapter for visual affect transfer with ASP-Net, which projects emotion-relevant visual features into CLIP text-embedding space to provide explicit semantic grounding for SDXL. Unlike nearby work that relies on multi-round optimization or MLLM reasoning, MooD replaces expensive inference with retrieval plus selective semantic injection, and it further introduces a semantic anchoring loss with Mahalanobis-distance-based attribute weighting so multiple object attributes are supervised according to their natural VA distributions. To support this, the authors build AffectSet, a 63,387-image VA-annotated dataset derived from EmoSet and Qwen2.5-VL-72B labels. On EmoEdit’s inference set, MooD reports strong gains in affective controllability and fidelity while running at 4.5 s/MP, though it remains dependent on a preconstructed affect pool and slightly trades SSIM for emotion-aligned transfer. This points toward future AIE systems that edit with continuous affect, retrieval-grounded semantics, and more controllable affective trajectories.
papers.abstract: Affective image editing (AIE) aims to edit visual content to evoke target emotions. However, existing methods often overlook inference efficiency and predominantly depend on discrete emotion representations, which to some extent limits their practical applicability and makes it challenging to capture complex and subtle human emotions. To tackle these gaps, we propose MooD, the first framework that directly leverages continuous Valence-Arousal (VA) values for fine-grained and efficient AIE. Specifically, we first introduce a VA-Aware retrieval strategy to bridge vague affective values and concrete visual semantics. Building upon this, MooD integrates visual transfer and semantic guidance to achieve controllable AIE. Furthermore, we construct AffectSet, a VA-annotated dataset to support model optimization and evaluation. Extensive qualitative and quantitative experimental results demonstrate that our MooD achieves superior performance in both affective controllability and visual fidelity while maintaining high efficiency. A series of ablation studies further reveal the crucial factors of our design. Our code and data will be made publicly open soon.

papers.title: TemPose-TF-ASF: Two-Stage Bidirectional Stroke Context Fusion for Badminton Stroke Classification
papers.summary: TemPose-TF-ASF: Two-Stage Bidirectional Stroke Context Fusion for Badminton Stroke Classification targets a specific failure mode in fine-grained sports vision: stroke labels in badminton are not determined by the current clip alone, because visually similar swings are disambiguated by what came just before and what tactic follows next. The paper challenges the common workflow of predicting each segment in isolation, or depending on ground-truth future annotations, and pivots from current-stroke classification toward bidirectional sequence refinement. The mechanical shift is TSCR, a two-stage training/inference loop in which a baseline TemPose-TF first produces preliminary stroke predictions, those predicted labels are reinterpreted as estimated adjacent-stroke context, and an ASF Stroke Fusion module embeds and fuses preceding and subsequent stroke types through a small MLP before joint optimization with the classifier. This is unusual because it makes semantic context a learnable control signal rather than an external label stream, and it is designed to be backbone-agnostic: ASF also improves BlockGCN, SkateFormer, and BST-CG-AP. On the cleaned ShuttleSet benchmark (40 matches, 33,429 strokes, 31 categories after merging rare classes), TemPose-TF-ASF improves over TemPose-TF from 0.835 to 0.854 accuracy and from 0.742 to 0.761 Macro-F1. The result suggests a broader research direction toward staged, context-recovering action models where predicted structure is fed back as inference-time semantic prior, though the paper also shows that heavier three-stage or Transformer-based predictors can be less stable and more error-prone.
papers.abstract: Accurate badminton stroke prediction is crucial for fine-grained sports analysis and tactical decision support. However, existing methods struggle to model rich temporal context. This paper introduces \emph{TemPose-TF-ASF (Adjacent-Stroke Fusion)}, a context-aware extension of \emph{TemPose}. It enhances stroke recognition by incorporating stroke-type information from both preceding and subsequent strokes. A two-stage training and inference strategy is adopted. Preliminary predictions from the baseline model are reused as estimated temporal context. These predictions guide the joint optimization of the \emph{ASF} module and the classifier. By explicitly modeling bidirectional temporal stroke dependencies, the proposed method can be seamlessly integrated into existing state-of-the-art models. Experiments on a large-scale badminton match dataset show consistent improvements over the baseline and its variants in terms of Accuracy and Macro-F1. Moreover, integrating \emph{ASF} into other advanced methods yields notable performance gains. These results demonstrate strong transferability and generalization capability.

papers.title: Low-Latency Embedded Driver Monitoring System with a Multi-Task Neural Network
papers.summary: Low-Latency Embedded Driver Monitoring System with a Multi-Task Neural Network attacks the bottleneck in camera-based driver monitoring: existing pipelines can infer drowsiness or distraction, but they usually chain multiple detectors and regressors, which makes latency too high for in-vehicle, edge-budget deployment. The paper explicitly challenges the assumption that accurate driver attention monitoring must be assembled from separate per-signal models and pivots from multi-stage CV workflows toward a single embedded inference graph that produces all cues in one pass. The mechanical shift is a lightweight MobileNet-v2-style multi-task CNN that jointly predicts 98 facial landmarks, both eyes’ openness and visibility, mouth opening, head pose (yaw/pitch/roll), and distraction classes for phone use and smoking, then feeds those outputs into a finite-state decision layer with calibration, PERCLOS-style aggregation, thresholded risk scoring, and hysteresis to avoid flickering alerts. Unusually, the system is trained on LaPa-derived supervision plus a small pseudo-labeled distraction set, using the same architecture to cover six parallel tasks and explicitly targeting real-time operation on Nvidia Jetson Nano and Xavier NX. The full pipeline also trims overhead with face ROI tracking via SORT and TensorRT-optimized NMS inside an SSD face detector. Results show a clear accuracy/efficiency tradeoff across tiny/small/large variants, with end-to-end latency meeting the 20 FPS acquisition constraint while preserving usable task performance. The paper points toward future driver-monitoring research that is more modular at the system level yet more consolidated at the model level, with edge-native multi-task inference, better weak labels, and heterogeneous accelerator deployment.
papers.abstract: Road traffic accidents remain a significant global concern, with the majority attributed to human factors such as driver distraction and fatigue. This study proposes a camera-based approach to derive useful indicators to assess driver attentiveness and alertness. The proposed pipeline jointly satisfies the stringent real-time requirements imposed by the critical application and minimizes the computational requirements to allow for deployment on a tight computational budget. To this end, we develop a lightweight multi-task neural network that predicts multiple indicators for the face region in a single forward pass. The developed model is integrated into a complete execution workflow to produce a real-time estimate of attentiveness, fatigue, and engagement in distracting activities.

papers.title: Automated In-the-Wild Data Collection for Continual AI Generated Image Detection
papers.summary: Automated In-the-Wild Data Collection for Continual AI Generated Image Detection attacks the central failure mode of AI-generated image detectors: models tuned on static benchmark generators collapse under temporal drift, post-processing, and newly released image models, so the bottleneck is no longer detector capacity but how to keep training data aligned with an evolving synthetic ecosystem. The paper pivots from the usual assumption that better architectures or one-shot benchmark training are enough toward a data-centric continual adaptation loop that explicitly tracks non-stationary distributions. Its core mechanical shift is an automated, weakly supervised fact-check retrieval pipeline: an LLM extracts textual references to AI-generated images from fact-check articles, a VLM filters candidate images into anchor examples, image-encoder similarity expands them, and segmentation removes screenshot/UI noise, yielding WildFC. That in-the-wild stream is then combined with generator-driven samples from 19 recent models in AIGenImages2026 and a replay buffer inside a continual-learning update rule, replacing closed-world training with periodic, dual-source refreshing. Unusually, the method treats real-world news and social-media circulation as supervision signals rather than mere evaluation noise, and it pairs synthetic and real images through semantic retrieval to keep the detector calibrated on hard negatives. Across RINE and SPAI, this produces +8.01% and +9.14% average accuracy gains while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, suggesting a future where AI forensics becomes continuously harvested, weakly labeled, and deployment-adaptive rather than benchmark-frozen.
papers.abstract: The rapid advancement of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) has introduced significant challenges for reliable AI-generated image detection. Existing detectors often suffer from performance degradation under distribution shifts and when encountering newly emerging generative models. In this work, we propose a data-centric continual adaptation framework for updating detectors in evolving environments. We show that both in-the-wild data and generator-driven data are essential for adapting detectors. We introduce an automated, weakly supervised pipeline for constructing in-the-wild datasets through fact-check article retrieval. Additionally, we demonstrate that incorporating even a small amount of generator-driven data during training enables effective adaptation to newly emerging models, while combining it with in-the-wild data within a continual learning framework enables robust adaptation and mitigates catastrophic forgetting. Extensive experiments on two state-of-the-art detectors show significant improvements of +9.14% and +8% in average accuracy, respectively.

papers.title: Self-Supervised Spatial And Zero-Shot Angular Super-Resolution by Spatial-Angular Implicit Representation For Rotating-View SNR-Efficient Diffusion MRI
papers.summary: Self-Supervised Spatial And Zero-Shot Angular Super-Resolution by Spatial-Angular Implicit Representation For Rotating-View SNR-Efficient Diffusion MRI attacks the bottleneck that rotating-view thick-slice dMRI is SNR-efficient yet scan-prohibitive because classical Nyquist super-resolution demands many slice orientations per diffusion direction, effectively making each q-vector an independently oversampled inverse problem. The paper pivots from the assumption that dense rotating views are necessary toward a joint spatial-angular field model that treats x-space and q-space as coupled, continuous coordinates. The mechanical shift is a self-supervised spatial-angular implicit neural representation: an MLP is trained end-to-end on anisotropic single-view-per-direction inputs, conditioned on a high-SNR b=0 structural prior extracted by an RDN and modulated by the diffusion direction through FiLM, while a fixed forward model enforces rotation plus thick-slice downsampling in the loss. Unlike nearby MRI super-resolution work that only reconstructs the seen directions, SA-INR also learns a continuous q-space representation, enabling zero-shot synthesis of unseen b-directions without extra acquisitions. In 2D UK Biobank simulations (2 mm isotropic ground truth, 8 subjects, 50 b=1000 directions, 40 train/10 held out), it reconstructs with 34.82 dB on trained directions and 33.08 dB on unseen ones under a 7× accelerated N=1 regime, surpassing bilinear baselines and improving downstream DTI FA and EV1 accuracy when synthesized directions are added. The result suggests a future dMRI paradigm of sparse acquisition plus learned angular densification, with the main limitation being the need for prospective 3D, motion-aware, multi-shell validation.
papers.abstract: Rotating-view thick-slice acquisition is highly SNR-efficient for mesoscale diffusion MRI (dMRI) but requires numerous rotating views to satisfy Nyquist sampling, resulting in long scan time. We propose a self-supervised Spatial-Angular Implicit Neural Representation (SA-INR) that reconstructs high-resolution dMRI from a single view per diffusion direction, representing a massive acceleration. Our model, an MLP conditioned on a b=0 structural prior and the b-direction via FiLM, is trained end-to-end on the anisotropic input. The framework not only accurately reconstructs the trained b-directions (spatial SR) but also learns a continuous q-space representation, enabling high-fidelity "zero-shot" synthesis of unseen b-directions (angular SR). On simulated data, our method achieved high fidelity for both trained (34.82 dB) and unseen (33.08 dB) directions. Most importantly, the synthesized angular data also improved the quantitative accuracy of downstream DTI model fitting. Our SA-INR framework breaks the classical sampling limits, paving the way for fast, quantitative high-resolution dMRI.

papers.title: Hyp2Former: Hierarchy-Aware Hyperbolic Embeddings for Open-Set Panoptic Segmentation
papers.summary: Hyp2Former: Hierarchy-Aware Hyperbolic Embeddings for Open-Set Panoptic Segmentation attacks the failure mode of open-set panoptic segmentation where valid unseen objects are either collapsed into background or recovered only by brittle uncertainty-and-clustering pipelines that ignore semantic structure. It challenges the flat-label assumption behind prior OPS systems and pivots from “detect anomalies after classification” toward “learn a transferable notion of objectness from the taxonomy itself,” so unknowns can emerge as semantically plausible instances even when their fine-grained class never appeared in training. The mechanical shift is an end-to-end extension of Mask2Former that keeps class and mask heads in Euclidean space but projects query embeddings into the Lorentz hyperbolic manifold, where a hierarchical proxy-anchor loss pulls each query not only toward its leaf-class proxy but also toward dynamically computed ancestor proxies via hyperbolic averages. That structured space is then reused at inference: low-confidence queries are filtered by distance to the hierarchy, scored by object-subtree consistency and semantic divergence, and the top-K are promoted as unknown instances without pseudo-labels, OOD data, or DBSCAN postprocessing. This is unusual relative to nearby OPS work because unknowns are not modeled as a separate class or learned from void examples; instead, abstraction depth itself becomes the control lever. Across Cityscapes, Lost&Found, and MS COCO with a ResNet-50 Mask2Former backbone, Hyp2Former reports the best unknown PQ and the smallest open-world performance drop, while also running faster than clustering-based baselines. The paper points toward taxonomy-aware geometric priors for dense open-world perception in robots and autonomous systems.
papers.abstract: Recognizing unknown objects is crucial for safety-critical applications such as autonomous driving and robotics. Open-Set Panoptic Segmentation (OPS) aims to segment known thing and stuff classes while identifying valid unknown objects as separate instances. Prior OPS approaches largely treat known categories as a flat label set, ignoring the semantic hierarchy that provides valuable structural priors for distinguishing unknown objects from in-distribution classes. In this work, we propose Hyp2Former, an end-to-end framework for OPS that does not require explicit modeling of unknowns during training, and instead learns hierarchical semantic similarities continuously in hyperbolic space. By explicitly encoding hierarchical relationships among known categories, the model learns a structured embedding space that captures multiple levels of semantic abstraction. As a result, unknown objects that cannot be confidently classified as known categories still remain in close proximity to higher-level concepts (e.g., an unknown animal remains closer to "animal" or "object" than to unrelated concepts such as "electronics" or "stuff") and can therefore be reliably detected, even if their fine-grained category was not represented during training. Empirical evaluations across multiple public datasets such as MS COCO, Cityscapes, and Lost&Found demonstrate that Hyp2Former outperforms existing methods on OPS, achieving the best balance between unknown object discovery and in-distribution robustness.

papers.title: Stylistic Attribute Control in Latent Diffusion Models
papers.summary: Stylistic Attribute Control in Latent Diffusion Models attacks the failure of text-to-image editing to vary style knobs such as contour width, blackpoint, local contrast, watercolor splatter, and stroke density without dragging content along for the ride. It challenges the assumption that prompt wording or global CFG can deliver Photoshop-like slider control, and pivots from instruction-based semantic editing toward parameterized stylistic directions learned from synthetic filter corpora. The mechanical shift is to generate stylized training pairs at multiple effect strengths, finetune only extra attention layers in a ControlNet branch with an explicit λA embedding, and then compose the learned stylistic guidance with the frozen Stable Diffusion/ControlNet denoising signal so content and style can be disentangled. To preserve stability, the method adds a regularization term that penalizes unintended latent differences outside intended edit regions, delays guidance activation early in the diffusion trajectory, and for real-image editing replaces standard null-text inversion with optimized null-parameter embeddings. Unlike nearby instruction-editing work, it does not ask the prompt to describe “thicker strokes” or “more watercolor”; it learns a continuous control space from synthetic filtered MS COCO and ArtBench-derived data. Experiments show smoother, more faithful edits than InstructPix2Pix, InstructDiffusion, StyleBooth, and null-text inversion, with better user preference and stable interpolation across λ, though at the cost of extra training data, added runtime, and imperfect inversions. This points toward diffusion systems with explicit, composable visual control surfaces rather than brittle natural-language edit prompts.
papers.abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models have revolutionized image synthesis and editing, but precise control over stylistic attributes remains a challenge, often causing unintended content modifications. We propose an approach for fine-grained parametric control of stylistic attributes in latent diffusion models by learning disentangled editing directions from synthetic datasets. We use guidance composition to close the domain gap between stylistically finetuned and foundation models, preserving the original image semantics while applying stylistic adjustments. To ensure consistent edits, we introduce a training regularization loss and enhance DDIM inversion with optimized null-conditional embeddings for real image editing. We validate our approach by learning from stylistically filtered synthetic datasets varying a range of stylistic attributes, including outlines, local contrast, watercolorization effects, and geometric patterns. Our evaluations demonstrate that compared to current text-based editing techniques, our method offers well-integrated, more precise and continuously adjustable stylistic modifications.

papers.title: StableMind: Source-Free Cross-Subject fMRI Decoding with Regularized Adaptation
papers.summary: StableMind: Source-Free Cross-Subject fMRI Decoding with Regularized Adaptation attacks the practical bottleneck in cross-subject brain decoding where a decoder pretrained on other subjects must adapt to a new subject with only about one hour of paired fMRI-image data, while the raw source-subject scans are inaccessible for privacy/storage reasons. The paper argues that the usual recipe—latent alignment plus lightweight fine-tuning—fails because adaptation is destabilized by two coupled failures: subject-dependent drift in voxel-to-latent projections and unreliable image-side supervision when limited fMRI cannot support fine-grained visual details. It pivots from “just align representations” toward explicitly regularizing the adaptation process itself at both the subject-transfer and cross-modal supervision levels. The mechanical shift is StableMind’s three-part loop: Cross-Subject Ridge Reuse fuses a target ridge mapper with an average of frozen source ridge projections and a weak cosine distillation prior; Feature-Level Brain Augmentation perturbs intermediate brain features in the Fourier domain by resampling amplitude statistics while preserving phase structure; and Difficulty-Aware Image Blur uses an EMA difficulty bank plus LayerCAM-derived saliency to blur easy samples more aggressively while preserving structure for harder ones, reducing overconfident detail matching. On NSD, under the unified 1-hour protocol across four held-out subjects, StableMind reaches 84.02% image retrieval and 81.66% brain retrieval, outperforming prior cross-subject methods with fewer trainable parameters. This points toward source-free, stability-aware neurodecoding systems that regularize both neural transfer and supervision reliability.
papers.abstract: Existing cross-subject fMRI decoding methods typically train a model on multiple scanned subjects and then adapt it to a new subject using substantial paired fMRI-image data. However, in realistic scenarios, new-subject fMRI data are often limited due to costly data acquisition, and raw data from previous subjects may be inaccessible, leading existing methods to suffer performance degradation during new-subject adaptation. In this paper, we identify that this degradation stems from two key issues: brain-side instability caused by large subject differences in fMRI responses, and image-side supervision unreliability caused by fine-grained visual details that are not reliably supported by limited fMRI signals. To address these challenges, we propose StableMind, a regularized adaptation framework designed to improve brain-side representation stability and image-side supervision reliability. (1) To stabilize brain representations, StableMind reuses ridge projections from the pretrained model as adaptation priors to constrain limited-data new-subject adaptation, and applies Fourier-based feature-level brain augmentation to improve robustness to individual variability. (2) To improve image supervision reliability, StableMind introduces difficulty-aware image blur for brain-image alignment, reducing the influence of fine-grained visual details that are weakly supported by limited fMRI signals while preserving stable visual structure. Experiments on the Natural Scenes Dataset under a unified 1-hour adaptation protocol demonstrate that StableMind achieves 84.02% image retrieval accuracy and 81.66% brain retrieval accuracy averaged over four subjects, surpassing the state-of-the-art method by 5.71% brain retrieval accuracy with fewer trainable adaptation parameters. Our code is available at https://github.com/lingeringlight/StableMind.

papers.title: Representation learning from OCT images
papers.summary: Representation learning from OCT images is a survey that attacks a field-level bottleneck rather than a single benchmark: retinal OCT analysis is still constrained by speckle noise, scarce expert labels, heterogeneous devices, and the fact that most models flatten intrinsically volumetric scans into isolated 2D B-scans, losing inter-slice continuity and clinical context. The paper challenges the old assumption that OCT can simply inherit natural-image CNN/ViT workflows and scale by more supervision; instead it traces a pivot from fully supervised disease classifiers toward unlabeled-data learning, generative modeling, 3D volumetric reasoning, multimodal fusion, and finally foundation models with vision-language interfaces. The mechanical shift is not a new network but a new research map: contrastive, reconstruction, and pretext objectives for self-supervision; VAEs, GANs, and diffusion models for learning the OCT distribution; 3D/temporal encoders for volume coherence and progression; and cross-modal pretraining with fundus/OCTA/metadata plus language-grounded report generation. Its differentiator is the unified taxonomy and problem formulation that aligns these paradigms with dataset and evaluation protocol choices, including patient-wise splitting and cross-device testing, exposing how many reported gains are inflated by leakage or single-vendor benchmarks. It concludes that the next frontier is clinically deployable OCT representation learning: volumetric foundation models, uncertainty-aware and fairness-aware training, federated/privacy-preserving learning, and concept-based interpretability that aligns latent features with retinal anatomy and disease progression.
papers.abstract: Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) has become one of the most used imaging modality in ophthalmology. It provides high-resolution, non-invasive visualization of retinal microarchitecture. The automated analysis of OCT images through representation learning has emerged as a central research frontier. This has mainly been driven by the clinical need to process large acquisition volumes. The objective is to reduce the reliance on expert annotation, and improve diagnostic consistency across devices and populations. This survey provides a comprehensive and structured review of representation learning methods for retinal OCT image analysis. It covers the period from early deep learning approaches to the most recent developments in foundation models and vision-language systems. We organize the literature along a principled taxonomy of learning paradigms, encompassing supervised learning with CNN-based and transformer-based architectures, self-supervised and semi-supervised methods, generative approaches, as well as 3D volumetric modeling, multimodal representation learning, and large-scale pretrained foundation models. For each paradigm, we analyze the core methodological contributions, identify persistent limitations, and trace the connections between successive approaches. We further provide a structured overview of publicly available OCT datasets, discuss evaluation protocol considerations, and present a unified problem formulation that situates each learning paradigm within a common mathematical framework. Building on this analysis, we identify and discuss the most pressing open research directions emerging in the literature. This includes volumetric foundation model pretraining, uncertainty-aware representation learning, federated and privacy-preserving training, fairness and bias mitigation, concept-based interpretability,...

papers.title: Rethinking the Need for Source Models: Source-Free Domain Adaptation from Scratch Guided by a Vision-Language Model
papers.summary: Rethinking the Need for Source Models: Source-Free Domain Adaptation from Scratch Guided by a Vision-Language Model attacks a hidden bottleneck in SFDA: most “source-free” methods still depend on a source-pretrained initialization, so they only remove source data, not source information, and their pseudo-label pipeline remains anchored in a model trained on the source domain. The paper pivots from the standard assumption that a source model is necessary toward a stricter ViL-only adaptation regime, ViL-Only Domain Adaptation (VODA), where training starts from random weights and uses only a frozen CLIP-style vision-language model plus unlabeled target images. The key mechanical shift is two-stage adaptation: first a pure ViL-guided warm-up, then Two-Stage Denoised-Region Distillation (TS-DRD), which forms supervision by combining the ViL prediction with the adapting model’s prediction to exploit complementary noise and construct a “denoised region” with cleaner pseudo-labels; prompts are jointly tuned on the ViL side while the student is optimized with mutual-information alignment, entropy balancing, and pseudo-label loss. Unlike nearby CLIP-guided SFDA work that still transfers from a source checkpoint, this method explicitly tests whether source initialization matters at all and finds it often contributes little under strong ViL guidance. Across Office-Home, VisDA, and DomainNet-126, TS-DRD matches or exceeds source-dependent SFDA baselines, including in open-set Office-Home, suggesting a direction where domain adaptation becomes genuinely source-information-free and more deployment-ready for privacy-sensitive or source-absent settings.
papers.abstract: Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) adapts source models to target domains without accessing source data, addressing privacy and transmission issues. However, existing methods still initialize from a source pre-trained model and thus are not truly source-free. Recent works have introduced Vision-Language (ViL) models to guide the adaptation process, in these methods, we observe that for the same target domain, different source models yield minimal variation in final results, indicating the source model itself has limited impact. Motivated by this, we propose ViL-Only Domain Adaptation (VODA) , a stricter setting that eliminates all dependencies on source domain, relying solely on a randomly initialized model, a ViL model, and unlabeled target data. We analyze the adaptation dynamics of VODA and introduce Two-Stage Denoised-Region Distillation (TS-DRD) , a two-stage framework that first warms up the model with ViL guidance, then seek a Denoised-Region inherent in both the ViL and adapting model, yielding cleaner supervision for distillation. Experiments on Office-Home, VisDA, and DomainNet-126 show that under VODA, TS-DRD achieves competitive or superior performance to existing SFDA methods that still use source models, demonstrating its effectiveness and the potential of the VODA setting.

papers.title: Validation of an AI-based end-to-end model for prostate pathology using long-term archived routine samples
papers.summary: Validation of an AI-based end-to-end model for prostate pathology using long-term archived routine samples attacks a clinical bottleneck that is easy to miss in benchmark-driven pathology AI: whether a Gleason grader that looks strong on contemporary slides still works on routine biopsy material stored for up to 17 years, across changing staining, preservation, scanners, and local laboratory practice. The paper implicitly challenges the assumption that external validation on recent, homogeneous cohorts is enough, and it also pushes back against the idea that general-purpose foundation encoders automatically solve batch effects and archival drift. The pivot is from scaling representation pretraining toward task-specific, end-to-end optimization and temporal robustness as the real generalization test. Mechanically, GleasonAI uses a UNet++ tissue detector followed by an EfficientNet-V2-S gated attention multiple instance learning pipeline trained end-to-end, then validated on a fully independent Swedish ProMort cohort of 10,366 biopsy cores from 1,028 patients across 14 regions, with expert re-grading to contemporary ISUP standards. A deterministic 10-model ensemble with test-time augmentation yields core-level grade calls, while exploratory runs compare against frozen UNI and Virchow2 features under the same MIL head. The unusual contribution is not just accuracy but archival invariance: quadratic-weighted kappa stays at 0.86 across geography and collection period, and AI grades show a monotonic prostate-cancer mortality gradient. This suggests a future where pathology archives become a long-horizon robustness benchmark and a retrospective prognostic substrate, though the authors note Swedish-only data and annotation/patching constraints.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is becoming a clinical tool for prostate pathology, but generalization across variations in sample preparation and preservation over prolonged time periods remains poorly understood. We evaluated GleasonAI, an end-to-end attention-based multiple instance learning model, on an independent validation cohort comprising 10,366 biopsy cores from 1,028 patients across 14 Swedish regions, using archival diagnostic specimens from the ProMort cohorts collected between 1998-2015. The model achieved an overall quadratic-weighted kappa of 0.86 for core-level ISUP grading, comparable to several experienced pathologists and consistent across geographic regions. Notably, performance remained stable across the 17-year collection period, demonstrating robustness to time-related variation in archival material, a property not consistently observed with foundation model-based approaches, with exploratory analysis demonstrating a significant prognostic gradient across AI-assigned grade groups for prostate cancer-specific mortality. These findings support the generalizability of the AI grading model and demonstrate the potential of pathology archives as a large-scale resource for AI development, validation, and retrospective prognostic research.

papers.title: Global-Local Feature Decoding with Adapter-Guided SAMv2 for Salient Object Detection
papers.summary: Global-Local Feature Decoding with Adapter-Guided SAMv2 for Salient Object Detection attacks the persistent bottleneck in salient object detection: foundation models such as SAMv2 generalize well, but their raw masks still break on subtle boundaries, low-contrast objects, and camouflaged scenes, while full fine-tuning is expensive and overfits when SOD data are limited. The paper explicitly pivots away from prompt-centric or end-to-end backbone adaptation toward a frozen hierarchical encoder plus task-specific decoding, replacing “train the big model” with “modulate a strong prior efficiently.” The mechanical shift is GLASSNet: the SAMv2 image encoder (HiERA-L) stays frozen, and lightweight spatially aware convolutional adapters are inserted at each encoding stage, cutting learnable encoder parameters by over 97%. Those adapted features then feed a dual-stream decoder, where a global branch uses criss-cross attention and multi-scale dilated convolutions to expand receptive field and capture long-range semantics, while a local branch uses Swin blocks and convolutional fusion to preserve edges, textures, and small structures; a medium-scale sub-branch bridges the two and auxiliary supervision is applied to balance them. Compared with nearby SAM-based SOD work, the differentiator is the combination of convolutional adaptation and explicit global-local decomposition rather than relying on prompts, LoRA-style tuning, or a single decoder. Trained on DUTS-TE and evaluated on HKU-IS, DUT-OMRON, ECSSD, PASCAL-S, and COD benchmarks like CAMO, COD10K, and NC4K, GLASSNet reports state-of-the-art accuracy with strong efficiency, suggesting a direction where frozen foundation models become dense-prediction backbones through minimal spatial adapters and context-specialized decoders.
papers.abstract: Salient Object Detection (SOD) remains an essential yet underexplored task in the era of large-scale vision models. Although foundation models like SAM exhibit strong generalization, their potential for SOD is not fully realized, and training or fully fine-tuning them is computationally expensive and prone to overfitting under limited data. To overcome these challenges, we introduce GLASSNet, a Global-Local feature decoding framework that uses SAMv2 as a frozen encoder paired with a lightweight, spatially aware convolutional adapter-reducing learnable encoder parameters by over 97%. To enhance saliency quality, GLASSNet employs a dual-decoder architecture: one decoder captures global, long-range semantics with an expanded receptive field, while the other captures fine local details such as edges and textures. Fusing these complementary cues yields saliency maps that combine global coherence with local precision, producing accurate final masks. Extensive experiments on standard SOD and camouflaged object detection benchmarks show that GLASSNet surpasses state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating the power of frozen foundation models combined with targeted adaptation and global-local decoding.

papers.title: Retrieving Any Relevant Moments: Benchmark and Models for Generalized Moment Retrieval
papers.summary: Retrieving Any Relevant Moments: Benchmark and Models for Generalized Moment Retrieval attacks the hidden single-answer assumption in video moment retrieval, where a query is forced to map to exactly one segment even when the real-world event repeats multiple times or never appears. The paper pivots from conventional VMR, which optimizes one-best localization, toward a set-valued retrieval problem that must return all relevant intervals or an empty set, making null-set rejection and multi-moment coverage first-class objectives. Its mechanical shift is not just a new label space but a new benchmark-and-training stack: Soccer-GMR, built from 5.5k soccer clips across 139 matches with 22.1k query-moment pairs, uses a duration-flexible semi-automated pipeline with LLM query extraction, balanced sampling, rule-based boundary expansion, and human/expert verification to create realistic in-domain negatives and dense positive cases. Evaluation is likewise redesigned with Rej-F1 for abstention, mR+@k for extra-hit retrieval beyond the first match, and G-mIoU@k for end-to-end set accuracy. On the modeling side, the paper replaces backbone surgery with a lightweight GMR Adapter that max-pools decoder slots into an existence branch for thresholded rejection, and separately tunes MLLMs with a GMR-specific GRPO reward that directly scores recall, IoU, and empty-set behavior. Compared with nearby work, this is unusual in unifying null rejection, multi-instance grounding, and duration-flexible data generation. Results show strong gains in rejection without harming localization, while also exposing that multi-moment retrieval and MLLM temporal grounding remain brittle, pointing toward abstention-aware, set-prediction video-language systems.
papers.abstract: Video Moment Retrieval (VMR) aims to localize temporal segments in videos that correspond to a natural language query, but typically assumes only a single matching moment for each query. This assumption does not always hold in real-world scenarios, where queries may correspond to multiple or no moments. Thus, we formulate Generalized Moment Retrieval (GMR), a unified setting that requires retrieving the complete set of relevant moments or predicting an empty set. To enable systematic study of GMR, we introduce Soccer-GMR, a large-scale benchmark built on challenging soccer videos that reflect general GMR scenarios, with realistic negative and positive queries. The benchmark is constructed via a duration-flexible semi-automated pipeline with human verification, enabling scalable data generation while maintaining high annotation quality. We further design a unified evaluation protocol with complementary metrics tailored for null-set rejection, positive-query localization, and end-to-end GMR performance. Finally, we establish strong baselines across two modeling paradigms: a lightweight plug-and-play GMR adapter for discriminative VMR models, and a GMR-tailored GRPO reward for fine-tuning multimodal large language models (MLLMs). Extensive experiments show consistent gains across all metrics and expose key limitations of current methods, positioning GMR as a more realistic and challenging benchmark for video-language understanding.

papers.title: Rethinking Low-Light Image Enhancement: A Log-Domain Intensity--Chromaticity Decoupling Perspective
papers.summary: Rethinking Low-Light Image Enhancement: A Log-Domain Intensity--Chromaticity Decoupling Perspective attacks the core LLIE failure mode that direct RGB-to-RGB restoration entangles exposure recovery, color correction, and noise suppression, causing unstable brightening, channel over-amplification, chromatic noise, and detail loss in severely underexposed scenes. The paper explicitly challenges the dominant assumption that better low-light enhancement comes from bigger RGB-space transformers or diffusion priors, and pivots from capacity scaling toward representation design: instead of predicting an enhanced RGB image directly, it reformulates the problem in a log-domain intensity–chromaticity space where a pixel is split into a max-channel intensity envelope and log-ratio chromaticity. The mechanical shift is a reversible decoupling transform plus constrained inverse reconstruction: ICDNet uses dual branches to separately model exposure compensation and chromatic consistency, an intensity-aware chromaticity gate to downweight unreliable dark-region color cues, cross-branch interaction blocks to share structure, and explicit non-positive/zero-anchor constraints so reconstructed channels remain bounded by the output intensity envelope. This is unusual relative to nearby LLIE work because it makes color stability a hard feasibility condition rather than an implicit loss outcome, and it couples that with lightweight space-to-channel folding for efficiency. On LOLv2-Real, MIT-Adobe FiveK, and LSRW it is competitive or superior, reaching 29.71 dB PSNR and 0.89 SSIM on LOLv2-Real, and it also improves DarkFace detection, though very high-precision localization under extreme darkness remains difficult. The paper points toward LLIE systems built around physically grounded latent spaces, constrained reconstruction, and deployment-efficient enhancement modules.
papers.abstract: Explicit reconstruction constraints derived from the decoupled representation are further imposed to suppress abnormal channel amplification and chromatic noise. Experiments on LOLv2-Real, MIT-Adobe FiveK, and LSRW show that the proposed method achieves competitive or superior quantitative and visual performance, reaching 29.71 dB PSNR and 0.89 SSIM on LOLv2-Real. DarkFace experiments further indicate improved downstream face detection under low-light conditions. Code and pretrained models are available at: https://github.com/mubaisam/ICD.

papers.title: AutoFocus: Uncertainty-Aware Active Visual Search for GUI Grounding
papers.summary: AutoFocus: Uncertainty-Aware Active Visual Search for GUI Grounding attacks the resolution gap that makes VLM-based GUI agents fail on dense, high-resolution interfaces: models can identify the right widget semantically yet miss the exact pixel because one-pass coordinate decoding is forced to localize tiny targets through resized inputs. It explicitly challenges the old workflow of fixed-anchor crops, heuristic grids, and reinforcement-learned zoom policies, pivoting from single-shot grounding toward uncertainty-driven test-time visual search. The core mechanical shift is to reinterpret token-level perplexity during coordinate generation as an intrinsic spatial ambiguity signal: AutoFocus samples multiple coordinate hypotheses, decomposes their x/y perplexities, and splats them into an anisotropic Gaussian density field that models directional uncertainty rather than a point estimate. That field drives both global and local region proposals, while Shape-Aware Zooming interpolates between tight anisotropic crops and square crops to preserve context around elongated UI structures. A separate visual prompt self-verification step decides whether refinement is needed, and a multi-image aggregation stage compares candidate marked regions to select the most consistent final coordinate. Unlike nearby GUI grounding work, it is entirely training-free, model-agnostic, and decouples predictor, verifier, and selector at inference. On ScreenSpot-Pro and ScreenSpot-V2, it consistently boosts general and GUI-specialized backbones such as Qwen2.5-VL, UI-TARS, GTA1-Qwen, and UI-Venus, with especially strong gains on small-icon and dense professional software cases, suggesting a future of uncertainty-aware foveated agents and test-time scaling built on internal generative signals.
papers.abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled autonomous GUI agents that translate natural language instructions into executable screen coordinates. However, grounding performance degrades in high-resolution interfaces, where dense layouts and small interactive elements expose a resolution gap between modern displays and model input constraints. Existing zoom-in strategies rely on fixed anchors, heuristic grids, or reinforcement learning, lacking a principled mechanism to adaptively determine where refinement is needed and how much spatial uncertainty should be explored. We propose AutoFocus, a training-free, uncertainty-aware active visual search framework for GUI grounding. Our key insight is that token-level perplexity in coordinate generation naturally reflects spatial uncertainty. Rather than committing to a single prediction, AutoFocus samples multiple coordinate hypotheses and converts their axial perplexities into an anisotropic gaussian spatial probability field, explicitly modeling directional uncertainty. Based on this field, we generate global and local region proposals and introduce Shape-Aware Zooming to balance tight localization with contextual preservation. A visual prompt-based aggregation step then selects the most consistent prediction via structured comparison. Extensive experiments on ScreenSpot-Pro and ScreenSpot-V2 demonstrate consistent improvements across both general-purpose and GUI-specialized VLMs.

papers.title: ViewSAM: Learning View-aware Cross-modal Semantics for Weakly Supervised Cross-view Referring Multi-Object Tracking
papers.summary: ViewSAM: Learning View-aware Cross-modal Semantics for Weakly Supervised Cross-view Referring Multi-Object Tracking attacks the annotation bottleneck in CRMOT, where prior systems need dense frame-level boxes and cross-view ID links to track language-specified objects across cameras. The paper challenges the assumption that foundation models like SAM2/SAM3 can be directly adapted as referring trackers; its empirical finding is that they still drift to distractors under occlusion and cannot preserve global identity across views, even with task-specific prompts. The pivot is away from end-to-end supervision-heavy cross-view tracking toward a weakly supervised pipeline that uses only coarse object category labels and raw synchronized multi-view video. The mechanical shift is two-stage: first, SAM3 is repurposed as a pseudo-label generator via Affinity-guided Cross-view Re-prompting, which associates tracklets by embedding affinity, then bi-directionally re-prompts them to refine cross-view trajectories and uses an MLLM to group them into referring-level pseudo labels; second, a SAM2-based ViewSAM learns view-aware cross-modal semantics, treating view-induced appearance changes as learnable conditions rather than nuisance variation. Its core modules are a learnable dynamic View Token, View-conditioned Cross-modal Alignment, Bias-aware Recalibration to recover from memory-induced drift, and a Consistency-guided Cross-view Tracking Head that enforces intra-/inter-view identity compactness. Evaluated on CRTrack, it achieves state-of-the-art weakly supervised results and stays competitive with fully supervised methods using only ~10% extra parameters, suggesting a future where scalable multi-camera reasoning is built from foundation-model pseudo supervision plus explicit view-conditioning and consistency learning.
papers.abstract: Cross-view Referring Multi-Object Tracking (CRMOT) aims to track multiple objects specified by natural language across multiple camera views, with globally consistent identities. Despite recent progress, existing methods rely heavily on costly frame-level spatial annotations and cross-view identity supervision. To reduce such reliance, we explore CRMOT under weak supervision by leveraging the capabilities of foundation models. However, our empirical study shows that directly applying foundation models such as SAM2 and SAM3, even with task-specific modifications, fails to accurately understand referring expressions and maintain consistent identities across views. Yet, they remain effective at producing reliable object tracklets that can serve as pseudo supervision. We therefore repurpose foundation models as pseudo-label generators and propose a two-stage framework for weakly supervised CRMOT, using only object category labels as coarse-grained supervision. In the first stage, we design an Affinity-guided Cross-view Re-prompting strategy to refine and associate SAM3-generated tracklets across cameras, producing reliable cross-view pseudo labels for subsequent training. In the second stage, we introduce ViewSAM, a CRMOT model built upon SAM2 that explicitly models view-aware cross-modal semantics. By formulating view-induced variations as learnable conditions, ViewSAM bridges the gap between view-variant visual observations and view-invariant textual expressions, enabling robust cross-view referring tracking with only approximately 10% additional parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ViewSAM achieves SOTA performance under weak supervision and remains competitive with fully supervised methods.

papers.title: Mamoda2.5: Enhancing Unified Multimodal Model with DiT-MoE
papers.summary: Mamoda2.5: Enhancing Unified Multimodal Model with DiT-MoE attacks the core bottleneck that high-quality video generation and instruction-based video editing are still trapped in dense, quadratic-cost DiT pipelines and fragmented specialist systems, where separate models, staged data syntheses, and autoregressive visual token prediction make long, editable video prohibitively slow and brittle. The paper pivots from “bigger dense video models” and task-specific editors toward a single AR-Diffusion architecture that unifies multimodal understanding, text-to-image/video generation, image editing, and video editing, with the mechanical shift coming from a fine-grained DiT-MoE backbone: 128 routed experts with Top-8 token routing, one shared expert, and sparse activation that scales to 25B total parameters while using only about 3B per forward pass. It further replaces autoregressive visual decoding with MetaQueries that turn the understanding stack into a conditioning bridge, uses a higher-compression 4×16×16 causal VAE to cut token count, and initializes MoE from a dense checkpoint via random-neuron upcycling rather than naïve duplication. For editing data, it departs from multi-stage controllable synthesis by sharing early denoising steps between prompt-paired generations to preserve structure. Training then combines joint few-step distillation with DiffusionNFT reinforcement learning to compress a 30-step editor into a 4-step, CFG-free student.
