Across VBench 2.0 and OpenVE-Bench, Mamoda2.5 reports top-tier open-source generation and state-of-the-art editing, matching or exceeding proprietary systems like Kling O1, while pointing toward sparse, task-agnostic multimodal models that optimize for production latency as a first-class objective.
papers.abstract: We present Mamoda2.5, a unified AR-Diffusion framework that seamlessly integrates multimodal understanding and generation within a single architecture. To efficiently enhance the model's generation capability, we equip the Diffusion Transformer backbone with a fine-grained Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) design (128 experts, Top-8 routing), yielding a 25B-parameter model that activates only 3B parameters, significantly reducing training costs while scaling up the model capacity. Mamoda2.5 achieves top-tier generation performance on VBench 2.0 and sets a new record in video editing quality, surpassing evaluated open-source models and matching the performance of current top-tier proprietary models, including the Kling O1 on OpenVE-Bench. Furthermore, we introduce a joint few-step distillation and reinforcement learning framework that compresses the 30-step editing model into a 4-step model and greatly accelerates model inference. Compared to open-source baselines, Mamoda2.5 achieves up to $95.9\times$ faster video editing inference. In real-world applications, Mamoda2.5 has been successfully deployed for content moderation and creative restoration tasks in advertising scenarios, achieving a 98% success rate in internal advertising video editing scenario.

papers.title: Human Activity Recognition Method for Moderate Violence Detection
papers.summary: Human Activity Recognition Method for Moderate Violence Detection attacks a narrow but operationally important failure mode in surveillance analytics: catching pushing as an early, moderate aggression signal before it escalates, especially when human operators miss brief incidents in crowded public spaces. The paper implicitly challenges the newer HAR habit of relying on end-to-end temporal deep models and richer interaction embeddings for every violence task, and instead pivots from appearance-heavy action recognition toward pose-geometry under deployment constraints. The mechanical shift is a hierarchical pipeline: YOLO11 does single-class person detection and tracking, YOLO11-Pose extracts 17 keypoints, and the system converts skeletons into biomechanical descriptors—torso inclination plus shoulder/hip and arm angles computed by dot products—before a Random Forest decides push versus normal only for two-person interactions. That replacement of sequence modeling with explicit kinematic features is the distinctive move, aimed at preserving real-time performance and edge-device feasibility. The authors evaluate the method on three progressively harder surveillance case studies: close frontal views, elevated medium-distance views, and a realistic high-altitude panoramic scene with distortion, noise, and occlusion. Precision reaches 0.98 in the controlled setting and drops to 0.72 in the most difficult real-world scenario, showing that pose-based cues are strong but fragile under extreme viewpoint change. The result points toward future work that adds temporal models such as LSTMs or Transformers, but only after a geometry-first detector has localized suspicious dyadic contact.
papers.abstract: Physical violence in public spaces is a significant public health concern, with minor incidents such as pushing often serving as precursors to more severe escalations. This research develops an automated system for the real-time detection of moderate physical violence, specifically pushing, in surveillance camera footage. The proposed solution integrates state-of-the-art computer vision models, utilizing YOLO11 and YOLO11-Pose for human detection and skeletal keypoint extraction. By calculating body inclination and joint angles between shoulders and hips, a Random Forest classifier was trained to distinguish between normal behavior and aggressive physical contact. The system's performance was evaluated through three progressive case studies representing increasing levels of difficulty. In controlled environments with frontal views, the model achieved a precision of 0.98. In the most challenging scenario, featuring high-altitude, steep-angle recordings from real-world surveillance infrastructure, the system maintained a precision of 0.72 despite significant perspective distortion and visual noise. These results demonstrate the feasibility of using skeletal analysis for early violence intervention in urban security contexts.

papers.title: SAIL: Structure-Aware Interpretable Learning for Anatomy-Aligned Post-hoc Explanations in OCT
papers.summary: SAIL: Structure-Aware Interpretable Learning for Anatomy-Aligned Post-hoc Explanations in OCT attacks a clinical bottleneck where OCT classifiers can score like experts yet generate Grad-CAM-style saliency that is diffuse, artifact-sensitive, and blind to retinal layer boundaries, making explanations too weak for trust and regulation. It explicitly challenges the assumption that post-hoc XAI alone can rescue a black-box classifier after the fact, and pivots from explanation-only visualization toward representation-level anatomy priors that make anatomically faithful attribution emerge naturally. The mechanical shift is a two-stage pipeline: first pretrain a U-Net-style encoder–decoder on retinal layer segmentation with Dice-plus-cross-entropy loss on Duke DME and NR206, then fine-tune for disease classification while fusing class-semantic encoder features with structure-preserving decoder features through a gated linear fusion module; unchanged post-hoc methods such as Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and HiResCAM are then applied to this fused space. Unusually, SAIL does not add an explanation head or regularize saliency directly; instead it changes the feature geometry so attribution is layer-aware by construction, and it evaluates this with anatomy-grounded metrics, Relevance Mass Accuracy and Relevance Rank Accuracy, plus layer-wise relevance summaries. Across public OCT benchmarks and a large real-world UF cohort for DME, AMD, glaucoma, and ERM, SAIL keeps AUROC near strong baselines while sharply improving retina-confined, clinically plausible explanations and reducing background leakage. Its limitation is that it operates on single B-scans and depends on segmentation masks that can be noisy in severe disease, pointing toward volumetric structure-aware models and clinician co-evaluation as the next step.
papers.abstract: Optical coherence tomography (OCT), a commonly used retinal imaging modality, plays a central role in retinal disease diagnosis by providing high-resolution visualization of retinal layers. While deep learning (DL) has achieved expert-level accuracy in OCT-based retinal disease detection, its "black box" nature poses challenges for clinical adoption, where explainability is essential for clinical trust and regulatory approval. Existing post-hoc explainable AI (XAI) methods often struggle to delineate fine-grained lesion structures, respect anatomical boundaries, or suppress noise, limiting the trustworthiness of their explanations. To bridge these gaps, we propose a Structure-Aware Interpretable Learning (SAIL) framework that integrates retinal anatomical priors at the representation level and couples them with semantic features via a fusion design. Without modifying standard post-hoc explainability methods, this representation yields sharper and more anatomically aligned attribution maps. Comprehensive experiments on diverse OCT datasets demonstrate that our structure-aware method consistently enhances interpretability, producing clinically meaningful and anatomy-aware explanations. Ablation studies further show that strong interpretability requires both structural priors and semantic features, and that properly fusing the two is critical to achieve the best explanation quality. Together, these results highlight structure-aware representations as a key step toward reliable explainability in OCT.

papers.title: OphMAE: Bridging Volumetric and Planar Imaging with a Foundation Model for Adaptive Ophthalmological Diagnosis
papers.summary: OphMAE: Bridging Volumetric and Planar Imaging with a Foundation Model for Adaptive Ophthalmological Diagnosis attacks the mismatch between ophthalmic AI and real clinical diagnosis: most prior foundation models learn from a single image type, yet ophthalmologists synthesize paired 3D OCT volumes and 2D en face OCT, and many deployment sites lack full 3D hardware. The paper pivots from single-modality pretraining and brittle late-fusion toward a paired multimodal foundation model that can learn cross-view correspondences while still degrading gracefully to one available input. The mechanical shift is a masked autoencoder extended with modality-specific 3D and 2D ViT encoders, bidirectional cross-attention fusion, and lightweight decoders, trained not only with masked reconstruction but also with cross-modal relation masking and multi-space semantic similarity consistency, so the model reconstructs semantic relations across modalities rather than merely pixels. Pretrained on 183,875 anatomically paired OCT/en face scans from 32,765 patients, OphMAE was evaluated on 17 downstream tasks over 48,340 paired scans from 8,191 patients, spanning AMD, DME, glaucoma staging, and systemic disease prediction. It outperformed RETFound, VisionFM, and OCTCube on most benchmarks, reaching 96.9% AUC for AMD and 97.2% for DME, while remaining useful in constrained settings with 93.7% AUC for AMD using only 2D en face input and retaining 95.7% AUC with just 500 labels. This suggests a direction toward clinically robust, modality-flexible medical foundation models that learn from aligned multimodal anatomy rather than fixed-input pipelines.
papers.abstract: The advent of foundation models has heralded a new era in medical artificial intelligence (AI), enabling the extraction of generalizable representations from large-scale unlabeled datasets. However, current ophthalmic AI paradigms are predominantly constrained to single-modality inference, thereby creating a dissonance with clinical practice where diagnosis relies on the synthesis of complementary imaging modalities. Furthermore, the deployment of high-performance AI in resource-limited settings is frequently impeded by the unavailability of advanced three-dimensional imaging hardware. Here, we present the Ophthalmic multimodal Masked Autoencoder (OphMAE), a multi-imaging foundation model engineered to synergize the volumetric depth of 3D Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) with the planar context of 2D en face OCT. By implementing a novel cross-modal fusion architecture and a unique adaptive inference mechanism, OphMAE was pre-trained on a massive dataset with of 183,875 paired OCT images derived from 32,765 patients. In a rigorous benchmark encompassing 17 diverse diagnostic tasks with 48,340 paired OCT images from 8,191 patients, the model demonstrated state-of-the-art performance, achieving an Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 96.9% for Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD) and 97.2% for Diabetic Macular Edema (DME), consistently surpassing existing single-modal and multimodal foundation models. Crucially, OphMAE exhibits robust engineering adaptability: it maintains high diagnostic accuracy, such as 93.7\% AUC for AMD, even when restricted to single-modality 2D inputs, and demonstrates exceptional data efficiency by retaining 95.7% AUC with as few as 500 labeled samples. This work establishes a scalable and adaptable framework for ophthalmic AI, ensuring robust performance across different tasks.

papers.title: PubMed-Ophtha: An open resource for training ophthalmology vision-language models on scientific literature
papers.summary: PubMed-Ophtha: An open resource for training ophthalmology vision-language models on scientific literature attacks the bottleneck that ophthalmology VLMs need large, precise image-text supervision, yet most available corpora are small report sets, template-captions, or flat figure packages that ignore how scientific figures are actually composed. It pivots from treating a paper figure as one compressed JPEG-plus-caption pair toward treating it as a hierarchical document object with panels, panel identifiers, individual images, modality labels, and mark-status supervision. The mechanical shift is a four-stage extraction pipeline that pulls figures directly from full-resolution PubMed Central PDFs, matches them back to article metadata, decomposes them with RetinaNet-based panel and image detectors, classifies each image as CFP/OCT/retinal/other plus marked vs unmarked, and then uses a two-step LLM caption procedure to first extract panel IDs and then assign panel-level subcaptions before geometric/OCR/LLM panel assembly. Unlike nearby biomedical VLM datasets that rely on PMC’s compressed images or weak synthetic captions, this resource preserves figure structure and clinical attention cues like arrows and bounding boxes. Across 15,842 open-access articles it yields 102,023 panel-caption pairs, with high extraction fidelity (median IoU 0.997), strong panel/image detection, and caption splitting reaching BLEU 0.913 against human annotations. The main limitation is that panel identifier detection remains the weakest stage, especially for complex layouts. The paper points toward structure-aware, literature-grounded multimodal training where the unit of supervision is not a whole figure but a semantically aligned panel record.
papers.abstract: Vision-language models hold considerable promise for ophthalmology, but their development depends on large-scale, high-quality image-text datasets that remain scarce. We present PubMed-Ophtha, a hierarchical dataset of 102,023 ophthalmological image-caption pairs extracted from 15,842 open-access articles in PubMed Central. Unlike existing datasets, figures are extracted directly from article PDFs at full resolution and decomposed into their constituent panels, panel identifiers, and individual images. Each image is annotated with its imaging modality -- color fundus photography, optical coherence tomography, retinal imaging, or other -- and a mark status indicating the presence of annotation marks such as arrows. Figure captions are split into panel-level subcaptions using a two-step LLM approach, achieving a mean average sentence BLEU score of 0.913 on human-annotated data. Panel and image detection models reach a mAP@0.50 of 0.909 and 0.892, respectively, and figure extraction achieves a median IoU of 0.997. To support reproducibility, we additionally release the human-annotated ground-truth data, all trained models, and the full dataset generation pipeline.

papers.title: Perceptual Flow Network for Visually Grounded Reasoning
papers.summary: Perceptual Flow Network for Visually Grounded Reasoning attacks the failure mode where LVLMs answer from language priors because standard MLE and even expert-box RLVR do not constrain the latent visual trajectory well enough, so hallucination persists while geometry-heavy supervision stays overly precise and semantically narrow. The paper explicitly challenges the assumption that the best visual prior is the most accurate detector output, showing that expert annotations can be worse for reasoning than looser, instance-specific evidence. It pivots from rigid imitation of static RoIs toward learning a self-parameterized posterior over perceptual behaviors: PFlowNet first generates a structured perceptual flow, then conditions answer generation on that flow, rather than coupling localization and reasoning in one brittle step. The mechanical shift is a two-stage latent-variable loop with a planning state plus perceptual states, encoded as analyze/localize tokens, trained by variational reinforcement learning with Sub-Trajectory Balance, a multi-dimensional reward that mixes contrastive evidence quality p+(ci|Iri) versus p−(ci|I\Iri), answer efficacy, and vicinal geometric shaping around expert priors via a Chamfer-IoU energy. This is unusual relative to nearby grounded-RL work because it rewards reasoning utility, not just geometric overlap, and uses verifier-based flow synthesis and difficulty-aware splitting for cold start and RFT. Initialized from Qwen3-VL-8B, it sets new SOTA on V* Bench (90.6%) and MME-RealWorld-lite (67.0%), but still depends on fixed flow structure and tuned ε, λ, suggesting a future where visual reasoning becomes more adaptive, exploratory, and latent-trajectory driven.
papers.abstract: Despite the success of Large-Vision Language Models (LVLMs), general optimization objectives (e.g., standard MLE) fail to constrain visual trajectories, leading to language bias and hallucination. To mitigate this, current methods introduce geometric priors from visual experts as additional supervision. However, we observe that such supervision is typically suboptimal: it is biased toward geometric precision and offers limited reasoning utility. To bridge this gap, we propose Perceptual Flow Network (PFlowNet), which eschews rigid alignment with the expert priors and achieves interpretable yet more effective visual reasoning. Specifically, PFlowNet decouples perception from reasoning to establish a self-conditioned generation process. Based on this, it integrates multi-dimensional rewards with vicinal geometric shaping via variational reinforcement learning, thereby facilitating reasoning-oriented perceptual behaviors while preserving visual reliability. PFlowNet delivers a provable performance guarantee and competitive empirical results, particularly setting new SOTA records on V* Bench (90.6%) and MME-RealWorld-lite (67.0%).

papers.title: SIAM: Head and Brain MRI Segmentation from Few High-Quality Templates via Synthetic Training
papers.summary: SIAM: Head and Brain MRI Segmentation from Few High-Quality Templates via Synthetic Training attacks the bottleneck that contrast-agnostic brain MRI segmentation has become dependent on hundreds of automatically labeled “silver-standard” templates, which bake in FreeSurfer-like biases, block new anatomical classes, and still fail when contrast or resolution shifts. It pivots from scaling label quantity toward annotation fidelity plus explicit control of variability, arguing that six expertly curated whole-head templates are enough if synthetic generation can randomize both intensity and shape. The mechanical shift is unusually concrete: templates are upsampled to 0.25 mm with one-hot sinc interpolation and smoothing, then subjected to high-resolution erosion/dilation and affine/elastic deformations to model cortical-thickness and deep-nuclei variability; label-to-image rendering uses partial-volume maps and randomized Gaussian-mixture intensities, bias field, motion, and noise, and 1000 offline synthetic pairs train a 3D nnU-Net ensemble. Unlike nearby synthetic segmentation work, SIAM does whole-head, 16-class labeling, explicitly adding CSF, vessels, dura mater, skull, skin, and other extra-cerebral tissues so inference becomes preprocessing-free and does not rely on skull stripping. Across eight heterogeneous datasets (N=301) spanning T1, T2, CT, neonates, pediatrics, high-field MRI, and test-retest HCP, SIAM matches or beats state of the art on high-resolution anatomy, is more stable across contrasts/reacquisitions, and better tracks subtle gray-matter atrophy, though very small structures and some biased references still limit Dice interpretation. This points toward a future of extensible, anatomically faithful synthetic training where model quality comes from controlled priors rather than massive noisy label farms.
papers.abstract: Synthetic training has recently advanced brain MRI segmentation by enabling contrast-agnostic models trained entirely on generated data. However, most existing approaches rely on hundreds of automatically labeled templates, introducing systematic biases and limiting their flexibility to incorporate new anatomical structures. We present the Segment It All Model (SIAM), a 3D whole-head segmentation framework for 16 anatomical structures, trained using only six high-quality, manually annotated templates. SIAM extends domain randomization to both intensity and shape domains: synthetic image generation ensures contrast variability, while high-resolution spatial transformations model anatomical differences in cortical thickness and deep nuclei morphology. Unlike prior synthetic models, SIAM simultaneously segments brain as well as extra-cerebral tissues, including cerebrospinal fluid, vessels, dura mater, skull, and skin, enabling fully automated, preprocessing-free analysis. Evaluation across eight heterogeneous datasets (N=301), that include multiple contrasts (T1-weighted, T2-weighted, CT) and span a wide range of ages, demonstrates that SIAM matches or outperforms state-of-the-art methods for brain structures, in addition to extending automated segmentation to non-brain structures. The model also exhibits superior consistency across contrasts and repeated acquisitions, together with improved sensitivity to subtle gray matter atrophy. We openly release the model and the label templates at https://github.com/romainVala/SIAM.

papers.title: Virtual Scanning for NSCLC Histology: Investigating the Discriminatory Power of Synthetic PET
papers.summary: Virtual Scanning for NSCLC Histology: Investigating the Discriminatory Power of Synthetic PET attacks the bottleneck that CT alone often cannot separate adenocarcinoma from squamous cell carcinoma in NSCLC, while real FDG PET/CT—though clinically valuable—adds cost, radiation, and access constraints. The paper implicitly challenges the assumption that histological subtyping must rely on acquired functional imaging or on anatomy-only classifiers, and pivots from “PET as a diagnostic scan” toward “PET as a learned auxiliary representation.” The mechanical shift is a frozen, pretrained 3D Pix2Pix CT-to-PET generator used in inference-only mode on thoracic volumes: cropped lung CT is converted into pseudo-PET patches with sliding-window synthesis and stitching, then fed alongside CT into the MINT multi-stage intermediate-fusion network, where modality-specific 3D ResNet features are repeatedly combined through 1×1×1 bottlenecks, elementwise multiplication, and residual reinjection. This is unusual because the synthetic modality is not fine-tuned on the target cohort, explicitly avoiding leakage and testing whether generated metabolic cues generalize across a 714-subject multi-center dataset. The synthetic PET is quantitatively plausible yet not interchangeable with real PET, showing a domain shift in sensitivity analyses, but it still lifts subtype discrimination over CT-only, improving AUC from 0.489 to 0.591 and GMean from 0.305 to 0.524 with statistically significant gains, especially by reducing SCC under-detection. The result points toward virtual-scanning pipelines, digital-twin oncology, and future multimodal systems that mine cross-modal proxies when physical functional imaging is unavailable.
papers.abstract: Accurate histological differentiation between adenocarcinoma (ADC) and squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is critical for personalized treatment in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). While [$^{18}$F]FDG PET/CT is a standard tool for the clinical evaluation of lung cancer, its utility is often limited by high costs and radiation exposure. In this paper, we investigate the feasibility of "virtual scanning" as a feature-enhancement strategy by evaluating whether synthetic PET data can provide complementary feature representations to supplement anatomical CT scans in histological subtype classification. We propose a framework that leverages a 3D Pix2Pix Generative Adversarial Network (GAN), pretrained on the FDG-PET/CT Lesions dataset, to synthesize pseudo-PET volumes from anatomical CT scans. These synthetic volumes are integrated with structural CT data within the MINT framework, a multi-stage intermediate fusion architecture. Our experiments, conducted on a multi-center dataset of 714 subjects, demonstrate that the inclusion of synthetic metabolic features significantly improves classification performance over a CT-only baseline. The multimodal approach achieved a statistically significant increase in the Area Under the Curve (AUC) from 0.489 to 0.591 and improved the Geometric Mean (GMean) from 0.305 to 0.524. These results suggest that synthetic PET scans provide discriminatory metabolic cues that enable deep learning models to exploit complementary cross-modal information, offering a potential feature-enhancement strategy for clinical scenarios where physical PET scans are unavailable.

papers.title: Does it Really Count? Assessing Semantic Grounding in Text-Guided Class-Agnostic Counting
papers.summary: Does it Really Count? Assessing Semantic Grounding in Text-Guided Class-Agnostic Counting attacks a subtle but consequential failure mode in open-world class-agnostic counting: models can return plausible counts while misreading the prompt and counting the most salient objects in the scene, even when the queried class is absent or heavily confounded by co-occurring categories. The paper challenges the inherited assumption that standard MAE/RMSE on mostly single-category benchmarks is enough to validate text-guided CAC, and pivots from “how close is the number?” toward “did the model actually ground the word in the image?” The mechanical shift is a benchmark redesign rather than a new counter: PrACo++ adds a negative-label test to probe whether absent classes are rejected, and a distractor test to see whether a requested class is stable in the presence of similar or competing categories; it pairs these with specialized metrics, including NMN, PCCN, and patch-based counting precision/recall/F1. The second key intervention is MUCCA, a 200-image real-world dataset with 11,576 annotated instances across 45 classes and 2–7 categories per image, specifically built to expose multi-class confusion that FSC-147-style single-category scenes hide. Across 10 SOTA methods, the study finds a striking gap between conventional counting scores and semantic trustworthiness, with some models producing negative-class counts rivaling positive ones and error rising with CLIP semantic similarity. This points the field toward semantically grounded architectures, prompt-rejection behavior, and evaluation protocols that treat counting as grounding, not just regression.
papers.abstract: Open-world text-guided class-agnostic counting (CAC) has emerged as a flexible paradigm for counting arbitrary object classes by using natural language prompts. However, current evaluation protocols primarily focus on standard counting errors within single-category images, overlooking a fundamental requirement: the ability to correctly ground the textual prompt in the visual scene. In this paper, we show that several state-of-the-art CAC models often struggle to determine which object class should be counted based on the given prompt, revealing a misalignment between textual semantics and visual object representations. This limitation leads to spurious counting responses and reduced reliability in real-world scenarios. To systematically address these limitations, we propose a new evaluation framework focused on model robustness and trustworthiness. Our contribution is two-fold: (i) we introduce PrACo++ (Prompt-Aware Counting++), a novel test suite featuring two dedicated evaluation protocols -- the negative-label test and the distractor test -- paired with new specialized metrics; and (ii) we present the MUCCA (MUlti-Category Class-Agnostic counting) evaluation dataset, a new collection of real-world images featuring multiple annotated object categories per scene, unlike existing CAC benchmarks that typically include a single category per image. Our extensive experimental evaluation of 10 state-of-the-art methods shows that, despite strong performance under standard counting metrics, current models exhibit significant weaknesses in understanding and grounding object class descriptions. Finally, we provide a quantitative analysis of how semantic similarity between prompts influences these failures. Overall, our results underscore the need for more semantically grounded architectures and offer a reliable framework for future assessment in open-world text-guided CAC methods.

papers.title: Seeing Realism from Simulation: Efficient Video Transfer for Vision-Language-Action Data Augmentation
papers.summary: Seeing Realism from Simulation: Efficient Video Transfer for Vision-Language-Action Data Augmentation attacks the bottleneck that VLA policies trained on simulation often memorize sterile trajectories and collapse under small shifts in lighting, layout, camera pose, or background, while real robot video remains expensive and slow to scale. It challenges the prevailing assumption that better generalization mainly comes from collecting more real-world demonstrations or directly training on raw simulated rollouts; instead, it pivots from data acquisition to simulation-to-real video rewriting, turning cheap simulator traces into realistic training videos that preserve task semantics and action trajectories. The mechanical shift is a four-stage augmentation pipeline: video semantic segmentation and captioning extract structured scene conditions, an LLM rewrites captions to diversify environment attributes, and a depth-conditioned video diffusion model synthesizes realistic frames from the edited text plus geometric control. What distinguishes the method is that it treats appearance diversity, trajectory fidelity, and scalability as one system problem: it adds a three-phase diffusion velocity-caching scheme that reuses video tokens across adjacent timesteps to cut generation time by over 60%, and a trajectory-level coreset selector that ranks samples by pretrained policy loss and Cosmos-Embed1 diversity on a k-NN graph, then suppresses redundant neighbors. Across Robotwin 2.0, LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and an AgileX Piper robot, the method improves RDT-1B by 8% and π0 by 5.1% on LIBERO-Plus, though gains are weaker on nearly in-distribution LIBERO and on viewpoint/geometry shifts. This points toward future embodied-AI pipelines where simulation becomes a controllable data engine for targeted, compute-aware, semantically faithful augmentation rather than a standalone training world.
papers.abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models typically rely on large-scale real-world videos, whereas simulated data, despite being inexpensive and highly parallelizable to collect, often suffers from a substantial visual domain gap and limited environmental diversity, resulting in weak real-world generalization. We present an efficient video augmentation framework that converts simulated VLA videos into realistic training videos while preserving task semantics and action trajectories. Our pipeline extracts structured conditions from simulation via video semantic segmentation and video captioning, rewrites captions to diversify environments, and uses a conditional video transfer model to synthesize realistic videos. To make augmentation practical at scale, we introduce a diffusion feature-reuse mechanism that reuses video tokens across adjacent timesteps to accelerate generation, and a coreset sampling strategy that identifies a compact, non-redundant subset for augmentation under limited computation. Extensive experiments on Robotwin 2.0, LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and a real robotic platform demonstrate consistent improvements. For example, our method improves RDT-1B by 8% on Robotwin 2.0, and boosts $π_0$ by 5.1% on the more challenging LIBERO-Plus benchmark. Code is available at: https://github.com/nanfangxiansheng/Seeing-Realism-from-Simulation.

papers.title: Unified Map Prior Encoder for Mapping and Planning
papers.summary: Unified Map Prior Encoder for Mapping and Planning attacks a practical bottleneck in autonomous driving: online mapping and end-to-end planning are still sensor-centric, while HD/SD vector maps, rasterized SD maps, and satellite imagery are treated as fragile add-ons because their formats differ, their poses drift, and their availability changes at test time. The paper implicitly challenges the old workflow of training a separate prior-injection model for one fixed modality or a fixed pair, and pivots from modality-specific fusion toward a powerset-robust encoder that can consume any subset of four priors without retraining. The mechanical shift is a two-branch UMPE design: vector priors are frame-wise SE(2)-prealigned, sinusoidally encoded, tokenized with confidence scores, then fused by dual cross-attention plus confidence-biased, presence-normalized channel gating; raster priors are passed through a shared FiLM-conditioned ResNet-18, micro-aligned in SE(2), and injected by zero-initialized residual fusion so the model starts from a do-no-harm BEV baseline and only adds useful evidence. Unusually, it orders fusion as vector first, raster second, encoding “geometry first, appearance second,” and uses SourceDropout to train missing-source robustness. On nuScenes and Argoverse2, UMPE lifts MapTRv2 and MapQR mapping mAP, and on VAD planning it cuts average trajectory error from 0.72 to 0.42 m and collision rate from 0.22% to 0.12%. This points toward future driving systems that treat heterogeneous priors as compositional, alignable signals rather than fixed inputs.
papers.abstract: Online mapping and end-to-end (E2E) planning in autonomous driving remain largely sensor-centric, leaving rich map priors, including HD/SD vector maps, rasterized SD maps, and satellite imagery, underused because of heterogeneity, pose drift, and inconsistent availability at test time. We present UMPE, a Unified Map Prior Encoder that can ingest any subset of four priors and fuse them with BEV features for both mapping and planning. UMPE has two branches. The vector encoder pre-aligns HD/SD polylines with a frame-wise SE(2) correction, encodes points via multi-frequency sinusoidal features, and produces polyline tokens with confidence scores. BEV queries then apply cross-attention with confidence bias, followed by normalized channel-wise gating to avoid length imbalance and softly down-weight uncertain sources. The raster encoder shares a ResNet-18 backbone conditioned by FiLM with scaling and shift at every stage, performs SE(2) micro-alignment, and injects priors through zero-initialized residual fusion, so the network starts from a do-no-harm baseline and learns to add only useful prior evidence. A vector-then-raster fusion order reflects the inductive bias of geometry first, appearance second. On nuScenes mapping, UMPE lifts MapTRv2 from 61.5 to 67.4 mAP (+5.9) and MapQR from 66.4 to 71.7 mAP (+5.3). On Argoverse2, UMPE adds +4.1 mAP over strong baselines. UMPE is compositional: when trained with all priors, it outperforms single-prior models even when only one prior is available at test time, demonstrating powerset robustness. For E2E planning with the VAD backbone on nuScenes, UMPE reduces trajectory error from 0.72 to 0.42 m L2 on average (-0.30 m) and collision rate from 0.22% to 0.12% (-0.10%), surpassing recent prior-injection methods. These results show that a unified, alignment-aware treatment of heterogeneous map priors yields better mapping and better planning.

papers.title: FoR-Net: Learning to Focus on Hard Regions for Efficient Semantic Segmentation
papers.summary: FoR-Net: Learning to Focus on Hard Regions for Efficient Semantic Segmentation attacks a specific bottleneck in dense prediction: lightweight semantic segmentation models often lose thin structures and object boundaries because they spread compute uniformly or depend on heavy global modeling to recover context. The paper challenges the assumption that better segmentation must come from denser multi-scale pyramids or transformer-style global attention, and pivots from “process everything” toward “explicitly select the hardest spatial regions and spend computation there.” The mechanical shift is a selector module that predicts a learned importance map over high-level features, then a hard Top-K mask turns that map into sparse activation, so only the most informative locations are further refined; this is paired with an auxiliary boundary supervision loss that teaches the selector to upweight boundary-like difficult regions. Unlike nearby soft-attention or multi-branch efficient segmentation work, FoR-Net uses explicit hard selection rather than diffuse weighting, and augments it with simple multi-scale convolutional branches (1×1, 3×3, 5×5, 7×7) instead of a heavy pyramid or transformer encoder. Evaluated on Cityscapes under resource-constrained settings with a ResNet-101 backbone, output stride 8, Group Normalization, and standard CE+Dice training, it reaches 80.49 mIoU and shows visibly better consistency on poles, signs, and object edges. The paper points toward region-aware inductive biases, sparse spatial routing, and adaptive hard-region selection as a direction for efficient segmentation research.
papers.abstract: We present FoR-Net, a lightweight architecture for semantic segmentation that focuses on identifying and enhancing hard regions. Instead of relying on heavy global modeling, FoR-Net adopts an efficient strategy that selectively emphasizes informative regions through a learned importance map and a Top-K activation mechanism. Specifically, a selector module predicts region-wise importance, enabling the model to focus on challenging areas such as thin structures and object boundaries. Multi-scale reasoning is achieved using convolutional branches with different receptive fields, allowing diverse spatial context aggregation. We evaluate FoR-Net on the Cityscapes benchmark under limited computational resources. Despite its lightweight design and standard training configuration, FoR-Net achieves competitive performance and demonstrates improved consistency in challenging regions. These results suggest that region-focused reasoning provides a simple yet effective inductive bias for efficient semantic segmentation.

papers.title: TOC-SR: Task-Optimal Compact diffusion for Image Super Resolution
papers.summary: TOC-SR: Task-Optimal Compact diffusion for Image Super Resolution attacks the deployment bottleneck in diffusion SR: high-quality restoration from LR images has been tied to large latent diffusion backbones and tens of denoising steps, making existing methods too slow and heavy for practical use. The paper explicitly challenges the assumption that one should directly train or prune a compact SR model end-to-end; instead it pivots from “compact restoration network first” toward “discover a compact generative prior first, then specialize it for SR.” The mechanical shift is a three-stage pipeline: expand Stable Diffusion 1.5’s latent space from 4 to 16 channels using latent-space distillation from a FLUX VAE teacher to reduce the VAE bottleneck for high-frequency detail; build a library of parameter-reduced surrogate U-Net blocks calibrated by feature-wise generative distillation; and search the architecture with epsilon-constrained Bayesian optimization, minimizing parameter count while enforcing a ceiling on noise-prediction deviation from the teacher. Unlike nearby compression work, candidate backbones are evaluated as plug-and-play stage substitutions without full retraining, then the chosen compact model is fine-tuned and distilled into a single-step SR generator conditioned on an upsampled LR latent. Trained on LS-DIR, FFHQ, and DIV2K with Real-ESRGAN degradations and evaluated on DIV2K-Val, RealSR, and DRealSR, TOC-SR reports 130M parameters, 273 GMACs, and 1-step inference while reaching 24.26 dB PSNR and 0.242 LPIPS on DIV2K-Val, suggesting future SR systems will be built by search-guided latent prior compression plus step distillation rather than scaling denoisers.
papers.abstract: Diffusion models have recently demonstrated strong performance for image restoration tasks, including super-resolution. However, their large model size and iterative sampling procedures make them computationally expensive for practical deployment. In this work, we present TOC-SR, a framework for building efficient one-step super-resolution models by first discovering a compact diffusion backbone. Starting from a sixteen-channel latent diffusion model, we construct parameter-efficient surrogate blocks using feature-wise generative distillation and perform architecture discovery using epsilon-constrained Bayesian Optimization to minimize model complexity while preserving generative fidelity. The resulting compact diffusion backbone achieves a 6.6x reduction in parameters and a 2.8x reduction in GMACs compared to the expanded diffusion model. We then adapt this backbone for super-resolution and distill the diffusion process into a single-step generator. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed approach enables efficient super-resolution while maintaining strong reconstruction quality.

papers.title: Linearizing Vision Transformer with Test-Time Training
papers.summary: Linearizing Vision Transformer with Test-Time Training attacks the practical bottleneck that linear-complexity vision models are usually only attractive if trained from scratch, while converting a strong pretrained Softmax Transformer into a linear model has been blocked by a representational mismatch that makes weight transfer fail or destabilize. The paper pivots away from the common assumption that linear attention must approximate Softmax with kernel tricks, low-rank activations, or partial grafting, and turns instead toward a structurally aligned substitute: Test-Time Training (TTT), whose two-layer dynamic internal model is close enough to Softmax attention to inherit pretrained Q/K/V/O and backbone weights directly. The mechanical shift is not just replacing attention with an O(N) module, but pairing full weight inheritance with two alignment levers: key instance normalization to remove the global key bias that Softmax had hidden via shift-invariance, and a lightweight locality enhancement via depthwise convolutions on queries and keys to restore the local bias Softmax naturally learns. This is unusual relative to nearby linearization work because it treats conversion as architectural compatibility plus representation surgery, rather than distillation or operator activation. Experiments span ImageNet-1K classification, DiT-XL/2 class-conditional generation, and Stable Diffusion 3.5 text-to-image generation. With only 8 epochs on DiT and about 1 hour of fine-tuning on SD3.5, the method recovers near-Softmax quality while cutting inference cost, yielding 1.32× and 1.47× speedups at 1K and 2K resolution. This points toward a future of pretrained-model rewiring, where efficiency comes from conversion-ready architectures rather than retraining.
papers.abstract: While linear-complexity attention mechanisms offer a promising alternative to Softmax attention for overcoming the quadratic bottleneck, training such models from scratch remains prohibitively expensive. Inheriting weights from pretrained Transformers provides an appealing shortcut, yet the fundamental representational gap between Softmax and linear attention prevents effective weight transfer. In this work, we address this conversion challenge from two perspectives: architectural alignment and representational alignment. We identify Test-Time Training (TTT) as a linear-complexity architecture whose two-layer dynamic formulation is structurally aligned with Softmax attention, enabling direct inheritance of pretrained attention weights. To further align representational properties, including key shift-invariance and locality, we introduce key instance normalization and a lightweight locality enhancement module. We validate our approach by linearizing Stable Diffusion 3.5 and introduce SD3.5-T$^5$ (Transformer To Test Time Training). With only 1 hour of fine-tuning on 4$\times$H20 GPUs, SD3.5-T$^5$ achieves comparable text-to-image quality to the fine-tuned Softmax model, while accelerating inference by 1.32$\times$ and 1.47$\times$ at 1K and 2K resolutions.

papers.title: HumanSplatHMR: Closing the Loop Between Human Mesh Recovery and Gaussian Splatting Avatar
papers.summary: HumanSplatHMR: Closing the Loop Between Human Mesh Recovery and Gaussian Splatting Avatar attacks the persistent mismatch between accurate 3D human geometry and photorealistic avatar rendering from monocular video: HMR backbones like ViT-based estimators often overfit 2D views and drift in global pose, while NeRF/3DGS avatar pipelines usually freeze pose and appearance into separate stages, so rendering quality can improve only by letting the mesh stop being geometrically correct. The paper pivots from “pose as a one-way deformation prior” toward a closed-loop system where pose estimation is continuously corrected by the rendering loss itself. Mechanically, HumanSplatHMR starts from off-the-shelf SMPL estimates from HMR2.0, initializes Gaussians on the mesh, and then jointly optimizes SMPL pose/global position and Gaussian parameters with photometric, segmentation, and metric depth supervision. Its differentiable renderer sends image-level errors back into the pose variables, while the Cloth-Aware Mesh-Embedded Loss (CAMEL) replaces hard mesh attachment or unconstrained learnable skinning with a loose cloth-margin coupling: point-to-mesh band loss, mesh coverage, Gaussian flatness, and surface-alignment terms keep splats near the body yet flexible for clothing and nonrigid detail. Unlike nearby human Gaussian splatting work that trades SMPL accuracy for view synthesis, this loop closes the geometry–appearance gap. On H36M, 3DPW, and NeuMan, it consistently improves pose errors and novel-view PSNR/SSIM over decoupled baselines, especially when initial SMPL estimates are noisy, pointing toward future avatar systems that refine human shape, motion, and appearance jointly in-the-wild.
papers.abstract: Accurately recovering human pose and appearance from video is an essential component of scene reconstruction, with applications to motion capture, motion prediction, virtual reality, and digital twinning. Despite significant interest in building realistic human avatars from video, this paper demonstrates that existing methods do not accurately recover the 3D geometry of humans. ViT-based approaches are not consistently reliable and can overfit to 2D views, while NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting-based avatars treat pose and appearance separately, limiting rendering generalization to new poses. To resolve these shortcomings, this paper proposes HumanSplatHMR, a joint optimization framework that refines 3D human poses while simultaneously learning a high-fidelity avatar for novel-view and novel-pose synthesis. Our key insight is to close the loop between geometric pose estimation and differentiable rendering. Unlike prior human avatar methods that rely on accurate human pose obtained through motion capture systems or offline refinement, which are impractical in in-the-wild scenarios, our approach uses only human mesh estimates from a state-of-the-art human pose estimator to better reflect real-world conditions. Therefore, instead of using the human pose only as a deformation prior, HumanSplatHMR backpropagates photometric, segmentation, and depth losses through a differentiable renderer to the pose parameters and global position. This coupling refines the global 3D pose over time, improving accuracy and alignment while producing better renderings from novel views. Experiments show consistent improvements over pose recovery baselines that omit image-level refinement and avatar baselines that decouple pose estimation from avatar reconstruction.

papers.title: Edge-Efficient Image Restoration: Transformer Distillation into State-Space Models
papers.summary: Edge-Efficient Image Restoration: Transformer Distillation into State-Space Models attacks the mismatch between restoration accuracy and real mobile latency: transformer-style restorers such as Restormer have strong global context modeling, but their attention kernels become the bottleneck on edge CPUs for high-resolution inputs, and FLOPs or memory no longer predict deployment cost. The paper pivots from hand-designing ever more efficient attention variants, and from monolithic attention-SSM fusion blocks, toward a modular block-substitution regime where transformer behavior is first distilled into lightweight MambaIR state-space surrogates and then searched as a discrete architecture problem. The core mechanical shift is feature-wise knowledge distillation at the block level: each SSM block is trained to mimic a matched transformer block output, turning hybridization into plug-and-play replacement rather than co-training a new operator. Efficient Network Search (ENS) then runs multi-objective Bayesian optimization with EHVI over an 8-D search space of full, partial, and compact surrogate choices, penalizing transformer usage as a proxy for latency and selecting knee-point Pareto candidates without repeated hardware profiling. Evaluated on GoPro, RealBlur, HIDE, Rain13K, SIDD, and related benchmarks, the ENS-discovered hybrids keep competitive PSNR/SSIM while cutting Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU latency dramatically, including 3.4x faster deblurring, 1.74x faster deraining, and 1.17x faster denoising versus Restormer. The result points toward architecture-agnostic, hardware-aware restoration pipelines built from distilled expressive-efficient block pairs rather than fixed backbones.
papers.abstract: We propose a modular framework for hybrid image restoration that integrates transformer and state-space model (SSM) blocks with a focus on improving runtime efficiency on edge hardware. While transformers provide strong global modeling through self-attention, their attention kernels incur substantial latency on mobile devices, especially for high-resolution inputs. In contrast, SSMs such as Mamba offer lineartime sequence modeling with lower runtime overhead but may underperform on fine grained restoration tasks. To balance accuracy and efficiency, we train lightweight SSM blocks as feature-distilled surrogates of transformer blocks and use them to construct hybrid U-Net-style architectures. To automatically discover effective block combinations, we introduce Efficient Network Search (ENS), a multi-objective search strategy that selects task-specific hybrid configurations from pre-aligned components. ENS optimizes restoration quality while penalizing transformer usage, serving as a lightweight proxy for latency and enabling architecture discovery without repeated hardware profiling. On a Snapdragon 8 Elite CPU, the Restormer baseline requires 10119.52 ms for inference. In contrast, ENS-discovered hybrids significantly reduce runtime: ENS-Deblurring runs in 2973 ms (3.4x faster), ENS-Deraining in 5816 ms (1.74x faster), and ENS-Denoising in 8666 ms (1.17x faster), while maintaining competitive restoration quality.

papers.title: IConFace: Identity-Structure Asymmetric Conditioning for Unified Reference-Aware Face Restoration
papers.summary: IConFace: Identity-Structure Asymmetric Conditioning for Unified Reference-Aware Face Restoration tackles the bottleneck in blind face restoration where severe corruption erases identity-critical evidence, while same-identity references can help but also tempt models to overcopy mismatched pose, expression, illumination, age, or makeup. It challenges the old workflow that treats degraded input and reference images symmetrically, or that requires separate reference-aware and no-reference systems, and pivots from dense feature transfer toward explicit role separation: references become a global identity controller, while the degraded image remains the spatial structure anchor. The mechanical shift is a unified single-checkpoint FLUX.2-klein-base-4B model with hybrid token concatenation plus two lightweight side paths: references are compressed through norm-weighted AdaFace aggregation into one image-only modulation anchor, and degraded tokens are reinforced by a low-rank residual plus block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory, splitting coarse layout and local facial detail. When references are absent, the same model falls back to degraded-image self-conditioning rather than a different architecture. This is unusual relative to nearby reference-based face restoration because it uses explicit identity-quality weighting, asymmetric conditioning, and optional-reference training in one model instead of relying on alignment, warping, or memory transfer alone. Trained on FFHQ-Ref with mixed blind degradations and evaluated on CelebA-Test-Ref, FFHQ-Ref Moderate/Severe, CelebHQRef100, and no-reference sets, it improves Ref-AdaFace and perceptual quality, especially in severe cases, while remaining strong in empty-reference mode; the main tradeoff is weaker PSNR/SSIM, pointing toward future restoration systems that treat auxiliary evidence as controllable identity priors rather than direct visual substitutes.
papers.abstract: Blind face restoration is highly ill-posed under severe degradation, where identity-critical details may be missing from the degraded input. Same-identity references reduce this ambiguity, but mismatched pose, expression, illumination, age, makeup, or local facial states can lead to overuse of reference appearance. We propose \textbf{IConFace}, a unified reference-aware and no-reference framework with identity--structure asymmetric conditioning. References are distilled into a norm-weighted global AdaFace identity anchor for image-only modulation, while the degraded image is reinforced as the spatial structure anchor through low-rank residuals and block-wise degraded cross-attention with two-route memory. The resulting single checkpoint exploits references when available and falls back to no-reference restoration when absent, improving identity consistency, fine-detail recovery, and degraded-only restoration quality in a unified model.

papers.title: VideoNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for Domain-Specific Action Recognition
papers.summary: VideoNet: A Large-Scale Dataset for Domain-Specific Action Recognition attacks the bottleneck that modern VLMs are effectively no longer judged on the very skill video once centered on: recognizing subtle, domain-specific actions whose differences hinge on motion order, tool use, or tiny pose variants rather than coarse scene cues. The paper challenges the old benchmark habit of relying on generic action labels or narrow sports/egocentric datasets, and pivots from “can prompting or bigger models recover this at test time?” toward “the field needs training data that actually teaches domain-specific action semantics.” The mechanical shift is twofold: first, VideoNet builds a benchmark of 1,000 actions across 37 domains with expert-verified labels and hard negatives that are filtered by an LLM plus a reasoning model to avoid ambiguous co-occurrence; second, it constructs the first large-scale training set for this problem by crawling web video, using Gemini 2.5 Flash to localize clips, WhisperX transcripts and titles to assign labels, and then fine-tuning Molmo2-4B on 160k–500k video QA pairs. The evaluation design is unusual because it explicitly separates multiple-choice recognition from few-shot binary in-context learning, exposing that VLMs exploit in-context videos poorly: open 8B models sit around 45% multiple-choice and only 59.2% binary, while few-shot gains are tiny and inconsistent compared with humans’ +13.6%. Fine-tuning on VideoNet beats all open-weight 8B models, suggesting a direction where future video research emphasizes domain-specific supervision, harder negatives, and post-training recipes over pure test-time scaling.
papers.abstract: Videos are unique in their ability to capture actions which transcend multiple frames. Accordingly, for many years action recognition was the quintessential task for video understanding. Unfortunately, due to a lack of sufficiently diverse and challenging data, modern vision-language models (VLMs) are no longer evaluated on their action recognition capabilities. To revitalize action recognition in the era of VLMs, we advocate for a returned focus on domain-specific actions. To this end, we introduce VideoNet, a domain-specific action recognition benchmark covering 1,000 distinct actions from 37 domains. We begin with a multiple-choice evaluation setting, where the difference between closed and open models is stark: Gemini 3.1 Pro attains 69.9% accuracy while Qwen3-VL-8B gets a mere 45.0%. To understand why VLMs struggle on VideoNet, we relax the questions into a binary setting, where random chance is 50%. Still, Qwen achieves only 59.2% accuracy. Further relaxing the evaluation setup, we provide $k\in\{1,2,3\}$ in-context examples of the action. Some models excel in the few-shot setting, while others falter; Qwen improves $+7.0\%$, while Gemini declines $-4.8\%$. Notably, these gains fall short of the $+13.6\%$ improvement in non-expert humans when given few-shot examples. Finding that VLMs struggle to fully exploit in-context examples, we shift from test-time improvements to the training side. We collect the first large-scale training dataset for domain-specific actions, totaling nearly 500k video question-answer pairs. Fine-tuning a Molmo2-4B model on our data, we surpass all open-weight 8B models on the VideoNet benchmark.

papers.title: Active Sampling for Ultra-Low-Bit-Rate Video Compression via Conditional Controlled Diffusion
papers.summary: Active Sampling for Ultra-Low-Bit-Rate Video Compression via Conditional Controlled Diffusion attacks the failure mode of learned video codecs at roughly ≤0.05 bpp: once the bitrate collapses, residual-coded and fixed-GOP pipelines over-smooth, and even diffusion codecs struggle because the decoder is generative while the conditioning budget is too small to be informative. The paper pivots from transmitting dense motion or repeatedly forecasting at the encoder toward active sampling of just enough side information for a conditional diffusion decoder to hallucinate the rest. The mechanical shift is ActDiff-VC: videos are split into variable-length segments by content-adaptive keyframe selection, then temporal dynamics are summarized by a budget-aware sparse set of long-range tracked point trajectories, extracted with AllTracker and entropy-coded as deltas; at decode time, a pre-trained DaS-style video diffusion model is controlled by the boundary keyframes plus sparse motion latents, with bidirectional boundary conditioning to stabilize segment transitions. Unlike nearby work such as fixed-GOP perceptual codecs or encoder-heavy diffusion baselines, it treats motion as a sparse control signal rather than a prediction residue, and it uses sketch-weighted trajectory selection to prioritize structurally informative regions. On UVG and MCL-JCV, it reports up to 64.6% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE, with KID/FID gains over strong learned codecs and better perceptual rate–distortion trade-offs than diffusion baselines. The main limitation is diffusion decoding latency, pointing toward faster samplers, distilled/consistency models, and more unified motion-estimation-plus-compression systems for ultra-low-rate perceptual video delivery.
papers.abstract: Diffusion models provide a powerful generative prior for perceptual reconstruction at ultra-low bitrates, but effective video compression requires controlling the generative process using highly compact conditioning signals. In this work, we present ActDiff-VC, a diffusion-based video compression framework for the ultra-low-bitrate regime. Our method partitions videos into variable-length segments, transmits keyframes only when needed, and summarizes temporal dynamics using a compact set of tracked point trajectories. Conditioned on these sparse signals, a conditional diffusion decoder synthesizes the remaining frames, enabling perceptually realistic reconstruction under severe rate constraints. To support this design, we introduce two mechanisms: content-adaptive keyframe selection and budget-aware sparse trajectory selection, which together enable compact yet effective conditioning for generative reconstruction. Experiments on the UVG and MCL-JCV benchmarks show that ActDiff-VC achieves up to 64.6\% bitrate reduction at matched NIQE, improves KID by up to 64.6\% and FID by up to 37.7\% at comparable bitrates against strong learned codecs, and delivers favorable perceptual rate--distortion trade-offs relative to learned and diffusion-based baselines in the ultra-low-bitrate regime.

papers.title: Pixel Perfect: Relational Image Quality Assessment with Spatially-Aware Distortions
papers.summary: Pixel Perfect: Relational Image Quality Assessment with Spatially-Aware Distortions attacks the bottleneck in full-reference IQA where MOS collection is expensive, noisy, and collapses heterogeneous artifacts into a single opaque scalar that cannot say where blur, noise, haze, or color errors occur. The paper pivots from absolute quality prediction toward relational, directional assessment: instead of asking “how good is this image?”, it asks how a test image differs from a reference, and whether those differences make it better or worse in specific regions. The mechanical shift is a two-stage system built on self-supervision: a semantic-aware synthetic distortion engine uses pretrained segmentation masks plus random Perlin/rectangular masks to locally apply a bank of six distortion operators and generate dense ground-truth distortion maps without any human labels; then a Mask2Former-style distortion predictor, driven by Swin features and distortion-specific queries, is trained with a weighted anti-symmetric loss so F(Itest, Iref) ≈ 1 − F(Iref, Itest), making the output directional rather than distance-like. A second Swin-based scoring network consumes the RGB image concatenated with the predicted distortion maps and learns a relational quality score from ordinally ranked image sets using hinge ranking plus InfoNCE, not MOS. Evaluated on 270 handheld mobile captures processed through multi-frame ISP networks of varying capacity, it produces monotonic rankings and interpretable spatial maps that expose local failure modes. The limitation is a finite synthetic distortion bank and same-scene ordinal tiers; the future points toward learned distortion operators, cross-scene relational scoring, and using dense quality maps as optimization or reward signals.
papers.abstract: Traditional image quality assessment (IQA) methods rely on mean opinion scores (MOS), which are resource-intensive to collect and fail to provide interpretable, localized feedback on specific image distortions. We overcome these limitations by shifting from absolute quality prediction to a relational and directional assessment. Our approach utilizes a self-supervised synthetic distortion engine to generate training data, eliminating the need for manual annotation. A distortion prediction network is trained with an anti-symmetric objective to produce spatially-aware, disentangled maps that identify the type, intensity, and direction of distortions relative to a reference image. Subsequently, a scoring network is trained via contrastive learning on ordinally ranked image sets to predict a relational quality score. Our method provides a more granular and interpretable approach to IQA for the targeted optimization of image processing algorithms without requiring any human-labeled quality scores.

papers.title: Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network for Rural Thematic Road Extraction
papers.summary: Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network for Rural Thematic Road Extraction targets a niche but consequential failure mode in precision agriculture map inference: turning sparse GNSS trajectory images from agricultural machinery into connected rural road topologies without losing thin paths or hallucinating field tracks. The paper challenges the common assumption in road extraction that a single encoder-decoder stream can downsample, fuse, and recover all useful signals; in this setting, downsampling behaves like an irreversible low-pass filter that erases high-frequency road traces, while dense field-operation noise creates fragmented or redundant networks. It pivots from mixed-frequency feature encoding toward explicit frequency separation and cross-frequency control. The mechanical shift is a “decompose-interact-reconstruct” pipeline: a Laplacian Multi-scale Separator first splits inputs into low-frequency semantic context and multi-scale high-frequency detail, then a Cross-Frequency Interaction Block routes structural detail through a High-Frequency Block with cascaded depthwise dilations while a Spatial Transformer models long-range connectivity in the semantic branch, and a Frequency Gated Modulation module uses low-frequency priors to gate and suppress noisy high-frequency responses. A Progressive Reconstruction Decoder then iteratively fuses scales to preserve topology. Unusual here is the explicit Laplacian pyramid logic for rural trajectory images, rather than generic attention or post-processing. On a real Henan Province agricultural trajectories dataset, LFINet reaches 92.54% F1 and 86.12% IoU, beating prior methods while remaining practical for field deployment; the limitation is that broader terrain generalization and graph-based vectorization remain open, pointing toward frequency-aware, topology-preserving mapping systems for sparse mobility data.
papers.abstract: Rural thematic road network construction aims to extract topological road structures from movement trajectory images of agricultural machinery. However, this task faces challenges where downsampling methods commonly used in existing studies tend to blur the sparse high-frequency road structures, and the heavy noise from dense field operations often leads to fragmented or redundant topologies in the extracted networks. To address these challenges, we propose LFINet, a Laplacian Frequency Interaction Network. The network begins with a Laplacian Multi-scale Separator (LMS) to decouple the image into low-frequency semantic contexts and high-frequency structural details. These components are then processed by the Cross-Frequency Interaction Block (CFIB) through a dual-pathway architecture in which a High-Frequency Block (HFB) refines local structures while a Spatial Transformer (ST) captures global semantics. Subsequently, a Frequency Gated Modulation (FGM) mechanism integrates the features from pathways by leveraging semantic contexts to calibrate the structural details. Finally, a Progressive Reconstruction Decoder iteratively fuses multi-scale features to ensure topological consistency. Experiments conducted on a real-world agricultural trajectories dataset from Henan Province, China, show that LFINet establishes a new state-of-the-art. Specifically, it achieves an F1-score of 92.54% and an IoU of 86.12%, surpassing the second-ranked method by 0.64% and 1.1%, respectively. This confirms its capability to effectively construct topological road networks from noisy and sparse field data.

papers.title: AlbumFill: Album-Guided Reasoning and Retrieval for Personalized Image Completion
papers.summary: AlbumFill: Album-Guided Reasoning and Retrieval for Personalized Image Completion attacks the failure mode where generic inpainting can reconstruct pixels but not a person’s identity, while prior reference-based completion quietly assumes the right exemplar is already handed to the model. The paper pivots from “fill the hole from local context or a provided reference” toward “first infer what is missing, then search the user’s own album for an identity-consistent witness.” Its mechanical shift is a training-free three-stage loop: a vision-language model performs masked visual reasoning on the occluded photo, generating semantic cues about the hidden region; those cues are fused with the visible crop into a composed retrieval query; and the top album match is fed to a reference-based diffusion completion model. Unlike nearby work in prompt-driven inpainting or direct multimodal editing, AlbumFill treats retrieval as the bottleneck, not generation, and makes the reference discoverability problem explicit. To support this setting, the authors build a 54K human-centric benchmark from CUFED by filtering people with YOLOv8 and clustering identities with InsightFace into 1,727 dominant-identity albums. Experiments with Qwen3-VL, LinCIR, and UniReal show that reasoning-derived prompts consistently improve retrieval, especially under large masks, and that wrong-identity references sharply degrade completion quality. The result argues that personalized restoration is moving toward reasoning-before-retrieve pipelines and album-level memory systems, where identity preservation depends on search over private visual collections rather than hallucination alone.
papers.abstract: Personalized image completion aims to restore occluded regions in personal photos while preserving identity and appearance. Existing methods either rely on generic inpainting models that often fail to maintain identity consistency, or assume that suitable reference images are explicitly provided. In practice, suitable references are often not explicitly provided, requiring the system to search for identity-consistent images within personal photo collections. We present AlbumFill, a training-free framework that retrieves identity-consistent references from personal albums for personalized completion. Given an occluded image and a personal album, a vision-language model infers missing semantic cues to guide composed image retrieval, and the retrieved references are used by reference-based completion models. To facilitate this task, we introduce a dataset containing 54K human-centric samples with associated album images. Experiments across multiple baselines demonstrate the difficulty of personalized completion and highlight the importance of identity-consistent reference retrieval. Project Page: https://liagm.github.io/AlbumFill/

papers.title: Learning Koopman operators for coupled systems via information on governing equations of subsystems
papers.summary: Learning Koopman operators for coupled systems via information on governing equations of subsystems attacks the bottleneck that EDMD becomes unstable and data-hungry when a nonlinear coupled system has high dimensionality, multiple interacting subsystems, and only limited trajectories. The paper challenges the usual assumption that Koopman approximation must be learned purely from snapshot pairs, and pivots from a fully data-driven scaling story toward a hybrid workflow that exploits partial physics: the differential equations of each subsystem are known even when the full coupling law is not. The mechanical shift is two-stage and unusually explicit: first, each subsystem’s governing ODE is converted into a local Koopman matrix through the adjoint/Perron–Frobenius duality and monomial basis expansion; second, these local matrices are assembled into a block-diagonal global Koopman matrix with zero interaction blocks, then refined online by EDMD as new coupled-system data arrive. This differs from nearby Koopman work that embeds topology or conservation laws directly into end-to-end EDMD, because here the prior is not abstract regularization but a structured initial operator built from subsystem dynamics, with coupling learned only in the residual update. On coupled Duffing and van der Pol oscillator networks, the method improves one-step and n-step prediction accuracy over batch EDMD, especially in low-data regimes, and yields a cleaner separation between decaying and persistent eigenmodes. The tradeoff is that strong coupling and large numbers of subsystems may break the blockwise prior and trigger curse-of-dimensionality issues, pointing toward scalable physics-guided operator learning for partial-model, data-scarce dynamical systems.
papers.abstract: Nonlinear coupled systems are ubiquitous in science and engineering. The analysis and modeling of such systems is challenging due to their high dimensionality and complex interactions among subsystems. In recent years, operator-theoretic methods based on the Koopman operator have attracted attention as a powerful tool for analyzing and modeling nonlinear dynamical systems. Extended dynamic mode decomposition (EDMD) is one of the most popular methods to approximate the Koopman operator. However, EDMD is a purely data-driven method, and it could be unstable and inaccurate for coupled systems under limited data availability. In this paper, we propose a method to learn the Koopman operator for coupled systems using the differential equations governing each subsystem. We also demonstrate its effectiveness through numerical experiments on coupled oscillator systems.

papers.title: QHyer: Q-conditioned Hybrid Attention-mamba Transformer for Offline Goal-conditioned RL
papers.summary: QHyer: Q-conditioned Hybrid Attention-mamba Transformer for Offline Goal-conditioned RL attacks the failure of offline goal-conditioned RL when static datasets are partially observable, history-dependent, and sparse-rewarded: in those regimes, Decision Transformer-style return-to-go becomes nearly binary and non-discriminative, so it cannot tell which sub-trajectories are worth stitching, while fixed-window local extractors in prior hybrids truncate variable-length dependencies rather than compressing them. The paper pivots from trajectory-level RTG conditioning toward state-conditioned, goal-reaching Q-values, and from fixed receptive-field sequence modeling toward content-adaptive memory. The mechanical shift is twofold: first, it replaces RTG with a flow-parameterized Q-estimator learned by maximum likelihood with normalizing flows, yielding an exact log-density for p(g|s,a) that can be used directly as conditioning tokens; then it feeds those Q tokens into a gated Hybrid Attention-Mamba backbone, where attention handles global goal-directed planning and Mamba’s selective state-space dynamics adapt memory per token via input-dependent ∆, rather than a hand-tuned convolution window. Unlike nearby value-guided transformers that keep RTG and merely regularize with critics, QHyer removes RTG entirely and uses Q as the control signal. Experiments on OGBench play/noisy manipulation and D4RL Maze show state-of-the-art success on both non-Markovian and Markovian datasets, with especially large gains on long-horizon maze stitching; the main limitation is weaker visual-noisy performance, pointing toward robust pixel-level density estimation and stochastic-transition extensions as the next frontier.
papers.abstract: Offline goal-conditioned RL (GCRL) learns goal-reaching policies from static datasets, but real-world datasets are often partially observable and history-dependent, exhibiting a mix of Markovian and non-Markovian that violate standard RL assumptions. History-aware sequence models such as Decision Transformer (DT) are a natural fit for long-term dependency modeling, yet pure attention is inefficient and brittle when handling local Markovian structure and long-range context simultaneously. Although recent hybrid architectures (e.g., LSDT) introduce local extractors to improve local dependencies modeling, the fixed-window extraction cannot adapt its effective memory to varying dependency lengths in temporally heterogeneous settings, often truncating long-range context rather than compressing its content adaptively. Moreover, sequential offline GCRL faces a key bottleneck: under sparse rewards, return-to-go (RTG) becomes non-discriminative across sub-trajectories, providing little guidance signal for stitching goal-reaching behaviors from diverse demonstrations. To address these, we propose \textbf{QHyer}, which replaces RTG with a flow-parameterized, state-conditioned goal-reaching Q-estimator to support stitching across demonstrations, and introduces a gated Hybrid Attention-Mamba backbone that performs content-adaptive history compression while preserving local dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \textbf{QHyer} achieves state-of-the-art performance on both non-Markovian and Markovian datasets, validating its effectiveness for diverse scenarios.

papers.title: Robust Conditional Conformal Prediction via Branched Normalizing Flow
papers.summary: Robust Conditional Conformal Prediction via Branched Normalizing Flow attacks the failure mode where conformal prediction still looks “valid” in marginal coverage but becomes unreliable at individual test inputs once calibration and test distributions drift, especially in multi-source domain generalization and other joint-shift regimes. The paper challenges the standard workflow of merely aligning marginal conformal-score histograms, which can hide conditional score mismatches, and pivots from score-level robustness toward invertible distribution transport for conditional coverage. The mechanical shift is a new robustness criterion, the conditional coverage gap (CCG) and its test-distribution average ICG, then a Wasserstein bound showing that conditional invalidity grows with joint distribution shift; this motivates Branched Normalizing Flow (BNF), a two-branch normalizing-flow architecture that separately maps inputs and labels, normalizes a test x toward the calibration distribution without needing y at inference, constructs an adaptive conformal set there, and then inverts only the Y-branch to recover a prediction set on the shifted test space with preserved guarantees. Unlike nearby conformal-O.T. or Wasserstein-regularized methods, BNF is explicitly built around invertibility plus branch separation, and its Augmented BNF variant injects Gaussian noise into the label branch to improve expressiveness beyond a deterministic monotone transform. Across nine synthetic and real-world datasets, including sales, traffic, healthcare, and epidemic forecasting, BNF+CQR reduces worst-slice coverage gap and improves conditional robustness, though it trades off some determinism and can enlarge sets; the paper points toward transport-based conformal inference that learns latent canonicalizations of shift rather than only post hoc calibration.
papers.abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) constructs prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees under the assumption that the calibration and test distributions are identical. However, under distribution shift, existing approaches primarily align marginal conformal score distributions, which is sufficient to preserve marginal coverage but does not control the conditional coverage error at individual test inputs. As a consequence, CP can remain unreliable in regions where the conditional score distributions are mismatched. In this work, we bound the conditional invalidity of CP under distribution shift in terms of the Wasserstein distance between the calibration and test distributions. This result highlights the role of invertible transport in mitigating conditional coverage degradation. Motivated by this insight, we introduce Branched Normalizing Flow (BNF), a two-branch architecture that normalizes a test input to the calibration distribution and transforms the prediction set of the normalized input back to the test distribution while preserving conditional guarantees. Empirically, BNF consistently improves conditional coverage robustness on nine datasets across a wide range of confidence levels.
