papers.title: Leveraging Data Symmetries to Select an Optimal Subset of Training Data under Label Noise
papers.summary: Leveraging Data Symmetries to Select an Optimal Subset of Training Data under Label Noise attacks the bottleneck that data-centric clean-subset selection breaks down when training points live in high-dimensional spaces: cutstats, which ranks samples by k-NN neighborhood label agreement, is only as good as the local neighbor graph, and that graph can become unreliable long before the downstream classifier is trained. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that raw-feature k-NN is a stable proxy for “cleanliness,” and pivots from robustness-at-training-time toward geometry-aware data deletion: instead of making the model tolerate noisy labels, it tries to reconstruct a near-noise-free subset before learning. The core mechanical shift is to replace Euclidean k-NN on x with k-NN on an invariant representation F(x) that collapses symmetry orbits, so the cutstats z-score is computed in symmetry-aware space; the theory ties subset error rates directly to k-NN accuracy, then shows why the Bayes approximation deteriorates with dimension and how orthogonal invariance (via norms) or permutation invariance (via sorting) restores it. Unlike nearby noisy-label work, this is classifier-agnostic and data-level, but also symmetry-explicit rather than loss-based or co-teaching. Experiments on synthetic orthogonal/permutation tasks, Rotated MNIST, Tetris, and CIFAR-10N show stronger subset accuracy and downstream performance than vanilla cutstats, entropy filtering, forgetting, herding, and even GCE in most settings. The limitation is dependence on faithful invariance; the future direction is learned approximate symmetry representations for subset selection across richer groups and modalities.
papers.abstract: The performance of machine learning models often relies on large labeled datasets; however, data collected from diverse sources can contain label noise. Recent work has shown that, in noisy settings, there may exist a subset of the training data on which models can achieve performance comparable to training on a noise-free dataset. A widely used method for identifying such subsets is cutstats, which employs k-nearest neighbors (k-NN) to detect low-noise samples. However, its performance on high-dimensional data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we formally establish that the performance of a classifier trained on a subset of a noisy dataset selected via cutstats is influenced by the accuracy of k-NN. We further demonstrate that, in noisy environments, exploiting data invariance and knowledge of underlying symmetries can significantly enhance the performance of k-NN, bringing it closer to the Bayes optimal classifier even in high-dimensional regimes. Finally, we show that for real-world scenarios, where information about the underlying invariance is only partially known, learnt invariant representations can still facilitate the identification of near-optimal subsets.

papers.title: How Label Imbalance Shapes Geometry: A General Spectral Analysis of Multi-Label Neural Collapse
papers.summary: How Label Imbalance Shapes Geometry: A General Spectral Analysis of Multi-Label Neural Collapse attacks the failure of the recent “tag-wise averaging” picture to explain terminal multi-label geometry when label co-occurrence is correlated and multiplicity-one data are long-tailed. The paper challenges the implicit balanced-symmetry assumption behind multi-label Neural Collapse and pivots from uniform averaging toward a spectral-control view in which the centered label covariance spectrum κm, extracted from the second-order moment matrix on the centered subspace, becomes the organizing invariant. The mechanical shift is concrete: starting from a PAL-loss affine lower bound, then combining Young’s inequality, centering projection, and a critical-point scaling identity, the authors prove that terminal geometry is controlled by κm and by class-frequency counts Njm rather than by naive combinatorial symmetry. This resolves the scaled-average aspect of the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture: higher-multiplicity prototypes are not uniformly averaged from constituent tags, but generated from multiplicity-one directions with explicit √r1,k weights, and classical tag-wise averaging appears only in the orthogonal, balanced special case. Unusual among nearby NC papers, the analysis treats label-set multiplicity as the natural collapse granularity and identifies weakest centered contrast directions as the bottleneck. Synthetic MLab-MNIST/CIFAR10 experiments with ResNet18, where only multiplicity-one samples are downsampled, show persistent NC2/NC3 distortions and better Gram alignment after D^{-1/2} scaling, supporting the theory and pointing toward label-spectrum-aware regularization and large-scale multi-label geometric diagnostics.
papers.abstract: This work investigates the phenomenon of Neural Collapse (NC) in multi-label classification, extending its conceptual framework from multi-class learning to general correlated and imbalanced multi-label settings. Although recent studies have identified a ''tag-wise averaging'' structure for multi-label features, this view relies on implicit assumptions of label balance and combinatorial symmetry. Consequently, it fails to account for the geometrical distortions caused by intrinsic label correlations and data imbalance, which are common in practice. We resolve the multiplicity-one imbalance conjecture raised by Li et al. (2024), showing that higher-multiplicity prototypes obey a class-frequency-weighted synthesis rule rather than uniform averaging. To address this, we propose a rigorous spectral-control framework to analyze the terminal phase of multi-label learning under general imbalanced conditions. We introduce the label covariance spectrum $κ_m$, a scalar controlling the distribution-dependent lower-bound geometry, derived from the second-order moment matrix of the label distribution. Contrary to the averaging perspective, our analysis reveals that the centered label covariance spectrum controls the stability of terminal geometry by quantifying the weakest centered inter-class contrast directions. We prove that the classical Tag-wise Averaging emerges only as a special case under perfect orthogonality. Numerical experiments on synthetic distributions validate our theoretical bounds. This work resolves the scaled-average aspect of the imbalance conjecture and establishes a unifying theoretical framework that extends Neural Collapse to complex, imbalanced multi-label settings.

papers.title: Stochastic Sparse Attention for Memory-Bound Inference
papers.summary: Stochastic Sparse Attention for Memory-Bound Inference attacks the long-context decoding bottleneck where autoregressive generation is no longer compute-bound but bandwidth-bound because every new token must stream the entire KV cache, making exact attention kernels and cache compression still pay the cost of touching essentially all keys and values. The paper pivots from the usual “preserve exact attention, just make it faster” or “truncate/compress the cache” mindset toward an inference-time stochastic estimator that reduces how much KV state is actually accessed without permanently discarding it. The mechanical shift is SANTA: sample S ≪ n_k indices from the post-softmax attention distribution, fetch only those value rows, and average them, turning the value stage into an unbiased gather-and-add estimator with no post-softmax multiplies; S2ANTA adds stratified/systematic sampling to cut variance and make the sampler GPU-friendly. A complementary Bernoulli qK^T estimator sparsifies the score stage with ternary query queries, making key access feature-sparse as well. Unlike top-k attention, this is not biased truncation, and unlike FlashInfer/FlashDecoding it is not a denser exact scan with better IO locality; instead it explicitly trades sample budget for bandwidth. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B, the method matches baseline accuracy at 32k-token contexts while reaching about 1.5× decode-step kernel speedup on an RTX 6000 Ada, with S2ANTA-prop outperforming the speculative flash variant at smaller budgets. This points toward sparse, multiplier-free, energy-aware inference and adaptive per-layer sampling policies.
papers.abstract: Autoregressive decoding becomes bandwidth-limited at long contexts, as generating each token requires reading all $n_k$ key and value vectors from KV cache. We present Stochastic Additive No-mulT Attention (SANTA), a method that sparsifies value-cache access by sampling $S \ll n_k$ indices from the post-softmax distribution and aggregates only those value rows. This yields an unbiased estimator of the post-softmax value aggregation while replacing value-stage multiply-accumulates with gather-and-add. We introduce stratified sampling to design variance-reduced, GPU-friendly variants, demonstrating $1.5\times$ decode-step attention kernel speedup over FlashInfer and FlashDecoding on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada while matching baseline accuracy at 32k-token contexts. Finally, we propose Bernoulli $qK^\mathsf{T}$ sampling as a complementary technique to sparsify the score stage, reducing key-feature access through stochastic ternary queries. Both methods are orthogonal to upstream techniques such as ternary quantization, low-rank projections, and KV-cache compression. Together, they point toward sparse, multiplier-free, and energy-efficient inference. We open-source our kernels at: https://github.com/OPUSLab/SANTA.git

papers.title: RefusalGuard: Geometry-Preserving Fine-Tuning for Safety in LLMs
papers.summary: RefusalGuard: Geometry-Preserving Fine-Tuning for Safety in LLMs attacks a concrete post-training failure mode: safety-aligned LLMs that are fine-tuned for downstream work often lose refusal behavior and become easier to jailbreak, even after exposure to only 10 synthetic harmful examples. The paper challenges the workflow assumption that parameter-efficient adaptation, benign utility tuning, or generic representation editing can be applied after alignment without disturbing the internal mechanism of refusal. It pivots from output-level robustness and dataset curation toward preserving the model’s own safety geometry in activation space. The mechanical shift is to freeze the base aligned model and learn ReFT-style low-rank hidden-state interventions while penalizing any component of the update that projects into a layer-specific refusal cone extracted from the aligned checkpoint; the total objective combines task loss with a geometry-preservation loss that keeps safety-mediating directions stable while letting adaptation proceed in complementary subspaces. Unlike nearby work such as LoRA, Circuit Breaker, or REPBEND, this method does not merely suppress harmful outputs or separate safe/unsafe states in a generic way; it explicitly preserves refusal structure as a geometric object, motivated by mechanistic evidence of directional drift, cone distortion, and task-safety interference under standard fine-tuning. Across LLaMA, Gemma, and Qwen models on AdvBench, DirectHarm4, and JailbreakBench, it holds attack success rates near the base aligned models while retaining competitive GSM8K, OpenOrca, and ARC utility, though stronger preservation trades off some task performance. This points toward geometry-aware continual adaptation where safety is maintained by constraining internal representational drift rather than patching behavior after the fact.
papers.abstract: Fine-tuning safety-aligned language models for downstream tasks often leads to substantial degradation of refusal behavior, making models vulnerable to adversarial misuse. While prior work has shown that safety-relevant features are encoded in structured representations within the model's activation space, how these representations change during fine-tuning and why alignment degrades remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the representation-level mechanisms underlying alignment degradation. Our analysis shows that standard fine-tuning induces systematic drift in safety-relevant representations, distorts their geometric structure, and introduces interference between task optimization and safety features. These effects collectively lead to increased harmful compliance. Motivated by these findings, we introduce REFUSALGUARD, a representation-level fine-tuning framework that preserves safety-relevant structure during model adaptation. Our approach constrains updates in hidden representation space, ensuring that safety-mediating components remain stable while allowing task-specific learning in complementary directions. We evaluate REFUSALGUARD across multiple model families, including LLaMA, Gemma, and Qwen, on adversarial safety benchmarks such as AdvBench, DirectHarm4, and JailbreakBench, as well as downstream utility tasks. Our approach achieves attack success rates comparable to base safety-aligned models while maintaining competitive task performance, significantly outperforming baselines.

papers.title: Deep learning-based pavement performance modeling using multiple distress indicators and road work history
papers.summary: Deep learning-based pavement performance modeling using multiple distress indicators and road work history attacks the bottleneck in pavement management where long-lived deterioration is usually forecast with Markov chains or hand-crafted regressions that assume a small set of condition states and ignore the interaction between distress evolution and prior interventions. The paper pivots from transition-probability thinking toward a data-driven, maintenance-aware sequence model: it treats 18 years of Texas Department of Transportation PMIS records as a 2D tensor of 21 flexible pavement indicators, 20 maintenance/rehabilitation dummy variables, and time-since-last-treatment, then feeds that raw history into CNN, LSTM, and CNN-LSTM architectures. The mechanical shift is not just “deep learning” but replacing feature extraction and feature selection with learned local filters over the time-indicator matrix plus recurrent memory for deterioration trajectory; road-work history becomes an explicit control signal rather than an exogenous nuisance. That makes the approach unusual relative to nearby pavement AI work, which often predicts a single roughness index or uses small engineered feature sets. On more than 100,000 pavement sections, the CNN generally outperformed LSTM and the hybrid model for over half the targets, with useful R2 values above 0.7 mainly for ride score and IRI-related measures, while sparse distress labels such as severe rutting and patching remained hard or numerically unstable because of little variance. The result points toward multi-output, intervention-aware infrastructure digital twins and broader deep sequence modeling for asset management under heterogeneous, low-signal deterioration regimes.
papers.abstract: The deterioration of pavement is a complex and dynamic process determined by different factors including material, environment, design, and some other unobserved variables. Accurate predictions of pavement condition can help maximize the use of available resources for pavement management agencies through better coordinated preservation and maintenance activities. This paper uses deep neural networks such as the convolutional neural network (CNN) and the long short-term memory (LSTM) to model the pavement deterioration process. In this paper, pavement condition data and maintenance and rehabilitation history collected by the Texas Department of Transportation over the past 18 years were used. Twenty-one flexible pavement condition indicators, including cracking, rutting, raveling, and roughness, collected from more than 100,000 pavement sections were included in the proposed models. Promising preliminary results were obtained. Case study results show that the proposed CNN model outperforms standard machine learning models in predicting pavement condition values.

papers.title: Training Non-Differentiable Networks via Optimal Transport
papers.summary: Training Non-Differentiable Networks via Optimal Transport attacks the failure mode where neural models contain hard thresholds, quantization, argmax routing, or black-box simulators so backpropagation is undefined and surrogate gradients become biased proxies rather than true training signals. It pivots away from the standard zeroth-order story of estimating smooth gradients from random perturbations and toward treating the model as a forward-only cost oracle over structured polytope probes. The core mechanical shift is PolyStep: parameters are partitioned into particles, each particle is rotated into a low-dimensional subspace, the loss is evaluated only at polytope vertices and intermediate probe points, and a softmax-weighted assignment over the particle-by-vertex cost matrix produces a barycentric displacement toward low-cost vertices. This is framed as the one-sided limit of regularized optimal transport, with the practical headline solver using a single fused softmax pass rather than Sinkhorn iterations, while a full OT variant adds target-marginal diversity when many particles compete. What is unusual is that OT is used not to relax discrete labels but to select descent directions in parameter space, making the optimizer compatible with genuinely non-differentiable forward passes and requiring only batched inference via vmap. Across hard-LIF spiking MNIST, int8 quantization, argmax attention, staircase activations, hard MoE routing, MAX-SAT up to 1M variables, and RL policy search, it beats all gradient-free baselines, reaching 93.4% on hard-LIF MNIST and matching OpenAI-ES under quantization, while still trailing exact-gradient Adam where gradients exist. The paper points toward forward-only training loops for neuromorphic, quantized, and discrete-control systems, with the key limitation that schedule choice and dimensionality ceilings remain binding.
papers.abstract: Neural networks increasingly embed non-differentiable components (spiking neurons, quantized layers, discrete routing, blackbox simulators, etc.) where backpropagation is inapplicable and surrogate gradients introduce bias. We present PolyStep, a gradient-free optimizer that updates parameters using only forward passes. Each step evaluates the loss at structured polytope vertices in a compressed subspace, computes softmax-weighted assignments over the resulting cost matrix, and displaces particles toward low-cost vertices via barycentric projection. This update corresponds to the one-sided limit of a regularized optimal-transport problem, inheriting its geometric structure without Sinkhorn iterations. PolyStep trains genuinely non-differentiable models where existing gradient-free methods collapse to near-random accuracy. On hard-LIF spiking networks we reach 93.4% test accuracy, outperforming all gradient-free baselines by over 60~pp and closing to within 4.4~pp of a surrogate-gradient Adam ceiling. Across four additional non-differentiable architectures (int8 quantization, argmax attention, staircase activations, hard MoE routing) we lead every gradient-free competitor. On MAX-SAT scaling from 100 to 1M variables, we sustain above 92% clause satisfaction while evolution strategies drop 8--12~pp. On RL policy search, we match OpenAI-ES on classical control and retain performance under integer and binary quantization that collapses gradient-based methods. We prove convergence to conservative-stationary points at rate $O(\log T/\sqrt{T})$ on piecewise-smooth losses, upgraded to Clarke-stationary on the headline architectures and extended to the piecewise-constant regime via a hitting-time bound. These rates match the known zeroth-order query-complexity lower bounds that all forward-only methods inherit. Code is available at https://github.com/anindex/polystep.

papers.title: Pandora's Regret: A Proper Scoring Rule for Evaluating Sequential Search
papers.summary: Pandora's Regret: A Proper Scoring Rule for Evaluating Sequential Search attacks the mismatch between how multiclass forecasts are usually scored and how real diagnostic workflows actually consume them: in sequential search, clinicians or troubleshooters test candidates one at a time until the truth is found, so a metric should punish not just low probability on the true class but also rank reversals that send costly distractors ahead of it. The paper pivots from the standard assumption that proper scoring rules only need to respect the marginal probability of the true label toward a sequential, pairwise ordering view of within-example competition. The mechanical shift is to derive the score from expected optimal search cost under the Pandora policy, where classes are tested in descending probability-to-cost ratio; this yields Pandora’s Regret, a closed-form, pairwise-additive, strictly proper score that is literally an affine transform of excess search cost. Unlike log loss, accuracy, or macro-F1, which the paper shows correspond to implicit parallel, top-1, or population-level decision models, Pandora’s Regret is explicitly order-sensitive and penalizes “distractors outrank the truth” failures. A one-parameter Beta family interpolates between smooth probability-sensitive penalties and harder rank-sensitive behavior, while preserving propriety for finite alpha. On 597 TIMM image models evaluated on MedMNIST DermaMNIST and OCTMNIST with task-specific clinical cost models, Pandora-based rankings better matched downstream diagnostic cost than standard metrics. The result points toward decision-structured evaluation criteria derived from deployment costs, and toward richer search-aware scoring for hierarchical, imperfect, and adaptive clinical or agentic inference.
papers.abstract: In sequential search, alternatives are tested until the true class is found. Standard proper scoring rules like log loss are local, ignoring the ranking of competitors and misaligning model evaluation with search utility. We show that sequential search induces a pairwise structure that overcomes this. By analyzing the expected cost of optimal search under varying testing costs, we derive Pandora's Regret: a closed-form, pairwise-additive, and strictly proper scoring rule. Pandora's Regret both elicits true probabilities and penalizes rank-reversing miscalibrations where distractors outrank the true class. Our construction yields a one-parameter Beta family that balances penalties for rank-swapping versus probability magnitude, while retaining a grounded interpretation as expected search cost. We prove that log loss, accuracy, and macro-F1 rely on implicit decision models misaligned with sequential search. Across 597 MedMNIST models, Pandora-based metrics better predict clinical diagnostic costs than standard alternatives, extending decision-theoretic scoring rule construction to the multiclass setting.

papers.title: PepSpecBench: A Unified Evaluation Benchmark for Peptide Tandem Mass Spectrometry Prediction
papers.summary: PepSpecBench: A Unified Evaluation Benchmark for Peptide Tandem Mass Spectrometry Prediction attacks a measurement problem in computational proteomics rather than a new predictor: reported gains in MS/MS spectrum prediction are obscured by incompatible preprocessing, mismatched output formats, and leakage-prone random splits that let models memorize peptide backbones across PTM variants. The paper pivots the field from chasing isolated leaderboard scores toward controlled, leakage-aware, physically diagnostic evaluation. Its mechanical shift is to harmonize PROSPECT and MassIVE-KB under one preprocessing scope, retrain six representative models, project every native output into a shared 234-dimensional canonical fragment-ion space, and enforce a strict backbone-disjoint split that blocks exact-sequence reuse. Unlike nearby benchmark work, PepSpecBench couples in-domain testing with a seven-subset cross-species zero-shot suite and inference-time perturbation probes that override normalized collision energy or force precursor-charge changes, exposing whether models actually condition on instrument metadata. In this setting, Prosit and Prosit Transformer lead in-domain, but naive random splits inflate apparent performance and the in-domain ranking fails to predict robustness: PredFull becomes the strongest OOD transfer model, while AlphaPeptDeep nearly collapses under charge perturbation and Prosit-family calibration only holds when multi-NCE training data exist. The main implication is that spectrum prediction is governed as much by split design, metadata sensitivity, and source distribution as by architecture, pointing future AI-for-proteomics research toward physically grounded, homology-aware, instrument-sensitive benchmarks and deployment protocols.
papers.abstract: Tandem mass spectrometry provides a high-throughput framework for identifying and quantifying proteins in complex biological samples. In computational proteomics, predicting peptide MS/MS spectra is a critical task, enabling downstream applications such as large-scale peptide identification and quantification. While deep learning architectures have substantially improved prediction accuracy, three evaluation challenges obscure the true progress of the field. First, inconsistent data preprocessing and incompatible model output spaces hinder fair model comparison. Second, flawed data splitting strategies can permit hidden sequence leakage and inflate reported performance. Third, existing evaluations typically lack comprehensive cross-species benchmarking and systematic assessment of model robustness to influential experimental conditions. To address these challenges, we propose PepSpecBench, a unified benchmark for peptide MS/MS spectrum prediction. PepSpecBench standardizes data preprocessing across complementary public datasets, enforces a strict backbone-disjoint splitting strategy to eliminate sequence leakage, and evaluates diverse architectures within a shared fragment-ion representation space. It further introduces a comprehensive multi-species evaluation suite and physically grounded metadata perturbation probes to assess model robustness and instrument awareness. We uncover previously unrecognized performance discrepancies and robustness limitations across six representative models, providing actionable insights for future model design, evaluation and practical deployment.

papers.title: TRAP: Tail-aware Ranking Attack for World-Model Planning
papers.summary: TRAP: Tail-aware Ranking Attack for World-Model Planning attacks a security gap that emerges when world-model agents choose actions by ranking many imagined futures rather than by a single reactive policy output: shallow backdoors aimed at one-step predictions, latent features, or immediate actions get diluted by multi-step dynamics and value aggregation, so they fail to reliably hijack long-horizon control. The paper pivots from the old attack assumption that influencing local trajectory content is enough toward a ranking-centric view of planning, where the bottleneck becomes the long-tailed structure of candidate trajectory scores and the small set of decision-critical “tail” rollouts that dominate selection. The core mechanical shift is TRAP’s inference-time, trigger-conditioned patch attack, which optimizes a tail-aware ranking loss over proxy rollouts to suppress those high-value trajectories, then uses dual gating losses to enforce directionality and prevent over-suppression, while TV and L2 regularization keep the patch spatially plausible. Unlike prior backdoor work that manipulates absolute scores or policy logits, TRAP directly perturbs the relative ordering of imagined futures, preserving clean-condition behavior while altering triggered planning outcomes. Evaluated in white-box settings on DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2 across DMControl, Crafter, and Atari, it consistently causes sustained return drops and high attack success rates, often near-perfect on control tasks, with only modest overhead and some reduction under image defenses. The result points toward a future research direction where security for world models must analyze trajectory-ranking robustness, not just prediction accuracy or policy integrity.
papers.abstract: World models enable long-horizon planning by internally generating and evaluating imagined trajectories, making them a promising foundation for generalist agents. However, this imagination-driven decision process also introduces new security risks. Existing backdoor attacks typically aim to manipulate local features, one-step predictions, or instantaneous policy outputs. While such objectives may suffice for weaker reactive models, they are often ineffective against world models, where the learned dynamics prior and planning process can absorb or wash out the effects of shallow perturbations. More importantly, we find that world models exhibit a distinct backdoor vulnerability rooted in the long-tailed ranking structure of imagined trajectories, where disrupting the ordering of a few decision-critical trajectories can systematically hijack planning. To exploit this vulnerability, we propose TRAP, a backdoor attack framework for world models that targets imagined trajectory ranking. TRAP combines a tail-aware ranking loss to focus optimization on decision-critical trajectories with dual gating mechanisms that stabilize optimization and regulate when and where the attack penalty is applied. Under trigger conditions, TRAP alters the relative ranking of imagined trajectories to redirect planning outcomes, while largely maintaining the normal ranking structure on clean inputs. Experiments on DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2 across diverse tasks show that TRAP consistently induces sustained behavioral deviations and significant performance degradation, highlighting the need for dedicated security evaluation of world-model-based agents.

papers.title: Flexi-LoRA with Input-Adaptive Ranks: Efficient Finetuning for Speech and Reasoning Tasks
papers.summary: Flexi-LoRA with Input-Adaptive Ranks: Efficient Finetuning for Speech and Reasoning Tasks attacks the bottleneck that static LoRA rank budgets waste capacity on easy inputs while under-allocating hard ones, especially when the same adapter must handle QA, multistep math, and speech. It challenges the usual PEFT assumption that one fixed low-rank setting can serve an entire task, and pivots from rank selection as a global hyperparameter or training-only randomization toward sample-level, complexity-conditioned computation that is preserved at inference. The mechanical shift is a difficulty-aware router that pools token embeddings, predicts an input-specific rank from an easy/hard supervision signal derived from task metrics, and applies that rank consistently across transformer layers during both training and test time; unlike AdaLoRA, which prunes by importance, or DyLoRA, which samples ranks without true inference-time adaptation, Flexi-LoRA learns when to spend more rank on harder examples and when to compress to cheaper ones. Across MRQA QA, GSM8K/MetaMathQA-style math reasoning, and LibriSpeech with LLaMA-3.2-1B/3B and Whisper, it dominates static LoRA and dynamic baselines in the performance-parameter tradeoff, with especially large gains on reasoning chains where instruction adherence and reasoning quality matter, while using roughly one-third the parameters of LoRA-8. The paper’s limitation is that rank dynamics matter unevenly by task, suggesting future work on layer-specific routers, richer complexity estimators, and broader MoE-like adaptive fine-tuning schemes.
papers.abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) have become essential for deploying large language models, yet their static parameter allocation remains suboptimal for inputs of varying complexity. We present Flexi-LoRA, a novel framework that dynamically adjusts LoRA ranks based on input complexity during both training and inference. Through empirical analysis across question answering, mathematical reasoning, and speech tasks, we demonstrate that maintaining consistency between training and inference dynamics is important for effective adaptation, particularly for sequential reasoning tasks. Our findings reveal that input-dependent parameter allocation achieves higher performance with fewer parameters by optimally matching rank configurations to question complexity. Furthermore, task-specific dependency on rank dynamics varies, with mathematical reasoning tasks exhibiting higher dependency than QA tasks. Successful adaptation manifests not only in correctness but also in reasoning quality and instruction adherence. Flexi-LoRA consistently outperforms static LoRA while using fewer parameters, with performance gains more pronounced on tasks requiring strict reasoning chains. Our approach realizes key benefits of mixture-of-experts frameworks through a more streamlined implementation, reducing parameter redundancy while improving model capabilities. We provide comprehensive empirical studies across diverse tasks, establishing a basis for future work in input-adaptive and efficient fine-tuning approaches.

papers.title: Multi-User Dueling Bandits: A Fair Approach using Nash Social Welfare
papers.summary: Multi-User Dueling Bandits: A Fair Approach using Nash Social Welfare attacks the failure mode of collapsing heterogeneous human preference data into a single average dueling-bandit objective, which can systematically erase minority users when each user has a different Condorcet winner. The paper pivots from standard single-preference or utilitarian multi-user bandits toward a fairness-first preference-learning view where utility is measured relative to each user’s own winner, not against a global average. The mechanical shift is twofold: it rescales each arm for user d as 2P_d(i,a*_d) using that user’s Condorcet winner a*_d as a personalized baseline, then optimizes Nash Social Welfare, i.e. the product of user utilities, so low utility for any one user sharply depresses the objective. This is unusual because it brings NSW, common in fair division and multi-agent reward settings, into the dueling-bandit regime where only pairwise comparisons are observed. The analysis constructs hard instances separating “good” and “bad” winners, proving the first fairness cost lower bound here, Ω(T^{2/3}min(K,D)^{1/3}), and showing matching upper-bound dependence up to logs. Algorithmically, Fair-Explore-Then-Commit and Fair-ε-Greedy add a multi-user Condorcet identification phase via a modified DKWT tournament, then estimate scores through targeted pairwise duels against identified winners and optimize empirical NSW. In simulations with clustered majority/minority preferences, the fair methods outperform utilitarian baselines on minimum welfare and Gini coefficient while retaining sublinear regret, pointing toward contextual, continuous-action, and sparse-feedback fair preference learning.
papers.abstract: Learning from human preference data is becoming a useful tool, from fine-tuning large language models to training reinforcement learning agents. However, in most scenarios, the model is trained on the average preference of all human evaluators, which, under large variations of preferences, can be unfair to minority groups. In this work, we consider fairness in dueling bandits, a standard framework for online learning from preference data. We assume that each user has a (potentially distinct) Condorcet winner, which is an arm preferred to every other arm. Using these user-specific Condorcet winners as reference points, we evaluate and score arms according to their performance relative to the corresponding winner. To promote fairness across heterogeneous users, we adopt the well-established Nash Social Welfare objective, which maximizes the product of user utilities, thereby inherently penalizing inequality and preventing the marginalization of any single user. Within this framework, we construct a hard instance to establish a regret lower bound of $Ω(T^{2/3}\min(K,D)^\frac{1}{3})$ for a time horizon $T$, $K$ arms, and $D$ users, which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first result quantifying the cost of fairness in dueling bandits with heterogeneous preferences. We then present the Fair-Explore-Then-Commit and Fair-$ε$-Greedy algorithms with a Condorcet winner identification phase. We further derive their regret upper bounds that match the lower-bound dependence on $T$ up to logarithmic factors.

papers.title: Retrieval with Multiple Query Vectors through Anomalous Pattern Detection
papers.summary: Retrieval with Multiple Query Vectors through Anomalous Pattern Detection attacks a concrete bottleneck in vector search: standard retrieval assumes one query embedding and ranks database points by a fixed distance, but complex reasoning, paragraph-level queries, and RAG workflows often produce multiple query vectors whose shared signal is erased by averaging or independent nearest-neighbor passes. The paper pivots from single-query cosine/inner-product retrieval, and from “collapse queries into one vector” or “retrieve each query separately,” toward detecting a joint anomalous pattern across the query set and using that pattern as the retrieval key. The mechanical shift is a two-step scan-statistic procedure: first, it z-standardizes query dimensions against the database distribution, identifies a subset of dimensions where the queries are jointly in the lower tail, and scores candidate dimension sets with a nonparametric Berk–Jones scan statistic; second, it scans the database for vectors that are anomalous on those same dimensions and returns them as the retrieved set, without requiring K to be specified a priori. This is unusual because it repurposes deep subset scanning, usually used for anomaly localization, as a retrieval operator rather than a detector. Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, Persona and Ailuminate text embeddings from LLaMA-3 8B Instruct, and a medical tabular dataset show larger query sets usually improve precision/recall, with the clearest gains from 1 to 8 queries and diminishing returns beyond that; the method is strongest on LLM embeddings, suggesting a future of retrieval systems that search for structured cross-query evidence rather than nearest neighbors.
papers.abstract: A classical vector retrieval problem typically considers a \emph{single} query embedding vector as input and retrieves the most similar embedding vectors from a vector database. However, complex reasoning and retrieval tasks frequently require \emph{multiple query vectors}, rather than a single one. In this work, we propose a retrieval method that considers multiple query vectors simultaneously and retrieves the most relevant vectors from the database using concepts from anomalous pattern detection. Specifically, our approach leverages a set of query vectors $Q$ (with $|Q|\geq 1$), and identifies the subset of vector dimensions within $Q$ that standout (anomalous) from the rest of dimensions. Next, we scan the vector database to retrieve the set of vectors that are also anomalous across the previously identified vector dimensions and return them as our retrieved set of vectors. We validate our approach on two image datasets, a text dataset, and a tabular dataset. Overall, we observe that, across most datasets, larger query sets lead to improved retrieval performance. The improvement is most pronounced when increasing the query sets from 1 to 8, while the gains become smaller beyond that.

papers.title: MER-DG: Modality-Entropy Regularization for Multimodal Domain Generalization
papers.summary: MER-DG: Modality-Entropy Regularization for Multimodal Domain Generalization tackles a specific multimodal domain generalization failure in which end-to-end late-fusion training overfits to source-specific cross-modal co-occurrences instead of learning features that survive new recording conditions. The paper challenges the usual assumption that optimizing only the fused prediction is enough for multimodal robustness; it shows this workflow induces “Fusion Overfitting,” where each encoder becomes useful mainly in combination with the others and loses standalone domain-invariant signal. The pivot is from representation alignment or fusion-centric optimization toward preserving per-modality diversity before fusion. Mechanically, MER-DG is an architecture-agnostic additive regularizer that maximizes each encoder’s feature entropy through two coupled terms: a marginal-entropy hinge that enforces a variance floor on every dimension, and a spectral-entropy term that maximizes the log-determinant of the correlation matrix to decorrelate features and prevent low-rank collapse. Unlike nearby MMDG methods that rebalance norms, decompose shared/specific subspaces, or flatten the loss landscape, MER-DG directly attacks encoder collapse induced by fusion training and can be plugged into standard fusion, CMC, SimMMDG, and CMRF. On EPIC-Kitchens and HAC, across video, audio, and optical flow in both multi-source and single-source settings, it improves average target accuracy by about 5% over vanilla fusion and about 2% over prior state of the art, while also restoring standalone encoder performance and improving corruption robustness. This points toward multimodal research that regularizes information retention inside each modality branch, not just the fused output.
papers.abstract: Deploying multimodal models in real-world scenarios requires generalization to new environments where recording conditions differ from training, a challenge known as multimodal domain generalization (MMDG). Standard architectures employ separate encoders for each modality and a fusion module, training the system end-to-end by optimizing on the fused features. In this paper, we identify that such joint optimization causes encoders to exploit cross-modal co-occurrences, statistical relationships between modalities that arise from source-specific recording conditions, rather than learning domain-invariant features. We term this failure mode Fusion Overfitting. To address this, we propose Modality-Entropy Regularization for Domain Generalization (MER-DG), which maximizes the entropy of each encoder's feature distribution to preserve feature diversity. MER-DG is architecture-agnostic and integrates into existing multimodal frameworks as an additive loss term. Extensive experiments on EPIC-Kitchens and HAC benchmarks demonstrate average improvements of approximately 5% over standard fusion and approximately 2% over state-of-the-art methods.

papers.title: AdamO: A Collapse-Suppressed Optimizer for Offline RL
papers.summary: AdamO: A Collapse-Suppressed Optimizer for Offline RL attacks the offline RL failure mode where bootstrapped TD critics self-amplify errors into extreme, unusable Q-values and value collapse, especially under fixed datasets and off-policy distribution shift. The paper pivots away from the usual assumption that collapse is mainly a backup-rule, target-network, normalization, or loss-regularization problem, and turns toward optimizer dynamics as the instability source itself. Its core mechanical shift is to model offline TD learning as a feedback system, analyze Adam’s critic updates as a second-order difference process, and derive a local stability law: the frozen dynamics converge iff the spectral radius of the augmented update operator is below one, with a practical Hurwitz-style sufficient condition on the TD operator. Rather than inject an orthogonality penalty into the task loss, which would pollute Adam’s moment estimates, AdamO adds a decoupled orthogonality correction on selected weight matrices, scaled by a task-alignment budget τ and correction strength κ so the optimizer can suppress geometry-induced TD error amplification while preserving Adam’s dissipative behavior. This is unusual among offline RL stabilizers because it targets parameter geometry at the optimizer level, not the critic objective level. On D4RL MuJoCo, AntMaze, Adroit, and FrankaKitchen, plugged into TD3+BC, IQL, ReBRAC, ACTIVE, PARS, and SQOG, AdamO broadly improves stability and returns, with especially large gains on sparse-reward AntMaze and brittle human Adroit tasks. The work points toward optimizer-aware RL theory, spectral stability certificates, and control-theoretic training rules for bootstrapped learning.
papers.abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) can fail spectacularly when bootstrapped temporal-difference (TD) updates amplify their own errors, driving the critic toward extreme and unusable Q-values. A key counterintuitive insight of this work is that collapse is not only a property of the backup rule or network architecture: optimizer dynamics themselves can directly trigger or suppress instability. From a control-theoretic viewpoint, we model offline TD learning as a feedback system and analyze Adam-based critic updates. This yields a necessary and sufficient condition for stability of the induced local update dynamics: within the regime we analyze, these dynamics are stable if and only if the spectral radius of the corresponding update operator is strictly below one. Further analysis suggests that standard Adam updates can inadvertently distort the parameter geometry, motivating explicit orthogonality constraints to prevent TD error amplification. To this end, we propose AdamO, an Adam-based optimizer with a decoupled orthogonality correction regulated by a strict task-alignment budget. We prove that this design theoretically guarantees worst-case task safety and preserves Adam's continuous-time dissipative dynamics. Empirically, AdamO is broadly compatible with diverse offline RL baselines, improving stability and returns across a broad suite of benchmarks.

papers.title: Misclassification Rate and Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in Graph Convolutional Networks via Subsampling Stability
papers.summary: Misclassification Rate and Privacy-Utility Trade-offs in Graph Convolutional Networks via Subsampling Stability attacks a missing theoretical bottleneck in private graph learning: existing GCN privacy work mostly perturbs features, edges, or gradients, but offers little rigorous explanation of when differential privacy actually preserves node-classification accuracy under structural edge changes. The paper pivots away from “add noise to training” toward a stability-first view in which a well-trained GCN is repeatedly evaluated on edge-subsampled subgraphs, then released only if the majority-vote prediction is provably stable under a Propose-Test-Release check. The mechanical shift is explicit: edges are retained independently with probability p_s, each subsampled graph is classified, the label vectors are aggregated by majority vote, and a Laplace-noised stability score determines whether to output the consensus or abstain with ⊥. Unlike nearby DP-GCN work, the analysis is not just a privacy proof but a coupled privacy-utility characterization: a margin-based flip-count bound plus matrix Bernstein control of Laplacian perturbation yields an upper bound on misclassification that depends directly on p_s, and Theorem 5 identifies feasible p_s ranges where privacy is nonvacuous and accuracy does not collapse. The setting is edge-level DP on graph-valued data, using a one-layer Lipschitz GCN and Hamming misclassification on node labels. The main implication is that p_s becomes a control knob governing both utility loss and privacy feasibility, suggesting future private graph learning should be designed as a stability-selection problem rather than a generic noise-injection problem.
papers.abstract: We study differential privacy (DP) in Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) through the framework of \textit{subsampling stability}. We derive upper bounds on the misclassification rate that depend explicitly on the subsampling probability $p_s$. Furthermore, we characterize the \textit{privacy--utility trade-off} by identifying feasible ranges of $p_s$; if $p_s$ is too large, the stability-based privacy condition becomes difficult to satisfy, yielding vacuous guarantees, whereas if it is too small, accuracy deteriorates. Our results provide the first rigorous theoretical framework for understanding subsampling stability in GCNs under DP.

papers.title: DBLP: Phase-Aware Bounded-Loss Transport for Burst-Resilient Distributed ML Training
papers.summary: DBLP: Phase-Aware Bounded-Loss Transport for Burst-Resilient Distributed ML Training attacks the mismatch between distributed training’s tolerance for some gradient loss and the network reality of short microbursts that trigger tail-latency collapse, training-time variance, and QoS loss. It challenges the old assumption behind both generic transport and prior bounded-loss schemes that all gradients should be treated uniformly with a fixed reliability budget; instead of optimizing only average latency at the application layer, it pivots toward transport decisions that are conditioned on the model’s learning phase. The mechanical shift is a software-only, hardware-agnostic transport protocol that sends gradient chunks over UDP-like unreliable channels while using TCP control signals, per-packet sequence/counter metadata to reject stale cross-round packets, and bitmap-driven retransmission only until a bounded loss threshold is met. DBLP’s distinguishing move is phase adaptivity: it detects critical learning regimes online from relative changes in gradient L2 norm, then switches between a strict P_low during critical regions and a relaxed P_high elsewhere, so retransmission policy follows training sensitivity rather than a fixed network rule. Evaluated on a 3-worker centralized all-reduce setup with EfficientNetB0/CIFAR-10, ResNet50/CIFAR-100, AlexNet/CIFAR-100, and GPT-2-S/WikiText-2, DBLP preserves comparable accuracy while tolerating up to 40% more gradient loss, cuts end-to-end training time by 24.4% on average and 33.9% max, and reaches up to 5.88× faster single-round latency under injected microbursts. This points toward training-aware transport, cross-DC bounded-loss collectives, and network scheduling that prioritizes communication only when convergence is truly fragile.
papers.abstract: Distributed machine learning (ML) training has become a necessity with the prevalence of billion to trillion-parameter-scale models. While prior work has improved training efficiency from the ML perspective at the application layer, it often fails to address transient congestion events at the network layer that introduce severe tail latency and training-time variability, thereby undermining the quality of service (QoS) of distributed ML training systems. Existing network optimizations treat all gradients equally and thus fail to integrate sufficient model-training insights into communication protocol design. In this paper, we present Dynamic Bounded-Loss Protocol (DBLP), a burst-resilient, training-phase-aware, and hardware-agnostic transport protocol that incorporates model-level tolerance properties into gradient communication. By dynamically adjusting gradient loss tolerance across training phases, DBLP reduces overall training time and mitigates tail-latency collapse during transient high-loss events (i.e., microbursts). Compared to the current state-of-the-art solution (baseline), DBLP tolerates significantly higher loss while achieving comparable test accuracy, and reduces end-to-end training time by an average of 24.4% and a maximum of 33.9%. At microburst events, DBLP achieves up to 5.88x single-round communication latency speedups over the baseline, preventing burst-induced tail-latency spikes and maintaining stable training performance.

papers.title: RamanBench: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Machine Learning on Raman Spectroscopy
papers.summary: RamanBench: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Machine Learning on Raman Spectroscopy attacks the bottleneck that Raman ML progress has been stalled by fragmented repositories, inconsistent preprocessing/evaluation, and one-off papers that overfit conclusions to a single instrument, dataset, or task. It implicitly challenges the old workflow of comparing only against PLS or a narrow Raman-specific CNN on private or hard-to-reproduce data, and pivots from method-specific hero results toward a living, community-maintained measurement system for the field. The mechanical shift is the construction of a fully reproducible benchmark stack: unified access to 74 experimentally measured datasets (16 newly released), 325,668 spectra, 163 supervised tasks across four domains, fixed 80/20 splits over three seeds, standardized classification/regression metrics, and aggregate ranking via normalized score plus Elo on a live leaderboard. Unlike nearby tabular or time-series benchmarks, RamanBench preserves the ordered wavenumber axis, high feature-to-sample ratios, correlated peaks, and low-SNR baseline distortions that define Raman spectra, then directly compares classical chemometrics, RamanNet-style models, tabular foundation models such as TabPFN/TabICL, and time-series classifiers like ROCKET and Arsenal. The unusual finding is that TFM methods and some time-series baselines outperform domain-specific architectures overall, yet no model generalizes across datasets and PLS still wins some individual targets, revealing a heterogeneous regime rather than a single winner. This points toward transfer learning, spectrum-specific foundation models, and benchmark-driven algorithm design for medical diagnostics, bioprocess control, and materials analysis.
papers.abstract: Machine Learning (ML) has transformed many scientific fields, yet key applications still lack standardized benchmarks. Raman spectroscopy, a widely used technique for non-invasive molecular analysis, is one such field where progress is limited by fragmented datasets, inconsistent evaluation, and models that fail to capture the structure of spectral data. We introduce RamanBench, the first large-scale, fully reproducible benchmark for ML on Raman spectroscopy, consisting of streamlined data access, evaluation protocols and code, as well as a live leaderboard. It unifies 74 datasets (including 16 first released with this benchmark) across four domains, comprising 325,668 spectra and spanning classification and regression tasks under diverse experimental conditions. We benchmark 28 models under a standardized protocol, including classical methods (e.g., PLS), Raman-specific (e.g., RamanNet), Tabular Foundation Model (TFM) (e.g., TabPFN), and time-series approaches (e.g., ROCKET). TFM consistently outperform domain-specific and gradient boosting baselines, while time-series models remain competitive. However, no method generalizes across datasets, revealing a fundamental gap. Therefore, we invite the community to contribute new approaches to our living benchmark, with the potential to accelerate advances in critical applications such as medical diagnostics, biological research, and materials science.

papers.title: How Can One Choose the Best CAM-Based Explainability Method for a CNN Model?
papers.summary: How Can One Choose the Best CAM-Based Explainability Method for a CNN Model? attacks the evaluation bottleneck in visual explainability: practitioners want to pick among CAM variants, but the field leans on IoU between thresholded heatmaps and bounding boxes, an assumption this paper argues is mismatched to saliency maps that have no natural shape and lose information when binarized. The pivot is from box-overlap scoring and subjective side-by-side visual judgment toward a human-centered ranking protocol that preserves the full heatmap signal. Mechanically, the authors use a pre-trained ResNet50 on 17 correctly classified Chihuahua images from ImageNet, collect 50 human bounding-box annotations to build normalized “annotation heatmaps,” generate saliency maps with nine CAM-based methods spanning gradient-based and activation-based families, and compute pairwise alignment under 12 distance metrics rather than a single IoU threshold. They then rank explainers by metric score and compare those rankings against crowdsourced human preferences from a separate Prolific study using Rank-Biased Overlap, so the evaluation target becomes agreement with human saliency preference, not geometric overlap. The unusual contribution is that it treats metric choice itself as the scientific object of study. Results suggest Manhattan and Correlation best track human perception, while LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM most often match the crowd; Grad-CAM performs poorly, and some activation-based methods are accurate but computationally expensive. This points toward human-alignment benchmarks, richer annotation geometries, and automatic selection of explainability methods under real deployment constraints.
papers.abstract: In recent years, several advances have been observed in Deep Learning with surprising results. Models in this area have been increasingly used in numerous applications, including those sensitive to human life, which require clear explanations and justifications. Various explainability methods have been proposed, but not many metrics to evaluate these methods. The most commonly used metric is the Intersection over Union (IoU). However, due to the characteristics of the results of the explainability methods, called saliency maps, which do not have a known shape, we hypothesise that there must be a better metric that allows one to find an explainability method that produces results that best resemble the human perception. We propose using different metrics to assess the similarity between human perception and the explanation saliency maps to find a better metric. An investigation was conducted employing a subset of the Chihuahuas images from ImageNet dataset. Several CAM-based explainability methods were used to generate saliency maps for each chihuahua image. Alignment was measured by applying distance metrics between the bounding box of human annotations and the saliency maps produced by each explainability method. Rankings of the best saliency maps were created using the results of the distance metrics and compared to the ranking obtained using people's choice, collected through crowdsourcing, of the best explanation saliency maps for each selected image. Comparison between rankings was performed using the Rank-Biased Overlap (RBO) metric. The results indicate the feasibility of our method to find the explainability method that best resembles human perception. In our experiments, the two metrics that best resemble human perception corresponded to Manhattan and Correlation. Besides, the best explainability methods regarding human perception were LayerCAM, Score-CAM, and IS-CAM.

papers.title: Robust and Explainable Divide-and-Conquer Learning for Intrusion Detection
papers.summary: Robust and Explainable Divide-and-Conquer Learning for Intrusion Detection attacks the bottleneck that payload-based intrusion detectors face in high-dimensional, noisy, class-imbalanced traffic: global models are expensive, brittle under adversarial perturbation, and opaque to analysts on memory-limited devices. The paper challenges the usual assumption that one should either scale up a monolithic classifier or impose a generic modular hierarchy; instead it pivots from global fitting and blind mixtures toward correlation-aware decomposition of the label space. The mechanical shift is SCAL: it builds class fingerprints from per-class compression code lengths on raw payloads, turns those fingerprints into a class-correlation matrix, then uses hierarchical clustering to group positively correlated attack types into subproblems. A learned instance distributor routes flows to local decision-tree classifiers, and SCAL explicitly falls back to a single learning problem when decomposition yields no benefit, making the partitioning conditional rather than mandatory. This is unusual relative to mixtures of experts or binary hierarchical classifiers because the split is driven by local traffic similarity and evaluated jointly on accuracy, cost, robustness, and explainability. On UNSW-NB15, CIC-IDS2017, IoT, and VNAT payload benchmarks, SCAL improves minority-class local F1 by up to 43.3%, shrinks model size by up to 257×, and remains as or more resilient than global baselines under Cube attack, while decision-tree pruning can compress local models into human-auditable rules. The work points toward adaptive, multi-objective security analytics where modularity is learned from data geometry rather than imposed by architecture.
papers.abstract: Machine learning-based intrusion detection requires complex models to capture patterns in high-dimensional, noisy, and class-imbalanced raw network traffic, yet deploying such models remains impractical on resource-constrained devices with limited processing power and memory. In this paper, we present a correlation-aware divide-and-conquer learning technique that decomposes a complex learning problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems. This enables lightweight models as simple as decision trees to be trained on focused subtasks, yielding up to 43.3% higher local accuracy and up to 257 times reduction in model size on real-world network intrusion detection datasets, while also improving adversarial robustness and explainability.

papers.title: Towards Systematic Generalization for Power Grid Optimization Problems
papers.summary: Towards Systematic Generalization for Power Grid Optimization Problems attacks a bottleneck in AI-for-optimization: ACOPF and SCUC are usually learned as separate surrogates, even though they live on the same transmission graph and share physical laws, so representations fragment and fail to transfer to unseen topologies or to coupled formulations like UC-ACOPF. The paper pivots from per-task, solver-supervised proxies toward a shared, physically grounded graph representation that can support heterogeneous grid optimization across static and temporal decision regimes. The mechanical shift is a typed heterogeneous graph transformer backbone with task-specific decoders: ACOPF decodes bus voltages and generator injections, while SCUC injects time-varying load and temporal embeddings before a Transformer predicts commitment and dispatch. Training replaces pure imitation with a hybrid objective combining solver supervision, physics-informed penalties for AC power balance, line limits, ramping, and capacity constraints, plus GradNorm balancing. For the coupled UC-ACOPF setting, the model freezes the shared encoder and performs unsupervised decoder-only adaptation using a hard coupling p = u · pAC and a consensus loss that aligns SCUC dispatch with ACOPF-derived dispatch. Evaluated on OPFData, UnitCommitment.jl, and IEEE cases 14/30/57/118, it improves ACOPF accuracy and SCUC commitment quality, transfers zero-shot across larger unseen grids, and reaches hundreds-fold faster inference than Juniper on UC-ACOPF, with the tradeoff of small residual feasibility gaps. This points toward grid foundation models that generalize across formulations, not just instances.
papers.abstract: AC Optimal Power Flow (ACOPF) and Security-Constrained Unit Commitment (SCUC) are fundamental optimization problems in power system operations. ACOPF serves as the physical backbone of grid simulation and real-time operation, enforcing nonlinear power flow feasibility and network limits, while SCUC represents a core market-level decision process that schedules generation under operational and security constraints. Although these problems share the same underlying transmission network and physical laws, they differ in decision variables and temporal coupling, and prior learning-based approaches address them in isolation, resulting in disjoint models and representations.We propose a learning framework that jointly models ACOPF and SCUC through a shared graph-based backbone that captures grid topology and physical interactions, coupled with task-specific decoders for static and temporal decision-making. Training includes solver supervision with physics-informed objectives to enforce AC feasibility and inter-temporal operational constraints. To evaluate generalization, we assess cross-case transfer on unseen grid topologies for ACOPF and SCUC without retraining, and systematic generalization on the UC-ACOPF problem using unsupervised, physics-based objectives and a power-dispatch consensus mechanism. Experiments across multiple grid scales demonstrate improved performance and transferability relative to existing learning-based baselines, indicating that the model can support learning across heterogeneous power system optimization problems.

papers.title: Large margin classifier with graph-based adaptive regularization
papers.summary: Large margin classifier with graph-based adaptive regularization attacks a specific weakness of Gabriel graph (GG) large-margin classifiers: their original fixed, classwise-outlier threshold is autonomous and hardware-friendly, but it bakes in a single pruning rule that can mis-handle overlap, noisy patterns, and class imbalance by discarding the same “low quality” support edges for every problem. The paper pivots from parameter-free graph learning toward tunable adaptive regularization, replacing the premise that GG classifiers should have one pre-established threshold with per-class hyperparameters that scale the quality index Q(xi)=Veq/Vdt before filtering. Mechanically, this changes which structural support vectors survive graph pruning: low-Q points in the margin/outlier region can be selectively removed while preserving the margin region, and the two classes can be given different thresholds so the majority class is filtered more aggressively and the minority class is retained, effectively shifting the decision surface. Unlike nearby work that treats GG pruning as a fixed heuristic, the method explicitly opens the solution space and optimizes hclass via Bayesian Optimization and cross-validation. Experiments on 15 UCI datasets plus KEEL Appendicitis compared flexible Chipclass, GMM-GG, and RBF-GG against their fixed-threshold versions, SVM, random forest, and kNN; Friedman/Bonnferroni-Dunn tests showed statistically better average ranks for the flexible GG variants. The result suggests a broader research direction: graph-structured classifiers that trade some autonomy for data-adaptive regularization, imbalance control, and tunable margin geometry.
papers.abstract: This paper introduces the use of per-class regularization hyperparameters in Gabriel graph-based binary classifiers. We demonstrate how the quality index used for regularization behaves both in the margin region and in the presence of outliers, and how incorporating this regularization flexibility can lead to solutions that effectively eliminate outliers while training the classifier. We also show how it can address class imbalance by generating higher and lower thresholds for the majority and minority classes, respectively. Thus, rather than having a single solution based on fixed thresholds, flexible thresholds expand the solution space and can be optimized through hyperparameter tuning algorithms. Friedman test shows that flexible thresholds are capable of improving Gabriel graph-based classifiers.

papers.title: TIJERE: A Novel Threat Intelligence Joint Extraction Model Based on Analyst Expert Knowledge
papers.summary: TIJERE: A Novel Threat Intelligence Joint Extraction Model Based on Analyst Expert Knowledge attacks the bottleneck in cyber threat intelligence extraction where joint NER+RE models collapse under overlapping relations, ambiguous relation names, and feature confusion that propagates errors into cybersecurity knowledge graphs. The paper pivots from pipeline extraction and standard single-tagging schemes toward a multisequence labeling representation (MSLR) that generates one full labeled sequence per entity pair, so the unit of prediction becomes the pair-specific relation context rather than the sentence alone. The mechanical shift is unusual: TIJERE injects analyst expert knowledge through two explicit domain features, entity type and entity mask, then concatenates them with contextual embeddings from SecureBERT+ and a BiGRU encoder, using CRF for NER and a dense-softmax head for RE. This replaces the common assumption that a generic language model can infer cyber semantics from text alone, and it also avoids relation-matching side modules that make “joint” systems behave like pipelines. Evaluated on DNRTI-JE, the first publicly available jointly labeled cybersecurity NER/RE dataset, curated from DNRTI into 13 entity types and 15 relations across 6,592 APT sentences, TIJERE reaches F1 above 0.93 for NER and 0.98 for RE. The main tradeoff is dependence on expert feature engineering and extra sequence duplication, which raises input dimensionality and training cost, but the result points toward domain-encoded, pairwise, ontology-aware extraction systems for real-time CTI pipelines and other structured-information domains.
papers.abstract: The extraction of entities and relationships from threat intelligence reports into structured formats, such as cybersecurity knowledge graphs, is essential for automated threat analysis, detection, and mitigation. However, existing joint extraction methods struggle with feature confusion, language ambiguity, noise propagation, and overlapping relations, resulting in low accuracy and poor model performance. This paper presents TIJERE, an innovative joint entity and relation extraction framework that formulates joint extraction as a multisequence labeling representation (MSLR) problem. Specifically, separate sequences are generated for each entity pair. Unlike prior tagging schemes, MSLR integrates expert domain features to enrich positional, contextual, and semantic representations of entities, thereby enhancing feature distinction and classification accuracy. Additionally, TIJERE reduces language ambiguity and enhances domain-specific generalization by leveraging SecureBERT+, a contextual language model fine-tuned on cybersecurity text. This improves both named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE). This paper also introduces DNRTI-JE, the first publicly available jointly labeled dataset for cybersecurity entity and RE, filling a crucial gap in cyber threat intelligence automation. Empirical evaluations on the curated DNRTI-JE dataset demonstrate that TIJERE achieves state-of-the-art performance, with F1-scores exceeding 0.93 for NER and 0.98 for RE, outperforming existing methods. Together, TIJERE and the standardized benchmarking DNRTI-JE dataset enable high-performance cybersecurity intelligence extraction, with transferable applications in healthcare, finance, and bioinformatics.

papers.title: Bringing Order to Asynchronous SGD: Towards Optimality under Data-Dependent Delays with Momentum
papers.summary: Bringing Order to Asynchronous SGD: Towards Optimality under Data-Dependent Delays with Momentum attacks the core failure mode of distributed async training: when compute time correlates with sample complexity, stale gradients are not random noise but a bias mechanism that overweights easy/fast examples and suppresses hard ones, while prior fixes either attenuate/discard delayed updates or assume delay–data independence and bounded gradients. The paper pivots from “delay compensation by filtering” toward preserving the full information content of delayed updates, and the mechanical shift is a momentum rule that assigns each arriving gradient the exact coefficient it would have in the ideal delay-free exponential trajectory, β(1−β)^{τ_t}, rather than treating staleness as something to downweight. That ordered-momentum viewpoint is paired with a virtual-momentum analysis that isolates the residual error to an O(M) missing-update term instead of a delay-dependent penalty, yielding optimal rates for smooth nonconvex objectives without the extra suboptimal (GLΔM/T)^{2/3} term and, in the previously unaddressed smooth convex case, the first data-dependent guarantee with M only in the vanishing O(D^2LM/T) term. In the convex setting the paper further turns to µ2-SGD, combining ordered asynchronous STORM with Anytime-GD’s parameter averaging (“double momentum”), which stabilizes training over a broad learning-rate range and reduces tuning to a single offline choice. This suggests a direction where async optimization becomes delay-aware without becoming delay-reactive, and where future work extends the ordered-momentum principle to adaptive optimizers such as Adam/AdamW.
papers.abstract: Asynchronous stochastic gradient descent (SGD) enables scalable distributed training but suffers from gradient staleness. Existing mitigation strategies, such as delay-adaptive learning rates and staleness-aware filtering, typically attenuate or discard delayed gradients, introducing systematic bias: updates from simpler or faster-to-process samples are overrepresented, while gradients from more complex samples are delayed or suppressed. In contrast, prior approaches to data-dependent delays rely on a Lipschitz assumption that yields suboptimal rates or leave the smooth, convex case unaddressed. We propose a momentum-based asynchronous framework designed to preserve information from delayed gradients while mitigating the effects of staleness. We establish the first optimal convergence rates for data-dependent delays in both convex and non-convex smooth setups, providing a new result for asynchronous optimization under standard assumptions. Additionally, we derive robust learning-rate schedules that simplify hyperparameter tuning in practice.

papers.title: NeuroViz: Real-time Interactive Visualization of Forward and Backward Passes in Neural Network Training
papers.summary: NeuroViz: Real-time Interactive Visualization of Forward and Backward Passes in Neural Network Training attacks the specific interpretability bottleneck that even simple fully connected networks remain opaque during learning: beginners can read equations or static graphs, but cannot see how activations, gradients, and parameter updates line up within an epoch. It implicitly challenges the old assumption that training understanding comes from post hoc loss curves or architecture diagrams, and pivots from static model inspection and inference-only explainers toward synchronized observation of the complete training cycle. The mechanical shift is a web-based TensorFlow.js simulator that lets users configure architecture, activation function, learning rate, and dataset, then animates green forward pulses, red backward pulses, and weight updates only after the corresponding error signal reaches each neuron, while dynamically updating per-neuron equations and pre- vs. post-update states. Unlike nearby tools such as TensorFlow Playground, Netron, CNN Explainer, or transformer visualizers, NeuroViz centers step-aligned backprop timing and neuron-level correspondence rather than just decision boundaries, layer topology, or activation snapshots. In a within-subject user study with 31 computer science and data science participants comparing six established tools, it achieved the highest SUS score (80.97), the lowest workload, and the highest trust rating, with over 70% reporting substantially greater transparency. The main limitation is scope: it currently covers feedforward networks only. This points toward future AI interfaces that make training dynamics observable, teachable, and debuggable across architectures, not merely explainable after the fact.
papers.abstract: Training neural networks is difficult to interpret, particularly for newcomers. We introduce NeuroViz, an interactive visualization tool that supports real-time exploration of fully connected neural network training. Users can configure network architecture, activation functions, learning rates, and datasets, then observe activations, weight updates, and loss progression. NeuroViz visualizes weight changes in direct correspondence with activation signals in both forward and backward passes, enabling users to distinguish pre- and post-update states within individual epochs and view dynamically updating per-neuron equations. We conduct a comparative user study with 31 participants against six established visualization tools and we achieved the highest usability score (SUS 80.97, in the 'excellent' range), with mean rankings of 2.47 for clarity and 2.23 for usefulness (lower is better). Over 70% of participants reported that the visualizations substantially increased their perception of neural network training transparency. The implemented instance is accessible at https://neuroviz.org.

papers.title: DR-SNE: Density-Regularized Stochastic Neighbor Embedding
papers.summary: DR-SNE: Density-Regularized Stochastic Neighbor Embedding attacks a specific failure mode of t-SNE-style visualization: embeddings that keep nearest neighbors intact while badly warping probability mass, inflating some clusters and collapsing others so that visual size no longer reflects sampling density. It challenges the long-standing assumption that dimensionality reduction is primarily a geometry-preservation problem and pivots from optimizing only conditional affinities toward jointly aligning conditional structure and relative density structure. The mechanical shift is simple but consequential: it augments the SNE KL objective with a density regularizer built from kNN-based normalized log-density estimates in both input and embedding spaces, so the model directly penalizes local volume distortion in a scale-invariant way. Unlike DensMAP and DenSNE, which enforce local scale consistency through radii or bandwidth proxies, DR-SNE aligns density statistics themselves, making the control lever interpretable through a single λ tradeoff and a warm-up early-exaggeration schedule. Across Digits, Fashion-MNIST, MNIST, PBMC single-cell RNA-seq, Shuttle, and a synthetic spiral, DR-SNE consistently raises density correlation while keeping trustworthiness competitive, and it improves downstream anomaly detection in density-sensitive regimes such as Thyroid/Shuttle-like settings. The limitation is the expected Pareto frontier: stronger density fidelity gradually weakens cluster sharpness and costs O(n^2) per iteration. This points toward a future line of dimensionality reduction where embeddings are tuned to preserve not just topology, but the statistical shape of data distributions.
papers.abstract: Dimensionality reduction methods such as t-SNE are designed to preserve local neighborhood structure but do not explicitly account for how probability mass is distributed, often leading to distortions of data density. We reformulate dimensionality reduction as the joint alignment of two components: (i) conditional structure, capturing local relationships, and (ii) relative density structure, captured via local density statistics. Based on this perspective, we introduce Density-Regularized SNE (DR-SNE), which augments the stochastic neighbor embedding objective with a density regularization term derived from normalized log-density estimates. Unlike prior approaches such as DensMAP and DenSNE, which rely on local scale consistency, DR-SNE directly aligns normalized density estimates, providing a simple and scale-invariant mechanism for preserving relative density variations. Empirically, DR-SNE improves density preservation while maintaining competitive neighborhood fidelity, and yields gains on density-sensitive tasks such as anomaly detection across multiple datasets.
