These results suggest that incorporating density information complements geometry-focused objectives in dimensionality reduction.

papers.title: Weight Clipping for Robust Conformal Inference under Unbounded Covariate Shifts
papers.summary: Weight Clipping for Robust Conformal Inference under Unbounded Covariate Shifts attacks the failure mode where weighted conformal prediction (WCP) loses coverage once the train-test density ratio is heavy-tailed, unbounded, or itself learned from sparse data: a few extreme calibration points can dominate the weighted score quantile, while unconstrained LSIF overfits the ratio and inflates undercoverage. The paper pivots away from the usual assumption that importance weights should approximate the true ratio as faithfully as possible, and away from post-hoc clipping as a mere numerical hack, toward a bias-controlled clipping layer built into the density-ratio estimation objective. The mechanical shift is clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF): optimize LSIF over the clipped class [0,B], learn a bounded ratio, then feed it into clipped weighted conformal prediction (CWCP) and compensate by inflating the nominal coverage target using an estimated clipping bias term. That bias is estimated directly from data, so the algorithm corrects both finite-sample variance and the systematic loss from truncation. Distinctively, the theory gives the first finite-sample guarantees for weight clipping in conformal inference, including two-sided dataset-conditional coverage bounds whose sample complexity does not blow up with higher moments of the true ratio. On iWildCam, shifted Gaussians, and Communities and Crime, CWCP is more stable than vanilla WCP under heavy tails, with the expected bias-variance tradeoff visible as B varies. This points toward conformal methods that treat robustness as controlled truncation plus explicit calibration, and toward finer shift-aware guarantees such as class-conditional or group-conditional coverage.
papers.abstract: Conformal prediction (CP) provides powerful, distribution-free prediction sets, but its guarantees rely on the exchangeability of training and test data, which is often violated in practice due to covariate shifts. While weighted conformal prediction (WCP) is designed to handle such shifts, it can suffer from significant undercoverage when the density ratio between the distributions is unbounded and/or must be learned. This is because of both overfitting in learning the density ratio, and high variance in estimating the nonconformity score threshold. To address this, we introduce clipped least-squares importance fitting (CLISF) as a reduced-variance method for density ratio estimation. Specifically, we show that density ratios learned using CLISF, when plugged into WCP, have bounded expected undercoverage. Furthermore, we show that the undercoverage can be corrected by running WCP with a slightly inflated coverage target; crucially, we are able to estimate the required level of inflation from the data. We provide the first theoretical guarantees for weight clipping in conformal inference, achieving dataset-conditional coverage with a sample complexity that does not blow up with the higher moments of the true density ratio -- a key limitation of prior work. We verify our results on real-world benchmarks and synthetic data.

papers.title: GETA-3DGS: Automatic Joint Structured Pruning and Quantization for 3D Gaussian Splatting
papers.summary: GETA-3DGS: Automatic Joint Structured Pruning and Quantization for 3D Gaussian Splatting attacks the deployment bottleneck that a single photorealistic 3DGS scene still occupies hundreds of megabytes to gigabytes, making mobile AR/VR and volumetric video infeasible, while prior compression pipelines split pruning, quantization, and entropy coding into separate heuristic stages with opacity thresholds, fixed bit-widths, and SH truncation rules that do not generalize across scenes or let users request a target rate or quality floor. The paper pivots from post-hoc, scene-tuned compression toward a white-box, end-to-end joint optimization directly on raw Gaussian primitives, not on an anchor surrogate. The mechanical shift is a 3DGS-aware GETA adaptation: a quantization-aware dependency graph treats each Gaussian as a pruneable group with five typed attribute subnodes plus SH-degree subnodes; a render-aware saliency replaces parameter-space Taylor scores with transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage; and a heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision quantizer is co-optimized with structural sparsity under projected partial saliency-guided descent and a four-stage warm-up/projection/joint/cool-down schedule. On Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, it reports about 5× storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS with no per-scene thresholds, while showing that uniform 6-bit quantization can cost up to 6.74 dB on view-dependent scenes, validating reverse-water-filling style bit allocation. The main limitation is that the current byte-budget controller is still non-binding and absolute quality trails entropy-coded codecs, but the paper points toward fully automatic, quality-safe 3D scene compression and future co-design with downstream entropy coding and dynamic 4D splatting.
papers.abstract: 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) is a state-of-the-art representation for real-time photorealistic novel-view synthesis, yet a single high-fidelity scene typically occupies hundreds of megabytes to several gigabytes, exceeding the budgets of mobile, immersive, and volumetric video platforms. Existing 3DGS compression methods (e.g., HAC++, FlexGaussian, LP-3DGS) treat pruning, quantization, and entropy coding as separate stages and rely on hand-tuned heuristics (opacity thresholds, fixed bit-widths, SH truncation), limiting cross-scene generalization and preventing users from specifying a target rate or quality budget. We propose GETA-3DGS, to our knowledge the first end-to-end automatic joint structured pruning and quantization framework for 3DGS. Building on GETA for joint pruning-quantization of deep networks, we contribute: (i) a 3DGS-aware quantization-aware dependency graph (QADG) treating each Gaussian primitive as a group with five attribute sub-nodes and degree-aware SH sub-nodes; (ii) a render-aware saliency fusing transmittance-weighted contribution, screen-space gradient, and pixel coverage into a Gaussian-level importance score; and (iii) a heterogeneous per-attribute mixed-precision scheme co-optimized with structural sparsity under a projected partial saliency-guided (PPSG) descent guarantee. On Mip-NeRF 360, Tanks and Temples, and Deep Blending, GETA-3DGS operates directly on raw Gaussian primitives rather than a post-hoc anchor representation, delivering ~5x storage reduction over Vanilla 3DGS with no per-scene thresholds. Bit-width policy is the dominant rate-distortion lever: a uniform 6-bit cap costs up to -6.74 dB on view-dependent scenes versus our heterogeneous allocation, matching an information-theoretic reverse-water-filling analysis we develop. GETA-3DGS is complementary to existing codecs: entropy coding (HAC++, CompGS) is downstream, so the two can be composed.

papers.title: Bridging the Gap Between Average and Discounted TD Learning
papers.summary: Bridging the Gap Between Average and Discounted TD Learning attacks the core bottleneck that average-reward TD policy evaluation is theoretically harder than discounted TD because the Bellman operator is not contractive, so earlier analyses either imposed tabular-excluding assumptions like Φθ≠ce, converged only to sample-path-dependent sets, or paid explicit dimension and quartic-condition-number penalties. The paper pivots away from treating average-reward TD as a messy special case of stochastic approximation and toward a steady-state constrained optimization view: the relative value function must satisfy a projected Bellman equation plus the centering constraint µ⊤W=0, and that structure becomes the control lever. The mechanical shift is a new TD update that resolves the double-sampling issue by sampling from two independent Markov trajectories in each iteration, making the µµ⊤(R+W) term unbiasedly estimable; the resulting expected update is shown, via gradient splitting, to behave like SGD on a composite seminorm objective. A companion single-chain variant replaces the second trajectory with an auxiliary tracker wt≈Φ⊤µ, in the spirit of GTD, but the headline result is the two-chain method: almost sure convergence to a unique deterministic fixed point, uniformly for tabular and linear function approximation, with last-iterate finite-sample rates ˜O(1/T) and sample complexity ˜O(ϵ−1η−2) without explicit d dependence. Experiments on ergodic tabular tasks from OpenAI Gym, MO-Gymnasium, random walks, grid worlds, taxi, deep sea, and fruit trees show improved value-space error. This points toward average-reward RL theory built around constrained operators, multi-trajectory sampling, and discounted-style finite-time guarantees.
papers.abstract: The analysis of Temporal Difference (TD) learning in the average-reward setting faces notable theoretical difficulties because the Bellman operator is not contractive with respect to any norm. This complicates standard analyses of stochastic updates that are effective in discounted settings. Although a considerable body of literature addresses these challenges, existing theoretical approaches come with limitations. We introduce a novel algorithm designed explicitly for policy evaluation in the average-reward setting, utilizing sampling from two Markovian trajectories. Our proposed method overcomes previous limitations by guaranteeing convergence to the unique solution of a properly defined projected Bellman equation. Notably, and in contrast to earlier work, our convergence analysis is uniformly applicable to both linear function approximation and tabular settings and does not involve explicit dimension-dependent terms in its convergence bounds. These results align with what is known to hold in the discounted setting. Furthermore, our algorithm achieves improved dependence on the problem's condition number, reducing the sample complexity from quartic, as in prior literature, to quadratic scaling, and thus matching the efficiency seen in the discounted setting.

papers.title: Sharpness-Aware Pretraining Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting
papers.summary: Sharpness-Aware Pretraining Mitigates Catastrophic Forgetting attacks the mismatch between what pretraining optimizers are normally tuned for—lowest base-model loss—and what actually matters after the model is modified by supervised fine-tuning or quantization: how much capability survives the next parameter shift. The paper challenges the implicit scaling story that a stronger checkpoint is always a better starting point, and pivots from optimizing pretraining performance in isolation toward optimizing the learning–forgetting tradeoff of the eventual downstream model. The mechanical shift is to bias pretraining toward flatter minima, using explicit sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) and two cheaper proxies—higher peak learning rates and shorter learning-rate annealing—that reduce loss curvature and thus parameter sensitivity. Unlike nearby work that treats forgetting as a fine-tuning-time continual-learning problem, this paper moves the control lever upstream into pretraining itself, then evaluates robustness under fixed downstream learning. Across OLMo-style models from 20M to 150M parameters and 4B–192B token budgets, plus a scale check on OLMo-2-1B mid-trained for 50B tokens, the authors show that SAM-trained checkpoints can forget up to 80% less at matched fine-tuning loss, with 31% less forgetting after MetaMath post-training and 40% less loss under 4-bit quantization. Their Hessian analysis links the gains to reduced directional sharpness along the fine-tuning update, not just lower pretraining loss, and the late-annealing-only SAM recipe suggests a practical path toward training checkpoints for adaptability, not just pretraining score.
papers.abstract: Pretraining optimizers are tuned to produce the strongest possible base model, on the assumption that a stronger starting point yields a stronger model after subsequent changes like post-training and quantization. This overlooks the geometry of the base model which controls how much of the base model's capabilities survive subsequent parameter updates. We study three pretraining optimization approaches that bias optimization toward flatter minima: Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM), large learning rates, and shortened learning rate annealing periods. Across model sizes ranging from 20M to 150M parameters, we find that these interventions consistently improve downstream performance after post-training on five common datasets with up to 80% less forgetting. These principles hold at scale: a short SAM mid-training phase applied to an existing OLMo-2-1B checkpoint reduces forgetting by 31% after MetaMath post-training and by 40% after 4-bit quantization.

papers.title: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network I
papers.summary: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network I attacks the bottleneck of turning observed heavy-tailed singular spectra in deep residual networks into a deterministic statement about how layerwise transport can or cannot drift across depth. It challenges the usual heuristic workflow that treats power-law exponents as descriptive statistics of trained weights, and instead pivots from post hoc exponent fitting toward quotient-geometric control of the Jacobian cocycle in O(d)\GL(d) via A↦A^TA and Cartan projection. The mechanical shift is a Frobenius-normalized, trace-d normalized power-law chart G_d(α), where exact spectra form a one-parameter Cartan orbit that is simultaneously a Gibbs family on ranks, a Fisher-information line, and a Bures–Wasserstein curve; the paper then proves slack-aware rigidity inequalities that bound exponent drift by measurable interface radial amplitude, non-backtracking slack, and residual chart variation. Unusually, it separates scalar top-radial control from full-spectrum control, requiring extra Bures/Hellinger residual terms for the latter, so a short exponent path does not imply a good spectral chart. The setting is finite-depth, width-d residual Jacobian chains with exact or approximate power-law singular values, plus effective-rank window bounds for finite-width tails. The main claim is that depth-L transport budgets force exponent drift of order (log M)/L in the zero-slack exact-chart case, with explicit augmentations when slack and residuals are present; this suggests future work on measurable spectral diagnostics, transport budgets, and geometry-aware training constraints for residual and transformer-like architectures.
papers.abstract: Deep residual architectures are modeled as products of near-identity Jacobians. This paper proves deterministic quotient-geometric estimates for singular spectra of Frobenius-normalized layer factors, emphasizing a normalized top-radial Cartan coordinate and fitted power-law chart. Full-rank factors are mapped from $\mathrm{GL}(d)$ to the positive cone by $A\mapsto A^\top A$, then to ordered eigenvalue data. Under Frobenius normalization, exact power-law spectra form a trace-normalized Cartan orbit. This orbit is a Gibbs family on ranks, a Fisher information line, and a Bures--Wasserstein curve with line element $d/4$ times Fisher information. The main rigidity theorem is a slack-aware margin inequality: interface radial amplitude, non-backtracking slack, and signed residual variation control displacement of the fitted Cartan coordinate. In the exact-chart zero-slack case, a depth-$L$ budget gives exponent drift of order $(\log M)/L$; generally, slack and residual increments augment the bound. We separate scalar top-radial from full-Cartan spectral control, which also needs Bures/Hellinger residual variation. We prove approximate-power-law and metric-chart versions, converse lower bounds, Fisher--KL/Bures action estimates, and near-identity expansions for normalized residual chains. Near-identity results verify transport budgets; chart quality remains measurable. Effective rank is a spectral-energy quantile, giving finite-width power-law tail bounds and robust rank-window transition estimates. Empirical static-weight exponent profiles serve as diagnostics; full verification also requires interface budgets, slacks, and residuals for the same operator chain.

papers.title: Detecting Adversarial Data via Provable Adversarial Noise Amplification
papers.summary: Detecting Adversarial Data via Provable Adversarial Noise Amplification attacks a longstanding but unproven defense intuition: that adversarial perturbations stay small in early layers and then grow through deep networks, yielding a detectable layerwise asymmetry, whereas prior detectors like Layer Regression used this effect heuristically without a mathematical guarantee. The paper pivots from empirical “nonuniform impact” stories and auxiliary detection modules toward a provable amplification criterion and a deployable inference-time signal. Its mechanical shift is a formal adversarial noise amplification theorem with sufficient conditions on activations and weight singular values, then a constructive training recipe that replaces ReLU with Leaky ReLU and adds a custom spectral regularizer, −λ∑log σmin(Wi), to increase the guaranteed amplification ratio dn/d1. Detection itself is unusually lightweight: the JPEG-based Amplification Detector sanitizes the input with JPEG compression, measures how much the network amplifies the sanitized-versus-original activation difference, and thresholds that ratio at test time, requiring no clean reference set, no extra detector network, and no adversarial training samples. Experiments span ImageNet-scale models (ResNet-50, Inception-v3, ViT, DeiT), CIFAR-10 WideResNet training, and robustbench models; adversarial examples from PGD, BIM, VMI, VNI, AutoAttack, and a purpose-built adaptive EOT attack show strong amplification for adversarial but not natural corruptions. The main claim is near-perfect AUROC with major resilience to adaptive evasion, suggesting a future direction where adversarial defense is built around provable internal signal amplification rather than post hoc feature comparison.
papers.abstract: The nonuniform and growing impact of adversarial noise across the layers of deep neural networks has been used in the literature, without a formal mathematical justification, to detect adversarial inputs and improve robustness. In this work, we study this phenomenon in detail and present a formal adversarial noise amplification theorem. We specify a set of sufficient conditions under which the adversarial noise amplification is mathematically guaranteed. Based on theoretical observations, we propose a novel training methodology with a custom spectral loss function and a specific architectural design to enhance the amplification signal for detecting adversarial data. Finally, we introduce a new, lightweight detection mechanism that leverages the enhanced amplification signal and operates entirely at inference time. To validate our approach, we demonstrate the detector's efficacy against both state-of-the-art attacks and a purpose-built adaptive attack, confirming that enhanced amplification can serve as a robust and reliable signal for adversarial defense.

papers.title: Adversarial Update-Based Federated Unlearning for Poisoned Model Recovery
papers.summary: Adversarial Update-Based Federated Unlearning for Poisoned Model Recovery attacks the post-detection problem in federated learning where malicious client updates have already contaminated the global model, so simply identifying and removing attackers is not enough; the model must be repaired without paying the full cost of retraining from scratch. The paper explicitly challenges the older recovery story that unlearning is a form of numerical rollback or complete-history replay, which either demands impractical storage or leaves residual poisoning. It pivots from reconstructing the training trajectory toward synthesizing counteracting updates: FAUN keeps only a short window of recent malicious updates, averages them into a representative harmful direction, then uses projected-gradient adversarial optimization on a small clean proxy dataset to amplify the worst-case effect of those updates and turn them into an elimination signal. The mechanical shift is to subtract these adversarially derived updates for only a few unlearning rounds, then hand control back to ordinary benign fine-tuning, so recovery is driven by a min-max elimination step rather than full restart. This is unusual because the server is not trying to approximate past training, but to actively push the poisoned model away from malicious directions while preserving benign progress. On MNIST, CIFAR-10, and AG News under IID and non-IID settings, with CNN, ResNet-18, and FastText models, FAUN matches retraining-level recovery, drives backdoor ASR near zero, and does so in far fewer rounds than FedRecover, FedEraser, or Crab. The work points toward lightweight, adversarially controlled unlearning as a general recovery primitive for federated security.
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) is vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where malicious clients upload manipulated updates to degrade the performance of the global model. Although detection methods can identify and remove malicious clients, the model remains affected. Retraining from scratch is effective but costly, and existing unlearning methods remain unsatisfactory in both effectiveness and efficiency. We propose Federated Adversarial Unlearning (FAUN), a lightweight framework that retains only a short window of malicious clients' updates and employs adversarial optimization on a proxy dataset to derive updates that eliminate malicious directions. Applying these updates for a few unlearning rounds, followed by benign fine-tuning, enables fast removal of malicious effects and stable recovery. Experiments on three canonical datasets show that FAUN achieves recovery comparable to retraining while requiring far fewer rounds and reduces attack success rates to near zero, confirming FAUN successfully eliminates the contributions of unlearned clients.

papers.title: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network II
papers.summary: Geometric and Spectral Alignment for Deep Neural Network II attacks the bottleneck that residual-network “alignment” is usually read from qualitative spectra or blocky heatmaps without a certified link from singular-vector transport to actual channel incidences. It pivots away from treating adjacent-layer similarity as a loose subspace-angle story and toward a deterministic, margin-verified static channel geometry built on residual Jacobian chains. The mechanical shift is to select an effective-rank window, transport the dominant singular subspaces across neighboring layers, then display that transport in physical channel coordinates as a Physical Alignment Matrix that decomposes orthogonally into core + overlap + noise; active-column gaps, pairwise overlap margins, and Frobenius noise bounds then define a static certificate radius under which the full transport and truncated transport preserve the same active supports, incidence graph, SRS sets, hub columns, and core/overlap/noise masks. Unlike nearby work that stops at spectral summaries, this paper explicitly separates source-mode incidence from fully physical input-output channel incidence and adds fitted-tail errors against a Gibbs–Cartan tail model, so the rank window itself becomes a certifiable object. The experiments instantiate these quantities on CNNs, language models, and vision/diffusion backbones, with block-energy heatmaps interpreted as finite-dimensional measurements rather than proof of domain membership. The main implication is that stable structural anatomy in deep nets can be certified at the interface level, pointing toward certificate-driven analysis of adapters, low-rank fine-tuning, and robust monitoring of channel rewiring in large residual models.
papers.abstract: This paper develops the angular and static-channel component of Geometric and Spectral Alignment for residual Jacobian chains. Starting from Cartan-coordinate rigidity and fitted effective-rank windows, we study how dominant singular subspaces are transported across adjacent layers and how the resulting finite matrices can be displayed in physical channel coordinates. The main results are deterministic, margin-verified results. We bound the error between full interface transport and its dominant-window truncation, add fitted-tail errors so that empirical spectra can be certified against the Gibbs--Cartan tail model, and distinguish source-mode incidence from fully physical input-output channel incidence. Given row groups and active supports, the Physical Alignment Matrix decomposes orthogonally as core plus overlap plus noise. Active-column gaps, pairwise overlap margins, and noise bounds combine into a static certificate radius under which the full transport and the truncated transport induce the same active supports, pairwise incidence graph, SRS sets, hub columns, and core/overlap/noise masks. The finer SC/SA/ST labels of the Invariant Channel Mapping require additional row-energy and profile-correlation margins, stated as explicit perturbation tests. The empirical section reports the matrices and block-energy heatmaps that measure these certificate quantities across CNNs, language models, and vision/diffusion backbones. The figures are interpreted as finite-dimensional measurements; complete membership in the Physical GSA certificate domain requires checking the numerical margin protocol stated in Section 10.

papers.title: Statistical Consistency and Generalization of Contrastive Representation Learning
papers.summary: Statistical Consistency and Generalization of Contrastive Representation Learning attacks a central gap in CRL theory: why contrastive pretraining, the backbone of CLIP-like foundation models, empirically benefits from very large negative sets even though prior bounds worsened with more negatives, and whether minimizing contrastive loss actually converges to optimal retrieval rather than only lowering surrogate gap. The paper pivots away from classification-style downstream analysis and from outer-only generalization arguments toward a retrieval-first, ranking-theoretic view: it measures downstream quality by an AUC-type population criterion over triples (x,y,y′), proves contrastive loss is statistically consistent with optimal ranking, and derives a calibration-style inequality linking excess contrastive risk to excess retrieval suboptimality. The mechanical shift is a compositional inner/outer decomposition of the log-sum-exp objective: the outer term tracks n anchor pairs, while the inner term isolates finite-negative sampling bias. That split enables sharper bounds via different tools in the two regimes—algorithmic stability for supervised CRL with independently sampled negatives, giving O(1/m + 1/√n), and uniform convergence for self-supervised CRL with shared negatives, giving O(1/√m + 1/√n). This is unusual because it makes negative-count scaling a first-class object rather than a nuisance. Experiments on large-scale vision-language CLIP training with DFN-sampled anchors and shared negative pools validate the predicted saturation and trade-off between m and n on zero-shot ImageNet and retrieval benchmarks. The paper points toward theory for optimizing negative sampling budgets and extending statistical consistency analysis to other foundation-model pretraining regimes, including more general OCE losses and possibly LLMs.
papers.abstract: Contrastive representation learning (CRL) underpins many modern foundation models. Despite recent theoretical progress, existing analyses suffer from several key limitations: (i) the statistical consistency of CRL remains poorly understood; (ii) available generalization bounds deteriorate as the number of negative samples increases, contradicting the empirical benefits of large negative sets; and (iii) the retrieval performance of CRL has received limited theoretical attention. In this paper, we develop a unified statistical learning theory for CRL. For downstream tasks, we evaluate retrieval quality using an AUC-type population criterion and show that the contrastive loss is \emph{statistically consistent} with optimal ranking. We further establish a \emph{calibration-style inequality} that quantitatively relates excess contrastive risk to excess retrieval suboptimality. For upstream training, we study both supervised and self-supervised contrastive objectives and derive generalization bounds of order $O(1/m + 1/\sqrt{n})$ and $O(1/\sqrt{m} + 1/\sqrt{n})$, respectively, where $m$ denotes the number of negative samples and $n$ the number of anchor points. These bounds not only explain the empirical advantages of large negative sets but also reveal an explicit trade-off between $m$ and $n$. Extensive experiments on large-scale vision--language models corroborate our theoretical predictions.

papers.title: STABLEVAL: Disagreement-Aware and Stable Evaluation of AI Systems
papers.summary: STABLEVAL: Disagreement-Aware and Stable Evaluation of AI Systems attacks the brittle part of modern benchmarking: human-judged system rankings that swing when annotator subsets, strictness, or adversarial raters change, because majority vote treats disagreement as disposable noise. The paper pivots from the older goal of recovering a single “true” label toward the newer goal of preserving uncertainty while producing reproducible agent comparisons, making ranking stability a first-class objective rather than an afterthought. The mechanical shift is a Bayesian latent-variable model with item-level latent correctness and annotator-specific confusion matrices, estimated by EM with Dirichlet priors, that outputs posterior expected item credit instead of hard labels; agent scores are then averages of this credit, with bootstrap confidence intervals and Kendall’s tau used to quantify stability under annotator subsampling. Unlike Dawid-Skene-style denoising, it explicitly refuses to collapse disagreement into consensus, because label recovery and evaluation stability are not the same target. Across synthetic stress tests varying adversarial, strict, lenient, and hard-item regimes, plus real benchmarks including MT-Bench (6,712 judgments from 65 annotators), ConvAbuse, QAGS, and MSLR, STABLEVAL/PEC is more stable than majority vote and often more robust than hard Dawid-Skene rankings, especially in high-disagreement settings; its tradeoff is that it can lose to Dawid-Skene on raw latent-label MSE and needs enough annotation density. This points toward disagreement-aware benchmarking, annotator modeling, and uncertainty-calibrated evaluation pipelines for both human and LLM judges.
papers.abstract: Human evaluation remains the primary standard for assessing modern AI systems, yet annotator disagreement, bias, and variability make system rankings fragile under standard majority vote aggregation. Majority vote discards annotator reliability and item-level ambiguity, often yielding unstable comparisons across annotator subsets. We introduce STABLEVAL, a disagreement-aware evaluation framework that models latent item correctness and annotator-specific confusion patterns to produce posterior expected item credit and calibrated agent-level scores. Unlike label-denoising approaches such as Dawid-Skene, STABLEVAL is explicitly designed for stable and uncertainty-aware system evaluation rather than hard label recovery. We formalize ranking stability as a first-class evaluation objective and analyze how aggregation methods preserve or distort underlying annotator behavior. Across controlled synthetic experiments and multiple real-world human-annotated benchmarks, majority vote exhibits increasing score error and ranking instability under annotator heterogeneity and adversarial noise, while STABLEVAL yields more stable and statistically grounded system rankings. These results demonstrate that modeling disagreement is essential for robust and reproducible AI evaluation.

papers.title: Boundary Mass and the Soft-to-Hard Limit in Mixture-of-Experts
papers.summary: Boundary Mass and the Soft-to-Hard Limit in Mixture-of-Experts studies the singular low-temperature limit that standard MoE intuition glosses over: softmax routing does not converge uniformly to hard routing because tie neighborhoods create a boundary layer where expert weights remain mixed even as temperature τ→0. The paper pivots from treating soft-to-hard as a benign annealing story toward a geometric singular-perturbation problem at the population level for squared-loss MoE regression. Its central mechanical shift is to replace global approximation arguments with a boundary-mass calculus: the probability that the top two router logits lie within an O(τ) margin controls the entire discrepancy. Under smoothness and transversality assumptions, coarea/tube formulas show this mass scales linearly in slab width, with the binary leading constant given by a surface integral over the routing interface. That yields quantitative O(τ) soft-to-hard risk bounds and, under compactness and uniform margin tails, Γ-convergence of soft objectives to the hard-routing objective. Unlike nearby MoE theory focused on identifiability or optimization heuristics, this work isolates the thin geometric interface as the bottleneck. It then uses that boundary-layer control in a teacher–student transfer theorem: favorable curvature and identifiability of the profiled hard-routing landscape can carry over to soft routing, including approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior away from teacher-equivalent partitions. A reduced two-expert Gaussian calculation further exhibits local symmetry breaking aligned with the teacher separator. This points toward future MoE theory where routing geometry, not just capacity or sparsity, becomes the key design variable.
papers.abstract: Softmax-routed mixture-of-experts models approach hard routing as the temperature tends to zero, but this limit is singular near routing ties. This paper studies that singularity at the population level for squared-loss MoE regression. The central object is the \emph{boundary mass}, namely the probability that the top two router scores are separated by only a small margin. Under smoothness and transversality assumptions on the router and input law, we prove coarea/tube estimates showing that this mass is linear in the slab width, with leading constant given by a surface integral over the routing interface in the binary case. These estimates yield quantitative soft-to-hard risk bounds and, under compactness and uniform margin control, $Γ$-convergence of the soft objectives to the hard-routing objective. The main conclusion is that the zero-temperature limit is controlled by a thin geometric layer around routing interfaces, not by the full input space. We then use this geometric core in two more model-dependent directions. In a teacher--student setting, we prove a conditional landscape-transfer principle showing that, when the profiled hard-routing problem has favorable identifiability and curvature and the relevant derivatives transfer at boundary-layer scale, small-temperature soft routing inherits approximate teacher recovery and strict-saddle behavior away from teacher-equivalent partitions. We also give a reduced two-expert Gaussian calculation that illustrates a local symmetry-breaking mechanism aligned with the teacher separator.

papers.title: LUMINA: A Grid Foundation Model for Benchmarking AC Optimal Power Flow Surrogate Learning
papers.summary: LUMINA: A Grid Foundation Model for Benchmarking AC Optimal Power Flow Surrogate Learning attacks a deployment bottleneck in ACOPF surrogates: models trained on one grid often look accurate in-distribution yet collapse when the network topology changes, precisely where operators need fast what-if analysis on unseen systems. The paper challenges the old workflow of reporting only single-topology MSE and implicit feasibility, and pivots from “predict the optimal solution” toward “learn topology-transferable, constraint-aware grid surrogates” under standardized pretraining, transfer, and adaptation tasks. The mechanical shift is LUMINA-Bench, built on OPFData with five representative cases (30, 57, 118, 500, 2000 buses), fixed splits, matched hyperparameter budgets, and unified evaluation that combines OPF solution error with topology-normalized power-balance and line-limit violations. It benchmarks homogeneous GCN/GAT/GIN/Graph Transformer models against heterogeneous RGAT/HeteroGNN/HGT/HEAT backbones, and directly compares MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian losses. What is distinctive is that the benchmark treats feasibility as a first-class objective and explicitly studies multi-topology pretraining and zero-shot transfer rather than isolated accuracy. The main finding is that heterogeneous models, especially HGT with AL, give the best accuracy-feasibility tradeoff and transfer robustness, while Transformers can be strong on small single-topology cases but degrade under shift; pretraining also cuts fine-tuning steps dramatically on large grids. The paper points toward foundation-style, feasibility-aware power-grid models, but also flags synthetic-data limits and residual failures around topologically complex, high-degree regions.
papers.abstract: AC optimal power flow (ACOPF) is foundational yet computationally expensive in power grid operations, driving learning-based surrogates for large-scale grid analysis. These surrogates, however, often fail to generalize across network topologies, a critical gap for deployment on grids not seen during training and for routine operational what-if studies. We introduce LUMINA-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark suite for ACOPF surrogate learning covering multi-topology pretraining, transfer, and adaptation. The benchmark evaluates homogeneous and heterogeneous architectures under single- and multi-topology learning settings using unified metrics that capture both predictive accuracy and physics-informed constraint violations. We additionally compare constraint-aware training objectives, including MSE, augmented Lagrangian, and violation-based Lagrangian losses, to characterize accuracy-robustness trade-offs across settings. Data processing, training, and evaluation frameworks are open-sourced as the LUMINA suite to support reproducibility and accelerate future research on feasibility-aware OPF surrogates.

papers.title: On the Optimal Sample Complexity of Offline Multi-Armed Bandits with KL Regularization
papers.summary: On the Optimal Sample Complexity of Offline Multi-Armed Bandits with KL Regularization attacks the open statistical question behind offline decision-making with reverse KL constraints: when does the regularizer really buy a fast O(ε^{-1}) rate, and when does the problem revert to ordinary O(ε^{-2}) offline bandit hardness? The paper challenges the earlier workflow of analyzing KL-regularized learning through broad function-approximation bounds and loose coverage notions, showing that in the tabular contextual MAB setting the right notion is single-policy concentrability at π*, not all-policy uniform coverage. The pivot is from treating KL as an always-fast stabilizer toward a phase-transition view indexed by regularization strength η: large regularization makes the objective curvature-dominated and rewards must be estimated across all supported arms, while small regularization makes the problem behave like standard best-arm identification. Mechanically, the paper sharpens KL-PCB by using tabular pessimistic reward estimates b(s,a)=\bar r-bonus and the closed-form KL policy bπ(·|s)∝πref(·|s)exp(η br(s,·)), then proves matching upper and lower bounds over both regimes. The unusual step is the lower-bound construction for the small-η regime: it leverages multiple optimal arms plus a novel pairing-and-counting argument to overcome the failure of standard Le Cam/Fano two-point methods to capture A-dependence. The result is a near-complete characterization, and it points toward extending KL-regularized sample-complexity theory to linear and general function approximation, where these phase transitions may persist but with richer geometry.
papers.abstract: Kullback-Leibler (KL) regularization is widely used in offline decision-making and offers several benefits, motivating recent work on the sample complexity of offline learning with respect to KL-regularized performance metrics. Nevertheless, the exact sample complexity of KL-regularized offline learning remains largely from fully characterized. In this paper, we study this question in the setting of multi-armed bandits (MABs). We provide a sharp analysis of KL-PCB (Zhao et al., 2026), showing that it achieves a sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(ηSAC^{π^*}/ε)$ under large regularization $η= \tilde{O}(ε^{-1})$, and a sample complexity of $\tildeΩ(SAC^{π^*}/ε^2)$ under small regularization $η= \tildeΩ(ε^{-1})$, where $η$ is the regularization parameter, $S$ is the number of contexts, $A$ is the number of arms, $C^{π^*}$ policy coverage coefficient at the optimal policy $π^*$, $ε$ is the desired sub-optimality, and $\tilde{O}$ and $\tildeΩ$ hide all poly-logarithmic factors. We further provide a pair of sharper sample complexity lower bounds, which matches the upper bounds over the entire range of regularization strengths. Overall, our results provide a nearly complete characterization of offline multi-armed bandits with KL regularization.

papers.title: Personalized Federated Learning for Gradient Alignment
papers.summary: Personalized Federated Learning for Gradient Alignment attacks the core pFL failure mode in heterogeneous, data-sparse clients: local updates are dominated by high-variance gradients, and then server aggregation further bends each client away from its own personalized optimum. It challenges the implicit assumption behind many FL optimizers and LoRA-based personalization schemes that stabilizing the shared model is enough; here the bottleneck is preserving client-specific descent directions through both local training and aggregation. The pivot is from global-drift control toward direction-preserving optimization: instead of only regularizing around the server model or separating adapters, the paper turns gradient alignment itself into the control lever for personalization. The mechanical shift in pFLAlign is two-stage: a client-specific multiplicative preconditioner adaptively rescales coordinates to suppress noisy directions while keeping reliable personalized signals, and an aggregation-robust personalization step reinitializes each round from a client offset and applies a probabilistically gated correction term that shrinks only those components likely misaligned with the client’s accumulated direction. Unusually, this is not just an optimizer heuristic; it is derived from PAC-Bayesian analyses that interpret preconditioner posterior updates and KL-based shrinkage as principled information-preservation mechanisms. On federated instruction tuning with LLaMA-3.2 and OPT-1.3B over Dolly-15K and FLAN under task-level heterogeneity, pFLAlign improves personalization and training stability, with GSNR and loss curves showing stronger alignment than FedProx, SCAFFOLD, FedDyn, and LoRA-based baselines. The paper points toward pFL research that treats optimization geometry, not only model architecture, as the main carrier of personalization.
papers.abstract: Personalized federated learning (pFL) aims to adapt models to client specific data distributions, yet it often fails to reliably preserve personalized information. Local training is hindered by high variance gradients induced by limited and heterogeneous client data, while aggregation further distorts client specific optimization directions. To address these challenges, we propose pFLAlign, a gradient alignment framework to maintain client specific information during both local training and aggregation. pFLAlign consists of two complementary mechanisms: one adapts local gradient directions to reduce variance during client side optimization, and the other mitigates aggregation induced distortion by realigning the global model with each client's personalized direction. Theoretically, we derive pFLAlign from a PAC Bayesian analysis, which reveals how personalized gradient alignment preserves client specific information. Our experiments and ablation studies show that pFLAlign consistently improves personalization performance and training stability, achieving state of the art results.

papers.title: Projection-Free Transformers via Gaussian Kernel Attention
papers.summary: Projection-Free Transformers via Gaussian Kernel Attention attacks the assumption that Transformer token mixing needs learned query, key, and value projections to define useful similarity geometry, asking instead whether attention can be reduced to a similarity-based diffusion operator over the residual stream. The pivot is away from projection-heavy softmax dot-product attention and toward a normalized Gaussian RBF kernel that computes affinities directly on per-head features, with each head learning only a bandwidth σh plus the standard output projection WO. Mechanically, GKA replaces QK⊤/√d with row-normalized Gaussian kernel regression over tokens, so attention becomes one step of feature-space smoothing on a fully connected similarity graph; in language modeling, causal and sliding-window constraints are enforced by masking and renormalizing the kernel rather than changing the block structure. This is unusual relative to nearby kernelized or efficient-attention work because it removes WQ, WK, WV entirely instead of approximating softmax while preserving projection-based similarity. Evaluated in DeiT-style ViTs on ImageNet-1K and in nanochat GPT-style depth-20 autoregressive models on FineWeb-Edu, GKA cuts attention parameters by about 75% and total compute by about 21–24% in vision, and reaches 0.42× parameters and 0.49× training FLOPs in language, with stable training and near-zero train-validation gap but modestly worse BPB at that scale. The main limitation is a wall-clock and memory penalty from exact N×N kernel materialization, pointing future research toward fused Gaussian attention kernels, compute-matched scaling, and hybrid projection-free/projected designs.
papers.abstract: Self-attention in Transformers is typically implemented as $\mathrm{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d})V$, where $Q=XW_Q$, $K=XW_K$, and $V=XW_V$ are learned linear projections of the input $X$. We ask whether these learned projections are necessary, or whether they can be replaced by a simpler similarity-based diffusion operator. We introduce \textbf{Gaussian Kernel Attention} (GKA), a drop-in replacement for dot-product attention that computes token affinities directly using a Gaussian radial basis function (RBF) kernel applied to per-head token features. Each head learns only a bandwidth parameter $σ_h$, while a single output projection $W_O$ preserves compatibility with the standard Transformer interface. GKA can be interpreted as normalized kernel regression over tokens, linking modern Transformer architectures to classical non-local filtering and kernel smoothing methods. We evaluate GKA in both vision and language modeling settings. For autoregressive language modeling within the \texttt{nanochat} framework, we implement causal masking and sliding-window constraints by masking and renormalizing the Gaussian kernel. At depth 20, a GKA model with $0.42\times$ the parameters and $0.49\times$ the total training FLOPs of a standard attention baseline trains stably, exhibits a near-zero train-validation gap, and demonstrates competitive behavior on standard benchmarks, albeit with higher bits-per-byte (BPB) at this compute scale. Overall, GKA provides a minimal, interpretable attention mechanism with an explicit locality scale, offering a dimension in the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for Transformer design.

papers.title: Combining Trained Models in Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Combining Trained Models in Reinforcement Learning attacks a practical bottleneck in DRL: agents are sample-hungry, brittle under task shift, and often expected to benefit from pretrained knowledge reuse without strong evidence that the gains are real or compute-fair. The paper pivots from the usual algorithmic story of “transfer, distill, or ensemble and you will improve” toward a literature-auditing question: under what source-target conditions does combining trained models actually beat a target-task agent trained from scratch? Its core mechanical shift is not a new controller but a PRISMA-guided systematic review that compresses 589 retrieved records down to 15 empirical studies, then codes them around source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and baseline fairness. That lens is unusual in nearby RL work because it treats gating, alignment, and compute-matched comparison as the real levers, not just reuse itself. The synthesis shows positive results cluster where source and target share substantial structure or where the method explicitly weights, gates, or aligns transferred knowledge; broad claims for ensembles and federated aggregation remain thin because evidence is sparse and mostly narrow. A provisional “independence spectrum” — seed, data, task, reward diversity — is proposed as a hypothesis for future benchmarking rather than a validated metric. The main implication is that the field should move from informal reuse claims toward benchmark suites with fixed targets, controlled overlap, and charged pretraining cost.
papers.abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has delivered strong results in domains such as Atari and Go, but it still suffers from high sample cost and weak transfer beyond the training setting. A common response is to reuse information from previously trained models through transfer, distillation, ensemble methods, or federated training instead of learning each target task from random initialization. The literature on these mechanisms is fragmented, and published comparisons are hard to interpret because tasks, baselines, and compute budgets differ. This paper presents a PRISMA-guided systematic review of empirical studies on pretrained knowledge reuse in DRL. Starting from 589 records retrieved from IEEE Xplore, the ACM Digital Library, and citation tracing, we screened 570 unique records and assessed 89 full texts. After applying the final eligibility criteria, 15 empirical studies remained in the main synthesis. We analyzed them qualitatively across three factors: source-target similarity, diversity among reused models, and the fairness of comparisons against from-scratch baselines. Three patterns recur across the surviving corpus. First, positive results are concentrated in settings where source and target tasks share substantial structure or where the method includes an explicit gating or alignment mechanism. Second, evidence for ensembles and federated aggregation is promising but sparse and mostly limited to narrow settings. Third, compute-matched comparisons are rare, which weakens claims about efficiency gains over stronger single-agent baselines. The paper contributes a narrower and internally consistent review scope, a study-level synthesis of empirical evidence, and a provisional independence spectrum that should be treated as a hypothesis for future benchmarking rather than a validated metric.

papers.title: Experience Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Large-scale UAV Teams in Hazardous Environments
papers.summary: Experience Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning for Large-scale UAV Teams in Hazardous Environments attacks the failure mode where federated RL inherits the wrong intuition from data-rich FL: in hazardous UAV missions, more participating learners do not create more training signal because safe experience is rate-limited by energy, mission length, and crash risk. The paper pivots from treating participation as a scaling lever toward treating replay structure and exposure as the bottleneck, arguing that under fixed experience budgets the key question is not how many learners aggregate, but which transitions are repeatedly reused and whether semantically critical “key experiences” reach the loss-dominant part of training. The mechanical shift is EC-HFRL: UAVs are organized into clusters that act as federated agents, with only a subset generating new trajectories while multiple intra-cluster learners sample from a shared cluster replay pool; standard FedAvg remains, but the analysis centers on how participation K and minibatch size b compress replay diversity. Unusually, the paper introduces analytically grounded diagnostics—Union Coverage Ratio, Key Enrichment Ratio, and Key TD Contribution—to show when prioritized replay surfaces task-relevant transitions versus merely amplifying redundant gradients. Experiments on 3D chemical leak detection and wildfire interception show minibatch structure dominates performance and that higher participation mainly increases replay reuse and energy cost, while success correlates more with key-experience admission than aggregation intensity. The result suggests a future direction of experience-budget-aware federated RL, where sampling, replay, and energy are co-designed around learning-signal composition rather than client count.
papers.abstract: Conventional federated learning assumes that greater learner participation improves training performance, by leveraging abundant, independently generated local data. However, in federated reinforcement learning (FRL) for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) teams in hazardous environments where experience generation is severely constrained by safety considerations, energy limitations, and mission duration, this assumption may break. This work introduces Experience-Constrained Hierarchical Federated Reinforcement Learning (EC-HFRL), a framework in which clusters act as federated learning agents, while multiple intra-cluster learners represent parallel learning resources that reuse a shared experience pool. We show that increasing participation does not necessarily improve learning performance. Instead, learning performance is strongly associated with experience reuse strategy and the dominance of key analytically identified gradient transition experiences within a cluster. In particular, minibatch size primarily determines effective replay exposure, while higher intra-cluster participation increases reuse level. Empirical results demonstrate that the performance regimes are strongly associated with the structure of the learning signal, rather than federated aggregation effects, clarifying the limited and secondary role of learner participation in experience-constrained FRL.

papers.title: Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients for Reliable Feature Attribution
papers.summary: Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients for Reliable Feature Attribution attacks a specific failure mode in path-based explanations: Integrated Gradients and even Guided IG can accumulate spurious gradients when the baseline-to-input trajectory cuts through noisy, low-density regions, so the resulting attribution is mathematically complete but semantically unfaithful. The paper pivots from input-space path engineering and latent interpolation methods that ignore the classifier’s logit surface toward a manifold-constrained attribution loop in which guidance happens in the latent space of a pre-trained VAE, then gets decoded back into input space. The mechanical shift is precise: at each step it computes latent gradients via the decoder Jacobian, selects the lowest-gradient latent dimensions, performs sparse axis-aligned updates in z, decodes intermediate states, and integrates gradients along that decoded trajectory. This is unusual because it couples Guided IG’s noise-avoidance objective with an explicit geometric prior: decoder-induced updates are argued to lie in the data manifold’s tangent structure, whereas input-space sparse updates drift off-manifold. Experiments on ImageNet, Oxford-IIIT Pet, and Oxford 102 Flower with VGG16, ResNet18, and InceptionV1, plus several VAEs (MAR, Stable Diffusion, Kandinsky), show improved DiffID, insertion, deletion, and lower LPIPS/path drift versus IG, GIG, EIG, and MIG. The main limitation is dependence on VAE domain alignment and the fact that insertion still trails some mask/localization methods, suggesting a future line of manifold-aware, multi-path, or decoder-diagnostic attribution methods.
papers.abstract: Feature attribution is central to diagnosing and trusting deep neural networks, and Integrated Gradients (IG) is widely used due to its axiomatic properties. However, IG can yield unreliable explanations when the integration path between a baseline and the input passes through regions with noisy gradients. While Guided Integrated Gradients reduces this sensitivity by adaptively updating low-gradient-magnitude features, input-space guidance still produces intermediate inputs that deviate from the data manifold. To address this limitation, we propose \emph{Manifold-Aligned Guided Integrated Gradients} (MA-GIG), which constructs attribution paths in the latent space of a pre-trained variational autoencoder. By decoding intermediate latent states, MA-GIG biases the path toward the learned generative manifold and reduces exposure to implausible input-space regions. Through qualitative and quantitative evaluations, we demonstrate that MA-GIG produces faithful explanations by aggregating gradients on path features proximal to the input. Consequently, our method reduces off-manifold noise and outperforms prior path-based attribution methods across multiple datasets and classifiers. Our code is available at https://github.com/leekwoon/ma-gig/.

papers.title: Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training for Long-Tailed Robustness via Geometric Alignment
papers.summary: Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training for Long-Tailed Robustness via Geometric Alignment attacks a specific failure mode of adversarial training: on long-tailed data, head-class-dominated min–max optimization produces collapsed tail margins, unstable boundaries, and adversarial examples that drift into semantically unsupported low-density regions. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that stronger PGD-style optimization alone suffices if one simply reweights losses or stages training; instead it pivots from loss-level balancing toward representation-space geometry and semantic support. The core mechanical shift is MCAT, a unified objective that replaces unconstrained inner maximization with manifold-supported PGD, adding a penalty for moving adversarial features away from class-conditional semantic manifolds approximated by frozen per-class generators in feature space, while simultaneously regularizing classifier weights toward a simplex ETF structure to equalize angular separation across classes. This is unusual relative to nearby long-tailed robustness work because it couples off-manifold control with explicit balanced geometry, rather than only reweighting, distilling, or adjusting margins at the decision level. On CIFAR-10-LT, CIFAR-100-LT, and Tiny-ImageNet-LT under ℓ∞ attacks and imbalance ratios up to 100, MCAT improves clean, PGD-20, AutoAttack, balanced, and tail-class robustness, with particularly large gains on tail classes. The theoretical contribution links minimum inter-class angle to robust margin lower bounds and shows manifold-constrained adversarial risk upper-bounds robust risk on high-density semantic regions. The limitation is an added generator-and-regularizer pipeline, but the direction is clear: robustness research is moving toward geometry-aware, support-aware adversarial training that treats class imbalance as a representation-shaping problem, not just an optimization imbalance.
papers.abstract: Adversarial training is effective on balanced datasets, but its robustness degrades under longtailed class distributions, where tail classes suffer high robust error and unstable decision boundaries. We propose Manifold-Constrained Adversarial Training (MCAT), a unified framework that enforces the semantic validity of adversarial examples by penalizing deviations from class-conditional manifolds in feature space, while promoting balanced geometric separation across classes via an ETF-inspired regularization. We provide theoretical results that link geometric separation to lower bounds on adversarially robust margins, and show that manifold-constrained adversarial risk upperbounds robust risk on high-density semantic regions. Extensive experiments on standard longtailed benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in overall, balanced, and tail-class adversarial robustness.

papers.title: KANs need curvature: penalties for compositional smoothness
papers.summary: KANs need curvature: penalties for compositional smoothness attacks a specific interpretability bottleneck in Kolmogorov-Arnold networks: well-fitting KANs often learn jagged, high-curvature activation functions that are impossible to read as scientific components, and the standard KAN penalty only shrinks activation magnitudes and concentrates mass on a few edges, so it cannot suppress oscillation. The paper pivots away from magnitude/sparsity-based regularization and away from relying on low-resolution grid extension as an implicit smoothness prior, toward an explicit smoothness control lever: an edge-wise, basis-agnostic bending-energy penalty on each learned univariate activation, implemented for cubic B-splines as a second-difference norm plus a SiLU curvature term, with cross terms dropped to avoid optimizer gaming. The mechanical shift is compositional: by working through the KAN chain rule, the paper shows layer Hessians are diagonal and derives a closed-form upper bound tying full-network curvature to the coefficient-space penalty, then uses that structure to motivate a richer path-weighted variant that reweights each edge by its expected upstream/downstream influence. Experiments on 2D function approximation, the Feynman symbolic-regression benchmark, high-grid overparameterized regimes, and a FastKAN RBF variant show substantially smoother activations at similar or better accuracy, improved stability under Adam and L-BFGS, and elimination of catastrophic failures at fixed high resolution. The result points toward smoothness-aware scientific machine learning where interpretability is enforced by compositional priors rather than post hoc visualization.
papers.abstract: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) offer a potent combination of accuracy and interpretability, thanks to their compositions of learnable univariate activation functions. However, the activations of well-fitting KANs tend to exhibit pathologically high-curvature oscillations, making them difficult to interpret, and standard regularization penalties do not prevent this. Here we derive a basis-agnostic curvature penalty and show that penalized models can maintain accuracy while achieving substantially smoother activations. Accounting for how function composition shapes curvature, we prove an upper bound on the full model's curvature relative to the curvature penalty, and use this to motivate richer forms of penalties. Scientific machine learning is increasingly bottlenecked by the trade-off between accuracy and interpretability. Results such as ours that improve interpretability without sacrificing accuracy will further strengthen KANs as a practical tool for both prediction and insight.

papers.title: DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning
papers.summary: DurableUn: Quantization-Induced Recovery Attacks in Machine Unlearning attacks the deployment gap in unlearning: models can pass BF16 privacy audits yet resurrect forgotten data after the routine move to low-bit inference. The paper pivots the field from treating forgetting as a static BF16 training outcome toward treating it as a precision-dependent property that must survive INT4 deployment, challenging the implicit assumption that unlearning metrics are invariant to quantization. The mechanical shift is adapter-space INT4 perturbation in the NF4+LoRA regime, evaluated on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU for seven unlearning methods; the authors introduce Q-INT4 as the missing robustness metric and show INT8 is benign while INT4 can amplify recovery by up to 22×, with harder datasets worsening the effect. Nearby unlearning work is differentiated by its focus on the LoRA adapter weight space rather than merged-weight PTQ, and by the discovery of an FA–RA–Q-INT4 trilemma: no baseline simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, preserved retain accuracy, and quantization robustness. To counter this, DURABLEUN-SAF adds a quantization-aware forgetting loss, backpropagating through INT4 rounding with a Straight-Through Estimator and warmup, effectively flattening the forget landscape under quantization. The result is a sharp phase transition: once robustness appears, retain accuracy collapses and cannot be tuned back. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method to obtain a stable 3/3 empirical durability certificate, pointing toward quantization-aware unlearning protocols, precision-adaptive certificates, and future selective robustness control in deployment-native privacy evaluation.
papers.abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove specified training data to satisfy privacy regulations such as GDPR. However, existing evaluations assume identical precision at unlearning and deployment, overlooking that production LLMs are deployed at low-bit precision. We show that INT4 quantization systematically restores forgotten content even when models pass compliance audits at bfloat16 (BF16), we term this the quantization recovery attack (QRA). We conduct the first systematic study of unlearning robustness under adapter-space INT4 quantization in the NF4+LoRA regime, evaluating seven methods on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct across TOFU, MUSE-News, and WikiBio-WPU. INT8 is benign; INT4 induces recovery of up to 22x, worsening with dataset difficulty. We identify the FA-RA-Q-INT4 trilemma: no method simultaneously achieves strong forgetting, high utility, and quantization robustness. A dense Pareto sweep reveals a sharp phase transition once robustness is achieved, retaining accuracy collapses regardless of further tuning. To address this, we propose DURABLEUN-SAF (Sharpness-Aware Forgetting), a quantization-aware objective using Straight-Through Estimator gradients through INT4 rounding. DURABLEUN-SAF is the only method to achieve a stable empirical (0.047, {BF16, INT8, INT4})- durability certificate: Q-INT4= 0.043 +- 0.002, cert rate= 3/3, versus SalUn's cert rate= 1/3 at its own published hyperparameters. We call for Q-INT4 to be adopted as a standard evaluation metric alongside FA and RA.

papers.title: Demographic-Aware Transfer Learning for Sleep Stage Classification in Clinical Polysomnography
papers.summary: Demographic-Aware Transfer Learning for Sleep Stage Classification in Clinical Polysomnography attacks the failure mode of treating sleep staging as if one population-agnostic model could generalize uniformly across patients, despite known shifts in EEG morphology and sleep architecture with sex, age, and obstructive sleep apnea severity. The paper pivots from the usual workflow of cross-dataset/domain adaptation toward within-cohort personalization: demographics become the stratification signal that defines what gets fine-tuned, not just what gets reported. The mechanical shift is a two-stage transfer pipeline in which a CNN+BiLSTM is first pre-trained on the full DREAMT clinical PSG cohort (100 subjects, 7 channels, 30-second AASM epochs), then reloaded and fine-tuned separately for subgroup slices defined by gender, age bands, AHI severity, and their pairwise combinations, with strict fold separation to prevent leakage and a reduced learning rate to preserve cohort-level representations. Unlike nearby sleep-staging work that chases a single stronger backbone or broader pretraining, this approach asks whether clinically meaningful demographic structure can be exploited as an optimization target. Across 37 subgroup configurations, 35 beat the baseline, with Cohen’s kappa gains of 0.9–12.9%; the strongest results come from M-Mild and under-50 Normal, while very small or severely fragmented subgroups sometimes degrade, revealing a tradeoff between personalization and subgroup size. The result points toward model banks indexed by clinical metadata and toward personalized sleep analytics that adapt to patient phenotype rather than averaging it away.
papers.abstract: Automated sleep stage classification typically employs a single population-agnostic model, disregarding established demographic variations in sleep architecture. Sleep patterns, however, differ substantially across gender, age, and obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) severity, indicating that a onesize-fits all approach may be suboptimal for diverse clinical populations. In this paper, we propose a two stage training strategy based on demographic stratification and transfer learning framework. We first pretrains a convolutional recurrent model on the full population and then fine tunes it independently for demographic subgroups defined by gender, age, and Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI) severity according to the AASM clinical standard. Using the DREAMT dataset comprising 100 clinical subjects and 7 PSG channels, we evaluate 37 fine-tuned configurations across single-axis and two-way demographic combinations. Results demonstrate that 35 of the 37 fine-tuned models outperform the baseline, with Cohen's kappa improvements ranging from 0.9 to 12.9%. These findings indicate that stratified fine tuning tailored to specific patient demographics yields substantially more accurate sleep staging than a single generalized model, offering a practical and clinically grounded paradigm for personalized sleep assessment.

papers.title: Break the Block: Dynamic-size Reasoning Blocks for Diffusion Large Language Models via Monotonic Entropy Descent with Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Break the Block: Dynamic-size Reasoning Blocks for Diffusion Large Language Models via Monotonic Entropy Descent with Reinforcement Learning attacks the bottleneck that fixed-size semi-autoregressive blocks in dLLMs break logical flow, force a one-size-fits-all decoding granularity across tasks, and produce unstable block-wise entropy when reasoning goes wrong. The paper pivots from static block partitioning and inference-only heuristics toward learning semantic step boundaries during post-training, arguing that reasoning should not be chopped by a constant block length but should expand or contract around each complete inference step. The mechanical shift is a plug-and-play RL layer, b1, that adds a learned block-ending indicator token τ_end and trains it with a Monotonic Entropy Descent reward: adjacent blocks are encouraged to show strictly decreasing block entropy, with a local pairwise surrogate that is theoretically equivalent to maximizing negative Spearman rank correlation over entropy ranks, plus an indicator reward to prevent degenerate short chains. Unlike AdaBlock-style newline truncation, this is learned inside the training loop and can wrap existing dLLM RL methods such as d1, wd1, and Diffu-GRPO. On LLaDA-8B-Instruct, across GSM8K, MATH500, Sudoku, and Countdown, b1 consistently improves zero-shot pass@1, especially on hard reasoning cases, while adding negligible overhead and often stabilizing training and accelerating convergence. The result points toward reasoning models that learn adaptive, uncertainty-monotone decoding schedules rather than relying on fixed block geometry.
papers.abstract: Recent diffusion large language models (dLLMs) have demonstrated both effectiveness and efficiency in reasoning via a block-based semi-autoregressive generation paradigm. Despite their progress, the fixed-size block generations remain a critical bottleneck for effective and coherent reasoning. 1. From a global perspective, different reasoning tasks would correspond to different optimal decoding block sizes, which makes a ``one-size-fits-all'' assumption ineffective. 2. Even within a single reasoning task, the rigid block partitioning would break the logical flow and reduce reasoning coherence. Through empirical observations, we reveal that for block-wise entropy, incorrect reasoning exhibits a fluctuating and unsteady trend between blocks, whereas the correctly generated tasks follow a consistent descending trend. Therefore, this paper proposes b1, a novel post-training framework for dLLMs that learns dynamic-size reasoning blocks via a Monotonic Entropy Descent objective with reinforcement learning to enhance reasoning coherence.b1 integrates seamlessly as a plug-and-play module with existing dLLM's post-training algorithms. Extensive experiments across various reasoning benchmarks showcase b1's consistent improvement over existing fixed-size block baselines. Our code has been released at https://github.com/YanJiangJerry/Block-R1.

papers.title: HELIX: Hybrid Encoding with Learnable Identity and Cross-dimensional Synthesis for Time Series Imputation
papers.summary: HELIX: Hybrid Encoding with Learnable Identity and Cross-dimensional Synthesis for Time Series Imputation attacks a specific imputation bottleneck in multivariate series: when values are missing heavily or in long blocks, attention-only models keep re-inferring feature relations layer by layer, and graph-based imputers depend on predefined spatial topology that breaks for heterogeneous variables or absent coordinates. The paper pivots from serial “encode time then propagate over features” workflows and from fixed graphs toward persistent feature identity anchors learned end-to-end. Its core mechanical shift is to attach every feature a learnable identity vector, concatenate it with the observed value, sinusoidal time encoding, and mask at each (t,i), then process the 4D tensor through a double-helix hybrid encoder that runs temporal MHA and feature MHA in parallel, crosses them in a second synthesis stage, and fuses all layers with multi-level averaging. This creates a soft adjacency bias: identity terms supply a data-independent compatibility prior, while context terms adapt to whatever observations remain, so cross-feature attention does not collapse when both values are missing. Evaluated under TSI-Bench protocols on PhysioNet2012, BeijingAir, ItalyAir, ETT-h1, and PeMS across Point-10/50/90, Block-50, and Subseq-50 patterns, HELIX ranks first in all 21 settings over 16 baselines. Unusually, its learned identities recover latent geographic and clinical structure without any graph supervision, and layerwise attention becomes progressively more aligned with that structure. The result suggests a future where multivariate time-series models are built around learned semantic identities plus cross-dimensional synthesis rather than topology assumptions or pure token mixing.
papers.abstract: Time series imputation benefits from leveraging cross-feature correlations, yet existing attention-based methods re-discover feature relationships at each layer, lacking persistent anchors to maintain consistent representations. To address this, we propose HELIX, which assigns each feature a learnable feature identity, a persistent embedding that captures intrinsic semantic properties throughout the network. Unlike graph-based methods that rely on predefined topology and assume homogeneous spatial relationships, HELIX learns arbitrary feature dependencies end-to-end from temporal co-variation, naturally handling datasets where features mix spatial locations with semantic variables. Integrated with hybrid temporal-feature attention, HELIX achieves the state-of-the-art performance, surpassing all 16 baselines on 5 public datasets across 21 experimental settings in our evaluation. Furthermore, our mechanistic analysis reveals that HELIX aligns learned feature identities and dependencies with latent physical and semantic structure progressively across layers, demonstrating that it more effectively translates cross-feature structure into imputation accuracy.

papers.title: Variational Matrix-Learning Fourier Networks for Parametric Multiphysics Surrogates
papers.summary: Variational Matrix-Learning Fourier Networks for Parametric Multiphysics Surrogates attacks the bottleneck that chiplet STCO repeatedly solves PDE-governed thermal, mechanical, and wave models with finite elements, making parametric exploration too slow for design loops. It implicitly challenges the assumption that physics-informed surrogates must be trained by iterative backpropagation on strong-form residuals, with manually tuned penalty weights and high-order automatic differentiation. The pivot is from residual-minimizing neural PDE solvers toward a weak-form, matrix-solvable surrogate: VMLFN uses a shallow log-space sine feature map with randomly sampled spectral frequencies, frequency-dependent decay regularization, and hard-embedded Dirichlet constraints, then freezes the hidden basis and solves only the output weights by enforcing stationarity of the variational energy functional. The mechanical shift is that PDE learning becomes a linear system KW=F assembled from first-order derivatives only, while a heuristic frequency-scanning stage selects a problem-adaptive ωmax via holdout residuals plus a stability penalty on coefficient magnitude. Compared with nearby PINN/ELM variants, this directly couples Fourier basis design, weak-form discretization, and direct linear algebra instead of nonlinear optimization. On five benchmarks spanning 3-D heat conduction, Helmholtz propagation, and heterogeneous thermoelastic warping, it matches or approaches FEM/PINN field accuracy while delivering orders-of-magnitude speedups, including zero-shot 4-D parametric inference over conductivity in milliseconds. The main tradeoff is that average error can be slightly worse than carefully tuned hard-constrained or variational PINNs, but the paper points toward adaptive, physics-structured surrogate solvers for real-time multiphysics co-design.
papers.abstract: Multiphysics simulation is critical for system-technology co-optimization (STCO) in chiplet-based design, but repeated finite-element solutions of PDE-governed problems are computationally expensive in parametric design exploration. This paper proposes a variational matrix-learning Fourier network (VMLFN) for efficient parametric multiphysics surrogate modeling. VMLFN constructs a log-space sine neural representation with randomly sampled spectral frequencies, frequency-dependent decay regulation, and embedded Dirichlet boundary conditions. With fixed hidden-layer parameters, the output-layer weights are determined by reformulating the governing PDEs into variational weak forms and enforcing the stationarity condition of the resulting energy functional. This converts physics-informed training into a linear matrix-solving problem, requiring only first-order derivatives and avoiding both high-order automatic differentiation and penalty-coefficient tuning.
