A heuristic frequency-scanning algorithm is further introduced to select a problem-adaptive maximum frequency that covers the dominant spectral range of the target problem. The proposed method is validated on heat conduction, solid mechanics, and Helmholtz wave propagation problems. Results from five benchmark cases demonstrate that VMLFN delivers accurate full-field predictions with substantial speedup over conventional physics-informed neural networks and repeated finite-element simulations.

papers.title: Graph Federated Unlearning for Privacy Preservation
papers.summary: Graph Federated Unlearning for Privacy Preservation tackles a gap in graph federated learning: even when raw user graphs stay local, withdrawn users’ signals can persist in shared parameters and continue leaking through membership inference or inversion after “client removal,” violating the right to be forgotten. The paper pivots away from the common assumption that federated privacy is satisfied once data never leaves the client, and away from brute-force retraining or generic machine-unlearning recipes that treat graph clients like iid records. Instead it moves from simply erasing a client toward erasing its information while keeping the graph system usable. The core mechanical shift is FedGCV, which combines gradient correction with virtual clients: unlearning updates are projected to stay orthogonal to the gradients of retained data, so forgetting a target client minimally disturbs other clients’ optimization, and the server synthesizes privacy-preserving proxy subgraphs with a VGAE, Laplacian spectral constraints, and feature sampling to replace removed clients without reconstructing their raw entities. This is unusual among nearby work because it repairs topology and embedding continuity explicitly, rather than only adding noise or ascent steps. Evaluated on Cora, CiteSeer, PubMed, and Tolokers under a user-withdrawal RTBF setting, it reports lower MIA rates and higher post-unlearning accuracy than seven baselines, sometimes even outperforming retraining on utility. The limitation is that it relies on controlled synthetic client generation and federated server orchestration, pointing toward future graph privacy systems that jointly model unlearning, topology-preserving replacement, and attack-aware evaluation.
papers.abstract: Graph federated learning (GFL) facilitates decentralized training on distributed graph data while keeping sensitive user information local, aligning with policies such as GDPR and CCPA that grant users the right to freely join or withdraw from learning systems. However, even decentralized, user information can persist after quitting, potentially propagating to central servers and then redistributing to malicious clients. This privacy leakage during user withdrawal, despite its importance, has received seldom attention in GFL. To fill the gap, we explore the potential of machine unlearning (MU) to thoroughly remove user information. However, classical MU methods are known to degrade overall performance, a problem that is exacerbated in GFL due to local message passing and global model collaboration. To this end, we make two adjustments to mitigate this challenge for GFL. First, we ensure unlearning updates that minimally affect overall performance, steering them in directions orthogonal to the gradients from learning other data. Second, we introduce virtual clients, maintained by the central server, to preserve graph topology and global embeddings without recovering information of removed entities. We conduct comprehensive experiments under a representative user-withdrawal scenario and propose a novel membership inference framework to rigorously evaluate and validate the reliability of our privacy preservation. The experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, which also surpasses the performance of seven state-of-the-art baseline methods.

papers.title: A Meta Reinforcement Learning Approach to Goals-Based Wealth Management
papers.summary: A Meta Reinforcement Learning Approach to Goals-Based Wealth Management tackles the bottleneck that goals-based wealth management (GBWM) is an individualized, multi-year control problem: each new investor combines yearly portfolio choice with the decision to take, skip, or partially fulfill time-specific financial goals, and classical dynamic programming (DP) must be re-solved from scratch for every parameter change, quickly hitting the curse of dimensionality. The paper pivots from exact per-instance optimization toward a pretrained zero-shot meta-policy: MetaRL is trained on thousands of synthetic GBWM tasks and then used at inference to output near-optimal goal-taking and portfolio actions for unseen investors in hundredths of a second. The core mechanical shift is a dual-agent PPO design, where one actor decides goal fulfillment and a second actor selects the next portfolio, both conditioned on a highly normalized 26-variable state encoding that compresses time, fundedness, future goal structure, and two Monte Carlo–based indicator states. This replaces DP’s backward recursion over raw case-specific state with a stationary policy learned across heterogeneous horizons, wealth levels, infusions, and efficient-frontier positions. Unusually, the model is explicitly tested out of distribution, including longer horizons, altered efficient frontiers, concurrent and partial goals, and stochastic inflation; despite training on one market regime, it averages 97.8% of DP utility and stays robust under regime shifts. The direction it points to is finance as a foundation-model-style control problem, where pretrained policies become reusable decision engines for larger, more stochastic wealth-planning spaces that exact optimization cannot handle.
papers.abstract: Applying concepts related to zero-shot meta-learning and pre-training of foundation models, we develop a meta reinforcement learning approach (denoted MetaRL) that is pre-trained on thousands of goals-based wealth management (GBWM) problems. Each GBWM problem involves a multiple year scenario over which the investor looks to optimally choose an investment portfolio each year and choose to fulfill all, some, or none of the different financial goals that arise each year. These choices seek to maximize the expected total investor utility obtained from the fulfilled financial goals. By eliminating separate training and optimization for each new investor problem, the MetaRL model in inference mode produces near-optimal dynamic investment portfolio and goal-fulfilling strategies for a new GBWM problem within a few hundredths of a second. This delivers expected utilities that are, on average, 97.8% of the optimal expected utilities (determined via Dynamic Programming). These results are remarkably robust to capital market regime changes, even when training uses only one capital market regime. Further, the MetaRL approach can enable solving problems with larger state spaces where Dynamic Programming becomes computationally infeasible.

papers.title: Differentiable Kernel Ridge Regression for Deep Learning Pipelines
papers.summary: Differentiable Kernel Ridge Regression for Deep Learning Pipelines attacks a practical bottleneck in modern deep learning: kernels are often treated as elegant but separate and too expensive alternatives to neural heads, so their use stops at small-scale theory or offline baselines. The paper pivots from the assumption that readouts must be parametric and trained end-to-end toward a lazy, localized kernel layer that can be dropped into standard PyTorch pipelines. Its mechanical shift is Sparse Kernels: an M-NN tessellated, differentiable variant of kernel ridge regression that defers most computation to inference time, solves only small local M×M systems, and exposes three independently fixed-or-learned parameter sets—feature representations, target values, and evaluation points. That decomposition is unusual relative to nearby deep-kernel or probe work because it makes the readout itself a controllable object for training-free transfer, nonlinear probing, and hybrid kernel-neural designs rather than just a posthoc evaluator. Across frozen ResNet-18 transfer on CIFAR-10, VGG-19 and ViT probing, and a Double DQN LunarLander case study, SK readouts match or outperform linear/MLP heads with far less training, and adding kernel modules can improve policy learning. The main tradeoff is query-time cost, bandwidth sensitivity, and a default globally discontinuous predictor, with continuity requiring extra machinery. The paper points toward deep learning systems where nonparametric local solvers, adaptive readouts, and kernel-neural composition become standard design levers.
papers.abstract: Deep neural networks dominate modern machine learning, while alternative function approximators remain comparatively underexplored at scale. In this work, we revisit kernel methods as drop-in components for standard deep learning pipelines. We introduce \emph{Sparse Kernels} (SKs), a differentiable, localized, and lazy variant of kernel ridge regression (KRR) that defers training to inference time and reduces to the solution of small local systems. We integrate SKs into PyTorch as modular layers that preserve end-to-end trainability, and we show that they expose three distinct sets of parameters -- feature representations, target values, and evaluation points -- each of which can be fixed or learned. This decomposition broadens the design space available to practitioners, enabling, in particular, training-free transfer, nonlinear probing, and hybrid kernel-neural models. Across convolutional networks, vision transformers, and reinforcement learning, SK-based modules serve two complementary roles: in some settings, they match the performance of trained neural readouts with substantially less training; in others, they augment existing models and improve their performance when used as additional components. Our results suggest that kernel methods, once made scalable and differentiable, can be readily integrated with deep learning rather than treated as a separate paradigm.

papers.title: When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference
papers.summary: When Attention Collapses: Residual Evidence Modeling for Compositional Inference attacks a specific compositional-inference failure mode: in additive superposition, where every observation token mixes multiple latent sources, slot attention repeatedly locks onto the same dominant component and leaves weaker ones unmodeled, a phenomenon the paper terms slot collapse. This challenges the object-centric assumption that attention can solve decomposition by assignment alone; under superposition, the bottleneck is not capacity or optimization but the absence of memory about what evidence has already been explained. The paper pivots from parallel softmax-over-slots reasoning toward stateful residual tracking, arguing that compositional inference needs an explicit explanatory budget rather than repeated access to the same full input. The mechanical shift is evidence depletion: a minimal sequential attention variant that carries a scalar evidence state per token, applies multiplicative depletion after each slot, and adds a log-evidence bias so later slots are steered toward unexplained tokens. Controlled ablations show that sequential processing by itself, parallel attention, and loss-based diversity regularizers all fail to stop collapse, while residual evidence modeling consistently succeeds. Across synthetic mixtures, real FUSS audio, and simulated ESA/NASA LISA gravitational-wave source inference, the method reduces collapse by up to an order of magnitude and, under identical architectures/data/losses, turns failure into calibrated multi-source posterior estimation. The paper points toward a broader research direction: attention mechanisms with explicit residual state for additive, high-dynamic-range scientific inference, and eventually hierarchical decompositions that track explanation over multiple levels.
papers.abstract: Compositional inference - the decomposition of observations into an unknown number of latent components - is central to perception and scientific data analysis. Attention-based models perform well when components are approximately separable, as in object-centric vision. Under additive superposition, however - where multiple components contribute to every observation - we identify a structural failure mode we term slot collapse: multiple slots converge to the same dominant component while weaker ones remain unrepresented. We trace this to a general limitation: attention is memoryless with respect to explained evidence. All slots repeatedly operate on the same input without accounting for what has already been explained, so gradients are dominated by the strongest component, inducing shared fixed points across slots. As a result, attention fails to enforce non-redundant allocation under additive superposition. We address this by introducing residual evidence modeling, instantiated via evidence depletion - a minimal modification combining multiplicative depletion with an attention bias. Controlled ablations show that parallel attention, sequential processing alone, and loss-based regularization fail to resolve collapse; evidence depletion, which adds residual state to sequential attention, consistently succeeds. Across synthetic benchmarks and real-world audio mixtures (FUSS), evidence depletion reduces slot collapse by up to an order of magnitude, generalizing beyond synthetic settings. On gravitational-wave source inference for the ESA/NASA LISA mission, under identical architectures, data, and losses, standard attention fails while evidence depletion prevents collapse and enables multi-source posterior estimation. These results show that under additive superposition, residual evidence tracking is the operative ingredient for preventing collapse and enabling compositional inference.

papers.title: A Near-optimal SQ Lower Bound for Smoothed Agnostic Learning of Boolean Halfspaces
papers.summary: A Near-optimal SQ Lower Bound for Smoothed Agnostic Learning of Boolean Halfspaces studies the bottleneck in learning halfspaces on {±1}^n when the benchmark itself is smoothed by independent bit flips at rate σ: instead of competing with the worst-case best halfspace, the learner competes with the best halfspace after noise, which was expected to soften agnostic hardness. The paper challenges the emerging assumption that this smoothing should permit substantially better-than-L1-regression algorithms on the Boolean cube, and pivots the field from “can smoothing make halfspaces easy?” toward “is L1 polynomial regression already the SQ frontier?” The mechanical shift is a near-matching SQ lower bound built without Gaussian rotations: it constructs an explicit bounded dual witness for the smoothed majority TρMajn by placing the Boolean generating function on the unit circle, turning it into a sine kernel, and averaging with signed Laguerre weights to annihilate the first m odd Fourier levels while preserving nontrivial correlation. That witness yields an L1 approximation lower bound for low-degree polynomials, then gets embedded into a hidden-direction family {Du} with tiny pairwise correlations; Feldman’s SDA/VSTAT framework converts this into an SQ hardness result. Combined with the standard L1-regression upper bound at degree O(log(1/ε)/σ), the paper shows any SQ learner must either use tolerance n^{-Ω(log(1+σ/ε^2)/σ)} or make 2^{Ω(√n)} queries, leaving only a logarithmic gap in σ and pointing toward sharper Boolean smoothed-hardness theory and possible non-SQ escapes.
papers.abstract: We study the complexity of smoothed agnostic learning of halfspaces on $\{\pm 1\}^n$ under the uniform distribution in the model of \citet{KM25} where each input coordinate is independently flipped with probability $σ\in (0, {1}/{2})$. We show that $L^1$ polynomial regression achieves complexity $\tilde{O}(n^{O(\log(1/\varepsilon)/σ)})$, and prove a nearly matching Statistical Query complexity lower bound of $n^{Ω(\log(1+σ/\varepsilon ^2)/σ)}$. This complements the recent work of \citet{DK26}, which established analogous bounds in the continuous setting under Gaussian marginals.

papers.title: ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic
papers.summary: ZNO: Stable Rational Neural Operators in the Z-Domain for Discrete-Time Dynamic attacks the mismatch between operator-learning machinery built around continuous-time PDE surrogates and the many system-identification problems that are native discrete-time, where the real bottleneck is learning stable, long-memory rational filters whose poles must stay inside the unit disk. It pivots away from Laplace/Fourier-style spectral parameterizations and the assumption that discrete traces should be modeled through an arbitrary sampling interval, toward direct z-plane dynamics with readable poles and causality baked into the layer. The mechanical shift is a low-rank MIMO rational recurrent layer: hidden channels are projected into r latent streams, each stream runs through stable pole-residue filters reparameterized by a smooth sigmoid onto |p|<ρmax, optionally augmented with a short FIR branch, then mixed back by a dense output map. This is unusual because it combines neural-operator lifting with classical IIR structure and interpretable discrete-time poles, rather than a generic state-space or attention block; an isomorphic s-plane ablation shows the gain is not merely coordinate choice, but the full rational recurrent design. On controlled ARMA, sixth-order IIR, and nonlinear NARX tasks, plus five public benchmarks including Silverbox, Wiener–Hammerstein, CED, Cascaded Tanks, and EMPS, ZNO is strongest on near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, often giving the lowest mean error under validation-selected settings, while remaining competitive rather than universal. The paper points toward neural operators with explicit stability constraints, discrete-domain inductive bias, and eventually spatio-temporal rational systems with formal robustness guarantees.
papers.abstract: We introduce the Z-Domain Neural Operator (ZNO), a causal neural operator whose layers are stable low-rank multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) rational filters parameterized directly in the $z$-plane. ZNO addresses a limitation of existing operator learning methods, many of which are primarily tailored for continuous-time problems, while a large class of system-identification problems is intrinsically discrete-time. The $z$-domain form expresses stability as a unit-disk pole constraint and makes learned discrete-time poles directly readable. The model combines low-rank channel mixing, smooth stable pole reparameterization, causal recurrence, and an optional short finite impulse response (FIR) branch in a single $z$-domain rational recurrent layer. Across controlled discrete system-identification experiments, ZNO's advantage is most evident when the target dynamics are stable rational systems with lightly damped poles near the unit circle. Under matched parameter budgets, ZNO is not uniformly dominant; however, with validation-selected configurations, the same architecture can achieve the lowest mean error across the controlled tasks. A five-bin difficulty sweep over near-unit-circle / long-memory dynamics shows that ZNO has the lowest mean error across memory regimes, from short (approximately 10 steps) to long (approximately 100-200 steps). On five public nonlinear system-identification benchmarks, ZNO is competitive with neural operator and state-space baselines, achieving the lowest mean error on benchmarks whose dynamics align with stable rational discrete-time filters, while classical or state-space baselines remain preferable on some systems. These results position ZNO as a strong model for stable rational discrete-time dynamics, especially in near-unit-circle and long-memory regimes, but not as a universal replacement for specialized system-identification methods.

papers.title: Predicting Post Virality with Temporal Cross-Attention over Trend Signals
papers.summary: Predicting Post Virality with Temporal Cross-Attention over Trend Signals attacks a specific bottleneck in social-media virality prediction: models that lean on static text, metadata, and community size miss that “same post, different week” can have radically different viral outcomes when public attention is shifting. The paper pivots from content-only and purely in-platform workflows toward aligning Reddit posts with exogenous real-world attention, using Wikipedia pageview spikes as a proxy for collective interest. The mechanical shift is ViralityNet, which replaces hand-crafted trend features with a temporal cross-attention block: title embeddings, body embeddings, nine structural features, a learned subreddit embedding, and a residual year embedding are fused, then queried against a daily sliding 7-day trends matrix built from the top-512 Wikipedia spike terms, with focal loss handling the 9.7% positive rate. Unlike nearby virality work that treats context as fixed or uses generic temporal features, this approach asks whether a post semantically matches the current attention landscape. On 788,873 Reddit posts from 10 subreddits spanning 2021–2023, labeled viral by a subreddit-stratified top-10% engagement rule with an absolute score floor, the full model reaches 0.3584 AUC-PR and 0.8355 AUC-ROC, modestly beating text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR; year embeddings matter more than trends, but trends add a complementary lift, especially in externally driven communities like r/technology and r/stocks. The limitation is that this is contemporaneous alignment, not true forward forecasting, pointing toward richer attention proxies, early-velocity signals, and cross-platform temporal grounding.
papers.abstract: Current models for predicting social media virality rely heavily on static textual and structural features, effectively ignoring the highly dynamic nature of trend signals. We study whether real-world attention signals can improve the prediction of social-media virality beyond what post text alone reveals. We introduce \model{}, an architecture that predicts Reddit post virality by fusing internal platform representations with exogenous temporal signals derived from Wikipedia pageview spikes. We frame virality as a binary classification task that accounts for differences in subreddit scale, labeling posts as viral if they exceed the 90th percentile of per-subreddit engagement and a minimum absolute score threshold. We introduce ViralityNET combines four post-level streams: title embeddings, body embeddings, structural metadata, and learned subreddit embeddings with a cross-attention block that queries a daily sliding-window trends matrix encoding the top-512 Wikipedia spike terms from the preceding seven days. Empirical results suggest that incorporating external attention signals yields consistent gains, outperforming text-only baselines by +0.015 AUC-PR and achieving an overall AUC-ROC of 0.836. Overall, we provide evidence that incorporating external attention signals yields measurable improvements over text-only baselines, highlighting the importance of real-world dynamics in shaping online virality.

papers.title: Binary Rewards and Reinforcement Learning: Fundamental Challenges
papers.summary: Binary Rewards and Reinforcement Learning: Fundamental Challenges dissects why RL with verifiable binary rewards so often yields diversity collapse: the usual “increase pass@1” story hides a structural degeneracy where every fully valid policy maximizes expected reward, so REINFORCE has no distinguished solution and can prefer a Dirac-like answer over a diverse valid distribution. The paper pivots from reward maximization toward a geometric distribution-matching view of KL-control, showing that the KL-penalized objective selects, as β→0, the filtered model p* = a(·|Y1), i.e. the base model conditioned on validity and also the I-projection of the base model onto the fully valid set. The mechanical shift is the exact Gibbs tilt p[β] ∝ a(y)e^{v(y)/β}, plus closed-form relations between β, the natural parameter λ=1/β, and the interpretable target validity rate μ; this reframes tuning as choosing a desired validity level rather than an opaque penalty. The unusual point is the asymmetry: p[β] converges to p* in forward KL, but KL(q||p*) is infinite for any full-support autoregressive policy, so the standard reverse-KL target cannot directly optimize toward p*. Under misspecification, the pressure to lower β amplifies validity at the expense of coverage, pushing optimization toward highly concentrated near-Dirac policies rather than the broad support of p*. A toy autoregressive/bigram experiment confirms this collapse. The paper points toward forward-KL and α-divergence objectives that reward coverage of p*’s support, suggesting future RLVR systems should be designed around divergence choice, not just reward scale.
papers.abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a standard approach for improving reasoning in language models, yet models trained with RLVR often suffer from diversity collapse: while single-sample accuracy improves, multi-sample coverage degrades, sometimes falling below the base model. We provide a structural account of this phenomenon grounded in the properties of binary rewards. Binary rewards create a fundamental degeneracy for policy gradient methods: the set of distributions maximizing expected reward is infinite, with no distinguished element. KL-control resolves this degeneracy by selecting, in the limit $β\to 0$, the filtered model $p_*:=a(\cdot\mid\mathcal{Y}_1)$ -- the base model conditioned on validity -- which is the unique fully valid distribution closest to the base model in KL divergence. This selection operates through a nontrivial asymmetry: the tilted distribution $p_{[β]}\propto a(y)\,e^{v(y)/β}$ converges to $p_*$ in forward KL as $β\to 0$, yet $p_*$ cannot serve as a direct optimization target because $\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,p_*)$ is infinite for any full-support policy $q$. We develop explicit formulas relating the hyperparameter $β$ to the more interpretable target validity rate $μ$. Under model misspecification -- the typical practical regime -- the pressure to decrease $β$ drives the optimizer toward highly concentrated distributions over a small number of valid outputs, collapsing toward ever fewer as $β$ decreases, rather than toward the filtered model. We illustrate this mechanism on a toy autoregressive experiment and discuss how alternative divergences that target $p_*$ directly -- as pursued empirically by \citet{kruszewski_whatever_2026} -- avoid this failure mode by rewarding coverage of $p_*$'s support rather than concentration on high-validity outputs.

papers.title: Statistically-Lossless Quantization of Large Language Models
papers.summary: Statistically-Lossless Quantization of Large Language Models tackles the deployment bottleneck where LLM quantization is forced into a false binary: lossy 4-bit compression such as GPTQ/AWQ versus exact lossless codecs that preserve weights but usually cannot accelerate inference. The paper pivots from optimizing a fixed low-bit compression target toward a fidelity-calibrated regime defined by “statistically-lossless” behavior under the stochastic sampling used in real serving. It separates task-lossless compression, where zero-shot accuracy stays inside natural run-to-run variance, from distribution-lossless compression, where the quantized model’s next-token distribution must be practically indistinguishable from the original. The mechanical shift is threefold: it introduces Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR), a coupling-based, directly interpretable overlap metric for token agreement; proves a gamma-squared variance law showing symmetric grids inject quadratically more noise than asymmetric grids on skewed weight ranges, making zero-point asymmetry necessary for distribution-lossless fidelity; and implements SLQ, a layer-wise non-uniform integer quantization pipeline with asymmetric quantization over 2–8 bits, multi-bitwidth Shapley sensitivity estimation, ILP-based allocation, and a single-point calibration trick that turns one benchmark measurement into a near-lossless bitwidth search. Unlike nearby work, it treats 4 bits as arbitrary rather than canonical and searches the entire precision spectrum. On Qwen3 and Llama-3.3 models, SLQ reaches task-lossless compression as low as 3.3 bits and distribution-lossless compression at 5–6.6 bits, with 1.7–3.6x throughput gains under vLLM/Humming. This suggests a future agenda of fidelity-constrained, distribution-aware compression and serving systems rather than benchmark-centric quantization.
papers.abstract: Model quantization has become essential for efficient large language model deployment, yet existing approaches involve clear trade-offs: methods such as GPTQ and AWQ achieve practical compression but are lossy, while lossless techniques preserve fidelity but typically do not accelerate inference. This paper explores the middle ground of statistically-lossless compression through three complementary notions of losslessness for quantized LLMs. First, task-lossless compression preserves zero-shot benchmark accuracy within natural sampling variance and remains achievable at aggressive bitwidths. Second, we formalize the stricter notion of distribution-lossless compression, requiring the quantized model's next-token distribution to be practically indistinguishable from the original, and propose the Expected Acceptance Rate (EAR), the maximum token-agreement probability under optimal coupling, as a directly interpretable fidelity metric (for example, EAR >= 0.99 indicates 99% agreement). Third, we prove a gamma-squared variance law showing that symmetric quantization inflates noise variance by gamma squared relative to asymmetric quantization, making asymmetry necessary for distribution-lossless fidelity but not for task-level preservation. Using SLQ, a layer-wise non-uniform method with asymmetric quantization and wide bitwidth search, we achieve task-lossless compression at well below 4 bits per parameter (as low as 3.3 bits depending on the model), distribution-lossless compression at 5 to 6 bits per parameter on average, and inference speedups of 1.7 to 3.6x relative to FP16 with optimized kernels. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/SLQ.

papers.title: Closed-Loop CO2 Storage Control With History-Based Reinforcement Learning and Latent Model-Based Adaptation
papers.summary: Closed-Loop CO2 Storage Control With History-Based Reinforcement Learning and Latent Model-Based Adaptation attacks the practical bottleneck in geological CO2 storage control: policies must react to uncertain reservoir dynamics using only the sparse well measurements actually available in operation, not the dense pressure/saturation fields that simulator studies often assume. The paper explicitly pivots from open-loop optimization and repeated online history matching toward a partially observable sequential decision problem trained offline on high-fidelity OPM/Flow reservoir simulations. Its core mechanical shift is to replace “current well response only” control with finite-history sequence encoding: a SAC controller ingests a rolling 20-step history of 9-sample well tensors through a gated transformer-style encoder, while asymmetric variants exploit privileged spatial states only inside critics or teacher critics during training. The most distinctive comparison is between privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and teacher-student controllers, plus a second-stage latent model-based adaptation loop. That adaptation reuses nominal latent dynamics and Dreamer-style imagined rollouts, with GRU, geometrically regularized GRU, RSSM, and Koopman-inspired backbones, then residual adapters retune for injector failure, leakage-induced dynamics and reward shift, and compartmentalized connectivity under a fixed simulator budget. Results show history-conditioned policies recover nearly all privileged-state performance, and latent model-based retuning beats direct model-free retuning in abnormal cases. This points toward simulator-budget-aware closed-loop control that learns deployable temporal representations and fast latent adaptation instead of repeatedly re-optimizing from scratch.
papers.abstract: Closed-loop management of geological CO2 storage requires control policies that adapt to uncertain reservoir behavior while relying on observations that are realistically available during operation. This work formulates CO2 injection and brine-production control as a partially observable sequential decision problem and studies deployable deep reinforcement-learning controllers trained with high-fidelity reservoir simulation. We first compare privileged-state, well-only, history-conditioned, masking-curriculum, and asymmetric teacher-student model-free policies in order to quantify the value of temporal well-response information and training-time privileged simulator states. We then evaluate a latent model-based adaptation pipeline that reuses nominal latent dynamics and retunes controllers under known injector failure, leakage-induced dynamics and reward shift, and compartmentalized reservoir connectivity. The results show that history-conditioned policies recover nearly all of the privileged-state performance while using only deployable well-level information, and that latent model-based retuning outperforms direct model-free retuning under the same scenario-specific real-simulator budget in the abnormal operating cases. The proposed framework therefore provides a simulator-budget-aware alternative to repeated online history matching and re-optimization for closed-loop CO2 storage control.

papers.title: Inducing Permutation Invariant Priors in Bayesian Optimization for Carbon Capture and Storage Applications
papers.summary: Inducing Permutation Invariant Priors in Bayesian Optimization for Carbon Capture and Storage Applications attacks the failure mode where standard Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization wastes samples because grouped injector and producer wells are unordered within each group, yet flattened kernels treat equivalent permutations as different designs. The paper pivots from vector-input BO and learned latent encoders toward symmetry-aware surrogates that bake exchangeability into the prior itself, arguing that CCS well placement under group control is fundamentally a set-structured optimization problem. The mechanical shift is GP-Perm: a Matérn GP whose distance is built from an auxiliary continuous control vector plus Sinkhorn divergences between injector sets, producer sets, and an explicit injector–producer interaction set of pairwise relative offsets, so the kernel compares empirical set measures rather than ordered coordinates. This differs from nearby set kernels by using a geometry-sensitive OT-based discrepancy instead of hard matching or simple pooled similarities, and differs from DKL-DS by avoiding repeated nonconvex retraining of a Deep Sets encoder inside the BO loop. They evaluate across seven synthetic permutation-invariant benchmarks and one realistic Johansen formation CCS case with OPM-Flow, using qLogEI, feasibility-aware acquisition, and snapped discrete well placement. Results show no single surrogate dominates all tasks, but permutation-invariant models are competitive and GP-Perm is especially strong on some coverage/medoid-style objectives, while the CCS study confirms the value of symmetry exploitation under expensive reservoir simulation. The work points toward BO systems that learn priors over structured physical symmetries, not just over coordinates.
papers.abstract: Bayesian Optimization is an iterative method, tailored to optimizing expensive black box objective functions. Surrogate models like Gaussian Processes, which are the gold standard in Bayesian Optimization, can be inefficient for inputs with permutation symmetries, as the most common kernels employed are better suited for vector inputs rather than unordered sets of items. Motivated by this issue, we turn to permutation invariant Bayesian Optimization for well placement in Carbon Capture and Storage projects. The high fidelity black box simulator is instructed to operate wells under group control, giving rise to permutation symmetries within injector and producer groups that cannot be exploited with standard GP kernels. In this work, our main contribution is a novel Gaussian Process kernel (GP-Perm) that encodes permutation invariance by comparing sets through a stable divergence between their induced empirical representations, and can be combined with standard kernels for additional vector-valued inputs. As a learned invariant baseline, we also consider a Deep Kernel Learning model (DKL-DS) using the Deep Sets architecture to learn a permutation-invariant embedding. We evaluate the proposed methodology across 8 use cases, comprising seven synthetic benchmarks and one realistic CCS case study (Johansen formation)

papers.title: Generalized Distributional Alignment Games for Unbiased Answer-Level Fine-Tuning
papers.summary: Generalized Distributional Alignment Games for Unbiased Answer-Level Fine-Tuning attacks a structural failure mode in answer-level fine-tuning (ALFT): when GRPO-style small batches estimate answer-frequency rewards through a logarithm, Jensen’s inequality injects an O(1/K) bias that is worst on rare answers, turning KL-based alignment into an anti-exploration penalty that can destabilize training and accelerate mode collapse. The paper pivots from the usual assumption that the canonical KL reward must be estimated directly toward a broader geometry-first view of the alignment game: it generalizes Distributional Alignment Games to arbitrary Bregman divergences, then asks which geometries admit exact finite-sample reward estimation. The core mechanical shift is replacing biased log-frequency estimation with two mathematically distinct control levers: for polynomial reward geometries, U-statistics on falling factorial counts give provably exact unbiased estimators; for the canonical KL game, the authors derive a globally optimal minimax polynomial estimator over Binomial counts, proving the best possible worst-case gradient bias is Θ(1/K^2) via Ditzian-Totik approximation bounds. They further synthesize these into a Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program estimator that keeps online training unchanged except for an O(1) lookup, while improving convergence stability. Unusual here is that the paper treats estimator design, not just policy optimization, as the bottleneck in ALFT, and ties convergence directly to approximation theory. The result points toward alignment methods built from bias-aware, geometry-selective, offline-calibrated reward estimators for low-sample RL on reasoning tasks.
papers.abstract: The Distributional Alignment Game framework provides a powerful variational perspective on Answer-Level Fine-Tuning (ALFT). However, standard algorithms for these games rely on estimating logarithmic rewards from small batches, introducing a systematic bias due to Jensen's inequality that can destabilize training. In this paper, we systematically resolve this structural estimation bias. First, we generalize the alignment game to arbitrary Bregman divergences, showing that for a family of geometries inducing polynomial rewards, we can construct provably exact and unbiased estimators using U-statistics. Second, for the canonical KL divergence game where an exact solution is impossible, we derive a globally robust minimax polynomial estimator that is provably optimal, achieving the fundamental statistical error limit of $Θ(1/K^2)$, which we establish via the Ditzian-Totik theorem. Finally, we synthesize these two approaches to propose a novel Variance-Optimal Augmented Polynomial Optimization Program (AQP) Estimator, proving that by systematically reducing variance, our method achieves not only optimal bias but also provably accelerated game convergence, leading to more efficient and stable training with zero online computational overhead.

papers.title: Reference-Sampled Boltzmann Projection for KL-Regularized RLVR: Target-Matched Weighted SFT, Finite One-Shot Gaps, and Policy Mirror Descent
papers.summary: Reference-Sampled Boltzmann Projection for KL-Regularized RLVR: Target-Matched Weighted SFT, Finite One-Shot Gaps, and Policy Mirror Descent attacks the bottleneck that online RLVR keeps rollout generation, verifier scoring, and reference-policy evaluation on the critical path, and challenges the assumption that any static weighted SFT on precomputed verified rollouts is automatically equivalent to the online optimizer. The pivot is from treating weighted likelihood as a generic reward-augmented heuristic toward identifying the exact sampler-weight product that induces the same policy as fixed-reference KL-regularized RLVR: the Boltzmann target policy. The mechanical shift is a density-ratio projection, where reference-sampled rollouts must be weighted by the prompt-normalized Boltzmann factor exp(r/β)/Z(x), making BOLT an empirical estimator of this projection rather than a policy-gradient method. What is unusual here is the policy-level equivalence proof plus the finite one-shot analysis: it separates irreducible stored-support cost βlog(1/π*(S_N|x)) from normalizer estimation, ESS variance, generalization, optimization, and approximation, showing why extra SFT epochs cannot repair missing reference coverage and exposing a temperature–coverage–variance frontier. In Qwen single-run experiments on GSM8K, MATH, and HumanEval, BOLT outperforms raw-reward Refit on the same reference rollouts while cutting optimization cost relative to GRPO; refreshed BOLT further improves best checkpoints by acting as KL policy mirror descent with sampler refresh. The paper points toward coverage-aware offline RLVR and iteration schemes where refresh, not longer fitting, is the main control lever.
papers.abstract: Online reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) turns checkable outcomes into a scalable training signal, but it keeps rollout generation, verifier scoring, and reference-policy evaluations on the optimization path. Static weighted supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on precomputed rollouts seems to remove this bottleneck, yet a weighted likelihood is not specified by rewards alone: its sampler and weights induce the policy being fit. This paper identifies the reference-sampled weighted-SFT objective whose induced policy equals the fixed-reference KL-regularized RLVR optimizer. The optimizer is the standard Boltzmann target policy, obtained by exponentially tilting the reference policy by verifier reward. Matching a weighted-SFT induced policy to this target forces density-ratio weights; in the reference-sampled subclass, this reduces uniquely, up to prompt scaling, to the prompt-normalized Boltzmann weight $\exp(r(x,y)/β)/Z(x)$. BOLT, a Boltzmann-Targeted SFT procedure, is the empirical estimator of this projection. The finite one-shot analysis separates the exact stored-support price $β\log(1/π^*(S_N\mid x))$ from partition estimation, effective-sample-size variance, generalization, optimization, and approximation errors. This decomposition explains why extra SFT epochs cannot repair missing reference-policy coverage and exposes the temperature--coverage--variance frontier. When coverage needs adaptive sampling, refreshed Boltzmann projections become KL policy mirror descent; finite inner solves enter as additive drift from the exact mirror step. Single-run Qwen experiments provide projection evidence for the target-matched weight, one-shot saturation, refreshed-sampler gains, and optimization-time savings, within the stated single-run scope.

papers.title: Efficient Preference Poisoning Attack on Offline RLHF
papers.summary: Efficient Preference Poisoning Attack on Offline RLHF studies the vulnerability of offline RLHF pipelines, especially log-linear DPO, to a fixed-dataset label-flip adversary who cannot inject new comparisons but can only corrupt existing preference pairs. The paper challenges the common security intuition that preference poisoning must be “large-scale” or require data injection; instead it shows that a single flipped comparison induces a parameter-independent shift in the DPO gradient, so the attack problem can be rewritten in gradient space as a structured binary sparse approximation problem. The pivot is from policy-space intuition toward combinatorial control of the first-order optimality condition: the attacker targets a gradient residual at a chosen policy parameter, and strong convexity turns small residual into policy closeness. The mechanical shift is twofold. BAL-A embeds the binary flip selection problem into a binary-aware lattice, augments each gradient atom with an identity penalty, then uses LLL reduction plus Babai nearest-plane to recover flip patterns under separation conditions that enforce 0/1 coefficients and minimum-flip optimality. BMP-A instead adapts binary matching pursuit to a non-normalized gradient dictionary, selecting examples by normalized correlation and yielding coherence-based recovery guarantees plus impossibility certificates for K-flip budgets. Experiments on synthetic dictionaries and the Stanford Human Preferences dataset show attack success is governed sharply by dictionary geometry, non-uniform norms, and coherence, pointing toward a broader research direction where RLHF robustness is analyzed as sparse control over preference geometry rather than as generic adversarial noise.
papers.abstract: Offline Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) pipelines such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) train on a pre-collected preference dataset, which makes them vulnerable to preference poisoning attack. We study label flip attacks against log-linear DPO. We first illustrate that flipping one preference label induces a parameter-independent shift in the DPO gradient. Using this key property, we can then convert the targeted poisoning problem into a structured binary sparse approximation problem. To solve this problem, we develop two attack methods: Binary-Aware Lattice Attack (BAL-A) and Binary Matching Pursuit Attack (BMP-A). BAL-A embeds the binary flip selection problem into a binary-aware lattice and applies Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász reduction and Babai's nearest plane algorithm; we provide sufficient conditions that enforce binary coefficients and recover the minimum-flip objective. BMP-A adapts binary matching pursuit to our non-normalized gradient dictionary and yields coherence-based recovery guarantees and robustness (impossibility) certificates for $K$-flip budgets. Experiments on synthetic dictionaries and the Stanford Human Preferences dataset validate the theory and highlight how dictionary geometry governs attack success.

papers.title: Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks
papers.summary: Pretraining on Sleep Data Improves non-Sleep Biosignal Tasks asks whether polysomnography should be treated not as a sleep-only endpoint but as a pretraining distribution for broader biosignal representation learning, attacking the bottleneck that wake EEG and ECG models still depend on large labeled corpora and brittle, task-specific feature engineering. The paper pivots from the dominant assumption that transfer should come from in-domain wake data toward using sleep as a physiologically rich self-supervised source, arguing that long continuous recordings, pathological waveforms, and tight EEG-EOG-EMG-ECG alignment create stronger inductive bias than montage density alone. Its core mechanical shift is sleep-only multimodal contrastive pretraining with a leave-one-out objective on 48,093 full-night PSGs, coupled to only minimal changes from SleepFM1: 1-second patches instead of 5-second patches, 2-minute windows, and 1-second latent sequences that preserve transient events relevant to seizure, slowing, and arrhythmia detection; downstream EEG fine-tuning optionally adds REVE-style 3D Fourier channel positional encoding. Evaluated on five EEG tasks and three ECG tasks, including external validation on PTB-XL, G12EC, and CPSC after fine-tuning on Code-15, the pretrained encoder consistently beats random initialization and is competitive with or better than specialized foundation models, with especially strong gains on TUAB, TUSL, CHB-MIT, and BrainCapture. A distinctive result is channel robustness: 95% of full performance is retained with only 16–50% of EEG channels and 41–75% of ECG leads, pointing toward future foundation models that are sensor-agnostic, low-burden, and transferable across adjacent clinical signal regimes.
papers.abstract: Sleep foundation models have recently demonstrated strong performance on in-domain polysomnography tasks, including sleep staging, apnea detection, and disease risk prediction. In this work, we investigate whether sleep biosignals can serve as an effective pretraining distribution for learning representations that transfer beyond sleep to adjacent domains. Following sleep foundation models, we perform sleep-only multimodal contrastive pretraining (with a leave-one-out objective) and evaluate transfer to non-sleep EEG and ECG, two well-benchmarked biosignal modalities with heterogeneous datasets and clinically meaningful downstream tasks. Across eight downstream tasks spanning multiple EEG and ECG datasets, sleep pretraining consistently improves performance relative to training from scratch. Moreover, on several tasks, we achieve performance competitive with or surpassing prior specialized state-of-the-art and foundation models.

papers.title: A Novel Preprocessing-Driven Approach to Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction Using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN)
papers.summary: A Novel Preprocessing-Driven Approach to Remaining Useful Life (RUL) Prediction Using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) attacks the bottleneck that aero-engine RUL models are usually optimized by architecture while input structuring is treated as a fixed afterthought, even though windowing, scaling, and label shaping determine whether degradation is learnable. It pivots from architecture-first attention stacking toward preprocessing as the main control lever: instead of sliding-window inflation and overlapping pads, the authors train a TCN directly on full CMAPSS engine lifecycles, z-score normalize sensors, cap RUL at 125 cycles, randomly trim 10–75 steps from sequence ends to mimic partial run-to-failure observation, and supervise a prediction at every timestep. The mechanical shift is dense per-timestep prognostics over complete trajectories, paired with a four-block residual TCN (dilations 1, 2, 4, 8, 16; 200 filters; receptive field 125) that can exploit global lifecycle shape while avoiding recurrent overhead. Evaluated on NASA C-MAPSS FD001–FD004 over 10 runs against CNN, RNN, LSTM, DCNN, BiGRU-TSAM, AGCNN, ATCN, and vanilla TCN, it reports the lowest mean RMSE on all subsets and the best Score on FD002 and FD004, with competitive scores elsewhere, suggesting preprocessing can rival or beat architectural novelty. Its unusual contribution is the claim that better data presentation, not deeper temporal modules, is the dominant gain source; its limitation is remaining sensitivity to capped-plateau and degradation-onset errors. This points toward RUL research that treats preprocessing, label
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of Remaining Useful Life (RUL) in aero-engines is vital for predictive maintenance, improved operational reliability, and reduced lifecycle costs. While deep learning approaches have demonstrated strong potential in this area, most existing methods focus primarily on model architecture design and treat input features uniformly, often neglecting the influence of data preprocessing. In this work, we propose a novel preprocessing pipeline that enhances RUL prediction by improving data quality and temporal representation before model training. Our approach leverages complete temporal sequences and generates RUL estimates at each timestep, enabling the model to capture fine-grained degradation dynamics and deliver continuous prognostic insights throughout the engine's operational life. To validate the effectiveness of the proposed pipeline, we conduct experiments on the NASA C-MAPSS dataset. Comparative evaluations against a suite of state-of-the-art neural models including CNN, RNN, LSTM, DCNN, TCN, BiGRU-TSAM, AGCNN, and ATCN, demonstrate that our approach consistently achieves superior accuracy and robustness in aero-engine RUL prediction. These results highlight the critical role of preprocessing in maximizing the effectiveness of neural prognostic models.

papers.title: MPCS: Neuroplastic Continual Learning via Multi-Component Plasticity and Topology-Aware EWC
papers.summary: MPCS: Neuroplastic Continual Learning via Multi-Component Plasticity and Topology-Aware EWC attacks the continual-learning bottleneck where plasticity and stability are usually treated as a single regularization tradeoff, then judged only by final task accuracy. Instead of assuming one hero mechanism can suppress forgetting, the paper pivots from stability-first, global-constraint continual learning toward a modular neuroplastic system whose behavior is shaped by interactions among eleven components: task-driven neurogenesis, Fourier-encoded inputs, meta-replay, mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task-similarity routing, adaptive growth control, continuous neuron-importance tracking, and EWC. The core mechanical shift is topology-aware EWC, which masks Fisher penalties to task-owned neuron subspaces so importance does not leak across tasks; this is paired with a three-objective evaluation protocol on MEP-BENCH, a 31-task benchmark spanning regression, classification, logic, and mixed tracks, scored by Perf, representation diversity, and gradient conflict rate rather than accuracy alone. What is unusual is that the paper uses ablation plus Pareto status as a compression oracle: it finds Fourier features are the load-bearing component, global EWC is net-negative in the high-similarity regime, and removing Pareto-dominated EWC plus Hebbian yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, which improves performance slightly while cutting compute 4.7×. The result suggests a future direction where continual learning is designed and pruned as an interacting plasticity ecology, with benchmark-specific regime analysis replacing universal regularization dogma.
papers.abstract: Continual learning systems face a fundamental tension between plasticity -- acquiring new knowledge -- and stability -- retaining prior knowledge. We introduce MPCS (Multi-Plasticity Continual System), a neuroplastic architecture that integrates eleven complementary mechanisms: task-driven neurogenesis, Fourier-encoded inputs, EWC regularization, meta-replay, mixed consolidation, hybrid gating, synapse pruning/regeneration, Hebbian updates, task similarity routing, adaptive growth control, and continuous neuron importance tracking. We evaluate MPCS on MEP-BENCH, a multi-track benchmark spanning 31 tasks across regression, classification, logic, and mixed domains, using a three-dimensional Pareto criterion over task performance (Perf), representation diversity (RD), and gradient conflict rate (GCR). Across 15 ablation configurations (3 seeds x 4 tracks x 2000 epochs), MPCS achieves a Normalized Efficiency Score of 94.2, placing it on the Pareto frontier among 9 of 14 gate-passing systems. Key findings: (i) Fourier encoding is the single most critical component (removal drops Perf by 30.7 pp and fails the MEP gate on 14% of tasks); (ii) global EWC degrades performance (NES = -4.2); topology-local EWC reduces this penalty (NES 90.5->91.8) but does not eliminate it; removing EWC entirely yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, the highest-Perf system -- establishing a monotone relationship in the high task-similarity regime (s_bar ~= 0.95): global EWC < topology EWC < no EWC; (iii) the Pareto status assessment is predictive: removing the two Pareto-dominated components (EWC + Hebbian) jointly yields MPCS_EFFICIENT, which improves Perf by 0.6 pp at 4.7x lower compute cost (127 vs. 602 min), validating the Pareto frontier as an actionable model-compression guide.

papers.title: Evaluating Tabular Representation Learning for Network Intrusion Detection
papers.summary: Evaluating Tabular Representation Learning for Network Intrusion Detection examines whether NetFlow-based NIDS can move beyond manual feature engineering and instead learn reusable tabular embeddings directly from traffic records, a pivot away from the old workflow where domain experts handcraft ports, flags, and aggregates to separate benign flows from rare attacks. The paper’s central bottleneck is not just average detection accuracy but the brittleness of representation choice under severe class imbalance, heterogeneous feature types, and cross-network distribution shift. Its mechanical shift is a decoupled benchmark: TabICL, SCARF, class-conditioned contrastive learning, and an autoencoder are pretrained on three benchmark NetFlow datasets, then frozen representations are probed with supervised classifiers and unsupervised anomaly detectors, with exhaustive hyperparameter search and AUC/F1/macro-F1 ranking; a BERT-style end-to-end transformer serves as the adjacent baseline. Unlike nearby work that optimizes one model on one dataset, this study explicitly tests source-to-target transfer without fine-tuning, revealing how much representation quality survives environment change. Results show no universal winner: TabICL is strongest on CIDDS in supervised settings, autoencoders are the most reliable for unsupervised anomaly detection, and supervised probing consistently beats anomaly detection. The key limitation is that rare attacks remain systematically missed across all methods, even when aggregate scores look strong. This suggests a future direction toward imbalance-aware tabular representation learning, domain-adaptive transfer, and security-focused evaluation protocols that measure rare-attack coverage and deployment robustness rather than benchmark averages alone.
papers.abstract: Classic Network Intrusion Detection Systems (NIDS) often rely on manual feature engineering to extract meaningful patterns from network traffic data. However, this approach requires domain expertise and runs counter to the widely adopted principle of modern machine learning and neural networks: that models themselves should learn meaningful representations directly from data. We investigate whether tabular representation learning techniques can improve intrusion detection performance by automatically learning robust feature representations for NetFlow data. This paper presents a systematic evaluation of state-of-the-art representation learning methods on benchmark NetFlow datasets, comparing against traditional autoencoders and end-to-end transformer baselines. We evaluate learned representations using both supervised classifiers and unsupervised anomaly detectors, with comprehensive hyperparameter exploration for each combination. Our results reveal strong dataset-model dependency, with no single approach consistently dominating across all scenarios. For supervised classification, TabICL achieves the best performance on CIDDS, while autoencoders follow closely and tie with end-to-end transformer models for the best average rank across datasets. Supervised approaches substantially outperform unsupervised anomaly detection methods, where no single combination consistently dominates as optimal choices depend on the dataset. Cross-dataset transfer experiments demonstrate that learned representations can generalize across network environments with appropriate method and classifier selection. However, transfer performance varies substantially depending on the source-target dataset combination, indicating sensitivity to distributional differences between network environments.

papers.title: Physics-Informed Neural Learning for State Reconstruction and Parameter Identification in Coupled Greenhouse Climate Dynamics
papers.summary: Physics-Informed Neural Learning for State Reconstruction and Parameter Identification in Coupled Greenhouse Climate Dynamics attacks the inverse problem of inferring indoor temperature and relative humidity in a greenhouse when sensing is sparse, noisy, and the governing exchange coefficients are unknown, a regime where purely data-driven forecasters interpolate poorly and lose physical meaning. The paper implicitly challenges the assumption that greenhouse climate can be learned as a black-box sequence prediction task; instead, it pivots from unconstrained neural regression toward physics-constrained state estimation and parameter recovery. The mechanical shift is a coupled PINN that embeds a reduced-order thermal–moisture ODE model directly into training, uses automatic differentiation to penalize residuals of both temperature and humidity dynamics, and treats the coefficients for relaxation, radiation, ventilation, and cross-coupling as trainable variables alongside the network weights. Unlike nearby work that either forecasts climate variables or hybridizes mechanistic models post hoc, this formulation learns latent trajectories and interpretable parameters in one optimization, with positivity-preserving coefficient parameterization and an initial-condition penalty. The benchmark is a controlled synthetic 72-hour diurnal forcing setup with only 25% temporal observations and Gaussian noise, making identifiability the core failure regime. Compared with a same-architecture pure neural baseline, the coupled PINN reduces temperature RMSE from 0.2139 to 0.1360 and humidity RMSE from 0.6225 to 0.3221, while recovering dominant parameters closely; weaker cross-couplings remain less identifiable. The result points toward physics-regularized digital twins and data-scarce environmental inference where control, calibration, and forecasting are unified.
papers.abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have recently emerged as a promising framework for integrating data-driven learning with physical knowledge. In this work, we propose a coupled PINN approach for the joint reconstruction of indoor temperature and humidity dynamics in greenhouse environments, together with simultaneous identification of key model parameters. The method incorporates a reduced-order physically motivated model into the learning process, enabling consistent estimation under sparse and noisy observations. The artificial intelligence contribution lies in the development of a coupled physics-informed neural learning framework that integrates governing dynamical constraints into neural network training, while the engineering application focuses on greenhouse climate state reconstruction and parameter identification. The proposed framework is evaluated on a controlled synthetic benchmark that mimics diurnal forcing conditions. Compared with a purely data-driven neural network baseline, the coupled PINN achieves improved reconstruction accuracy, reducing temperature and humidity errors while maintaining high coefficients of determination. The improvement is particularly pronounced in the humidity channel, where latent moisture dynamics are more difficult to infer from limited measurements. In addition to accurate state reconstruction, the method successfully recovers the dominant physical parameters governing the system dynamics, demonstrating its ability to learn interpretable representations beyond data interpolation. These results highlight the potential of physics-informed learning for greenhouse climate modeling and, more broadly, for data-scarce environmental systems.

papers.title: Recurrent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Chemotherapy Control under Partial Observability
papers.summary: Recurrent Deep Reinforcement Learning for Chemotherapy Control under Partial Observability attacks a concrete clinical bottleneck: chemotherapy dose optimization is a sequential control problem in which the agent must balance tumor suppression, normal-cell preservation, and drug toxicity, yet real patients are only partially observed and noisy, so the standard RL assumption of full state access breaks exactly where treatment safety matters most. The paper pivots from memoryless continuous-control formulations of chemo dosing toward a POMDP view in which hidden physiological variables, especially the normal-cell population, must be inferred from history rather than read directly. The mechanical shift is a recurrent TD3 design with separate LSTM actor and critic networks, trained on subsequences from replay with zero-initialized hidden states, a 32-step context window, and masking at episode boundaries, so the policy conditions on observation-action-reward trajectories instead of single states. To isolate the effect of partial observability, PK/PD variability is fixed and noise is injected only into observations and ODE dynamics in AhnChemoEnv from DTR-Bench. Against feed-forward TD3, SAC, and recurrent PPO, recurrence gives only modest gains when the full state is visible but becomes decisively more stable under partial observability, with far lower cross-seed variance, more consistent tumor suppression, and better normal-cell recovery. This suggests a research direction where clinically deployable RL will be memory-augmented, offline-friendly, and explicitly designed for hidden-state inference and safety-constrained treatment control.
papers.abstract: Chemotherapy dose optimization can be formulated as a dynamic treatment regime, requiring sequential decisions under uncertainty that must balance tumor suppression against toxicity. However, most reinforcement learning approaches assume full observability of the patient state, a condition rarely met in clinical practice. We investigate whether memory-augmented policies can improve chemotherapy control under partial observability. To this end, we employ a recurrent TD3-based approach with separate LSTM actor-critic networks and evaluate it on the AhnChemoEnv benchmark from DTR-Bench, considering both off-policy and on-policy recurrent architectures against feed-forward TD3 and Soft Actor-Critic. Pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic variability are held fixed to isolate hidden-state uncertainty and observation noise and to avoid confounding effects from inter-patient variability. Across ten random seeds, recurrence yields modest benefit under full observability but substantially stronger and more stable performance under partial observability, with more consistent tumor suppression and improved normal-cell preservation. These findings indicate that memory-based policies are particularly beneficial when clinically relevant state information is incomplete or noisy.

papers.title: StreamIndex: Memory-Bounded Compressed Sparse Attention via Streaming Top-k
papers.summary: StreamIndex: Memory-Bounded Compressed Sparse Attention via Streaming Top-k attacks the long-context failure mode in DeepSeek V3.2/V4 Compressed Sparse Attention where the “lightning indexer” is not the attention kernel but the true bottleneck: public implementations materialize a [B, S, H_I, T] FP32 score tensor before top-k, which at H_I=64 and V4-Flash ratio m=4 becomes 256 GB at S=65,536 and OOMs on a single H200. The paper pivots away from the usual long-context assumption that the attention matmul or KV cache is the memory problem, and toward the indexer step itself, exploiting the separability of the scoring function to make top-k commute with streaming order. The mechanical shift is a Triton-based chunked partition-merge driver that computes scores on [cS, cT] tiles, applies a per-tile causal mask, takes local top-k, and merges into a running heap without ever instantiating the full intermediate; a tiny indexer_score kernel reduces head-wise scores inline in FP32, and a causal-ratio parameter keeps mask memory O(cS·cT). On synthetic-but-realistic V4-shaped inputs, StreamIndex extends the runnable regime from S=65,536 OOM to S=1,048,576 with 6.21 GB peak HBM, while matching materialize-then-topk with bit-exact set parity at small S and near-perfect recall across tile-size sweeps. The unusual contribution is not a new sparsity pattern but a drop-in streaming realization of the existing CSA semantics, suggesting future work on fused indexer-attention kernels, multi-GPU long-context inference, and memory-first compiler/runtime designs for sparse LLM serving.
papers.abstract: DeepSeek-V3.2 and V4 introduce Compressed Sparse Attention (CSA): a lightning indexer (a learned scoring projection over compressed keys) scores them, the top-k are selected per query, and a sparse attention kernel reads only those. Public CSA implementations materialize a [B, S, H_I, T] FP32 score tensor before the top-k reduction. With H_I=64 indexer heads and the V4-Flash compression ratio m=4, that intermediate is 256 GB at sequence length S=65,536, exceeding any single-GPU high-bandwidth-memory (HBM) budget. We present StreamIndex, a Triton implementation of the CSA pipeline whose central component is a chunked partition-merge top-k driver that never materializes the full intermediate. On synthetic-but-realistic V4-shaped inputs at the indexer-step (layer) level on a single NVIDIA H200, the materialize path runs out of memory (OOMs) at S=65,536 with V4-Flash dimensions; StreamIndex runs the same indexer to S=1,048,576 with 6.21 GB peak HBM, a 32x regime extension. Set-overlap recall against the materialize ground truth is bit-exact at small S where both fit; across three 5-point design-space sweeps (chunk size, key-tile size, top-k), mean recall rounds to 1.0000 with min recall at least 0.9980 in every cell. The chunked driver composes with TileLang's pipelined attention kernel: at S=262,144 with V4-Flash dimensions, the materialize indexer paired with TileLang attention OOMs while the chunked indexer paired with the same attention runs in 1.97 s at 18.56 GB peak. Our contribution targets the indexer step; we make no claim of a faster attention kernel or of real-checkpoint end-to-end behavior. Code: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/StreamIndex.

papers.title: Gradient Boosted Risk Scores
papers.summary: Gradient Boosted Risk Scores attacks the bottleneck that point-based clinical and financial risk scores are usually built by fitting linear, logistic, or Cox regressions and then binning coefficients into human-readable tables, a workflow that both hard-codes linearity and throws away information during post-processing. The paper pivots from “fit a predictive model, then compress it into a score” toward directly learning the score as the model: a gradient-boosting procedure restricted to decision stumps that sequentially adds threshold rules on single variables, with each stump contributing fixed point increments on the identity, log-odds, or log-hazard scale. The mechanical shift is important: thresholds can be user-specified or quantile-derived, pseudo-residuals drive stump selection, leaf values are updated in closed form for regression, classification, and survival, and identical split conditions are aggregated across boosting rounds to yield a compact additive table rather than a larger rule ensemble. Unlike RuleFit or explainable boosting machines, the output is not a post-hoc explanation or smooth shape function but an explicitly human-computable scoring system. On twelve tabular datasets, including UK Biobank heart failure, CKD, COPD, diabetes, and several open benchmarks, GBRS matches or approaches XGBoost while producing much smaller scores, averaging 60% fewer rules than AutoScore for classification and 16% fewer for time-to-event tasks. The result points toward a research trajectory where interpretable models are optimized natively for compactness, auditability, and manual evaluation, not merely approximated after the fact.
papers.abstract: Risk scores are an interpretable and actionable class of machine learning models with applications in medicine, insurance, and risk management. Unlike most computational methods, risk scores are designed to be computed by a human by attributing points to a data sample based on a limited set of criteria. The most common approaches for generating risk scores use linear regressions to estimate the effect of selected variables. We propose a simple and effective approach towards building compact and predictive risk scores. We provide an algorithm based on gradient boosting that is capable of modeling nonlinear effects, along with a C++ implementation with Python and R bindings. Through extensive empirical evaluation on twelve tabular datasets spanning regression, classification, and time-to-event tasks, we show that our method achieves competitive predictive performance while producing substantially more compact scores than regression-based alternatives, with 60% fewer rules for classification tasks and 16% fewer rules for time-to-event tasks on average, compared to AutoScore.

papers.title: HARMES: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Wearable Human Activity Recognition with Motion, Environmental Sensing and Sound
papers.summary: HARMES: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Wearable Human Activity Recognition with Motion, Environmental Sensing and Sound attacks the bottleneck that ADL recognition from wrist wearables fails when motion signatures are ambiguous, especially for near-identical household routines such as washing hands versus washing dishes or putting away dishes versus cleaning out the dishwasher. It challenges the old assumption that a single IMU stream or lab-scripted benchmark is enough, and pivots from uni-modal, controlled HAR toward privacy-preserving, in-home, cross-subject multi-modal sensing. The mechanical shift is concrete: synchronized dual-wrist wearable IMUs, wrist microphone audio, and a left-wrist atmospheric sensor pack (humidity, temperature, pressure) are recorded from 20 participants in their own homes over 80.53 hours, with on-the-fly labels, a three-clap synchronization protocol, and LOPO evaluation on 15 ADLs plus free-form background activity. Unlike nearby HAR datasets that stop at IMU+audio or rely on cameras, HARMES is the first public dataset to combine this sensor trio and to include both wrists and left-handed users. The benchmark uses late fusion over DeepConvLSTM IMU branches, an audio CNN-LSTM, and handcrafted humidity features. Results show the strongest configuration is IMU_R+IMU_L+audio, reaching 0.76 macro F1, versus 0.62 for dual IMUs alone and 0.70 for audio alone; humidity is weak alone yet reveals activity-specific context, especially for water-related classes, though its gains do not yet translate into better fusion performance. This points toward adaptive, longer-horizon, context-aware fusion and self-supervised wearable HAR under realistic home noise and null-class regimes.
papers.abstract: With each sensing modality exhibiting inherent strengths and limitations, multi-modal approaches for wearable Human Activity Recognition (HAR) are becoming increasingly relevant -- particularly for recognizing Activities of Daily Living (ADLs), where individual modalities often produce ambiguous signals for similar or complex activities. This work introduces HARMES, a multi-modal wearable dataset combining three wrist-recorded modalities: motion sensing via an Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), atmospheric environmental sensors (humidity, temperature, and pressure), and audio. Collected from 20 participants performing household activities in their own homes, HARMES totals over 80 hours of recorded data, with approximately three hours of labeled activity data per participant across 15 ADL classes. To the best of our knowledge, HARMES is the first dataset to combine this particular sensor trio, and it is nearly six times larger than the previously largest wrist-inertial-acoustic HAR dataset. In an extensive benchmark, we evaluate cross-subject generalization and conduct an ablation study revealing that modality contributions are activity-dependent and can provide complementary value, particularly for activities that are ambiguous from motion data alone. HARMES is freely available at Zenodo, alongside example code for loading the dataset and training models on GitHub.

papers.title: Isotropic Fourier Neural Operators
papers.summary: Isotropic Fourier Neural Operators attacks a specific mismatch inside Fourier Neural Operators: PDE surrogates are often trained with Fourier kernels that are free to learn orientation-specific mode couplings even when the underlying physics is isotropic, so the learned operator can waste capacity, overfit coordinate conventions, and fail to generalize under flips, transposes, and rotations. The paper pivots from the common FNO scaling story of “more modes, more parameters, more accuracy” toward symmetry-constrained operator learning, arguing that the bottleneck is not just parameter count but symmetry violation in the Fourier kernel itself. The mechanical shift is to replace the full complex Fourier-mode tensor with a smaller upper-triangular isotropic kernel, then generate the complete kernel by enforcing D4 spatial equivariance through reflections in x and y, transposition, and the real-valued conjugacy constraints of real fields. This makes the Fourier layer itself symmetry-preserving without expensive group convolutions, unlike nearby equivariant operators. In 2D this collapses the free kernel parameters by roughly 16×, and the paper claims an analogous 96× reduction in 3D. On the 2D Darcy flow benchmark trained with 1000 examples at 128×128 resolution, Iso-FNO uses 0.57M parameters versus 4.2M for the standard FNO, slightly underfits training error but improves test L2 and H2 error, and exactly generalizes to flipped and transposed inputs where vanilla FNO breaks. The result points toward neural operators whose next frontier is not only resolution invariance, but built-in geometric invariance and symmetry-aware parameter generation across field types and dimensions.
papers.abstract: Fourier Neural Operators are deep learning models that learn mappings between function spaces and can be used to learn and solve partial differential equations (PDEs), in some cases significantly faster than traditional PDE solvers. Within the model are Fourier layers, which apply linear transformations directly to the Fourier modes, with parameters depending on the wave numbers. However, most physical systems are isotropic, with the results being independent of the coordinate system chosen, but the linear transformations do not necessarily respect these symmetries. We propose a modification to the linear transformations that ensures spatial symmetries are respected, called the Isotropic Fourier Neural Operator, which both improves model performance and reduces the number of parameters by up to a factor of 16 in 2D and 96 in 3D.

papers.title: Gradient-Discrepancy Acquisition for Pool-Based Active Learning
papers.summary: Gradient-Discrepancy Acquisition for Pool-Based Active Learning attacks the acquisition bottleneck in pool-based active learning: under a tight labeling budget, which unlabeled points should be queried when uncertainty sampling and core-set style coverage are both brittle under dataset shift and model choice.
