The paper pivots from treating informativeness as predictive ambiguity or geometric spread toward a gradient-statistics mismatch view, inspired by a Luo et al. generalization-bound term, where a candidate is valuable if its training signal is most misaligned with what the current labeled set already explains. The mechanical shift is concrete: for each unlabeled point, the method forms a pseudo-label from the current model, computes a last-layer gradient embedding, and scores the point by gradient discrepancy between the candidate singleton and a labeled-set reference statistic; grad then takes the top-b discrepancies, while grad_seq updates the reference after each within-batch pick to suppress redundancy without explicit clustering. This is unusual relative to BADGE and EGL because it uses gradients not mainly for diversity or expected update magnitude, but as a reference-misalignment criterion that can be implemented efficiently with cached mean gradient embeddings. Experiments span 20 Newsgroups, ISOLET, OpenML 155, CIFAR-10, STL-10, CINIC-10, GTSRB, and SVHN with MLP, ResNet-18, VGG-16, and LeNet backbones. The main result is that DF-based querying is consistently competitive and often strongest overall, especially in later rounds, with grad typically more reliable than grad_seq, though early-stage gains can favor BADGE. This suggests a future AL direction centered on bound-inspired gradient herding, class-conditional reference updates, and shift-aware acquisition.
papers.abstract: The effectiveness of active learning hinges on the choice of the acquisition criterion by which a learning algorithm selects potentially informative data points whose label is subsequently queried. This paper proposes a novel gradient-based acquisition criterion, derived from a generalization bound introduced by Luo et al. (2022). This criterion can be applied in lieu of uncertainty measures in uncertainty sampling, or incorporated into diversity-based methods that consider the spread of sampled points in addition to the uncertainty of their labels. We provide a theoretical justification of the proposed acquisition criterion, and demonstrate its effectiveness in an empirical evaluation.

papers.title: Selective Prediction from Agreement: A Lipschitz-Consistent Version Space Approach
papers.summary: Selective Prediction from Agreement: A Lipschitz-Consistent Version Space Approach attacks a bottleneck in selective classification that standard confidence thresholding and learned reject heads do not solve: in a fixed unlabeled pool, when are labels actually identifiable from the few queried labels plus structural assumptions? The paper pivots from treating abstention as low confidence toward treating it as lack of forced agreement over a version space, replacing probabilistic uncertainty with certification over Lipschitz-constrained classification heads in a fixed embedding space. The mechanical shift is a two-sided margin-envelope construction: queried points supply center-margin lower bounds, Lipschitz propagation yields certified lower and upper envelopes for every class at every pool point, and the predictor outputs a class only when all heads in the Lipschitz-consistent version space agree; otherwise it abstains. This differs from conformal prediction and ordinary robustness certification because the feasible label set is not distribution-free or perturbation-based but explicitly tied to a constraint-defined hypothesis class on a transductive pool. The paper also introduces a monotone submodular geometric proxy for budgeted querying—union-of-balls coverage in representation space—so greedy acquisition retains the standard 1−1/e guarantee. On transductive CIFAR-10 and SVHN test pools with ResNet-18 embeddings, greedy acquisition expands certified coverage under small budgets and yields competitive risk-coverage curves on the certified domain, while remaining deliberately conservative. The work points toward certification-first selective prediction, iterative pseudo-label closure, and more relaxed sampled forcing rules that preserve interpretability while increasing coverage.
papers.abstract: We consider selective classification with abstention in the fixed-pool (or transductive) setting, where the unlabeled pool is given beforehand and only a subset of points can be queried for labels. Our main insight is to view selective prediction through agreement: given queried labels and Lipschitz margin constraints in an embedding space, the version space of Lipschitz-consistent classification heads is well defined. We obtain upper and lower Lipschitz margin bounds that define, for each pool point, a set of certified valid labels containing the prediction of every head in the version space. The model therefore predicts only when the label is forced (i.e., all consistent heads agree), and abstains otherwise. We also propose a monotone submodular geometric proxy for budgeted querying, and show that a greedy algorithm retains the standard approximation factor.

papers.title: Gradient-Gated DPO: Stabilizing Preference Optimization in Language Models
papers.summary: Gradient-Gated DPO: Stabilizing Preference Optimization in Language Models attacks a specific failure mode in preference tuning: DPO’s negative updates on rejected answers can create “squeezing,” where probability mass collapses onto a high-confidence argmax while both chosen and unrelated responses are suppressed, even in softmax logistic regression. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that pairwise preference loss alone is enough, and pivots from redesigning the objective toward controlling the gradient geometry of alignment. The mechanical shift is a detached, probability-aware gate applied only to the rejected-response term: Gate-DPO computes a valley statistic from the rejected sample, either a sequence-level geometric mean probability or a token-level lower-tail quantile, then uses a smooth sigmoid gate g(x) to attenuate the rejected gradient when the response already lies in a low-probability “valley,” while leaving the chosen-response gradient unchanged. This is unusual relative to nearby remedies like Extend, IPO, and Cal-DPO because it does not alter the preference objective itself; it modulates destructive gradient flow directly and is explicitly modular with those losses. Across Pythia-410M, Qwen-0.5B, and LLaMA-7B on Anthropic-HH and UltraFeedback, Gate-DPO consistently reduces squeezing, improves chosen-response likelihood, and preserves more of the overall distribution; token-level gating is strongest, and small gated models can outperform larger ungated ones. The result suggests a future alignment direction centered on gradient-dynamics-aware optimization rather than scale-only improvement, with open questions around on-policy training and broader task-level validation.
papers.abstract: Preference optimization has become a central paradigm for aligning large language models with human feedback. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) simplifies reinforcement learning from human feedback by directly optimizing pairwise preferences, removing the need for reward modeling and policy optimization. However, recent work shows that DPO exhibits a squeezing effect, where negative gradients applied to rejected responses concentrate probability mass on high-confidence predictions while suppressing alternative responses. This phenomenon arises even in simple softmax models and can lead to systematic probability collapse during training. We introduce Gradient-Gated Preference Optimization (Gate-DPO), a method that stabilizes training by modulating rejected gradients according to the model's probability geometry. When updates target extremely low-probability responses, the gate attenuates harmful gradients while preserving standard optimization behavior. Gate-DPO addresses this optimization pathology without modifying the underlying preference objective and is complementary to existing methods such as extended SFT, IPO, and Cal-DPO. Experiments across multiple architectures and preference datasets show that Gate-DPO consistently reduces squeezing and improves chosen-response likelihood. Mass-dynamics analysis further reveals healthier optimization behavior, with improved preferred responses and reduced suppression of the overall distribution. Notably, smaller gated models can exhibit stronger chosen-response improvements than larger ungated models, suggesting that controlling gradient dynamics, rather than scale alone, is key to stable and efficient alignment.

papers.title: CNNs for Vis-NIR Chemometrics: From Contradiction to Conditional Design
papers.summary: CNNs for Vis-NIR Chemometrics: From Contradiction to Conditional Design argues that the field’s recurring CNN-design disputes are not random inconsistency but the predictable result of unchecked moderators in water-dominated near-infrared measurements. The paper attacks a practical bottleneck: the same 1D-CNN can look best in one chemometrics study and worst in another because authors compare small versus large kernels, shallow versus deep stacks, raw spectra versus preprocessing, and single-domain training versus transfer learning under incompatible split strategies, tuning budgets, and shift exposure. It pivots from searching for a universal “best” architecture toward conditional design: architecture and preprocessing are treated as functions of spectral physics, dataset regime, and deployment scenario. The core mechanical shift is to replace template-driven model selection with ERF-aware design rules, where kernel width, depth, dilation, and multi-scale branches are matched to the width of informative spectral structure, especially broad water-band deformations in SSC-like tasks, while preprocessing is co-optimized rather than fixed upstream. Unusually, the review elevates validation protocol to a hidden hyperparameter, arguing that random splits can dominate rankings and inflate apparent gains relative to season-, batch-, or instrument-blocked evaluation. It also reframes interpretability as a falsification protocol, requiring ablation, stability, and sanity checks instead of plausibility-only saliency plots. The main claim is not a new network, but a methodological reorientation toward reproducible, physics-aware, deployment-aligned comparison, pointing future Vis-NIR AI toward factorial benchmarks, transfer-cost reporting, and shift-stress testing.
papers.abstract: Near-infrared (NIR; a.k.a.\ NIRS) deep-learning studies in chemometrics increasingly report mutually inconsistent conclusions regarding convolutional neural network (CNN) design, including small versus large kernels, shallow versus deep architectures, raw spectra versus preprocessing, and single-domain training versus transfer learning. As a result, the same architecture can appear superior in one study and inferior in another, creating a practical impasse for chemometric practitioners. In this review, we argue that these contradictions are not evidence of irreconcilable methods but a structurally expected consequence of uncontrolled moderating variables. Specifically, we trace recurring disagreements to (i) the indirect nature of Vis--NIR measurement in water-dominated matrices, (ii) mismatch between effective receptive field (ERF) and the width of informative spectral structure, and (iii) validation design (including split strategy, hyperparameter tuning budget, and exposure to deployment-like shifts) acting as a hidden hyperparameter that can dominate model ranking. Building on evidence from published chemometrics and spectroscopy studies, we propose a conditional design framework that links architecture and preprocessing choices to spectral physics, dataset regime, and intended deployment scenario. Overall, the proposed perspective moves DL Chemometrics from template-driven architecture selection toward reproducible, physics-aware, and deployment-aligned model comparison.

papers.title: CARD: Coarse-to-fine Autoregressive Modeling with Radix-based Decomposition for Transferable Free Energy Estimation
papers.summary: CARD: Coarse-to-fine Autoregressive Modeling with Radix-based Decomposition for Transferable Free Energy Estimation attacks the bottleneck that free-energy estimation is either classical-but-expensive, because alchemical FEP/TI/BAR demand long MD sampling, or deep-learning-fast-but-fragile, because prior zero-free-energy proposals such as flow-based DeepBAR are system-specific and do not transfer across atom counts or topologies. The paper pivots from “learn a better estimator for one target system” toward “learn a universal tractable proposal over arbitrary molecular systems,” replacing alchemical intermediate states with a zero-free-energy generative model whose log density can be inserted into BAR/MBAR for direct absolute free-energy computation. The core mechanical shift is a radix-based bijection that converts aligned 3D coordinates into mixed discrete-continuous sequences: coarse radix digits are generated first, then finer residuals, so the transformer autoregressively resolves global geometry before local detail. CARD further conditions on system context, uses topology-guided or distance-based atom ordering, injects reference-geometry distances into attention, and models bounded coordinate residuals with a Beta Mixture Model instead of Gaussian output heads. A two-stage objective combines exact NLL with an energy-alignment loss against force-field energies, correcting sampling bias. Across vacuum-to-toluene/water solvation, MM-to-ANI-2x endstate correction, and zero-shot aqueous tautomer shifts, CARD matches classical accuracy on unseen molecules and diverse topologies while delivering about 40× faster inference. The limitations—quadratic transformer scaling and PCA alignment instability for symmetric structures—point toward future transferable molecular generative models with stronger invariances and extension to larger protein-ligand systems.
papers.abstract: Estimating free energy differences quantifies thermodynamic preferences in molecular interactions, which is central to chemistry and drug discovery. Despite fruitful progress, existing methods still face key limitations: classical computational approaches remain prohibitively expensive due to their reliance on extensive molecular dynamics simulations, while deep learning-based methods are constrained by either less-expressive generative models or input dimensions tied to a specific system, resulting in negligible generalization. To address these challenges, we propose CARD, a generative framework that employs a novel radix-based decomposition to bijectively convert 3D coordinates into mixed discrete-continuous sequences, enabling coarse-to-fine autoregressive modeling with enhanced expressiveness. Notably, the model corresponds to a distribution with zero free energy, serving as a proposal for absolute free energy computation of arbitrary systems without relying on alchemical pathways. Experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate that CARD matches the accuracy of classical computational methods on unseen systems with diverse topologies, while achieving an approximately 40-fold speedup in inference.

papers.title: Spectral Model eXplainer: a chemically-grounded explainability framework for spectral-based machine learning models
papers.summary: Spectral Model eXplainer: a chemically-grounded explainability framework for spectral-based machine learning models attacks a specific spectroscopy bottleneck: post-hoc XAI methods such as SHAP, PFI, and VIP explain individual wavelengths or channels, but spectral reasoning is physically continuous and chemically organized into zones, so their outputs fragment peaks, ignore collinearity, and require ad hoc aggregation before a scientist can read them. The paper pivots from feature-attribution on raw variables toward zone-native, domain-anchored explanation of already-trained spectral classifiers, replacing isolated scores with expert-defined spectral intervals summarized by one-component PCA, quantile-defined logical predicates, perturbation-based predicate relevance, and a directed weighted ranking graph whose global importance is Local Reaching Centrality. The mechanical shift is especially distinctive: each zone is compressed into a PCA score, thresholds are then back-projected into threshold spectra in natural measurement units, turning an abstract predicate like a zone score cutoff into a visual boundary that can be overlaid on measured XRF/GRS spectra. Unlike nearby XAI work, SMX is model-agnostic and post-hoc across PLS, SVM, and MLP, yet still outputs chemically interpretable predicates rather than hundreds of variable scores. Evaluated on eight real XRF/Gamma-ray datasets plus a synthetic ground-truth benchmark, it matched baselines in faithfulness, improved domain alignment and stability in several settings, and produced simpler explanations than PFI/VIP while remaining comparable to SHAP; the main limitation is dependence on pre-specified zone quality and its current focus on classification. This points toward spectral-native XAI that is physically grounded, perturbation-driven, and usable for screening, calibration, and decision support.
papers.abstract: Spectral-based machine learning models have been increasingly deployed in chemometrics and spectroscopy, where predictive accuracy is as important as explainability. Current employed eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) methods are largely adapted from tabular or generic multivariate domains, assigning relevance to isolated spectral variables rather than to the chemically meaningful spectral zones. Widely adopted tools such as SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), Permutation Feature Importance (PFI), and Variable Importance in Projection scores (VIP) were not designed for the physical continuity and high collinearity of spectral data, and their variable-level outputs require post-hoc aggregation to recover zone-level information. This study introduces the Spectral Model eXplainer (SMX), a post-hoc, global, model-agnostic XAI framework that explains spectral classifiers through expert-informed spectral zones. SMX summarizes each zone via PCA, defines quantile-based logical predicates, estimates predicate relevance with perturbation in stochastic subsamples, and aggregates bag-wise rankings in a directed weighted graph summarized by Local Reaching Centrality. A key component is threshold spectrum reconstruction, which back-projects predicate boundaries to the original spectral domain in natural measurement units, enabling direct visual comparison with measured spectra. The method was evaluated on eight real spectral datasets (six based on X-ray Fluorescence--XRF and two based on Gamma-ray Spectrometry) and one synthetic benchmark with known gr

papers.title: MSMixer: Learned Multi-Scale Temporal Mixing with Complementary Linear Shortcut for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting
papers.summary: MSMixer: Learned Multi-Scale Temporal Mixing with Complementary Linear Shortcut for Long-Term Time Series Forecasting attacks the failure mode of lightweight forecasters that compress a long look-back into one temporal resolution and then miss either fast oscillations, daily/weekly cycles, or slow macro-trends. It pivots from the old choice between expensive attention and single-scale MLP/linear baselines toward a deliberately simple multi-scale representation: instead of one projection, MSMixer runs three channel-independent branches on the same window at 1×, 4×, and 16× average-pooled resolutions, each with its own two-layer MLP, then lets a learned softmax gate reweight the branches per dataset. The mechanical shift is completed by a DLinear complementary shortcut that re-injects full-window trend/seasonality context, counteracting the information loss caused by coarse down-sampling; RevIN is used to stabilize distribution shift, and all paths are fused with a learnable scalar. Compared with nearby work like TimeMixer or PatchTST, the design is flatter and cheaper: fixed pooling replaces adaptive decomposition, independent per-scale MLPs replace interscale mixing, and the model stays O(T) with only 112K parameters at H=96. On ETTh1/ETTh2 and ETTm1/ETTm2 under standard chronological splits and three seeds, it achieves the lowest average MSE among lightweight models (0.357), wins 12 of 16 settings, and is best or second-best on 9 of 16 versus Transformer baselines while using 5× fewer parameters than PatchTST. The paper points toward data-driven scale selection, cross-variate extensions, and lightweight multi-scale feature extractors for larger forecasting systems.
papers.abstract: Long-term time series forecasting requires models that simultaneously capture rapid oscillations, medium-range periodicities, and slowly evolving macro-trends from a fixed look-back window. Existing lightweight MLP-based models typically operate on a single temporal resolution, limiting their ability to explicitly model patterns at multiple scales. We propose MSMixer, a channel-independent multi-scale MLP architecture that addresses this limitation through three complementary innovations: (i) three parallel scale branches at down-sample factors {1x, 4x, 16x} with independent MLP blocks, (ii) a learnable softmax gate that dynamically weighs branch outputs, and (iii) a DLinear complementary shortcut that provides full-window trend and seasonality context. MSMixer contains only 112K parameters at H=96 and runs at O(T) complexity. Evaluated on four ETT benchmarks with standard chronological splits and three random seeds, MSMixer achieves the lowest average MSE (0.357) among lightweight models, outperforming DLinear (0.386, -7.4%) and NLinear (0.365, -2.1%), winning 12 of 16 configurations. Against five Transformer-based baselines from the literature, MSMixer achieves best or second-best MSE in 9 of 16 configurations while using 5x fewer parameters than PatchTST. Ablation and sensitivity analyses confirm the complementary contributions of the multi-scale branches and the DLinear shortcut.

papers.title: Federated Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Mobile Crowdsensing under Incomplete Information
papers.summary: Federated Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Mobile Crowdsensing under Incomplete Information attacks the bottleneck that optimal mobile crowdsensing task participation is defined by perfect non-causal knowledge of future tasks, competitors, batteries, channels, and budgets—information that real energy-harvesting mobile units never have, especially when availability changes, regions of interest filter who can even bid, and repeated collisions waste scarce energy. The paper pivots away from centralized allocation and snapshot matching toward a fully decentralized learning game in which each mobile unit optimizes its own proposal, compute, and transmit-power decisions from local experience, while still coordinating through federation rather than raw-data sharing. The mechanical shift is FDRL-PPO: each unit runs PPO locally on a Markov-game state containing battery, averaged channel, location, and task descriptors, then uploads only learned model weights; the platform aggregates them with reward-weighted federated averaging, and each unit blends global and local parameters via a reward-based mixing factor χk, letting new or weakly informed units borrow policy structure without exposing private trajectories, battery states, or channels. Unlike nearby MCS RL work that is either fully centralized, purely independent, or assumes static participation, this scheme is designed for fragmented learning, join-ins/drop-outs, and competition plus cooperation. On synthetic and real commercial MCS data, it nearly matches the optimal complete-information upper bound while improving task completion, fairness, collision rate, and energy use over IPPO, MAPPO, greedy, and random baselines. This points toward privacy-preserving federated control loops for dynamic edge markets where policy sharing substitutes for impossible global knowledge.
papers.abstract: Mobile crowdsensing (MCS) is a distributed sensing architecture that utilizes existing sensors on mobile units (MUs) to perform sensing tasks. A mobile crowdsensing platform (MCSP) publishes the sensing tasks and the MUs decide whether to participate in exchange for money. The MCS system is dynamic: the task requirements, the MUs' availability, and their available resources change over time. The MUs aim to find an efficient task participation strategy to maximize their income while the MCSP focuses on maximizing the number of completed tasks. As optimal strategies require perfect non-causal information about the MCS system, which is unavailable in realistic scenarios, the main challenge is to find an efficient task participation strategy for the MUs under incomplete information. To this end, a novel fully decentralized federated deep reinforcement learning algorithm, FDRL-PPO, is proposed. FDRL-PPO enables every MU to learn its own task participation strategy based on its experiences, available resources, and preferences, without relying on perfect non-causal information about the MCS system. To replenish their batteries, the MUs rely on energy harvesting. As a result, their available energy varies over time, leading to varying availability and fragmented learning experiences. To mitigate these challenges, the proposed approach leverages federated learning, enabling MUs to collaboratively improve their models without sharing private raw data like their own experiences. By exchanging only learned models, MUs collectively compensate for individual limitations, and find more scalable, robust, and efficient task participation strategies. Comprehensive evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that FDRL-PPO consistently outperforms benchmark algorithms in terms of task completion ratio, fairness in task completion, energy consumption, and number of conflicting proposals.

papers.title: Visual Latents Know More Than They Say: Unsilencing Latent Reasoning in MLLMs
papers.summary: Visual Latents Know More Than They Say: Unsilencing Latent Reasoning in MLLMs attacks a specific failure mode in multimodal latent reasoning: existing latent visual tokens may become semantically richer during training yet still fail to influence the final answer, because the shared autoregressive objective learns a shortcut that routes prediction through direct visual input and pushes latents into transition-like, almost silenced states. This challenges the common assumption behind Latent Visual Reasoning that better latent supervision and answer training can be optimized jointly in one parameter space without interference. The paper pivots away from training-time entanglement and toward inference-time disentanglement: instead of updating backbone weights, it freezes the MLLM and optimizes only the latent reasoning span per instance. The mechanical shift is two-stage. Stage I performs query-guided contrastive latent–visual alignment, ranking image patches by query relevance and assigning each latent token distinct positive/negative patch chunks to improve semantic specificity while avoiding collapse. Stage II then applies a confidence-progression reward, using stochastic local exploration to make token distributions along the latent span progressively more concentrated, thereby forcing the answer path to actually traverse the latent reasoning rather than bypass it. Across eight benchmarks and four backbones, this yields consistent gains, especially on perception-heavy and illusion-prone tasks, with strong results on Qwen2.5-VL, R1-OneVision, and VLAA-Thinking. The main implication is a shift toward test-time latent control as a new lever for multimodal reasoning, though at the cost of added per-sample inference optimization and dependence on patch selection and reward design.
papers.abstract: Continuous latent-space reasoning offers a compact alternative to textual chain-of-thought for multimodal models, enabling high-dimensional visual evidence to be integrated without explicit reasoning tokens. However, we identify a previously overlooked optimization pathology in existing latent visual reasoning methods: although visual latents become semantically enriched during training, their contribution to final answer prediction is systematically suppressed. Within the shared parameter space, the autoregressive objective favors shortcut reliance on direct visual input, driving latent tokens toward transition-like states rather than informative reasoning content. We term this phenomenon Silenced Visual Latents. To address it, we disentangle the two conflicting objectives by directly optimizing the latent reasoning at inference time, keeping backbone parameters frozen. In Stage I, visual latents are warmed up via query-guided contrastive latent--visual alignment, improving semantic quality while preventing latent collapse. In Stage II, the latent reasoning is further optimized via a confidence-progression reward, which incentivizes predicted token distributions along the latent span to become progressively more concentrated, routing predictions through the latent reasoning rather than bypassing it. Experiments across eight benchmarks and four model backbones show that inference-time latent optimization, without any parameter updates, effectively unleashes the suppressed reasoning capacity of visual latents.

papers.title: Bolek: A Multimodal Language Model for Molecular Reasoning
papers.summary: Bolek: A Multimodal Language Model for Molecular Reasoning attacks a specific bottleneck in drug-discovery AI: molecular property systems are either accurate but opaque scores, or fluent language models whose explanations are weakly tied to the actual molecule. The paper challenges the assumption that molecule-text caption alignment and SMILES-only prompting are enough for reasoning, pivoting from “describe the molecule” toward verifiable, feature-centered chemical judgment. The mechanical shift is minimal but sharp: a compact Qwen3-4B-Instruct decoder is given a single learned Morgan-fingerprint token (2048-bit, radius 2) through a projector, then jointly fine-tuned on large-scale alignment tasks for molecule description, RDKit/Mordred descriptor prediction, and substructure detection, plus synthetic chain-of-thought supervision on 15 TDC binary endpoints built from literature priors and top random-forest descriptors. Unlike graph-heavy or tool-augmented nearby work, Bolek keeps the architecture almost unchanged and instead makes the molecular signal natively addressable inside language. On TDC, it beats its base model on all yes/no endpoints and 13/15 CoT endpoints, raising mean ROC/PR AUC from 0.55 to 0.76, and outperforms TxGemma-9B-Chat on 13/15 binary tasks despite being less than half the size. Its explanations are unusually grounded, citing numeric descriptors 10–100× more often and matching RDKit for TPSA, MolLogP, and MolWt (ρ=0.87–0.91). The remaining limitation is that CoT supervision can over-impose coarse priors, especially when Morgan bits miss stereochemistry or conformation, pointing toward RL with verifiable rewards and richer fused molecular views.
papers.abstract: Molecular property models increasingly support high-stakes drug-discovery decisions, but their outputs are often difficult to audit: classical predictors return scores without rationale, while language models can produce fluent explanations weakly grounded in the input molecule. We introduce Bolek, a compact multimodal language model that grounds natural-language reasoning in molecular structure by injecting a Morgan fingerprint embedding into an instruction-tuned text decoder. Bolek is fine-tuned on molecular alignment tasks, including molecule description, RDKit descriptor prediction, and substructure detection, and on downstream reasoning over 15 TDC binary classification tasks using synthetic chains-of-thought anchored in concrete molecular features. Across these tasks, Bolek outperforms its Qwen3-4B-Instruct base on all endpoints in yes/no mode and on 13 of 15 in chain-of-thought mode, raising mean ROC/PR AUC from 0.55 to 0.76. It also outperforms TxGemma-9B-Chat on 13 of 15 binary classification tasks despite being less than half its size. Bolek's explanations are more grounded than those of the baseline LLMs: it cites numerical descriptors 10-100x more often per chain-of-thought, and the cited values agree strongly with RDKit for key descriptors such as TPSA, MolLogP, and MolWt (Spearman rho = 0.87-0.91). Generalisation extends beyond the training panel: on 15 unseen TDC classification endpoints, Bolek matches TxGemma on five, and it produces non-trivial rank correlations on three held-out regression endpoints despite never seeing downstream regression during training. These results suggest that targeted modality injection and reasoning supervision tied to verifiable molecular features can yield compact, auditable molecular reasoning models.

papers.title: A decoupled diffusion planner that adapts to changing cost limits by using cost-conditioned generation for safety and reward gradients for performance
papers.summary: A decoupled diffusion planner that adapts to changing cost limits by using cost-conditioned generation for safety and reward gradients for performance attacks a specific offline safe RL failure mode: deployment-time budgets are not fixed, yet most planners either hard-code one constraint level or rely on noisy cost-classifier guidance that drifts off safe support as horizons grow. The paper challenges the assumption that safety and reward should be handled as competing gradients, and instead pivots from classifier-guided or autoregressive budget adaptation toward sampling a constrained trajectory distribution in which the cost limit is a support restriction and reward is only a preference within that region. The mechanical shift is Safe Decoupled Guidance Diffusion (SDGD): classifier-free guidance conditions the diffusion model directly on the desired cost limit to bias generation toward feasible trajectories, while reward-gradient guidance then refines return without using a cost predictor at reverse steps. To stop reward optimization from dragging samples into higher-cost regions, Feasible Trajectory Relabeling retrains the reward head on relabeled returns that penalize prefix-infeasible trajectories, so the learned reward gradient is less aligned with cost-increasing directions; the authors also give a first-order analysis showing this suppresses reward-induced cost drift under prefix-restorative alignment. On the DSRL benchmark of 38 tasks, SDGD adapts without retraining, satisfies the safety constraint on 36/38 tasks, and achieves the highest reward among safe methods on 21 tasks. This points toward budget-conditioned generative planners whose control lever is the sampling geometry itself, not post hoc constraint correction.
papers.abstract: Offline safe reinforcement learning often requires policies to adapt at deployment time to safety budgets that vary across episodes or change within a single episode. While diffusion-based planners enable flexible trajectory generation, existing guidance schemes often treat reward improvement and constraint satisfaction as competing gradient objectives, which can lead to unreliable safety compliance under cost limits. We reinterpret adaptive safe trajectory generation as sampling from a constrained trajectory distribution, where the budget restricts the trajectory region, and reward shapes preferences within that region. This perspective motivates Safe Decoupled Guidance Diffusion (SDGD), which conditions classifier-free guidance on the cost limit to bias sampling toward trajectories satisfying the specified limit, while using reward-gradient guidance to refine trajectories for higher return. Because direct reward guidance can increase return while also steering samples toward trajectories with higher cumulative cost, we introduce Feasible Trajectory Relabeling (FTR) to reshape reward targets and discourage such directions. We further provide a first-order sampling-time analysis showing that FTR suppresses reward-induced cost drift under a prefix-restorative alignment condition. Extensive evaluations on the DSRL benchmark show that SDGD achieves the strongest safety compliance among baselines, satisfying the constraint on 94.7% of tasks (36/38), while obtaining the highest reward among safe methods on 21 tasks.

papers.title: A Closed-Form Persistence-Landmark Pipeline for Certified Point-Cloud and Graph Classification
papers.summary: A Closed-Form Persistence-Landmark Pipeline for Certified Point-Cloud and Graph Classification attacks the bottleneck that persistence-diagram methods are usually tuned by held-out validation and judged only by upper stability bounds, which leaves no way to know whether a diagram vectorization preserves separability or can certify a single prediction. The paper pivots from learned, calibration-dependent topological summaries toward an analytically fixed pipeline, PLACE, that uses persistent-homology signatures but replaces the Mitra-Virk n-fold coordinate composition with a sparse landmark summation over a multiscale birth-death grid. The mechanical shift is decisive: each diagram point is passed through compactly supported hat coordinates, the block weights are chosen in closed form by maximizing the distortion constant λ(ν), descriptor choice is ranked without validation by either a Ledoit-Wolf Mahalanobis margin or the simpler Δ/√ℓ surrogate, and prediction-time certification is decided once from training statistics via Pinelis and Gaussian plug-in radii, with no per-example overhead. Unusual relative to nearby TDA work, PLACE is simultaneously a classifier, a descriptor selector, and a per-prediction certificate, while still retaining an explicit lower distortion bound on bottleneck geometry under a non-interference condition. Across 12 benchmarks, including Orbit5k, MUTAG, COX2, and heterogeneous chemical-graph pools, it is strongest among diagram-based methods on Orbit5k, matches the best topology baseline on MUTAG and COX2, and shows that failures on NCI1/NCI109 come from descriptor blindness and pool coverage limits rather than the embedding itself. This points toward certified, data-adaptive topological learning with analytically controlled feature selection and eventually continuous landmark configurations.
papers.abstract: We introduce PLACE (Persistence-Landmark Analytic Classification Engine), a closed-form pipeline for classifying point clouds and graphs through their persistent-homology signatures. Three quantitative guarantees -- a margin-based excess-risk rate, a closed-form descriptor-selection rule, and a per-prediction certificate -- are derived from training labels alone, with no learned weights or held-out calibration. The embedding sums Mitra-Virk single-point coordinate functions over a sparse landmark grid; closed-form weights maximize a structural distortion constant $λ(ν)$ (a Lipschitz lower bound on $\mathcal{D}_n$ under non-interference). (i) An $O(kR/(Δ\sqrt{m_{\min}}))$ margin bound, driven by class-mean separation $Δ$ and embedding radius $R$, matched by a sample-starved minimax lower bound. (ii) The Mahalanobis margin under Ledoit-Wolf-shrunk covariance is the strongest closed-form descriptor selector on a heterogeneous 64-descriptor chemical-graph pool (mean Spearman $ρ\approx +0.54$ across 10 benchmarks, positive on 9 of 10); the isotropic surrogate $Δ/\sqrt\ell$ admits a closed-form selection-consistency rate on homogeneous (14-15 descriptor) protein/social pools. (iii) A training-time-decided certificate with no per-prediction overhead, in non-asymptotic Pinelis and asymptotic Gaussian plug-in forms. Empirically, PLACE is the strongest diagram-based method on Orbit5k and matches the strongest topology-based baseline within statistical noise on MUTAG and COX2. The remaining gaps fall into two diagnosable regimes: descriptor blindness on NCI1/NCI109, and pool-coverage limits elsewhere. Both radii exceed the firing threshold $\hatΔ/2$ on every benchmark at our training-set sizes, dominated by the $\sqrt\ell$ scaling of the multivariate-norm bound; the per-prediction certificate is constructive but not yet operational at these sizes.

papers.title: Trust, but Verify: Peeling Low-Bit Transformer Networks for Training Monitoring
papers.summary: Trust, but Verify: Peeling Low-Bit Transformer Networks for Training Monitoring attacks the blind spot that training loss and aggregate convergence curves cannot tell whether a transformer is actually well optimized layer by layer, especially when expensive decoder-only LLMs are trained once, then frozen or reused under quantization where fragile layers can quietly cap performance. It challenges the implicit assumption that a smooth end-to-end loss curve means effective optimization, and pivots from monolithic solver diagnostics or representation probes toward data-aware certification of training quality. The mechanical shift is a layer-wise “peeling” construction: freeze a trained transformer, cache intermediate hidden states, then locally re-optimize lightweight YES reference models one layer at a time against those intermediate targets, using alternative target permutations and, in low-bit settings, delayed quantization/proximal updates to build conservative achievable baselines. Unlike probing, the baseline is not a classifier on top of activations; unlike standard ablations, it produces a reference trajectory that can be compared directly to the trained model throughout training. Experiments on a 4-layer LLaMA-style model on WikiText-2, OpenLLaMA-3B fine-tuning on WikiText-103, and channel-wise 1-bit/ternary regimes show these YES bounds often match or surpass the trained network at intermediate epochs, exposing optimization inefficiency, layer redundancy, and cases where apparent convergence is not effective optimality. The paper points toward certification-centric monitoring, architecture-aware early stopping, and pruning/fine-tuning strategies for low-bit transformer training.
papers.abstract: Understanding whether deep neural networks are effectively optimized remains challenging, as training occurs in highly nonconvex landscapes and standard metrics provide limited visibility into layer-wise learning quality. This challenge is particularly acute for transformer-based language models, where training is expensive, models are often reused in frozen form, and poorly optimized layers can silently degrade performance. We propose a layer-wise peeling framework for monitoring training dynamics, in which each transformer layer is locally optimized against intermediate representations of the trained model. By constructing lightweight, layer-specific reference solutions and projecting layers onto multiple intermediate outputs via different permutations, we obtain achievable baselines that enable fine-grained diagnosis of under-optimized layers. Experiments on decoder-only transformer models show that these layer-wise reference bounds can match or even surpass the trained model at various stages of training, exposing inefficiencies that remain hidden in aggregate loss curves. We further demonstrate that this analysis remains effective under binarization and quantized settings, where training dynamics are particularly fragile. Across all numerical results, the proposed bounds consistently separate apparent convergence from effective optimality, highlighting optimization opportunities that are invisible when relying on training loss alone.

papers.title: Enhancing RL Generalizability in Robotics through SHAP Analysis of Algorithms and Hyperparameters
papers.summary: Enhancing RL Generalizability in Robotics through SHAP Analysis of Algorithms and Hyperparameters targets a practical failure mode in robotics RL: policies that look strong in one simulator collapse under MuJoCo↔PyBullet transfer because performance is hypersensitive not just to environment shift but to the exact algorithm and hyperparameter recipe used to train them. The paper challenges the usual RL generalization workflow that treats configuration tuning as a black-box search problem or attributes transfer failure mainly to domain randomization and environment design; it pivots from “find a better policy by more training” toward “diagnose which configuration components induce domain sensitivity, then select configurations that minimize the gap.” The core mechanical shift is a SHAP-based framework that trains many actor-critic agents (PPO, A2C, DDPG, SAC) across four Gymnasium locomotion tasks, measures source-target generalization gaps over 9,600 models, and fits a surrogate explainer whose Shapley values decompose the gap into algorithm and hyperparameter contributions. Unusually, the method is paired with a theoretical link between Shapley values, Lipschitz-style sensitivity to physics parameters, and minimal generalization gap, turning explainability into an optimization lever rather than a post hoc interpretation tool. The experiments show consistent cross-task impact patterns, strong interaction effects such as learning_rate×gamma in DDPG, and successful SHAP-guided selection that predicts favorable A2C settings and pathological SAC settings. This points toward a future of explainable RL configuration control, where transfer robustness is engineered through sensitivity-aware hyperparameter governance rather than brute-force sweeps.
papers.abstract: Despite significant advances in Reinforcement Learning (RL), model performance remains highly sensitive to algorithm and hyperparameter configurations, while generalization gaps across environments complicate real-world deployment. Although prior work has studied RL generalization, the relative contribution of specific configurations to the generalization gap has not been quantitatively decomposed and systematically leveraged for configuration selection. To address this limitation, we propose an explainable framework that evaluates RL performance across robotic environments using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to quantify configuration impacts. We establish a theoretical foundation connecting Shapley values to generalizability, empirically analyze configuration impact patterns, and introduce SHAP-guided configuration selection to enhance generalization. Our results reveal distinct patterns across algorithms and hyperparameters, with consistent configuration impacts across diverse tasks and environments. By applying these insights to configuration selection, we achieve improved RL generalizability and provide actionable guidance for practitioners.

papers.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning for Detecting Structural Anomalies in European Regional Statistics
papers.summary: Unsupervised Machine Learning for Detecting Structural Anomalies in European Regional Statistics attacks a validation bottleneck in official statistics: range edits, ratio checks, and single-variable z-scores can miss regions that are not extreme on any one indicator but form unusual multivariate socioeconomic configurations. The paper pivots from treating anomaly detection as a one-dimensional data-quality screen toward using it as a structural diagnostic for regional divergence in the European Statistical System. Its mechanical shift is to represent each NUTS2 region as a standardized point in a four-indicator space drawn from Eurostat API data for 2022—GDP per capita in PPS, unemployment, tertiary educational attainment, and population density—then compare five detectors (univariate z-scores, Mahalanobis distance, Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, and One-Class SVM) and require at least three votes to label a region a structural anomaly. That ensemble rule is unusual nearby work because it deliberately filters method-specific noise and separates “atypical but real” regional profiles from likely errors. On 260 regions, the unsupervised methods consistently surface 11 anomalies, including Brussels, Vienna, Berlin, Prague, Hamburg, and Istanbul on the high-performing metropolitan side, and Castilla-La Mancha, Extremadura, Northern Hungary, and Slovak regions on the disadvantaged side. The main implication is that multivariate anomaly scores can complement existing statistical QA by flagging territorially meaningful divergence early; the limitation is that the cross-sectional four-variable design ignores temporal dynamics and spatial autocorrelation, pointing toward longitudinal and spatially aware anomaly detection for future regional monitoring.
papers.abstract: Ensuring the coherence of regional socio-economic statistics is a central task for national statistical institutes. Traditional validation tools, such as range edits, ratio checks, or univariate outlier detection, are effective for identifying extreme values in individual series but are less suited for detecting unusual combinations of indicators in high-dimensional settings. This paper proposes an unsupervised machine learning framework for identifying structurally atypical regional profiles within Europe using publicly available Eurostat data. We construct a cross-sectional dataset of NUTS2 regions (2022) covering four key indicators: GDP per capita in PPS, unemployment rate, tertiary educational attainment, and population density. We apply and compare five anomaly detection techniques, univariate z-scores, Mahalanobis distance, Isolation Forest, Local Outlier Factor, and One-Class SVM, and classify a region as a structural anomaly if it is flagged by at least three of the five methods. The findings show that machine learning methods identify a consistent set of regions whose multivariate profiles diverge substantially from the EU-wide pattern. These include both highly developed metropolitan economies (Brussels, Vienna, Berlin, Prague) and regions with persistent socio-economic disadvantages (Central and Western Slovakia, Northern Hungary, Castilla-La Mancha, Extremadura), as well as Istanbul, whose profile differs markedly from EU capital regions. Importantly, these anomalies do not necessarily signal data quality issues; rather, they reflect meaningful structural divergence that warrants analytical or policy attention. The proposed framework is fully reproducible, scalable, and compatible with existing validation workflows, offering a flexible tool for early detection of unusual regional configurations within the European Statistical System.

papers.title: SpecKV: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Compression-Aware Gamma Selection
papers.summary: SpecKV: Adaptive Speculative Decoding with Compression-Aware Gamma Selection attacks a serving bottleneck that most speculative decoding systems leave untouched: the speculation length γ is usually frozen at 4, even though the best draft-window size changes with task difficulty and, more subtly, with how the target model is compressed. The paper pivots from the old assumption that γ is a static tuning knob independent of deployment regime toward a per-step control problem where compression and speculation are coupled optimization dimensions. The mechanical shift is lightweight but consequential: instead of hand-picking a universal γ, SpecKV reads zero-cost signals already produced by the draft model—mean/max entropy and mean/min confidence—and feeds them, along with compression level, into a tiny MLP that predicts acceptance rate for candidate γ values and selects the γ maximizing expected tokens per speculation step. The approach is unusual in that it does not add another draft model, tree search, or target-side probe; it turns discarded uncertainty traces into a controller for runtime adaptation. Using a manual speculative decoding loop over Llama 3.2 1B/3B on a single RTX 3090, the authors profile 5,112 step-level records across FP16, INT8, and NF4 plus code, math, chat, and summarization prompts, showing that optimal γ shifts upward under INT8 but differs by task. SpecKV-fast reaches a 56.0% improvement over fixed-γ=4 with only 0.34 ms overhead, suggesting a future of compression-aware, online adaptive decoding and broader inference controllers for quantized LLM serving.
papers.abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a small draft model to propose candidate tokens that a larger target model verifies. A critical hyperparameter in this process is the speculation length~$γ$, which determines how many tokens the draft model proposes per step. Nearly all existing systems use a fixed~$γ$ (typically~4), yet empirical evidence suggests that the optimal value varies across task types and, crucially, depends on the compression level applied to the target model. In this paper, we present \textbf{SpecKV}, a lightweight adaptive controller that selects~$γ$ per speculation step using signals extracted from the draft model itself. We profile speculative decoding across 4~task categories, 4~speculation lengths, and 3~compression levels (FP16, INT8, NF4), collecting 5,112 step-level records with per-step acceptance rates, draft entropy, and draft confidence. We demonstrate that the optimal~$γ$ shifts across compression regimes and that draft model confidence and entropy are strong predictors of acceptance rate (correlation~$\approx 0.56$). SpecKV uses a small MLP trained on these signals to maximize expected tokens per speculation step, achieving a 56.0\% improvement over the fixed-$γ$=4 baseline with only 0.34\,ms overhead per decision ($<$0.5\% of step time). The improvement is statistically significant ($p < 0.001$, paired bootstrap test). We release all profiling data, trained models, and notebooks as open-source artifacts.

papers.title: NeuroState-Bench: A Human-Calibrated Benchmark for Commitment Integrity in LLM Agent Profiles
papers.summary: NeuroState-Bench: A Human-Calibrated Benchmark for Commitment Integrity in LLM Agent Profiles attacks the blind spot of outcome-only agent evaluation: a multi-turn system can land the right final answer while silently losing the commitments that make its reasoning coherent, auditable, and robust. The paper pivots from endpoint success toward probe-elicited commitment consistency, explicitly rejecting the old assumption that hidden activations or final answers alone reveal whether an agent preserved bindings, sources, constraints, or repaired contradictions correctly. Its mechanical shift is benchmark-defined side-query probing: each deterministic task carries gold commitment structure plus separate probes asked over the full prompt, so the benchmark measures whether elicited commitments match the task’s intended state without appending probes back into the solve trace. NeuroState-Bench releases 144 tasks and 306 probes across eight cognitively motivated failure families, clean and distractor variants, and three difficulty bands, then human-calibrates the construct with 104 sampled task units, 216 raw annotations, and 108 adjudicated rows. The main 32-profile grid spans a fixed local subset and matched hosted frontier models, revealing that task success and commitment integrity diverge sharply: 31 of 32 profiles reorder when integrity replaces success, the success leader is not the integrity leader, and integrity rankings are more stable under distractors. HCCIS-CORE is the primary confidence-free score, outperforming legacy and neural-augmented comparators on construct alignment and PR-AUC while exposing a future direction where agent benchmarks score state preservation, provenance fidelity, and failure diagnostics rather than only completion.
papers.abstract: Outcome-only evaluation under-specifies whether an evaluated agent profile preserves the commitments required to solve a multi-turn task coherently. NeuroState-Bench is a human-calibrated benchmark that operationalizes commitment integrity through benchmark-defined side-query probes rather than inferred hidden activations. The released inventory contains 144 deterministic tasks and 306 benchmark-defined side-query probes spanning eight cognitively motivated failure families, paired clean and distractor variants, and three difficulty bands. The main 32-profile evaluation contains a fixed 16-profile local subset and a matched 16-profile hosted large-model subset evaluated through the same benchmark pipeline. Human calibration uses the final merged reporting scope: 104 sampled task units, 216 raw annotations, and 108 adjudicated task rows, with weighted kappa = 0.977 and ICC(2,1) = 0.977. Empirically, task success and commitment integrity diverge across this expanded grid: the success leader is not the integrity leader, 31 of 32 profiles change rank when integrity replaces task success, and integrity rankings are more stable under distractor perturbation. The primary confidence-free score HCCIS-CORE reaches 0.8469 AUC and 0.6992 PR-AUC for post-probe diagnostic discrimination of terminal task failure; the legacy full heuristic variant HCCIS-FULL reaches 0.7997 AUC and 0.6410 PR-AUC. Probe accuracy and state drift achieve slightly higher ROC-AUC, 0.8587, and better Brier/ECE, while HCCIS-CORE has substantially higher point-estimate PR-AUC and remains more closely tied to the benchmark's intended construct. The exploratory neural-augmented variant HCCIS+N is weaker overall, and a randomized subspace control approaches chance. NeuroState-Bench therefore contributes a calibrated evaluation axis for exposing commitment failures over a broader model grid than the original local-only subset.

papers.title: Sheaf-Theoretic Planning: A Categorical Foundation for Resilient Multi-Agent Autonomous Systems
papers.summary: Sheaf-Theoretic Planning: A Categorical Foundation for Resilient Multi-Agent Autonomous Systems attacks the failure of classical event calculus and situation calculus to reason under unobserved interventions, interrupted plans, and belief-reality divergence in open worlds, where a closed-world assumption turns missing observations into inconsistency rather than uncertainty. The paper pivots from monolithic, globally indexed temporal logic toward a local-to-global geometric view of time as an interval category equipped with a Grothendieck topology, so that “truth” is carried by compatible local sections rather than a single timeline. The mechanical shift is to represent world, memory, and goal as sheaves over the same site, encode actions as natural transformations on F World, and cast abduction as a pullback that recovers the action histories explaining a mismatch between remembered and observed states. This is unusual compared with nearby planning work because inconsistency is not patched by ad hoc non-monotonic rules; instead, failure to glue local observations becomes the signal for abductive repair, while multi-agent consensus is handled through sheaf gluing and, in the spectral extension, sheaf Laplacians and cohomology as obstruction diagnostics. The implementation path is concrete: a Prolog-based event-calculus core with tabling, TCP-linked Raspberry Pi 4B plus ESP32 robots, and restricted action-search horizons reportedly solve cases with up to 1000 time points and 50 fluents in under 2 seconds. The paper points toward verified, decentralized, proof-carrying autonomous swarms where resilience is a categorical property of the reasoning substrate rather than an external patch.
papers.abstract: The challenge of engineering autonomous agents capable of navigating the stochastic and adversarial nature of the physical world has historically resided at the intersection of symbolic logic and control theory. Traditional multi-agent system (MAS) frameworks have relied heavily on monolithic logical models -- primarily variations of the event calculus and situation calculus -- to represent action, change, and temporal persistence. While these classical systems provide robust solutions to the frame problem through mechanisms like circumscription and successor state axioms, they are inherently limited by a closed-world assumption that fails in the face of unobserved agent interventions, plan interruptions, and divergent belief-reality states. The paradigm of Sheaf-Theoretic Planning (STP) emerges as a transformative alternative, grounding the problem of multi-agent coordination under the mathematical structures of topos theory and sheaf semantics. This report provides an exhaustive analysis, justification, and extension of the STP framework, exploring its categorical foundations, implementation feasibility, and role in the future of resilient autonomous systems.

papers.title: CyberAId: AI-Driven Cybersecurity for Financial Service Providers
papers.summary: CyberAId: AI-Driven Cybersecurity for Financial Service Providers attacks the mismatch between European financial SOC pressure and the actual bottleneck of reasoning capacity: enterprise SIEMs miss most MITRE ATT&CK coverage, alerts outrun analysts, and regulated institutions need audit-ready responses under DORA, NIS2, and the EU AI Act. The paper pivots away from the current assumption that a frontier model or a monolithic cloud copilot can directly replace security tooling, toward a hybrid multi-agent control plane that reasons over classical SIEM/XDR telemetry and writes its conclusions back into the security stack. The mechanical shift is a shared on-premise runtime with a Main Agent/CRA router, a Reporting capability, and specialist subagents for threat intel, vulnerability assessment, compliance, behavioral analysis, incident response, forensics, and DevSecOps, each operating on partitioned RAG and deterministic tool wrappers under bounded human-in-the-loop autonomy. What differentiates CyberAId from nearby agentic cyber work is its insistence on privacy-preserving federation of accumulated agent knowledge across institutions, not raw logs; its use of signed, versioned “skills” as composable adaptation units; and its optional capability packs for digital-twin adversarial validation, eBPF kernel telemetry, and quantum-token authentication. The platform is positioned for four finance-heavy failure regimes—client impersonation, AML, retail-banking incident response, and HFT resilience—where it aims to turn each deployment into a continuously refined collective defense, while acknowledging the open problem of production-grade evaluation and skill-poisoning resistance.
papers.abstract: European financial institutions face mounting regulatory pressure while their security operations centres remain constrained not by data or staffing but by reasoning capacity: enterprise SIEMs cover only a fraction of MITRE ATT&CK techniques, two thirds of SOC teams cannot keep pace with alert volumes, and the majority of breaches are preceded by alerts that are generated but never investigated. Frontier large language models now achieve state-of-the-art results on isolated cybersecurity tasks (one-day vulnerability exploitation, code-level patching, intrusion detection) yet no narrow win constitutes a platform that can compose across functions, persist multi-tenant state, map findings to regulatory regimes and survive an audit. This position paper argues that the right unit of construction is a hybrid multi-agent system in which specialised LLM subagents reason over classical SIEM/XDR telemetry rather than replacing it, share accumulated agent state across institutions through privacy-preserving federation, and can connect to complementary capability packs such as quantum-based authentication, digital twins for adversarial validation, and eBPF-based kernel telemetry. We present CyberAId, a model-agnostic, on-premise-deployable platform in which a Main Agent coordination layer, a Reporting capability, and specialist subagents operate within a shared runtime under bounded human-in-the-loop autonomy, organised around four falsifiable design principles, and aligned with relevant regulations. CyberAId will be validated at four representative financial use cases (client impersonation, anti-money-laundering for payment service providers, retail-banking incident response, and high-frequency-trading resilience) and propose skill-based agent adaptation as the most promising research direction for turning each deployment into a contribution to a continuously refined collective defence.

papers.title: Disentangling Intent from Role: Adversarial Self-Play for Persona-Invariant Safety Alignment
papers.summary: Disentangling Intent from Role: Adversarial Self-Play for Persona-Invariant Safety Alignment attacks a specific failure mode in aligned LLMs: the refusal boundary is not just bypassed by malicious wording, but shifted by persona framing, so the same harmful intent becomes safe-looking compliance when embedded in a role prompt. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that jailbreak defense can be solved by iterating attacks alone or by treating persona as harmless style; instead, it pivots from prompt-level robustness toward structural invariance of safety decisions under persona perturbations. The mechanical shift is a two-sided adversarial self-play loop called Persona-Invariant Alignment: Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) replaces flat genetic search with lineage-based credit propagation plus UCB exploration to discover transferable “high-risk” personas, while Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) decouples safety from persona by using the persona-free output as a stop-gradient teacher and constraining persona-conditioned outputs with a unilateral forward KL regularizer, jointly optimized with DPO and SFT. Unlike nearby red-teaming work, this is not just more attack diversity; it explicitly models persona as a causal confounder and learns invariance as a control lever. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, evaluated with WildGuard across SafeRLHF, StrongREJECT, XSTest, HarmBench, and related benchmarks, PLE reaches near-saturation attack success on OOD harmful sets, while PICL sharply lowers ASR and largely preserves general capability and benign compliance, suggesting a future direction of mechanistically grounded, invariant safety alignment under distribution shifts in user role, tone, and identity.
papers.abstract: The growing capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have driven their widespread deployment across diverse domains, even in potentially high-risk scenarios. Despite advances in safety alignment techniques, current models remain vulnerable to emerging persona-based jailbreak attacks. Existing research on persona-based jailbreak has primarily focused on attack iterations, yet it lacks systemic and mechanistic constraints on the defense side. To address this challenge, we propose Persona-Invariant Alignment (PIA), an adversarial self-play framework that achieves co-evolution through Persona Lineage Evolution (PLE) on the attack side and Persona-Invariant Consistency Learning (PICL) on the defense side. Theoretically, PICL is grounded in the structural separation hypothesis, using a unilateral KL-divergence constraint to enable the structural decoupling of safety decisions from persona context, thereby maintaining safe behavior under persona-based jailbreak attacks. Experimental results demonstrate that PLE efficiently explores high-risk persona spaces by leveraging lineage-based credit propagation. Meanwhile, the PICL defense method significantly reduces the Attack Success Rate (ASR) while preserving the model's general capability, thereby validating the superiority and robustness of this alignment paradigm. Codes are available at https://github.com/JiajiaLi-1130/PIA.

papers.title: A Language for Describing Agentic LLM Contexts
papers.summary: In A Language for Describing Agentic LLM Contexts, the paper attacks a reproducibility bottleneck in agentic systems: the real object being tuned is not just prompt wording but the time-varying structure of what each LLM call sees—system instructions, user turns, tool outputs, summaries, memories, and reasoning traces—yet teams still communicate that structure with prose, code spelunking, or ad hoc diagrams that cannot precisely compare two context-assembly strategies. The pivot is away from prompt engineering and toward a formal description language for context engineering, treating the prompt as a discrete-time architecture rather than a static text artifact. The mechanical shift is Agentic Context Description Language (ACDL), which replaces content-centric descriptions with symbolic templates and typed namespaces for sys/env/resp data, time indexing, conditional and iterative replay of history, fragments for reusable prompt substructures, and renderable diagrams that make message roles, ordering, and cross-step references explicit. Unlike PromptML, PDL, or POML, ACDL is not an execution language for building agents; it is an implementation-agnostic specification of how context is assembled and evolves. The paper demonstrates ACDL on ReAct variants, OpenCode/OpenClaw loops, Gemini Plays Pokémon Blue, and DeepSeek-V4-style reasoning retention, showing that even small structural choices such as whether reasoning traces stay in history can shift MINT performance by several points. The main implication is a future where agent papers publish machine-readable context blueprints, enabling sharper comparison, easier reimplementation, and eventually human-to-AI communication of prompt architecture.
papers.abstract: Large language models are increasingly used within larger systems ("LLM agents"). These make a sequence of LLM calls, each call providing the LLM with a combination of instructions, observations, and interaction history. The design of the encoded information and its structure play a central role in the quality of the resulting system, leading to efforts spent on context engineering. It is therefore critical to communicate the composition of the LLM context in a system, and how it evolves over time. Yet, no standard exists for doing so: context construction is typically conveyed through informal prose, ad hoc diagrams, or direct inspection of code, none of which precisely capture how a prompt evolves across interaction steps or how two context representation strategies differ. To remedy this, we introduce the Agentic Context Description Language (ACDL), a language for specifying the structure and dynamics of LLM input contexts in a precise, readable, and standard manner, along with visualizations. ACDL provides constructs for specifying context aspects such as role message sequences, dynamic content, time-indexed references, and conditional or iterative structure, capturing the full architecture of a prompt independently of any particular implementation. ACDL diagrams can be hand drawn on a whiteboard, or written in formal language which can then be rendered. We describe the language, demonstrate it by documenting several existing systems and their variants, and encourage the community to adopt it for describing LLM systems context, both in day-to-day communication and in papers. Tooling, examples and documentation are available at www.acdlang.org.

papers.title: Moira: Language-driven Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Pair Trading
papers.summary: Moira: Language-driven Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Pair Trading attacks the credit-assignment bottleneck in pair trading, where losses can come from a bad asset-pair abstraction, poor execution, or their interaction, yet scalar returns do not reveal which level failed. It challenges the standard move toward flat end-to-end policies, pure reward optimization, and gradient fine-tuning of LLM agents, and instead pivots from numeric-only trading policies toward a language-grounded hierarchical control loop. The mechanical shift is unusually concrete: a high-level Selector LLM chooses an asset pair once per episode from aggregated prices plus summarized news/sentiment, while a low-level Trader LLM executes long/short/close/hold actions inside the episode on the fixed pair; both policies are frozen models improved exclusively by prompt updates, not weight updates. Trajectory-level textual critiques act as a “textual gradient” for the Trader, and episode-level feedback refines the Selector, so abstraction updates and execution adaptation happen on different time scales and reduce cross-level non-stationarity. On a real U.S. equity setup with 10 liquid stocks and daily news, Moira outperforms statistical baselines, an RL baseline, flat-LLM trading, and Moira ablations, reaching 59.11% annualized return, 3.791 Sharpe, 9.637 Sortino, 34.777 Calmar, and only 1.7% max drawdown. The result suggests a future direction where language serves as an editable policy representation for hierarchical decision-making in delayed-feedback, partially observable domains beyond finance.
papers.abstract: Many sequential decision-making problems exhibit hierarchical structure, where high-level semantic choices constrain downstream actions and feedback is delayed and ambiguous. Learning in such settings is challenging due to credit assignment: performance degradation may arise from flawed abstractions, suboptimal execution, or their interaction. We study this challenge through pair trading, a domain that naturally combines long-horizon semantic reasoning for asset pair selection with short-horizon execution under partial observability. We formulate pair trading as a hierarchical reinforcement learning problem and propose a language-driven optimization framework in which both high-level and low-level policies are parameterized by large language models (LLMs) and optimized exclusively through prompt updates. Our approach leverages pretrained LLMs as hierarchical policies and uses trajectory- and episode-level textual feedback to adapt abstractions and execution without gradient-based fine-tuning. By explicitly separating abstraction selection from execution, the framework reduces non-stationarity across hierarchical levels and enables targeted adaptation under delayed feedback. Experiments on real-world market data show consistent improvements over traditional and LLM-based baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of language-driven hierarchical reinforcement learning.

papers.title: 12 Angry AI Agents: Evaluating Multi-Agent LLM Decision-Making Through Cinematic Jury Deliberation
papers.summary: 12 Angry AI Agents: Evaluating Multi-Agent LLM Decision-Making Through Cinematic Jury Deliberation attacks a specific failure mode in multi-agent LLMs: when twelve persona-conditioned agents are meant to deliberate, they often do not actually persuade one another, but anchor on first positions and produce parallel monologues that end in stalemate. It challenges the assumption that multi-agent debate, panel judging, or jury-of-LLMs systems automatically create genuine mind-changing, and pivots from treating deliberation as a scaling story toward treating it as a susceptibility-to-update problem. The mechanical shift is a film-grounded benchmark built from Sidney Lumet’s 12 Angry Men: twelve canon-faithful juror personas, an eight-item murder case file injected into each system prompt, Microsoft AutoGen’s SelectorGroupChat orchestration, structured vote checks every 12 turns, and an early-stop rule after three unchanged voting rounds. The study contrasts GPT-4o, representing heavy RLHF and safety-consistency, with Llama-4-Scout, representing lighter alignment, across baseline, open-minded prompt, and no-initial-vote conditions (18 runs total). The unusual move is using a known narrative cascade as ground truth for persuasion order, then measuring vote-change timelines, first-flip order, and cascade velocity rather than generic benchmark accuracy. Results are stark: 17/18 runs end in hung jury; GPT-4o is rigid under every intervention, while Llama is prompt-sensitive, yields more vote changes, and alone reaches NOT_GUILTY once when initial vote anchoring is removed. The paper claims RLHF intensity, not capability, predicts deliberative rigidity, pointing toward models and interfaces optimized for controlled flexibility rather than mere consistency.
papers.abstract: What if the twelve jurors of Sidney Lumet's 12 Angry Men (1957) were not men, but large language models? Would the one juror who disagrees still be able to change everyone's mind? This paper instantiates that scenario as a multi-agent benchmark for LLM deliberation: twelve agents, each conditioned on a film-faithful persona, debate the film's murder case using multi-agent framework. Two models representing opposite ends of the RLHF spectrum are tested: GPT-4o (closed-source, heavy alignment) and Llama-4-Scout (open-weight, lighter alignment), across three conditions (baseline, open-minded prompt, no initial vote), with N = 3 replications per cell (18 runs total). Three findings emerge. (i) Seventeen of eighteen runs end in a hung jury (a state where the jury fails to reach a unanimous verdict); the film's central event, gradual minority-to-majority persuasion, almost never occurs, indicating that anchoring is the dominant failure mode of current LLMs in this setting. (ii) The two models exhibit sharply different internal dynamics: GPT-4o produces a mean of 1.0 vote changes per run across all conditions, while Llama-4-Scout ranges from 2.0 (baseline) to 6.0 (open-minded prompt), and is the only model to reach a NOT\_GUILTY verdict (1 of 3 runs in the no-initial-vote condition). The same ``open-minded'' instruction is internalized by Llama and ignored by GPT-4o. (iii) This asymmetry suggests that the intensity of RLHF alignment training, not model capability, is the primary determinant of deliberative flexibility in multi-agent settings. Flexibility, not capability, tracks human deliberation. The work is framed as an exploratory study and discusses implications for jury-of-LLMs evaluation and multi-agent debate.

papers.title: TumorXAI: Self-Supervised Deep Learning Framework for Explainable Brain MRI Tumor Classification
papers.summary: TumorXAI: Self-Supervised Deep Learning Framework for Explainable Brain MRI Tumor Classification attacks the bottleneck that multi-class brain tumor MRI diagnosis is label-starved, class-imbalanced, and too heterogeneous for conventional supervised CNN pipelines to generalize reliably; it pivots from annotation-hungry transfer learning toward representation learning on unlabeled scans, then back into low-label classification via linear probing and fine-tuning. The mechanical shift is a ResNet-50 backbone pretrained with four SSL regimes—SimCLR, BYOL, DINO, and MoCo v3—using strong augmentations (random crop/flip/rotation/blur/brightness shifts) to force invariances before supervised heads are fit on 4,448 MRIs spanning 17 tumor types. Unlike nearby medical-imaging work that stops at accuracy, this study couples SSL with XAI post hoc: Grad-CAM, Grad-CAM++, and Eigen-CAM are used to localize the regions driving predictions and to check whether the learned features correspond to clinically plausible tumor cues. SimCLR is the standout, reaching 99.64% accuracy/precision/recall/F1 in fine-tuning, 97.27% test accuracy, and the cleanest t-SNE and confusion-matrix structure, while DINO’s sharp linear-evaluation collapse signals that not all SSL objectives transfer equally well in this regime. The paper’s main implication is that unlabeled MRI can be turned into a scalable diagnostic representation bank, but the remaining limitation is single-sequence coverage and imperfect generalization, pointing toward multimodal MRI, cross-dataset validation, and trust-calibrated SSL+XAI pipelines for clinical deployment.
