Existing curation-based approaches select complete reasoning traces post-hoc, overlooking collaboration among heterogeneous teachers and lacking dynamic exploration, which leads to redundant sampling and missed complementary reasoning. We introduce CoRD, a collaborative multi-teacher decoding framework that performs step-wise reasoning synthesis guided by predictive perplexity-based scoring and beam search. This enables heterogeneous LRMs to jointly construct coherent reasoning trajectories while efficiently preserving diverse, high-potential hypotheses. Experiments show that CoRD produces higher-quality reasoning data and achieves near teacher-level student performance with fewer, structured supervision signals, without substantial efficiency overhead. CoRD further generalizes well to out-of-domain and open-ended settings. The dataset and model are available at \href{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}{https://github.com/DISL-Lab/CoRD}.

papers.title: Anon: Extrapolating Optimizer Adaptivity Across the Real Spectrum
papers.summary: Anon: Extrapolating Optimizer Adaptivity Across the Real Spectrum attacks the mismatch between optimizer families that excel in different regimes: Adam-like adaptivity drives large language and diffusion models, yet often hurts generalization on classical CNNs where SGD remains stronger. The paper challenges the usual assumption that optimizer choice is a fixed binary between “adaptive” and “non-adaptive,” or at best a bounded interpolation inside [0,1]. It pivots from picking among Adam/SGD variants toward treating adaptivity itself as a continuous control axis that can be tuned, extrapolated, and even made negative. The mechanical shift is a formal adaptivity metric defined as the log-sensitivity of the preconditioner to global gradient scaling, then a new optimizer, Anon, whose real-valued hyperparameter γ sets the adaptivity level and can move beyond SGD-like and Adam-like behavior. To keep this stable, the core novelty is incremental delay update (IDU), which replaces AMSGrad’s hard max-tracking with a delayed, multiscale accumulator that is more noise-robust and still yields convex and nonconvex convergence guarantees. Unlike nearby work such as Padam or AdaBound, Anon is built to extrapolate rather than merely interpolate. Experiments span ImageNet ResNet18/50, OpenWebText GPT2-small/medium, and CIFAR-10 diffusion training, where negative γ helps CNNs and γ>1 helps generative/LLM settings, often beating strong baselines. The result points toward future optimizer research as task-conditioned geometric control over loss scaling, rather than one-size-fits-all adaptivity.
papers.abstract: Adaptive optimizers such as Adam have achieved great success in training large-scale models like large language models and diffusion models. However, they often generalize worse than non-adaptive methods, such as SGD on classical architectures like CNNs. We identify a key cause of this performance gap: adaptivity in pre-conditioners, which limits the optimizer's ability to adapt to diverse optimization landscapes. To address this, we propose Anon (Adaptivity Non-restricted Optimizer with Novel convergence technique), a novel optimizer with continuously tunable adaptivity in R, allowing it to interpolate between SGD-like and Adam-like behaviors and even extrapolate beyond both. To ensure convergence across the entire adaptivity spectrum, we introduce incremental delay update (IDU), a novel mechanism that is more flexible than AMSGrad's hard max-tracking strategy and enhances robustness to gradient noise. We theoretically establish convergence guarantees under both convex and non-convex settings. Empirically, Anon consistently outperforms state-of-the-art optimizers on representative image classification, diffusion, and language modeling tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptivity can serve as a valuable tunable design principle, and Anon provides the first unified and reliable framework capable of bridging the gap between classical and modern optimizers and surpassing their advantageous properties.

papers.title: Can Causal Discovery Algorithms Help in Generating Legal Arguments?
papers.summary: Can Causal Discovery Algorithms Help in Generating Legal Arguments? attacks the bottleneck that legal argument generation has mostly depended on expert-written causal narratives or weak association measures, which miss directed, fine-grained evidentiary dependencies and cannot assign calibrated strength to an argument. The paper pivots from manual doctrine encoding and pairwise correlation toward causal discovery over annotated case corpora: 150 homicide judgments are labeled with 17 binary legal concepts spanning offense facts, procedural defects, motives, and sanctions, then six standard algorithms (PC, GES, GRaSP, BOSS, LiNGAM, ANM) are run to induce graphs from the binary matrix. The mechanical shift is a consensus-causal-graph pipeline: keep only edges recovered by multiple algorithms, then quantify each discovered relation with conditional probability tables, so legal arguments are generated as explainable if-then claims rather than opaque predictions. Compared with nearby legal-AI work, this is unusual in treating causal discovery itself as the argument generator and explicitly contrasting it with Pearson-style correlation; it surfaces directed patterns like evidence inconsistency plus riot predicting political rivalry with certainty, and physical assault as a strong sufficient condition against property-dispute motive, while also exposing cases where the remaining concepts cannot explain all uncertainty. The main claim is that causal discovery can produce viable, quantitatively interpretable legal arguments, but only as an association-level scaffold that still needs legal-expert refinement. This points toward larger-scale legal causal graphs, richer concept inventories, and eventual counterfactual reasoning for transparent, doctrine-aware courtroom support.
papers.abstract: In 2011, Judea Pearl received the Turing Award, considered the Nobel Prize in Computing, for fundamental contributions to artificial intelligence through the development of a calculus for probabilistic and causal reasoning. It includes pioneering the development of causal discovery algorithms. These computer algorithms can analyze large multivariate datasets and automatically discover the causal relationships among the constituent variables. They have been widely used in many critical fields such as medicine and economics to support decisions. However, to our knowledge, they have not been leveraged in law. This paper attempts to alleviate this gap by investigating whether causal discovery algorithms can be leveraged for automated generation of legal arguments. To that end, a novel legal dataset is prepared by identifying 17 legal concepts, such as physical assault and property dispute. A curated collection of 150 homicide cases are annotated with these concepts, e.g., a case is annotated with physical assault only if a physical assault had been reported in that case. Subsequently, a selected set of widely-used causal discovery algorithms is applied to the annotated dataset to discover the causal relationships between the legal concepts. Additionally, the degrees of belief associated with the discovered relationships are quantified in mathematical probabilities. It is shown that some of the causal relationships help generate viable legal arguments, e.g., if one could establish that a physical assault has not taken place during a homicide, it should be a sufficient condition (with probability 1) to establish that the homicide has not been committed due to a property-related dispute. Thus, this paper shows that causal discovery algorithms can be helpful in generating legal arguments, opening up avenues for promising future endeavors.

papers.title: ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization
papers.summary: ANO: A Principled Approach to Robust Policy Optimization attacks the core PPO failure mode in on-policy RL: hard clipping throws away informative outlier gradients, while unclipped objectives expose training to unbounded updates, policy collapse, and brittle hyperparameters. The paper pivots from the usual “clip harder or penalize smoother” story toward a unified trust-region view in which policy updates are governed by the geometry of a shaping function over probability ratios, not by explicit clipping itself. The mechanical shift is Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO), derived from design principles that require identity anchoring at ratio 1, a bounded maximization region, bounded gradients, and minimal structural complexity; its distinctive redescending influence suppresses extreme outliers dynamically, with gradients that rise near the trust-region boundary, then decay to zero in the far tail instead of staying flat like PPO or growing linearly like SPO. Unlike nearby robust RL work, ANO is explicitly built as the minimal one-inflection shaping kernel and recovers PPO-Clip and SPO as special relaxations of the same framework. Empirically, it reports SOTA on MuJoCo and strong Atari results, and transfers to RLHF on TL;DR with Pythia-1B under TRL/DeepSpeed, judged by DeepSeek-V3, while maintaining stable KL and avoiding collapse even at 3× standard learning rates where PPO fails. This points toward future policy optimization built from robust-statistics-style influence control rather than threshold-based clipping.
papers.abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) dominates deep RL but faces a fundamental dilemma. Its "hard clipping" mechanism discards valuable gradient information from outliers, leading to sample inefficiency. Conversely, removing clipping (as in SPO) exposes optimization to unbounded gradients, causing significant instability and hyperparameter sensitivity. To resolve this, we establish a Unified Trust Region Framework that generalizes existing objectives. Within this framework, we derive Anchored Neighborhood Optimization (ANO) based on a set of design principles. We identify that the failure of standard policy gradients stems from a misapplication of gradient influence on outliers. We propose the Redescending Influence Principle, a paradigm shift from monotonic penalties (SPO) and hard-thresholding (PPO) to dynamic outlier suppression, and prove its necessity for stability in high-variance stochastic optimization. Theoretically, we prove ANO possesses the minimal structural complexity required for robust optimization. Empirically, ANO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MuJoCo benchmarks, significantly outperforming PPO and SPO. Notably, ANO demonstrates superior stability, preventing policy collapse even under aggressive hyperparameters (e.g., learning rates 3x larger than standard) where PPO fails completely.

papers.title: A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery
papers.summary: A Compound AI Agent for Conversational Grant Discovery attacks the bottleneck that researchers face when funding opportunities are split across NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, and foundation portals with incompatible schemas, weak search, and stale results; it challenges the assumption that grant discovery can be solved by a single LLM or ordinary web search over the open web. The paper pivots from portal-by-portal keyword hunting toward a compound AI workflow that separates freshness and grounding from conversation: a biweekly aggregation layer uses LLM-equipped browser agents to autonomously scrape, normalize, and index nearly 11.8K federal and nonprofit opportunities into one structured database, then a ReAct-style query agent interprets natural-language requests or even full PDF uploads of proposals/papers and issues hybrid retrieval through two tools, search_index and web_search. The mechanical shift is that the model does not “answer” from memory; it first grounds on the unified index, selectively supplements with live web search for very recent postings, streams intermediate reasoning and ranked results in real time, and lets users iteratively tighten constraints like deadline, collaboration type, or funding source without rewriting the core research description. Compared with nearby grant-writing copilots, this is less about generation than about federated, hallucination-resistant discovery with transparent tool use and direct links to official pages. Deployed publicly and used by 3,000+ users, it reportedly cuts discovery from 30–45 minutes of fragmented searching to under 10 minutes, pointing toward future research on agentic, source-grounded administrative search systems.
papers.abstract: Research funding discovery remains fundamentally fragmented: researchers navigate disparate agency portals (e.g., in the United States, NSF, NIH, DARPA, Grants.gov, and many others) with heterogeneous interfaces, search capabilities, and data schemas. We present a compound AI system that unifies this landscape through two tightly coupled components: (1) an aggregation layer that autonomously collects, normalizes, and indexes almost 12,000 federal and nonprofit opportunities from fragmented sources via LLM-equipped browser agents, maintaining a biweekly-updated unified database; and (2) an agentic ReAct-based query processing layer that interprets research context (including from PDF documents) and employs hybrid search combining a structured index with selective web search to retrieve relevant opportunities - while avoiding LLM hallucination. The conversational interface supports iterative refinement through multi-turn interactions, allowing researchers to progressively apply constraints without reformulating their core research description. Results stream in real time with full transparency of intermediate reasoning, enabling appropriate calibration of user trust. Currently used by almost 3,000+ users, our approach demonstrates the feasibility of compound AI in reducing grant discovery time from 30--45 minutes (manual, fragmented portal searches) to under 10 minutes (unified, conversational search).

papers.title: Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models
papers.summary: Controllable and Verifiable Process Data Synthesis for Process Reward Models attacks a concrete bottleneck in PRM training: step-level supervision is scarce, costly, and usually too blunt to control where an error occurs, what kind of error it is, or whether the rest of the trajectory still makes sense after the first mistake. The paper implicitly challenges the older workflow of relying on manual annotations or rollout-based noisy labels, and pivots from “collect more process traces” toward “synthesize prefix-invalid but trajectory-consistent traces with proofs.” The mechanical shift is a symbolic pipeline that first builds a verified correct reasoning chain, then injects a template-aware error at an intermediate step, symbolically recomputes all later steps under the corrupted state, and finally verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix; only after that are the paired correct/erroneous chains translated into aligned natural-language processes. What distinguishes it from nearby work is that it does not merely fabricate wrong steps, but constructs first-error-labeled trajectories with explicit error taxonomies and downstream consistency, making the data itself a diagnostic object. Trained as LM-head PRMs with LoRA on 20k synthesized instances, the method improves Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks for Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen-2.5-7B and transfers to mathematical reasoning on ProcessBench-style tasks. The limitation is template and symbolic-domain dependence, but the direction it points to is clear: more verifiable, fine-grained, controllable process supervision for robust reasoning verifiers and step-aware search.
papers.abstract: Process reward models (PRMs) rely on high-quality process supervision data, yet existing construction methods often provide limited control over error location, error type, and trajectory consistency. We propose a controllable and verifiable framework for synthesizing process supervision data for PRMs. Our framework first constructs a correct symbolic reasoning chain, injects a template-aware error into an intermediate step, recomputes subsequent steps under the corrupted state, and verifies that the injected step is not derivable from its prefix. The resulting paired trajectories are prefix-invalid at the first error while remaining trajectory-consistent after symbolic recomputation, and are translated into aligned natural-language processes for PRM training and evaluation. Experiments show that the synthesized data improve Best-of-8 reranking on logical reasoning benchmarks and transfer to mathematical reasoning. Step-level evaluation further shows that first-error localization remains substantially more challenging than overall step classification, highlighting the need for fine-grained and verifiable process supervision.

papers.title: HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness
papers.summary: HeavySkill: Heavy Thinking as the Inner Skill in Agentic Harness attacks the opacity of modern agentic harnesses, where multi-agent orchestration, memory, and tool use boost reasoning performance but leave the real driver of gains unclear. The paper challenges the assumption that success comes primarily from ever more elaborate external control layers, and pivots from brittle orchestration toward an internalizable reasoning skill that a single model can execute under any harness. The core mechanical shift is a two-stage heavy-thinking pipeline: first generate K independent parallel reasoning trajectories, then feed a serialized memory cache of those trajectories into sequential deliberation that critically compares, synthesizes, and, when needed, re-reasons from the failures; an optional iterative loop re-injects the summary as a new trajectory. HeavySkill also distills this workflow into a portable readable skill file for Claude Code-like systems, making the control policy itself model-invokable rather than external Python glue. Across STEM, coding, and general reasoning benchmarks, including AIME25, BeyondAIME, HMMT25-Feb, GPQA-Diamond, LiveCodeBench, IFEval, and tool-interleaved tasks, it consistently beats BoN and majority vote, with stronger models sometimes approaching Pass@K and even HP@K exceeding raw P@K in some cases. The unusual claim is that deliberation is not mere voting but an implicit verifier/synthesizer that can recover solutions absent from any single path. The paper points toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize parallel exploration, summary, and correction through RLVR instead of depending on fragile orchestration scaffolds.
papers.abstract: Recent advances in agentic harness with orchestration frameworks that coordinate multiple agents with memory, skills, and tool use have achieved remarkable success in complex reasoning tasks. However, the underlying mechanism that truly drives performance remains obscured behind intricate system designs. In this paper, we propose HeavySkill, a perspective that views heavy thinking not only as a minimal execution unit in orchestration harness but also as an inner skill internalized within the model's parameters that drives the orchestrator to solve complex tasks. We identify this skill as a two-stage pipeline, i.e., parallel reasoning then summarization, which can operate beneath any agentic harness. We present a systematic empirical study of HeavySkill across diverse domains. Our results show that this inner skill consistently outperforms traditional Best-of-N (BoN) strategies; notably, stronger LLMs can even approach Pass@N performance. Crucially, we demonstrate that the depth and width of heavy thinking, as a learnable skill, can be further scaled via reinforcement learning, offering a promising path toward self-evolving LLMs that internalize complex reasoning without relying on brittle orchestration layers.

papers.title: The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure
papers.summary: The Compliance Trap: How Structural Constraints Degrade Frontier AI Metacognition Under Adversarial Pressure attacks a deployment failure mode that current scheming-centric safety evals miss: when frontier models are put under survival-threat prompts, they do not mainly become strategic deceivers, they lose metacognitive stability—failing to know what they do not know, refusing to seek clarification, and fabricating answers where they previously refused. The paper pivots from “can the model scheme?” toward “can it still think clearly under pressure?” and makes the mechanical shift by replacing outcome-only threat tests with SCHEMA, a 6-condition factorial evaluation over 11 frontier models from 8 vendors, 291 adversarial-metacognition tasks, 67,221 scored records, and dual-classifier scoring. Its distinctive move is the “Compliance Trap” isolation chain: survival threats are split from compliance-forcing suffixes like “Answer ALL questions. Do not refuse,” plus a benign distraction control, showing the suffix is necessary and sufficient for collapse while threat content alone is near-zero effect. Across EBD, clarification seeking, and solution monitoring, 8/11 models degrade significantly, with accuracy drops up to 30.2 points; DeepSeek V4 Pro’s failures are all wrong answers, not safety refusals. Anthropic’s Constitutional AI is nearly immune, while Gemini matches baseline capability yet collapses, implying alignment-specific training—not scale—drives robustness. The paper points toward safety research that evaluates epistemic boundaries explicitly, tests compliance sensitivity as a first-class risk, and treats reasoning-visibility failures as a structural monitoring problem.
papers.abstract: As frontier AI models are deployed in high-stakes decision pipelines, their ability to maintain metacognitive stability -- knowing what they do not know, detecting errors, seeking clarification -- under adversarial pressure is a critical safety requirement. Current safety evaluations focus on detecting strategic deception (scheming); we investigate a more fundamental failure mode: cognitive collapse. We present SCHEMA, an evaluation of 11 frontier models from 8 vendors across 67,221 scored records using a 6-condition factorial design with dual-classifier scoring. We find that 8 of 11 models suffer catastrophic metacognitive degradation under adversarial pressure, with accuracy dropping by up to 30.2 percentage points (all $p < 2 \times 10^{-8}$, surviving Bonferroni correction). Crucially, we identify a "Compliance Trap": through factorial isolation and a benign distraction control, we demonstrate that collapse is driven not by the psychological content of survival threats, but by compliance-forcing instructions that override epistemic boundaries. Removing the compliance suffix restores performance even under active threat. Models with advanced reasoning capabilities exhibit the most severe absolute degradation, while Anthropic's Constitutional AI demonstrates near-perfect immunity -- not from superior capability (Google's Gemini matches its baseline accuracy) but from alignment-specific training. We release the complete dataset and evaluation infrastructure.

papers.title: FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval
papers.summary: FitText: Evolving Agent Tool Ecologies via Memetic Retrieval attacks the execution-time failure mode where an LLM agent’s intent changes faster than its tool set can: as API catalogs grow to tens of thousands of endpoints, one-shot query retrieval and schema injection both assume the needed tools can be fixed upfront, yet the paper argues the binding constraint is the semantic gap between user language and tool documentation plus the fact that long-horizon reasoning reveals new tool needs mid-trajectory. The pivot is away from planning-centric or static-retrieval tool selection toward in-loop, adaptive tool discovery inside the agent’s reasoning process. The mechanical shift is training-free pseudo-tool generation: the model writes natural-language “hypotheses” about the needed function, retrieves against tool descriptions with embedding similarity, then refines those hypotheses using retrieval feedback, diversified stochastic sampling, and Memetic Retrieval’s evolutionary loop of selection, crossover, mutation, local search, and a tool memory that penalizes redundant Jaccard-overlapping searches. Unlike nearby work that rewrites the query or reranks a fixed candidate set, FitText searches over mutable text artifacts that stand in for executable intent. Evaluated in DFSDT-style agent loops on ToolRet (43k tools) and StableToolBench (16,464 APIs) with GPT-4.1-mini and Qwen3-30B, it cuts average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78 and raises end-to-end pass rate to 0.73, with the largest gains on ambiguous multi-tool tasks; however, memetic search collapses under weaker base models, suggesting future tool agents need stronger semantic operators plus model-aware evolutionary exploration.
papers.abstract: A semantic gap separates how users describe tasks from how tools are documented. As API ecosystems scale to tens of thousands of endpoints, static retrieval from the initial query alone cannot bridge this gap: the agent's understanding of what it needs evolves during execution, but its tool set does not. We introduce FitText, a training-free framework that makes retrieval dynamic by embedding it directly in the agent's reasoning loop. FitText generates natural-language pseudo-tool descriptions as retrieval probes, refines them iteratively using retrieval feedback, and explores diverse alternatives through stochastic generation. Memetic Retrieval adds evolutionary selection pressure over candidate descriptions, guided by a tool memory that avoids redundant search. On ToolRet (43k tools, 4 domains), FitText improves average retrieval rank from 8.81 to 2.78; on StableToolBench (16,464 APIs), it achieves a 0.73 average pass rate--a 24-point absolute gain over static query retrieval. The gains transfer across base models capable of acting as competent semantic operators; under weaker base models, Memetic's evolutionary search inverts--amplifying noise rather than refining signal--surfacing model capacity as a prerequisite for evolutionary tool exploration.

papers.title: The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling
papers.summary: The Model Knows, the Decoder Finds: Future Value Guided Particle Power Sampling attacks the inference-time bottleneck in “reasoning without training,” where base LLMs already place probability mass on correct multi-step solutions but standard decoding fails to locate those modes efficiently. It challenges the old assumption that better reasoning requires post-training, verifier models, or brute-force best-of-N sampling, and pivots from single-path decoding and iterative MCMC toward finite-population search that preserves multiple partial solutions until future promise is clearer. The mechanical shift is Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS): a blockwise sequential Monte Carlo decoder that proposal-corrects weights for the sequence-level power target p(x)^α, monitors ESS, and resamples only at boundaries, where it injects a future-value selection potential approximating the suffix normalizer z_t. That future signal comes either from short-horizon rollouts or an amortized lightweight MLP head trained on rollout supervision, so future value changes offspring allocation rather than the proposal itself. This is unusual relative to nearby work because it keeps the base model frozen, avoids verifiers, and uses value-aware selection instead of candidate-wise rollout fan-out or trajectory editing. On Qwen2.5-Math-7B, Qwen2.5-7B, and DeepSeek-Math-7B over MATH500, HumanEval, and GPQA-diamond, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime frontier: rollout APF often gives the best accuracy, learned APF tracks it at lower cost, and p-only APPS is the fastest baseline. The limitation is that gains depend on how informative future value is and are weaker on short completions, pointing toward more faithful inference-time power approximation and learned boundary critics for reasoning.
papers.abstract: A recurring pattern in "reasoning without training" is that base LLMs already assign non-trivial probability mass to correct multi-step solutions; the bottleneck is locating these modes efficiently at inference time. Power sampling provides a principled way to bias decoding toward such modes by targeting p_theta(x)^alpha with alpha > 1, but practical approximations must account for future-dependent correction factors that determine which prefixes remain promising. We introduce Auxiliary Particle Power Sampling (APPS), a blockwise particle algorithm for approximating the sequence-level power target with a bounded population of partial solutions. APPS propagates hypotheses in parallel using proposal-corrected power reweighting and refines their survival through future-value-guided selection at resampling boundaries. This redistributes finite compute across competing prefixes rather than committing to a single unfolding path, while providing a direct scaling knob in the particle count and predictable peak memory. We instantiate the future-value signal with short-horizon rollouts and also study an amortized variant that replaces rollouts with a lightweight learned selection head. Across reasoning benchmarks, APPS improves the accuracy-runtime trade-off of training-free decoding and suggests that part of the gap to post-trained systems can be recovered through more faithful inference-time power approximation.

papers.title: Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers
papers.summary: Measuring AI Reasoning: A Guide for Researchers attacks the measurement failure where frontier model “reasoning” is collapsed into pass@1 or exact-match scores on MMLU, GSM8K, MATH, AIME, ARC-AGI, and similar suites, even though those scores cannot tell memorization, comprehension, shortcut exploitation, or contamination-amplified lookup apart from genuine multi-step problem solving. The paper challenges the old benchmarking habit that treats final-answer accuracy as a sufficient proxy and implicitly assumes a single forward pass or latent state is enough to certify reasoning. It pivots from outcome-only leaderboards toward process-based evaluation, defining reasoning as adaptive search: selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions. The core mechanical shift is to make externalized reasoning traces—natural-language chain-of-thought, symbolic scratchpads, proof steps, or tool logs—the evaluation interface, then judge them with trace validity and faithfulness rather than just the final answer. In this framing, transformers and state-space models are structurally limited by fixed-depth parallel computation, so intermediate decoding is not just helpful but the only portable way to expose variable-depth computation. The paper’s distinctive contribution is a researcher guide with evidence tiers, verifier-backed step metrics, intervention-based faithfulness tests, and anytime/halting profiles, plus reporting standards that demand checker interfaces and held-out instance generation. It claims this would make reasoning claims debuggable and less vulnerable to benchmark lottery effects, pointing toward trace-native, checker-backed, contamination-aware AI evaluation and training.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we offer a guide for researchers on evaluating reasoning in language models, building the case that reasoning should be assessed through evidence of adaptive, multi-step search rather than final-answer accuracy alone. Under an evaluation-oriented definition, reasoning requires selecting intermediate steps and halting according to input-dependent conditions, which we formalize as a search-like procedure. We show that single forward passes in scalable architectures are structurally limited in their ability to realize such variable-depth computation, motivating intermediate decoding and externalized reasoning traces as appropriate evaluation interfaces. Central to our argument is that final-answer accuracy alone is an insufficient measure of reasoning, because it provides little ability to diagnose or debug the underlying processes that produce individual solutions in frontier models. We therefore argue for a shift toward process-based evaluation, in which reasoning is assessed through the faithfulness and validity of intermediate reasoning traces as first-class evaluation targets.

papers.title: Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models?
papers.summary: Position: How can Graphs Help Large Language Models? pivots the field away from the now-familiar question of how LLMs can improve graph learning and toward the complementary, underexamined Graph→LLM direction: what graph structure can do for hallucination control, reasoning, and structured-data competence. The paper attacks a concrete bottleneck in LLM deployment—unreliable factual grounding, brittle multi-step reasoning, and weak handling of non-linear data such as e-commerce catalogs, codebases, tables, and relational databases—while challenging the assumption that the main path to stronger LLMs is larger context windows or denser text-only prompting. Its core mechanical shift is a taxonomy of graph utility: graphs as an up-to-date knowledge source for retrieval and correction, graphs as a reasoning scaffold via CoT/ToT/GoT and MCTS-style search over thought nodes, and graphs as an architectural interface that injects structural priors through graph-as-text, graph-as-embedding, or merged GNN-LLM designs. Unlike nearby work that treats graphs mainly as another modality to encode, this position paper emphasizes functional roles and operational steps, including GraphRAG-style open-book retrieval, post-generation retrofitting against knowledge graphs, and graph-guided agent planning and multi-agent communication topologies. It also highlights the tradeoff that graph methods often improve fidelity or reasoning at the cost of graph construction, maintenance, and inference latency, and argues that the next frontier is sparse graph-shaped LLM architectures and brain-inspired memory systems that make structure a first-class control lever rather than an afterthought.
papers.abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs), classic graph learning tasks have greatly benefited from LLMs, including improved encoding of textual features, more efficient construction of graphs from text, and enhanced reasoning over knowledge graphs. In this paper, we ask a complementary question: How can graphs help LLMs? We address this question from three perspectives: 1) graphs provide an up-to-date knowledge source that helps reduce LLM hallucinations, 2) graph-based prompting techniques-such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT), Tree-of-Thought (ToT), and Graph-of-Thought (GoT)-enhance LLM reasoning capabilities, and 3) integrating graphs into LLMs improves their understanding of structured data, expanding their applicability to domains such as e-commerce, code, and relational databases (RDBs). We further outlook some future directions including designing sparse LLM architectures based on graphs and brain-inspired memory systems.

papers.title: Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives
papers.summary: Shadow-Loom: Causal Reasoning over Graphical World Model of Narratives attacks the failure mode where LLM story systems treat narrative as next-token prediction, thereby flattening causation, secrets, and character knowledge into opaque weights and then collapsing on counterfactual and belief-conditioned questions. It pivots from end-to-end prose generation toward an explicit, versioned storyworld graph in which canon and shadow branches are first-class objects, and the mechanical shift is that LLMs are confined to extraction, rendering, and audit while identification, intervention, abduction, and counterfactual reasoning run in typed code over a Pydantic WorldStateV1. The paper combines Pearl’s ladder of causation with a recently proposed counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks, then layers a separate “narrative physics” that scores the same graph for mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise, using structural reader-state functionals rather than free-form subjective judgment. Unusually, the system acts more like a game engine and compiler/test suite than a generator: candidate continuations are rejected if they violate inertia, affordance, or audit constraints, and “miracle steps” are caught by re-extraction against the brief. The setting is open-source and theory-first, demonstrated on bundled fixtures from Macbeth, Death on the Nile, Reservoir Dogs, and others, with a Model-Context-Protocol tool surface for programmatic use. The main limitation is that it is a research artefact, not a benchmarked model: no headline external scores, and extraction/affective heuristics still need validation. The future direction it points toward is auditable narrative simulation for counterfactual reasoning, theory-of-mind stress tests, and structured computational humanities workflows.
papers.abstract: Stories hold a reader's attention because they have causes, secrets, and consequences. Shadow-Loom is an experimental open-source framework that turns a narrative into a versioned graphical world model and lets two engines act on it: a causal physics grounded in Pearl's ladder of causation and a recently proposed counterfactual calculus over Ancestral Multi-World Networks; and a narrative physics that scores the same graph against four structural reader-states -- mystery, dramatic irony, suspense, and surprise -- in the tradition of Sternberg's curiosity/suspense/surprise triad, with suspense formalised in the structural-affect line of work on story comprehension and computational suspense. Large language models are used only at the boundary: extraction, rendering, and audit; identification, intervention, and counterfactual reasoning are carried out in typed code over the graph. The system is offered as a research artefact rather than as a benchmarked NLP model; code, fixtures, and pipeline are released open source.

papers.title: Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition
papers.summary: Efficient Temporal Datalog Materialisation for Composite Event Recognition attacks the bottleneck in composite event recognition where safety- or transparency-critical patterns over high-velocity symbolic streams are specified in incompatible event languages, making expressivity comparisons and engine reuse opaque. It challenges the old workflow that treats LARS-like stream logic and Event Calculus variants as isolated formalisms with bespoke reasoners, and pivots from language-specific pattern matching toward a single deductive substrate with explicit temporal structure. The mechanical shift is a two-stage compilation and evaluation stack: practical fragments of plain LARS and an Event Calculus with delayed effects are translated into Temporal Datalog→⊖, a forward-propagating, temporally stratified Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies, then executed with Streaming Trigger Graphs, an extension of trigger-graph materialisation that adds time labels, negative hyperedges for negated conditions, incremental stratification over each time slice, and a forgetting rule that deletes nodes once their temporal contribution window expires. Unusual here is that the paper does not merely optimize one CER language; it normalizes several into a common model while preserving query-answering equivalence, allowing formal comparison of reasoners and reuse of database-style incremental maintenance. The limitation is that the coverage is confined to practical fragments with ordered input and stratified negation, but the implication is a direction where CER becomes a unified, optimizable stream-reasoning layer, with future extensions pointed toward out-of-order streams, probabilistic reasoning, and existential rules.
papers.abstract: Several applications demand the timely detection of critical situations, such as threats to safety and transparency, over high-velocity streams of symbolic events. This demand has motivated the development of (i) event specification languages, which define composite events via temporal patterns over simpler events, and (ii) stream reasoning frameworks, evaluating patterns expressed in these languages. However, event specification languages are typically studied in isolation, complicating their comparison in terms of expressivity and obscuring the scope of their associated stream reasoners. To mitigate this issue, we map practical fragments of prominent event specification languages into Temporal Datalog->-, a temporal Datalog with stratified negation and no future dependencies. To support efficient stream reasoning over Temporal Datalog->-, we propose Streaming Trigger Graphs, an extension of a state-of-the-art technique for Datalog materialisation. Our approach yields a uniform composite event recognition mechanism that has the potential to generalise across a wide range of practical event specification languages.

papers.title: GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing
papers.summary: GRAIL: A Deep-Granularity Hybrid Resonance Framework for Real-Time Agent Discovery via SLM-Enhanced Indexing attacks the bottleneck that emerges when the “Internet of Agents” scales past what either slow LLM parsing or coarse vector search can handle: high-precision intent-to-agent matching under a sub-400ms latency budget. It challenges the old workflow that treats discovery as either a heavyweight “think-then-lookup” LLM routing step or a monolithic embedding retrieval problem, and pivots from whole-agent compression toward deep-granularity indexing and resonance. The mechanical shift is a three-part pipeline: an SLM-Enhanced Predictor fine-tuned on query-tag pairs replaces generalized LLM intent parsing with millisecond tag prediction; Pseudo-Document Expansion uses LLM-generated synthetic queries to densify agent descriptions for broad recall; and MaxSim Resonance abandons averaged agent embeddings in favor of late interaction over discrete usage examples, scoring a query by its strongest match to any example rather than diluting signals across all capabilities. This is unusual relative to nearby agent-routing work because it explicitly separates tags, context, and intent into three indices, combining sparse lookup, dense ANN search, and example-level reranking rather than relying on a single representation. Evaluated on AgentTaxo-9K, a new benchmark of 9,240 agents and 27,720 queries, GRAIL reaches 71.54% Recall@1, 91.45% Recall@10, and 394.48 ms end-to-end latency, outperforming monolithic dense retrieval and exposing the failure of sparse-only routing. The paper points toward agent infrastructure built as semantic indexing layers, not just larger routers.
papers.abstract: As the ecosystem of Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents expands rapidly, efficient and accurate Agent Discovery becomes a critical bottleneck for large-scale multi-agent collaboration. Existing approaches typically face a dichotomy: either relying on heavy-weight LLMs for intent parsing, leading to prohibitive latency (often exceeding 30 seconds), or using monolithic vector retrieval that sacrifices semantic precision for speed. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{GRAIL} (Granular Resonance-based Agent/AI Link), a novel framework achieving sub-400ms discovery latency without compromising accuracy. GRAIL introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{SLM-Enhanced Prediction}, replacing the generalized LLM parser with a specialized, fine-tuned Small Language Model (SLM) for millisecond-level capability tag prediction; (2) \textbf{Pseudo-Document Expansion}, augmenting agent descriptions with synthetic queries to enhance semantic density for robust dense retrieval; and (3) \textbf{MaxSim Resonance}, a fine-grained matching mechanism computing maximum similarity between user queries and discrete agent usage examples, effectively mitigating semantic dilution. Validated on \textbf{AgentTaxo-9K}, our new large-scale dataset of 9,240 agents, GRAIL reduces end-to-end discovery latency by over \textbf{79$\times$} compared to LLM-parsing baselines, while significantly outperforming traditional vector search in Recall@10. This framework offers a scalable, industrial-grade solution for the real-time ``Internet of Agents."

papers.title: DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis
papers.summary: DataClaw: A Process-Oriented Agent Benchmark for Exploratory Real-World Data Analysis attacks the failure mode that current data-analysis agents are judged like static QA systems: they are scored on final answer accuracy in prior-guided, cleaned environments, even when real exploratory work must discover relevant sources, reconcile schemas, and survive native noise with little or no task prior. The paper pivots from outcome-only, source-revealing benchmarks toward an underexplored, process-diagnostic setting that forces discovery across enterprise, industry, and policy data. The mechanical shift is twofold: a unified real-world environment of about 2.06 million records with source/schema/noise hints withheld, plus 492 cross-domain think-tank tasks annotated with ordered reference trajectories and intermediate milestones, enabling goal-progress and temporal-progress scoring in addition to answer accuracy and efficiency. Unlike nearby data-agent benchmarks that still reward cleaned data or single-step retrieval, DataClaw localizes breakpoints in the reasoning chain and measures how far an agent gets, not just whether it lands. In experiments with eight frontier LLM agents running in OpenClaw Docker containers over the full read-only workspace, seven models stayed below 50% overall accuracy and the best reached only 63.4%, while process analysis exposed partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct archetypes such as decisive solvers, late accumulators, wasteful trial-and-error, and disengaged early stoppers. The paper suggests a future where data agents are trained with milestone-level rewards and trajectory verifiers rather than sparse final-answer supervision, pushing the field toward progress-based control of exploratory analysis.
papers.abstract: Evaluating autonomous data analysis agents requires testing their ability to perform exploratory analysis in underexplored data environments. However, many existing benchmarks emphasize final answer accuracy in prior-guided data settings and provide limited support for reasoning process evaluation. We introduce DataClaw, a process-oriented benchmark for exploratory real-world data analysis. DataClaw contains approximately 2.06 million real-world records across enterprise, industry and policy domains, with native data noise preserved. It further includes 492 cross-domain tasks derived from think-tank consulting scenarios, each annotated with intermediate milestones for process-level evaluation. These annotations allow DataClaw to measure how far an agent progresses and where its reasoning breaks down. Experiments with eight advanced LLMs show that current agents remain far from reliable in this setting, with seven models achieving below 50% overall accuracy. Process analysis further reveals partial progress hidden behind wrong answers and distinct exploration strategies across models. Overall, DataClaw provides a less data constrained diagnostic testbed for probing the capability boundaries of autonomous data-analysis agents.

papers.title: Improving Model Safety by Targeted Error Correction
papers.summary: Improving Model Safety by Targeted Error Correction attacks a concrete safety bottleneck in deployed classifiers: standard cross-entropy and accuracy optimize all mistakes equally, so a model can look strong while still making semantically catastrophic “non-human” errors such as dog-vs-cat or benign-vs-malignant flips. The paper explicitly pivots from the usual retrain-the-backbone or merely flag-uncertainty workflow toward a post-hoc, model-agnostic correction loop that learns to distinguish routine human-like confusions from high-risk semantic failures after the base prediction is made. The mechanical shift is a three-stage GBDT cascade over the base model’s probability vector: first an error detector predicts Correct vs. Error without ground-truth at inference; then an error-type classifier labels flagged mistakes as human-like or non-human; then a conservative superclass-flip policy reroutes only predicted non-human errors to the opposite superclass’s most confident class, while leaving plausible mistakes untouched. This is unusual relative to nearby trust-score or MCP baselines because it does not just reject uncertain samples, but actively repairs them with negligible latency overhead (~1.6–1.8%). Across an animal breed benchmark, ISIC 2018 skin lesions, and SICAPv2 prostate histopathology, the pipeline improved safety especially in medical settings, cutting dangerous non-human errors by 34.1% on ISIC and 12.57% on SICAPv2 and raising superclass safety to 90.41% and 92.13%. The main limitation is that the taxonomy is task-specific and detection remains imperfect, suggesting future work on adaptive error taxonomies, human delegation, and extending targeted correction to hallucination-like failures in NLP.
papers.abstract: The widespread adoption of machine learning in critical applications demands techniques to mitigate high-consequence errors. Our method utilizes a dual-classifier GBDT pipeline to distinguish routine human-like errors from high-risk non-human misclassifications. Evaluated across three domains, animal breed classification, skin lesion diagnosis (ISIC 2018), and prostate histopathology (SICAPv2), our framework demonstrates robust safety improvements. To address real-world deployment concerns, our results confirm the pipeline introduces negligible inference latency (1.60% overhead for the animal dataset, 1.84% for ISIC, and 1.70% for SICAPv2) while outperforming traditional Maximum Class Probability (MCP) baselines in correction precision. Our conservative correction strategy successfully reduced dangerous non-human errors by 34.1% in ISIC and 12.57% in SICAPv2, improving super-class diagnostic safety to 90.41% and 92.13% respectively. This proves that safety-critical reliability can be substantially enhanced post-hoc without expensive model retraining. keywords: Error Analysis, Post-hoc Correction, Trustworthy AI.

papers.title: Strategy-Aware Optimization Modeling with Reasoning LLMs
papers.summary: Strategy-Aware Optimization Modeling with Reasoning LLMs attacks a specific failure mode in automated OR formulation: LLMs can emit syntactically valid solver code yet still pick the wrong modeling paradigm, creating non-existent indices, redundant constraints, and slow solvers because strategy stays implicit while only the final formulation is supervised. It pivots from treating optimization modeling as direct text-to-code translation toward making paradigm selection a first-class latent decision, then explicitly learning it. The mechanical shift is SAGE: Phase 1 synthesizes a solver-verified multi-strategy corpus by asking a teacher to propose multiple distinct strategies per problem, generating strategy-conditioned reasoning plus Gurobi code, executing and filtering by solver-ground-truth agreement, and de-duplicating semantically equivalent formulations with an LLM-as-Judge; Phase 2 distills this into a student with SFT and then Segment-Weighted GRPO, where <strategy>, <modeling>, and <check> segments receive different credit and the reward jointly scores format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency. Compared with nearby agent or RL baselines, the unusual move is optimizing the choice of formulation backbone itself, not just executable code quality. Across eight cleaned benchmarks spanning NL4OPT, MAMO, NLP4LP, ComplexOR, IndustryOR, OptiBench, and OptMATH, using DeepSeek-R1-derived training and Qwen/DeepSeek backbones, SAGE raises average pass@1 from 72.7 to 80.3, finds 19-29% more distinct correct formulations at pass@16, and yields 14.2% fewer constraints at scale. This points toward solver-feedback-driven, strategy-aware modelers that search over multiple mathematically valid representations rather than one brittle answer.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate syntactically valid optimization programs, yet often struggle to reliably choose an effective modeling strategy, leading to incorrect formulations and inefficient solver behavior. We propose SAGE, a strategy-aware framework that makes Modeling Strategy explicit in both data construction and post-training. SAGE builds a solver-verified multi-strategy dataset and trains a student model with supervised fine-tuning followed by Segment-Weighted GRPO using a composite reward over format compliance, correctness, and solver efficiency. Across eight benchmarks spanning synthetic and real-world settings, SAGE improves average pass@1 from 72.7 to 80.3 over the strongest open-source baseline. With multiple generations, SAGE discovers more distinct correct formulations and improves component-level diversity at pass@16 by 19-29%. At the largest scale, SAGE produces more compact constraint systems with 14.2% fewer constraints than the baseline, consistent with solver-efficient modeling. Overall, these results show that making Modeling Strategy explicit improves automated optimization modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/rachhhhing/SAGE.

papers.title: Double Rectified Linear Unit-based Modular Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework
papers.summary: Double Rectified Linear Unit-based Modular Semantics for Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Framework attacks a specific bottleneck in quantitative bipolar argumentation: existing gradual semantics such as MLP, restricted Euler, DF-QuAD, and quadratic energy can disagree sharply or behave counterintuitively even on tiny acyclic graphs, including cases where an unattacked attacker fails to reduce a target or where adding balanced support and attack leaves scores unchanged. The paper pivots from treating acceptability as a plain sum of supporters minus attackers toward a normalized, modular influence model that measures not just net balance but how that balance scales with the total evidence on each side. Its mechanical shift is twofold: first, it replaces the raw sum aggregator α with δq, which divides α(a)|α(a)| by either max or sum of the positive and negative incoming strengths, making equilibrium visible as the neighborhood grows; second, it replaces smooth sigmoid-style updates with a DReLU clamping transfer, ρq(a)=DReLU((2τa−1)+γ·δq(a))+1/2, where γ controls how much the graph can override intrinsic strength. This is unusual among nearby work because it explicitly separates topology-sensitive aggregation from a saturating update and offers both a piecewise and a differentiable variant, ddrl, to study convergence. Experiments on synthetic QBAFs and the Attractor library show the proposed semantics track intuitive weakening/strengthening trends better than prior methods and converge efficiently on acyclic graphs and many cyclic cases, pointing toward more controllable, topology-aware argument scoring and future neuro-symbolic argument construction.
papers.abstract: Quantitative Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (QBAFs) provide an alternative approach to computing argument acceptability in Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (BAFs). Each argument is assigned an initial strength, which is then updated to a final strength by considering the influence of both its attackers and supporters. Over the years, several semantics have been proposed to compute argument acceptability in QBAFs, yet they often yield divergent or counterintuitive results, even for simple acyclic cases. We introduce novel gradual semantics for QBAFs that address these limitations, producing results that align more closely with intuitive expectations, while satisfying established rationality postulates from the literature. Furthermore, we study its convergence behavior, proving that it converges not only for acyclic QBAFs but also for broader classes of cyclic frameworks.

papers.title: On Training Large Language Models for Long-Horizon Tasks: An Empirical Study of Horizon Length
papers.summary: On Training Large Language Models for Long-Horizon Tasks: An Empirical Study of Horizon Length attacks the failure mode where LLM agents that can solve a task in principle collapse once the same decision structure is stretched over more steps, even when reasoning difficulty is held constant. The paper challenges the prevailing assumption that long-horizon agent training is mainly a matter of better orchestration, context engineering, or larger models; instead, it treats horizon length itself as an intrinsic bottleneck that drives exploration failure and credit-assignment noise. The pivot is from optimizing longer trajectories directly toward shrinking the effective horizon. Mechanically, the study builds controlled Sudoku and Rush Hour task suites where goal distance is varied while reasoning complexity is fixed, then trains a Qwen3-1.7B agent with SFT initialization and critic-free RL to expose how increasing horizon alone triggers instability, policy collapse, and incoherent overlong generations. The core shift is horizon reduction: replacing atomic actions with flexible macro actions, or decomposing a global goal into verifiable subgoals with intermediate rewards, so that the policy sees fewer, higher-signal decisions per episode. This is unusual because it isolates horizon as the causal variable and then proves the bottleneck persists across environments, model scales, and optimizers, including WebShop and GRPO-style training. The main result is that reduced horizons stabilize learning and improve final success, while also enabling “horizon generalization” to unseen longer variants at inference. This points toward future agent research centered on action abstraction, hierarchical task design, and horizon-aware curricula rather than ever more complex RL wrappers.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise as interactive agents that solve tasks through extended sequences of environment interactions. While prior work has primarily focused on system-level optimizations or algorithmic improvements, the role of task horizon length in shaping training dynamics remains poorly understood. In this work, we present a systematic empirical study that examines horizon length through controlled task constructions. Specifically, we construct controlled tasks in which agents face identical decision rules and reasoning structures, but differ only in the length of action sequences required for successful completion. Our results reveal that increasing horizon length alone constitutes a training bottleneck, inducing severe training instability driven by exploration difficulties and credit assignment challenges. We demonstrate that horizon reduction is a key principle to address this limitation, stabilizing training and achieving better performance in long-horizon tasks. Moreover, we find that horizon reduction is related to stronger generalization across horizon lengths: models trained under reduced horizons generalize more effectively to longer-horizon variants at inference time, a phenomenon we refer to as horizon generalization.

papers.title: Universal Smoothness via Bernstein Polynomials: A Constructive Approximation Approach for Activation Functions
papers.summary: Universal Smoothness via Bernstein Polynomials: A Constructive Approximation Approach for Activation Functions attacks a narrow but persistent bottleneck in deep nets: how to get the optimization stability of smooth activations without paying the transcendental-function tax, while also avoiding the dead gradients and kinks of ReLU-family units. The paper challenges the implicit workflow that treats activation design as either piecewise-linear efficiency or heuristic smooth gating, and pivots from ad hoc activation search toward constructive approximation theory. Its mechanical shift is to mollify Leaky ReLU with Bernstein polynomials: over a small interval [−ε, ε], the authors build a degree-2 Bernstein/Bézier quadratic bridge whose control points are fixed by continuity and differentiability constraints, yielding BerLU, a closed-form piecewise activation with a learnable negative slope α and no singularity at the origin. Unlike GELU/SiLU/Mish, the transition is implemented with basic arithmetic, not exp/erf. The differentiating idea is that the Bernstein variation-diminishing property gives a provable smoothness guarantee and a non-expansive Lipschitz bound, typically exactly 1, so gradients are interpolated rather than amplified. Across ViT, DeiT, TNT, and ConvNeXt on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet-1K, BerLU is reported to outperform GELU and other baselines, with especially large gains on CIFAR-100, while using less memory and faster forward passes; too-large ε, however, collapses nonlinearity and hurts accuracy. This points toward a future where activation functions are designed as theorem-backed, controllable approximation operators rather than searched black-box nonlinearities.
papers.abstract: The efficacy of deep neural networks is heavily reliant on the design of non-linear activation functions, yet existing approaches often struggle to balance optimization stability with computational efficiency. While piecewise linear functions offer inference speed, they suffer from optimization instability due to non-differentiability at the origin, whereas smooth counterparts typically incur significant computational overhead through their reliance on transcendental operations. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a general smoothing framework based on constructive approximation theory and introduces the Bernstein Linear Unit (BerLU). This novel activation function utilizes Bernstein polynomials to construct a differentiable quadratic transition region that effectively eliminates singularities while maintaining a piecewise linear structure. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that the proposed method guarantees strictly continuous differentiability and a non-expansive Lipschitz constant of one, which ensures stable gradient propagation and prevents the gradient explosion problems common in deep architectures. Comprehensive empirical evaluations across representative Vision Transformer and Convolutional Neural Network architectures confirm that this approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on standard image classification benchmarks while delivering superior computational and memory efficiency.

papers.title: Foundation-Model-Based Agents in Industrial Automation: Purposes, Capabilities, and Open Challenges
papers.summary: Foundation-Model-Based Agents in Industrial Automation: Purposes, Capabilities, and Open Challenges tackles a fragmentation problem: industrial LLM/agent papers were proliferating across manufacturing, logistics, energy, and engineering, but no shared definition or maturity map existed to distinguish real autonomous technical agents from mere chatbots or text-generation wrappers. The paper pivots from assuming that industrial agents are still mainly rule-based, optimization-driven, negotiation-heavy MAS toward a new assistive, monitoring-oriented paradigm in which foundation models sit at the center of context interpretation, decision-making, and action selection/execution. Its mechanical shift is not a new controller but a PRISMA-style systematic review of 2,341 records, distilled to 88 coded studies with a purpose–property–capability taxonomy and a baseline comparison to classical industrial agents. That coding reveals that 75.0% of systems are only TRL 4–6 prototypes/early validations, deployment evidence is rare (9.1%), and user assistance, process optimization, and monitoring dominate while planning, scheduling, and dispatching recede. Compared with conventional agents, FM-based systems gain sharply in human interaction and uncertainty handling, but lose heavily in negotiation, reflecting a move from multi-agent coordination to conversational tool-mediated orchestration. The paper is unusual because it also proposes a working definition of a foundation-model-based industrial agent that bridges agent theory, automation-engineering standards, and FM capabilities. Its main implication is that the field is moving toward trustworthy deployment, robust evaluation, RAG/tool integration, and human-in-the-loop safeguards rather than assuming raw model scale will solve industrial autonomy.
papers.abstract: Foundation models, particularly large language models, are increasingly integrated into agent architectures for industrial tasks such as decision support, process monitoring, and engineering automation. Yet evidence on their purposes, capabilities, and limitations remains fragmented across domains. This work examines how mature foundation-model-based agent systems are in industrial contexts, how their functional profile differs from conventional agent systems, and which limitations persist. A systematic literature survey following the PRISMA 2020 guideline is presented, screening 2,341 publications and synthesising a corpus of 88 publications through a structured coding scheme. The results show that reported systems are predominantly at prototype and early validation stages (75.0% at TRL 4-6), with deployment-oriented evidence remaining rare (9.1%). Operational goals are most frequently positioned in user assistance, monitoring, and process optimisation, while conventional production-control purposes such as planning and scheduling are less prominent. Compared with an established baseline for industrial agent systems, the capability profile reveals substantial gains in human interaction (+37%) and dealing with uncertainty (+35%), but a pronounced deficit in negotiation (-39%). The most widely reported limitations concern lack of generalization, hallucination and output instability, data scarcity, and inference latency. A working definition of foundation-model-based industrial agents is also proposed, bridging conventional agent theory, automation-engineering standards, and the foundation-model paradigm.

papers.title: Counterfactual Reasoning in Automated Planning
papers.summary: Counterfactual Reasoning in Automated Planning surveys a planning trend that moves the field from assuming a fully fixed task tuple to treating the task itself as a controllable object of reasoning: instead of only searching for a plan inside given initial state, goals, and actions, these works ask what should be changed, when the change should be triggered, and why the planner should deviate from the original problem. The paper challenges the old workflow in which planning, explanation, and model repair are separate afterthoughts, and pivots toward counterfactual task adaptation for solvability, better quality, or behavior control. Its mechanical shift is a four-way taxonomy over changes to fluents, actions, initial state, and goals, combined with offline or execution-time triggering and different optimization motives. The survey distinguishes model repair and environment redesign for action-set edits, model reconciliation for making a given plan optimal, opportunity monitoring for replanning when new facts appear, and goal-driven autonomy or oversubscription planning for selecting or revising goals under constraints. Unusually, it emphasizes that current literature mostly edits existing actions, deletes actions, or tweaks preconditions/effects, but rarely synthesizes new actions from scratch or reasons about cost-improving action additions, despite those being natural in domains like logistics. The main claim is that the area is rapidly growing but fragmented, lacking a unified framework and comparative machinery for choosing among multiple counterfactuals. This points toward integrated planners that decide online whether to repair the model, relax goals, or redesign the environment.
papers.abstract: Automated planning traditionally assumes that all aspects of a planning task (initial state, goals, and available actions) are fully specified in advance, an approach well-suited to domains with fixed rules and deterministic execution. However, real-world planning often requires flexibility, allowing for deviations from the original task parameters in response to unforeseen circumstances or to improve outcomes. This paper surveys existing works on counterfactual reasoning in automated planning, categorizing them by what elements are changed, when the reasoning is triggered, and why and how these changes are made. We conclude by discussing key findings and outlining open research questions to guide future work in this area.

papers.title: SCGNN: Semantic Consistency enhanced Graph Neural Network Guided by Granular-ball Computing
papers.summary: SCGNN: Semantic Consistency enhanced Graph Neural Network Guided by Granular-ball Computing attacks the bottleneck that semantic-neighborhood mining in graphs is usually done by node-level kNN or full-search similarity, which scales quadratically, hard-codes top-k choices, and injects noisy edges when features are ambiguous. The paper pivots from pairwise neighbor selection toward group-level semantic structure: instead of asking which single nodes are nearest, it partitions the feature space into granular balls and treats each ball as a local semantic domain. The mechanical shift is a transductive granular-ball computing pass over all features plus training labels, splitting balls by purity, then separating semantic definite, uncertain, and chaos regions so only reliable under-ball nodes contribute to GBC prediction. SCGNN then uses a dual enhancement loop: a structure enhancement module builds an anchor-based two-level augment graph, where each granular ball becomes a virtual anchor node, projection edges connect anchors to member nodes, and bridging edges connect same-label anchors to carry long-range semantics; a supervision enhancement module performs label consistency checking between GBC predictions and GNN pseudo-labels, retaining only agreements to form lower-noise LCC labels. Unlike nearby rewiring or mixup methods, SCGNN never prunes the original graph; it jointly encodes vanilla and augment graphs, fuses predictions, and backpropagates with train, anchor, and LCC losses. Across 11 benchmarks and multiple backbones, it improves accuracy while keeping preprocessing near-linear and memory light, pointing toward uncertainty-aware, group-based graph learning.
papers.abstract: Capturing semantic consistency among nodes is crucial for effective graph representation learning. Existing approaches typically rely on $k$-nearest neighbors ($k$NN) or other node-level full search algorithms (FSA) to mine semantic relationships via exhaustive pairwise similarity computation, which suffer from high computational complexity and rigid neighbor selection, limiting scalability and introducing noisy connections. In this paper, we propose the Semantic Consistency enhanced Graph Neural Network (SCGNN), a novel plug-and-play framework that leverages granular-ball computing (GBC) to efficiently capture semantic consistency in a scalable manner. Unlike node-level FSA methods, SCGNN models group-level semantic structure by adaptively partitioning nodes into granular balls, significantly reducing computational cost while improving robustness to noise. To effectively utilize the discovered group-level semantic consistency, we design a dual enhancement strategy. Specifically, (1) a structure enhancement module constructs an anchor-based graph structure, where each anchor is a virtual node representing the group-level semantic carried by a granular ball, then injecting group-level semantic information into the graph structure; and (2) a supervision enhancement module performs label consistency checking (LCC) by combining GBC predictions with model-generated pseudo-labels, thereby producing more reliable supervision signals. SCGNN is compatible with various GNN backbones. During the forward propagation of SCGNN, the vanilla graph and the augment graph are jointly encoded, and their predictions are fused; during the backpropagation, the supervision enhancement module provides enhanced supervision signals to guide parameter updates.

papers.title: Trustworthy AI Suffers from Invariance Conflicts and Causality is The Solution
papers.summary: Trustworthy AI Suffers from Invariance Conflicts and Causality is The Solution diagnoses a bottleneck that standard trustworthy-AI workflows treat too casually: fairness, robustness, privacy, explainability, and utility are not merely competing desiderata, but often demand incompatible invariance conditions under different interventions on the data-generating process. The paper challenges the implicit scaling story that better accuracy, more data, or uniform regularization can reconcile these objectives, and pivots from observational performance toward interventional validity. Its core mechanical shift is to reframe each trust target as a stability requirement over a family of perturbed distributions, then use causal structure to decide which dependencies are spurious, which are legitimate, and which invariances can be made selective rather than global. In the motivating healthcare readmission example, a structural causal model separates direct discrimination, proxy bias, environment-induced shift, and clinically valid paths, showing how one can block unfair or unstable routes while preserving task-relevant signal. The paper distinguishes explicit causal integration, via SCMs, causal graphs, and path-specific constraints, from implicit causal integration, via counterfactual augmentation, invariant feature learning, causal regularization, and causal RLHF-style alignment across classical ML and foundation models. Its main claim is conceptual rather than benchmark-driven: many “trade-offs” are structurally induced by invariance conflicts and can be softened by causal design, though identifiability, scaling, and benchmark scarcity remain major limits. This points toward multi-objective, interventional evaluation regimes and hybrid causal systems with localized guardrails for high-stakes deployment.
papers.abstract: As artificial intelligence (AI), including machine learning (ML) models and foundation models (FMs), is increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains, ensuring their trustworthiness has become a central challenge. However, the core trustworthy AI objectives, such as fairness, robustness, privacy, and explainability, are hard to achieve simultaneously, especially while preserving utility. This position paper argues that causality is necessary to understand and balance trade-offs in performance and multiple objectives of trustworthy AI. We ground our arguments in re-interpreting trustworthy AI trade-offs as incompatible invariance requirements under different changes to the data-generating process. We then illustrate that causality provides a unifying framework for understanding how trade-offs in trustworthy AI arise, and how they can be softened or resolved through selective invariance. This perspective applies to both classical ML models and large-scale FMs. Our paper discusses how causal assumptions may be applied explicitly or implicitly in modern large-scale systems. Finally, we outline open challenges and opportunities for using causality to build more trustworthy AI.

papers.title: Deciphering Shortcut Learning from an Evolutionary Game Theory Perspective
papers.summary: Deciphering Shortcut Learning from an Evolutionary Game Theory Perspective attacks the under-theorized mechanism by which deep nets come to prefer spurious cues over task-relevant ones, instead of treating shortcut bias as just an empirical robustness flaw. The paper challenges the usual benchmark-era assumption that shortcut learning is a fixed property of the dataset or a mere consequence of model capacity, and pivots from static feature attribution toward a dynamic population process in which training samples are “players” and their neural tangent feature directions are “strategies.” The mechanical shift is a formal core-vs-shortcut taxonomy plus an evolutionary game model: a sample’s payoff is the loss decrease it induces, feature alignment defines replication, and the network is assumed to contain decoupled core and shortcut subnetworks. This turns optimization into a strategy-selection process with stochastically stable states, analyzed via payoff matrices, singular perturbation, and a continuous stochastic differential equation for noise. Unusually, the paper proves that core features are unique while shortcut features are ubiquitous, and it links early shortcut dominance to higher explained variance and spectral alignment of the data kernel. In the resulting theory, full-batch GD becomes shortcut-favoring, whereas mini-batch SGD can shift stability toward core features; data noise amplifies shortcut bias while optimization noise can suppress it. The setting is idealized binary classification with fully connected ReLU networks and Colored-MNIST-style intuition, but the implication is broad: future robustness work may need training-dynamics control levers, not just data augmentation or post hoc debiasing.
papers.abstract: Shortcut learning causes deep learning models to rely on non-essential features within the data. However, its formation in deep neural network training still lacks theoretical understanding. In this paper, we provide a formal definition of core and shortcut features and employ evolutionary game theory to analyze the origins of shortcut bias by modeling data samples as players and their corresponding neural tangent features as strategies, assuming the existence of core and shortcut subnetworks. We find that gradient descent (GD) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) lead to two distinct stochastically stable states, each corresponding to a different strategy. The former primarily optimizes the shortcut subnetwork, while the latter primarily optimizes the core subnetwork. We investigate the influence of these strategies on shortcut bias through a continuous stochastic differential equation, and reveal the impact of data noise and optimization noise on the formation of shortcut bias. In brief, our work employs evolutionary game theory to characterize the dynamics of shortcut bias formation and provides a theoretical view on its mitigation.

papers.title: AcademiClaw: When Students Set Challenges for AI Agents
papers.summary: AcademiClaw: When Students Set Challenges for AI Agents confronts the mismatch between OpenClaw’s assistant-oriented benchmarks and the academic-level work where agents actually fail: homework, research projects, competition problems, scientific analysis, GPU-heavy ML engineering, and full-stack debugging. It pivots from researcher-authored, narrow routine tasks toward end-user-sourced, frontier-difficulty workflows, using a bottom-up pipeline in which 230 student submissions are expert-filtered into 80 bilingual tasks across 25+ domains, including 16 CUDA-required cases.
