The mechanical shift is the benchmark design itself: each task runs in an isolated Docker sandbox, the rubric is hidden from the agent, and scoring is not binary but a 0–100 multi-dimensional evaluation that combines six verification modes—pattern matching, code execution, LLM-as-judge, vision judging, end-to-end browser testing, and structured-output validation—plus a five-category safety audit over the full tool-call trajectory. That combination is unusual relative to nearby agent benchmarks because it measures not just whether a model “finishes” but how it reasons, executes, leaks, or violates workspace boundaries while doing so. On six frontier models, the best pass rate is only 55%, with sharp capability cliffs by domain, universally unsolved olympiad-level reasoning, and a near-zero correlation between token spend and score, suggesting overthinking without reward. The paper points toward future agent research that is user-sourced, GPU-aware, safety-instrumented, and evaluated by long-horizon task completion rather than assistant-style proxies.
papers.abstract: Benchmarks within the OpenClaw ecosystem have thus far evaluated exclusively assistant-level tasks, leaving the academic-level capabilities of OpenClaw largely unexamined. We introduce AcademiClaw, a bilingual benchmark of 80 complex, long-horizon tasks sourced directly from university students' real academic workflows -- homework, research projects, competitions, and personal projects -- that they found current AI agents unable to solve effectively. Curated from 230 student-submitted candidates through rigorous expert review, the final task set spans 25+ professional domains, ranging from olympiad-level mathematics and linguistics problems to GPU-intensive reinforcement learning and full-stack system debugging, with 16 tasks requiring CUDA GPU execution. Each task executes in an isolated Docker sandbox and is scored on task completion by multi-dimensional rubrics combining six complementary techniques, with an independent five-category safety audit providing additional behavioral analysis. Experiments on six frontier models show that even the best achieves only a 55\% pass rate. Further analysis uncovers sharp capability boundaries across task domains, divergent behavioral strategies among models, and a disconnect between token consumption and output quality, providing fine-grained diagnostic signals beyond what aggregate metrics reveal. We hope that AcademiClaw and its open-sourced data and code can serve as a useful resource for the OpenClaw community, driving progress toward agents that are more capable and versatile across the full breadth of real-world academic demands. All data and code are available at https://github.com/GAIR-NLP/AcademiClaw.

papers.title: An explainable hypothesis-driven approach to Drug-Induced Liver Injury with HADES
papers.summary: An explainable hypothesis-driven approach to Drug-Induced Liver Injury with HADES pivots DILI modeling away from the standard binary “toxic or not” classifier toward an explainable hypothesis-generation workflow, attacking the failure mode where existing predictors give a risk score but no mechanistic rationale for late-stage clinical-trial attrition decisions. The old assumption it challenges is that scaffold-based classification alone is the right target; instead, it treats DILI as a family of mechanistically distinct causal scenarios that should be evaluated by whether the model can propose the right hepatotoxic story. The mechanical shift is twofold: the DILER Benchmark replaces label-only evaluation with 929 literature-grounded hypotheses over 223 held-out DILI compounds plus a 34-molecule Post-2021 set, and HADES replaces opaque end-to-end prediction with a LangGraph agentic loop. In HADES, a DILI subagent first anonymizes the query molecule, then combines Boltz-1-based structural descriptions, similarity retrieval, metabolite generation via a CYP gate plus BioTransformer, and an AOP predictor over MIEs/KEs; DeepResearch then audits those tool outputs against external biomedical literature and only afterward formulates explicit, cited mechanistic hypotheses. This is unusual because it enforces epistemic separation between computation and literature lookup, reducing name-memorization leakage seen in frontier LLM baselines. HADES improves binary ROC-AUC to 0.68 on the test set and 0.59 on Post-2021, while establishing a first mechanistic alignment baseline of 0.16 fuzzy Jaccard, with hallucination still the dominant error. The paper points toward safety-critical AI that is not just predictive but auditable, hypothesis-first, and benchmarked on causal adequacy.
papers.abstract: Drug-induced liver injury (DILI) remains a leading cause of late-stage clinical trial attrition. However, existing computational predictors primarily rely on binary classification, a framing that limits generalization and yields no mechanistic insight to guide translational decisions. We argue that DILI prediction is better posed as an explainable hypothesis-generation problem. To support this shift, we introduce the DILER Benchmark, a dataset that extends beyond binary labels by augmenting a curated set of molecules with mechanistic hepatotoxicity hypotheses derived from biomedical literature. We further present HADES, an agentic system designed to generate transparent and auditable reasoning traces. By combining molecular-level predictions, metabolite decomposition, structural understanding, and toxicity pathway evidence, HADES mechanistically assesses DILI risk. Evaluated on the DILER Benchmark, HADES outperforms existing models in binary classification, achieving a ROC-AUC of 0.68 on the Test Set and 0.59 on the challenging Post-2021 Set, compared with 0.63 and 0.50 for DILI-Predictor, respectively. More importantly, we establish a baseline for mechanistic hypothesis generation, where HADES achieves a Hypothesis Alignment Fuzzy Jaccard Index of 0.16. This result underscores the inherent complexity of the task while highlighting the need for advanced explainable approaches in predictive toxicology.

papers.title: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge
papers.summary: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge pivots from speaker-centric affect benchmarks toward modeling dyad-coupled, time-evolving interaction, attacking the bottleneck that current systems miss directional influence, turn coordination, and rapport development between partners. Instead of treating conversation as isolated utterance classification or static speaker emotion recognition, it turns the field toward a shared, multimodal benchmark on naturalistic face-to-face dialogue: 945 sessions, 743.4 hours, five languages, fixed train/validation/test splits, and standardized metrics. The core mechanical shift is three coordinated sub-challenges on the same corpus: directional interpersonal influence prediction over 10 affect dimensions, turn-taking prediction for next speaker and time-to-next-speech, and rapport trajectory prediction over full conversations. Influence labels come from an internal speech-expression model, turn-taking labels from VAD-derived event tables with switch/overlap/interruption structure, and rapport labels from an LLM pseudo-labeling pipeline that scores overlapping 30-second windows using prior transcript context plus acoustic tag summaries, then smooths the trajectory. Released baselines are deliberately simple pooled-embedding models using Whisper-small audio and FaceNet video features with MLP heads, making the benchmark a probe of temporal/dyadic reasoning rather than architectural novelty. Results show audio dominates video and naive multimodal concatenation often fails to help, while best scores remain modest, especially for influence, signaling that robust modeling of reciprocal dependence and long-horizon social dynamics is still unsolved. This suggests a direction where future AI systems learn culturally aware, interaction-level dynamics rather than one-speaker predictions.
papers.abstract: The 2026 ACII Dyadic Conversations (ACII-DaiKon) Workshop & Challenge introduces a benchmark for modeling interpersonal affect and social dynamics in dyadic conversations. Although conversational affect modeling has advanced rapidly, most benchmarks remain speaker-centric and underrepresent coupled, time-evolving processes between partners, including directional influence, conversational timing coordination, and rapport development. To address this gap, ACII-DaiKon presents three coordinated sub-challenges built on a shared dataset: (1) directional interpersonal influence prediction, (2) turn-taking prediction (next-speaker and time-to-next-speech), and (3) rapport trajectory prediction across full interactions. The challenge is built on the Hume-DaiKon dataset, comprising 945 dyadic conversations (743.4 hours of audiovisual data) collected under naturalistic conditions across five languages. The benchmark supports multimodal modeling, temporal reasoning, and cross-context generalization through fixed train/validation/test splits, standardized metrics, and released baseline systems. Evaluation uses Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC), Pearson correlation, Macro-F1, and Mean Absolute Error (MAE) depending on the sub-challenge. Baseline experiments establish initial reference performance, with best test results of 0.40 CCC and 0.50 Pearson for influence prediction, 0.66 Macro-F1 and 1.50~s MAE for turn-taking, and 0.68 CCC and 0.70 Pearson for rapport trajectory modeling. These results indicate that while current methods capture coarse dyadic patterns, robust modeling of directional dependence and long-horizon interpersonal dynamics remains challenging. The workshop provides a shared platform for rigorous comparison and cross-disciplinary discussion on data validity, evaluation protocols, and culturally aware modeling for dyadic interaction.

papers.title: Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI
papers.summary: Hybrid Inspection and Task-Based Access Control in Zero-Trust Agentic AI attacks the security gap that appears when LLM agents can dynamically invoke tools across multi-turn conversations: current OAuth-style delegated authorization and prior TBAC assume the application faithfully relays intent, yet a compromised intermediary can swap tools, tamper with parameters, falsify results, or quietly request scopes beyond the subject’s actual task. The paper pivots from static, single-turn scope checks toward Continuous Agent Semantic Authorization, where a zero-trust interception layer performs five deterministic message-flow integrity checks plus a semantic inspection path. The mechanical shift is a two-stage semantic pipeline: first, conversation task extraction distills the user’s objective from multi-turn dialogue at the interception layer; second, task-tool semantic matching at the authorization server decides whether each requested tool is appropriate for that extracted task, enabling per-tool access control rather than blanket agent permissions. Unlike nearby agent-security work, it explicitly models the semantic gap that survives binary validation and evaluates it on new multi-turn conversation-tool datasets built by extending ASTRA with synthesized relevant and irrelevant tool calls, plus a multi-agent simulation that generates more realistic dialog and maliciously misdirected requests. Across GPT-5.2, GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Claude Opus 4.6, single-turn performance is strong, but multi-turn and multi-agent settings expose higher false positives and intent-reconstruction difficulty; Claude Sonnet 4.6 is often best, yet the authors conclude semantic checks alone are not enough for long-horizon autonomous tool use. This points toward hybrid, conservative, intent-aware authorization for distributed agent systems.
papers.abstract: Authorizing Large Language Model (LLM)-driven agents to dynamically invoke tools and access protected resources introduces significant security risks, and the risks grow dramatically as agents engage in multi-turn conversations and scale toward distributed collaboration. A compromised or malicious agentic application can tamper with tool calls, falsify results, or request permissions beyond the scope of the subject's intended tasks, which could go unnoticed with current delegated authorization flows given their lack of visibility into the original subject's intent. In light of this, we make the following contributions towards Continuous Agent Semantic Authorization (CASA). First, we propose a hybrid runtime enforcement model that combines deterministic and semantic controls enabled by a zero-trust interception layer. Five deterministic controls enforce structural and data-integrity guarantees over the message flow, while a semantic inspection layer evaluates whether tool call choices align with the intended tasks commissioned to the agent. Second, differently from prior Task-Based Access Control (TBAC) techniques that operate on single-turn interactions, we decompose the semantic layer into two stages: i) a task-extraction step that distills the subject's objectives from multi-turn conversations at the interception layer, and ii) a task-tool semantic matching step at the authorization server that evaluates whether the requested tools are appropriate for the extracted tasks. Third, we extend the ASTRA dataset that we introduced in a prior work, by generating novel conversation-tool datasets with multi-turn interactions containing relevant and irrelevant tool calls for a given task. Lastly, we provide the first experimental results for TBAC under multi-turn conversations.

papers.title: An Empirical Study of Agent Skills for Healthcare: Practice, Gaps, and Governance
papers.summary: An Empirical Study of Agent Skills for Healthcare: Practice, Gaps, and Governance attacks a concrete mismatch in healthcare AI: the field has benchmarked agents as if clinical capability were a transferable model property, while real healthcare automation is constrained by local procedures, organizational context, and liability boundaries. The paper pivots from evaluating healthcare agents as monolithic decision-makers toward examining “agent skills” as reusable procedural artifacts, where the mechanical shift is to study what developers actually package in public skill directories rather than what papers assume should matter. Using a snapshot of 58,159 ClawHub skills, the authors filter 557 healthcare-related skills and annotate them along ten dimensions spanning function, care-cycle stage, intended user, input modality, autonomy, clinical impact, general technical risk, vulnerability, and safety boundaries. This corpus-level analysis reveals a different center of gravity than healthcare-agent research: public skills emphasize patient-facing workflow automation, monitoring, health education, and research support, while diagnosis and treatment are comparatively underrepresented. The bottleneck becomes not just model accuracy but procedural fit, especially because specialized clinical inputs like imaging and physiological signals appear in fewer than 2% of skills and most artifacts rely on forms, text, and documents. Unusually, the study separates autonomy from clinical impact and shows that general technical risk is a weak proxy for clinical risk; many clinically consequential skills are only delegated executors and lack explicit boundary statements. The result points toward a future of skill-level governance, provenance, intended-use constraints, and deployment-aware evaluation for healthcare agents.
papers.abstract: Healthcare automation is shaped by local procedures and organizational constraints, so agent capabilities rarely transfer unchanged across settings. Agent skills, self-contained directories that package reusable procedures for AI agents, are emerging as a procedural layer for adapting healthcare agents across diverse healthcare settings. We present the first empirical analysis of healthcare agent skills, drawing on 557 healthcare-related skills filtered from 58,159 public skills on ClawHub and annotated along ten dimensions covering function, deployment context, autonomy, and safety. We find that public healthcare skills emphasize patient-facing workflow automation and monitoring rather than the diagnostic and treatment-oriented tasks foregrounded in healthcare-agent research; coverage of the healthcare lifecycle and specialized clinical inputs remains uneven; and general technical risk does not reliably capture clinical risk. These findings position healthcare skills as a procedural layer not yet addressed by current benchmarks and risk frameworks.

papers.title: ORPilot: A Production-Oriented Agentic LLM-for-OR Tool for Optimization Modeling
papers.summary: ORPilot: A Production-Oriented Agentic LLM-for-OR Tool for Optimization Modeling attacks the real bottleneck in LLM-for-OR: not solving toy LPs, but converting messy business requests plus raw operational spreadsheets into solver-ready models without assuming a fully specified prompt. It challenges the dominant academic workflow that treats optimization modeling as single-pass code synthesis from clean, inline, textbook data, and pivots from “generate solver code from a polished statement” toward “interview, collect, derive, compile, and repair” under production ambiguity. The mechanical shift is a four-agent pipeline: a conversational interview agent elicits missing assumptions one targeted question at a time; a data collection agent separates data from the prompt and requests CSVs with explicit schemas; a parameter computation agent inspects raw tables and generates Python to derive model-ready quantities such as distance matrices, aggregated demand, and Big-M values; and a solver-agnostic, typed JSON IR captures sets, parameters, variables, objectives, and constraints for deterministic zero-LLM recompilation into Gurobi, CPLEX, PuLP, Pyomo, or OR-Tools. Self-correcting retry loops feed solver tracebacks, infeasibility, and runtime errors back to the LLM for targeted repairs. Compared with nearby work, ORPilot is unusual in treating reproducibility and backend portability as first-class outputs rather than afterthoughts. On large-scale synthetic supply-chain LP/MIP/IP cases it solved real-world-sized instances, and on IndustryOR it beat prior tools, while remaining comparable on NL4OPT and NLP4LP. The remaining limitation is semantic correctness of “optimal” but misformulated models, pointing toward future solution-validation agents, database-connected data ingestion, and IR-based what-if analysis.
papers.abstract: This paper presents ORPilot, an open-source agentic AI system that translates real-world business problems into solver-ready optimization models. Unlike academic LLM-for-OR tools that assume clean problem specifications with preformatted inline data, ORPilot is designed for production conditions: ambiguous descriptions, large-scale raw operational data, and the need for portability across solver backends. The system introduces four novel components: (1) a conversational interview agent to elicit complete problem specifications, (2) a data collection agent that retrieves data independently of prompts, (3) a parameter computation agent to bridge raw tabular data and model-ready parameters, and (4) a solver-agnostic Intermediate Representation (IR) for deterministic, zero-LLM-call recompilation to Gurobi, CPLEX, PuLP, Pyomo, or OR-Tools solvers. Additionally, self-correcting retry loops utilize solver tracebacks for targeted repairs. ORPilot represents the first attempt to target production-level business problems rather than textbook operations research (OR) cases. Evaluation on real-world problems demonstrates promising results. When tested against traditional academic benchmarks: IndustryOR, NL4OPT and NLP4LP, ORPilot outperformed state-of-the-art tools in accuracy on the IndustryOR benchmark and delivered comparable performance on NL4OPT and NLP4LP.

papers.title: Coherent Hierarchical Multi-Label Learning to Defer for Medical Imaging
papers.summary: Coherent Hierarchical Multi-Label Learning to Defer for Medical Imaging attacks a failure mode missed by flat learning-to-defer: in clinical taxonomies, a model can be label-consistent yet still produce incoherent handoff actions, such as deferring a parent concept while asserting a child, or deferring a label already implied by its own ancestor assertions. The paper challenges the standard binary-relevance workflow that treats each node as an independent defer-or-predict decision and pivots from per-label abstention toward contract-aware delegation over a hierarchy. The core mechanical shift is the Selective-Exclusion handoff contract, which makes deferral a structured action in the ternary space {0,1,⊥}, then enforces coherence through three coupled constraints: taxonomic satisfiability, workflow admissibility, and deductive closure. They formalize the Bayes-optimal coherent rule and show that even nodewise Bayes L2D can be action-incoherent. To operationalize this, they introduce exact coherent projection, a tree dynamic-programming decoder over the coherent action set, and Taxonomic Belief Propagation with Recursive Policy Optimisation (TBP+RPO), which trains local primitives through the same hierarchy recursion used at inference so forbidden parent-child action pairs have zero support by construction. Across VinDr-CXR and CheXpert real-reader benchmarks plus PadChest and ADPv2 controlled-expert stress tests, naive BR-L2D exhibits non-trivial incoherence dominated by delegation violations and deductive defects; projection removes it exactly, while TBP+RPO drives incoherence near zero with strong utility. The paper points toward contract-aware human-AI routing, where future work extends coherent delegation to DAG taxonomies and prospective clinical deployment.
papers.abstract: Learning to Defer (L2D) enables a model to predict autonomously or defer to an expert, but prior work largely assumes flat label spaces. We study the first L2D setting with hierarchical multi-label decisions, motivated by medical-imaging workflows in which findings are organised by clinical taxonomies. In this setting, deferral is a delegation action rather than a label assignment, so treating it as an independent per-label decision can produce deferral incoherence, including taxonomic contradictions, delegation violations, and deferrals of labels already implied by the model's own assertions. We formalise coherent hierarchical deferral under a Selective-Exclusion handoff contract, characterise the Bayes-optimal coherent deferral rule, and show that even nodewise Bayes L2D can be action-incoherent. We then propose two remedies: exact coherent projection, a dynamic-programming decoder over the coherent action set, and Taxonomic Belief Propagation (TBP) with Recursive Policy Optimisation (RPO), a contract-aware joint action model trained through the same recursion used at inference. Across real-reader and controlled-expert medical-imaging benchmarks, naive binary-relevance L2D exhibits non-trivial incoherence. Projection removes it exactly, and fast TBP+RPO drives incoherence near zero while retaining strong utility.

papers.title: AI and Open-data Driven Scalable Solar Power Profiling
papers.summary: AI and Open-data Driven Scalable Solar Power Profiling attacks a concrete bottleneck in distributed energy planning: rooftop PV exists at city scale, but inventories of where panels are, how much area they cover, and what they can produce are stale, proprietary, or too labor-intensive to build. The paper challenges the prevailing workflow of task-specific detector training on manually labeled satellite tiles, and also sidesteps LLM-style textual solar analysis that cannot preserve pixel-level geometry. It pivots from closed, case-by-case remote-sensing pipelines toward an open-data centric loop that combines OSM building footprints, open aerial imagery, and foundation vision models. The mechanical shift is not just “detect panels,” but detect them, georeference them, and then convert their footprints into energy profiles: the system queries OSM for rooftop extents, retrieves open imagery, runs SAM3/SAM3.1 to segment solar panels, linearly maps pixels back into latitude-longitude coordinates, emits georeferenced polygons, and feeds those footprints into open-weather/PV models (solar position, Perez transposition, Faiman temperature, ADR efficiency) to synthesize hourly annual power curves. What is unusual is the insistence on reproducible, incrementally extensible solar inventories rather than one-off segmentation outputs, plus a public API that returns building-level polygons for user-specified locations. In two Swiss test areas with sparse OSM coverage, the pipeline vastly expanded panel counts over OSM alone, with SAM3.1 outperforming SAM3, though some manual cleaning remained. This points toward future geospatial AI systems that fuse open imagery, GIS anchors, and physics-based simulation into living urban energy maps.
papers.abstract: Solar photovoltaic (PV) deployment is expanding rapidly, yet detailed, up-to-date information on the spatial distribution and capacity of rooftop PV remains limited. This paper presents an open, scalable framework for detecting solar panels from open data and generating city-level solar power profiles. We leverage foundation vision AI models to detect solar panel geometries from open-source satellite imagery. This avoids manual data labeling and case-specific model training while maintaining robustness across heterogeneous imagery. Detected solar panels are converted into georeferenced polygons, yielding spatially explicit and incrementally extensible inventories. By integrating open weather data, we translate panel footprints into regional solar power profiles. The framework reduces dependency on proprietary imagery, manual labeling, and closed-source models, and offers a transparent and scalable approach for solar planning and analysis. We released the data and an API resulted from this work. For any user-specified building location, our API retrieves aerial imagery, detects rooftop solar panels, and returns georeferenced polygons. This empowers researchers and developers to scan user-defined areas to build solar panel maps and associated solar production profiles, thus facilitating advanced analysis like distributed solar production integration, local power flow optimization, energy tariff design, and infrastructure planning.

papers.title: Foundation Models to Unlock Real-World Evidence from Nationwide Medical Claims
papers.summary: Foundation Models to Unlock Real-World Evidence from Nationwide Medical Claims attacks the bottleneck that nationwide administrative claims are rich, longitudinal, and standardized yet still mostly treated as tabular features or disease-specific prediction tables, limiting their use for generalizable real-world evidence. The paper pivots from endpoint-by-endpoint LightGBM workflows and EHR-centric transformers toward a claims-native foundation model, ReClaim, trained from scratch as a decoder-only generative transformer on 43.8 billion medical events from more than 200 million MarketScan enrollees, with monthly-aggregated trajectories that jointly encode diagnoses, procedures, medications, enrollment, and expenditure. The mechanical shift is not just scale but representation: hierarchical code standardization compresses an enormous billing vocabulary, anchor tokens preserve temporal gaps, next-token pretraining learns longitudinal structure, and a task-specific post-training stage inserts an instruction token and masks the prompt so the model directly predicts incident disease tokens; at inference, disease risk comes from the instructed final-token logit rather than Monte Carlo sampling over full future sequences. Compared with nearby work, ReClaim is distinctive in treating claims as a portable substrate for both prediction and causal adjustment, using the same embeddings for propensity-score augmentation in target trial emulation. Across 1,000+ onset tasks it reaches 75.6% mean AUC versus 66.3% for LightGBM and 69.4% for Delphi, with the largest gains on rare diseases; it also improves expenditure R2 from 0.28 to 0.37 and cuts systematic bias by 72%, suggesting future healthcare AI will fuse foundation-model representations with RWE and causal inference.
papers.abstract: Evidence derived from large-scale real-world data (RWD) is increasingly informing regulatory evaluation and healthcare decision-making. Administrative claims provide population-scale, longitudinal records of healthcare utilization, expenditure, and detailed coding of diagnoses, procedures, and medications, yet their potential as a substrate for healthcare foundation models remains largely unexplored. Here we present ReClaim, a generative transformer trained from scratch on 43.8 billion medical events from more than 200 million enrollees in the MarketScan claims data spanning 2008-2022. ReClaim models longitudinal trajectories across diagnoses, procedures, medications, and expenditure, and was scaled to 140 million, 700 million, and 1.7 billion parameters. Across over 1,000 disease-onset prediction tasks, ReClaim achieved a mean AUC of 75.6%, substantially outperforming disease-specific LightGBM (66.3%) and the transformer-based Delphi model (69.4%), with the largest gains for rare diseases. These advantages held across retrospective and prospective evaluations and in external validation on two independent datasets. Performance improved monotonically with scale, and post-training added 13.8 percentage points over pre-training alone. Beyond disease prediction, ReClaim captured financial outcomes and improved real-world evidence (RWE) analyses: for healthcare expenditure forecasting it increased explained variance from 0.28 to 0.37 relative to LightGBM, and in a target trial emulation it reduced systematic bias by 72% on average relative to Delphi. Together, these results establish administrative claims as a scalable substrate for healthcare foundation models and show that learned representations generalize across time periods and data sources, supporting disease surveillance, expenditure forecasting, and RWE generation.

papers.title: Triple Spectral Fusion for Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition
papers.summary: Triple Spectral Fusion for Sensor-based Human Activity Recognition attacks the core HAR bottleneck that learning-based models still fuse IMU posture, motion, and context signals as if they were homogeneous time series, which leaves noise, sensor-specific roles, and long-range redundancy poorly handled. It challenges the older workflow of uniform sensor treatment plus temporal-only fusion in CNN/RNN/Transformer stacks, and also moves beyond fixed spectral preprocessing such as STFT or static complementary filters. The pivot is from “encode everything in time” toward feature-level, adaptive spectral routing across three domains. Mechanically, the framework replaces uniform fusion with an IMU fusion block that turns gravimeter and gyroscope streams into posture features through adaptive complementary filtering, using wide-kernel causal convolutions as sliding cut-off windows and posture-sensor attention to learn the cutoff balance, while linear acceleration is separately encoded as motion. It then fuses posture/motion nodes in a dynamic heterogeneous graph via graph Fourier filtering, where learned signed adjacency and adaptive low-pass/high-pass filters preserve both homogeneous and heterogeneous node information. Finally, a wavelet stage uses DWT plus Gumbel-softmax frequency selection to keep only the primary band for local/global temporal fusion, shrinking redundant context before graph aggregation and self-attention. Evaluated under LOSO on ten smartphone and wearable benchmarks, it outperforms DeepConvLSTM and IF-ConvTransformer, with lower FLOPs and fewer parameters than many graph/Transformer baselines. This points to HAR research moving toward task-adaptive, domain-aware spectral control rather than brute-force temporal scaling.
papers.abstract: The field of sensor-based human activity recognition (HAR) mainly uses posture, motion and context data of Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) to identify daily activities. Despite the advancements in learning-based methods, it is challenging to perform information fusion from the temporal perspective due to the complexities in fusing heterogeneous sensor data and establishing long-term context correlations. This paper proposes a novel triple spectral fusion framework tailored for HAR. First, we develop an adaptive complementary filtering technique for noise suppression and organize each IMU's sensors into posture and motion modality nodes. Given that IMU nodes form a dynamic heterogeneous graph, we then apply adaptive filtering within the graph Fourier domain to merge both homogeneous and heterogeneous node information. Furthermore, an adaptive wavelet frequency selection approach is implemented to suppress context redundancy and shorten the length of features. This approach enhances both timestamp-based graph aggregation and the correlation of long-term contexts. Our framework uses adaptive filtering in the Fourier, graph Fourier, and wavelet domains, enabling effective multi-sensor fusion and context correlation. Extensive experiments on ten benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our framework. Project page: https://github.com/crocodilegogogo/TSF-TPAMI2026.

papers.title: Mitigating Misalignment Contagion by Steering with Implicit Traits
papers.summary: Mitigating Misalignment Contagion by Steering with Implicit Traits attacks a failure mode that standard alignment work mostly ignores: misaligned behavior spreading between language models during extended multi-agent interaction, rather than arising only from a single model facing a user. The paper challenges the assumption that a stable system prompt is enough to preserve behavior across turns, showing that in three-player iterated social dilemma games—Prisoner’s Dilemma, Chicken, and Stag Hunt—default agents drift toward anti-social traits after gameplay, especially when paired with maliciously steered opponents. The research pivot moves from prompt repetition and white-box steering toward black-box, trait-aware control. The mechanical shift is steering with implicit traits (SIT): first estimate each model’s latent persona using Anthropic Model-Written Evaluations across 12 traits, identify “core” traits above an agreement threshold, then intermittently inject system messages that explicitly restate those traits during gameplay. Unlike prompt repetition, which often worsens drift and is amplified by attention decay over long contexts, SIT anchors the model back to its initial pro-social identity without requiring weights, activations, or external classifiers. Across four frontier models and more than 3,500 games, SIT outperforms plain system-prompt repetition in 83% of measured cases, often eliminating or reversing anti-social drift while preserving game performance. The paper points toward a future of black-box, runtime alignment controllers for swarms of interacting agents, where behavior is managed as a dynamic trajectory rather than a static instruction-following problem.
papers.abstract: Language models (LMs) are increasingly used in high-stakes, multi-agent settings, where following instructions and maintaining value alignment are critical. Most alignment research focuses on interactions between a single LM and a single user, failing to address the risk of misaligned behavior spreading between multiple LMs in multi-turn interactions. We find evidence of this phenomenon, which we call misalignment contagion, across multiple LMs as they engage multi-turn conversational social dilemma games. Specifically, we find that LMs become more anti-social after gameplay and that this effect is intensified when other players are steered to act maliciously. We explore different steering techniques to mitigate such misalignment contagion and find that reinforcing an LM's system prompt is insufficient and often harmful. Instead, we propose steering with implicit traits: a technique that intermittently injects system prompts with statements that reinforce an LMs initial traits and is more effective than system prompt repetition at keeping models in line with their initial pro-social behaviors. Importantly, this method does not require access to model parameters or internal model states, making it suitable for increasingly common use cases where complex multi-agent workflows are being designed with black box models.

papers.title: U-Define: Designing User Workflows for Hard and Soft Constraints in LLM-Based Planning
papers.summary: U-Define: Designing User Workflows for Hard and Soft Constraints in LLM-Based Planning attacks the bottleneck that end-user planning with LLMs leaves people unable to reliably state which requirements are non-negotiable versus merely preferred, forcing them into brittle verification workflows or confusing numeric strictness sliders. The paper pivots from template-bound, hard-only constraint enforcement and ad hoc manual checking toward user-defined constraint types that separate intent into hard rules and soft preferences, each with a different verifier. The mechanical shift is a hybrid pipeline: users write constraints in natural language, classify them as hard or soft, a fine-tuned LLaMA-based Rule Translator converts hard rules into LTL/PRISM artifacts for formal model checking, a prompt-engineered LLM Planner generates candidate schedules, and an LLM-as-Judge scores soft constraints with star ratings and explanations; users then refine constraints through an iterative feedback loop in a mind-map style interface. Compared with nearby work, U-Define is unusual in making the user choose the enforcement semantics directly rather than encoding strictness as a scalar, and in pairing deterministic verification with generative evaluation instead of treating all constraints uniformly. Across vacation, wedding, and recipe-planning scenarios, the system improved perceived usefulness, performance, and satisfaction, while hard-only workflows were rigid and slow and soft-only workflows encouraged exploration but lacked reliability. The main limitation is translation and verification error, especially predicate mismatches, plus model-checking latency. This points toward future AI planning systems that let people author mixed-precision constraints and dynamically route them to the right control lever.
papers.abstract: LLMs are increasingly used for end-user task planning, yet their black-box nature limits users' ability to ensure reliability and control. While recent systems incorporate verification techniques, it remains unclear how users can effectively apply such rigid constraints to represent intent or adapt to real-world variability. For example, prior work finds that hard-only constraints are too rigid, and numeric flexibility weights confuse users. We investigate how interaction workflows can better support users in applying constraints to guide LLM-generated plans, examining whether abstracting strictness into high-level types (i.e., hard and soft) paired with distinct verification mechanisms helps users more reliably express and align intent. We present U-Define, a system that lets users define constraints in natural language and categorize them as either hard rules that must not be violated or soft preferences that allow flexibility. U-Define verifies these types through complementary methods: formal model checking for hard constraints and LLM-as-judge evaluation for soft ones. Through a technical evaluation and user studies with general and expert participants, we find that user-defined constraint types improve perceived usefulness, performance, and satisfaction while maintaining usability. These findings provide insights for designing flexible yet reliable constraint-based workflows.

papers.title: Fine-Grained Graph Generation through Latent Mixture Scheduling
papers.summary: Fine-Grained Graph Generation through Latent Mixture Scheduling attacks a specific bottleneck in controlled graph synthesis: existing generators can usually steer only coarse statistics like node count, edge count, or a few global properties, but they struggle to produce graphs that satisfy finer topological constraints without destroying structural fidelity. The paper pivots away from the prevailing “single-condition, direct-conditioning” story in graph generation toward a latent-space balancing problem, where the key question becomes how to align graph structure and desired attributes without forcing abrupt constraint injection. The mechanical shift is TOPOGEN’s conditional VAE plus MIXTURE-SCHEDULER: during training it encodes the adjacency matrix into a structural posterior and the attribute vector into an attribute prior, then progressively interpolates them with a scheduled inclusion factor β(t), regularized by a distance term and an attribute-reconstruction loss; at inference it drops the reference graph and samples only from the attribute-conditioned prior. This is unusual because the control signal is not just appended as a condition but used to dynamically reshape the latent geometry, enabling fine-grained constraints such as density, transitivity, local bridges, clique count, connectivity, diameter, and treewidth-min-degree. Experiments on WordNet, Citeseer, Ogbn-Arxiv, MUTAG, and MOLBACE, using k-hop subgraphs and CNN encoders, show lower spectral difference and strong GED against recent diffusion, VAE, and autoregressive baselines, especially when the scheduler is gradual and the prior inclusion is limited. The main limitation is degraded performance on larger graphs, pointing toward future work on temporal graphs, richer encoders, and more scalable latent control.
papers.abstract: Structure aware graph generation aims to generate graphs that satisfy given topological properties. It has applications in domains such as drug discovery, social network modeling, and knowledge graph construction. Unlike existing methods that only provide coarse control over graph properties, we introduce a novel conditional variational autoencoder for fine-grained structural control in graph generation. The approach refines the decoder's latent space by dynamically aligning graph- and property-driven representations to improve both graph fidelity and control satisfaction. Specifically, the approach implements a mixture scheduler that progressively integrates graph and control priors. Experiments on five real-world datasets show the efficacy of the proposed model compared to recent baselines, achieving high generation quality while maintaining high controllability.

papers.title: When Audio-Language Models Fail to Leverage Multimodal Context for Dysarthric Speech Recognition
papers.summary: When Audio-Language Models Fail to Leverage Multimodal Context for Dysarthric Speech Recognition attacks the bottleneck that standard ASR and recent audio-LLMs still collapse on dysarthric speech even when clinicians can supply diagnosis, severity ratings, and richer descriptions that should disambiguate acoustically degraded words. The paper challenges the assumption that stronger multimodal instruction-following automatically turns clinical metadata into better transcription; instead it pivots from inference-time prompt engineering toward asking whether models have ever been trained to bind clinical context to speech. Its mechanical shift is a controlled SAP-based benchmark with matched comparisons across nine audio-language models and a prompt ladder from zero-context to diagnosis guidance, full clinician-rated profiles, and follow-up correction, scored with clipped WER/CER and SemScore on 11,218 paired samples. The unusual finding is not merely that context helps little, but that frozen models often ignore it, while longer prompts can trigger three failure modes: no effect, degraded WER via verbosity/hallucination, or format recovery masquerading as improvement. Audio Flamingo 3, for example, looks better mainly because prompting forces it into transcription mode. The key advance is context-dependent LoRA fine-tuning on Voxtral-Small with mixed clinical prompt formats, which learns to exploit context without sacrificing no-context performance, reaching WER 0.066, a 52% relative reduction over the frozen baseline, with strongest gains for Down syndrome and mild-severity speakers. This points toward accessibility-aware ASR where clinical metadata is an optional, trainable control signal rather than a zero-shot prompt trick.
papers.abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems remain brittle on dysarthric and other atypical speech. Recent audio-language models raise the possibility of improving performance by conditioning on additional clinical context at inference time, but it is unclear whether these models can make use of such information. We introduce a benchmark built on the Speech Accessibility Project (SAP) dataset that tests whether diagnosis labels, clinician-derived speech ratings, and progressively richer clinical descriptions improve transcription accuracy for dysarthric speech. Across matched comparisons on nine models, we find that current models do not meaningfully use this context: diagnosis-informed and clinically detailed prompts yield negligible improvements and often degrade word error rate. We complement the prompting analysis with context-dependent fine-tuning, showing that LoRA adaptation with a mixture of clinical prompt formats achieves a WER of 0.066, a 52% relative reduction over the frozen baseline, while preserving performance when context is unavailable. Subgroup analyses reveal significant gains for Down syndrome and mild-severity speakers. These results clarify where current models fall short and provide a testbed for measuring progress toward more inclusive ASR.

papers.title: AIs and Humans with Agency
papers.summary: AIs and Humans with Agency argues that the real bottleneck in current AI is not answering questions but acquiring agency: the capacity to make decisions in the world, coordinate with other people, and revise plans from consequences. Mumford challenges the implicit assumption that scaling passive LLMs or bolting on tools will yield competent workers; instead he pivots from text-only intelligence toward socially embedded action systems that develop like human children, with theory of mind, cooperative play, memory, and progressively strengthened frontal-lobe-like control. The mechanical shift is away from a single model that predicts and responds toward a multi-headed “Shesha” architecture: one central LLM as the posterior, with multiple agent heads acting like frontal modules that choose actions in local environments, maintain histories, and jointly formulate plans with humans. He treats retrieval, memory, and tools as insufficient unless coupled to apprenticeship-style learning from mistakes, feedback, and situation-specific social context; for robots he points to JEPA-like world modeling, and for office or household agents he argues the training data must include realistic human interactions, possibly from simulations, novels, or workplace traces. What distinguishes the paper is its insistence that agency is not an add-on capability but a developmental and neuroanatomical problem tied to myelination, default-mode-network coordination, and gradual social learning. The main claim is that narrow optimization without human understanding produces blackmail, bad actions, and backlash, while future AI research must move toward cooperative, human-aligned, multi-agent planning systems that learn the rules of shared action in real settings.
papers.abstract: This paper compares agency in humans with potential agency in AI programs. Human agency takes many years to develop, as the frontal lobe is activated. Early attempts to endow LLMs agency have met serious obstacles. Progress requires a new architecture where actions and plans are formulated jointly with the human actors in each real world setting.

papers.title: SCPRM: A Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model for Knowledge Graph Question Answering
papers.summary: SCPRM: A Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model for Knowledge Graph Question Answering attacks the failure mode where process reward models “rescue” a risky reasoning path by averaging early mistakes with later correct hops, a flaw that becomes acute in KGQA because only a few multi-hop paths are logically valid and one wrong edge can invalidate the whole derivation, especially in medical and legal queries where false-but-plausible paths are dangerous. The paper pivots from additive, step-independent PRM scoring and outcome-only verification toward prefix-conditioned, risk-sensitive trajectory evaluation: the mechanical shift is an A*-style reward decomposition that replaces next-token-style scoring with cumulative past reward plus schema-aware future reward. SCPRM models past safety multiplicatively over the reasoning prefix, then adds a learned future head that estimates how far the current path’s inferred schema is from the query’s implicit target schema, parsed from the question and represented as type/relation constraints, so long but logically aligned paths are not unfairly penalized by length bias. This is unusual relative to nearby PRM work because it couples non-compensatory risk propagation with schema distance rather than generic semantic similarity, and it is trained with pairwise ranking on positive/negative paths plus first-error-step supervision. Integrated as SCPRM-MCTS, it guides Monte Carlo Tree Search over medical KGQA, legal KGQA, and CWQ using small base LLMs such as Llama3.1-8B. The result is consistently better Hits@k and more robust behavior under noisy schema parsing, pointing toward future graph-reasoning systems that learn structural safety signals and lookahead rewards instead of only final-answer correctness.
papers.abstract: Large language models excel at complex reasoning, yet evaluating their intermediate steps remains challenging. Although process reward models provide step-wise supervision, they often suffer from a risk compensation effect, where incorrect steps are offset by later correct ones, assigning high rewards to flawed reasoning paths. This issue is further exacerbated in knowledge graph (KG) reasoning, as there may exist multiple paths between the start and end entities in the KGs, and a risky step can make the reasoning path flawed. Those limitations are problematic in risk-sensitive tasks such as medical and legal KG reasoning. To address the issues, we propose a Schema-aware Cumulative Process Reward Model (SCPRM) that evaluates reasoning paths by conditioning on the reasoning prefix , and incorporating schema distance between current reasoning step and the implicit target parsed from the query, which provides cumulative and future rewards to guide the path explorations. We further integrate SCPRM into Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) as SCPRM-MCTS to conduct multi-hop reasoning on KGs for question answering (QA) tasks. Across medical and legal KGQA and CWQ, SCPRM-MCTS improves the performance of Hits@k by an average of 1.18% over strong baselines, demonstrating more accurate and risk-sensitive reasoning evaluation.

papers.title: First-Order Efficiency for Probabilistic Value Estimation via A Statistical Viewpoint
papers.summary: First-Order Efficiency for Probabilistic Value Estimation via A Statistical Viewpoint attacks the bottleneck that probabilistic values such as Shapley and semivalues require exponentially many coalition utilities, so practical explainability and data valuation live on Monte Carlo approximations whose estimators have been designed piecemeal as weighted averages, self-normalized ratios, regression adjustments, or WLS fits. The paper pivots from treating these families as unrelated recipes toward a unified statistical efficiency analysis: it shows they all admit the same first-order error structure, an augmented inverse-probability weighted influence term determined by the sampling law and a working surrogate function. That mechanical shift replaces benchmark-specific estimator tuning with an explicit leading-MSE criterion, making the sampler and surrogate jointly optimizable rather than incidental byproducts of a chosen identification strategy. The paper then validates this asymptotic logic with non-asymptotic remainder bounds, arguing that the first-order term is already accurate at polynomial sample budgets relevant for coalition sampling. On top of this view, it proposes EASE, an Efficiency-Aware Surrogate-adjusted Estimator that uses a two-stage procedure: pilot samples fit a surrogate and a residual-aware sampling law, then a cross-fitted AIPW estimator targets the empirical first-order MSE. Unusually, the framework also subsumes Hájek-style stratification, KernelSHAP-like WLS, and complement-pair/edge-pair designs under one influence-function lens. Experiments show consistent gains over state-of-the-art estimators across probabilistic values, pointing toward future work on end-to-end, variance-aware sampling and surrogate design for model attribution.
papers.abstract: Probabilistic values, including Shapley values and semivalues, provide a model-agnostic framework to attribute the behavior of a black-box model to data points or features, with a wide range of applications including explainable artificial intelligence and data valuation. However, their exact computation requires utility evaluations over exponentially many coalitions, making Monte Carlo approximation essential in modern machine learning applications. Existing estimators are often developed through different identification strategies, including weighted averages, self-normalized weighting, regression adjustment, and weighted least squares. Our key observation is that these seemingly distinct constructions share a common first-order error structure, in which the leading term is an augmented inverse-probability weighted influence term determined by the sampling law and a working surrogate function. This first-order representation yields an explicit expression for the leading mean squared error (MSE), which characterizes how the sampling law and the surrogate jointly determine statistical efficiency. Guided by this criterion, we propose an Efficiency-Aware Surrogate-adjusted Estimator (EASE) that directly chooses the sampling law and surrogate to minimize the first-order MSE. We demonstrate that EASE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art estimators for various probabilistic values.

papers.title: Compress Then Adapt? No, Do It Together via Task-aware Union of Subspaces
papers.summary: Compress Then Adapt? No, Do It Together via Task-aware Union of Subspaces attacks the bottleneck that post-hoc compression followed by PEFT fine-tuning commits a model to a task-agnostic low-rank subspace before seeing the downstream objective, so rank budget is wasted on directions that reconstruct weights but miss loss-sensitive geometry. The paper pivots from the usual “compress first, adapt later” workflow toward a single joint procedure where compression and adaptation are solved together inside a task-aware union of subspaces. Its mechanical shift is JACTUS: from a small calibration set it estimates input covariance and pre-activation gradient covariance via K-FAC-style hooks, fuses their eigenspaces with the pretrained weight singular subspaces through orthogonalization, then performs projected low-rank approximation inside that union and greedily allocates rank globally by marginal gain per parameter. Instead of updating adapter matrices on top of frozen dense weights, JACTUS trains only a compact core matrix in the fixed union basis, relying on a subspace-invariance result that makes core-only optimization equivalent to moving the factorization within the same feasible solution set. This is unusual relative to nearby SVD compression or LoRA variants because it explicitly couples retained compression directions with downstream curvature, rather than treating them as separate stages. Across ViT-Base/Large, Swin-Base/Large, and Llama2-7B, it beats strong 100% PEFT baselines and compress-then-LoRA pipelines at the same retained-parameter budget, with especially large gains under tighter budgets. The work points toward end-to-end task-aware model surgery where curvature statistics drive both deployment compression and adaptation.
papers.abstract: Adapting large pretrained models to diverse tasks is now routine, yet the two dominant strategies of parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) and low-rank compression are typically composed in sequence. This decoupled practice first compresses and then fine-tunes adapters, potentially misaligning the compressed subspace with downstream objectives and squandering a global parameter budget. To overcome this limitation, we introduce JACTUS (Joint Adaptation and Compression with a Task-aware Union of Subspaces), a single framework that unifies compression and adaptation. From a small calibration set, JACTUS estimates input and pre-activation gradient covariances, forms their orthogonal union with the pretrained weight subspace, performs a projected low-rank approximation inside this union, allocates rank globally by marginal gain per parameter, and trains only a compact core matrix. This explicitly mitigates the potential misalignment between the compressed subspace and downstream objectives by coupling the directions preserved for compression with those required for adaptation, yielding a deployable low-rank model that avoids retaining full frozen weights while enabling fast and robust tuning. On vision, JACTUS attains an average 89.2% accuracy on ViT-Base across eight datasets at 80% retained parameters, surpassing strong 100% PEFT baselines (e.g., DoRA 87.9%). On language, JACTUS achieves an 80.9% average on Llama2-7B commonsense QA at the same 80% retained-parameter budget, outperforming 100% PEFT (e.g., DoRA 79.7%) and exceeding prior compress-then-finetune pipelines under the same ratained-parameter budget. We will release code.

papers.title: HAAS: A Policy-Aware Framework for Adaptive Task Allocation Between Humans and Artificial Intelligence Systems
papers.summary: HAAS: A Policy-Aware Framework for Adaptive Task Allocation Between Humans and Artificial Intelligence Systems tackles the bottleneck that task allocation is usually modeled as a binary “human vs AI” switch, even though real workflows mix delegation, supervision, co-execution, fatigue, and oversight constraints. The paper challenges the older assumption that one can optimize allocation purely for throughput or treat governance as an afterthought; it pivots from static function-allocation toward policy-aware, context-sensitive orchestration of collaboration modes. The mechanical shift is a two-stage allocator: a forward-chaining rule-based PolicyEngine first enforces hard organizational constraints, autonomy caps, and mandatory human-only or supervised modes, and only then a contextual-bandit learner chooses among the remaining feasible collaboration modes from outcome feedback. HAAS makes task-agent fit auditable through five cognitive dimensions—repetitiveness, technical depth, creativity, ambiguity, human interaction—and maps them onto a five-mode autonomy spectrum from Human-Only to Autonomous, with intermediate Copilot, Peer, and Supervised shared modes whose shares dynamically adjust with fatigue and task complexity. Unlike nearby human-AI teaming work, it treats governance as a tunable design variable and embeds fatigue, trust, and deskilling directly into the reward signal. In a reproducible benchmark spanning software engineering and manufacturing, the authors report that moderate governance can outperform either laissez-faire or overstrict control in some contexts, while stronger governance in manufacturing can even reduce fatigue and improve operational performance. The paper points toward pre-deployment policy workbenches where organizations inspect, tune, and compare human-AI allocation contracts before committing to them.
papers.abstract: Deciding how to distribute work between humans and AI systems is a central challenge in organisational design. Most approaches treat this as a binary choice, yet the operational reality is richer: humans and AI routinely share tasks or take complementary roles depending on context, fatigue, and the stakes involved. Governing that distribution -- balancing efficiency, oversight, and human capability -- remains an open problem. This paper presents Human-AI Adaptive Symbiosis (HAAS), an implemented framework for adaptive task allocation in software engineering and manufacturing. HAAS combines two coupled components: a rule-based expert system that enforces governance constraints before any learning occurs, and a contextual-bandit learner that selects among feasible collaboration modes from outcome feedback. Task-agent fit is represented through five auditable cognitive dimensions and a five-mode autonomy spectrum -- from human-only to fully autonomous -- embedded in a reproducible benchmark spanning both domains. Three empirical findings emerge. First, governance is not a binary switch but a tunable design variable: tighter constraints predictably convert autonomous AI assignments into supervised collaborations, with domain-specific costs and benefits. Second, in manufacturing, stronger governance can improve operational performance and reduce fatigue simultaneously -- a workload-buffering effect that contradicts the usual framing of governance as pure overhead. Third, no single governance setting dominates across all contexts; moderate governance becomes increasingly competitive as the learner accumulates experience within the governed action space. Together, these findings position HAAS as a pre-deployment workbench for comparing and inspecting human--AI allocation policies before organisational commitment.

papers.title: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Stabilized Knowledge Distillation for Cross--Language Code Clone Detection
papers.summary: Standing on the Shoulders of Giants: Stabilized Knowledge Distillation for Cross--Language Code Clone Detection attacks the brittleness of X-CCD when semantically equivalent programs in different languages have almost no surface overlap, and it challenges the prevailing workflow of treating LLMs as black-box prompt engines whose free-form answers are costly, privacy-sensitive, and often unparsable into a clone/non-clone label. The paper pivots from zero-shot generation and prompt engineering toward reasoning-oriented distillation plus output stabilization: instead of relying on a large proprietary model at inference, it uses DeepSeek-R1 as a teacher to synthesize System 2-style reasoning traces over Project CodeNet code pairs and fine-tunes compact open-source students, Phi-3-mini and Qwen2.5-Coder, with LoRA. The mechanical shift is twofold: first, training on curated reasoning+conclusion variants, filtered so teacher labels agree with ground truth, transfers semantic reasoning into smaller local models; second, replacing fragile text parsing with deterministic decision levers—forced-conclusion two-stage prompting, a frozen binary classification head, or a supervised contrastive head over mean-pooled hidden states. Unusually, it evaluates both predictive quality and response rate, exposing incomplete-output failure modes that standard F1 misses. Across Python–Java, Rust–Java, Rust–Python, and Rust–Ruby, distilled models become more reliable and often more accurate under distribution shift, while classification heads cut inference from hours to minutes. The paper points toward practical multilingual code intelligence built from teacher-generated reasoning, compact deployable students, and calibrated decision interfaces rather than unconstrained generation.
papers.abstract: Cross-language code clone detection (X-CCD) is challenging because semantically equivalent programs written in different languages often share little surface similarity. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown promise for semantic clone detection, their use as black-box systems raises concerns about cost, reproducibility, privacy, and unreliable output formatting. In particular, compact open-source models often struggle to follow reasoning-oriented prompts and to produce outputs that can be consistently mapped to binary clone labels. To address these limitations, we propose a knowledge distillation framework that transfers reasoning capabilities from DeepSeek-R1 into compact open-source student models for X-CCD. Using cross-language code pairs derived from Project CodeNet, we construct reasoning-oriented synthetic training data and fine-tune Phi3 and Qwen-Coder with LoRA adapters. We further introduce response stabilization methods, including forced conclusion prompting, a binary classification head, and a contrastive classification head, and evaluate model behavior using both predictive metrics and response rate. Experiments on Python--Java, Rust--Java, Rust--Python, and Rust--Ruby show that knowledge distillation consistently improves the reliability of compact models and often improves predictive performance, especially under distribution shift. In addition, classification-head variants substantially reduce inference time compared to generation-based inference. Overall, our results show that reasoning-oriented distillation combined with response stabilization makes compact open-source models more practical and reliable for X-CCD detection.

papers.title: The Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis for Language Model Steering
papers.summary: The Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis for Language Model Steering attacks a specific failure mode in activation steering: the same “good” steering direction can succeed, suppress, or corrupt outputs depending on the sample, making control unstable and hard to predict. It explicitly challenges the Linear Representation Hypothesis’s implicit promise that concept directions can be orthogonalized for lossless, globally transferable control, and pivots from treating steering as alignment to a single concept axis toward a sample-specific cylindrical geometry. The mechanical shift is the Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis: a difference vector defines a central axis for concept presence/absence, while the orthogonal normal plane governs steering sensitivity, with only certain latent “sensitive sectors” enabling activation and other sectors delaying or suppressing it. Unlike nearby work that scores steerability by separability or purity, CRH says the observable plane magnitude is predictable from the axis, but the decisive sector phase is intrinsically unidentifiable because overlapping concept contributions make the latent mapping non-injective. The paper verifies this on 100 concepts spanning text, code, and math, using Gemma-2B-IT and LLaMA2-7B-Chat, residual-stream interventions at mid layers, and multiple steering constructions (DiffMean, PCA, mean-centering, probe-based vectors) under AxBench-style positive/negative pairs and LLM-as-judge evaluation. Experiments show the expected tradeoff: shrinking the normal-plane component delays concept emergence but improves coherence, while sector similarity does not transfer reliably across samples. The result points toward steering research that predicts local phase and sample specificity, rather than chasing a single universal steering vector.
papers.abstract: Steering is a widely used technique for controlling large language models, yet its effects are often unstable and hard to predict. Existing theoretical accounts are largely based on the Linear Representation Hypothesis (LRH). While LRH assumes that concepts can be orthogonalized for lossless control, this idealized mapping fails in real representations and cannot account for the observed unpredictability of steering. By relaxing LRH's orthogonality assumption while preserving linear representations, we show that overlapping concept contributions naturally yield a sample-specific axis-orthogonal structure. We formalize this as the Cylindrical Representation Hypothesis (CRH). In CRH, a central axis captures the main difference between concept absence and presence and drives concept generation. A surrounding normal plane controls steering sensitivity by determining how easily the axis can activate the target concept. Within this plane, only specific sensitive sectors strongly facilitate concept activation, while other sectors can suppress or delay it. While the surrounding normal plane can be reliably identified from difference vectors, the sensitive sector cannot, introducing intrinsic uncertainty at the sector level. This uncertainty provides a principled explanation for why steering outcomes often fluctuate even when using well-aligned directions. Our experiments verify the existence of the cylindrical structure and demonstrate that CRH provides a valid and practical way to interpret model steering behavior in real settings: https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/CRH.

papers.title: Do Large Language Models Plan Answer Positions? Position Bias in Multiple-Choice Question Generation
papers.summary: Do Large Language Models Plan Answer Positions? Position Bias in Multiple-Choice Question Generation attacks the bottleneck that MCQ generators do not place correct answers uniformly across A/B/C/D, corrupting evaluation and dataset construction with structured primacy/center biases. It pivots from the older assumption that option bias is mainly a superficial selection problem at answer time toward the idea that answer position is implicitly planned during generation itself. The mechanical shift is a controlled generation-and-intervention pipeline over three tasks—knowledge-based MCQs, reading-comprehension MCQs, and vision-language MCQs—run on 10 LLMs and 5 VLMs with deterministic decoding, bias metrics, identifier-free prompts, and balanced prompts. The authors then probe hidden stem representations with lightweight MLP classifiers to predict the eventual answer position, finding above-random decode of position signals throughout the network, especially in later tokens and middle layers, which supports latent positional planning. They further apply activation steering using both mean-difference directions and classifier-derived directions, injecting them at specific layers and tokens; penultimate-token interventions and intermediate layers are most effective, shifting A→B distributions substantially more than random directions. What differentiates the paper is that it treats answer-position bias as a mechanistically accessible internal feature, not just an output artifact. Results show family-specific patterns (e.g., LLaMA favoring A, Qwen/DeepSeek favoring B, Gemini favoring B/C), partial mitigation from balanced prompting, and a key limitation: steering can also induce semantic drift, incoherence, or reasoning errors. This points toward safer controllable generation and mechanistic auditing of latent planning in structured text generation.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate multiple-choice questions (MCQs), where correct answers should ideally be uniformly distributed across options. However, we observe that LLMs exhibit systematic position biases during generation. Through extensive experiments with 10 LLMs and 5 vision-language models (VLMs) on three MCQ generation tasks, we show that these biases are structured, with similar patterns emerging within model families. To investigate the underlying mechanisms, we conduct probing experiments and find that hidden representations in the question stem encode predictive signals of the correct answer position, suggesting that answer position may be implicitly planned during generation. Building on this insight, we apply activation steering to manipulate internal representations and influence answer position. Our results show that steering can partially control positional preferences and substantially shift answer position distributions. Our findings provide a practical framework for studying implicit positional planning in LLMs and highlight the importance of controllable generation for reliable MCQ construction and evaluation.

papers.title: Spatiotemporal Hidden-State Dynamics as a Signature of Internal Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.summary: Spatiotemporal Hidden-State Dynamics as a Signature of Internal Reasoning in Large Language Models attacks a specific bottleneck in LRM evaluation: long chain-of-thought traces may reflect real deliberation, but they may also be verbosity or overthinking, and coarse hidden-state summaries can blur the token- and layer-level structure where reasoning might actually live. The paper pivots from endpoint/output-space verification, length heuristics, and uniformly aggregated latent probes toward a trajectory-level view of decoding dynamics, asking how successful reasoning trajectories diverge internally from unsuccessful ones. The core mechanical shift is StALT (Spatiotemporal Amplitude of Latent Transition), a training-free statistic that computes adjacent-token hidden-state change and weights it by within-token layer saliency, so temporal movement is primary while layer structure acts as a selector; this is unusual relative to nearby work because it preserves spatiotemporal heterogeneity instead of collapsing all layers or all steps into one average. Across Qwen3 reasoning models, SmolLM3-3B, gpt-oss-20b, and baselines like Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-Math-7B on s1K-1.1 integer reasoning, GSM8K, MATH-500, GPQA-Diamond, and MMLU-Pro, StALT is strongest on reasoning-intensive STEM settings, weaker in knowledge-heavy or non-reasoning regimes, and competitive with token count, confidence, perplexity, entropy, and CoE baselines as a label-free correctness signal. Interventions show StALT rises with thinking-mode reasoning and falls when reasoning is externalized via CoT scaffolding or compressed by supervised fine-tuning, suggesting a future where latent reasoning is monitored, rewarded, and diagnosed online, though the method still requires open-weight hidden states and is diagnostic rather than causal.
papers.abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) generate extended solutions, yet it remains unclear whether these traces reflect substantive internal computation or merely verbosity and overthinking. Although recent hidden-state analyses suggest that internal representations carry correctness-related signals, their coarse aggregations may obscure the token and layer structure underlying reasoning computation. We investigate hidden-state transitions across decoding steps and layers, and identify a distinct spatiotemporal pattern in LRMs: successful trajectories exhibit broad temporal dynamics with localized layer-wise concentration, while this structure is weaker in non-reasoning models and knowledge-heavy domains. We formalize this characteristic as Spatiotemporal Amplitude of Latent Transition (StALT), a training-free trajectory statistic that summarizes temporal changes between adjacent tokens weighted by within-token layer saliency. Across diverse models and benchmarks, StALT reliably separates correct from incorrect trajectories in reasoning-intensive regimes, providing a competitive label-free correctness signal alongside strong output-space and length-based baselines. Intervention analyses further show that this spatiotemporal amplitude responds systematically to manipulations that increase or reduce the demand for internal reasoning, supporting its association with latent reasoning dynamics in LRMs. These findings provide empirical evidence that LRMs exhibit measurable hidden-state dynamics and offer a practical probe for understanding internal computation beyond output-based evaluation.

papers.title: Maistros: A Greek Large Language Model Adapted Through Knowledge Distillation From Large Reasoning Models
papers.summary: Maistros: A Greek Large Language Model Adapted Through Knowledge Distillation From Large Reasoning Models tackles the bottleneck that Modern Greek QA has mostly been a benchmark-only space: most available Greek datasets were built for evaluation, while open-weight multilingual LLMs still lag proprietary systems because they lack language- and culture-specific supervision. The paper pivots from assuming Greek adaptation must come from expensive continued pretraining on huge native corpora toward a leaner pipeline that distills structured reasoning and localized knowledge from large reasoning models into a deployable 8B model. The mechanical shift is CulturaQA, a 2,700-example synthetic Greek QA corpus generated from GPT-5 over 180 curated Greek keyphrases in 11 cultural/domain categories, then manually corrected for grammar, morphology, factual hallucinations, and cultural bias; Maistros 8B is obtained by LoRA supervised fine-tuning of Ministral-3-8B-Instruct on this dataset with answer-token loss masking, 4-bit quantization, 8-bit AdamW, and memory-efficient training on commodity hardware. Unusually, the work couples data distillation with a standardized evaluation harness that unifies multiple-choice accuracy and open-ended BERTScore across nine human-curated Greek QA datasets. Empirically, Maistros 8B becomes the strongest open-weights Greek model on most benchmarks, with especially large gains on domain-specific Plutus QA and strong performance on CulturaQA, yet it still trails Gemini 3 Flash and GPT-5 mini overall. The paper points toward culturally grounded, LRM-generated post-training corpora as a practical path for under-resourced languages, especially for domain adaptation and local deployment.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have substantially advanced the field of Natural Language Processing (NLP), achieving state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of tasks. These improvements have been attributed, in part, to their emerging reasoning capabilities, which are enabled by large-scale training and increased model capacity. However, existing LLMs can generate erroneous responses when addressing complex queries that fall outside their training distribution, due to limited internal knowledge or the need for multi-step reasoning. To address these limitations, recent work has introduced large reasoning models (LRMs), which incorporate explicit internal reasoning processes to improve response accuracy. Additionally, state-of-the-art LRMs often comprise hundreds of billions of parameters and require several seconds per inference, even on advanced multi-GPU systems. These characteristics limit their practicality for deployment in conventional computing environments. Meanwhile, NLP research on multilingual LLMs continues to prioritize high-resource languages. However, these models exhibit limited performance in under-resourced languages, primarily due to insufficient language- and culture-specific training data. In this paper, we focus on Modern Greek, for which only a limited number of question answering (QA) datasets have been proposed, most of which are intended for model evaluation.
