To address this research gap in Greek QA, we make the following contributions: (i) CulturaQA, a high-quality LRM-generated and human-curated dataset, for Greek LLM training and evaluation; (ii) a memory-efficient LLM evaluation framework adaptable to diverse languages and QA tasks; (iii) Maistros 8B, a state-of-the-art open-weights Greek LLM developed via knowledge distillation and fine-tuning on CulturaQA; and (iv) a comprehensive evaluation of nine LLMs across nine human-curated Greek QA datasets.

papers.title: StressEval: Failure-Driven Dynamic Benchmarking for Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.summary: StressEval: Failure-Driven Dynamic Benchmarking for Knowledge-Intensive Reasoning in Large Language Models attacks the evaluation bottleneck where static knowledge-intensive benchmarks are contaminated, overfit, and no longer diagnostic, while many dynamic refreshers simply make tasks harder by drifting into unanswerable or uncontrolled adversarial tricks. The paper pivots from generating new benchmark items from scratch toward turning observed model failures into the substrate for synthesis, so the benchmark is built around explicit failure modes rather than leaderboard novelty. The mechanical shift is a three-stage pipeline: structured error analysis reconstructs a failed reasoning trace and compresses it into a semi-structured difficulty card with bottleneck step and root cause; dual-perspective instance synthesis then regenerates follow-up items either as knowledge stress, freezing the original context and re-querying a canonicalized “knowledge black box,” or as reasoning stress, creating a synthetic virtual world and a reasoning skeleton that inherits the same trap; finally, multi-criteria LLM gating keeps only grounded, unambiguous, difficulty-faithful instances. Unlike nearby dynamic-benchmark work, it separates missing-fact failures from reasoning breakdowns to avoid root-cause entanglement and preserve controllability. Seeding from HotpotQA, ComplexWebQ, and WTQ, it builds Dynamic OneEval and evaluates open and proprietary LLMs, finding substantially larger performance drops than on the original benchmarks while retaining explicit failure labels for more actionable iteration. The main limitation is that reasoning-stress faithfulness is still harder to preserve than knowledge-stress, pointing toward future generator-critic-verifier ensembles and continual failure mining for benchmark evolution.
papers.abstract: Static benchmarks for LLMs are increasingly compromised by contamination and overfitting especially on knowledge intensive reasoning tasks While recent dynamic benchmarks can alleviate staleness they often increase difficulty at the expense of answerability and controllability In this paper we propose StressEval a failure driven data synthesis framework that turns observed model failures into dynamic challenging and controllable test instances StressEval consists of three stages first it constructs a semi structured difficulty card that identifies the failed reasoning step and its root cause second it applies a dual perspective instance synthesis method that targets both knowledge gaps and reasoning breakdowns while preserving the underlying difficulty factors and third it applies a gating mechanism to retain only grounded unambiguous instances Seeding from multiple knowledge intensive reasoning datasets we employ StressEval to build Dynamic OneEval a focused suite of challenging dynamic benchmark Across several state of the art LLMs Dynamic OneEval yields substantially larger performance drops than the original benchmarks while retaining explicit difficulty factors enabling more actionable iteration

papers.title: Learn-to-learn on Arbitrary Textual Conditioning: A Hypernetwork-Driven Meta-Gated LLM
papers.summary: Learn-to-learn on Arbitrary Textual Conditioning: A Hypernetwork-Driven Meta-Gated LLM attacks the bottleneck that conventional LLMs adapt poorly to corpus heterogeneity and small condition shifts, while finetuning induces catastrophic forgetting and standard meta-learning on full weights is too complex to scale. It pivots away from prompt-only control, LoRA/adapter-style weight edits, and bi-level meta-updating toward condition-driven control of the model’s internal nonlinearity: the mechanical shift is to “reopen” the β parameter inside SwiGLU, turning each FFN into a β-SwiGLU whose activation slope is dynamically set by a lightweight hypernetwork. Rather than generating low-rank weights, the hypernetwork reads arbitrary textual conditions—task, domain, persona, style, sentiment, emotion—through the LLM’s own embedding layer, cross-attention compression, and layer embeddings, then emits per-layer β values; the backbone LLM stays frozen and only the meta-controller is trained with supervised cross-entropy plus an L2 penalty that keeps β near the original SiLU regime. This is unusual compared with nearby work because it meta-controls activation geometry instead of parameters, framing adaptation as neuromodulation-like gain control and information bottleneck compression. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, across Persona-Chat, AdaptSum, CrossFit/UnifiedQA, GYAFC, MIC, SST, and SNI-based zero-shot benchmarks, it claims better adaptation and generalization than prompting, SFT, LoRA, MetaICL, and Text-to-LoRA, including reasonable transfer to unseen tasks and condition types. The paper points toward a future where LLM adaptation is governed by small condition-to-dynamics controllers rather than ever larger task-specific rewiring.
papers.abstract: Conventional LLMs may suffer from corpus heterogeneity and subtle condition changes. While finetuning can create the catastrophe forgetting issue, application of meta-learning on LLMs is also limited due to its complexity and scalability. In this paper, we activate the meta-signal of $β$ within the SwiGLU blocks, resulting in a meta-gating mechanism that adaptively adjusts the nonlinearity of FFN. A hypernetwork is employed which dynamically produces $β$ on textual conditions, providing meta-controllability on LLMs. By testing on different condition types such as task, domain, persona, and style, our method outperforms finetuning and meta-learning baselines, and can generalize reasonably on unseen tasks, condition types, or instructions. Our code can be found in https://github.com/AaronJi/MeGan.

papers.title: Enhancing Judgment Document Generation via Agentic Legal Information Collection and Rubric-Guided Optimization
papers.summary: Enhancing Judgment Document Generation via Agentic Legal Information Collection and Rubric-Guided Optimization tackles the bottleneck that judgment drafting is not just text generation but a two-part legal inference problem: retrieving the exact statutes and precedents that anchor the major premise, then composing a document whose conviction, sentencing, citation, and reasoning chain are all legally defensible. It explicitly challenges the dominant workflow of standard RAG plus supervised fine-tuning, which assumes passive semantic retrieval and imitation learning are sufficient, despite failures in evidence recall, hallucinated statutory references, and stylistically fluent but logically invalid judgments. The pivot is from static retrieve-then-generate toward agentic planning and reward-aligned legal reasoning. Judge-R1’s core mechanical shift is a multi-route retrieval pipeline in which an LLM-driven agent decomposes verbose case facts into subqueries about constitutive elements and sentencing factors, retrieves from multiple sources, reranks and filters candidates, and fuses this agentic path with a dense retrieval backbone; generation is then warmed up with SFT and sharpened with GRPO using a rubric that separately scores legal correctness, structural professionalism, and reasoning quality. Unusual relative to nearby legal RAG work, the model optimizes retrieval and drafting jointly with explicit legal utility signals rather than generic answer quality. On the JuDGE benchmark of 2,505 Chinese fact-judgment pairs, using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B-Thinking, it improves recall and precision of legal evidence, raises citation accuracy, and yields more coherent reasoning/judgment sections. This points toward agentic, rubric-trained LLMs for high-stakes document synthesis in law and other regulated domains.
papers.abstract: Automating the drafting of judgment documents is pivotal to judicial efficiency, yet it remains challenging due to the dual requirements of comprehensive retrieval of legal information and rigorous logical reasoning. Existing approaches, typically relying on standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Supervised Fine-Tuning, often suffer from insufficient evidence recall, hallucinated statutory references, and logically flawed legal reasoning. To bridge this gap, we propose Judge-R1, a unified framework designed to enhance LLM-based judgment document generation by jointly improving legal information collection and judgment document generation. First, we introduce Agentic Legal Information Collection, which employs a dynamic planning agent to retrieve precise statutes and precedents from multiple sources. Second, we implement Rubric-Guided Optimization, a reinforcement learning phase utilizing Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a comprehensive legal reward function to enforce adherence to judicial standards and reasoning logic. Extensive experiments on the JuDGE benchmark demonstrate that Judge-R1 significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both legal accuracy and generation quality.

papers.title: Counting as a minimal probe of language model reliability
papers.summary: Counting as a minimal probe of language model reliability attacks the mismatch between fluent benchmark success and true procedural reliability: models that ace math, code, and document tasks may still be unable to preserve a simple rule-defined variable over many steps. The paper pivots from knowledge-dependent leaderboards and long-context claims toward a fully mechanical assay, Stable Counting Capacity, where a model counts repeated symbols until it fails. This strips away semantics, ambiguity, and tokenization artifacts, and replaces benchmark-style question answering with an adaptive randomized ladder that measures the exact length at which counting collapses. Across more than 100 model variants, capacity stays far below advertised context windows, and the failure mode is not graceful degradation but abrupt collapse into rounded guesses, blank outputs, or formatting artifacts. The mechanical shift is that counting is treated as a probe of bounded internal state trajectories: in open-weight models like Gemma 3 27B-it and Qwen 3.5 35B-A3B, a linearly readable count coordinate appears during success, then disappears at the boundary, with teacher-forced logit margins and activation patching showing that the model carries a finite set of count-like states rather than an abstract counter. Extra test-time compute and reasoning tokens do not reliably restore lost state. The result is a warning that benchmark gains can mask brittle procedure execution, and it points toward architectures with persistent variables, explicit memory, recurrence, and verifiable execution traces.
papers.abstract: Large language models perform strongly on benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, coding and document analysis, suggesting a broad ability to follow instructions. However, it remains unclear whether such success reflects general logical competence, repeated application of learned procedures, or pattern matching that mimics rule execution. We investigate this question by introducing Stable Counting Capacity, an assay in which models count repeated symbols until failure. The assay removes knowledge dependencies, semantics and ambiguity from evaluation, avoids lexical and tokenization confounds, and provides a direct measure of procedural reliability beyond standard knowledge-based benchmarks. Here we show, across more than 100 model variants, that stable counting capacity remains far below advertised context limits. Model behavior is consistent neither with open-ended logic nor with stable application of a learned rule, but instead with use of a finite set of count-like internal states, analogous to counting on fingers. Once this resource is exhausted, the appearance of rule following disappears and exact execution collapses into guessing, even with additional test-time compute. These findings show that fluent performance in current language models does not guarantee general, reliable rule following.

papers.title: A Multimodal Dataset for Visually Grounded Ambiguity in Machine Translation
papers.summary: A Multimodal Dataset for Visually Grounded Ambiguity in Machine Translation attacks a specific failure mode in MMT: models can translate fluent sentences while still missing the visually intended sense of an ambiguous span, and prior ambiguity benchmarks either contain text-solvable cases, VQA-style prompts, or metrics that do not match open-ended translation with paraphrase freedom. The paper pivots from evaluating “overall translation quality plus indirect visual benefit” toward span-level visual disambiguation as the primary object of study, replacing candidate-based or surface-overlap scoring with VIDA, a 2,500-instance English–Chinese dataset built through a three-stage curation funnel from 26,452 image-text pairs, dual-LLM ambiguity verification, GPT-4o disambiguated translation generation, and full human validation. Its mechanical shift is twofold: a Disambiguation-Centric Metric suite, where a Qwen3-8B LLM-as-judge binary classifier checks whether annotated ambiguous expressions are resolved correctly at the term and instance level, and a chain-of-thought SFT recipe that forces models to ground the image, identify ambiguity, refine only the ambiguous part, and re-check the output. Tested on Qwen2.5-VL-7B and InternVL3-8B under vanilla, SFT, and CoT-SFT, the work finds that standard SFT improves general translation metrics, but CoT-SFT more reliably improves disambiguation accuracy, especially on OOD VIDA-Sent and VIDA-CollN cases; it also reveals a tradeoff where extra reasoning can cause overthinking and surface-quality regressions. This suggests a future line of MMT research centered on reasoning-supervised visual grounding, tighter ambiguity-focused evaluation, and preference/RL-style optimization for robust disambiguation.
papers.abstract: Ambiguity resolution is a key challenge in multimodal machine translation (MMT), where models must genuinely leverage visual input to map an ambiguous expression to its intended meaning. Although prior work has proposed disambiguation-oriented benchmarks that provide supportive evidence for the role of vision, we observe substantial issues in data quality and a mismatch with translation scenarios. Moreover, existing ambiguity-oriented evaluations are not well suited to broader ambiguity types in open-ended translation. To address these limitations, we present VIDA (Visually-Dependent Ambiguity), a dataset of 2,500 carefully curated instances in which resolving an annotated ambiguous source span requires visual evidence. We further propose Disambiguation-Centric Metrics that use an LLM-as-a-judge classifier to verify whether annotated ambiguous expressions are resolved correctly at the span level. Experiments with two state-of-the-art Large Vision Language Models under vanilla inference, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and our chain-of-thought SFT (CoT-SFT) show that while SFT improves overall translation quality, CoT-SFT yields more consistent gains in disambiguation accuracy, especially on out-of-distribution subsets, indicating a stronger generalization for resolving diverse ambiguity types.

papers.title: What Single-Prompt Accuracy Misses: A Multi-Variant Reliability Audit of Language Models
papers.summary: What Single-Prompt Accuracy Misses: A Multi-Variant Reliability Audit of Language Models attacks the hidden failure mode that a single benchmark score can erase: reliability depends not just on model quality but on prompt template, calibration definition, and evaluator logic. The paper pivots away from the default workflow of ranking open-weight language models by one canonical prompt toward a pipeline-aware audit that evaluates 15 1–8B models, with main claims on 10 instruct models, across five classification/reasoning benchmarks, five prompt variants, and two verbal-confidence phrasings. The mechanical shift is a per-(model × dataset × variant) measurement grid that jointly records accuracy, token-probability calibration, verbal-confidence calibration, verbal parse rate, and prompt-perturbation spread, then contrasts raw vs label-set-normalized ECE and raw answer extraction vs repairable scoring. That design exposes two evaluator-side failures: label normalization changes ECE materially, and a chain-of-thought ARC-Challenge prompt scored by first-character matching collapses apparent accuracy by 72–88%, with regex re-parsing or constrained decoding recovering 93.8% and 102.7% of the lost performance, showing the model was not the bottleneck. It also shows confidence signals are fragile: on MMLU-Pro, every primary model is verbally overconfident relative to both accuracy and token confidence, and verbal parseability can fail on specific variants. Prompt robustness does not track size reliably, so the paper points toward reliability reporting as a deployment-readiness profile, not a single score.
papers.abstract: Single-prompt accuracy is the dominant way to benchmark language models, but it can miss reliability failures that matter. We evaluate a 15-model open-weight corpus, with the main reliability analyses focused on 10 instruct models across five classification and reasoning benchmarks under five prompt variants each, measuring accuracy, token-probability calibration, verbal-confidence calibration, verbal parse rate, and prompt-perturbation spread for every (model x dataset x variant) cell. We find three broad results. First, evaluation design can materially change the conclusion. Switching Expected Calibration Error (ECE) token from a raw to a label-set-normalised definition changes per-cell calibration by a mean absolute 0.149. More strikingly, pairing a chain-of-thought prompt with a first-character evaluator on ARC-Challenge reduces apparent accuracy by 72-88% across all five primary models; two independent repair procedures recover 93.8% and 102.7% of the lost performance, indicating an evaluator-side rather than model-side failure. Second, confidence signals are fragile. On MMLU-Pro, every primary model verbally reports confidence substantially above both its accuracy and its token-probability confidence on the same rows, and verbal parse rate can collapse for a single model on a single prompt variant. Third, prompt robustness does not track parameter count reliably. Across 10 instruct models, the correlation between model size and prompt-perturbation spread ranges from -0.244 to 0.474 across benchmarks. Taken together, these results show that reliability conclusions for small language models depend not only on the model being evaluated, but also on the evaluation pipeline used to measure it. We argue that calibration definitions, evaluator logic, verbal parseability, and prompt robustness should be reported explicitly when making reliability claims.

papers.title: Methods, Data, and Conceptual Change: Reflections from Two Quantitative Diachronic Case Studies
papers.summary: Methods, Data, and Conceptual Change: Reflections from Two Quantitative Diachronic Case Studies tackles a methodological bottleneck in historical linguistics: the assumption that semantic change is directly recoverable from lexical frequencies, independent of corpus structure. Rather than treating diachronic meaning as a property waiting to be extracted, the paper pivots from surface-level frequency counting toward method–dataset fit, showing that what “change” looks like depends on whether the corpus can support stable syntax, reliable lemmatization, or only aggregated lexical context. The first case study uses quad-based concept modelling on EEBO-TCP’s highly heterogeneous Early Modern English print archive (c.1470s–1690s, ~765M words), where spelling variation and uneven grammatical regularity make dependency analysis fragile; the mechanical shift is normalization, lemmatization, and four-lemma co-occurrence quads assembled into “constellations” around focal terms. The second uses SynFlow on a subset of the Royal Society Corpus 6.0.4 (1750–1799), re-parsed with Stanza despite OCR noise and residual preprocessing error; here the shift is from linear proximity to dependency slots (subject, object, adjectival modification, prepositional complementation), letting concepts such as air, water, and acid be tracked through changing syntactic profiles. The paper’s distinctive contribution is comparative: liberty/freedom diverge in discursive constellations, while air becomes chemically differentiated in relational usage. It argues that historical semantic analysis should move toward transparent, context-sensitive, corpus-aware modeling, where method choice is itself part of the explanation of conceptual change.
papers.abstract: This discussion paper reflects on how quantitative approaches to historical linguistics interact with dataset properties. Drawing on two worked examples, we examine English data using quad-based concept modelling of Early Modern English discourse in EEBO-TCP (c. 1470s-1690s; 765M words) alongside SynFlow analysis of scientific writing in Royal Society Corpus 6.0.4 (1750-1799; drawn from a 78.6M-token open corpus). Through parallel comparison, the paper explores how each approach operationalises concepts, the data assumptions they entail, and the diachronic interpretations they support. We argue that comparative methodological reflection clarifies the limits of purely lexical, frequency-based approaches and highlights how dataset structure shapes the kinds of semantic change that quantitative methods can reliably detect.

papers.title: Pair2Score: Pairwise-to-Absolute Transfer for LLM-Based Essay Scoring
papers.summary: Pair2Score: Pairwise-to-Absolute Transfer for LLM-Based Essay Scoring attacks a concrete mismatch in automated essay scoring: comparative judgments are often easier and more locally informative to learn from, but deployment needs calibrated absolute trait scores. The paper challenges the common workflow of training an LLM directly on rubric labels as if pairwise signal were either unnecessary or only useful as a joint auxiliary loss, and pivots from direct absolute regression toward staged relative-to-absolute transfer. The mechanical shift is a two-stage LLaMA-3.2-1B + LoRA pipeline: Stage 1 trains a directional Siamese ranker with a bias-free utility head on pairwise comparisons derived from absolute grammar, vocabulary, and syntax labels; Stage 2 then reuses that pairwise knowledge for absolute prediction via either warm-started adapters or embedding-fusion, with no prompting and lightweight task-specific heads. What is unusual is that the paper treats transfer configuration itself as the main variable, not merely the existence of pairwise supervision, and evaluates it under a co-rotated five-fold fold/seed protocol that exposes stochasticity rather than averaging it away. On the Feedback Prize ELLIPSE AES traits, the best transfer variant beats the absolute-only baseline on all three traits in mean QWK, but improvements are fragile: one-epoch pairwise training transfers more reliably than prolonged ranking, and stronger Stage 1 pairwise accuracy barely correlates with Stage 2 QWK. This suggests future work should focus on richer transfer operators, progressive distillation, and cross-dataset relative-to-absolute scoring where comparative supervision becomes an intermediate representation rather than an end in itself.
papers.abstract: Many scoring applications require absolute predictions, while pairwise comparisons can provide a simpler learning objective. We present Pair2Score, a two-stage learning framework that transfers pairwise comparisons into absolute scoring with parameter-efficient LLaMA adaptation. Stage 1 trains a directional Siamese ranker on pairwise comparisons derived from absolute trait labels; Stage 2 trains an absolute predictor using configurable transfer strategies (warm-start and embedding-fusion variants). We evaluate on rubric-aligned Automated Essay Scoring (AES) traits (grammar, vocabulary, syntax) under a five-fold protocol that co-rotates held-out fold and random seed. At the trait level, the best-performing transfer variant improves quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) over an absolute-only baseline for all three traits. However, not all transfer configurations help: a one-epoch pairwise stage transfers more reliably than extended pairwise training, and transfer configuration -- not just the inclusion of a pairwise stage -- determines whether downstream scoring benefits.

papers.title: Enhanced LLM Reasoning by Optimizing Reward Functions with Search-Driven Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Enhanced LLM Reasoning by Optimizing Reward Functions with Search-Driven Reinforcement Learning attacks a bottleneck that typical RL-for-reasoning pipelines leave untouched: even when the base model and optimizer are fixed, math-reasoning gains remain highly sensitive to the hand-designed reward function, and coarse outcome rewards can miss whether the model is actually reasoning or merely guessing. The paper pivots from the usual “optimize policy under a fixed reward” mindset toward “optimize the reward specification itself,” treating reward design as the primary search variable. The mechanical shift is a generate–validate–train–rank loop in which Kimi K2 proposes executable Python reward functions, a sandbox rejects unsafe or malformed code, each candidate is screened with 500-step GRPO on a frozen Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct + LoRA stack, and GSM8K F1 rankings are fed back into the next generation round. Over five rounds, 50 candidates are explored; the reward search is not just additive, because a random 5-reward control collapses while ranked feedback improves. The most distinctive rewards are structural/process rewards on the <thinking> block—counting reasoning steps, arithmetic traces, and numeric density—while three protected base rewards always enforce format and answer correctness. This yields a mean F1 rise from 0.596 to 0.632, a top individual reward at 0.787 F1, and a best ensemble at 0.795 F1 and 0.660 accuracy, beating a base-only GRPO baseline at 0.609 F1. The paper points toward automated, auditable reward discovery and learned reward composition for reasoning agents.
papers.abstract: Mathematical reasoning is a key benchmark for large language models. Reinforcement learning is a standard post-training mechanism for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models, yet performance remains sensitive to the design of the reward function that drives policy optimization. This paper introduces a search-driven framework that treats the reward specification itself as an object of optimization. The setting of interest is one in which the base model is held fixed and the reward specification is the primary remaining design lever. Candidate reward functions are generated by a frontier language model, validated automatically, screened through 500-step Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) training runs on a Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct base model with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), and ranked by F1 on the GSM8K test set. Ranked summaries from prior rounds are then fed back into the next round of generation. Over five rounds, the search produces 50 candidate rewards. The mean F1 rises from 0.596 in Round 1 to 0.632 in Round 5, and the top individual reward reaches F1 = 0.787. Seven ensemble configurations of top-ranked rewards are evaluated. The best ensemble achieves F1 = 0.795 (95% bootstrap CI [0.756, 0.832]) and accuracy 0.660 [0.635, 0.686], a 0.19 absolute F1 gain over a base-rewards-only GRPO baseline (F1 = 0.609). Pairwise McNemar tests with Bonferroni correction show all five-or-more-reward configurations are statistically indistinguishable at α = 0.05/21. A three-seed re-training of the best ensemble yields F1 of 0.785. A randomly drawn 5-reward control collapses to F1 = 0.047, which shows that the ranked-feedback loop, not the additive signal of having more rewards, drives the gain.

papers.title: EditPropBench: Measuring Factual Edit Propagation in Scientific Manuscripts
papers.summary: EditPropBench: Measuring Factual Edit Propagation in Scientific Manuscripts attacks the failure mode where a local factual revision in a paper leaves semantically dependent prose stale, e.g., changing a dataset from 215 to 80 documents while phrases like “medium-scale” or “a few hundred items” remain uncorrected. It challenges the implicit assumption behind many editing and knowledge-editing benchmarks that success can be judged by the direct target alone or by short ripple probes; the pivot is from local string replacement and QA-style ripple tests toward manuscript-level cascade propagation under sentence-level dependency supervision. The mechanical shift is a controlled synthetic benchmark with a fact graph per item, partitioning sentences into direct targets, required downstream updates, and protected unrelated text, then scoring edits with Edit-Ripple Adherence (ERA) plus collateral-damage checks across three editing protocols and adversarial probes. Unlike nearby document-editing work, it explicitly tests implicit and free-form qualitative qualifiers, where substitution rules fail and semantic checking is required; it also uses judge-assisted scoring to credit natural paraphrases that preserve the new fact without canonical alias overlap. On 122 paraphrased manuscripts, five LLM editors span hard-stratum ERA 0.148–0.705, yet even the best system misses about 30% of required cascades, and direct editing outperforms plan-then-edit on hard cells. The paper’s audit of recent cs.CL benchmark/dataset papers, where 37.2% contain fact-dependent qualitative claims, suggests a future direction of cascade-aware scientific writing tools that combine revision generation with explicit dependency tracking and post-edit consistency checking.
papers.abstract: Local factual edits in scientific manuscripts often create non-local revision obligations. If a dataset changes from 215 to 80 documents, claims such as 'medium-scale' or 'a few hundred items' may also become stale, even though they do not repeat the edited number. We introduce EditPropBench, a benchmark for measuring whether LLM editors propagate factual edits through dependent manuscript claims. Each item contains an ML/NLP-style synthetic manuscript, a targeted edit, and a controlled fact graph with sentence-level labels for direct targets, required downstream updates, and protected unrelated text. EditPropBench provides a controlled manuscript-level benchmark with sentence-level dependency supervision, three editing protocols, adversarial metric probes, stress-test variants, and a metric suite centered on Edit-Ripple Adherence (ERA). On the hard implicit/free-form stratum, five LLM editing systems span ERA 0.148--0.705; even the strongest misses roughly 30% of required cascade updates. A mixed-stratum stress test shows that LLMs retain a positive advantage over deterministic substitution baselines when easy substitution-solvable cases are included. Finally, an audit of recent arXiv cs.CL benchmark and dataset papers finds fact-dependent qualitative claims in 37.2% of papers. EditPropBench shows that current LLM editors can repair many implicit consequences of factual edits, but reliable scientific revision still requires cascade-aware checking.

papers.title: CLaC at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Response Clarity Detection in Political Discourse
papers.summary: CLaC at SemEval-2026 Task 6: Response Clarity Detection in Political Discourse attacks the brittle boundary between direct answers, vague hedges, and outright evasions in 3,993 U.S. presidential interview question-answer pairs, where both class imbalance and annotator disagreement make the Clear Reply/Ambivalent split the central failure mode. The paper implicitly challenges the usual assumption that more end-to-end fine-tuning or longer-context architectures should win on low-resource classification: instead, it pivots from parameter-heavy adaptation toward inference-time prompt design and label-hierarchy exploitation, while also testing whether partial encoder adaptation can rescue conventional models. The mechanical shift is concrete: eight encoders are optimized through a four-stage pipeline using weighted cross-entropy, dropout, head/truncation sweeps, and selective unfreezing of only the top 25% of layers, and the best LLMs are driven by FS27 few-shot prompting plus “enriched input” that concatenates the full interviewer turn, then further strengthened by subcategory-augmented label definitions and majority-vote ensembling. Unusually, multilingual encoders underperform alone but strengthen the final ensemble when combined with English-only models, and open-weight model size does not predict LLM quality. The result is that prompt-based LLM ensembles beat fine-tuned encoders on both tasks (80 macro-F1 on Task 1; 59 on Task 2), while Longformer’s extended window fails to help, suggesting future work should focus on hierarchy-aware prompting, ensemble diversity, and context selection rather than brute-force scaling.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we present our system for SemEval-2026 Task 6 (CLARITY) on response clarity and evasion detection in question-answer pairs from U.S. presidential interviews, comparing fine-tuned encoders with prompt-based LLMs. Our LLM ensemble achieves 80 macro-F1 on the 3-class Task 1 (9th/41) and 59 on the 9-class Task 2 (3rd/33). Across 8 transformer encoders optimized through a four-stage pipeline, partial encoder layer unfreezing outperforms full fine-tuning by a wide margin. Combining English and multilingual encoders further improves ensemble performance over either family alone, despite multilingual models being individually weaker. Prompt-based LLMs, without any task-specific parameter updates, outperform fine-tuned encoders, particularly on minority classes; among open-weight LLMs, parameter count does not predict performance. Enriched input, concatenating the full interviewer turn, improves LLM performance but not that of encoders, an effect that persists with Longformer's extended context window, suggesting the divergence is not attributable to sequence-length capacity alone in our settings. The Clear Reply/Ambivalent boundary remains the dominant failure mode, mirroring the disagreement among human annotators. Our code, prompts, model configurations, and results are publicly available.

papers.title: ARGUS: Policy-Adaptive Ad Governance via Evolving Reinforcement with Adversarial Umpiring
papers.summary: ARGUS: Policy-Adaptive Ad Governance via Evolving Reinforcement with Adversarial Umpiring attacks a production bottleneck in multimodal ad moderation: regulatory policies change faster than annotation pipelines, so historical labels become stale, new mandates create contradictory supervision, and “gray-area” ads evade binary classifiers. It challenges the old assumption that governance can be handled by static fine-tuning, replay, or continual-learning regularizers on a fixed label space; instead it pivots from memorizing past policy boundaries toward continuously evolving policy reasoning. The mechanical shift is a three-stage GRPO loop: Stage I Policy Seeding blends sparse gold samples for new rules with a curated slice of historical data to establish initial policy sense; Stage II Adversarial Label Rectification replaces single-label supervision with a Prosecutor-Defender-Umpire debate, where a prosecutor argues violation, a defender argues compliance, and a RAG-grounded umpire adjudicates using retrieved policy clauses and reference cases to overwrite stale labels with rectified verdicts plus CoT rewards; Stage III Latent Knowledge Discovery mines high-uncertainty “latent noncompliant” samples, adds a Skeptic agent, and uses tripartite dialectical triangulation to surface deceptive violations hidden in compliant-labeled history. Compared with nearby ad-governance work, ARGUS is unusual in treating policy drift as a reward-design problem rather than a reannotation problem. On a 680k industrial image-text dataset and ToxiCN MM recast as emerging-policy adaptation, it preserves historical performance while improving emerging-policy precision/recall, and in online A/B testing lowers violation leakage while raising audit automation. This points toward policy-evolving moderation systems that learn from debate, retrieval, and dynamic rewards rather than static labels.
papers.abstract: Online advertising governance faces significant challenges due to the non-stationary nature of regulatory policies, where emerging mandates (e.g., restrictions on education or aesthetic anxiety) create severe label inconsistencies and reasoning ambiguities in historical datasets. In this paper, we propose ARGUS, a policy-adaptive governance system that enables evolving reinforcement through multi-agent adversarial umpiring. ARGUS addresses the sparsity of new policy data by employing a three-stage framework: (1) Policy Seeding for initial perception; (2) Adversarial Label Rectification, which utilizes a ``Prosecutor-Defender-Umpire'' architecture to resolve conflicts between stale labels and new mandates; and (3) Latent Knowledge Discovery, which employs a tripartite dialectical discussion to unearth sophisticated, ``gray-area'' violations. By leveraging RAG-enhanced policy knowledge and Chain-of-Thought synthesis as dynamic rewards for reinforcement learning, ARGUS synchronizes its reasoning pathways with evolving regulations. Extensive experiments on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that ARGUS significantly outperforms traditional fine-tuning baselines, achieving superior policy-adaptive learning with minimal gold data.

papers.title: An Information-theoretic Propagation Denoising and Fusion Framework for Fake News Detection
papers.summary: An Information-theoretic Propagation Denoising and Fusion Framework for Fake News Detection attacks the bottleneck that propagation-based fake news detectors break down when comment trees are incomplete and when LLM-generated “filled-in” interactions are noisy, hallucinated, or attribute-inconsistent. It implicitly challenges the recent workflow of naively role-playing missing users and directly fusing all synthetic propagation as if it were equally trustworthy; the pivot is from “generate more interaction evidence” toward “estimate which generated evidence is actually usable.” The mechanical shift is InfoPDF: it first constructs attribute-specific synthetic propagation graphs with frozen LLMs, then encodes real and synthetic views with Siamese GNN branches, models each fused attribute view as a Gaussian latent distribution, and converts posterior uncertainty into a reliability score that drives adaptive weighting before multi-head self-attention fusion. Training replaces simple supervision with an information-theoretic objective that jointly compresses away synthetic noise, aligns real and synthetic propagation representations, and preserves task sufficiency for both fake news detection and user-attribute prediction. This is unusual because reliability is not a static prior but an instance-wise latent quantity, and because the framework includes an LLM-free inference mode that reconstructs synthetic view embeddings from real propagation for deployment. On Twitter, CED, and PHEME, across GCN/GAT/GraphSAGE backbones, InfoPDF improves general, noisy, and early-detection settings and yields more discriminative clustering, suggesting a future where social misinformation systems rely on uncertainty-aware, attribute-conditioned propagation simulation rather than unfiltered generation.
papers.abstract: Incomplete propagation data significantly hinders robust fake news detection. Recent approaches leverage large language models to simulate missing user interactions via role-playing, thereby enriching propagation with synthetic signals. However, such propagation data is intrinsically unreliable, and directly fusing it can lead to biased representations, leading to limited detection performance. In this paper, we alleviate the unreliability of synthetic propagation from the mutual information perspective and propose a novel information-theoretic propagation denoising and fusion (InfoPDF) framework to learn effective representations from both real and synthetic propagation. Specifically, we first generate attribute-specific synthetic propagation using large language models. Then we model each synthetic propagation graph as a probabilistic latent distribution to guide reliability-aware adaptive fusion with real propagation. During training, we design a mutual information-based objective to learn compressed and task-sufficient propagation representations. It jointly suppresses noisy signals across attribute-specific synthetic propagation, maintains consistency between real and synthetic propagation representations, and ensures task sufficiency for fake news detection and attribute prediction. Experiments on three real-world datasets show that InfoPDF consistently achieves superior performance across various fake news detection tasks. Further analysis demonstrates that InfoPDF can estimate attribute-level reliabilities and learn more discriminative propagation representations.

papers.title: Reliability-Oriented Multilingual Orthopedic Diagnosis: A Domain-Adaptive Modeling and a Conceptual Validation Framework
papers.summary: Reliability-Oriented Multilingual Orthopedic Diagnosis: A Domain-Adaptive Modeling and a Conceptual Validation Framework attacks the failure mode that fluent multilingual LLMs can still be unsafe for structured clinical labeling: in English, Hindi, and Punjabi orthopedic notes, zero-shot instruction-tuned models produce unstable calibration, language-conditional variance, and weak label grounding, especially in low-resource Hindi/Punjabi. The paper challenges the common assumption that generative fluency or sheer model scale is enough for clinical decision support, and pivots from benchmark-style accuracy chasing toward reliability-oriented, safety-critical classification. The mechanical shift is twofold: first, it replaces task-only fine-tuning with IndicBERT-HPA, a domain-adaptive encoder that routes shared IndicBERT representations through language-specific orthopedic adapter heads and a bottleneck projection into a clinically discriminative subspace; second, it adds a conceptual deterministic agent-based validation layer that separates prediction from authorization via evidence-consistency checks, language-risk screening, confidence thresholds, and conservative human-in-the-loop deferral, including an Unknown label as an explicit safety exit. On a curated multilingual corpus of roughly 18,000 balanced notes for controlled evaluation and about 45,000 natural notes per language, across six diagnostic categories, IndicBERT-HPA achieves the strongest cross-lingual F1/AUROC/AUPRC and more predictable deployment behavior than XLM-RoBERTa, DistilBERT, or mDeBERTa, while zero-shot LLMs remain unreliable. The main implication is that reliable clinical AI will move toward specialized multilingual architectures plus structured validation governance, not just larger promptable models.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed for clinical decision support including multilingual diagnosis in low-resource settings. However, their reliability, calibration and safety characteristics remain insufficiently understood for structured, high-risk tasks. We present a system-level analysis of multilingual orthopedic diagnosis from free-text clinical notes in English, Hindi and Punjabi. We evaluate three modeling regimes: (i) task-aligned multilingual transformer encoders, (ii) a task-fine-tuned baseline (DistilBERT), and (iii) a domain-adaptive architecture tailored to orthopedic text (IndicBERT-HPA). These models are compared with zero-shot, instruction-tuned LLMs to assess suitability for structured diagnostic classification. Results indicate that while LLMs exhibit strong linguistic fluency, they show unstable calibration and reduced reliability under structured multilingual conditions, particularly in low-resource languages. These findings are specific to zero-shot evaluation and do not imply limitations of fine-tuned models. Domain-adaptive specialization substantially improves cross-lingual discrimination and confidence behavior. IndicBERT-HPA, with language-specific orthopedic adapter heads achieves consistently strong performance across six diagnostic categories and more predictable deployment characteristics than task-only adaptation. Building on these observations, we outline a conceptual deterministic agent-based validation framework for future implementation, formalizing evidence checks, language-sensitive validation and conservative human-in-the-loop gating. Reliable multilingual clinical decision support requires specialized architecture, explicit reliability analysis, and structured validation for safety-critical systems.

papers.title: A Systematic Benchmark of Machine Transliteration Models for the Tajik-Farsi Language Pair: A Comparative Study from Rule-Based to Transformer Architectures
papers.summary: A Systematic Benchmark of Machine Transliteration Models for the Tajik-Farsi Language Pair: A Comparative Study from Rule-Based to Transformer Architectures attacks a practical but under-served failure mode in Central Asian NLP: converting between Tajik Cyrillic and Persian Arabic script when the target alphabet omits short vowels and the source/target differ in grapheme granularity, making naive multilingual Seq2Seq transliteration brittle. The paper pivots from the old assumption that subword-tokenized pretrained models should transfer cleanly to low-resource transliteration toward a script-aware benchmark centered on character- and byte-level mechanisms. Its mechanical shift is the construction of a 328,253-pair multi-domain parallel corpus from crowdsourced lists, lexicographic pairs, Shahnameh and Masnavi texts, official terminology, news, and correspondences, then a stratified 40,000-pair sample used to compare a rule-based 70/47 substitution baseline, LSTM+attention, a compact char Transformer, a new from-scratch G2P Transformer with label smoothing and greedy decoding, mBART and mT5 with LoRA, and byte-level ByT5. What differentiates it from nearby work is the controlled, reproducible benchmark across six model families and both transliteration directions, explicitly testing tokenization granularity as the central variable. ByT5 is the clear winner at 87.4 chrF++ Tajik→Farsi and 80.1 reverse, while the lightweight G2P Transformer beats mBART despite far less data, and mT5 collapses, showing subword mismatch. The result points toward byte/character-first transliteration systems, plus external benchmarks and rare-name handling for deployment in low-resource, cross-script search and text access.
papers.abstract: This paper presents the first comprehensive comparative analysis of modern machine learning architectures for transliteration between Tajik (Cyrillic script) and Persian (Arabic script). A key contribution is the creation and validation of a unique parallel corpus aggregated from multiple heterogeneous sources, including crowdsourced projects, lexicographic pairs, parallel texts of "Shahnameh", diplomatic articles, texts of "Masnavi-i Ma'navi", official terminology lists, and transliterated correspondences. The initial dataset comprised 328,253 sentence pairs; a representative subset of 40,000 pairs was formed using stratified random sampling. The experiment compared six classes of models: rule-based baseline, LSTM with attention, character-level Transformer, G2P Transformer (trained from scratch), pre-trained multilingual models (mBART, mT5 with LoRA), and byte-level ByT5. Results demonstrate the overwhelming superiority of ByT5 (chrF++ 87.4 for Tajik to Farsi, 80.1 for reverse). The G2P Transformer significantly outperformed mBART (72.3 vs. 62.2 chrF++) despite limited data. Models using subword tokenization (mT5) failed completely (chrF++ less than 18.5). The findings demonstrate that for accurate transliteration of the Tajik-Farsi pair, architectures operating at the byte or character level are unequivocally more effective than traditional multilingual Seq2Seq models relying on subword tokenization.

papers.title: Compositional Multi-hop Factual Error Correction via Decomposition-and-Injection
papers.summary: Compositional Multi-hop Factual Error Correction via Decomposition-and-Injection attacks the bottleneck that single-span factual error correction breaks down on multi-hop claims, where the error is not a local token swap but a compositional inconsistency distributed across several evidence-linked reasoning steps, and where paired correction data are scarce. The paper explicitly challenges the old atomic-fact assumption behind mask-then-correct and mask-then-corrupt workflows, which assume claims can be fixed by local masking or independent span corruption. It pivots from claim-level editing toward reasoning-structure editing: CECoR decomposes a verified multi-hop claim into an interpretable program with QUESTION, VERIFY, and PREDICT steps, injects a controlled factual perturbation into only one step at a time, then recomposes the corrupted program into a fluent incorrect claim. That decomposition-and-injection loop is the core mechanical shift, because it synthesizes non-local, coupled errors such as coordinated entity substitutions or relational/temporal violations while preserving coherence, and a filtering stage keeps only fluent, truly multi-hop, evidence-contradicting pairs. Training then combines supervised fine-tuning on these pseudo-parallel pairs with reinforcement learning on naturally incorrect claims to align outputs with factuality, similarity, and fluency. Evaluated on HOVER, FEVEROUS, FECDATA, and noisy BM25-retrieved evidence, CECoR beats distantly supervised baselines and few-shot LLM prompting, generalizes to single-hop correction, and exposes how surface overlap metrics can misread unchanged outputs. This points toward future FEC systems built around program-level decomposition, controllable synthetic supervision, and evidence-grounded editing loops for compositional reasoning.
papers.abstract: Factual Error Correction (FEC) aims to revise inaccurate text into statements that are factually consistent with external evidence. Although recent methods perform well on single-hop correction, they often treat claims as atomic units and struggle with multi-hop cases that require compositional reasoning across multiple evidence sources. This challenge is further amplified by limited paired data and difficulties in locating semantic errors within complex reasoning chains. We present CECoR (Compositional Error Correction via Reasoning-aware Synthesis), a reasoning-aware framework that introduces a Decomposition and Injection paradigm for compositional error correction. CECoR decomposes multi-hop claims into interpretable reasoning steps and injects controlled perturbations to synthesize high-quality training pairs. A two-stage learning strategy combining supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning improves factual accuracy and robustness. Comprehensive evaluations show that CECoR achieves strong performance on multi-hop benchmarks, outperforming both distantly supervised methods and few-shot LLM baselines. It also generalizes effectively to single-hop correction and remains stable under noisy evidence, demonstrating its versatility for real-world factual correction.

papers.title: Structural Dilemmas and Developmental Pathways of Legal Argument Mining in the Era of Artificial Intelligence
papers.summary: Structural Dilemmas and Developmental Pathways of Legal Argument Mining in the Era of Artificial Intelligence argues that the field’s main bottleneck is not simply too little data or insufficient model accuracy, but the absence of a structured representational layer that can reconcile legal theory’s expressive richness with computational tractability. The paper surveys how the field has moved from rule-based legal summarization and feature-engineered classifiers to deep learning and LLMs, yet still fails on fine-grained legal reasoning because current workflows flatten argumentation into sentence- or premise-conclusion labels, leaving support, attack, exception, and nested relations under-modeled. Its pivot is from isolated task optimization and generic NLP scaling toward a representation-centered research program. The mechanical shift is to build an extensible intermediate structure over propositions rather than sentences, with standardized annotation guidelines, reusable corpora, and explicit relation types such as support, attack, combination, match, and identity; this structure is intended to serve both human annotation and machine learning. Unlike nearby work that treats LLMs as stand-alone extractors, it turns toward a hybrid legal-AI stack: domain-pretrained or fine-tuned LLMs plus retrieval-augmented generation and structured legal knowledge graphs, especially for statutes, case law, and doctrinal constraints. The paper also emphasizes multi-agent collaboration among legal experts, computational scientists, and machines, and ties evaluation to standardized benchmarks. Its main claim is that cumulative progress in legal argument mining will come from coordinating data, theory, and models around shared structure, pointing toward computable legal reasoning systems rather than ad hoc classifiers.
papers.abstract: Against the backdrop of rapid advances in artificial intelligence, legal argument mining has emerged as an important research area linking legal texts with intelligent analysis, carrying significant theoretical and practical implications. Existing studies have primarily developed along three dimensions: data, technology, and theory. At the data level, raw legal texts and annotated corpora constitute the foundational resources. At the technological level, research paradigms have evolved from rule-based systems and traditional machine learning to large language models (LLMs). At the theoretical level, argumentation theory and legal dogmatics provide important references for modeling argumentation structures. However, despite ongoing progress, the overall development of legal argument mining remains relatively slow. Building on a systematic review of existing research, this study conducts an in-depth analysis and finds that this is due not only to data scarcity or technical limitations, but more fundamentally to the lack of a structured representational approach that reconciles theoretical expressiveness with computational feasibility. Specifically, this challenge manifests in dilemmas in data standardization, obstacles to effective modeling, and limitations in domain adaptation. In response, the study proposes several key directions for future research. It aims to provide a reframing of key problems and a pathway for future development in legal argument mining, while leaving specific models and implementation schemes for further investigation.

papers.title: Decoding-Time Debiasing via Process Reward Models: From Controlled Fill-in to Open-Ended Generation
papers.summary: Decoding-Time Debiasing via Process Reward Models: From Controlled Fill-in to Open-Ended Generation attacks the practical failure mode where LLMs reproduce social stereotypes in downstream use, while standard fixes such as RLHF, DPO, or curated retraining are too expensive, require weight access, and can blunt unrelated capabilities. The paper pivots from changing model parameters toward treating fairness as a black-box decoding problem: instead of retraining, it searches over candidate tokens at inference time and uses a separate Process Reward Model to score each candidate for both bias and fluency. The mechanical shift is stepwise, not global: a PRM judges single tokens, then three increasingly structured control loops use that judgment—Best-of-N selection, Sequential critique-and-revise, and Constitutional self-audit—so the generator is steered by token-level fairness feedback rather than prompt-only persuasion. This is unusual because it imports PRM-style process supervision from math reasoning into social bias mitigation, and it extends beyond template fill-in to open-ended generation by debiasing each word on the fly, gated by a lightweight Bias Guard that only triggers on potentially biased words. Evaluated on GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 3 4B, and Qwen 2.5 3B over a 200-prompt English/Urdu benchmark spanning eight bias categories, Sequential gives the strongest and most consistent gains, raising mean bias by up to +0.40 while preserving fluency; Best-of-N often collapses on smaller models, revealing dependence on candidate diversity, and Urdu remains harder. The paper points toward black-box, token-level control systems where fairness, cost, and capability are jointly optimized at decoding time.
papers.abstract: Large language models pick up social biases from the data they are trained on and carry those biases into downstream applications, often reinforcing stereotypes around gender, race, religion, disability, age, and socioeconomic status. The standard fixes (retraining on curated data or fine-tuning with human feedback) are expensive, need access to model weights, and risk degrading the model on other tasks. In this paper we take a different route: we debias the model at decoding time, treating bias mitigation as a structured search over candidate tokens without ever touching model weights. A separate Process Reward Model (PRM) acts as a judge, scoring each candidate for both fairness and fluency. We design three schemes of increasing sophistication (Best-of-N selection, Sequential critique-and-revise, and Constitutional self-audit) and evaluate them on four models (GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3.2 3B, Gemma 3 4B, Qwen 2.5 3B) across a 200-prompt bilingual benchmark in English and Urdu covering eight bias categories. Sequential debiasing proves the most effective, raising mean bias scores by up to +0.40 over baseline while preserving (and sometimes improving) fluency. We then extend all three schemes to open-ended generation, where each token is debiased on the fly, and introduce a lightweight Bias Guard gate that fires only on potentially biased words, keeping overhead near 2x for well-calibrated models. A formal overhead metric that separates generator cost from judge cost reveals that Best-of-N is effectively free on the generator side in a native implementation. GPT-4o-mini, included as a strong proprietary anchor, confirms that the framework scales with model capability; the three open-weight models show where current small-scale LLMs still struggle.

papers.title: MolViBench: Evaluating LLMs on Molecular Vibe Coding
papers.summary: MolViBench: Evaluating LLMs on Molecular Vibe Coding targets a blind spot at the intersection of code synthesis and cheminformatics: current LLMs may write runnable Python yet still mishandle RDKit semantics, stereochemistry, or workflow ordering, and existing evaluations split along the wrong axis—general code benchmarks ignore chemistry while chemistry benchmarks mostly test recall or property prediction, not executable programs. The paper pivots from asking whether models “know molecules” or “can code” toward whether they can synthesize chemically correct, executable molecular workflows under natural-language intent, i.e., molecular vibe coding. The core mechanical shift is a benchmark/evaluation stack built around 358 curated tasks spanning 12 drug-discovery workflows and five Bloom-style cognitive levels, from single-API recall to end-to-end virtual screening pipeline design, all constrained to RDKit-centric Python functions with gold implementations. Instead of exact-string scoring, MolViBench uses a layered protocol: compile/execution checks, type-aware exactness over heterogeneous outputs, and an AST-based API-semantic fallback that credits correct RDKit workflows when outputs are structurally incomparable or legitimately բազմivalent. Unusually, it also compares three deployment paradigms—direct generation, incremental repair from traceback feedback, and coder-tester agent collaboration—showing that localized repair is more robust than wholesale rewriting. Across nine frontier coding LLMs, Claude Opus 4.6 Thinking with incremental repair leads at 39.7% Pass@1, yet all models collapse below 10% on Level 5, implying that long-horizon molecular pipeline orchestration remains the bottleneck. This points toward chemically grounded agentic coding systems with execution feedback, semantic validation, and workflow-aware repair loops.
papers.abstract: Molecular Vibe Coding, a paradigm where chemists interact with LLMs to generate executable programs for molecular tasks, has emerged as a flexible alternative to chemical agents with predefined tools, enabling chemists to express arbitrarily complex, customized workflows. Unlike general coding tasks, molecular coding imposes a distinctive challenge that LLMs should jointly equip programming, molecular understanding, and domain-specific reasoning capabilities. However, existing benchmarks remain disconnected. General code generation benchmarks such as HumanEval and SWE-bench require no chemistry knowledge, while chemistry-focused benchmarks such as S^2-Bench and ChemCoTBench evaluate knowledge recall or property prediction rather than executable code generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce MolViBench, the first benchmark tailored for Molecular Vibe Coding. MolViBench comprises 358 curated tasks across five cognitive levels, ranging from single-API recall to end-to-end virtual screening pipeline design, spanning 12 real-world drug discovery workflows. To rigorously assess generated code, we also propose a multi-layered evaluation framework that combines type-aware output comparison and AST-based API-semantic fallback analysis, which jointly measures executability and chemical correctness. We systematically evaluate 9 frontier coding LLMs and compare three real-world Molecular Vibe Coding paradigms, providing a practical and fine-grained testbed for diagnosing LLMs' coding capabilities in AI-accelerated molecular discovery.

papers.title: When Correct Isn't Usable: Improving Structured Output Reliability in Small Language Models
papers.summary: When Correct Isn't Usable: Improving Structured Output Reliability in Small Language Models attacks a deployment failure mode that standard task benchmarks hide: 7–9B models can solve GSM8K or MATH but still fail to emit parsable JSON with required fields, making “correct” answers unusable downstream. The paper pivots from treating the problem as pure reasoning quality toward structured communication under a hard output contract, challenging the implicit assumption that a good system prompt or grammar-based constrained decoding is enough. Its mechanical shift is to optimize the system prompt itself via AloLab, a black-box iterative loop that queries the target model, deterministically scores both mathematical correctness and JSON validity, then uses a stronger meta-agent (Claude Sonnet 4.5) to rewrite the prompt from observed failure traces and preserve success patterns. This is unusual because it requires no fine-tuning, gradients, demonstrations, or model access, yet directly targets model-specific format pathologies such as markdown-fence wrapping or LaTeX-in-JSON escape failures. Across three open 7–9B models and a GPT-4o probe, naive prompting yields high task accuracy but 0% output accuracy, while AloLab reaches 84–87% output accuracy on GSM8K, 34–40% on MATH, and 95.2% on GPT-4o GSM8K, with near-NAIVE latency; constrained decoding is slower by 3.6×–8.2× and can hurt task quality. The paper points toward prompt-level control planes and black-box reliability optimization as a distinct research direction for structured-output AI systems.
papers.abstract: Deployed language models must produce outputs that are both correct and format-compliant. We study this structured-output reliability gap using two mathematical benchmarks -- GSM8K and MATH -- as a controlled testbed: ground truth is unambiguous and the output contract is strict (JSON with required fields). We evaluate three 7-9B models under five prompting strategies and report output accuracy -- the joint event of mathematical correctness and valid JSON structure -- as the primary metric. A systematic format failure emerges: NAIVE prompting (no system prompt) achieves up to 85% task accuracy on GSM8K but 0% output accuracy across all models and datasets. REFERENCE prompting (a minimal hand-written JSON format prompt) fares little better, yielding 0% output accuracy for two of four models tested. Constrained decoding enforces syntactic validity but incurs 3.6x-8.2x latency overhead and in several settings degrades task performance substantially. To overcome this limitation, we developed AloLab, an iterative system-prompt optimizer (meta-agent: Claude Sonnet 4.5) requiring only black-box API access to the target model; it reaches 84-87% output accuracy on GSM8K and 34-40% on MATH across five independent runs per model, with 29/30 paired McNemar comparisons against the best static prompt significant at p < 0.05, at near-NAIVE inference latency and without model fine-tuning. The same format failure extends to GPT-4o (OpenAI, 2024), a proprietary closed-source model: REFERENCE achieves 0% output accuracy due to systematic markdown-fence wrapping, while AloLab reaches 95.2% [94.8, 95.6]. An ablation replacing the Sonnet 4.5 meta-agent with Claude 3 Haiku reduces mean output accuracy to 61.0% and increases run-to-run standard deviation from <1 pp to 21.8 pp, confirming that meta-agent capability is a primary driver of optimization quality.

papers.title: InfoLaw: Information Scaling Laws for Large Language Models with Quality-Weighted Mixture Data and Repetition
papers.summary: InfoLaw: Information Scaling Laws for Large Language Models with Quality-Weighted Mixture Data and Repetition attacks a concrete pretraining bottleneck: when high-quality web data is scarce, upweighting it in data-limited or overtrained LLM runs raises repetition, and standard compute-based scaling laws then mis-predict loss across mixture recipes, making optimal recipe choice underdetermined. The paper pivots from the old assumption that compute C alone organizes scaling toward a data-aware view in which training is an information-accumulation process, where quality sets information density and repetition creates scale-dependent diminishing returns. The mechanical shift is a LayerMix protocol that buckets Common Crawl into six quality tiers, samples each tier with controllable weights and repetition, then fits two learned levers—an exponential quality-density function f_d and a scale law λ(N)=a·ln(N)+b—inside an effective-information formula info=∑_d f_d M_d log(K)(1−e^(−λ(N)R_d/log(K))). Loss is then regressed as a power law over this information, collapsing different mixtures, model sizes, and token budgets onto a single curve. Unlike nearby mixture-scaling work, InfoLaw explicitly models repetition saturation and extrapolates across unseen recipes, larger scales, and higher overtraining ratios. Trained on 27 runs from 252M to 1.2B parameters and evaluated on ARC, HellaSwag, MMLU, and TriviaQA, it predicts unseen recipes up to 7B/425B tokens with 0.15% mean and 0.96% max absolute loss error, and it finds better token mixes for 2.5B without extra training. This points toward future research on theory-grounded data-recipe optimization rather than compute-only scaling.
papers.abstract: Upweighting high-quality data in LLM pretraining often improves performance, but in datalimited regimes, especially under overtraining, stronger upweighting increases repetition and can degrade performance. However, standard scaling laws do not reliably extrapolate across mixture recipes or under repetitions, making the selection for optimal data recipes at scaling underdetermined. To solve this, we introduce InfoLaw (Information Scaling Laws), a data-aware scaling framework that predicts loss from consumed tokens, model size, data mixture weights, and repetition. The key idea is to model pretraining as information accumulation, where quality controls information density and repetition induces scaledependent diminishing returns. We first collect the model performance after training on datasets that vary in scale, quality distribution, and repetition level. Then we build up the modeling for information so that information accurately predicts those model performance. InfoLaw predicts performance on unseen data recipes and larger scale runs (up to 7B, 425B tokens) with 0.15% mean and 0.96% max absolute error in loss, and it extrapolates reliably across overtraining levels, enabling efficient data-recipe selection under varying compute budgets.

papers.title: Is It Novel and Why? Fine-Grained Patent Novelty Prediction Based on Passage Retrieval
papers.summary: Is It Novel and Why? Fine-Grained Patent Novelty Prediction Based on Passage Retrieval attacks the bottleneck in patent examination where novelty is not a claim-level yes/no problem but a feature-to-passage matching problem: examiners must decide which specific claim features are disclosed in which passages of the closest prior art, and which feature makes the claim novel. The paper explicitly challenges the older workflow of binary claim classification, arguing that it is both too coarse for practice and vulnerable to shortcut learning from claim length, reference numerals, and domain-specific phrasing. The pivot is from predicting novelty directly toward evidence-grounded, feature-level retrieval and reasoning. Its mechanical shift is FiNE-Patents, a new dataset of 3,658 first claims from 3,163 EPO applications annotated with feature-level prior-art references extracted from European Search Opinion documents, plus a three-part evaluation protocol: passage retrieval, novel-feature identification, and claim-level novelty prediction. The system decomposes each claim into features, retrieves passages from a single closest prior art document for each feature, labels features as fully/partially/not disclosed, then aggregates these judgments into a final novelty decision; the hierarchical variant runs feature analyses separately with LLMs before synthesis. Compared with embedding and ROUGE baselines, open-weight Qwen reasoning workflows improve retrieval and novel-feature detection, while fine-tuned classifiers on claim text alone can still reach 75% accuracy via spurious correlations. The unusual contribution is not just performance but a diagnostic benchmark and adversarial test set that expose shortcut reliance, pointing the field toward transparent, citation-backed patent reasoning systems and multi-document examiner assistants.
papers.abstract: Novelty assessment is a critical yet complex task in the examination process for patent acceptance, requiring examiners to determine whether an invention is disclosed in a prior art document. The process involves intricate matching between specific features of a patent claim and passages in the prior art. While prior work has approached novelty prediction primarily as a binary classification task at the claim level, we argue that this formulation is susceptible to spurious correlations and lacks the granularity required for practical application. In this work, we introduce FiNE-Patents (Fine-grained Novelty Examination of Patents), a novel dataset comprising 3,658 first patent claims annotated with fine-grained, feature-level prior art references extracted from European Search Opinion (ESOP) documents. We propose shifting the evaluation paradigm from simple binary classification to a joint retrieval and abstract reasoning task at the feature level, requiring models to identify specific passages from a prior art document that disclose individual claim features, and to identify which features of a claim make it novel. We implement and evaluate LLM-based workflows that decompose claims into features, analyze each feature against prior art, and finally derive a claim-level novelty prediction. Our experiments demonstrate that these workflows outperform embedding-based baselines on passage retrieval and novel feature identification. Furthermore, we show that unlike trained classifiers, LLMs are robust against spurious correlations present in the claim-level novelty classification task. We release the dataset and code to foster further research into transparent and granular patent analysis.

papers.title: Automatic Reflection Level Classification in Hungarian Student Essays
papers.summary: Automatic Reflection Level Classification in Hungarian Student Essays attacks the bottleneck that reflective writing assessment is labor-intensive, subjective, and especially underdeveloped for Hungarian, a morphologically rich low-resource language where prior automated reflection work has been concentrated in English, German, or multilingual settings. The paper pivots from the assumption that pretrained transformers are the default solution for document understanding toward a more constraint-aware comparison of classical feature engineering versus language-specific fine-tuning under severe class imbalance. Its mechanical shift is concrete: a 1,954-essay, expert-annotated corpus from multiple academic years is labeled on a four-level reflection scale, then modeled with TF-IDF and semantic document embeddings on one side, and huBERT/PULI-BERT-Large fine-tuned for document-level classification on the other, with overlap chunking to bypass 512-token limits. Rather than treating imbalance as a side issue, the study systematically tests class weighting, SMOTE-style oversampling, ensemble boosters, backtranslation with embedding-similarity filtering, and focal/dice/cross-entropy losses, plus a broad ablation study. The unusual result is that the best overall score comes from a shallow SMOTEBoost + Qwen3-embedding pipeline, not a transformer, reaching about 71% averaged accuracy/F1/ROC-AUC, while transformers trail slightly overall but generalize better to minority reflection levels. This suggests a future direction where reflective analytics for educational NLP is built less around brute-force scaling and more around data regime, imbalance control, and benchmark designs that separate majority-class performance from real minority-class sensitivity.
papers.abstract: Reflective thinking is a key competency in education, but assessing reflective writing remains a time-consuming and subjective task for education experts. While automated reflective analysis has been explored in several languages, Hungarian language was not researched extensively. In this paper, we present the first comprehensive study on automatic reflection level classification in Hungarian student essays. We used a large, expert-annotated Hungarian dataset consisting of 1,954 reflective essays collected over multiple academic years and labeled on a four-level reflection scale. We investigate two approaches: (1) classical machine learning models using TF-IDF and semantic embedding features, and (2) Hungarian-specific transformer models fine-tuned for document-level reflection classification. To address the strong class imbalance in the dataset, we systematically examine class weighting, oversampling, data augmentation, and alternative loss functions. An extensive ablation study is conducted to analyze the contribution of each modeling and balancing strategy. Our results show that shallow machine learning models with appropriate feature engineering achieve strong overall performance, reaching up to 71% overall score averaged over accuracy, F1-score, and ROC AUC metrics, while transformer-based models achieve slightly lower overall score (68%) averaged over the same metrics, but demonstrate better generalization on minority reflection classes. These findings highlight the continued relevance of classical methods for low-resource settings and the robustness of transformer models for imbalanced classification. The proposed dataset and experimental insights provide a solid foundation for future research on automated reflective analysis in Hungarian and other morphologically rich languages.

papers.title: HalluScan: A Systematic Benchmark for Detecting and Mitigating Hallucinations in Instruction-Following LLMs
papers.summary: HalluScan: A Systematic Benchmark for Detecting and Mitigating Hallucinations in Instruction-Following LLMs attacks the practical bottleneck that hallucination detection research has been fragmented into one-off method papers, single-family evaluations, and domain-specific scores that cannot support deployment decisions. It pivots from asking whether a detector works in isolation toward asking which detector works, for which model, in which domain, and at what cost. The mechanical shift is a unified 72-configuration benchmark spanning 6 detection methods, 4 open-weight instruction-tuned model families, and 3 domains (scientific TruthfulQA, open-domain QA via Natural Questions, and commonsense ARC-Challenge), paired with a new composite metric, HalluScore, that blends factual error rate, semantic coherence, and fabrication rate and correlates with expert judgment (r=0.41). Unlike nearby hallucination work that optimizes a single AUROC or relies on brute-force verification, HalluScan also introduces Adaptive Detection Routing (ADR), a risk-aware controller that sends low-risk cases to cheap self-consistency or semantic-entropy checks, medium-risk cases to NLI, and only high-risk cases to retrieval-augmented verification, cutting cost 2.0x with only 0.1% AUROC loss. The benchmark’s error-cascade decomposition shows hallucination modes vary sharply by domain, with commonsense hardest and self-evaluation notably overconfident. Results favor entailment-based detection: NLI Verification reaches AUROC 0.88, RAV 0.66, while semantic entropy and self-consistency can underperform badly in sparse-sample settings. This points toward cost-aware, evidence-grounded, calibration-sensitive hallucination control rather than monolithic “best detector” claims.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse natural language processing tasks, yet they remain susceptible to hallucinations -- generating content that is factually incorrect, unfaithful to provided context, or misaligned with user instructions. We present HalluScan, a comprehensive benchmark framework that systematically evaluates hallucination detection and mitigation across 72 configurations spanning 6 detection methods, 4 open-weight model families, and 3 diverse domains.
