We introduce three key contributions: (1) HalluScore, a novel composite metric that achieves a Pearson correlation of r = 0.41 with human expert judgments; (2) Adaptive Detection Routing (ADR), an intelligent routing algorithm achieving 2.0x cost reduction with only 0.1% AUROC degradation; and (3) systematic error cascade decomposition revealing substantial variation in hallucination error types across domains. Our experiments reveal that NLI Verification achieves the highest overall AUROC of 0.88, while RAV achieves the second-highest AUROC of 0.66.

papers.title: PC-MNet: Dual-Level Congruity Modeling for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection via Polarity-Modulated Attention
papers.summary: PC-MNet: Dual-Level Congruity Modeling for Multimodal Sarcasm Detection via Polarity-Modulated Attention attacks the failure mode of multimodal sarcasm detectors that treat sarcasm as a similarity-fusion problem, where cross-modal attention and late concatenation blur the very contradictions that signal irony. It pivots from similarity-seeking alignment and uniform fusion toward contradiction-seeking verification, arguing that sarcastic utterances are better represented by polar-opposite relations at the atomic level and by context-driven incongruity at the conversational level. The mechanical shift is a hierarchical decoupling pipeline: YOLOv8-based region-guided semantic alignment removes background clutter, then polarity-modulated attention injects learnable polarity distances into text-to-vision/audio attention so contradiction, not resemblance, is amplified; parallel bipartite heterogeneous graphs extract composition-level conflicts without monolithic over-smoothing; a scalar congruity routing mechanism compresses high-dimensional branch outputs into priors that steer a prior-guided contextual RGAT; and a two-stage asymmetric optimization warms the polarity manifold with native valence scores before inconsistency-aware contrastive learning shapes a generalized incongruity manifold. This is unusual in that it explicitly isolates atomic, composition, and contextual conflicts rather than fusing them end-to-end, and it uses scalar routing to block feature entanglement. On MUStARD, MUStARD++, and balanced debiased variants, PC-MNet reaches 81.64 Macro-F1 and 82.46% accuracy, beating the strongest multimodal baseline by 3.14 F1. Its main limitation is weaker handling of dry irony with sparse observable cues, pointing toward future multimodal LLMs and streaming multi-party dialogue models with commonsense priors.
papers.abstract: Multimodal sarcasm detection, which aims to precisely identify pragmatic incongruities between literal text and nonverbal cues, has gained substantial attention in multimodal understanding. Recent advancements have predominantly relied on naïve similarity-based attention mechanisms and uniform late fusion strategies.Furthermore, given that functional entanglement restricts traditional late fusions, we incorporate a scalar congruity routing mechanism and a prior-guided contextual graph. This mechanism anchors a generalized incongruity manifold through a two-stage asymmetric optimization driven by inconsistency-aware contrastive learning, selectively fusing only the most discriminative multi-granularity evidence. Extensive experiments on the \texttt{MUStARD} benchmark and its spurious-correlation-mitigated balanced datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves new state-of-the-art performance, surpassing the strongest multimodal baseline by a substantial 3.14\% improvement in Macro-F1. By architecturally isolating atomic, composition, and contextual conflicts. This work provides a robust, decoupled paradigm for modeling subtle pragmatic incongruities in human communication.

papers.title: Leveraging Argument Structure to Predict Content Hatefulness
papers.summary: Leveraging Argument Structure to Predict Content Hatefulness tackles a specific bottleneck in hate-speech and information-disorder detection: malicious messages often look more persuasive because they are organized as arguments, yet most prior systems flatten them into whole-text classification and ignore how premises, conclusions, and verifiability cues jointly shape hateful intent. The paper pivots from lexical message-level screening toward argument-component reasoning, asking whether the structure of a claim can reveal the hatefulness of the entire post. Its mechanical shift is a set of fixed-length encodings over the WSF-ARG+ white-supremacy-forum corpus, where each message is represented as ordered argument slots and then selectively augmented with premise-only structure, conclusion-conditioned-on-premises, checkworthiness labels from ClaimBuster-style annotation, and oracle hatefulness labels for individual components. Rather than training a large neural model, the authors probe this representation space with logistic regression, SVM, random forest, and XGBoost under 5-fold stratified cross-validation. The unusual move is that they test whether premises alone can carry the signal and find they often do: pure structure reaches about 0.701 macro-F1, while adding component-level hate annotations lifts performance to 0.957 F1, showing that argumentative decomposition can be far more discriminative than the raw message. Checkworthiness alone is weaker and can hurt small models, underscoring a capacity bottleneck. The result points toward future hate-speech research that integrates argument mining, verifiable-claim signals, and component-level moderation rather than treating hateful content as a flat classification problem.
papers.abstract: Information disorder is a challenging phenomenon that affects society at large. This phenomenon entails the diffusion of misleading, misinforming, and hateful content online. In different contexts, one aspect of the problem may prevail, but overall, this is a broad problem that requires comprehensive solutions. While each dimension of the problem (hate speech, disinformation, misinformation, etc.) requires in-depth analysis, in this paper, we look into the possibility of argument structure to provide relevant information to link these different areas of the problem. In particular, we focus on the WSF-ARG+ dataset, which consists of white supremacy forum messages annotated in terms of argument structure (premises and conclusion). There, we leverage the checkworthiness and hatefulness annotations of the argument components to obtain insights into the hatefulness of the whole message. Our results show promising insights (up to 96% F1), indicating the possibility of extending this direction in the future to tackle hateful content identification and information disorder countering.

papers.title: ATLAS: Article Tracking, Linking, and Analysis of Swedish Encyclopedias
papers.summary: ATLAS: Article Tracking, Linking, and Analysis of Swedish Encyclopedias attacks the bottleneck that historical encyclopedia digitization usually stops at OCR, leaving entry boundaries, headwords, entity types, edition-to-edition drift, and external knowledge links buried in noisy text. The paper challenges the implicit workflow that “digitized” means searchable text, pivoting from raw transcription toward structured knowledge restoration and longitudinal article tracking across multiple editions. The mechanical shift is a text-only post-OCR pipeline that first converts paragraphs into headword masks and entry segments, then classifies entries into Person/Location/Other, then performs cross-edition entity resolution with sentence-transformer embeddings in Qdrant, and finally links only those encyclopedia entries whose Swedish Wikipedia counterparts explicitly cite Nordisk familjebok via Wikidata P1343. Unlike nearby historical NLP work that treats NER or entity linking as isolated tasks, ATLAS composes them into a conservative, edition-aware control loop with symmetry checks, headword agreement, and similarity thresholds to reduce false matches. It is applied to the four editions of Nordisk familjebok published from 1876 to 1951, including the incomplete third edition and inconsistent markup across editions; the authors build silver and manually corrected datasets for headword extraction and 6,000-entry type annotation. Results show 97.8% headword extraction F1, 93.4% type-classification F1, about 93–95% precision for cross-edition matching, and 10,964 Wikidata links at 85% precision but only 16.5% recall, revealing a precision-heavy tradeoff. The paper points toward richer historical-knowledge graphs, temporal encyclopedia comparison, and scalable provenance-aware cultural-heritage NLP.
papers.abstract: The digitization of old encyclopedias represents an important step to improve access to historically structured knowledge. Often, however, this process does not go beyond an optical character recognition, leaving all the underlying structure unexploited. In addition, many encyclopedias had multiple editions reflecting the evolution of knowledge. The lack of structure in the raw text makes it difficult to track changes across these editions. In this work, we built a pipeline to restore the text structure, where we extract the headwords and identify entries; categorize the entities; match entries across editions; and link entries to a Wikidata item. We applied this pipeline to the four major editions of \textit{Nordisk familjebok}, an authoritative Swedish encyclopedia published between 1876 and 1951. We could extract the headwords with an F1 score of 97.8\% and we obtained an F1 score of 93.4\% on the headword classification. On a small-scale evaluation, we reached a 93\% precision on the cross-edition matching, 85\% precision and 16.5\% recall on the Wikidata linking. This shows that an automated approach to digitized historical knowledge is possible. This should facilitate the preservation of general knowledge and the understanding of knowledge transmission. The datasets and programs are available online.

papers.title: Accurate Legal Reasoning at Scale: Neuro-Symbolic Offloading and Structural Auditability for Robust Legal Adjudication
papers.summary: Accurate Legal Reasoning at Scale: Neuro-Symbolic Offloading and Structural Auditability for Robust Legal Adjudication attacks the bottleneck that frontier LRMs can paraphrase legal clauses yet still fail on production-grade adjudication when the clause contains nested eligibility logic, date-sensitive pricing, and arithmetic that must be identical across repeated transactions. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that legal reasoning should stay a runtime, probabilistic “reasoning-as-compute” problem; instead it pivots from end-to-end LRM interpretation toward a compile-time legal compiler model. The mechanical shift is Amortized Intelligence: one LLM pass translates a contract into Deterministic Autonomous Contract Language (DACL), a typed graph IR with primitives for procedures, logical clauses, range clauses, and pricing formulas, after which adjudication is delegated to deterministic graph execution with a visually auditable trace and clause-level versioning. Unlike nearby neuro-symbolic work that stops at extraction or static graphing, this system couples constrained semantic mapping, symbolic tool use, and structural audit logs in a live agent loop built for enterprise workflows. Evaluated on four real commercial agreements from healthcare, energy, logistics, and municipal billing over 400 synthetic production-like events, the DACL agent ran with gpt-5-mini orchestration against GPT-5.2, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, and Gemini 3 Pro baselines, reaching 99.5% accuracy, mitigating the “reasoning cliff,” and cutting compute by over 90%, with most baseline failures coming from variable-dependency errors rather than arithmetic. The paper points toward AI systems that are compiled, auditable, and economically scalable for regulated domains.
papers.abstract: Legal texts often contain computational legal clauses--provisions whose understanding requires complex logic. While frontier Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) can describe such clauses, building production-ready systems is limited by reasoning errors and the high cost of inference. We propose Amortized Intelligence, a neuro-symbolic approach where we use an LLM once to translate a legal text into Deterministic Autonomous Contract Language (DACL): a typed graph intermediate representation. Adjudication then relies on deterministic graph executions with a visually auditable trace. In comparison against runtime LRM baselines (including GPT-5.2 and Gemini 3 Pro), our DACL-based Agent achieves near-perfect consistency and mitigates the "reasoning cliff" observed in probabilistic models. The system reduces compute costs by over 90% in high-volume workflows while satisfying the strict auditability requirements of legal adjudication.

papers.title: A multilingual hallucination benchmark: MultiWikiQHalluA
papers.summary: A multilingual hallucination benchmark: MultiWikiQHalluA attacks the blind spot that most hallucination evaluations are English-only and answer-level, obscuring whether faithfulness failures transfer to lower-resource languages and whether tokenization or multilingual pretraining changes the failure mode. The paper pivots from static English factuality benchmarks toward a multilingual, context-grounded faithfulness benchmark built on MultiWikiQA, where questions have verbatim answers in Wikipedia passages, and uses the LettuceDetect framework to synthesize hallucinated answers across 306 languages. The mechanical shift is two-stage: GPT-5-mini plus RAGFactChecker rewrites gold answers under sampled hallucination intensities, with span annotations projected to token labels, then mmBERT-small is fine-tuned as a token-level hallucination classifier for 30 European languages. This is unusual because it replaces coarse human judgment or binary answer scoring with localized unsupported-span detection, enabling the same test set to be scored comparably across English, Danish, German, and Icelandic. On five models—Qwen3-0.6B/14B, Gemma-3-12B-IT, cogito-v1-preview-qwen-32B, and cogito-v1-preview-llama-70B—the classifiers find consistently higher hallucination rates in lower-resource languages, especially Icelandic, with Qwen3-0.6B reaching up to 60% of answers containing at least one hallucination. Larger models generally do better, but size is not monotonic, suggesting architecture and multilingual training mix matter as much as parameter count. The work points toward multilingual, token-level, resource-aware hallucination diagnostics and toward separating genuine faithfulness failures from tokenizer artifacts.
papers.abstract: Most hallucination evaluations focus on English, leaving it unclear whether findings transfer to lower-resource languages. We investigate faithfulness hallucinations, defined as model-generated content that is fluent and plausible but diverges from the provided input or is internally inconsistent. Leveraging the multilingual MultiWikiQA dataset, we utilize the LettuceDetect framework to create synthetic hallucination datasets for 306 languages, from which we train token-level hallucination classifiers for 30 European languages. In this work, we present evaluations of model hallucinations on a selection of languages: English, Danish, German, and Icelandic. Using these classifiers, we evaluate the hallucination rates for Qwen3-0.6B, Qwen3-14B, Gemma-3-12B-IT, cogito-v1-preview-qwen-32B, and cogito-v1-preview-llama-70B. Our classifiers reveal notably higher hallucination rates for Qwen3-0.6B (up to 60\% of answers containing at least one hallucination, peaking in Icelandic) and generally lower rates for larger models, with cogito-v1-preview-qwen-32B and cogito-v1-preview-llama-70B performing best on most languages. Hallucination rates are consistently higher for lower-resource languages, particularly Icelandic.

papers.title: Revisiting Semantic Role Labeling: Efficient Structured Inference with Dependency-Informed Analysis
papers.summary: Revisiting Semantic Role Labeling: Efficient Structured Inference with Dependency-Informed Analysis attacks a practical bottleneck in SRL deployment: AllenNLP’s maintenance-mode, per-predicate inference pipeline recomputes the same sentence encoding for every predicate, making structured predicate-argument labeling slow and awkward for modern encoders even though explicit SRL remains crucial for controllability and interpretability. The paper pivots from the recent assumption that LLMs should absorb semantics implicitly toward a modernized encoder-based SRL stack that keeps PropBank-style structure explicit while optimizing the inference path itself. The mechanical shift is sentence-level caching plus predicate-conditioned reuse: one tokenization/encoder pass is shared across all predicates in a sentence, then predicate spans are appended as [CLS]|sent|[SEP]|pred|[SEP] inputs, eliminating redundant recomputation and yielding 10× faster inference on CoNLL-2012/OntoNotes 5.0. With BERT-base the system matches AllenNLP’s F1, while RoBERTa and DeBERTa raise performance further inside the same structured framework. What differentiates the paper is its dependency-informed analysis layer: a diagnostic analyzer merges repeated spans, categorizes span violations such as same_head, pp_attach, and subtree_attach, and separates fixable structural errors from cases needing human review. In zero-shot gpt-oss-120b experiments, dependency cues improve structural stability more than role classification, and the same explicit structure supports English–French SRL projection with less boundary drift. This points toward SRL as a fast, auditable control layer for multilingual transfer and for grounding foundation models in externally checkable semantic structure.
papers.abstract: Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) provides an explicit representation of predicate-argument structure, capturing linguistically grounded relations such as who did what to whom. While recent NLP progress has been dominated by large language models (LLMs), these systems often rely on implicit semantic representations, often lacking explicit structural constraints and systematic explanatory mechanisms. Traditionally, SRL systems have often relied on AllenNLP; however, the framework entered maintenance mode in December 2022, limiting compatibility with evolving encoder architectures and modern inference requirements. We revisit structured SRL modeling, introducing a modernized encoder-based framework that preserves explicit predicate-argument structure while enabling inference 10 times faster. Using BERT-base, the model attains comparable predictive performance, and RoBERTa and DeBERTa further improve F1 performance within the same framework. We adopt a dependency-informed diagnostic methodology to characterize span-level inconsistencies and conduct a representation-level analysis of LLM behavior under dependency-informed structural signals. Results indicate that dependency cues primarily improve structural stability. Finally, we illustrate how the framework's explicit predicate-argument structure can support multilingual SRL projection as a downstream application.

papers.title: Benchmarking Retrieval Strategies for Biomedical Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Controlled Empirical Study
papers.summary: Benchmarking Retrieval Strategies for Biomedical Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Controlled Empirical Study attacks a practical bottleneck in biomedical RAG: once retrieval is the only thing grounding GPT answers, which retriever actually helps when errors can affect clinical meaning? The paper challenges the common assumption that “more retrieval sophistication” or generic dense search automatically wins, and instead pivots from model-centric scaling toward a retrieval-isolation study where generation is frozen and only the retriever changes. The mechanical shift is a controlled swap of five strategies—Dense Vector Search, BM25+dense hybrid, Cross-Encoder Reranking, Multi-Query Expansion, and MMR—inside one modular pipeline with GPT-4o-mini, ChromaDB, and text-embedding-3-small held constant, then scored on 250 rag-mini-bioasq QA pairs with contextual precision, contextual recall, faithfulness, answer relevancy, and 95% confidence intervals plus a no-context lower bound. Unusually, the study treats retrieval as a measurable control lever rather than a hidden subsystem: cross-encoder reranking, which adds query-document interaction only on a 30-candidate shortlist, wins on precision and composite score; dense retrieval remains a formidable baseline with the best recall; multi-query diversification backfires by injecting noise; and MMR buys diversity at the cost of recall and answer relevancy. The no-context ablation shows retrieval is doing the real work. This points toward biomedical RAG research that is more benchmarked, retrieval-calibrated, latency-aware, and query-adaptive rather than simply larger or more generative.
papers.abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) offers a well-established path to grounding large language model (LLM) outputs in external knowledge, yet the question of which retrieval strategy works best in a high-stakes domain such as biomedicine has not received the controlled, multi-metric treatment it deserves. This paper presents a systematic empirical comparison of five retrieval strategies -- Dense Vector Search, Hybrid BM25 + Dense retrieval, Cross-Encoder Reranking, Multi-Query Expansion, and Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) -- within a biomedical question-answering RAG pipeline. All strategies share a fixed generation model (GPT-4o-mini), a common vector store (ChromaDB), and OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small embeddings, ensuring that observed differences are attributable to retrieval alone. Evaluation is conducted on 250 question-answer pairs drawn from a preprocessed subset of the BioASQ benchmark (rag-mini-bioasq) using four DeepEval metrics: contextual precision, contextual recall, faithfulness, and answer relevancy, each reported with 95% confidence intervals. A no-context ablation is included as a lower bound. Cross-Encoder Reranking achieves the best composite score (0.827) and highest contextual precision (0.852), confirming that query-document interaction yields measurable retrieval gains. Multi-Query Expansion, despite its recall-oriented design, produces the weakest contextual precision (0.671), suggesting naive query diversification introduces retrieval noise. MMR sacrifices answer relevancy for diversity, while the Dense baseline (composite 0.822) falls within 0.005 points of the top strategy. All RAG conditions dramatically outperform the no-context ablation on answer relevancy (0.658-0.701 vs. 0.287), confirming the practical value of retrieval. The full pipeline, hyperparameters, and evaluation code are publicly available.

papers.title: SemEval-2026 Task 7: Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures
papers.summary: SemEval-2026 Task 7: Everyday Knowledge Across Diverse Languages and Cultures frames the bottleneck as not model scaling but whether LLMs can answer mundane, culturally anchored questions without defaulting to Westernized stereotypes or Wikipedia-like surface facts. It pivots from monolingual, web-derived culture benchmarks toward a manually constructed, native-speaker-curated evaluation set spanning 33 language–culture pairs, with a strong emphasis on low-resource communities across continents. The mechanical shift is that BLEnD is used strictly as held-out evaluation: annotators, recruited through language leads, created question–answer pairs from lived experience without LLMs or search engines, then validated and normalized; participants could not train on, fine-tune on, or few-shot from the data. The task splits into SAQ, where systems must generate brief free-text answers and are scored by exact matching plus language-specific normalization rather than semantic similarity, and MCQ, where models choose the culturally correct option among region-specific alternatives, forcing locale-conditioned reasoning rather than memorized trivia. This setup is unusual because the same English question can map to different correct answers by region, exposing cultural misalignment, option-order sensitivity, and evaluation failures in morphologically rich languages; a human check for Farsi showed lemma scoring can undercount performance by 18 points. Across 62 final teams, GPT-4.1 remained a strong baseline, but RAG, region-aware prompting, and multi-agent debate dominated top systems. The paper points toward culturally grounded retrieval, locale-specific adaptation, and better evaluation metrics for under-represented languages and everyday knowledge.
papers.abstract: We present our shared task on evaluating the adaptability of LLMs and NLP systems across multiple languages and cultures. The task data consist of an extended version of our manually constructed BLEnD benchmark (Myung et al. 2024), covering more than 30 language-culture pairs, predominantly representing low-resource languages spoken across multiple continents. As the task is designed strictly for evaluation, participants were not permitted to use the data for training, fine-tuning, few-shot learning, or any other form of model modification. Our task includes two tracks: (a) Short-Answer Questions (SAQ) and (b) Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQ). Participants were required to predict labels and were allowed to submit any NLP system and adopt diverse modelling strategies, provided that the benchmark was used solely for evaluation. The task attracted more than 140 registered participants, and we received final submissions from 62 teams, along with 19 system description papers. We report the results and present an analysis of the best-performing systems and the most commonly adopted approaches. Furthermore, we discuss shared insights into open questions and challenges related to evaluation, misalignment, and methodological perspectives on model behaviour in low-resource languages and for under-represented cultures.

papers.title: Dependency Parsing Across the Resource Spectrum: Evaluating Architectures on High and Low-Resource Languages
papers.summary: Dependency Parsing Across the Resource Spectrum: Evaluating Architectures on High and Low-Resource Languages attacks a practical bottleneck in syntactic tooling: the assumption that transformer encoders are universally superior for dependency parsing collapses once treebanks shrink to a few hundred or thousand sentences, especially in African-language settings. The paper pivots from the default “pre-trained transformers everywhere” scaling story toward a resource-sensitive comparison of inductive bias, showing that a simpler Biaffine LSTM can be the better parser when supervision is scarce. Its core mechanical shift is not a new parser, but a new evaluation lens: four parsers—Biaffine LSTM, Stack-Pointer Network, AfroXLMR-large, and RemBERT—are compared across ten typologically diverse languages using LAS/UAS plus a normalized relative error rate (RER) that measures each model’s error against the Biaffine baseline, then modeled against log training size and MATTR morphological complexity. On the AfriSUD languages plus French, Romanian, and Afrikaans, the Biaffine LSTM consistently wins in low-resource regimes, while transformers recover and surpass it only once training sets reach roughly 800–1,400 sentences; higher MATTR further penalizes transformers after controlling for corpus size, with AfroXLMR partially buffering that effect. The Stack-Pointer model underperforms almost everywhere, suggesting that added decoding complexity and error propagation are liabilities under data scarcity. This points toward future work on crossover-aware architecture selection, better morphology-sensitive diagnostics, and controlled subsampling studies for low-resource syntax.
papers.abstract: Transformer-based models achieve state-of-the-art dependency parsing for high-resource languages, yet their advantage over simpler architectures in low-resource settings remains poorly understood. We evaluate four parsers -- the Biaffine LSTM, Stack-Pointer Network, AfroXLMR-large, and RemBERT -- across ten typologically diverse languages, with a focus on low-resource African languages. We find that the Biaffine LSTM consistently outperforms transformer models in low-resource regimes, with transformers recovering their advantage as training data increases. The crossover falls within a resource range typical of treebanks for under-resourced languages. Morphological complexity (measured via MATTR) emerges as a significant secondary predictor of transformers' relative disadvantage after controlling for corpus size. These results indicate that the Biaffine LSTM may be better suited for syntactic tool development in low-resource regimes until sufficient annotated data is available to leverage the representational capacity of pre-trained transformers.

papers.title: Beating the Style Detector: Three Hours of Agentic Research on the AI-Text Arms Race
papers.summary: Beating the Style Detector: Three Hours of Agentic Research on the AI-Text Arms Race shows how a released empirical NLP study can be rerun, stress-tested, and repurposed from a human-style-editing question into a detector-evasion question in a few hours once an agentic-research harness replaces week-scale manual analysis. The paper attacks the bottleneck that reproducing preregistered NLP experiments is slow and fragile when analysis code is missing, and it challenges the implicit workflow in which humans hand-edit LLM drafts and treat style similarity as the only endpoint. The pivot is from “can a person make an LLM draft sound like them?” toward “how easily can a frontier LLM lower its own detectability?” Mechanically, the authors reimplement all seven preregistered hypotheses from the ACL 2026 study on personal-style post-editing, then add a leakage-free held-out protocol and an AI-text arms-race benchmark: 648 mimic drafts, LUAR-MUD embeddings, leave-authors-out linear SVM detection, six confound diagnostics, and a closed-loop adversarial rewriting run with T=20 feedback iterations against a frozen detector. Unlike nearby work, the generator is evaluated both as a style mimic and as an adaptive adversary, with the human reduced to reviewer-in-the-loop. Under the held-out test, GPT-5.5 and Claude Opus 4.7 close 71–75% of the style gap to the same-author ceiling and beat human post-editing on about 80% of tasks; detection remains strong (AUC 0.93–1.00), but Opus can already flip two of five held-out mimics across the decision boundary. The paper points toward inference-time, agentic, detector-aware optimization as a new front in AI-text research.
papers.abstract: Reproducing an empirical NLP study used to take weeks. Given the released data and a modern agentic-research harness, we redo every experiment of a recent ACL\,2026 study on personal-style post-editing of LLM drafts -- and add three new ones -- with the human investigator acting only as a reviewer-in-the-loop. We reproduce all seven preregistered hypotheses and recover the paper's headline correlation between perceived self-similarity and embedding-measured self-similarity to three decimal places ($r{=}{+}0.244$, $p{<}10^{-8}$, $n{=}648$). Under a leakage-free held-out protocol, GPT-5.5 and Claude\,Opus\,4.7 close $71$--$75\,\%$ of the style gap to the same-author ceiling on $324$ paired tasks, against $24\,\%$ for the human post-edit, and beat the human post-edit on $\sim$$80\,\%$ of tasks. We then frame the same data as an AI-text detection arms race. A leave-authors-out linear SVM on LUAR-MUD embeddings reaches AUC $0.93$--$1.00$ across approaches; six diagnostics show that GPT-5.5 detection is mostly a length confound while Opus detection is a genuine stylistic signature. Given $T{=}20$ feedback iterations against the frozen detector, an Opus agent flips two of five held-out test mimics to the human half-space and shrinks every margin by an order of magnitude. With moderate effort against a known detector, a frontier LLM can already efficiently lower its own AI-detection probability. All code, $648$ mimic drafts, trained detectors, diagnostics, and adversarial trajectories are released.

papers.title: Synthetic Users, Real Differences: an Evaluation Framework for User Simulation in Multi-Turn Conversations
papers.summary: Synthetic Users, Real Differences: an Evaluation Framework for User Simulation in Multi-Turn Conversations attacks the validity bottleneck in chatbot evaluation: LLM-based user simulation is increasingly used to replace costly real-user studies, yet most realism checks judge synthetic dialogues one-by-one and collapse mismatch into coarse “realistic/not realistic” labels. The paper pivots from sample-level imitation toward distributional auditing, asking not whether a synthetic conversation looks plausible in isolation, but whether simulated users reproduce the same behavioral patterns as real users across task scenarios. Its core mechanical shift is realsim, a framework that first extracts a scenario from each real dialogue, replays that scenario with different simulators, then annotates both real and synthetic conversations along 8 realism dimensions spanning communicative function, user state, and surface form: intent, feedback, emotion, identity, knowledge, length, linguistics, and error. Those annotations are compared with total variation distance, Wasserstein distance, semantic similarity, and across-domain correlation, making drift visible at the level of user friction rather than aggregate success. Instantiated on 1K multi-turn task-focused real dialogues curated from WildChat and LMSYS-1M across 16 domains, the framework exposes a distinctive failure mode: simulated users are systematically easier interlocutors, more verbose, more readable, more positive, and less likely to introduce breakdowns, which can make simulator-based evaluation overly optimistic. It also finds strong domain variation, implying that general-purpose user simulators may be insufficient and that future work should build domain-calibrated user models and realism benchmarks grounded in real conversational distributions.
papers.abstract: There is growing interest in exploring user simulation as an alternative to gathering and scoring real user-chatbot interactions for AI chatbot evaluation. For this purpose, it is important to ensure the realism of the simulation, i.e., the extent to which simulated dialogues reflect real dialogues users have with chatbots. Most existing methods evaluating simulation realism produce coarse quality signal and remain solely at the level of individual dialogues. To support more rigorous evaluation in this area, we propose realsim, an evaluation framework that enables practitioners to take a distributional view of real vs. simulated dialogues along 8 dimensions, covering attributes related to the communicative functions of the interaction, user states, and the surface form of user messages. We then instantiate the framework with a curated dataset of 1K multi-turn task-focused real user-chatbot dialogues that cover 16 domains of chatbot applications. Overall, we find that simulated users tend to struggle at capturing communication frictions that real users introduce to interactions, which could make evaluations based on such simulations overly optimistic. We also observe variability in performance across different domains, which may indicate a need for domain-specific user simulators.

papers.title: Mapping Discourse Reframing: A Multi-Layer Network Approach to Italian HPV Vaccine Discourse on X (2010-2024)
papers.summary: Mapping Discourse Reframing: A Multi-Layer Network Approach to Italian HPV Vaccine Discourse on X (2010-2024) tackles the bottleneck that longitudinal misinformation studies often erase the earliest, sparsest signs of reframing by using conservative network pruning that only preserves already-robust discourse. The paper pivots from treating social media discourse as a single filtered graph toward a two-layer view of narrative formation: a core layer for stable coalitions and a coverage layer for fringe hashtags that may later become politically or conspiratorially activated. The mechanical shift is a core–coverage projection over weighted hashtag co-occurrence networks built from 4,895 Italian X posts spanning 2010–2024, segmented into three epochs (2010–2014, 2015–2019, 2020–2024). Core communities are extracted with edge weight ≥2, k-core ≥2, and hierarchical Girvan-Newman detection; the coverage layer keeps all weight ≥1 links, then assigns fringe hashtags to cores by strongest weighted connectivity. Unusually, this is not just a denser graph but an interpretability-preserving “computational zoom lens” that retains the long tail while mapping where reframing attaches to prevention-oriented backbones. With manually labelled skeptical and conspiratorial seed tweets as weak supervision, the method recovers substantially more long-tail problematic hashtags than a single-layer k-core baseline. Empirically, the discourse moves from an early prevention/screening core, to a mid-period rise of a dense skepticism coalition, to a 2020–2024 core saturated with Big Pharma, eugenics, WHO, and COVID-adjacent frames. The result suggests future work on multi-layer discourse tracking, seed-guided narrative mining, and cross-domain monitoring of how fringe claims structurally mature into dominant coalitions.
papers.abstract: Understanding how online narratives travel through coalitions is critical for identifying information disorder, yet computational analyses often rely on conservative network constructions that erase initially sparse but salient signals. This paper proposes a novel multi-layer framework that captures low-frequency signals of emerging information disorder allowing for locating where online discourse is reframed and amplified over time. The use case is 14 years of Italian discourse on X regarding the Human Papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine across three pivotal epochs (2010-2024). Utilizing hashtag co-occurrence networks, we introduce a dual-layer approach. We first identify robust core discourse coalitions through conservative community detection, revealing a stable prevention-oriented backbone contrasted with increasingly separable skepticism coalitions. We then introduce a coverage layer and project fringe hashtags into core coalitions based on weighted connectivity. Using a manually labelled set of skeptical and conspiratorial seed tweets, we demonstrate that this core-coverage projection significantly improves the recovery of long-tail, problematic hashtags while preserving an interpretable coalition structure. Our findings characterize the structural maturation of polarized narratives and provide a methodology for mapping how discourse is reframed and amplified by information disorder over time.

papers.title: ContextualJailbreak: Evolutionary Red-Teaming via Simulated Conversational Priming
papers.summary: ContextualJailbreak: Evolutionary Red-Teaming via Simulated Conversational Priming attacks the bottleneck that automated jailbreak search has mostly stayed trapped in single-turn prompt space, even though contextual priming across prior turns is a stronger failure mode than raw request phrasing. The paper challenges the assumption that a jailbreak is just a static text artifact and pivots from optimizing prompts toward evolving the full simulated conversational trajectory that leads up to the harmful request. The mechanical shift is a black-box evolutionary fuzzing loop over role-labeled multi-turn dialogues, driven by a graded 0–5 harm score from a two-level judge, so partially harmful outputs are retained as useful search signal instead of being discarded under binary success/failure. Search is steered by five semantic mutators—roleplay, scenario, expand, troubleshooting, and mechanistic—with troubleshooting and mechanistic introduced here as novel operators that specifically exploit diagnostic and causal-reasoning frames. Unlike nearby work such as Crescendo or PAIR, ContextualJailbreak delivers the primed dialogue in a single API call while still searching over conversational context itself. On 50 HarmBench behaviors across qwen3-8B, gpt-oss:20B, gpt-oss:120B, and llama3.1:70B, it reaches 100% ASR@4 on three open-source targets and 90% on gpt-oss:120B, beating four baselines by 31–96 points; the same attacks transfer strongly to GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5, and Gemini-3-flash but much less to Claude, revealing provider-level asymmetry. The paper points toward trajectory-aware safety evaluation and context-robust alignment defenses.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass safety alignment and elicit harmful responses. A growing body of work shows that contextual priming, where earlier turns covertly bias later replies, constitutes a powerful attack surface, with hand-crafted multi-turn scaffolds consistently outperforming single-turn manipulations on capable models. However, automated optimization-based red-teaming has remained largely limited to the single-turn setting, iterating over static prompts and lacking the ability to reason about which forms of conversational priming induce compliance. While recent multi-turn, search-based approaches have begun to bridge this gap, the mutator design space underlying effective primed dialogues remains largely unexplored. We present ContextualJailbreak, a black-box red-teaming strategy that performs evolutionary search over a simulated multi-turn primed dialogue. The strategy leverages a graded 0-5 harm score from a two-level judge as an in-loop signal, enabling partially harmful responses to guide the search process rather than being discarded. Search is driven by five semantically defined mutation operators: roleplay, scenario, expand, troubleshooting, and mechanistic, of which the last two are novel contributions of this work. Across 50 representative HarmBench behaviors, ContextualJailbreak achieves an ASR of 100% on gpt-oss:20B, 100% on qwen3-8B, 100% on llama3.1:70B, and 90% on gpt-oss:120B, outperforming four single- and multi-turn baselines by 31-96 percentage points on average. The 40 maximally harmful attacks discovered against gpt-oss:120B transfer without adaptation to closed frontier models, achieving 90.0% on gpt-4o-mini, 70.0% on gpt-5, and 70.0% on gemini-3-flash, but only 17.5% on claude-opus-4-7 and 15.0% on claude-sonnet-4-6, revealing a pronounced provider-level asymmetry in alignment robustness.

papers.title: Fuzzy Fingerprinting Encoder Pre-trained Language Models for Emotion Recognition in Conversations: Human Assessment and Validity Study
papers.summary: Fuzzy Fingerprinting Encoder Pre-trained Language Models for Emotion Recognition in Conversations: Human Assessment and Validity Study attacks the ERC failure mode where RoBERTa-like encoder PLMs, though strong on macro-F1, collapse minority emotions into the dominant Neutral class on highly imbalanced dialogue corpora such as DailyDialog, while also remaining opaque about why a label was chosen. The paper pivots from the usual “fine-tune a black-box encoder plus softmax head” workflow toward prototype-based, human-readable classification: instead of learning an entangled decision boundary, it derives class-specific Fuzzy Fingerprints (FFPs) from the pooled [CLS] latent activations of context-dependent utterance embeddings, ranks the most active dimensions, keeps only top-k cells, fuzzifies their membership scores, and then classifies a test utterance by fuzzy similarity via intersection aggregation against an emotion fingerprint library. The mechanical shift is especially distinctive because it turns hidden vector activations into sparse fuzzy prototypes that can be visualized, compared, and audited, replacing post-hoc explanation with explanation built into inference itself. On DailyDialog, RoBERTa+FFP reduced overclassification into Neutral, peaked at K=300, and stayed near state-of-the-art while using a single RoBERTa rather than heavier multi-encoder systems like CoMPM; larger K values were already competitive with the full 768-dimensional representation. A human validity study on 69 disagreement cases with three expert annotators favored FFP labels 59% to 41%, suggesting closer alignment with nuanced emotional perception. This points toward future ERC systems that are prototype-grounded, lightweight, and explicitly calibrated to human interpretability and annotation ambiguity.
papers.abstract: In Emotion Recognition in Conversations (ERC), model decisions should align with nuanced human perception and ideally provide insights on the classification process. Standard encoder pre-trained language models (PLMs) are the state-of-the-art at these tasks but offer little insight into why a certain prediction is made. This is especially problematic in imbalanced datasets, where most utterances are labeled as neutral, making these models frequently misclassify minority emotions as the majority neutral class. To tackle this issue, we introduced a novel, interpretable approach to ERC by combining PLMs with Fuzzy Fingerprints (FFPs). FFP provide class-specific prototypes that reflect the characteristic class activation patterns in the PLM's latent space. They are derived by ranking and fuzzifying the activations of the pooled conversational context-dependent embeddings across training instances for each emotion. At inference time, each input utterance is similarly fuzzy fingerprinted and matched to the emotion prototypes using a fuzzy similarity function based on the aggregation of the intersection of the fuzzy sets that define each FFP. Experimental results show that FFP integration reduces overclassification into the neutral class and human evaluation further supports the adequacy of FFP predictions. Our proposed method thus bridges the gap between deep neural inference and human perception, performing at state-of-the-art level while simultaneously offering valuable insights into the classification procedure.

papers.title: mdok-style at SemEval-2026 Task 9: Finetuning LLMs for Multilingual Polarization Detection
papers.summary: mdok-style at SemEval-2026 Task 9: Finetuning LLMs for Multilingual Polarization Detection attacks the bottleneck of spotting online polarization before it hardens into hate speech, offensive discourse, and social fragmentation, across 22 languages and three distinct objectives: binary detection, polarization type, and rhetorical manifestation. The paper implicitly challenges the old assumption that multilingual safety classification can be handled by small BERT-like encoders or by training separate language-specific models; instead it pivots from narrow, surface-form-sensitive text classification toward a single robust multilingual mid-size LLM adapted with parameter-efficient QLoRA sequence-classification. The mechanical shift is twofold: combine all languages into one training pool to force task learning over language memorization, and duplicate training texts into anonymized, lower-cased, upper-cased, and homoglyphified variants so the detector learns meaning under obfuscation, casing noise, and visually deceptive character substitutions. This inherits the authors’ mdok robustness recipe from machine-generated text detection, but repurposes it for multilingual polarization, and extends the multiclass setup to the multi-label structure of subtasks 2 and 3. They fine-tune Qwen3-32B for detection and Gemma-3-27B-pt for the harder multi-label tasks, using tiny batches, one epoch, and validation-selected checkpoints. The system beats the baseline in about two thirds of cases, averages +3% macro F1 on detection and +4% on type, but remains weak on manifestation, especially rare labels and low-resource languages. The paper points toward multilingual robustness research that combines PEFT, adversarial-style augmentation, and future appraisal-theoretic affect signals.
papers.abstract: SemEval-2026 Task 9 is focused on multilingual polarization detection. Specifically, it covers the identification of multilingual, multicultural and multievent polarization along three axes (in subtasks), namely detection, type, and manifestation. Online polarization presents a concern, because it is often followed by hate speech, offensive discourse, and social fragmentation. Therefore, its detection before it escalates is crucial for a safer and more inclusive online space. We have coped with this SemEval task by finetuning mid-size LLMs for the sequence-classification task using the QLoRA parameter-efficient finetuning technique. The training data augmented the multilingual (22 languages) training sets by anonymized, lower-cased, upper-cased, and homoglyphied counterparts, making the detection more robust.

papers.title: mdok-style at SemEval-2026 Task 10: Finetuning LLMs for Conspiracy Detection
papers.summary: mdok-style at SemEval-2026 Task 10: Finetuning LLMs for Conspiracy Detection attacks the bottleneck of detecting whether a short Reddit comment expresses a conspiracy belief when the labeled set is small, class-imbalanced, and socially noisy, where vanilla LLM finetuning is brittle and expensive. It implicitly challenges the assumption that conspiracy detection should rely on hand-engineered psycholinguistic features or small encoder classifiers, and instead pivots from machine-generated text detection toward label-efficient binary moderation of social-media narratives. The mechanical shift is a mdok-style robustness pipeline around Qwen3-32B: QLoRA finetuning for binary sequence classification, plus four augmentation levers—anonymization of emails/users/phones, lowercasing and uppercasing to suppress casing artifacts, and homoglyphication to harden against character-level obfuscation—while only 10% of each synthetic variant is mixed in and duplicates are removed. It then adds self-training, using the model to pseudo-label unlabeled dev/test text and keeping only extreme-confidence silver labels (≥0.99 positive, ≤0.01 negative) to expand training without amplifying noise. This is unusual because the approach is not a new conspiracy-specific architecture but a transfer of a detector trained for machine-generated text, showing that obfuscation-resilient finetuning can generalize across adjacent binary text-regimes. On the official PsyCoMark task, the submitted system reached 0.78 Macro F1 and 8th/52, with only modest gains over non-self-trained variants and a much smaller DeBERTa close behind, highlighting a compute-vs-accuracy tradeoff and pointing toward future semi-supervised, robustness-first adaptation for misinformation and social-risk detection.
papers.abstract: SemEval-2026 Task 10 is focused on conspiracy detection. Specifically, the goal is to detect whether a Reddit comment expresses a conspiracy belief. Our submitted mdok-style system utilizes data augmentation and self-training (to cope with a rather small amount of training data) to finetune the Qwen3-32B model for a binary text-classification task. The submitted system is very competitive, ranking in the 85th percentile (8th out of 52 submissions). The results shown that our approach, which originated in machine-generated text detection, can be used for conspiracy detection as well.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through Orchestration Traces
papers.summary: Reinforcement Learning for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems through Orchestration Traces attacks the bottleneck that emerges when LLM agents stop being isolated tool users and become coordinated teams: RL must optimize not just per-agent tokens or tool calls, but spawning, delegation, communication, aggregation, and stopping inside a mutable team graph. The paper explicitly challenges the old single-agent-trajectory and fixed-agent MARL assumption, pivoting from state/action rollouts toward orchestration traces, temporal interaction graphs whose nodes include sub-agent spawn, message, tool, return, and aggregate events. The mechanical shift is to treat the orchestration trace as the first-class audit object, then organize the literature by three coupled axes: eight reward families, eight credit/signal-bearing units from token to team, and five orchestration sub-decisions (when to spawn, whom to delegate to, how to communicate, how to aggregate, when to stop). Unlike nearby LLM-agent work, it emphasizes that message-level counterfactual credit is still extremely sparse and that the stopping decision lacks any explicit RL training method in the curated pool. The setting is a May 4, 2026 evidence audit of 84 retained papers plus industrial anchors from Kimi Agent Swarm, OpenAI Codex, and Anthropic Claude Code, revealing a scale gap between public deployment envelopes and open academic evaluation regimes. Its main contribution is not a new optimizer but a replayable taxonomy and JSON trace schema that should steer future work toward trace-conditioned critics, orchestration-native rewards, and MAS-specific benchmarks.
papers.abstract: As large language model (LLM) agents evolve from isolated tool users into coordinated teams, reinforcement learning (RL) must optimize not only individual actions but also how work is spawned, delegated, communicated, aggregated, and stopped. This paper studies RL for LLM-based multi-agent systems through orchestration traces: temporal interaction graphs whose events include sub-agent spawning, delegation, communication, tool use, return, aggregation, and stopping decisions. Using this lens, we identify three technical axes. First, reward design spans eight families, including orchestration rewards for parallelism speedup, split correctness, and aggregation quality. Second, reward and credit signals attach to eight credit- or signal-bearing units from token to team; explicit counterfactual message-level credit remains especially sparse in our curated pool. Third, orchestration learning decomposes into five sub-decisions: when to spawn, whom to delegate to, how to communicate, how to aggregate, and when to stop. In our curated pool as of May 4, 2026, we found no explicit RL training method for the stopping decision. We connect academic methods to public industrial evidence from Kimi Agent Swarm, OpenAI Codex, and Anthropic Claude Code. The resulting scale gap is a gap between publicly reported deployment envelopes and open academic evaluation regimes, not independent verification of industrial training traces. We release the artifact at https://github.com/xxzcc/awesome-llm-mas-rl, including an 84-entry tagged paper pool, a 32-record exclusion log, scripted corpus statistics, and a minimal JSON schema for replayable orchestration traces.

papers.title: FlexSQL: Flexible Exploration and Execution Make Better Text-to-SQL Agents
papers.summary: FlexSQL: Flexible Exploration and Execution Make Better Text-to-SQL Agents attacks the failure mode of text-to-SQL agents on large analytical warehouses: they must disambiguate schemas, interpret vague terms, and ground filters in real data, yet most systems still lock into a retrieve-once, generate-once, repair-later pipeline that cannot recover from wrong table choices or mistaken column semantics. The paper pivots from static schema linking and post-hoc syntax repair toward on-demand database interaction throughout reasoning: the agent can call GetSchema, GetTableCol, GetColValues, FindRows, SQLExecutor, and PythonExecutor at any point, so schema discovery, value inspection, and verification queries become part of planning rather than a prelude. The core mechanical shift is a two-stage plan-to-program loop that first samples diverse execution plans to cover multiple interpretations, then instantiates each plan in either SQL or Python, with repair that can backtrack from code-level errors to plan-level and schema-level revisions when execution reveals deeper mistakes. This is unusual relative to nearby text-to-SQL work because flexibility spans both exploration and execution, and because Python is treated as a first-class intermediate for multi-step analytical logic rather than a mere fallback. On Spider2-Snow and Spider2-SQLite, using gpt-oss-120b, FlexSQL reaches 65.4% on Spider2-Snow and beats stronger open-source baselines such as gpt-o3 and DeepSeek-R1; integrating the same tool discipline into Claude Code yields over 10% relative gain. The analysis suggests a future where enterprise text-to-SQL is built as an interactive, test-time-search agent over live databases, not a fixed decoder over frozen schema context.
papers.abstract: Text-to-SQL over large analytical databases requires navigating complex schemas, resolving ambiguous queries, and grounding decisions in actual data. Most current systems follow a fixed pipeline where schema elements are retrieved once upfront and the database is only revisited for post-hoc repair, limiting recovery from early mistakes. We present FlexSQL, a text-to-SQL agent whose core design principle is flexible database interaction: the agent can explore schema structure, inspect data values, and run verification queries at any point during reasoning. FlexSQL generates diverse execution plans to cover multiple query interpretations, implements each plan in either SQL or Python depending on the task, and uses a two-tiered repair mechanism that can backtrack from code-level errors to plan-level revisions. On Spider2-Snow, using gpt-oss-120b, FlexSQL achieves a 65.4\% score, outperforming strong open-source baselines that use stronger, larger models such as gpt-o3 and DeepSeek-R1. When integrated into a general-purpose coding agent (as skills in Claude Code), our approach yields over 10\% relative improvement on Spider2-Snow. Further analysis shows that flexible exploration and flexible execution jointly contribute to the effectiveness of our approach, highlighting flexibility as a key design principle. Our code is available at: https://github.com/StringNLPLAB/FlexSQL

papers.title: ViM-Q: Scalable Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Vision Mamba Model Inference on FPGA
papers.summary: ViM-Q: Scalable Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Vision Mamba Model Inference on FPGA attacks the deployment bottleneck that Vision Mamba’s linear-time promise does not automatically translate into edge efficiency: on FPGA, ViM’s linear projections suffer from dynamic activation outliers that break static PTQ, low-bit uniform weight quantization wastes levels on heavy-tailed weights, and the GPU-friendly associative scan used for selective SSMs clashes with FPGA streaming dataflow. The paper pivots from treating quantization as a model-only compression problem toward a full algorithm-hardware co-design problem, replacing generic post-training quantization and scan-based acceleration with hardware-aware quantization plus a streaming accelerator tailored to ViM’s operator mix. The mechanical shift is a W4A8 path that combines dynamic per-token activation quantization with per-channel smoothing and per-block 4-bit APoT weights, then maps all linear layers onto a unified LUT-based engine that turns multiply-heavy arithmetic into precomputed shift-add indexing, while preserving precision through activation pre-shifting and fused dequantization. For the recurrent SSM core, it abandons associative scan in favor of a fine-grained pipelined state-space engine that parallelizes the state dimension but keeps token recurrence intact, with runtime-parameterizable tiling so one design spans ViM variants and input resolutions without recompilation. Implemented on an AMD ZCU102 and evaluated on ViM-t/s/b over ImageNet-1k at batch size 1, ViM-Q reports 4.96× average speedup and 59.8× energy efficiency gain over a quantized RTX 3090 baseline, suggesting a direction where future Mamba systems are co-designed for edge dataflow, not just algorithmic sparsity.
papers.abstract: Vision Mamba (ViM) models offer a compelling efficiency advantage over Transformers by leveraging the linear complexity of State Space Models (SSMs), yet efficiently deploying them on FPGAs remains challenging. Linear layers struggle with dynamic activation outliers that render static quantization ineffective, while uniform quantization fails to capture the weight distribution at low bit-widths. Furthermore, while associative scan accelerates SSMs on GPUs, its memory access patterns are misaligned with the streaming dataflow required by FPGAs. To address these challenges, we present ViM-Q, a scalable algorithm-hardware co-design for end-to-end ViM inference on the edge. We introduce a hardware-aware quantization scheme combining dynamic per-token activation quantization and per-channel smoothing to mitigate outliers, alongside a custom 4-bit per-block Additive Power-of-Two (APoT) weight quantization. The models are deployed on a runtime-parameterizable FPGA accelerator featuring a linear engine employing a Lookup-Table (LUT) unit to replace multiplications with shift-add operations, and a fine-grained pipelined SSM engine that parallelizes the state dimension while preserving sequential recurrence. Crucially, the hardware supports runtime configuration, adapting to diverse dimensions and input resolutions across the ViM family. Implemented on an AMD ZCU102 FPGA, ViM-Q achieves an average 4.96x speedup and 59.8x energy efficiency gain over a quantized NVIDIA RTX 3090 GPU baseline for low-batch inference on ViM-tiny. This co-design shows a viable path for deploying ViM models on resource-constrained edge devices.

papers.title: Principles and Guidelines for Randomized Controlled Trials in AI Evaluation
papers.summary: Principles and Guidelines for Randomized Controlled Trials in AI Evaluation tackles the credibility gap in “human uplift” studies, where AI systems are judged through benchmark scores, leaderboards, or loose audits that do not isolate causal effects on people. The paper challenges the old assumption that model output alone is a sufficient proxy for real-world value, and pivots from benchmark-centric evaluation toward randomized controlled trials that randomize humans to AI-access conditions and measure changes in behavior, performance, and wellbeing. The mechanical shift is a codified evaluation stack: it imports the Shadish et al. four-validity framework, adds a fifth principle for transparency, repeatability, and verification from TOP, and operationalizes all five into 33 AI-specific requirements with rationales, implementation steps, and evidence bases. Unusual relative to nearby AI-eval work, it treats human performance as the primary endpoint, explicitly handles model versioning, contamination and spillover, AI literacy stratification, compliance tracking, cluster randomization, practical significance, and heterogeneity, while also requiring a graded openness ladder from disclosure to sharing, verification, reproducibility, robustness, and replicability. Across software engineering, economics, clinical trials, and psychology, it turns scattered best practices into a design tool, review rubric, and emerging field standard. The guidelines recommend preregistration, pinned model versions, blinded assessment, effect sizes and CIs, and a stricter p<0.005 threshold for novel claims. The result is a governance-oriented blueprint for moving AI evaluation from descriptive claims to causal, auditable evidence, though it remains normative, resource-intensive, and not yet empirically validated.
papers.abstract: This work establishes a foundational framework for standardizing AI evaluation RCTs (sometimes called human uplift studies). Drawing on established experimental practices from disciplines with established RCT traditions, including software engineering, economics, clinical and health sciences, and psychology, we adopt the (Shadish et al., 2002) four-validity framework and extend it with a fifth principle on transparency, repeatability, and verification adapted from the Transparency and Openness Promotion (TOP) Guidelines (Center for Open Science, 2025). We operationalize all five principles into 33 guidelines adapted for AI evaluation RCT contexts, expressed as requirements with rationales, implementation instructions, and evidence bases. We position the principles and guidelines as serving three key roles for AI evaluation RCTs: a design tool for planning studies, an evaluation rubric for assessing existing work, and a blueprint for standard setting as the field converges on norms. Our framework extends prior work by centering evaluation on human performance rather than model output alone, formalizing causal inference through RCT methodology for AI contexts, integrating heterogeneity analysis and practical significance assessment, implementing a graded transparency and repeatability framework, and addressing AI-specific challenges including model versioning, human-AI interaction dynamics, contamination and spillover effects, and equitable impact assessment.

papers.title: H3: A Healthcare Three-Hop Index for Physician Referral Network Prediction
papers.summary: H3: A Healthcare Three-Hop Index for Physician Referral Network Prediction tackles the bottleneck that Medicare referral graphs are sparse, disassortative, and dominated by a few mega-hubs, so standard triadic-closure heuristics and GNN link predictors either miss clinically meaningful indirect collaborations or overfit hub-mediated noise. The paper pivots from the usual assumption that local overlap or latent embeddings are enough toward an explicitly path-based, healthcare-specific inductive bias: referral affinity is inferred through weighted three-hop routes rather than shared neighbors. The mechanical shift is a parameter-free scoring rule over paths vi→vk→vl→vj that combines link-weight amplification (α), degree-based normalization for intermediates and targets (β, γ), and a redundancy penalty p_k^(j)=max(log(1+q_k^(j))^η,p_min) that suppresses repeated hub connectors when many parallel paths are merely incidental patient mobility. H3 is unusual because it stays fully decomposable and auditable: each predicted dyad can be traced to specific intermediary physicians and patient-sharing volumes, unlike black-box embeddings. Evaluated on CMS Physician Shared Patient Patterns across all U.S. states and 30/90/180-day windows, it is tested in both within-period recovery and cross-period temporal-shift prediction, with AUPRC, MRR, NDCG@100, SP@100, and SL@100. Across low-, mid-, and high-degree regimes, H3 consistently outperforms classical heuristics and GNN baselines, especially in sparse settings and early-retrieval metrics, while remaining computationally scalable via sparse-matrix acceleration. The limitation is that topology alone cannot resolve extreme hub ambiguity or cold-start physicians, pointing future work toward hybrid structural-plus-metadata, continuous-time referral modeling.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of physician referral links is essential for optimizing care coordination and reducing fragmentation in healthcare delivery. However, existing computational methods, ranging from triadic closure heuristics to graph neural networks, fail to capture the intrinsic properties of physician referral networks, including sparsity, disassortative degree mixing, and hub-dominated topology. Here, we propose H3, a healthcare three-hop index that addresses these limitations by modeling indirect referral pathways through intermediate physicians, with degree-based normalization and a redundancy penalty to mitigate hub-mediated noise. Using Medicare Physician Shared Patient Patterns data, we evaluate H3 under two complementary prediction regimes: within-period prediction, which assesses recovery of contemporaneous referral links under sparse conditions, and cross-period prediction, which tests robustness to temporal shift as referral windows expand. Across both regimes, H3 consistently outperforms classical heuristics and deep learning-based baselines. Unlike black-box neural network approaches, H3 produces fully decomposable predictions traceable to specific intermediary physicians, offering a transparent and deployable solution for referral network completion.

papers.title: Trees and Graphs with Non Log-concave Dominating Set Sequence via AI Tools
papers.summary: Trees and Graphs with Non Log-concave Dominating Set Sequence via AI Tools attacks the long-open belief that dominating-set counts d0(G),…,dn(G) might at least be unimodal, or more strongly log-concave, for all graphs and especially trees; the paper challenges the older workflow of searching only by hand or by exhaustive enumeration, which is blocked by the computational intractability of dominating-set counting on general graphs. The pivot is from trying to prove log-concavity as a universal structural law toward actively mining discrete counterexamples and then isolating the regimes where log-concavity still survives. The mechanical shift is the use of PatternBoost, a transformer-based reinforcement-learning system that bootstraps its own training data without internet or literature access: graphs and trees are represented as adjacency-bit vectors or Prüfer codes, scored by a reward tied to the local log-concavity defect dk+2dk−dk+1^2, and iteratively improved by greedy edge edits plus transformer-guided proposal generation. Unlike nearby AI-for-math work that leans on pretrained language models, this is de novo combinatorial optimization over exact graph objects. In parallel, the authors give a polytope interpretation of dominating sets, prove the continuous volume analogue is always log-concave by Brunn-Minkowski, and show a large class of legged caterpillars remains log-concave, so the failures are genuinely discrete integer-point phenomena. Their main result is new non-log-concave graphs/trees, plus a family with arbitrarily many violations via a modified Bautista–Ramos construction; PatternBoost found one-break examples but not the multi-break family, pointing toward future AI systems that can discover and navigate highly structured extremal graph constructions.
papers.abstract: We give new examples of graphs and trees with dominating set sequences that are not log-concave. These examples were generated by PatternBoost, a transformer-based reinforcement learning software developed by Charton-Ellenberg-Wagner-Williamson. We also show: for any positive integer $m$, there exists a tree whose dominating set sequence is not log-concave for at least $m$ indices by modifying a similar construction of Bautista-Ramos for the independent set sequence. We show that a large class of caterpillar graphs has log-concave dominating set sequences. A continuous analogue of the sequence is also log-concave for all graphs.

papers.title: Foundations of Riemannian Geometry for Riemannian Optimization: A Monograph with Detailed Derivations
papers.summary: Foundations of Riemannian Geometry for Riemannian Optimization: A Monograph with Detailed Derivations attacks a practical bottleneck in manifold-based optimization: the theory needed for learning on nonlinear spaces is classical, but the key objects are usually presented in coordinate-free shorthand that makes implementation, debugging, and algorithm design difficult. The paper explicitly pivots from abstract differential-geometry expositions toward coordinate-level, matrix-form derivations that can be dropped into numerical code, replacing “elegant but sparse” formulations with step-by-step constructions. Its mechanical shift is to build the pipeline from topology and smooth manifolds through tangent/cotangent spaces, tensor calculus, metric tensors, Levi-Civita connections, curvature, geodesics, and Ricci calculus, then derive the Riemannian gradient, Hessian, exponential and logarithm maps, retractions, and vector transport in computation-ready form. What differentiates it from nearby Riemannian optimization work is not a new optimizer, but a unified derivation stack that specializes the general machinery to Stiefel, Grassmann, and SPD manifolds, including explicit formulas for projections, canonical and affine-invariant metrics, QR-based and polar-style retractions, and differentiated transport rules. The setting is optimization on matrix manifolds arising in machine learning, signal processing, and robotics; the main claimed contribution is to bridge the gap between differential geometry and practical algorithm development. Its limitation is that it adds no new benchmarked method, but it points toward a future where geometric ML is built from standardized, implementation-facing manifold primitives rather than abstract formulas.
papers.abstract: Riemannian geometry provides the fundamental framework for optimization on nonlinear spaces such as matrix manifolds, which arise in machine learning, signal processing, and robotics. While the underlying theory is classical, existing literature often presents results at a high level of abstraction, omitting the detailed coordinate-level derivations required for implementation and algorithm development. This work provides a self-contained and rigorous treatment of the foundations of Riemannian geometry, with a focus on explicit derivations tailored to Riemannian optimization. We systematically develop the key geometric structures -- including tangent and cotangent spaces, tensor calculus, metric tensors, Levi-Civita connections, curvature, and geodesics -- emphasizing step-by-step derivations in coordinates and matrix form. Building on these foundations, we derive the Riemannian gradient, Hessian, exponential map, and retraction in a form suitable for numerical computation. We further specialize these constructions to important matrix manifolds, including the Stiefel, Grassmann, and SPD (Symmetric Positive Definite) manifolds, providing explicit formulas widely used in optimization and geometric machine learning. This monograph develops a unified and implementation-oriented treatment of Riemannian geometry for optimization on manifolds. Its main contribution is the systematic organization and detailed derivation of classical geometric constructions in forms directly usable for algorithm design and numerical implementation. By connecting coordinate-level differential geometry with matrix-manifold formulas, the monograph bridges the gap between abstract theory and practical computation, and provides a reference for researchers and practitioners working in Riemannian optimization and related fields.

papers.title: Entanglement is Half the Story: Post-Selection vs. Partial Traces
papers.summary: Entanglement is Half the Story: Post-Selection vs. Partial Traces attacks a specific bottleneck in quantum machine learning tensor networks: the field has usually compared classical tensor networks and quantum tensor networks mainly by bond dimension and entanglement capacity, while leaving the actual reduction mechanism—post-selection versus partial trace—underexamined. The paper pivots from “more qubits, bigger bond dimension” toward a resource view in which the key control knob is how much quantum constraint is enforced on Hilbert-space reduction. Its mechanical shift is to define a unified hybrid tensor network (HTN) in which an isometric core is paired with diagonal reduction operators, then tune those operators between the two edge cases: identities for quantum channels with partial traces, and one-hot reductions for classical-like post-selection. This lets classical tensor networks be inferred on a quantum computer through ancilla post-selection, and it turns limited post-selection into a trainable hyperparameter, implemented either as a threshold t or weighting w in the normalized loss. Compared with nearby hybrid TN work, the architecture is not a loose classical-quantum stack but a single formalism that makes the interpolation mathematically explicit. On an MPS-style model, the Iris dataset, and a 7×7 MNIST binary task, the post-selection parameter dominated bond dimension in affecting training and test behavior: small t improved training but induced overfitting, while h=0 on MNIST reached 99.68% testing accuracy and h=1 collapsed into a barren plateau. The paper points toward QML that treats post-selection as an allocatable control resource and uses it to diagnose encodings and failure modes.
papers.abstract: While tensor networks have their traditional application in simulating quantum systems, in the recent decade they have gathered interest as machine learning models. We combine the experience from both fields and derive how quantum constraints placed on a tensor network manifest a change in capabilities. To this end, we employ a method of inference of classical tensor networks on a quantum computer to define a hybrid architecture. This hybrid tensor network is a practical unified framework for it's classical and quantum tensor network edge cases. We identify post-selection as the important property on which this interpolation hinges. The amount of post-selection corresponds to the level to which quantum constraints are enforced on the tensor network. On this basis, we propose a new hyperparameter which controls the transition between the hybrid and the quantum tensor network. In the comparison of classical and quantum tensor networks it complements the bond dimension. Quantum machine learning is improved by using the hyperparameter to allocate the practically limited post-selection to the quantum model in a trainable manner.

papers.title: From Experimental Limits to Physical Insight: A Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Interpreting Searches Beyond the Standard Model
papers.summary: From Experimental Limits to Physical Insight: A Retrieval-Augmented Multi-Agent Framework for Interpreting Searches Beyond the Standard Model attacks the bottleneck that BSM collider results are split across prose, tables, HEPData records, and exclusion plots, forcing physicists to manually reconcile heterogeneous evidence before they can compare limits or reinterpret searches. It challenges the old workflow that treats literature retrieval as text-only PDF question answering and assumes a single LLM can infer physics from narrative summaries alone; instead it pivots from document search toward evidence-grounded scientific reconstruction. The mechanical shift is HEP-CoPilot’s multimodal pipeline: LaTeX papers and HEPData CSVs are decomposed into normalized evidence chunks, embedded into a PostgreSQL/pgvector store, then routed by a Mission Control/Router pair through specialized Text, Table, Figure, Equation, Terminologist, Reasoning, Plotter, and Synthesis agents orchestrated with LangGraph. Unusually, the system operates directly on the numerical measurements underlying published exclusion contours, reconstructing observed 95% CL limit curves and overlaying results across analyses rather than only summarizing them. Evaluated on recent CMS searches for heavy stable charged particles, displaced-jet long-lived particles, and stop searches at 13 TeV/13.6 TeV, it could recover limits, identify lifetime-sensitivity gaps around intermediate cτ regimes, and compare channel scales across papers, with LLM-as-a-Judge scoring favoring the grounded multi-agent outputs over direct PDF prompting. The main tradeoff is limited corpus scale and dependence on structured HEPData, pointing toward future AI systems that blend figure digitization, uncertainty-aware verification, and larger domain-specific reasoning agents for full scientific reinterpretation loops.
papers.abstract: Modern searches for physics beyond the Standard Model produce rapidly expanding literature containing heterogeneous information, including textual analyses, numerical datasets, and graphical exclusion limits.
