Integrating these distributed sources remains a time-consuming and manual process for physicists. We present HEP-CoPilot, a retrieval-augmented multi-agent AI framework for the exploration and interpretation of high-energy physics literature. The system unifies textual information from publications, structured experimental data from HEPData, and reconstructed physics plots within a multimodal retrieval and reasoning architecture. By combining retrieval-augmented language models with coordinated agent workflows, it enables evidence-grounded reasoning over experimental analyses and structured interpretation of collider results. We evaluate the framework on recent CMS searches for physics beyond the Standard Model. Case studies show that HEP-CoPilot can retrieve relevant measurements, reconstruct exclusion limits directly from HEPData records, and perform cross-paper comparisons of experimental constraints. This enables consistent, physics-aware comparison across analyses without manual data integration. These results demonstrate that retrieval-augmented AI systems can function as scientific co-pilots for particle physics, facilitating navigation of complex literature, structuring heterogeneous evidence, and accelerating the interpretation pipeline for new physics searches.

papers.title: TRACED: In vivo imaging of extracellular intrinsic diffusivity, tortuosity, cell size distribution and cell density in human glioma patients
papers.summary: TRACED: In vivo imaging of extracellular intrinsic diffusivity, tortuosity, cell size distribution and cell density in human glioma patients attacks a specific diffusion-MRI bottleneck: at intermediate diffusion times, there is no reliable analytical model for how extracellular signal time dependence emerges from realistic tissue packing, so standard two-compartment workflows can estimate cellularity and mean radius but leave extracellular diffusivity ill-defined or confounded by cell-boundary hindrance. The paper pivots from assuming uniform cells and a free or static extracellular compartment toward jointly inferring a lognormal cell-size distribution, intracellular volume fraction, extracellular intrinsic diffusivity, and extracellular tortuosity from multi-b, multi-Δ acquisitions. The mechanical shift is a simulation-driven biophysical forward model over randomly packed spheres, where Monte Carlo signals train neural-network surrogates for intracellular distribution effects and for time-dependent extracellular diffusivity; these differentiable surrogates are then embedded in Sim2PINN, a two-stage physics-informed transfer-learning loop that warm-starts from large synthetic dictionaries and self-supervisedly adapts to noisy patient voxels with Rician-aware fitting. In eight mixed-grade glioma patients scanned on a high-gradient Connectome system, TRACED produced full-brain parameter maps in seconds and fit image-localized histology in two cases, while simulations showed lower bias than the simple two-compartment model, especially when cell-size variability would otherwise inflate radius estimates. The unusual contribution is treating extracellular diffusivity as a biologically meaningful intrinsic parameter rather than a residual fit term, pointing toward clinically optimized diffusion protocols and more mechanistic biomarker discovery for tumor infiltration, ECM remodeling, and treatment response.
papers.abstract: The lack of analytical models describing diffusion time dependence at intermediate time scales in complex tissue microstructure limits the accurate quantification of extracellular diffusivity and tissue microstructure. We introduce TRACED, a biophysical model that incorporates diffusion time dependence in cell distributions to quantify pathologically-relevant properties in solid tumors. Neural networks were trained on Monte Carlo diffusion simulations using sphere distribution-based geometries to enable the rapid computation of time-dependent diffusion MRI signals in cell populations of variable cell size. Model sensitivity and fit performance were assessed via simulation. Diffusion data from eight mixed-grade glioma patients was fitted using the TRACED model. Data fitting was performed using a novel physics-informed transfer learning pipeline, Sim2PINN. In two patients, cell size measurements were compared directly with image-localized histology. Simulation results indicate improved parameter estimation compared to the simple two-compartment model. TRACED enabled the simultaneous in vivo quantification of intracellular volume fraction, cell size distribution, extracellular intrinsic diffusivity, and tortuosity in glioma patients. Neural network implementations of diffusion time-dependence and tortuosity showed behavior consistent with coarse-graining and effective medium theory, respectively. Future work will explore the clinical utility of TRACED parameters in additional patients.

papers.title: ARA: Agentic Reproducibility Assessment For Scalable Support Of Scientific Peer-Review
papers.summary: ARA: Agentic Reproducibility Assessment For Scalable Support Of Scientific Peer-Review attacks the bottleneck that reproducibility review has become too procedurally rich for human referees to reconstruct at modern publication scale: the missing dependencies, preprocessing choices, parameterizations, execution conditions, and result-to-evidence links that determine whether a paper can actually be reproduced. It challenges the old workflow of checklist-based transparency screening or full execution-based replication packages by pivoting from artifact-centered verification toward document-level structured reasoning over the paper itself. The mechanical shift is an LLM-agent pipeline that first extracts a directed workflow graph with four stages—sources, methods, experiments, sinks—grounded in literal quotes and schema-constrained outputs, then assigns ordinal reconstructability scores to nodes and aggregates them into orthogonal content and structural reproducibility scores, with geometric-mean composition and explicit penalties for disconnected inputs, orphan outputs, dangling references, broken source-to-sink paths, and fragmented subgraphs. Compared with nearby peer-review or replication agents, ARA is unusual in treating reproducibility as graph reconstruction rather than code execution, and in remaining domain-agnostic without requiring repositories, web retrieval, or replication reports. Evaluated on 213 ReScience C articles, plus ReproBench and GoldStandardDB, it yields about 61% accuracy overall and the best reported scores on the two external benchmarks, while remaining stable across LLMs and temperatures. The limitation is that methods and experiments remain harder than sources and sinks, but the paper points toward next-generation peer review that uses agentic workflow tracing to triage reproducibility risk at scale.
papers.abstract: Scientific peer review increasingly struggles to assess reproducibility at the scale and complexity of modern research output. Evaluating reproducibility requires reconstructing experimental dependencies, methodological choices, data flows, and result-generating procedures, which often exceeds what human reviewers can provide. Agentic Reproducibility Assessment (ARA) formalizes reproducibility assessment as a structured reasoning task over scientific documents. Given a paper, ARA extracts a directed workflow graph linking sources, methods, experiments, and outputs, then evaluates its reconstructability using structural and content-based scores for reproducibility assessments. Experiments on 213 ReScience C articles - the largest cross-domain benchmark of human-validated computational reproducibility studies considered to date - demonstrate ARA's generalizability and consistent workflow reconstruction and assessment across LLMs, model temperatures, and scientific domains. ARA achieves ~61% accuracy on three benchmarks, and the highest accuracy reported on ReproBench (60.71% vs. 36.84%) and GoldStandardDB (61.68% vs. 43.56%), highlighting its potential to complement human review at scale and enabling next-generation peer review. Code and Data available: https://github.com/AndresLaverdeMarin/agentic_reproducibility_assessment.

papers.title: Biological Spatial Priors Regularize Foundation Model Representations for Cross-Site MSI Generalization in Colorectal Cancer
papers.summary: Biological Spatial Priors Regularize Foundation Model Representations for Cross-Site MSI Generalization in Colorectal Cancer attacks the clinical bottleneck that MSI prediction from H&E whole-slide images often collapses when a model trained at one institution is deployed on slides from another scanner, stain protocol, or site. It challenges the assumption that stronger pathology foundation-model features alone solve generalization, showing that UNI2-h and Virchow2 still carry site-specific texture alongside MSI morphology. The pivot is from treating slide classification as a pure representation-scaling problem toward injecting disease-specific spatial biology as an inductive bias. The mechanical shift is simple but distinct: tile features are augmented before TransMIL self-attention with a peripheral distance scalar that proxies proximity to the tumor invasive margin, where the Crohn’s-like lymphocytic reaction concentrates in MSI-H colorectal cancer, and with an alternative local immune neighborhood log LYM/TUM ratio computed from nearby tiles via an NCT-CRC-HE-100K probe. This differs from generic positional encoding because the coordinate signal is biologically motivated rather than merely geometric. On TCGA-COAD (137 slides), then external TCGA-READ evaluation without retraining (50 slides, almost all MSS), TransMIL+peripheral distance raised internal AUC slightly and pushed READ MSS specificity to 1.000 with UNI2-h, outperforming the strongest reference; the local immune prior was more encoder-dependent and less consistent cross-site. The paper suggests a direction where computational pathology shifts from larger encoders to biologically grounded spatial regularizers that stabilize attention around conserved tissue architecture instead of site artifacts.
papers.abstract: Predicting microsatellite instability (MSI) status from routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) whole slide images (WSIs) offers a practical alternative to molecular testing, but models trained at one institution tend to generalize poorly to slides acquired at a different site. Foundation model representations, despite their generality, still encode site-specific texture alongside the conserved biological morphology underlying MSI. We investigate whether tile-level spatial priors derived from known MSI histology can guide these representations toward more site-invariant features. We introduce a biologically motivated spatial prior based on peripheral distance encoding, reflecting the Crohn's-like peripheral lymphocytic reaction at the tumor invasive margin, and evaluate a secondary local immune neighborhood encoding reflecting the lymphocyte-to-tumor ratio in each tile's immediate spatial neighborhood. Both priors are injected into a TransMIL aggregator before self-attention, allowing the transformer to integrate spatial biological context with UNI2-h or Virchow2 features across all attention layers. We evaluate six foundation model and MIL aggregator combinations as a reference, then assess the effect of each spatial prior. Training on TCGA-COAD (137 slides) and evaluating externally on TCGA-READ (50 slides) without retraining, peripheral distance encoding achieves MSI AUC 0.959 +/- 0.012 on COAD and MSS specificity 1.000 on READ, compared to 0.957 and 0.939 for the strongest reference configuration. Local immune neighborhood encoding achieves comparable internal AUC but lower cross-site specificity, suggesting margin proximity encodes a more site-invariant biological signal than local immune density. Results suggest biologically grounded spatial priors act as regularizers that reduce reliance on site-specific imaging patterns.

papers.title: The Design and Composition of Structural Causal Decision Processes
papers.summary: The Design and Composition of Structural Causal Decision Processes tackles the bottleneck of modeling decision-making in computing and digital-economy systems that are modular, resource-constrained, and time-discounted, where standard MDP/POMDP workflows silently assume rational belief formation and a fixed, exogenous discount factor. The paper pivots from state-centric dynamic programming toward a Pearlian causal decision formalism that can represent decision constraints, open root variables with no specified distribution or structural equation, and endogenous limits on cognition and patience. Its core mechanical shift is to extend Structural Causal Influence Models into Structural Causal Decision Models (SCDMs), then make them composable: subsystems can be glued along “bridge” variables and, under an orthomodularity/d-separation condition, solved by backward induction over sequential decompositions. Building on that, a Structural Causal Decision Process (SCDP) turns a recurring SCDM plus a discount variable into a dynamic stochastic optimization problem, allowing the model to endogenize memory formation and variable discounting rather than hiding them inside a Bayesian filter. Unusually, the framework is explicitly more expressive than POMDPs because it does not require the agent to maintain rational beliefs; the agent’s limited information and memory can be part of the model itself. The paper illustrates the formalism with two-period consumption, habit formation, and consumption-plus-portfolio allocation, and shows that decomposition reduces solution cost from naive policy search to smaller subproblems, with dynamic iterations benefiting similarly. The result points toward composable, causally grounded simulators for policy, mechanism design, digital twins, and multi-agent resource-rational modeling.
papers.abstract: We present two new classes of causal models of decision-making agents. Our approach is motivated by the needs of modeling the economics of computing systems. These systems are composed of subsystems and can exhibit endogenous limits on cognitive resources and value discounting. Structural Causal Decision Models (SCDMs) expand on Structural Causal Influence Models. Like SCIMs, they explicitly represent the causal relationships between model variables and the payoffs of agent decisions. Additionally, agent decisions can be constrained by their causal antecedents, and SCDMs can have open root variables for which no probability distribution or structural equation is given. We show that SCDMs have a well-defined and computationally useful property of composability. Building on SCDMs, we then define a Structural Causal Decision Process (SCDP) as a recurring SCDM with a discount variable. SCDPs benefit from the useful composition properties of SCDMs. Moreover, SCDPs are strictly more expressive than POMDPs because they do not assume rational belief formation. Indeed, an SCDP can endogenously model the memory-formation process, and is thus useful for modeling resource rational agents in dynamic settings. SCDPs are also capable of modeling variable discounting, a tool used widely in social scientific modeling. We pose that SCDPs are a useful framework for policy simulation for the digital economy, mechanism design for information systems, and digital twin modeling of cyberinfrastructure.

papers.title: Dimensionality-Aware Anomaly Detection in Learned Representations of Self-Supervised Speech Models
papers.summary: Dimensionality-Aware Anomaly Detection in Learned Representations of Self-Supervised Speech Models attacks the blind spot in S3M robustness analysis: standard similarity metrics and global rank summaries can say representations changed, but not how perturbations locally deform the learned manifold or whether that deformation predicts ASR collapse. The paper pivots from comparing layer representations as whole tensors toward measuring layer-wise local geometry via Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID), turning the question from “are features aligned?” to “does neighborhood expansion signal a corrupted speech manifold?” Its GRIDS pipeline runs clean LibriSpeech utterances plus matched target-SNR benign corruptions (Gaussian, babble, competing speech) and ℓ2-PGD adversarial examples, with both MSE-targeted and ASR-aligned CTC attacks, through WavLM and wav2vec 2.0. The mechanical shift is a kNN-based, layer-by-layer LID estimator over frame embeddings, pooled with harmonic means into condition-level trajectories and then compressed into a 12-dimensional utterance signature for logistic-regression anomaly detection. What is unusual is that it treats geometry itself as the diagnostic signal, not transcripts, logits, or final-layer embeddings; and it separates local distortion from global dimensional collapse. The main finding is that perturbations raise LID, adversarial inputs often preserve early-layer elevation at higher SNR, and LID increases co-occur with WER rises; the 12-feature detector reaches AUROC 0.78–1.00 on adversarial-vs-benign classification. This points toward transcript-free monitoring, geometry-aware safety auditing, and multi-scale robustness probes for future speech foundation models.
papers.abstract: Self-supervised speech models (S3Ms) achieve strong downstream performance, yet their learned representations remain poorly understood under natural and adversarial perturbations. Prior studies rely on representation similarity or global dimensionality, offering limited visibility into local geometric changes. We ask: how do perturbations deform local geometry, and do these shifts track downstream automatic speech recognition (ASR) degradation? To address this, we present GRIDS, a framework using Local Intrinsic Dimensionality (LID) across layer-wise representations in WavLM and wav2vec 2.0. We find that LID increases for all low signal-to noise ratio (SNR) perturbations and diverges at high SNR: benign noise converges toward the clean profile, while adversarial inputs retain early-layer LID elevation. We show LID elevation co-occurs with increased WER, and that layer-wise LID features enable anomaly detection (AUROC 0.78-1.00), opening the door to transcript-free monitoring in S3Ms.

papers.title: Universality in Deep Neural Networks: An approach via the Lindeberg exchange principle
papers.summary: Universality in Deep Neural Networks: An approach via the Lindeberg exchange principle attacks the bottleneck in infinite-width theory that universality for deep random networks had been mostly qualitative or confined to Gaussian weights, leaving no sharp quantitative control for general weight laws in strong metrics. The paper pivots from the usual “prove the Gaussian process limit” workflow toward a layerwise exchange analysis that tracks the finite-width network directly and measures its deviation from the Gaussian limit in 2-Wasserstein distance. The mechanical shift is a deep-network Lindeberg principle: weights are successively replaced by Gaussian variables with matched first two moments, and the recursive structure of the network is used as a smoothing amplifier to prevent the dimension blowup that would kill a naive Taylor expansion. For zero biases this requires high activation regularity, while with Gaussian biases the extra noise provides enough smoothing to reduce the smoothness demand to C^3 and even recover total variation and W1 bounds. In the fully connected setting with hidden widths n_1,…,n_L and finite third-moment weights, the authors prove quantitative convergence to the infinite-width Gaussian process with rates of order 1/sqrt(n_L)+(\sum_{k<L} n_k^{-1/2})^{1/2}? no, precisely a dominant 1/sqrt(n_L)+(\sum_{k=1}^{L-1} n_k^{-1/2})^{1/4} term, and show that Gaussian biases restore optimal 1/sqrt(n_k) rates in weaker metrics. This points toward a research direction where universality is studied as a controlled exchange process, not just an asymptotic limit, enabling metric-sensitive theory for broader random-initialization regimes.
papers.abstract: We consider the infinite-width limit of a fully connected deep neural network with general weights, and we prove quantitative general bounds on the $2$-Wasserstein distance between the network and its infinite-width Gaussian limit, under appropriate regularity assumptions on the activation function. Our main tool is a Lindeberg principle for Deep Neural Networks, which we use to successively replace the weights on each layer by Gaussian random variables.

papers.title: Static Analysis of Recursive SHACL
papers.summary: Static Analysis of Recursive SHACL attacks the missing compile-time reasoning layer for RDF shape specifications: instead of asking whether one graph validates one document, it asks whether every graph that validates one SHACL document must also validate another, now including recursive shape definitions and targets rather than only bare shape expressions. That pivot moves the field from validation-centric, instance-specific checking toward data-independent containment/equivalence analysis over entire document specifications, challenging the old assumption that recursive SHACL can be compared with the same simplicity as nonrecursive constraints. The mechanical shift is semantic: under supported and stable model semantics, implication becomes undecidable even for the ALCIO fragment, showing that multiple possible shape assignments make containment computationally uncontrollable; under well-founded semantics, however, each graph has a unique assignment, and the paper translates SHACL documents into the full hybrid µ-calculus, with positive derivations encoded by a least fixed point and unfounded negative information by nested greatest fixed points. This translation is then coupled to a worst-case optimal automata-based decision procedure via two-way alternating parity tree automata, yielding single-exponential decision time for shape implication and satisfiability, plus decidability of finite variants. The result is unusual in making a clear semantic fault line between nondeterministic brave semantics and a unique-model semantics, while also exposing a novel bridge between well-founded models and fixed-point modal logic. The paper points toward richer recursive shape languages, tighter direct algorithms, and transferable reasoning techniques for Datalog-with-negation and ontology-linked graph constraints.
papers.abstract: SHACL (Shapes Constraint Language) expresses constraints on RDF data by means of so-called shapes. Its central service is validation: verifying whether a data graph complies with a SHACL document. But so far, there are no static analysis services to compare documents. In this paper, we study the following problem: decide whether all graphs that validate one SHACL document also validate another. Unlike previous works that have considered the implication of shape expressions only, we consider documents comprising (recursive) shape definitions and targets. We show that implication (a.k.a. containment) is undecidable under the supported and the stable model semantics, even for the fragment that uses the description logic ALCIO for shape expressions. Under the well-founded semantics, in surprising contrast, it is decidable in single exponential time. Our key technical contribution is a translation of SHACL under the well-founded semantics into the full hybrid mu-calculus, revealing a novel link between well-founded models and a fixed point modal logic, and a worst-case optimal automata-based decision procedure.

papers.title: Multi-fidelity surrogates for mechanics of composites: from co-kriging to multi-fidelity neural networks
papers.summary: Multi-fidelity surrogates for mechanics of composites: from co-kriging to multi-fidelity neural networks addresses the bottleneck that composite mechanics prediction is dominated by expensive repeated high-fidelity simulations and experiments across a vast design space of layup, structure, loading, and manufacturing history, where anisotropy, hierarchical coupling, nonlinear damage, and process-induced variability create regime-dependent fidelity gaps. The review implicitly challenges the older workflow that treats each scale or solver as a separate model, or assumes a simple linear correction from cheap to expensive data, and pivots from “single-fidelity, single-scale” surrogate thinking toward explicit information fusion across heterogeneous fidelity levels. The core mechanical shift is the replacement of isolated regression with structured cross-fidelity coupling: classical Gaussian-process and Kriging families, especially co-kriging, coregionalization, autoregressive formulations, nonlinear autoregressive GP, and multi-fidelity deep GP, are organized as ways to model discrepancy, cross-fidelity correlation, and uncertainty while remaining sample-efficient; the second half then turns toward multi-fidelity neural networks, including fused architectures and transfer-learning schemes that pretrain on abundant low-cost data and recalibrate with sparse trusted high-fidelity labels. What distinguishes the paper is its composite-specific lens: it separates forward prediction, inverse optimization, and workflow integration, and emphasizes failure regimes where simulations and experiments diverge because damage evolution and manufacturing history break fidelity alignment. The review’s result is a research map showing that future composite AI will likely be hybrid, uncertainty-aware, and regime-adaptive, with active selection of fidelity sources and physics-informed fusion replacing brute-force data accumulation.
papers.abstract: Composite materials exhibit strongly hierarchical and anisotropic properties governed by coupled mechanisms spanning constituents, plies, laminates, structures, and manufacturing history. This intrinsic complexity makes predictive modeling of composites expensive, because repeated experiments and high-fidelity simulations are needed to cover large design spaces of material, structure, and manufacturing. Multi-fidelity surrogate modeling addresses this challenge by combining abundant, less expensive data with limited high-accuracy data to recover reliable high-fidelity predictions. This review presents a structured overview of multi-fidelity modeling for composite mechanics, covering Gaussian-process or Kriging-based methods, including co-Kriging, coregionalization models, autoregressive formulations, nonlinear autoregressive Gaussian processes, multi-fidelity deep Gaussian processes, and multi-fidelity neural networks. Their distinctions are examined in terms of cross-fidelity correlation, discrepancy representation, uncertainty quantification, and scalability. Selected examples of their applications to composites are introduced according to the roles that multi-fidelity surrogates play in engineering problems, including forward prediction for rapid exploration of material design spaces, inverse optimization for composite parameter identification and design search under limited high-fidelity access, and workflow integration, where heterogeneous data sources, constraints, and validation requirements determine model utility. Open question discussions highlight recurring challenges specific to composites, such as regime-dependent fidelity gaps associated with nonlinear damage and manufacturing history, mismatches between simulations and experiments, and uncertainty propagation across multi-fidelity models.

papers.title: Stable Blanket with Hidden Variables and Cycles
papers.summary: Stable Blanket with Hidden Variables and Cycles tackles the failure of stabilized regression’s graphical story when the data-generating graph is no longer a fully observed DAG: latent common causes and feedback loops can both distort the Markov blanket of the response and invalidate the predictor set that is stable across environments. The paper pivots away from the standard “parents-children-spouses in an acyclic SCM” assumption toward causal graphs with hidden variables and cycles, using ADMGs with m-separation for latent-variable settings and DG/DMG models with σ-separation for cyclic settings, then unifying them in a combined hidden-variable-plus-cycle regime. The core mechanical shift is not a new estimator but a new separation-and-pruning calculus: it introduces intervened sub-districts, district colliders, and SCC/component-district analogues to identify which observed predictors must be removed as intervention-sensitive descendants and which remain in the stable frontier. Unlike nearby work that characterizes only the ordinary Markov blanket, this paper constructs intervention-stable predictor sets under multiple interventions, proves when they are minimal or unique, and even relaxes the no-intervention-on-dis(Y) assumption by giving a sufficient collider condition for stability. In the hidden-variable case, the stable blanket becomes the complement of collider descendants in the latent projection; in the cyclic case, SCCs replace local neighborhoods as the basic unit. The main implication is a more realistic graphical target for invariant prediction in biology, neuroscience, and econometrics, and the future direction is toward identifiability and data-driven recovery of these stable blankets under faithfulness.
papers.abstract: Stabilized regression aims to identify a set of predictors whose conditional relationship with a response variable remains invariant across different environments. Existing graphical characterizations of the stable blanket are mainly developed for structural causal models (SCMs) without hidden variables or causal cycles. However, latent variables and feedback relationships naturally arise in many applications, and they can change both the Markov blanket and the set of predictors that remain stable under interventions. This paper studies stable blankets in graphical causal models with hidden variables, causal cycles, and both features simultaneously. For models with hidden variables, we use acyclic directed mixed graphs (ADMGs) and $m$-separation to characterize the Markov blanket and to construct intervention-stable predictor sets. We introduce the notion of an intervened sub-district and use it to describe how interventions may affect districts connected to the response. For models with cycles, we work with directed graphs (DGs) and directed mixed graphs (DMGs) together with $σ$-separation, treating strongly connected components (SCCs) as the basic graphical units. We then combine these ideas to analyze models with both hidden variables and cycles. The main results give graphical characterizations of Markov blankets, stable frontiers, and stable blankets in these generalized settings. In particular, we identify conditions under which the response is conditionally independent of intervention variables given a suitable predictor set, and we describe when such sets are minimal or unique. These results extend the graphical interpretation of stabilized regression beyond acyclic fully observed models.

papers.title: Adaptive Estimation and Inference in Semi-parametric Heterogeneous Clustered Multitask Learning via Neyman Orthogonality
papers.summary: Adaptive Estimation and Inference in Semi-parametric Heterogeneous Clustered Multitask Learning via Neyman Orthogonality attacks the failure mode where clustered multitask learning breaks once each task carries its own high-dimensional or even infinite-dimensional nuisance structure: naive pooling contaminates target estimation, while single-task double machine learning cannot borrow strength across related tasks. The paper pivots from homogeneous, aligned-feature multitask assumptions toward a semiparametric clustering view in which only the low-dimensional target parameters share latent groups, and nuisances remain fully task-local. The mechanical shift is a two-stage adaptive fused orthogonal estimator: first, task-specific pilot fits provide coarse similarity scores, then pairwise fusion penalties are calibrated from those pilots and used inside a joint optimization over Neyman-orthogonal losses with sample splitting/cross-fitting. Unlike fixed fusion or centroid clustering, this keeps all cross-task sharing in the target space while orthogonalization suppresses nuisance-estimation bias, letting heterogeneous tasks live in different feature spaces and estimation pipelines. The theory proves exact latent cluster recovery, pooled parametric rates scaling with cluster size, and asymptotic normality matching an oracle that knows the true partition, with a residual bias term only when within-cluster heterogeneity is too large. Simulations on partially linear models, ATE, and DID, plus a U.S. residential energy elasticity study, show stable cluster recovery and sharper inference than strong multitask baselines. This points toward a broader research direction where multitask learning becomes inference-aware, adaptive to unknown task structure, and robust to semiparametric nuisance complexity.
papers.abstract: We study clustered multitask learning in a semiparametric setting where tasks share a latent cluster structure in their target parameters but exhibit heterogeneous, potentially infinite-dimensional nuisance components. Such heterogeneity poses a major challenge for existing multitask learning methods, which typically rely on aligned feature spaces or homogeneous task structures. To address this challenge, we propose an adaptive fused orthogonal estimator that integrates Neyman-orthogonal losses with data-driven pairwise fusion penalties. Our framework leverages task-specific pilot estimates to calibrate the fusion penalties and combines adaptive aggregation with orthogonalization to mitigate the impact of nuisance-parameter estimation error. Theoretically, we show that the proposed estimator achieves exact recovery of the latent clustering with high probability and attains pooled parametric convergence rates proportional to cluster size. Moreover, we establish asymptotic normality and show that, asymptotically, our estimator matches the performance of an oracle procedure that knows the true clustering in advance. Empirically, we show that the proposed method consistently outperforms strong baselines in various simulation setups. A real-world application to U.S. residential energy consumption demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach in uncovering meaningful regional clustering in electricity price elasticity, showcasing the efficacy of our method.

papers.title: Extrapolation in Statistical Learning with Extreme Value Theory
papers.summary: Extrapolation in Statistical Learning with Extreme Value Theory attacks the bottleneck that ordinary empirical-risk learning is built for interpolation under Q=P, bounded supports, or sub-Gaussian tails, and therefore becomes unreliable exactly where many AI systems now matter most: record-breaking weather, rare medical or industrial failures, heavy-tailed responses, and out-of-distribution prediction. The paper pivots from bulk-centered machine learning and classical domain adaptation toward a tail-first statistical learning view in which extrapolation is justified by asymptotic tail structure rather than by extra target-domain samples. Its mechanical shift is to replace generic neural or nonparametric flexibility with extreme-value representations: univariate peaks-over-threshold and GEV/GPD approximations, multivariate regular variation, conditional extremes, and geometric limit-set descriptions, all typically after Pareto or Laplace marginal transforms. Across supervised and unsupervised tasks, the review emphasizes learning on the “effective” sample size k≪n from exceedances, angular classifiers for extreme covariates, tail-quantile regression via Weissman/GPD extrapolation, weighted GPD likelihoods with forests/boosting/NNs, lasso-style angular regression, tail conformal prediction, and generative models that separately learn radius and angle for extremes. Unusually, it treats anomaly detection, GenAI, rare-event classification, and dimension reduction as one connected research vector governed by asymptotic dependence versus independence. The main implication is that some non-asymptotic guarantees can scale with k, but the field still depends on simplifying tail assumptions and lacks robust evaluation protocols. This points toward future AI systems that fuse large-scale neural models with explicit tail geometry, uncertainty control, and tail-aware validation.
papers.abstract: Extreme value theory provides rigorous theory and statistical tools for extrapolation in machine learning, particularly in settings where traditional methods struggle due to data scarcity in the tails. A broad range of tasks benefit from these advances, including regression and classification beyond the training data, extreme quantile regression, supervised and unsupervised dimension reduction, generative artificial intelligence and anomaly detection. This review synthesizes recent developments in these fields at the intersection of statistical learning and extreme value theory, with a focus on principled methods based on asymptotically motivated representations of the tail of univariate and multivariate distributions. We consider different theoretical frameworks for both asymptotically dependent and independent data and discuss how they translate into efficient statistical methods for extrapolation to extreme regions. By addressing both theoretical and practical aspects, we offer a comprehensive overview of the state-of-the-art in this quickly evolving field, and identify promising directions for future research.

papers.title: MIRA: A Score for Conditional Distribution Accuracy and Model Comparison
papers.summary: MIRA: A Score for Conditional Distribution Accuracy and Model Comparison attacks the bottleneck that conditional generative models and Bayesian posteriors are usually judged with coverage plots, SBC ranks, or Bayes factors that either require multiple ground-truth draws, train classifiers, or make evidence computation the hard part. It pivots from frequentist region-by-region pass/fail diagnostics toward a scalar, sample-based calibration score that can rank models using only joint samples (x,y) from the true process and posterior samples from a candidate. The mechanical shift is to build randomized regions R around a candidate draw yr via a center c and a distance metric, count candidate mass n inside R, and ask for the Bayesian probability p(k|n) that the single true sample y* also lands in R; under the null, equal-mass regions plus a Uniform[0,1] mass prior yield a closed-form Laplace-rule expression, whose expectation defines the Mira score with a theoretical calibrated value 2/3 and variance 1/18, and a lower bound of 1/2. Unlike nearby work, it never trains a neural critic and stays usable in high dimensions by reducing comparison to one-dimensional mass counts. Experiments on toy posteriors, MNIST conditional generation, and strong-lensing inference show it separates overconfident, underconfident, biased, and misspecified priors/noise, while also enabling direct posterior validation for Bayesian model comparison. Its limitation is that the basic score is necessary rather than sufficient, but it points toward scalable posterior auditing and model ranking in simulation-based inference.
papers.abstract: We introduce Mira, a sample-based score for assessing the accuracy of a candidate conditional distribution using only joint samples from the true data-generating process. Relying on the principle that distributions coincide if they assign equal probability mass to all regions, we derive an analytic expression for the Mira statistic, whose average defines the Mira score. This formulation further allows us to compute theoretical reference values and uncertainty estimates when the candidate distribution matches the true one. This framework enables model comparison by quantifying the alignment between the conditional distribution of a candidate model and the true data generating process. Consequently, Mira enables Bayesian model comparison through direct posterior validation, bypassing the challenging evidence computation. We demonstrate its effectiveness across several toy problems and Bayesian inference tasks.

papers.title: The Causal Description Gap: Information-Theoretic Separations Across Pearl's Hierarchy
papers.summary: The Causal Description Gap: Information-Theoretic Separations Across Pearl's Hierarchy asks a quantitative version of Pearl’s hierarchy question: not whether observational, interventional, and counterfactual answers differ, but how many additional bits are needed to specify higher-rung answer oracles once lower-rung answers are fixed. It challenges the usual identifiability workflow that treats non-identification as a yes/no obstruction and instead pivots from “can lower-rung data determine the causal query?” toward “what is the residual description length of the missing causal mechanism?” The mechanical shift is to measure query-class description length via Kolmogorov complexity of the answer oracle induced by an SCM, then prove lower bounds by counting distinct higher-rung oracles compatible with the same lower-rung oracle. The paper’s concrete constructions are binary acyclic SCM families: rooted trees with constant observational description length but Θ(n log n) interventional gaps, and a bipartite copy/AND schema where all 2^{m^2} graphs share the same passive distribution yet single-node interventions decode the hidden adjacency matrix, giving a Θ(n^2) observational-to-interventional gap that is degree-sensitive optimal up to O(nd log(en/d)+n log n) for finite-gate-schema SCMs. The quadratic separation survives ε-accurate total-variation descriptions for ε<1/4, and a modular XOR family shows even the full hard-do interventional oracle can leave a Θ(n) counterfactual gap. The paper’s distinctive contribution is the ambiguity-to-bits principle: causal uncertainty is literally the logarithm of residual higher-rung ambiguity, suggesting future work on how sparsity, positivity, noise, faithfulness, and active interventions shrink causal description gaps.
papers.abstract: Pearl's causal hierarchy shows that observational, interventional, and counterfactual queries are qualitatively distinct. We ask a quantitative version of this question: how many additional bits are needed to specify higher-rung causal answers once lower-rung answers are known? We formalize this via query-class description length, the Kolmogorov complexity of the answer oracle induced by an SCM for a class of queries. Our main construction gives binary acyclic SCMs whose observational distribution has constant description length, while the single-variable interventional answer oracle has description length $Θ(n^2)$. A degree-sensitive upper bound shows that finite-gate-schema SCMs of indegree $d$ have observational-interventional gap at most $O(nd \log(en/d) + n \log n)$, making the quadratic construction order-optimal in the dense regime and a rooted-tree construction order-optimal for bounded indegree. The quadratic separation persists under $\varepsilon$-accurate total-variation descriptions for every fixed $\varepsilon < 1/4$. At the next rung, the full hard-do interventional oracle can still leave a $Θ(n)$ counterfactual description gap. A general ambiguity-to-bits theorem and Shannon analogue show that these gaps equal the logarithm of residual higher-rung ambiguity up to lower-order terms.

papers.title: Measuring Differences between Conditional Distributions using Kernel Embeddings
papers.summary: Measuring Differences between Conditional Distributions using Kernel Embeddings attacks the core bottleneck of deciding whether two conditional laws are the same from paired joint samples, a problem that appears in causal effect checking, covariate-shift diagnosis, and calibration but had been split across incompatible kernel formulations. The paper pivots from treating conditional two-sample testing, conditional discrepancy measurement, and kernel embedding estimators as separate tricks toward a single operator-theoretic hierarchy called conditional maximum mean discrepancy (CMMD). The mechanical shift is to replace one-off embeddings with three nested “levels” of the same divergence: CMMD0 compares conditional mean operators directly, CMMD1 compares conditional mean embeddings averaged over the covariate distribution, and CMMD2 compares joint mean embeddings; all are linked by operator-based smoothing through powers of the covariance operator CXX, yielding a general level-s CMMD with explicit assumptions and monotone relationships. This is unusual in that it does not just propose another test statistic; it unifies previously fragmented literature and shows that higher levels are progressively smoother and weaker, so the “best” conditional metric depends on whether one wants support-sensitive or covariate-agnostic discrepancy. On the estimation side, beyond reviewing kernel ridge-style plug-in estimators, the paper introduces a doubly robust estimator that stays consistent if either the conditional mean model or propensity model is correct, shifting the inference loop toward semiparametric robustness. Experiments on synthetic settings and MNIST-based conditional testing show CMMD detects complex conditional dependence with Type I control, pointing future work toward adaptive level selection, robust conditional comparison under misspecification, and kernel-based auditing of deployed predictive systems.
papers.abstract: Comparing conditional distributions is a fundamental challenge in statistics and machine learning, with applications across a wide range of domains. While proposed methods for measuring discrepancies using kernel embeddings of distributions in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) provide powerful non-parametric techniques, the existing literature remains fragmented and lacks a unified theoretical treatment. This paper addresses this gap by establishing a coherent framework for studying kernel-based methods to measure divergence between conditional distributions through what we refer to as conditional maximum mean discrepancy (CMMD). The CMMD consists of a family of metrics which we call levels, with three special cases each using a different type of RKHS embedding: CMMD$_0$ (conditional mean operators), CMMD$_1$ (conditional mean embeddings), and CMMD$_2$ (joint mean embeddings). We additionally introduce a general level $s$ CMMD, clarifying the required assumptions, and establishing mathematical connections between the levels through the lens of operator-based smoothing. In addition to reviewing previously proposed estimators, we introduce a novel doubly robust estimator for the CMMD that maintains consistency provided at least one of the underlying models is correctly specified. We provide numerical experiments demonstrating that the CMMD effectively captures complex conditional dependencies for statistical testing.

papers.title: Active multiple matrix completion with adaptive confidence sets
papers.summary: Active multiple matrix completion with adaptive confidence sets attacks the fixed-budget problem of completing K low-rank matrices simultaneously when the learner may choose which matrix to query at each round, a setting motivated by market segmentation and polling where regions/tasks differ in size and unknown rank. The paper pivots away from passive, uniform, rank-aware matrix completion and from Bernoulli-style one-shot sampling toward an active pure-exploration view: the bottleneck becomes how to allocate observations across heterogeneous matrices so that the hardest task does not dominate the final loss. The mechanical shift is MALocate, an anytime doubling-schedule algorithm that uses trace-regression with multi-sampling to build honest adaptive confidence sets from double-observed entries, then routes future samples to the matrix maximizing a rank-normalized uncertainty score d_k^2 B_k(t)T_k(t)^(-1/p). Each selected matrix is refit with square-root nuclear-norm lasso on one half of the new data and its error is estimated on the other half via an unbiased repeated-entry estimator, so exploration is driven by uncertainty rather than known complexity. Unlike nearby active matrix completion or bandit work, the strategy is provably rank-adaptive without tuning to unknown noise or ranks, and it explicitly depends on repeated measurements being possible. The main claim is minimax-optimality up to log factors: for max loss it matches a lower bound of order (Σ_k r_k d_k^3)/n, while synthetic experiments show the allocation rule outperforms uniform sampling and tracks an oracle, pointing toward a broader research direction where adaptive confidence sets become the control primitive for multi-task active learning.
papers.abstract: In this work, we formulate a new multi-task active learning setting in which the learner's goal is to solve multiple matrix completion problems simultaneously. At each round, the learner can choose from which matrix it receives a sample from an entry drawn uniformly at random. Our main practical motivation is market segmentation, where the matrices represent different regions with different preferences of the customers. The challenge in this setting is that each of the matrices can be of a different size and also of a different rank which is unknown. We provide and analyze a new algorithm, MAlocate that is able to adapt to the unknown ranks of the different matrices. We then give a lower-bound showing that our strategy is minimax-optimal and demonstrate its performance with synthetic experiments.

papers.title: Middle-mile logistics through the lens of goal-conditioned reinforcement learning
papers.summary: Middle-mile logistics through the lens of goal-conditioned reinforcement learning attacks the bottleneck that parcel routing over hub-and-truck networks stops being a shortest-path problem once finite truck capacity makes each assignment alter other parcels’ feasibility, so exact MIP-and-heuristic solvers collapse on realistic time-expanded instances. The paper pivots from per-parcel routing and hand-crafted consolidation heuristics toward a multi-object, goal-conditioned MDP in which the full time-expanded logistics graph is the state, choosing a truck for a parcel is the action, and delivery success is the sparse 0/1 reward. The mechanical shift is to replace global exact optimization with model-free PPO over graph neural networks, but only after aggressively compressing the state into small parcel-specific feature graphs: nodes within a K-hop radius of the parcel start and goal, augmented with resistance-distance-to-goal and relative-time features, valid-action masking, and optional phantom parcel weights to preserve capacity interference from pruned-away parcels. This is unusual relative to nearby combinatorial-RL work because it explicitly models multi-object congestion, not just single-route improvement, and because it provides an open-source Gym-like environment for logistics benchmarking. In experiments on sampled 10-hub, 50-step, 200-parcel networks from an extended Barabási–Albert generator, the GNN policy generalizes across resampled instances and beats a linear parameterization, but supervised learning on sampled routes and even a greedy resistance-distance baseline outperform the learned RL policy, exposing how hard optimal credit assignment remains. The paper points toward learned logistics solvers that combine goal-conditioned control, graph compression, and perhaps model-based search or direct advantage estimation.
papers.abstract: Middle-mile logistics describes the problem of routing parcels through a network of hubs linked by trucks with finite capacity. We rephrase this as a multi-object goal-conditioned MDP. Our method combines graph neural networks with model-free RL, extracting small feature graphs from the environment state.

papers.title: Black-box optimization of noisy functions with unknown smoothness
papers.summary: Black-box optimization of noisy functions with unknown smoothness attacks the sample-efficiency bottleneck in expensive bandit-style function search: given only noisy evaluations of an arbitrary-dimensional objective, how can one optimize when the only useful regularity is local smoothness around a global maximizer, but the smoothness rate is unknown? The paper challenges the older workflow in which optimistic tree-search methods such as HOO, HCT, or StoSOO require a hand-set metric, Lipschitz constant, or partition-quality lower bound, and it pivots from metric-centric smoothness assumptions toward a partitioning-aware notion of difficulty measured directly by a new near-optimality dimension tied to the hierarchical cells, not to any ambient distance. The mechanical shift is POO, parallel optimistic optimization: it runs a scheduled family of HOO instances in parallel over a grid of $(\nu,\rho)$ values, with the grid chosen so that one instance is near the unknown optimal smoothness, and it reuses evaluations across instances when they hit the same point, preserving theory while cutting waste in practice. Unlike nearby work that only covers easy zero-dimension cases or requires a known quality parameter, POO handles harder functions where the optimal basin’s upper and lower envelopes disagree in order, including instances with zero quality. Finite-time analysis gives simple regret within only a $\sqrt{\ln n}$ factor of the best smoothness-aware optimistic optimizer, and experiments on a function with misleading local geometry show POO nearly matches the best-tuned HOO while avoiding catastrophic mis-specification. This points toward anytime meta-optimizers that adaptively hedge over structural priors instead of committing to one smoothness model.
papers.abstract: We study the problem of black-box optimization of a function f of any dimension, given function evaluations perturbed by noise. The function is assumed to be locally smooth around one of its global optima, but this smoothness is unknown. Our contribution is an adaptive optimization algorithm, POO or parallel optimistic optimization, that is able to deal with this setting. POO performs almost as well as the best known algorithms requiring the knowledge of the smoothness. Furthermore, POO works for a larger class of functions than what was previously considered, especially for functions that are difficult to optimize, in a very precise sense. We provide a finite-time analysis of POO's performance, which shows that its error after n evaluations is at most a factor of sqrt(ln n) away from the error of the best known optimization algorithms using the knowledge of the smoothness.

papers.title: Online Generalised Predictive Coding
papers.summary: Online Generalised Predictive Coding attacks the bottleneck of truly online triple estimation: inferring hidden states, learning model parameters, and estimating state/observation precisions from a single pass over nonstationary data, without revisiting past samples or waiting for convergence as offline DEM, Kalman-style smoothing, or SMC2 can. The paper pivots from batch variational filtering toward a temporally stratified predictive-coding loop in which fast Bayesian belief updates track latent states at every observation, while parameters and precisions are updated only on a slower schedule, treating them as contextual variables that “slaved” the rapid dynamics. The mechanical shift is the Online Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (ODEM) algorithm: a one-layer generalised predictive coding model built in generalised coordinates of motion, with Laplace/variational free-energy minimisation, Ozaki-style state updates, accumulated gradients for E- and M-steps, and a constant per-iteration cost that comes from mean-field carryover between time points. Unlike nearby online methods that assume linear state spaces or fixed noise covariances, ODEM explicitly models nonlinear and potentially chaotic dynamics and uses higher-order derivatives to avoid closed-form transition densities or particle simulation. In simulations on noisy Generalised Lotka–Volterra data, ODEM tracked latent trajectories even when the inference model was Lorenz rather than GLV, with higher motion orders improving parameter certainty and precision estimates stabilising over time. The result suggests a direction where adaptive inference systems continuously reweight uncertainty and learn dynamics online, toward biologically grounded, real-time belief updating in complex environments.
papers.abstract: This paper introduces an extension of generalised filtering for online applications. Generalised filtering refers to data assimilation schemes that jointly infer latent states, learn unknown model parameters, and estimate uncertainty in an integrated framework -- e.g., estimate state and observation noise -- at the same time (i.e., triple estimation). This framework appears across disciplines under different names, including variational Kalman-Bucy filtering in engineering, generalised predictive coding in neuroscience, and Dynamic Expectation Maximisation (DEM) in time-series analysis. Here, we specialise DEM for ``online'' data assimilation, through a separation of temporal scales. We describe the variational principles and procedures that allow one to assimilate data in a way that allows for a slow updating of parameters and precisions, which contextualise fast Bayesian belief updating about the dynamic hidden states. Using numerical studies, we demonstrate the validity of online DEM (ODEM) using a non-linear -- and potentially chaotic -- generative model, to show that the ODEM scheme can track the latent states of the generative process, even when its functional form differs fundamentally from the dynamics of the generative model. Framed from a neuro-mimetic predictive coding perspective, ODEM offers a biologically inspired solution to online inference, learning, and uncertainty estimation in dynamic environments.

papers.title: ParaRNN: An Interpretable and Parallelizable Recurrent Neural Network for Time-Dependent Data
papers.summary: ParaRNN: An Interpretable and Parallelizable Recurrent Neural Network for Time-Dependent Data attacks the bottleneck that vanilla RNNs, though expressive for time-dependent data, are too opaque and too sequential to fit the statistical modeling setting, where one wants both forecast skill and a readable dynamical story. The paper pivots away from the implicit assumption that a single dense recurrent matrix must entangle all memory into one hidden state, and also away from diagonal-recurrent simplifications that speed computation by deleting exactly the periodic structure statistics often need. Its mechanical shift is to replace one monolithic recurrent core with multiple small recurrent units running in parallel, plus an aggregation map, so the overall hidden state admits an additive decomposition into recurrence features. Using block-diagonal similarity ideas and real Jordan decomposition, ParaRNN exposes Type R features with exponential decay and Type C features with damped sinusoidal decay, making the dynamics legible as statistical time-series motifs rather than opaque activations. Unusually, the design is both interpretability-first and parallelization-first: unlike diagonal RNNs, it retains complex conjugate blocks and thus periodic memory. The experiments on three sequential modeling tasks, including time-series and permuted sequential MNIST settings, show performance comparable to vanilla RNNs while improving efficiency and interpretability. Theoretical results establish approximation capacity and non-asymptotic prediction error bounds, and the paper argues that ds = 2 is often enough because low-order recurrence features dominate in practice. This points toward a research direction where RNNs are treated as decomposable statistical objects whose memory can be engineered, measured, and parallelized feature by feature.
papers.abstract: The proliferation of large-scale and structurally complex data has spurred the integration of machine learning methods into statistical modeling. Recurrent neural networks (RNNs), a foundational class of models for time-dependent data, can be viewed as nonlinear extensions of classical autoregressive moving average models. Despite their flexibility and empirical success in machine learning, RNNs often suffer from limited interpretability and slow training, which hinders their use in statistics. This paper proposes the Parallelized RNN (ParaRNN), a novel model composed of multiple small recurrent units. ParaRNN admits an additive representation that decouples recurrent dynamics into interpretable components, whose behavior can be characterized through recurrence features. This interpretability enables its applications in nonparametric regression for time-dependent data, while the design also allows efficient parallelization. The approximation capacity and non-asymptotic prediction error bounds in a nonparametric regression setting are established for ParaRNN. Empirical results on three sequential modeling tasks further demonstrate that ParaRNN achieves performance comparable to vanilla RNNs while offering improved interpretability and efficiency.

papers.title: Random-Effects Algorithm for Random Objects in Metric Spaces
papers.summary: Random-Effects Algorithm for Random Objects in Metric Spaces attacks the bottleneck that repeated outcomes in modern digital-health and scientific cohorts are often non-Euclidean random objects—distributions, graphs, and other metric-space-valued measurements—so standard additive mixed models and even Hilbert-space functional random-effects models cannot express subject-specific heterogeneity without forcing a false vector-space structure. The paper pivots from linear random-effects on functions toward Fréchet-style modeling of arbitrary metric-space responses, replacing direct addition of latent effects with an anchor-based surrogate: each observed response is used as a reference object, squared distances to anchors are log-transformed into scalar targets, and a separate scalar mixed-effects regression is fit per anchor. The core mechanical shift is therefore a massively univariate, parallelizable loop of GPBoost models that combines boosted trees for fixed effects with Gaussian random intercepts, then reconstructs a personalized prediction by selecting the observed metric object whose predicted anchor-distance profile best matches the individual’s estimated profile. Using M-estimation theory, the authors show the resulting Fréchet prediction target is consistently estimated under a working random-effects formulation, even though the latent effect is never represented directly in the original metric space. Experiments on NHANES and CGMCR daily trajectories, CGM-derived 2-Wasserstein glucose distributions, and thresholded Laplacian graphs show gains in three settings, with the biggest improvement on multivariate distributions and a near-null case for unstable graphs. This points toward future metric-learning systems built from anchorized surrogates, subject-level personalization, and scalable uncertainty-aware Fréchet inference.
papers.abstract: Across many scientific disciplines, multiple observations are collected from the same experimental units, and in modern datasets these observations often arise as non-Euclidean random objects. In such settings, the incorporation of random effects is a critical modeling step for efficient estimation and personalized prediction. Although mixed-effects models are well established for scalar outcomes and, more recently, for functional data in Hilbert spaces, general random-effects frameworks for objects in metric spaces remain underdeveloped. In this paper, we propose a nonlinear Fréchet-based algorithm for random-effects modeling of arbitrary random objects defined on a metric space. Using M-estimation theory, we establish conditions under which the proposed metric-space prediction target is consistently estimated under a working random-effects formulation. We then evaluate the empirical performance of the proposed method using both synthetic data and digital health datasets that require practical tools for analyzing random objects in metric spaces, such as multivariate probability distributions and random graphs. We show that, although our method is developed beyond Hilbert spaces, it can outperform existing Hilbert space-based methods.

papers.title: Phone2Act: A Low-Cost, Hardware-Agnostic Teleoperation System for Scalable VLA Data Collection
papers.summary: Phone2Act: A Low-Cost, Hardware-Agnostic Teleoperation System for Scalable VLA Data Collection attacks the bottleneck that Vision-Language-Action progress is no longer limited by model scale alone but by the cost and fragmentation of manipulation data collection: existing leader-follower rigs, VR interfaces, and platform-specific teleop stacks are too expensive or too tightly coupled to robot embodiments to let small labs build training corpora. The paper pivots from robot-centric teleoperation toward a data-first, embodiment-agnostic pipeline, replacing specialized controllers with a commodity smartphone and replacing per-robot code paths with standardized ROS 2 topics plus interchangeable bridge nodes. The mechanical shift is the use of ARCore on a phone as a stable 6-DoF spatial controller at 50 Hz, paired with a “floating zero” clutch that lets the operator rebase the phone midtask, workspace clamps and zero-jump filtering for safe command arbitration, and a Universal Recorder that synchronizes multi-camera RGB with robot state at 20 Hz and exports episodes directly in LeRobot format, eliminating postprocessing and making data immediately VLA-ready. Unlike nearby work that treats teleoperation as an interface problem, Phone2Act treats it as a cross-platform dataset compiler, demonstrated on both low-cost bimanual SO-101 arms and an industrial Dobot CR5 without core software changes. With only 130 episodes, it fine-tunes GR00T-N1.5 to 90% success on a multi-stage pick-and-place task, suggesting a future where scalable robot learning depends on cheap, standardized, train-ready demonstration infrastructure rather than bespoke hardware.
papers.abstract: Collecting diverse, high-quality manipulation data for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model training remains prohibitively expensive for many research groups, as existing teleoperation frameworks rely on specialized hardware or are tightly coupled to specific robot platforms. We present Phone2Act, a low-cost, hardware-agnostic teleoperation framework that transforms a commodity smartphone into a 6-DoF robot controller via Google ARCore. Built on a modular ROS 2 architecture, Phone2Act decouples control logic from hardware specifics through interchangeable bridge nodes, supporting platforms from industrial cobots to low-cost bimanual arms without code modification. A Universal Recorder synchronizes multi-camera RGB streams with robot state feedback and exports demonstrations natively in the LeRobot dataset format, eliminating post-processing and enabling immediate VLA fine-tuning. We validate the framework by fine-tuning GR00T-N1.5 on 130 collected episodes, achieving a 90% success rate on a real-world multi-stage pick-and-place task deployed on a physical Dobot CR5.

papers.title: Sonar-GPS Fusion for Seabed Mapping in Turbid Shallow Waters with an Autonomous Surface Vehicle
papers.summary: Sonar-GPS Fusion for Seabed Mapping in Turbid Shallow Waters with an Autonomous Surface Vehicle attacks the bottleneck of building dense, georeferenced seabed mosaics where optical methods fail and sonar-only mosaics drift over long, overlapping trajectories because FLS positioning is coarse and frame alignment accumulates error. It challenges the implicit assumption that either local sonar registration alone, or full SLAM-style landmark/graph machinery, is the right answer for this deployment; instead it pivots from navigation-centric reconstruction toward a lightweight, field-deployable 2-D mosaicing pipeline tuned for turbid oyster-farm surveys. The mechanical shift is a three-part loop: FMT estimates pairwise local motion between consecutive sonar frames, an EKF fuses GPS, IMU, and compass into smooth global priors, and nonlinear least-squares optimization enforces consistency between local FMT residuals and global pose constraints before a variance-based blender fuses overlapping pixels. Unusually, the method explicitly uses GPS not as a precise mapper but as a drift anchor, then adds EMA-smoothed short- and long-term variance maps, adaptive candidate filtering, and MAD trimming to suppress striping while preserving seabed texture. In 15 ASV trials over a structured 3–4 ft-deep oyster farm with a 2×4 quadrat layout, the system achieved sub-meter reconstruction and reduced drift RMSE by 9.5% versus the FMT-only baseline, with sharper quadrat geometry than averaging or prior sonar blending. The limitation is its near-planar 2-D assumption and offline runtime, pointing toward onboard, online, map-and-classify workflows for oyster inventory estimation and informative path planning.
papers.abstract: Accurate seabed mapping is essential for habitat monitoring and infrastructure inspection. In turbid, shallow coastal waters, such as shellfish aquaculture farms, the effectiveness of traditional optical methods is limited. Autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) equipped with forward-looking sonar (FLS) offer a promising alternative. However, existing sonar-based systems face challenges in achieving fine resolution mapping over long trajectories due to low-resolution positioning measurements and accumulated drift over long trajectories. In this paper, we present a drift-resilient seabed mapping framework that integrates local FLS frame alignment using the Fourier-Mellin transform (FMT) with global trajectory optimization based on an extended Kalman filter (EKF) that fuses global positioning system (GPS), inertial measurement unit (IMU), and compass data. A variance-based image blending strategy is used to further reduce visual artifacts in overlapping regions. Field trials on a structured oyster farm site show that our framework helps reduce drift in RMSE by 9.5% relative to the FMT-only baseline. This framework also enables sub-meter reconstruction accuracy and preservation of high-resolution textures needed for oyster inventory estimation within the mapped areas.

papers.title: VILAS: A VLA-Integrated Low-cost Architecture with Soft Grasping for Robotic Manipulation
papers.summary: VILAS: A VLA-Integrated Low-cost Architecture with Soft Grasping for Robotic Manipulation attacks the bottleneck that current vision-language-action robotics depends on expensive, vertically integrated hardware stacks and explicit force-sensing/control to handle fragile objects, making real-world VLA deployment inaccessible and brittle. The paper pivots from high-cost proprietary manipulation ecosystems and sensor-heavy compliant control toward a modular, affordable stack in which end-to-end VLA policies are trained and run on a Fairino FR5 arm, a Jodell RG52-50 parallel gripper, a GELLO teleoperation leader, and dual RealSense cameras, all coordinated through a ZMQ-based data-collection/deployment layer. The core mechanical shift is the kirigami-based PEBA soft extension attached to the gripper: instead of estimating contact forces online, the gripper is given predictable passive deformation under compression, turning compliance into a material property rather than a learned control problem. Unusually, VILAS is not just a new robot but a reproducible benchmarked platform: the authors fine-tune π0, π0.5, and GR00T N1.6 from public checkpoints on the same 100-episode teleoperated dataset, then evaluate them on sequential grape grasping and zero-shot cherry transfer. Results show π0.5 wins single-grasp success, but GR00T N1.6 is strongest on multi-grasp continuity, revealing that action-horizon design and deployment behavior matter as much as raw model quality. This points toward future work on low-cost embodied foundation-model testbeds, longer-horizon manipulation, and mechanically intelligent end-effectors that reduce reliance on sensing-heavy control.
papers.abstract: We present VILAS, a fully low-cost, modular robotic manipulation platform designed to support end-to-end vision-language-action (VLA) policy learning and deployment on accessible hardware. The system integrates a Fairino FR5 collaborative arm, a Jodell RG52-50 electric gripper, and a dual-camera perception module, unified through a ZMQ-based communication architecture that seamlessly coordinates teleoperation, data collection, and policy deployment within a single framework. To enable safe manipulation of fragile objects without relying on explicit force sensing, we design a kirigami-based soft compliant gripper extension that induces predictable deformation under compressive loading, providing gentle and repeatable contact with delicate targets. We deploy and evaluate three state-of-the-art VLA models on the VILAS platform: pi_0, pi_0.5, and GR00T N1.6. All models are fine-tuned from publicly released pretrained checkpoints using an identical demonstration dataset collected via our teleoperation pipeline. Experiments on a grape grasping task validate the effectiveness of the proposed system, confirming that capable manipulation policies can be successfully trained and deployed on low-cost modular hardware. Our results further provide practical insights into the deployment characteristics of current VLA models in real-world settings.

papers.title: A Semantic Autonomy Framework for VLM-Integrated Indoor Mobile Robots: Hybrid Deterministic Reasoning and Cross-Robot Adaptive Memory
papers.summary: A Semantic Autonomy Framework for VLM-Integrated Indoor Mobile Robots: Hybrid Deterministic Reasoning and Cross-Robot Adaptive Memory attacks the deployment bottleneck where indoor robots can already follow metric goals in ROS 2/Nav2 but cannot reliably execute intent-level instructions like “go somewhere I can sit and relax” without paying VLM latency and session-by-session amnesia. The paper challenges the assumption that semantic navigation should route every query through a language model or learn a fast-path classifier from labeled data; instead it pivots from always-VLM, simulation-heavy semantic navigation toward a layered edge-robot autonomy stack that resolves routine cases deterministically and reserves VLM calls for genuine ambiguity. The mechanical shift is the six-layer Semantic Autonomy Stack plus a seven-step parametric resolver that matches graph nodes, POI IDs, attributes, class/proximity cues, and especially promoted memory preferences before escalating; the fast path runs in under 0.1 ms, needs no training data, camera, or GPU, and handled 88% of decisions. Memory is not just recall but a control lever: a five-category M1–M5 taxonomy compiles audit logs into a compact digest, promotes repeatedly VLM-resolved instruction→node mappings into deterministic M3 rules, and shares those rules across robots via a read-only digest. On two physical differential-drive robots with Raspberry Pi 5 onboard compute and Qwen 3.5:4b on a workstation GPU, the system achieved 100% semantic resolution and transfer accuracy over 82 scenario decisions, with a 103,000× latency drop after promotion. This points toward fleet robotics where semantic behavior is audited, reusable, and locally cheap rather than re-reasoned from scratch.
papers.abstract: Autonomous indoor mobile robots can navigate reliably to metric coordinates using established frameworks such as ROS 2 Navigation 2, yet they lack the ability to interpret natural language instructions that express intent rather than positions. Vision-Language Models offer the semantic reasoning required to bridge this gap, but their inference latency (2-9 seconds per decision on consumer hardware) and session-by-session amnesia limit practical deployment. This paper presents the Semantic Autonomy Stack, a six-layer reference framework for semantically autonomous indoor navigation, and validates a complete instance featuring hybrid deterministic-VLM reasoning and cross-robot adaptive memory on physical robots with off-the-shelf edge hardware. A seven-step parametric resolver handles 88% of instructions in under 0.1 milliseconds without invoking a language model, camera, or GPU; only genuinely ambiguous instructions escalate to VLM reasoning. A five-category semantic memory framework with explicit scope taxonomy (global environment knowledge, per-operator preferences, per-robot capabilities) enables cross-session learning and cross-robot knowledge transfer: preferences learned through VLM interactions on one robot are promoted to deterministic resolution and transferred to a second robot via a shared compiled digest, achieving a measured latency reduction of 103,000-fold. Experimental validation on two custom-built differential-drive robots across 82 scenario-level decisions and three sessions demonstrates 100% semantic transfer accuracy (33/33, 95% CI [0.894, 1.000]), 100% semantic resolution accuracy, and concurrent multi-robot operation feasibility - all on Raspberry Pi 5 platforms with no onboard GPU, requiring zero training data.

papers.title: Beyond Specialization: Robust Reinforcement Learning Navigation via Procedural Map Generators
papers.summary: Beyond Specialization: Robust Reinforcement Learning Navigation via Procedural Map Generators attacks the hidden bottleneck in DRL navigation: policies look strong on the map family they were hand-designed around, then collapse when corridor topology, obstacle density, or dead-end structure shifts. The paper challenges the usual assumption that “more training maps” is enough, and pivots from manual scenario curation and single-layout specialization toward systematic structural diversity as the unit of generalization. The mechanical shift is twofold: MuRoSim is extended with four navigability-guaranteed procedural generators—sparse, maze, graph, and Wave Function Collapse—and policies are cross-evaluated across 1,000 seeded maps per generator to expose asymmetric transfer. That protocol reveals a sparse-trained specialist dropping to 3.3% success on mazes, while a policy trained on the combined generator set reaches 91.5 ± 1.1% mean success with the lowest seed variance. A second shift replaces purely reactive local control with explicit A* subgoal directions, not just recurrence: injecting five waypoint direction vectors into the observation raises success from 90.2 ± 1.4% to 98.9 ± 0.4%, whereas GRU memory mainly helps the reactive baseline and adds little once subgoals are present. The result is unusual because it compares generator type, planner guidance, recurrence, and classical Carrot+A* control in one controlled 2D LiDAR/RoboMaster sim-to-real setup, showing learned speed adaptation as the decisive advantage at high velocity and pointing toward hierarchical navigation policies that learn their own subgoal streams under procedurally generated curricula.
papers.abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) navigation policies often overfit to the structure of their training environments, as environmental diversity is typically constrained by the manual effort required to design diverse scenarios. While procedural map generation offers scalable diversity, no prior work systematically compares how different generator types affect policy generalization.
