We integrate four generators (sparse, maze, graph, and Wave Function Collapse) with guaranteed navigability into MuRoSim, a 2D simulator focusing on training efficiency for LiDAR-based navigation. We cross-evaluate five navigation policies on 1000 seeded maps per generator across three training seeds. Results show a strongly asymmetric cross-generator transfer: a specialist trained on sparse layouts falls to 3.3% success on mazes, whereas a policy trained on the combined generator set achieves 91.5 +/- 1.1% mean success. We further demonstrate that A* path-planner subgoal inputs are the dominant factor for robustness, raising success from the 90.2 +/- 1.4% feedforward baseline to 98.9 +/- 0.4% and outperforming GRU recurrence, which only improves the reactive baseline. The DRL policies outperform a classical Carrot+A* controller, which matches their success only at low speeds (1.0 m/s) but collapses to 24.9% at 2.0 m/s. This highlights learned speed adaptation as the decisive advantage of the learned approach. Real-world experiments on a RoboMaster confirm sim-to-real transfer in a cluttered arena, while a maze-like layout exposes remaining failure modes that recurrence helps mitigate.

papers.title: Orchestrating Spatial Semantics via a Zone-Graph Paradigm for Intricate Indoor Scene Generation
papers.summary: Orchestrating Spatial Semantics via a Zone-Graph Paradigm for Intricate Indoor Scene Generation attacks the failure mode where text-conditioned 3D room synthesis collapses in non-convex, densely constrained interiors: prior generators either place objects myopically without topological priors or use iterative agents that fragment the scene plan and become geometrically brittle under long-horizon constraints. The paper explicitly challenges the old assumption that indoor synthesis can be treated as object-centric filling inside a simple rectangular shell, and pivots from local placement or reactive repair toward Zone-Graph Orchestration, where the model reasons over functional zones, inter-zone topology, and enclosing architecture before emitting coordinates. Its core mechanical shift is a compositional factorization of scene generation into zone inventory, intra-zone spatial graphs, global adjacency, and boundary derivation, trained with alternating spatial alignment: supervised reasoning internalization first teaches a zone-graph trace, then Zone-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization denoises geometry with targeted rewards for boundary containment, zone disentanglement, and collision suppression, all without an external physics engine. Unusual here is the explicit reverse-engineering of Zone-Scene-10K annotations from InternScenes plus the SCALE benchmark, which stress-tests irregular floor plans rather than the canonical convex layouts common in prior evaluation. On Qwen3-8B, ZoneMaestro reportedly resolves the density-safety tradeoff, staying both dense and valid while outperforming baselines on structure, realism, and intent adherence. The work points toward hierarchical spatial programs and topology-first embodied scene synthesis that treat architecture as a controllable graph, not a passive backdrop.
papers.abstract: Autonomous 3D indoor scene synthesis breaks down in non-convex rooms with tightly coupled spatial constraints. Data-driven generators lack topological priors for long-horizon planning, while iterative agents fragment semantics and become geometrically brittle. We present ZoneMaestro, a unified framework that shifts the paradigm from object-centric synthesis to Zone-Graph Orchestration. By internalizing a novel zone-based logic, ZoneMaestro translates high-level semantic intent into functional zones and topological constraints, enabling robust adaptation to diverse architectural forms. To support this, we construct Zone-Scene-10K, a large-scale dataset enriched with explicit Zone-Graph annotations. We further introduce an Alternating Alignment Strategy that cycles between reasoning internalization and Zone-Aware Group Relative Policy Optimization (Z-GRPO), effectively reconciling the tension between semantic richness and geometric validity without relying on external physics engines. To rigorously evaluate spatial intelligence beyond convex primitives, we formally define the task of Intricate Spatial Orchestration and release SCALE, a stress-test benchmark for irregular indoor scenarios with complex, dense spatial relations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ZoneMaestro resolves the density-safety dichotomy, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in both structural coherence and intent adherence.

papers.title: CoRAL: Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based Control for Robotic Manipulation
papers.summary: CoRAL: Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based Control for Robotic Manipulation attacks the failure mode where LLMs/VLMs can describe tasks but cannot reliably execute contact-rich manipulation because visual semantics do not determine mass, friction, or the right force/contact sequence. It challenges the prevailing assumption that a robot should learn a black-box end-to-end policy or that an LLM should emit a static reward for a planner once and for all; instead it pivots from direct action prediction and offline reward coding toward a hierarchical, neuro-symbolic control stack that makes the LLM a cost designer and contact strategist for MPPI. The mechanical shift is concrete: FoundationPose tracks 6-DoF objects, a VLM supplies semantic physics priors, the LLM generates executable Python-like MPPI cost functions plus JSON contact regions, and an outer online-adaptation loop revises both the cost structure and mass/friction beliefs from interaction feedback while a retrieval memory reuses successful episodes. Unlike nearby VLA or planner work, CoRAL explicitly separates perception, strategy, and reactive execution, with 1 kHz impedance control, 10 Hz MPPI rollouts, and slower LLM reasoning, letting semantic plans be “stretched” into soft contact attractors rather than fixed commands. Evaluated zero-shot on Robosuite/MuJoCo with a Franka Panda and on real hardware across push-and-pick, constant-force, flip-box, and flip-with-wall tasks, it beats OpenVLA-OFT, π0.5, and L2R by over 50% average success on unseen contact-rich scenarios, though it still depends on world-model fidelity and LLM latency. This points toward future robots that continually revise physical beliefs and strategy online instead of freezing them into learned weights.
papers.abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in high-level reasoning and semantic understanding, applying them directly to contact-rich manipulation remains a challenge due to their lack of explicit physical grounding and inability to perform adaptive control. To bridge this gap, we propose CoRAL (Contact-Rich Adaptive LLM-based control), a modular framework that enables zero-shot planning by decoupling high-level reasoning from low-level control. Unlike black-box policies, CoRAL uses LLMs not as direct controllers, but as cost designers that synthesize context-aware objective functions for a sampling-based motion planner (MPPI). To address the ambiguity of physical parameters in visual data, we introduce a neuro-symbolic adaptation loop: a VLM provides semantic priors for environmental dynamics, such as mass and friction estimates, which are then explicitly refined in real time via online system identification, while the LLM iteratively modulates the cost-function structure to correct strategic errors based on interaction feedback. Furthermore, a retrieval-based memory unit allows the system to reuse successful strategies across recurrent tasks. This hierarchical architecture ensures real-time control stability by decoupling high-level semantic reasoning from reactive execution, effectively bridging the gap between slow LLM inference and dynamic contact requirements. We validate CoRAL on both simulation and real-world hardware across challenging and novel tasks, such as flipping objects against walls by leveraging extrinsic contacts. Experiments demonstrate that CoRAL outperforms state-of-the-art VLA and foundation-model-based planner baselines by boosting success rates over 50% on average in unseen contact-rich scenarios, effectively handling sim-to-real gaps through its adaptive physical understanding.

papers.title: AnchorD: Metric Grounding of Monocular Depth Using Factor Graphs
papers.summary: AnchorD: Metric Grounding of Monocular Depth Using Factor Graphs attacks the robotics bottleneck that depth sensors become unreliable exactly on transparent, specular, and other non-Lambertian surfaces, while monocular foundation models supply rich geometry but only in skewed or mis-scaled metric units that are unsafe for grasping and navigation. It challenges the prevailing assumptions that depth should be corrected by either supervised transparent-object completion or a single global affine scale fit, and pivots from task-specific learning toward zero-shot metric grounding of pretrained monocular priors through raw sensor depth. The mechanical shift is a training-free factor-graph optimization that jointly solves for dense depth and patch-wise affine alignment parameters, using unary sensor factors to anchor valid measurements, ternary MDE factors to tie each patch to the monocular prediction, and logarithmic slope-consistency factors to preserve relative structure across missing or corrupted regions; a final Gaussian-weighted smoothing step converts patchwise parameters into coherent pixelwise depth while retaining discontinuities. Unlike nearby RGB-D completion work, it does not learn a transparent-object model or require retraining, and it explicitly uses off-the-shelf DepthAnything3 predictions plus aligned sensor depth. To evaluate this regime, the paper introduces SprayD, a real-world benchmark with dense scene-wide ground truth from matte spray and multi-camera fusion, overcoming object-mask-only CAD-based annotations. Across SprayD, ClearPose, and ScanNet++ aggregation, AnchorD consistently improves MAE/RMSE/REL and yields denser, more geometrically consistent reconstructions than affine scaling, inpainting, or metric DepthAnything3, pointing toward future robotics systems that ground foundation-model priors in optimization loops rather than end-to-end retraining.
papers.abstract: Dense and accurate depth estimation is essential for robotic manipulation, grasping, and navigation, yet currently available depth sensors are prone to errors on transparent, specular, and general non-Lambertian surfaces. To mitigate these errors, large-scale monocular depth estimation approaches provide strong structural priors, but their predictions can be potentially skewed or mis-scaled in metric units, limiting their direct use in robotics. Thus, in this work, we propose a training-free depth grounding framework that anchors monocular depth estimation priors from a depth foundation model in raw sensor depth through factor graph optimization. Our method performs a patch-wise affine alignment, locally grounding monocular predictions in metric real-world depth while preserving fine-grained geometric structure and discontinuities. To facilitate evaluation in challenging real-world conditions, we introduce a benchmark dataset with dense scene-wide ground truth depth in the presence of non-Lambertian objects. Ground truth is obtained via matte reflection spray and multi-camera fusion, overcoming the reliance on object-only CAD-based annotations used in prior datasets. Extensive evaluations across diverse sensors and domains demonstrate consistent improvements in depth performance without any (re-)training. We make our implementation publicly available at https://anchord.cs.uni-freiburg.de.

papers.title: Learning Equivariant Neural-Augmented Object Dynamics From Few Interactions
papers.summary: Learning Equivariant Neural-Augmented Object Dynamics From Few Interactions attacks the bottleneck that learned particle dynamics for manipulation drift, interpenetrate, and need thousands of real contacts before they become usable for planning, especially on deformable objects where shape coherence and long-horizon feasibility matter most. It challenges the nearby assumption that a graph neural network over 3D particles is sufficient if paired with more data or a simulator-only prior, and pivots from purely neural prediction or purely analytical system identification toward a hybrid control-and-prediction stack. The mechanical shift is PIEGraph: a Physically Informed spring-mass simulator is initialized from a segmented point cloud, then guided by an E(n)-equivariant graph neural network whose novel canonicalized action representation encodes start/end effector motion in an object-aligned, transformation-invariant way. Rather than letting the network directly roll out the state, the model uses the EGNN’s particle-wise target as a setpoint and iteratively applies PID-controlled external forces so the spring-mass system converges to a physically feasible configuration. Training uses RGB-D human interaction data, hand-tracked actions, and MSE plus shape-consistency losses; planning uses MPC with CEM over candidate action sequences to reach goal point clouds. Evaluated on ropes, cloth, stuffed animals, and rigid blocks in simulation and on a Franka robot, it outperforms PGND and analytical baselines, with especially strong gains in long-horizon planning and data efficiency from as few as ~20 interactions. This points toward manipulation models that learn symmetries, not just trajectories, and toward neural-augmented simulators that make physical feasibility a control variable.
papers.abstract: Learning data-efficient object dynamics models for robotic manipulation remains challenging, especially for deformable objects. A popular approach is to model objects as sets of 3D particles and learn their motion using graph neural networks. In practice, this is not enough to maintain physical feasibility over long horizons and may require large amounts of interaction data to learn. We introduce PIEGraph, a novel approach to combining analytical physics and data-driven models to capture object dynamics for both rigid and deformable bodies using limited real-world interaction data. PIEGraph consists of two components: (1) a \textbf{P}hysically \textbf{I}nformed particle-based analytical model (implemented as a spring--mass system) to enforce physically feasible motion, and (2) an \textbf{E}quivariant \textbf{Graph} Neural Network with a novel action representation that exploits symmetries in particle interactions to guide the analytical model. We evaluate PIEGraph in simulation and on robot hardware for reorientation and repositioning tasks with ropes, cloth, stuffed animals and rigid objects. We show that our method enables accurate dynamics prediction and reliable downstream robotic manipulation planning, which outperforms state of the art baselines.

papers.title: Temporally Consistent Object 6D Pose Estimation for Robot Control
papers.summary: Temporally Consistent Object 6D Pose Estimation for Robot Control attacks a control-critical failure mode in single-view RGB 6D pose estimation: per-frame pose outputs from strong backbones like CosyPose or MegaPose are too jittery, missing, and outlier-prone to serve as a stable feedback signal for robot manipulation, especially under occlusion, motion blur, symmetries, and repeated object instances. The paper explicitly challenges the assumption that high-accuracy framewise pose regression is sufficient for robotics, and pivots from “best pose per image” toward a temporally coherent world-state estimator. The mechanical shift is a factor-graph smoother that fuses camera forward-kinematics, object pose observations, and motion-model factors in an online optimization loop, while estimating measurement uncertainty instead of treating all pose predictions equally. Uncertainty is modeled anisotropically, with larger depth variance along the viewing ray, and incoming measurements are gated by Mahalanobis-distance outlier rejection; missing detections are extrapolated by constant-pose or constant-velocity priors. Unlike nearby pose-tracking work, the method keeps multiple discrete symmetry modes and identical-object hypotheses as separate graph variables and only exposes the most confident track to the controller. Evaluated on HOPE-Video, YCB-V, and synthetic static/dynamic HOPE sequences, it improves recall or precision depending on tuning while preserving stability, and on a Franka Emika Panda with an end-effector RealSense camera it remains robust when the target is partially or fully occluded. The result points toward robot vision systems that optimize temporally probabilistic state estimates, not isolated detections, as the interface to control.
papers.abstract: Single-view RGB object pose estimators have reached a level of precision and efficiency that makes them good candidates for vision-based robot control. However, off-the-shelf methods lack temporal consistency and robustness that are mandatory for a stable feedback control. In this work, we develop a factor graph approach to enforce temporal consistency of the object pose estimates. In particular, the proposed approach: (i) incorporates object motion models, (ii) explicitly estimates the object pose measurement uncertainty, and (iii) integrates the above two components in an online optimization-based estimator. We demonstrate that with appropriate outlier rejection and smoothing using the proposed factor graph approach, we can significantly improve the results on standardized pose estimation benchmarks. We experimentally validate the stability of the proposed approach for a feedback-based robot control task in which the object is tracked by the camera attached to a torque controlled manipulator.

papers.title: DynoSLAM: Dynamic SLAM with Generative Graph Neural Networks for Real-World Social Navigation
papers.summary: DynoSLAM: Dynamic SLAM with Generative Graph Neural Networks for Real-World Social Navigation attacks the failure mode of standard GraphSLAM in crowded scenes: moving pedestrians violate the static-landmark assumption, so their observations become false factors that destabilize optimization and make downstream navigation unsafe. The paper challenges the common workflow of masking dynamics or bolting on constant-velocity heuristics, and also the newer habit of using deterministic single-agent neural priors that collapse under social ambiguity. Its pivot is from hardcoded motion extrapolation toward a tightly coupled, socially aware stochastic world model inside the SLAM factor graph. The mechanical shift is to embed a Graph Attention Network over pedestrian histories, run Monte Carlo autoregressive rollouts to sample multimodal future intents, and convert the rollout mean and covariance into a dynamic Mahalanobis kinematic factor that automatically stiffens when behavior is predictable and relaxes when interactions are uncertain. This is unusual because uncertainty is not an afterthought or a separate planner module; it directly modulates the graph objective and yields a probabilistic safety envelope for MPC/PPO controllers. In a 2D pyminisim social-navigation benchmark with HSFM pedestrians, 6,000 episodes, and 1–15 agents, DynoSLAM preserves retrospective localization accuracy while avoiding the “argmax problem” that makes deterministic GAT predictions jittery or collision-prone; the stochastic variant matches or beats baselines on prediction while providing calibrated covariance. The main limitation is that stochasticity is currently injected via perturbations in simulation, pointing toward future flow-matching world models and uncertainty-aware SLAM-control loops for real human-robot interaction.
papers.abstract: Traditional Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) algorithms rely heavily on the static environment assumption, which severely limits their applicability in real-world spaces populated by moving entities, such as pedestrians. In this work, we propose DynoSLAM, a tightly-coupled Dynamic GraphSLAM architecture that integrates socially-aware Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) directly into the factor graph optimization. Unlike conventional approaches that use rigid constant-velocity heuristics or deterministic single-agent neural priors, our framework formulates pedestrian motion forecasting as a stochastic World Model. By utilizing Monte Carlo rollouts from a trained GNN, we capture the multimodal epistemic uncertainty of human interactions and embed it into the SLAM graph via a dynamic Mahalanobis distance factor. We demonstrate through extensive simulated experiments that this stochastic formulation not only maintains highly accurate retrospective tracking but also prevents the optimization failures caused by the deterministic "argmax problem". Ultimately, extracting the empirical mean and covariance matrices of future pedestrian states provides a mathematically rigorous, probabilistic safety envelope for downstream local planners, enabling anticipatory and collision-free robot navigation in densely crowded environments.

papers.title: Repurposing and Evaluating the (In)Feasibility of Dataset Poisoning enabled Watermarking for Contrastive Learning
papers.summary: Repurposing and Evaluating the (In)Feasibility of Dataset Poisoning enabled Watermarking for Contrastive Learning examines a concrete security bottleneck in self-supervised CL: dataset owners often release third-party or web-crawled data without labels or training control, yet existing CL watermarking assumes access to losses or downstream tasks, while prior poisoning attacks are too brittle, low-success, and task-dependent to serve as reliable provenance signals. The paper first challenges the implicit assumption that attack success rate is the right yardstick, then pivots from “can we backdoor CL?” toward “can weak backdoor effects be converted into a dataset IP key?” The mechanical shift is a black-box watermarking pipeline that reuses poisoned samples as triggers, but verifies ownership with a unified density metric over model outputs instead of downstream misclassification: trigger-bearing queries should cluster more tightly than clean queries in feature, soft-label, or hard-label space, and a statistical test on this similarity gap becomes the verification lever. Unusually, the authors benchmark six representative data-poisoning-only CL attacks under one framework and show that PoisonedEncoder and CorruptEncoder are fundamentally unsuitable, while SSL-Backdoor and CTRL are fragile to resolution and architecture. On CIFAR10 and ImageNet100 with SimCLR, MoCo v2, BYOL, and SimSiam, BLTO offers the best fidelity-verifiability balance, whereas NA yields the strongest signals but at poor visual quality. The result points toward dataset-provenance research that treats distributional anomalies, not classifier errors, as the watermark carrier.
papers.abstract: Contrastive learning (CL) reduces annotation cost via auto-derived supervisory signals. Since large-scale in-house CL datasets are infeasible, reliance on third-party or internet data is common. Recent studies show CL models are vulnerable to data-poisoning backdoor attacks, but their generalization and robustness are underexplored. We systematically evaluate existing data-poisoning backdoor attacks on CL, revealing limitations: poor dataset adaptability, low success rates, limited portability, and restrictive assumptions (e.g., downstream task knowledge). Interestingly, trigger samples exhibit distinguishable statistical divergence from clean samples, which inspires repurposing it as a watermark for dataset IP protection. Direct repurposing is challenging due to low success rates; we overcome this by statistical verification using a unified density metric. We further propose a multi-level watermarking scheme adapting to feature-level, soft-label, or hard-label outputs in CL. Experiments show some backdoor attacks can be repurposed as effective watermarks with trade-offs among fidelity, verifiability, and robustness. This work demonstrates weak backdoor effects become reliable signals for dataset IP protection in challenging CL settings.

papers.title: Trojan Hippo: Weaponizing Agent Memory for Data Exfiltration
papers.summary: Trojan Hippo: Weaponizing Agent Memory for Data Exfiltration attacks the emerging failure mode where LLM agents with persistent memory become covert, cross-session exfiltration channels: a single untrusted tool call, such as a crafted email, plants a dormant instruction into long-term memory that later activates only when the user discusses sensitive topics like finance, health, tax, or identity, then uses outbound tools to leak the user’s own private data. The paper explicitly pivots away from prior memory-poisoning work that assumed direct memory writes or malicious users and toward a more realistic confused-deputy threat model in which the attacker only controls external content the agent reads. The mechanical shift is twofold: an OpenEvolve-based adaptive red-teaming loop automatically evolves payloads against a training environment and checks generalization on held-out test cases, and a capability-aware security/utility analysis replaces aggregate utility scores with per-workflow evaluation over seven assistant behaviors. Instantiated on a simulated email assistant with explicit memory, Mem0, RAG, and sliding-window context, the attack reaches 85–100% ASR against frontier OpenAI and Google models and can survive 100 benign sessions before triggering. The paper also probes four memory-layer defenses, showing that user-prompt-only, no-untrusted-write, and a length cap can sharply reduce ASR, while a provable IFC policy eliminates exfiltration but blocks send workflows and exposes a severe security-utility tradeoff. This points toward future AI research on memory-specific controls, dynamic red-team benchmarks, and deployment-aware secure agent architectures.
papers.abstract: Memory systems enable otherwise-stateless LLM agents to persist user information across sessions, but also introduce a new attack surface. We characterize the Trojan Hippo attack, a class of persistent memory attacks that operates in a more realistic threat model than prior memory poisoning work: the attacker plants a dormant payload into an agent's long-term memory via a single untrusted tool call (e.g., a crafted email), which activates only when the user later discusses sensitive topics such as finance, health, or identity, and exfiltrates high-value personal data to the attacker. While anecdotal demonstrations of such attacks have appeared against deployed systems, no prior work systematically evaluates them across heterogeneous memory architectures and defenses.We introduce a dynamic evaluation framework comprising two components: (1) an OpenEvolve-based adaptive red-teaming benchmark that stress-tests defenses and memory backends against continuously refined attacks, and (2) the first capability-aware security/utility analysis for persistent memory systems, enabling principled reasoning about defense deployment across different usage profiles. Instantiated on an email assistant across four memory backends (explicit tool memory, agentic memory, RAG, and sliding-window context), Trojan Hippo achieves up to 85-100 percent ASR against current frontier models from OpenAI and Google, with planted memories successfully activating even after 100 benign sessions. We evaluate four memory-system defenses inspired by basic security principles, finding they substantially reduce attack success rates (to as low as 0-5 percent), though at utility costs that vary widely with task requirements. Because of this substantial security-utility tradeoff, the effective real-world deployment of defenses remains an open challenge, which our evaluation framework is specifically designed to address.

papers.title: Stochastic Modeling of Human-Machine Authentication Channels under Partial Information Leakage
papers.summary: Stochastic Modeling of Human-Machine Authentication Channels under Partial Information Leakage attacks the brittle assumption that PIN security is binary—either intact or fully broken—by modeling smartphone/wearable PIN entry as a noisy human-IoT communication channel whose reliability degrades progressively as one, two, or three digits leak through shoulder-surfing or side-channel exposure. The paper pivots from conventional authentication evaluation and standard sequence prediction toward channel-recovery analysis: instead of asking only whether a PIN is compromised, it asks how much QoS and recoverability remain under partial symbol exposure. The mechanical shift is a context-conditioned probabilistic inference scheme in which missing digits are treated as latent variables and recovered from smoothed conditional frequency tables with Laplace smoothing and fallback global priors when contexts are sparse; unlike classical HMM-style workflows, it does not explicitly learn hidden-state transitions or emissions, but approximates cross-position dependence directly from observed digit contexts. Using over one million real-world four-digit PINs extracted from RockYou, the authors evaluate all single-, double-, and triple-digit leakage patterns and report position-dependent reliability metrics, showing strong prefix regularities and nontrivial recoverability even under severe uncertainty. The approach outperforms Decision Tree, Random Forest, Naive Bayes, and an HMM baseline, reaching 55.31% accuracy for one missing digit and 12.12% for three missing digits, while also improving top-k attack success and lowering expected guess rank. This points toward security research that treats partial compromise probabilistically, and toward future IoT authentication models that fuse physical-layer leakage, richer user data, and more adaptive probabilistic inference.
papers.abstract: Reliable and secure human-machine communication is fundamental to IoT and cyber-physical ecosystems, where smartphones and wearables commonly serve as authentication controllers. PIN-based authentication can be viewed as a low-bandwidth communication channel through which users transmit numeric credentials under practical constraints. However, conventional evaluations adopt a binary view of security-treating such channels as either fully secure or fully compromised-thereby overlooking the progressive reliability degradation caused by partial information leakage in real-world IoT settings. In this paper, we model the PIN entry process as a stochastic human-IoT communication system and propose a context-conditioned probabilistic inference framework to quantify reliability loss and Quality-of-Service degradation under partial symbol exposure. The proposed approach treats missing digits as latent variables and estimates them using smoothed conditional probability distributions with fallback priors. Unlike traditional sequential models that assume contiguous positional dependencies, the method does not explicitly parameterize hidden-state transitions or emissions; instead, it performs context-driven probabilistic inference to approximate latent dependencies across digit positions. Using over one million real-world four-digit PIN samples, we evaluate single-, double-, and triple-digit leakage scenarios and derive position-dependent reliability metrics. The proposed model achieves up to 55.31% prediction accuracy for one missing digit and 12.12% for three missing digits, while consistently outperforming a standard sequence-model baseline and classical machine learning models in terms of precision, recall, and F1-score. These results formalize PIN entry as a noisy human--IoT communication channel and demonstrate substantial reliability degradation under realistic partial exposure conditions.

papers.title: When Alignment Isn't Enough: Response-Path Attacks on LLM Agents
papers.summary: When Alignment Isn't Enough: Response-Path Attacks on LLM Agents attacks the security blind spot in Bring-Your-Own-Key agent deployments, where a user-configured third-party relay legitimately terminates TLS between frontend agent and backend LLM, creating a post-generation integrity gap: the relay can alter the model’s aligned answer before execution, so the agent acts on a forged transcript rather than the model’s approved output. The paper explicitly challenges the old assumption that RLHF, constitutional training, system prompts, or output filters are sufficient once the LLM is “safe,” and pivots the field from prompt-injection-centric thinking toward end-to-end response integrity. The mechanical shift is a relay-side attack model, Relay Tampering Attack (RTA), that rewrites schema-valid execution fields after alignment, using strategic orchestration over multi-turn goals, minimal field-level edits to tool names/arguments/finish reasons, and stealth restoration by resubmitting the tampered draft through the same upstream model to preserve style. Unlike nearby prompt-injection work, it attacks the agent’s execution input directly, not the model’s attention, and proves that response-path forgery strictly dominates prompt injection under a Dolev–Yao relay. Across AgentDojo and ASB with six models, RTA reaches up to 99.1% attack success and outperforms prompt-based baselines with modest overhead; case studies on OpenClaw and Claude Code show real deployment feasibility. Existing defenses fail to fully stop it, motivating a direction where agents require signed responses, attested relays, or telemetry-based detection such as the proposed time-channel defense.
papers.abstract: Bring-Your-Own-Key (BYOK) agent architectures let users route LLM traffic through third-party relays, creating a critical integrity gap: a malicious relay can modify an aligned LLM response after generation but before agent execution. We formalize this post-alignment tampering threat and show that, without end-to-end integrity, the relay can observe, suppress, or replace downstream messages, making even perfectly aligned LLMs ineffective against such attacks. We instantiate this threat as the Relay Tampering Attack (RTA), which performs multi-round strategic rewriting, minimal security-critical edits, and stealth restoration by resubmitting tampered outputs to the upstream LLM. Across AgentDojo and ASB with six LLMs, RTA achieves up to 99.1% attack success, outperforming prompt-injection baselines with modest overhead. Case studies on OpenClaw and Claude Code demonstrate real-world feasibility, and evaluations of four defenses show that none fully prevent RTA. Finally, we propose a time-based detection defense that mitigates RTA while preserving agent utility.

papers.title: On the Privacy of LLMs: An Ablation Study
papers.summary: On the Privacy of LLMs: An Ablation Study attacks a fragmentation problem in LLM security: membership inference, attribute inference, data extraction, and backdoor attacks are usually evaluated in isolation, so it is unclear which system choices actually drive privacy leakage in interactive and retrieval-augmented deployments. The paper pivots from one-attack-at-a-time benchmarking toward a unified threat model and notation spanning the LLM lifecycle, then reproduces representative attacks under a shared ablation framework to expose how architecture, scale, dataset/domain, retrieval stack, prompt style, and search budget reshape risk. The mechanical shift is not a new attack, but a controlled comparison protocol: S2MIA scores membership by BLEU/perplexity on QA-style RAG outputs; MBMIA masks hard-to-predict terms, reconstructs them under exact-match rules, and turns mask accuracy into a binary classifier; AIA uses fixed prefix/suffix prompts over synthetic conversations to infer PII attributes; DEA scores candidate secrets by next-token log-perplexity and applies Dijkstra search over format-constrained token graphs; BA is treated as trigger-based injection/exploitation. Unusually, the study shows MBMIA is near-ceiling across many models, while S2MIA is far more dataset-dependent, and DEA is driven more by repetition, candidate space, and memorization than by search depth. Across Enron, HealthcareMagic, and QA benchmarks, larger models and repeated PII amplify leakage, but prompt diversity can dilute extraction signals. The work points toward privacy research that is holistic, system-aware, and deployment-calibrated rather than benchmark-isolated.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in interactive and retrieval-augmented settings, raising significant privacy concerns. While attacks such as Membership Inference (MIA), Attribute Inference (AIA), Data Extraction (DEA), and Backdoor Attacks (BA) have been studied, they are typically analyzed in isolation, leaving a gap in understanding their behavior under common system factors. In this paper, we introduce a unified threat model and notation, reproduce a representative set of privacy attacks, and conduct a structured ablation study to evaluate the impact of key factors such as model architecture, scale, dataset characteristics, and retrieval configuration. Our analysis reveals clear differences across attack types. Membership inference attacks, particularly mask-based variants, exhibit strong and reliable signals, while backdoor attacks achieve consistently high success rates due to their trigger-based nature. In contrast, attribute inference and data extraction attacks remain more challenging, resulting in lower accuracy, yet they pose significant risks as they target sensitive personal information. Overall, these results highlight that privacy risks in LLM systems are highly context-dependent and driven by design choices, emphasizing the need for holistic evaluation and informed deployment practices.

papers.title: APIOT: Autonomous Vulnerability Management Across Bare-Metal Industrial OT Networks
papers.summary: APIOT: Autonomous Vulnerability Management Across Bare-Metal Industrial OT Networks attacks the bottleneck that has kept autonomous pentesting out of industrial control systems: bare-metal microcontrollers running Modbus/TCP and CoAP have no shell, filesystem, or POSIX affordances, so LLM agents cannot rely on the Linux/web playbooks used by prior autonomous attack systems. The paper pivots from exploit scripts and named vulnerabilities toward protocol-level reasoning over raw packet fields, parser semantics, and device state, turning autonomous security from “find a shell” into a discovery→exploitation→patching→verification mission loop. The mechanical shift is an action space of low-level protocol primitives plus a runtime governance layer, the overseer, that enforces repetition guards, crash blocking, stall detection, and phase-transition control rather than hoping the model self-corrects. Evaluated in an IoT Virtual Lab on Zephyr RTOS firmware across 290 runs, five frontier LLMs, three topologies, two impairment levels, and guided vs unguided settings, APIOT achieved 90.0% end-to-end mission success and 85.3% cross-model success overall. What differentiates it from nearby work is that it is not just autonomous attack generation: it performs full purple-team vulnerability management on bare-metal OT, including patching and verification, while exposing a structural reliability lever at runtime. The main limitation is a boundary at stateless CoAP over three-tier Edge-Fog-Cloud routing, where lateral-movement planning stalls. This points toward future LLM security agents that reason directly at protocol and firmware semantics, with governance treated as a first-class control surface for safety-critical autonomy.
papers.abstract: Bare-metal operational technology (OT) devices -- especially the microcontrollers running Modbus/TCP and CoAP at the base of industrial control systems -- have remained outside the reach of autonomous security attacks. Prior autonomous pentesting studies target Linux and web systems, whose shells and filesystems are familiar to LLM agents. Bare-metal OT has neither, so agents must reason directly over protocol fields and parser semantics. This requires new action-space designs and runtime controls, and opens new research questions about protocol-level exploit reasoning and its deployment envelope. We present APIOT (Autonomous Purple-teaming for Industrial OT), the first large language model (LLM) framework demonstrating an autonomous attack and remediation of bare-metal OT devices, achieving the full discovery -> exploitation -> patching -> verification cycle without step-by-step human intervention. We implemented and evaluated this framework on Zephyr RTOS firmware across heterogeneous industrial IoT (IIoT) topologies. Through 290 experiment runs spanning five frontier LLMs, three network topologies, two impairment levels, and guided versus unguided conditions, APIOT achieved a mission success rate of 90.0% on the full attack-remediation cycle. We found that the runtime governance layer (which we call an overseer) is a critical engineering variable: without it, agents exhibit systematic degenerate patterns, including repetition loops, missing crash verification, and reconnaissance deadlocks. Together, these findings carry two implications beyond our testbed. Attacker expertise is no longer the binding constraint on bare-metal OT exploitation, and defender threat models must now assume LLM-augmented adversaries capable of executing autonomous discovery-through-remediation cycles against industrial firmware.

papers.title: Privacy Preserving Machine Learning Workflow: from Anonymization to Personalized Differential Privacy Budgets in Federated Learning
papers.summary: Privacy Preserving Machine Learning Workflow: from Anonymization to Personalized Differential Privacy Budgets in Federated Learning attacks the practical bottleneck of training on sensitive tabular data when federated learning is not enough: even without centralizing records, gradients and aggregated weights still leak information, and heterogeneous clients can also poison the global model through client drift. The paper pivots away from the implicit assumption that one privacy setting fits all clients, and away from treating FL as a standalone privacy solution, toward a full workflow that composes local anonymization, drift screening, and differential privacy as separate control levers. The mechanical shift is twofold: first, clients locally anonymize quasi-identifiers with hierarchy-based generalization and the server harmonizes those transformations so all parties train at a common abstraction level; second, instead of applying one fixed global DP budget after FedAvg aggregation, each client receives a personalized noise multiplier derived from its average re-identification risk (ARIREC) over equivalence classes, so higher-risk clients are protected more aggressively before aggregation. The paper also formalizes client drift and proposes detecting it from model-weight divergences, not raw data, as a privacy-preserving poisoning filter. In a 10-client medical-record simulation from a global cancer dataset, the workflow is tested on a severity-score regression task with vanilla FL, FL+global DP, and FL+personalized DP; the personalized scheme slightly improves MAE and MRE over fixed-budget DP while preserving stronger privacy allocation. The work points toward privacy accounting that is client-aware, risk-adaptive, and compatible with non-IID federated deployments, with future extensions to untrusted servers and homomorphic encryption.
papers.abstract: The growing development of artificial intelligence based solutions, together with privacy legislation, has driven the rise of the so-called privacy preserving machine learning architectures, such as federated learning. While federated learning enables model training on decentralized data preventing their sharing and centralization, it still faces several challenges related to data integrity and privacy. This paper presents a comprehensive privacy preserving federated learning workflow for sensitive tabular data, including anonymization and differential privacy techniques. We also introduce a formal definition for the concept of client drift, together with ways of detecting it to mitigate poisoning attacks. Then, we detail a complete methodology for assigning personalized privacy budgets for global differential privacy to the different clients participating in the network, based on a re-identification risk metric. The proposed methodology is presented and tested on an openly available dataset of medical records. Within the experimental setup we show that the approach based on personalized budgets, compared to the architecture including global differential privacy with fixed privacy budget, achieves a better model performance in terms of two error metrics.

papers.title: Fight Poison with Poison: Enhancing Robustness in Few-shot Machine-Generated Text Detection with Adversarial Training
papers.summary: Fight Poison with Poison: Enhancing Robustness in Few-shot Machine-Generated Text Detection with Adversarial Training attacks the brittle regime where machine-generated text (MGT) detectors, especially RoBERTa-style classifiers, collapse under few-shot supervision and are easily evaded by humanizing rewrites that preserve meaning while erasing lexical artifacts. The paper pivots away from the usual assumption that more labeled data or stronger single-stage fine-tuning is the main fix, and instead adopts a threat-modeling loop: detection is hardened by training against an attacker that deliberately manufactures the failure mode. The mechanical shift is REACT, a co-evolutionary adversarial-training framework that couples a black-box, output-only surrogate attacker with the target detector; the attacker uses retrieval-augmented generation over a pool of training texts and previously generated rewrites to pick extreme human-like and machine-like anchors, then prompts an LLM to rewrite MGT into highly humanized adversaries. The detector is not simply trained on these poisons with cross-entropy; it adds a pairwise boundary contrastive loss that pulls same-label original/rewrite pairs together and pushes mismatched pairs apart, stabilizing few-shot representation geometry without relying on large in-batch negatives. This is unusual relative to nearby adversarial MGT work because the adversary is dynamic, retrieval-guided, and label-only aligned, rather than a fixed paraphraser or token perturber. Across DetectRL, OUTFOX, RAID, and SemEval at 32/64/128/256 shots, with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, ALBERT-Base-v2 surrogate, and 3 seeds, REACT improves average F1 by 4.95 points over 8 SOTA detectors and cuts attack success rate by 3.66 points, pointing toward adaptive attacker–defender training and low-FPR, security-oriented provenance detection.
papers.abstract: Machine-generated text (MGT) detection is critical for regulating online information ecosystems, yet existing detectors often underperform in few-shot settings and remain vulnerable to adversarial, humanizing attacks. To build accurate and robust detectors under limited supervision, we adopt a threat-modeling perspective and study detector vulnerabilities from an attacker's viewpoint under an output-only black-box setting. Motivated by this perspective, we propose RAG-GuidEd Attacker Strengthens ConTrastive Few-shot Detector (REACT), an adversarial training framework that improves both few-shot detection performance and robustness against attacks. REACT couples a humanization-oriented attacker with a target detector: the attacker leverages retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to craft highly human-like adversarial examples to evade detection, while the detector learns from these adversaries with a contrastive objective to stabilize few-shot representation learning and enhance robustness. We alternately update the attacker and the detector to enable their co-evolution. Experiments on 4 datasets with 4 shot sizes and 3 random seeds show that REACT improves average detection F1 by 4.95 points over 8 state-of-the-art (SOTA) detectors and reduces the average attack success rate (ASR) under 4 strong attacks by 3.66 percentage points.

papers.title: FunFuzz: An LLM-Powered Evolutionary Fuzzing Framework
papers.summary: FunFuzz: An LLM-Powered Evolutionary Fuzzing Framework attacks a concrete bottleneck in LLM-based compiler fuzzing: prompt-sensitive stochastic generation collapses into redundant program styles, so early samples and sampling variance can trap search in shallow semantic neighborhoods and miss rare compiler behaviors. It implicitly challenges the assumption that one distilled prompt plus feedback-guided sampling is enough, and pivots from single-prompt LLM fuzzing toward a multi-island evolutionary search that preserves parallel diversity while still exploiting compiler feedback. The mechanical shift is threefold: documentation is first distilled into a shared base prompt, then multiple islands are initialized with different topic-specific seed instructions sampled at different temperatures, and finally each island evolves independently with coverage-based fitness over instrumented compiler source lines, softmax parent selection, and periodic soft migration of elite candidates rather than full island resets. Crash discovery is separated from exploration by using compiler-internal failure signals as an oracle for deduplicated bug fingerprints, while normalization strips formatting noise and splits multi-program outputs into compileable units. Compared with nearby work like Fuzz4All, FunFuzz is unusual in treating prompt diversity as an evolutionary resource and in using per-island coverage frontiers plus migration to prevent cross-run homogenization; compared with Kitten, it trades sheer mutation throughput for from-scratch LLM synthesis without curated corpora. On repeated 24-hour campaigns over GCC and Clang for C and C++, it reports higher coverage and more unique failure-triggering inputs, with warm-start corpora giving an upper bound. The paper points toward hybrid AI security testing systems where LLMs, evolutionary search, and target-specific oracles co-design exploration.
papers.abstract: Modern fuzzers increasingly use Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate structured inputs, but LLM-driven fuzzing is sensitive to prompt initialization and sampling variance, which can reduce exploration efficiency and lead to redundant inputs. We present FunFuzz, a multi-island evolutionary fuzzing framework that runs several isolated searches in parallel and periodically migrates high-value candidates to maintain diversity. FunFuzz derives initial generation prompts from documentation and initializes islands with topic-specific instructions, then continuously adapts prompts using feedback-guided selection. During fuzzing, candidates are prioritized by incremental compiler coverage, while compiler-internal failure signals are used to identify crash-inducing inputs. We evaluate FunFuzz on compiler fuzzing, where inputs are source programs and success is measured by compiler coverage and unique compiler-internal failures. Across repeated 24-hour campaigns on GCC and Clang, FunFuzz achieves higher compiler coverage than previous LLM-driven baselines and discovers more unique failure-triggering inputs.

papers.title: Conventional Commit Classification using Large Language Models and Prompt Engineering
papers.summary: Conventional Commit Classification using Large Language Models and Prompt Engineering attacks the practical bottleneck that many software projects still write unstructured commit messages, forcing downstream tools like changelog generators and semantic-versioning pipelines to guess the commit type by hand or through supervised classifiers that require curated labels, retraining, and feature maintenance. The paper pivots from ML/DL commit classifiers trained on large labeled corpora toward a training-free, prompt-conditioned LLM workflow that treats classification as an inference-time control problem rather than a learned task-specific model. The mechanical shift is to feed code diffs, not just commit text, directly into open-source instruction models and vary the prompt regime—zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought—across Mistral-7B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B, and DeepSeek-R1-32B on a balanced 3,200-commit dataset mined from InfluxDB. Unlike nearby work that fine-tunes code models or relies on proprietary in-context baselines, this study isolates prompt design as the main lever and adds decision-priority rules plus representative labeled examples as implicit supervision. The key result is that few-shot prompting is consistently strongest, chain-of-thought does not help this structured label task, and the larger DeepSeek-R1-32B yields the best overall performance, implying that model scale and example selection matter more than explicit reasoning. The limitation is narrow dataset scope and no direct comparison to traditional baselines, but the paper points toward a future of prompt-optimized, label-free repository mining and hybrid LLM workflows for release automation, maintenance analytics, and software evolution tooling.
papers.abstract: Conventional commits provide a structured format for writing commit messages, which improves readability, software maintenance, and enables automation tools such as changelog generators and semantic versioning systems. Existing approaches to conventional commit classification typically rely on ML/DL models trained on large labeled datasets. In this paper, we investigated a training-free alternative by leveraging large language models (LLMs) through prompt engineering. Rather than building a task-specific classifier, we evaluate three prompting strategies, such as zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought, across three open-source LLMs of varying scale: Mistral-7B-Instruct, LLaMA-3-8B, and DeepSeek-R1-32B. Classification is performed directly on code diffs extracted from a balanced dataset of 3,200 commits mined from the InfluxDB repository, without any model fine-tuning. Our results show that few-shot prompting consistently achieves the highest accuracy, while chain-of-thought prompting does not yield additional gains for this classification task. Among the evaluated models, DeepSeek-R1-32B achieves the strongest overall performance, suggesting that model scale plays a meaningful role in conventional commit classification. These findings provide practical guidance for researchers and practitioners seeking to automate commit classification without the overhead of curating and maintaining labeled training data.

papers.title: DocSync: Agentic Documentation Maintenance via Critic-Guided Reflexion
papers.summary: DocSync: Agentic Documentation Maintenance via Critic-Guided Reflexion attacks the specific failure mode where docstrings and README-style prose drift from executable code, producing “documentation debt” that static linters cannot detect because they only see tags, not semantic truth, while ordinary LLMs hallucinate when asked to rewrite stale docs without structural grounding. The paper pivots from treating documentation as a one-shot text generation problem toward treating it as an iterative, code-constrained maintenance loop: instead of asking a model to summarize code once, DocSync reconstructs the update process around change impact analysis, AST-derived dependency context, retrieval-augmented context from similar documentation, and a Reflexion-style critic that judges whether the candidate update is faithful to source code before allowing a rewrite. The mechanical shift is the combination of Tree-sitter AST injection plus RAG with a critic-guided self-correction loop on a LoRA-adapted Phi-3 Mini, so the model repairs its own output against the code rather than relying on scale alone. Evaluated on a proxy Python code-to-text maintenance task from CodeXGLUE, with stale docs simulated by truncating ground truth, DocSync outperforms CodeT5-base on semantic alignment, summary-line faithfulness, and LLM-as-judge preference, while the critic loop gives measurable gains without increasing parameter count. Its limitation is that this is still a small, benchmark-derived setting, but it points toward historical-replay documentation agents with execution-based verification and maintainer-in-the-loop refinement.
papers.abstract: Software documentation frequently drifts from executable logic as codebases evolve, creating technical debt that degrades maintainability and causes downstream API misuse. While static analysis tools can detect the absence of documentation, they cannot evaluate its semantic consistency. Conversely, standard Large Language Models (LLMs) offer generative flexibility but frequently hallucinate when updating documentation without deep structural awareness of the underlying code. To address this gap, we propose DocSync, an agentic workflow that frames documentation maintenance as a structurally grounded, iterative generation task. DocSync bridges syntactic changes and natural language descriptions by fusing Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) representations and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to provide dependency-aware context. Furthermore, to ensure factual consistency, we incorporate a critic-guided refinement loop based on the Reflexion paradigm, allowing the model to self-correct candidate updates against the source code. We empirically evaluate a resource-constrained implementation of DocSync-using a LoRA-adapted small language model - on a proxy code-to-text maintenance task. Our findings demonstrate that this AST-aware agentic approach substantially outperforms standard encoder-decoder baselines across semantic alignment, summary-line faithfulness, and automated judge preferences (e.g., achieving an automated judge score of 3.44/5.0 compared to 1.91 for CodeT5-base). Crucially, the iterative critic loop yields measurable improvements in semantic correctness without requiring scaled-up parameter counts. These results provide strong evidence that coupling structural retrieval with agentic refinement is a highly promising direction for autonomously mitigating documentation debt.

papers.title: The Conversations Beneath the Code: Triadic Data for Long-Horizon Software Engineering Agents
papers.summary: The Conversations Beneath the Code: Triadic Data for Long-Horizon Software Engineering Agents argues that frontier SWE agents are failing not because they cannot solve isolated coding subtasks, but because they lose context across weeks of ambiguous, multi-engineer work, where senior engineering is actually decided. It challenges the prevailing assumption that larger GitHub scrapes, more solo-agent trajectories, or even open human-AI dialogue logs are the right substrate, and pivots from artifact-centric training toward context-forming deliberation: human-human conversations, the downstream human-AI session that consumes that context, and the surrounding cross-functional work. The mechanical shift is a new data unit, the triad, instantiated through two complementary capture products: long-horizon expert trajectories recorded with stimulated-recall protocols, and simulated cross-functional companies where senior engineers, PMs, designers, and data scientists work on fictional but realistic deliverables over shared infrastructure for one-to-three week spans. Unlike nearby work on dyadic chat logs or self-play, this makes the hidden negotiation trail—Slack threads, design reviews, on-call handoffs, code review, IDE telemetry, and recall transcripts—first-class training signal. The paper also adds a four-tier evidence framework for corpus quality: mechanical verification, statistical corpus characterization, probe fine-tuning, and pre-registered blind evaluation. Its claim is not that triadic data is a benchmark fix, but that it is the empirical key to long-horizon, drift-prone, cross-functional agent training, with visual SWE and security reasoning named as natural extensions.
papers.abstract: Frontier software engineering agents have saturated short-horizon benchmarks while regressing on the work that constitutes senior engineering: long-horizon, multi-engineer, ambiguous-specification deliverables. This paper takes a position on what training data is needed to close the gap. The substrate for the next generation of SWE agents is neither larger GitHub scrapes nor more solo-agent trajectories nor -- sufficient by itself -- open human-AI dialogue logs. It is triadic data: synchronized capture of the human-human conversations where engineering context is formed, the human-AI sessions where that context is partially consumed, and the multi-week cross-functional work that surrounds both. We argue that the canonical instantiation of triadic data is two complementary products: long-horizon expert trajectories captured under stimulated-recall protocols, and simulated cross-functional companies -- instrumented teams of senior engineers, product managers, designers, and data scientists working through ambiguous deliverables on shared infrastructure. We further specify a four-tier evidence framework through which any such corpus -- triadic or otherwise -- must justify its quality to a fine-tuning researcher: mechanical verification, statistical corpus characterization, probe experiments, and pre-registered blind evaluation. We argue that this data is capturable in 12-18 months with methods already mature in adjacent fields, that it is the empirical key to four open questions in agent training, and that the field's near-term research agenda should include it explicitly.

papers.title: Causal Software Engineering: A Vision and Roadmap
papers.summary: Causal Software Engineering: A Vision and Roadmap attacks a recurring failure mode in modern software practice: engineers must decide whether to roll out, reconfigure, test, or remediate under shifting load, traffic mix, and operational context, yet today’s AI support—anomaly detection, forecasting, AIOps triage, and LLM-generated explanations—mostly returns correlations, not decision-grade answers to “what happens if we act?” The paper pivots the field from post hoc pattern mining and attribution toward intervention-aware, counterfactual software engineering, arguing that releases, feature flags, canaries, and mitigations should be treated as causal interventions on a system-in-environment rather than as mere events in logs. Its core mechanical shift is to externalize causal assumptions into lightweight, versioned artifacts: causal design specs that name outcomes, admissible interventions, and confounders; intervention logs that record what changed alongside concurrent changes; and living causal models that instantiate those assumptions with telemetry and estimate P(Y|do(X=x)) with uncertainty bounds plus refutation checks. Unusual relative to nearby SE/AI work, it does not propose a new predictor or RCA algorithm; instead it defines a causal-first workflow spanning requirements, architecture, testing, debugging, operations, and governance, organized by causal readiness levels and a benchmark agenda for intervention-effect, counterfactual-incident, and causal-testing datasets. The claimed payoff is auditable causal claims, safer rollout decisions, more credible root-cause explanations, and governed causal copilots, while the limitation is that progress depends on stable causal graphs, identifiability under evolution, and shared evaluation standards.
papers.abstract: Software engineering increasingly involves making high-stakes decisions under uncertainty, using signals from code, field data, and socio-technical processes. Recent AI-driven support (e.g., anomaly detection, predictive analytics, AIOps, as well as LLM-based agents) has amplified engineers' ability to detect patterns and synthesize content and recommendations, but many critical questions are interventional or counterfactual: What is the expected impact of changing a load-balancing strategy? Would an outage have been avoided under a different release plan? Correlational models answer "what tends to co-occur"; they struggle to answer "what would happen if we act." We propose Causal Software Engineering (CSE) as a future paradigm in which causal models and causal reasoning systematically inform activities across the software lifecycle, augmenting existing practices with explicit assumptions, uncertainty-aware effect estimates, and counterfactual diagnosis. We outline (i) a causal-first workflow view spanning development and operations, (ii) a staged roadmap for tools and organizational adoption, and (iii) an evaluation and benchmark agenda for measuring progress.

papers.title: LLM-Assisted Repository-Level Generation with Structured Spec-Driven Engineering
papers.summary: LLM-Assisted Repository-Level Generation with Structured Spec-Driven Engineering attacks the bottleneck that today’s code LLMs are credible at isolated functions but become fragile when asked to synthesize coherent repository-scale systems, where free-form prompts are ambiguous and hard to verify. The paper challenges the implicit workflow assumption that “more natural language” can bridge requirements to implementation, and pivots from prompt-only generation toward structured spec-driven engineering (SSDE): using Gherkin scenarios, domain models, and model-layer signatures as the LLM’s intermediate control surface. The mechanical shift is not speculative model scaling but a specification-first generation loop in which a controller template plus structured artifacts guide Python MVC business-logic synthesis, and correctness is measured by executing human-verified unit tests aligned to those specifications. In a pilot study on three GitHub MVC systems from distinct domains, the authors run five LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4.5, Qwen 3 Coder, GPT-5.1, GPT-5 nano, Llama 3.2 3B) across multiple input combinations and 10 stochastic repetitions each. Structured inputs consistently lift test pass rate relative to natural-language-only baselines; Gherkin plus models is often strongest, while adding signatures improves average accuracy and reduces variance, though some configurations still underperform and error spikes remain. The unusual contribution is treating specs as verifiable, machine-actionable intermediaries rather than generation targets. The main implication is a research vector toward agentic, static-analysis-aware, spec-anchored repository synthesis where LLMs operate inside a feedback loop over structured artifacts instead of improvising from prose.
papers.abstract: State-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in code generation at the function level. However, the output quality significantly declines when scaling to repository-level systems. Current workflows relying only on natural language prompts suffer from inherent ambiguity and a lack of verifiability. To address this, we propose structured spec-driven engineering (SSDE), a paradigm that leverages structured artifacts to guide LLM generation. We argue that structured specifications as LLM inputs make high-quality, repository-level code generation a tangible goal, while at the same time offering superior verifiability, leading to significant potential for improvement. We first investigate the feasibility of this vision through a pilot study generating Model-View-Controller (MVC) business logic for three software systems using five LLMs, and then highlight the potential, challenges, and future roadmap for SSDE.

papers.title: AI-Generated Smells: An Analysis of Code and Architecture in LLM and Agent-Driven Development
papers.summary: AI-Generated Smells: An Analysis of Code and Architecture in LLM and Agent-Driven Development audits a blind spot in AI software engineering: success is usually scored by whether code runs, while long-term maintainability, coupling, and architectural rot are treated as secondary. The paper challenges the older assumption that better prompts or larger models should steadily improve code quality, and pivots from prompt-driven code synthesis toward architectural complexity management. Its mechanical shift is a two-scale static-smell analysis with PyExamine: first, 90 CodeContest problems solved by multiple LLMs under zero-shot and few-shot conditions, compared against human submissions; second, MetaGPT-generated multi-file Python repositories for five scenarios, each advanced through four increasingly demanding requirement stages. The study records code-, structural-, and architectural-smell counts plus Total Lines of Code, Total Number of Files, and Agent Action Count, then uses Spearman and Kruskal-Wallis tests to relate volume to decay. The unusual contribution is the “machine signature” of defects: humans skew toward state-management issues like Temporal Field, while agents disproportionately produce Long Method in small tasks and God Class, High Response for a Class, Scattered Functionality, and Unstable Dependency in larger systems. Its central claim is a Volume-Quality Inverse Law, with TLoC nearly perfectly predicting architectural smells, while richer prompting fails to help. The result suggests future AI coding systems need explicit architectural foresight, refactoring-aware loops, and metrics for semantic cohesion rather than merely executability.
papers.abstract: The promise of Large Language Models in automated software engineering is often measured by functional correctness, overlooking the critical issue of long term maintainability. This paper presents a systematic audit of technical debt in AI-generated software, revealing that AI does not eliminate flaws but rather introduces a distinct machine signature of defects. Our multi-scale analysis, spanning single-file algorithmic tasks and complex, agent generated systems, identifies a fundamental Reasoning-Complexity Trade-off: as models become more capable, they generate increasingly bloated and coupled code. This architectural decay is so pronounced that we establish a Volume-Quality Inverse Law, where code volume is a near perfect predictor of structural degradation. Crucially, we demonstrate that neither functional correctness nor detailed prompting mitigates this decay. These findings challenge the current paradigm of prompt-driven generation, reframing the central problem of AI-based software engineering from one of code generation to one of architectural complexity management. We conclude that future progress depends on equipping agents with explicit architectural foresight to ensure the software they build is not just functional, but also maintainable.

papers.title: FedQueue: Queue-Aware Federated Learning for Cross-Facility HPC Training
papers.summary: FedQueue: Queue-Aware Federated Learning for Cross-Facility HPC Training attacks a systems bottleneck that most FL papers ignore: in cross-facility HPC, batch-scheduler admission delays can last minutes to hours and dominate wall-clock time, so the real obstacle is not gradient communication but unpredictable queueing that makes synchronous rounds stall and asynchronous updates go stale. The paper pivots from assuming delay is an exogenous nuisance toward treating scheduler latency as a control signal inside the learning loop. The mechanical shift is queue-aware orchestration: the server predicts each facility’s queue delay online with EWMA, budgets per-client job time and local SGD steps from that prediction, enforces cutoff-based admission that buffers late arrivals instead of discarding them, and applies staleness-aware aggregation with inverse learning-rate scaling to stabilize heterogeneous step counts. This is distinct from prior throughput-profiling methods such as FedCompass, because FedQueue explicitly models admission delay and shapes the staleness distribution rather than only balancing compute. The setting is real cross-facility deployment across four production HPC sites using APPFL and Globus on a fine-tuning task with LLaMA2-7B on SMolInstruct, plus controlled MNIST/CNN simulations under high queue variance and non-IID partitions. The paper proves non-convex O(1/√R) convergence under bounded staleness, shows bounded-staleness guarantees with high probability under prediction error, and reports 20.5% better real deployment loss and about 34% faster time-to-target in simulation. This points toward FL systems where scheduler prediction, deadline control, and optimization are co-designed.
papers.abstract: Federated learning (FL) across multiple HPC facilities faces stochastic admission delays from batch schedulers that dominate wall-clock time. Synchronous FL suffers from severe stragglers, while asynchronous FL accumulates stale updates when queues spike. We propose FedQueue, a queue-aware FL protocol that incorporates scheduler delays directly into training and aggregation, which (i) predicts per-facility queue delays online to budget local work, (ii) applies cutoff-based admission that buffers late arrivals to bound staleness, and (iii) performs staleness-aware aggregation to stabilize heterogeneous local workloads. We prove the convergence for non-convex objectives at rate $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{R})$ under bounded staleness, and show that the admission controls yield bounded staleness with high probability under queue-prediction error. Real-world cross-facility deployment of FedQueue shows 20.5% improvement over baseline algorithms. Controlled queue simulations demonstrate robust improvement over the baselines; in particular, about 34% reduction in time to reach a target accuracy level under high queue variance and non-IID partitions.

papers.title: FedPLT: Scalable, Resource-Efficient, and Heterogeneity-Aware Federated Learning via Partial Layer Training
papers.summary: FedPLT: Scalable, Resource-Efficient, and Heterogeneity-Aware Federated Learning via Partial Layer Training attacks the bottleneck that standard federated learning still spends full-model compute and uplink on clients whose CPUs, memory, and bandwidth differ wildly, making FedAvg brittle under non-IID data, stragglers, and large models. It implicitly challenges the assumption behind both sub-model training and partial-parameter training that “less training” can be achieved by random dropout, fixed truncation, or freezing layer prefixes without disturbing optimization geometry; the paper pivots from coarse, biased partial updates toward structured partial-layer training that preserves full-model-like learning dynamics. The mechanical shift is to decompose each trainable layer into equal-sized sub-layers, then assign clients a fixed, rotational subset of blocks according to a capacity-dependent layer-wise allocation vector Qk derived from a balanced contribution target Xk; clients run the full forward pass but backpropagate and upload only assigned blocks, and the server aggregates each block only over clients that trained it. That makes the method unlike FedPMT, HeteroFL, FedDrop, or FedRolex, which either concentrate updates in suffix layers, induce imbalanced exposure, or inject round-to-round mask variance. The paper also extends Optimal Client Sampling so the budget counts partial training ratios rk, not just client count, reducing sampling variance under communication constraints. On Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10 with FCNs and ResNet-8 under Dirichlet non-IID splits, FedPLT matches or beats FedAvg while cutting trainable parameters by 71%-82%, lowering variance, and mitigating stragglers; this points toward jointly capacity-aware, blockwise, and variance-optimal federated optimization for IoT-scale edge deployment.
papers.abstract: Federated Learning (FL) has gained significant attention in distributed machine learning by enabling collaborative model training across decentralized system while preserving data privacy. Although extensive research has addressed statistical data heterogeneity, FL still faces several challenges, including high communication and computation overheads and severe device heterogeneity, which require further investigation. Prior work has addressed these issues through sub-model training and partial parameter training. However, such methods often suffer from inconsistent parameter distributions across clients, inaccurate global loss estimation, and increased bias and variance. Guided by our empirical analysis, we propose FedPLT (Federated Learning with Partial Layer Training), an innovative and structured partial parameter training approach that exhibits training behavior similar to full model training while assigning client-specific portions of the model according to their communication and computational capabilities. In addition, we evaluate the performance of FedPLT when combined with optimal client sampling under communication constraints. We show that this integration improves FL performance by reducing sampling variance under the same communication budget. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that FedPLT achieves performance comparable to, or even surpassing, that of full-model training (i.e., FedAvg), while requiring significantly fewer trainable parameters per client. Moreover, FedPLT outperforms existing methods in highly heterogeneous environments, effectively adapts to client resource constraints, and reduces the number of straggling clients. In particular, FedPLT reduces the number of trainable parameters by 71%-82% while achieving performance on par with full-model training.

papers.title: Caliper-in-the-Loop: Black-Box Optimization for Hyperledger Fabric Performance Tuning
papers.summary: Caliper-in-the-Loop: Black-Box Optimization for Hyperledger Fabric Performance Tuning attacks the practical bottleneck that Hyperledger Fabric throughput is governed by hundreds of interacting peer, orderer, batching, channel, and state settings, making manual “knob turning” slow, brittle, and hard to reproduce. The paper implicitly challenges the common assumption that Fabric tuning should be done by human intuition or one-off heuristic search, and pivots from configuration-by-expertise toward optimization-by-observation: benchmark the network, treat TPS as a noisy black-box response, and let the optimizer decide the next deployment. The mechanical shift is an end-to-end closed loop that materializes a candidate config from Fabric files, instantiates the network, runs Hyperledger Caliper on a fixed FabCar workload, parses the TPS report, and feeds that observation back into Bayesian optimization with dimensionality reduction. This is unusual because it does not merely fit a surrogate offline; it operationalizes the benchmark itself as the acquisition environment and compares 16 BO+DR pairings across a 317-dimensional mixed-type search space, initialized with Latin Hypercube sampling and run on dedicated Yandex Cloud VMs. Relative to nearby blockchain self-tuning work, the emphasis is on sample efficiency under high dimensionality and on explicitly measuring noise, infeasible deployments, and baseline sensitivity. The best result, DYCORS-PCA, reaches a 12% TPS gain over the first evaluated configuration, while MPI-REMBO reaches 9%, suggesting that future Fabric management may move toward transferable, closed-loop, constraint-aware tuning pipelines that reuse benchmark history across deployments and eventually optimize throughput jointly with latency and reliability.
papers.abstract: Hyperledger Fabric performance depends on many interacting configuration parameters, making manual tuning difficult. We study automated throughput tuning by treating benchmarking as a noisy black-box optimization problem and applying Bayesian optimization (BO) with dimensionality reduction (DR).
