We implement an end-to-end Caliper-in-the-loop pipeline that deploys candidate configurations, benchmarks them, and updates the optimizer from observed throughput. The search space, derived from Fabric configuration files, has 317 dimensions. In a cloud testbed, we evaluate 16 BO+DR variants and a random-search baseline. The best method, DYCORS-PCA, achieves a 12% TPS improvement relative to the first evaluated configuration, while MPI-REMBO achieves 9%. These results suggest that BO with DR is a practical approach for high-dimensional Hyperledger Fabric tuning, while also highlighting the role of measurement noise in interpreting gains.

papers.title: (POSTER) From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications
papers.summary: (POSTER) From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications attacks the “blank page bottleneck” in sensor applications, where scientists can collect streams but struggle to turn them into portable Pegasus workflows spanning edge, cloud, and specialized devices because workflow logic, containerization, provisioning, and placement demand too much infrastructure expertise. It pivots from code-first, greenfield workflow engineering toward intent-first pattern reuse: instead of asking users to write a full distributed application, the method starts from a validated Pegasus DAG template and lets an AI assistant adapt the pattern to a new scientific use case while preserving dependency structure. The core mechanical shift is a 5-step loop—describe science, ground with a prior workflow, generate a skeleton, refine with human-in-the-loop debugging, then scale via Pegasus catalogs and HTCondor placement—so AI generates executable workflow code but configuration, site mappings, and resource placement carry the edge-to-core deployment burden. Unusually, the paper grounds LLM assistance in explicit, readable, execution-agnostic Pegasus workflows rather than standalone scripts, and demonstrates cross-domain chaining from Orcasound hydrophones to air quality, earthquake, and soil moisture pipelines on FABRIC, including BlueField-3 DPUs and Raspberry Pis without redesigning the abstract DAG. From a novice user perspective, runnable workflows emerge in 3–4 prompts and 1–1.5 days per workflow, with most effort shifting to runtime debugging rather than workflow logic. The limitation is that the evaluation measures development productivity more than application performance, pointing toward AI systems that co-design workflows, placement, and execution feedback across the computing continuum.
papers.abstract: Scientists increasingly rely on sensor-based data; however transforming raw streams into insights across the edge-to-cloud continuum remains difficult due to the breadth of expertise required to coordinate the necessary data and computation flow. This paper introduces a pattern-based, AI-assisted methodology for rapid development of sensor-driven applications. Using Pegasus workflows executing on the FABRIC testbed, we demonstrate a 5-step development loop that shifts workflow construction and deployment from code-first to intent-first design. Starting from an existing Orcasound hydrophone workflow as a reusable template, we generate and refine workflows for air quality, earthquake, and soil moisture monitoring applications. We further show how these workflows extend to edge resources-including BlueField-3 DPUs and Raspberry Pis-through configuration and placement rather than workflow redesign. Our evaluation, from the perspective of a novice Pegasus user, shows that AI-assisted pattern reuse compresses multi-stage workflow development to 1-1.5 days per workflow while preserving the rigor and portability of workflow-based execution.

papers.title: From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications
papers.summary: From Sensors to Insight: Rapid, Edge-to-Core Application Development for Sensor-Driven Applications attacks the “blank page bottleneck” in sensor science, where moving from a sensing question to a runnable edge-to-cloud pipeline is slowed by workflow syntax, containerization, provisioning, and emerging edge hardware like DPUs. It challenges the assumption that scientists should author these systems greenfield or hand-code infrastructure details, and pivots from code-first deployment toward intent-first, pattern-grounded workflow reuse. The mechanical shift is a five-step loop on Pegasus over FABRIC: describe the science in plain English, ground the request with a validated Pegasus template, have an AI assistant generate a production-ready workflow skeleton, refine it with human-in-the-loop debugging and resource tuning, then scale the same abstract DAG across laptop, cluster, cloud, or edge placements without redesign. The unusual move is not unconstrained code generation, but constraining Claude and other LLMs with an existing Orcasound hydrophone workflow so task dependencies stay intact while domain-specific ingestion, preprocessing, inference, and outputs are swapped for air quality, earthquake, and soil moisture applications. The paper also shows edge integration by steering I/O-heavy stages to FABRIC DPUs and even a Raspberry Pi via standard Pegasus/HTCondor site mappings, preserving portability and provenance. Across case studies, the main result is faster time-to-first-runnable-workflow and reduced iteration effort, though peak application performance is not the evaluation target. This points toward future AI workflow agents that learn from validated scientific patterns, suggest placements and optimizations, and support longer-lived distributed sensor pipelines.
papers.abstract: Scientists increasingly rely on sensor-based data, yet transforming raw streams into insights across the edge-to-cloud continuum remains difficult. Provisioning heterogeneous infrastructure and managing execution on emerging platforms like Data Processing Units typically requires cross-domain expertise, creating significant barriers to rapid prototyping. This paper introduces an experience-driven methodology for the rapid development of sensor-driven applications. By combining pattern-based workflow engineering with AI-assisted development-implemented via Pegasus on the FABRIC testbed - we utilize an existing Orcasound hydrophone workflow as a reusable template. We introduce a pattern-based engineering methodology to generate and refine workflows for air quality, earthquake, and soil moisture monitoring. Furthermore, we show how these abstract structures are extended to edge resources through modular configuration and placement. Our evaluation focuses on user productivity and practical lessons rather than peak performance. Through these case studies, we illustrate how AI-assisted, pattern-based development lowers the entry barrier for non-experts and enables iterative exploration of sensor-driven applications across distributed infrastructures.

papers.title: Evolving Token Communication with Parametric Memory Network
papers.summary: Evolving Token Communication with Parametric Memory Network attacks the bandwidth bottleneck in semantic token communication for wireless image transmission over MIMO fading channels: even when tokens are compact, sending full semantic tokens still wastes capacity, while prior fixes mostly rely on token dropping, adaptive compression, or explicit caches. The paper pivots from “transmit more cleverly” and “retrieve from external memory” toward “transmit only a consistent prefix and reconstruct the missing suffix inside the receiver.” Its mechanical shift is a prefix-truncation protocol plus a parametric memory network whose semantic memory is stored implicitly in learnable weights rather than a datastore lookup; full tokens are first quantized into a codebook, truncated tokens are paired with labels of their corresponding full tokens, and kNN-based teacher distributions are distilled into a pretrained GPT-2-based recovery module that predicts codeword distributions for incomplete tokens and recovers the full token by expectation over codebook entries. This makes inference a forward pass, not nearest-neighbor retrieval, which is unusual relative to nearby evolving-memory and knowledge-assisted semantic communication work. The system is tested on 64×64 FFHQ images with a Swin Transformer encoder/decoder over 2×2 MIMO Rayleigh fading channels, at SNR 0–20 dB and bandwidth ratios down to 1/40 effective CBR. An online evolution loop periodically retrains the memory and the whole system on newly observed test samples, improving robustness under distribution shift. The method consistently beats the evolving cache benchmark, by up to 1.09 dB PSNR, pointing toward adaptive, parameterized receiver-side semantic memory as a future direction for efficient communication.
papers.abstract: Token communication has emerged as a promising framework for efficient wireless transmission by representing source data as compact semantic tokens. However, transmitting full semantic tokens still incurs considerable communication overhead. In this paper, we propose an evolving semantic token communication system with a parametric memory network over MIMO fading channels. Specifically, only an equal-length prefix of each semantic token is transmitted, which reduces transmission cost while preserving a consistent token structure for receiver-side recovery. At the receiver, a parametric memory network is introduced to reconstruct the missing suffix information from the received token prefixes, where semantic memory is stored implicitly in the network parameters. To realize this design, full semantic tokens are first organized into a codebook, and truncated tokens are paired with the codeword labels of their corresponding full tokens. Based on these token-label pairs, kNN-based teacher distributions are constructed to fine-tune a pretrained GPT-2-based recovery module, which learns to infer the codeword distribution of each incomplete token and recover the corresponding complete semantic token. In addition, an online evolution strategy is developed to periodically update the parametric memory network and the entire system using newly observed test samples, thereby improving adaptability under distribution shifts. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method consistently outperforms the existing evolving memory benchmark under different channel conditions and channel bandwidth ratios, with up to 1.09 dB PSNR improvement.

papers.title: SwiftChannel: Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Deep Learning-Based 5G Channel Estimation
papers.summary: SwiftChannel: Algorithm-Hardware Co-Design for Deep Learning-Based 5G Channel Estimation attacks the bottleneck that 5G MIMO/mmWave channel estimation must be both accurate and sub-millisecond on resource-limited edge hardware, while prior DL channel estimators usually optimize NMSE first and leave latency, memory traffic, and deployability as an afterthought. The paper pivots from algorithm-only model design toward an end-to-end co-design loop that treats the least-squares (LS) pilot estimate as a low-resolution spatial-frequency image and reconstructs the full channel matrix with a lightweight CNN plus parameter-free attention, replacing expensive self-attention with a zero-parameter masking mechanism and pixel-shuffle upsampling. The mechanical shift is then extended by a three-stage compression pipeline—knowledge distillation, convolution re-parameterization, and quantization-aware training—that turns the teacher into a compact INT8-ready student without collapsing accuracy, and by an HLS-generated FPGA accelerator that co-implements the LS estimator and compressed network with fine-grained streaming, tiled convolutions, local BRAM caching, and a pipelined pixel-shuffle path to eliminate buffering stalls. Tested on a Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC with 28 GHz 5G NR settings, CDL-B training, and unseen CDL-A/C/D/E channel profiles, SwiftChannel reaches 0.883 ms latency, up to 24.8× speedup and over 33× energy-efficiency gain versus GPU baselines, while generalizing robustly across noise and mobility but showing a notable drop on the sparse CDL-A mismatch. This points toward wireless AI that is natively hardware-shaped, low-precision, and standard-aware rather than merely accurate in software.
papers.abstract: Channel estimation is crucial in 5G communication networks for optimizing transmission parameters and ensuring reliable, high-speed communication. However, the use of multiple-input and multiple-output (MIMO) and millimeter-wave (mmWave) in 5G networks presents challenges in achieving accurate estimation under strict latency requirements on resource-limited hardware platforms. To address these challenges, we propose SwiftChannel, an algorithm-hardware co-design framework that integrates a hardware-friendly deep learning-based channel estimator with a dedicated accelerator. Our approach employs a convolutional neural network enhanced with a parameter-free attention mechanism, which effectively reconstructs full-resolution spatial-frequency domain channel matrices from low-resolution least squares (LS) estimates. We further develop a multi-stage model compression pipeline combining knowledge distillation, convolution re-parameterization, and quantization-aware training, resulting in substantial model size reduction with negligible accuracy loss. The hardware accelerator, implementing the compressed model and the LS estimator on FPGA platforms using High-level Synthesis (HLS), features a fine-grained pipeline architecture and optimized dataflow strategies. Tested on a Zynq UltraScale+ RFSoC, the accelerator achieves sub-millisecond latency, providing up to 24x speed-up and over 33x improvement in energy efficiency compared to GPU-based solutions. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the proposed design generalizes not only across various noise levels and user mobilities, but also to a variety of unseen channel profiles, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. By unifying algorithmic innovation with hardware-aware design, our work presents a future-proof channel estimation solution for 5G MIMO systems.

papers.title: Real-Time Text Transmission via LLM-Based Entropy Coding over Fixed-Rate Channels
papers.summary: Real-Time Text Transmission via LLM-Based Entropy Coding over Fixed-Rate Channels attacks the streaming bottleneck that appears when a predict-then-code compressor is used not offline, but for text arriving at human reading speed and draining through a constant-rate link: variable-length codewords create queueing delay, so the real failure mode is not just excess bits but decodability latency and block buffering. The paper challenges the usual compression-first assumption that the best coder is the one nearest Shannon; it pivots from static lossless coding toward a compression–delay Pareto analysis for real-time transmission, separating irreducible algorithmic delay from hardware-dependent compute delay. The mechanical shift is to use a causal LLM as the sequential predictor, then compare Shannon, Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at multiple block sizes, and gzip under a FIFO channel model that tracks when each token becomes decodable. What is unusual is that modern tokenization is treated as the enabling granularity: BPE/SentencePiece tokens of roughly 3–4 characters make Huffman’s 1-bit overhead small enough that zero-delay decoding becomes competitive. Experiments on five Project Gutenberg books with GPT-2 (124M) and Llama 3.2 (3B) show that Llama cuts bits per character by about 38%, which can over-provision the same channel and shift the optimal coder from arithmetic coding toward simpler Huffman. The result suggests a future where communication design is increasingly predictor-centric, with the next frontier in distillation, quantization, lossy streaming, and real hardware/wireless latency evaluation.
papers.abstract: Learning, prediction, and compression are intimately connected: a model that accurately predicts the next symbol in a sequence can be coupled with a source coder to compress that sequence near its information-theoretic limit. When tokenized characters arriving at a fixed reading pace are encoded into variable-length codewords and streamed over a fixed-rate channel, a queue forms whose per-token delay depends on the mean and variance of the bit lengths and on the coder's algorithmic latency. This paper investigates the compression--delay tradeoff that arises when a causal language model serves as the sequential predictor within a predict-then-code architecture for real-time text transmission. Several coding schemes are compared: Shannon (ideal), Huffman, arithmetic coding, rANS at various block sizes, and gzip. The analysis separates algorithmic delay, inherent to the coder, from computational delay, which shrinks as hardware improves. Huffman is the practical choice for over-provisioned channels, with zero algorithmic delay and modest compression overhead. Arithmetic coding achieves near-optimal compression at the cost of decodability delay. Findings are validated across two scales: GPT-2 (124M) and Llama~3.2 (3B), a twenty-five-fold parameter range. This scaling yields an approximately 38\% reduction in bits per character, effectively over-provisioning the channel and thereby changing which coder is optimal.

papers.title: Optimization of CV-QKD Under Practical Constraints
papers.summary: Optimization of CV-QKD Under Practical Constraints attacks the bottleneck that makes continuous-variable quantum key distribution hard to deploy economically: once transmitter and receiver pulse-shaping/matched filters have finite FIR length and the DAC/ADC have finite bit depth, mode mismatch creates intersymbol interference and extra noise that sharply erode secure key rate, so idealized “infinite-resolution, perfectly matched, transmitter-only” tuning no longer predicts real performance. The paper pivots from analytical or hand-designed filter choices toward hardware-aware end-to-end optimization, and from static design rules toward adaptive control of the actual SKR objective. The mechanical shift is a REINFORCE reinforcement-learning loop that jointly updates transmitter FIR taps, receiver FIR taps, and mean photon number, using SKR as reward after parameter estimation infers effective transmittance and excess noise from received data; notably, it avoids gradient computation and does not require an explicit analytic channel model, making it closer to a deployable experimental control policy than a conventional offline optimizer. In the simulated Gaussian-modulated CV-QKD chain—4 samples/symbol, finite-bandwidth optical low-pass filtering, 0.2 dB/km fiber loss, and b-bit converters—the learned filters suppress ISI and compensate quantization-induced noise. Compared with a root-raised-cosine baseline, the method yields large SKR gains, near-optimal behavior with only about 20–40 taps and 10–11 bits, and extends range from roughly 60 km to nearly 100 km. This points toward closed-loop, constraint-aware quantum communication design where learning directly co-optimizes photonic hardware and security metrics.
papers.abstract: Using reinforcement learning, we optimize for practical hardware constraints, including limited FIR filter taps at the transmitter and receiver, mean photon number and finite DAC/ADC resolution. Under these realistic conditions, the proposed approach achieves significant performance improvements.

papers.title: Dueling DDQN-Based Adaptive Multi-Objective Handover Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks
papers.summary: Dueling DDQN-Based Adaptive Multi-Objective Handover Optimization for LEO Satellite Networks attacks the mobility-management bottleneck in dense LEO constellations: as satellites race overhead and footprints overlap, UEs face frequent handovers, blocking from capacity limits, and signaling/switching overhead that conventional satellite-selection rules cannot balance. The paper challenges the old workflow of handover design as either fixed-rule heuristics or RL with static scalar rewards, which implicitly assumes constant preference weights and therefore breaks under changing load, user density, and satellite availability. It pivots from optimizing a single handover criterion or a predefined trade-off curve toward adaptive multi-objective control, where throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost are learned jointly as a time-varying policy problem. The mechanical shift is a dueling DDQN agent operating on an MDP whose state includes candidate satellite rates, visible-set composition, satellite load vector, and previous association; the reward uses adaptive weights α(t), β(t), γ(t) rather than fixed λ coefficients, while DDQN reduces overestimation and the dueling decomposition separates state value from action advantage to resolve cases where overlapping satellites have near-tied utilities. Unusually, the framework is initialized from expert trajectories such as MSHBO to mitigate cold start, and baselines are adapted to online operation with sliding windows. In a 298-satellite Telesat Lightspeed simulation, it reports up to 10.3% throughput gain and near-zero blocking under typical load, suggesting future LEO control stacks will increasingly use adaptive multi-objective RL to steer resource-aware association rather than handover thresholds alone.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we propose a dueling double deep Q-network (DDQN)-based adaptive multi-objective handover framework for LEO satellite networks. The proposed method enables dynamic trade-off learning among throughput, blocking probability, and switching cost under time-varying network conditions. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms conventional baselines, achieving up to 10.3% throughput improvement and near-zero blocking under typical operating conditions.

papers.title: Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
papers.summary: Quality-Aware Exploration Budget Allocation for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning attacks a coordination failure mode in cooperative MARL where rare joint strategies sit behind sparse bottlenecks and a single fixed intrinsic-reward weight β can either drown the team reward and collapse coordination or stay too weak to ever discover the needed joint behavior. The paper pivots from the common assumption that “more exploration is always better if tuned once per task” toward a two-level control view: exploration must be scheduled over training and also rationed across agents according to the reliability of each agent’s intrinsic signal. The mechanical shift is a return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) for the global β trajectory, combined with Reward Signal Quality (RSQ), an SNR-based per-agent allocation rule that amplifies exploration for agents with stable intrinsic-reward statistics and suppresses noisy ones instead of distributing budget uniformly. Unlike nearby work that adapts entropy or uses hard constraints, this framework explicitly treats intrinsic motivation as a noisy communication channel and approximates water-filling with a clipped affine modulation that preserves the ordering of the information-optimal allocation while avoiding irreversible zeroing. It is completed by Successor Distance intrinsic rewards, whose quasimetric structure naturally creates inter-agent quality gaps detectable by RSQ. Evaluated on seven cooperative benchmarks spanning MPE corridor/tag, SMAX 3s5z/27m, and MABrax ant, ant-ball, and halfcheetah, the method achieves top or tied-top returns and markedly better stability, especially on bottleneck-heavy tasks. The result points toward exploration systems that are quality-aware, agent-specific, and dynamically budgeted rather than globally uniform.
papers.abstract: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) requires agents to discover joint strategies in a combinatorially large state-action space, yet effective coordination configurations are exceedingly rare. Intrinsic motivation, which augments task rewards with novelty bonuses, is a popular approach for driving exploration, but its effectiveness hinges on the exploration intensity $β$, where too large a value overwhelms the task signal and causes coordination collapse, while too small a value prevents discovery of rare strategies. We address two complementary challenges: adapting $β$ globally over training, and allocating the exploration budget across agents whose intrinsic reward signals vary in reliability. Our framework combines a return-conditioned sigmoid schedule (RCB) for global intensity control with a per-agent Reward Signal Quality (RSQ) metric that concentrates the exploration budget on agents with reliable signals. The core insight is that agents receiving noisy intrinsic rewards should explore less aggressively, and this allocation can be determined automatically from signal-to-noise statistics. Successor Distance (SD), a quasimetric intrinsic reward, naturally produces distinguishable per-agent signal quality, completing the framework with convergence and ordering preservation guarantees. On seven cooperative benchmarks (MPE, SMAX, MABrax), our method achieves top-tier returns across all environments.

papers.title: Coopetition-Gym v1: A Formally Grounded Platform for Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Strategic Coopetition
papers.summary: Coopetition-Gym v1: A Formally Grounded Platform for Mixed-Motive Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Strategic Coopetition attacks the benchmark gap where MARL has been split between fully cooperative and zero-sum settings, leaving the interior mixed-motive regime—platform alliances, open-source coalitions, reputation markets, and reciprocity—under-instrumented. It pivots from procedural or game-engine benchmarks toward formally grounded coopetition substrates with analyzable payoffs, calibrated interdependence coefficients D_ij, and validated case studies. The mechanical shift is a two-layer design that separates environment payoff from learning reward: every one of 20 environments, grouped into TR-1 interdependence/complementarity, TR-2 trust/reputation, TR-3 collective action/loyalty, and TR-4 sequential reciprocity, can be run under private, integrated, or cooperative reward modes, making reward-type ablation the core causal probe. This is unusual relative to Melting Pot, SMAC, or OpenSpiel because it combines continuous scalar cooperation actions, closed-form or numerically tractable equilibria, seven game-theoretic oracle baselines, and four historically calibrated cases—Samsung-Sony LCD, Renault-Nissan Alliance, Apache HTTP Server, and Apple iOS App Store—reproducing rubric outcomes at 98.3, 81.7, 86.7, and 87.3%. The release also exposes Gymnasium and PettingZoo Parallel/AEC interfaces, 126 reference policies, and a 25,708-run training corpus plus 1,116-run behavioral audit. The result suggests a future of MARL benchmarking where relational structure, reward mutuality, and auditability are explicit control levers rather than hidden confounds.
papers.abstract: We present Coopetition-Gym v1, a benchmark platform for mixed-motive multi-agent reinforcement learning under strategic coopetition. The platform comprises twenty environments organized into four mechanism classes that correspond to four foundational technical reports: interdependence and complementarity (arXiv:2510.18802), trust and reputation dynamics (arXiv:2510.24909), collective action and loyalty (arXiv:2601.16237), and sequential interaction and reciprocity (arXiv:2604.01240). Each environment carries a closed-form payoff structure and a calibrated interdependence matrix derived from the corresponding report. Every environment exposes a parameterized reward layer configurable across three structurally distinct modes (private, integrated, cooperative). This separation of payoff from reward enables reward-type ablation, the platform's principal methodological apparatus. Four of the twenty environments are calibrated against historically documented coopetitive relationships and reproduce their outcomes at 98.3, 81.7, 86.7, and 87.3 percent on the validation rubric (Samsung-Sony LCD, Renault-Nissan Alliance, Apache HTTP Server, Apple iOS App Store). The platform exposes Gymnasium, PettingZoo Parallel, and PettingZoo AEC interfaces and ships 126 reference algorithms: 16 learning algorithms, 7 game-theoretic oracles, 2 heuristic baselines, and 101 constant-action policies. A reference experimental study trained the 16 learning algorithms on every environment under every reward configuration with seven random seeds, producing a 25,708-run training corpus and a 1,116-run behavioral audit corpus, both released under CC-BY-4.0 with Croissant 1.0 metadata. Coopetition-Gym v1 is the first platform to combine continuous-action mixed-motive environments, parameterized reward mutuality, calibrated interdependence coefficients, game-theoretic oracle baselines, and validated case studies.

papers.title: LLM-enabled Social Agents
papers.summary: LLM-enabled Social Agents argues that the central bottleneck in LLM-based multi-agent systems is not dialogue fluency or task decomposition, but the failure mode where agents sound competent while remaining socially unintelligible because they are weakly grounded in roles, norms, intentions, relationships, and context. The paper explicitly challenges the current workflow that treats sociality as an external prompt layer or a post hoc personalization feature, and pivots from generic capability bundles and role labels toward persona-operationalized role definitions as the representational baseline for social agency. The mechanical shift is a hybrid symbolic-textual persona schema, split into a Persona Core (stable traits, values, boundaries, responsibilities, commitments) and a Persona State (dynamic priorities, interpretations, relationship status, memory-informed updates), which is injected into LLM-centered deliberation and complemented by memory, normative structures, and control mechanisms. This makes the agent’s internal reasoning sensitive to who it is, what role it performs, and what obligations follow, rather than merely what the latest prompt asks. Unusually, the paper is conceptual rather than empirical: it compares personas against role labels, norm rule sets, institutional role models, and relationship/commitment models, arguing that persona descriptions are the most actionable baseline for LLMs while still requiring broader social scaffolding. It also points to synthetic persona datasets and benchmark-style evaluation for consistency, role fidelity, commitment persistence, and social intelligibility, suggesting a future research direction where social agents are built and measured as role-enacting systems, not just fluent responders.
papers.abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed agent-agent and human-agent interaction by enabling software, physical, and simulation agents to communicate and deliberate through natural language. Yet fluent language use does not by itself yield socially intelligible behaviour. Most current systems remain weakly grounded in roles, norms, intentions, and contextual constraints, limiting their capacity for meaningful participation in social environments. This paper develops a conceptual baseline for LLM-enabled social agents by arguing that they should be grounded in role definitions operationalized through persona descriptions. On this basis, we outline research directions for representation, hybrid control, and evaluation. The paper concludes that persona-based role definitions are a necessary foundation for turning language competence into social behaviour.

papers.title: When Stress Becomes Signal: Detecting Antifragility-Compatible Regimes in Multi-Agent LLM Systems
papers.summary: When Stress Becomes Signal: Detecting Antifragility-Compatible Regimes in Multi-Agent LLM Systems attacks a specific evaluation blind spot in agentic LLMs: standard robustness tests ask whether performance survives perturbation, but they miss whether semantic stress exposes structured variation that later adaptation could exploit. The paper pivots from treating contradiction, overload, ambiguity, and temporal drift as nuisance noise toward treating them as measurable stressors that deform an architecture’s response geometry. Its core mechanical shift is CAFE, a statistical measurement layer that first defines a controlled expected stress distribution, then uses multi-dimensional judge signals—coherence, grounded novel inference, contradiction resolution, and structural preservation—to fit a multi-output polynomial response surface, invert that surface to reconstruct an architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution, and finally compare expected versus observed dispersion with a convex-potential distributional Jensen Gap. Unlike nearby work on debate, self-refinement, or ensemble gains, CAFE does not optimize outputs directly; it detects whether the system is in an antifragility-compatible regime. On a controlled banking-risk benchmark with 50 clean prompts and 500 stressed variants, five architectures were tested: flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, and ensemble. All lost roughly one third of judged quality under stress, yet all showed positive Jensen Gaps with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero, meaning degradation coexisted with expandable stress geometry. The paper’s implication is a research program where stress-aware routing, memory updates, and architecture selection are triggered by detected stress modes, and where future systems are judged not just by surviving adversity but by learning from it.
papers.abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems are increasingly used to solve complex tasks through decomposition, debate, specialization, and ensemble reasoning. However, these systems are usually evaluated in terms of robustness: whether performance is preserved under perturbation. This paper studies a different question: whether semantic stress exposes structured variation that could support future antifragile learning. We introduce CAFE, a statistical framework for detecting antifragility-compatible regimes in multi-agent architectures. CAFE models a controlled expected distribution of semantic stressors, reconstructs an architecture-specific observed effective stress distribution from multi-dimensional judge signals, and compares both distributions using a distributional Jensen Gap under a convex stress potential. A positive gap does not imply immediate performance improvement; instead, it indicates a convex-expansive deformation of the observed stress distribution, suggesting that the architecture exposes learnable stress structure. We evaluate CAFE on a banking-risk analysis benchmark with five multi-agent architectures: flat, hierarchical, debate, meta-adaptive, and ensemble. Across all architectures, semantic stress reduces average judged quality by roughly one third. Yet all architectures exhibit positive distributional Jensen Gaps with bootstrap confidence intervals above zero. These results show that immediate quality degradation can coexist with statistically detectable antifragility-compatible stress geometry. CAFE is therefore not an antifragile learner itself, but a measurement layer for identifying when and where antifragility learning may be worth applying.

papers.title: LLM-Augmented Semantic Steering of Text Embedding Projection Spaces
papers.summary: LLM-Augmented Semantic Steering of Text Embedding Projection Spaces addresses the bottleneck that low-dimensional text-embedding projections often organize documents by the model’s latent geometry rather than by the analyst’s intended semantic relation, forcing users to adapt to the map instead of reshaping it. The paper challenges prior semantic interaction workflows that encode intent indirectly through geometric constraints, metric learning, or retraining-heavy model updates, and pivots from parameter steering toward language-mediated intent externalization. Its core mechanical shift is: analysts group a few example documents in the projection, an LLM converts those groups into explicit natural-language cluster cards and document-level semantic augmentations, selectively extends that intent to related documents only when evidence is sufficient, and then injects the semantics back into document representations via text augmentation or embedding-level blending, all without retraining the embedding or UMAP model. Unlike label-based or dataset-wide annotation schemes, the approach uses interpretable intermediate descriptions with inclusion/exclusion criteria, making the steering process inspectable and conservative rather than exhaustive. In a product-review case study, the same corpus is reorganized under category and sentiment perspectives; in simulation on 112 IEEE VIS papers embedded with text-embedding-3-small and projected with UMAP, semantic steering improves global silhouette alignment and local neighborhood consistency over the baseline, while random text perturbations degrade performance. Minimal interaction, roughly five examples per group, is enough to saturate gains, and embedding blending provides continuous control through α-interpolation. The work points toward intent-dependent semantic workspaces where projections become revisable analytical instruments, with future directions in richer intent forms, scalable selective propagation, and tighter human-LLM loops for visual analytics.
papers.abstract: Low-dimensional projections of text embeddings support visual analysis of document collections, but their spatial organization may not reflect the relationships an analyst intends to examine. Existing semantic interaction approaches encode semantic intent indirectly through geometric constraints or model updates, limiting interpretability and flexibility. We introduce LLM-augmented semantic steering, which enables analysts to express semantic intent by grouping a small set of example documents within the projection. A large language model externalizes this intent as natural-language representations and selectively extends it to related documents; the resulting semantic information is then incorporated into document representations via text augmentation or embedding-level blending, without retraining the underlying models. A case study illustrates how the same corpus can be reorganized from different semantic perspectives, while simulation-based evaluation shows that semantic steering improves global and local alignment with target semantic structures using only minimal interaction. Embedding-level blending further enables continuous and controllable steering of projection layouts. These results position projection spaces as intent-dependent semantic workspaces that can be reshaped through explicit, interpretable, language-mediated interaction.

papers.title: Cripping AI: Reimagining AI Through Lived Disability Experiences
papers.summary: Cripping AI: Reimagining AI Through Lived Disability Experiences reframes the central bottleneck in disability-oriented AI as not merely poor accessibility or low benchmark performance, but the ableist assumptions baked into what counts as a task, a label, a “normal” user, and a correct output. Pivots from making AI merely accessible toward cripping AI, a crip-theory-based framework that exposes disability politics, centers cripistemologies, and respects crip labor: instead of treating disabled people as passive recipients of accommodation, it treats them as co-definers of goals, labels, and evaluation. The mechanical shift is not a new model architecture but a new research loop for the full AI pipeline, replacing able-bodied ground truth and one-size-fits-all benchmarks with disability-situated interpretation, community-led annotation, and context-sensitive evaluation grounded in lived workflows. The paper’s three cases make the pivot concrete: sign language AI moves away from hearing/interpreter-centered translation and tokenized sign annotations toward deaf signing practices, translanguaging, and modular multimodal systems; visual assistive AI moves away from sighted captions and “objective” descriptions toward blind users’ triangulation, everyday uncertainty, and multi-source sense-making; speech AI for stuttering moves away from fluency correction and clinical severity metrics toward comfort, dignity, verbatim captioning, and speaker-led annotation of disfluency. What distinguishes the paper is its refusal to treat accessibility as a fixed technical endpoint; it instead proposes a justice-oriented research program spanning diverse bodyminds, the whole AI ecosystem, and collaboration with other anti-ableist, decolonial, feminist, and queer AI efforts.
papers.abstract: Drawing on crip theory, this paper proposes cripping AI as a guiding framework to center lived disability experiences in AI research and development. Moving beyond calls to make AI "accessible" to people with disabilities, cripping AI seeks to: (1) reveal and dismantle ableist assumptions embedded in how AI is imagined, designed, and evaluated; (2) center disabled ways of knowing (i.e., cripistemologies); (3) respect disabled labor in co-creating accessible practices. We demonstrate how to apply our framework with three cases: deafness and sign language AI, blindness and visual assistive AI, and stuttering and speech AI. We end by outlining three directions for future work, including cripping AI with diverse human bodyminds, across the entire AI pipeline and ecosystem, and in collaboration with other justice-oriented AI efforts.

papers.title: ProPACT: A Proactive AI-Driven Adaptive Collaborative Tutor for Pair Programming
papers.summary: ProPACT: A Proactive AI-Driven Adaptive Collaborative Tutor for Pair Programming attacks a specific failure mode in collaborative programming support: adaptive systems usually model each coder separately, react after breakdowns, and miss the timing-sensitive regulation of attention, effort, and coordination that pair debugging actually depends on. The paper pivots from individual-centric, retrospective monitoring toward collaboration-as-the-unit-of-instruction and forecast-driven dyadic regulation. Its mechanical shift is a multimodal learner model built from dual eye tracking and pupillometry, operationalizing Joint Visual Attention (JVA), Joint Mental Effort (JME), and individual mental effort, then feeding these signals into an XGBoost forecaster that predicts emerging suboptimal collaboration states up to 30 seconds ahead. Those predictions do not merely alert; they drive a hierarchical adaptive policy that escalates from always-on text selection to gaze-awareness cues, dialogue prompts, GitHub Copilot autocomplete, and finally task-based hints, while fading support when collaboration is already productive. Unusual relative to nearby AIED work, ProPACT explicitly regulates the dyad’s shared process rather than optimizing only task output, and it encodes pedagogy as a minimally intrusive intervention ladder. In a within-subject study with 26 undergraduate and master’s pair-programming dyads solving logical bugs, proactive feedback improved debugging success, reduced time on task, increased feedback uptake, and raised post-intervention JVA and JME. The result suggests a future where AI tutors anticipate collaborative breakdowns and orchestrate shared regulation in real time, but the reliance on specialized sensing and a controlled lab setting limits immediate deployment.
papers.abstract: Effective pair programming depends on coordination of attention, cognitive effort, and joint regulation over time, yet most adaptive learning systems remain individual-centric and reactive. This paper introduces ProPACT, a proactive AI-driven adaptive collaborative tutor that treats collaboration itself as the object of instruction. ProPACT constructs a multimodal dyadic learner model based on Joint Visual Attention (JVA), Joint Mental Effort (JME), and individual mental effort, and employs an XGBoost-based forecasting model to predict emerging suboptimal collaboration states up to 30 seconds in advance. These predictions drive a hierarchical adaptive policy that delivers minimally intrusive scaffolds while fading support during productive collaboration. A within-subject study with 26 pair-programming dyads shows that proactive feedback significantly improves debugging success, task efficiency, feedback uptake, and post-intervention gains in JVA and JME, demonstrating the potential of forecast-driven dyadic adaptivity for real-time collaborative learning regulation.

papers.title: ShiftLIF: Efficient Multi-Level Spiking Neurons with Power-of-Two Quantization
papers.summary: ShiftLIF: Efficient Multi-Level Spiking Neurons with Power-of-Two Quantization attacks the bottleneck that standard LIF neurons in edge-oriented SNNs collapse rich membrane dynamics into binary spikes, while prior multi-level neurons often fix that with uniform quantization that mismatches the heavy-tailed, zero-concentrated membrane-potential distribution and forces costly MAC-style synaptic multiplies. The paper pivots from “make the neuron more complex” toward “change the spike alphabet to match both signal statistics and hardware,” replacing uniform integer levels with a logarithmically spaced power-of-two set {0, 2−K, …, 1}. The mechanical shift is a shift-quantized activation plus proportional soft reset: membrane values are clamped, mapped to the largest admissible dyadic level, and then subtracted by the emitted spike magnitude, so small activations get finer resolution while synaptic products become bit-shifts and integer accumulation rather than general multiplication. This is unusual relative to nearby SNN work because it improves expressiveness without abandoning multiplier-free computation, and its surrogate-gradient training is explicitly matched to the whole quantization support rather than a single firing threshold. Evaluated with identical spiking LeNet-style backbones and surrogate training across wireless, acoustic, motion, and neuromorphic vision benchmarks, ShiftLIF achieves the best average accuracy while keeping synaptic energy close to binary LIF and usually below INT-LIF; gains are strongest on continuous sensing, with smaller benefit on event-based vision. The result points toward distribution-aware, hardware-aligned spike alphabets as a broader direction for energy-efficient neuromorphic learning.
papers.abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs) are promising for edge sensing due to their event-driven computation and temporal filtering capability. However, standard leaky integrate-and-fire (LIF) neurons communicate only through binary spikes, which severely limit representational capacity. Existing multi-level spiking neurons improve information transmission, but often rely on uniform quantization that mismatches membrane-potential distributions or introduces costly synaptic multiplications. In this paper, we propose ShiftLIF, a multi-level spiking neuron that maps membrane potentials to a logarithmically spaced power-of-two spike set. This design provides finer representation in the small-amplitude regime, where membrane potentials are densely concentrated, while enabling multiplier-free synaptic computation through bit-shift and accumulation operations. As a result, ShiftLIF improves spike-level expressiveness without sacrificing the hardware-friendly nature of standard SNN computation. We evaluate ShiftLIF on 10 datasets spanning wireless, acoustic, motion, and visual sensing tasks. Results show that ShiftLIF consistently matches or exceeds the accuracy of existing multi-level spiking neurons while maintaining synaptic energy consumption close to standard binary LIF. These results indicate that ShiftLIF provides a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for cross-modal edge sensing.

papers.title: SNNF: An SNN-based Near-Sensor Noise Filter for Dynamic Vision Sensors
papers.summary: SNNF: An SNN-based Near-Sensor Noise Filter for Dynamic Vision Sensors attacks the bottleneck that DVS event streams, while sparse and low-power, are flooded by spurious Background Activity noise that forces downstream pipelines to waste memory, bandwidth, and always-on compute. It implicitly challenges the dominant filter recipe of storing high-bit timestamps in SAE/TS-style surfaces or using ANN/hand-coded spatiotemporal rules that scale poorly with sensor resolution. The pivot is from timestamp-centric, per-pixel memory toward a learned near-sensor classifier built on 1-bit Event-Based Binary Images and a tiny spiking network: the mechanical shift is an EBBI stack updated in round-robin FIFO fashion, local 5×5 patch extraction from multiple recent binary frames, and a single-hidden-layer fully connected SNN that decides signal versus noise from spike accumulation rather than multiplier-heavy MACs. This is unusual because it explicitly trades away exact temporal values, then recovers discrimination through learned spatiotemporal structure in binary event patches, making the memory footprint collapse to one bit per pixel per EBBI while preserving temporal context through stacked windows. Evaluated on DND21 driving data with leak and shot noise at 346×260 resolution, SNNF reaches AUC 0.89, slightly above MLPF, while using 8-bit weights, 12-bit membrane states, and a 30-neuron hidden layer; FPGA and 65 nm ASIC results show 29 and 44.4 Meps with sharply lower area and power than ANN-based designs. This points toward co-designed event sensing where denoising is pushed directly into ultra-compact spiking hardware at the sensor edge.
papers.abstract: Dynamic Vision Sensors (DVS) exhibit exceptional dynamic range and low power consumption, making them ideal for edge applications in the Internet of Video Things (IoVT). However, their output is often degraded by spurious Background Activity (BA) noise, leading to unnecessary computational overhead. This paper proposes SNNF, a near-sensor BA noise filter that integrates a compact Event-Based Binary Image (EBBI) representation, a parallel memory architecture, and a single-layer Spiking Neural Network (SNN) classifier. Trained on representative DVS data, the SNN distinguishes signal events from noise with an AUC of 0.89 on standard datasets. The binary-array-based EBBI eliminates timestamp dependency, significantly reducing memory footprint. Moreover, the SNN's spike-based computation replaces power-hungry multipliers with simple accumulation logic and minimizes inter-neuron data width, resulting in an extremely hardware-efficient design. FPGA implementation results show that SNNF reduces memory and logic resources to approximately 11% and 40%, respectively of state-of-the-art filters, while achieving a throughput of 29 Mega events per second (Meps). In a 65 nm CMOS ASIC implementation, SNNF achieves 44.4 Meps with an area and power consumption of only ~13% and <5% of the corresponding ANN-based designs. These results demonstrate that SNNF provides an excellent balance between filtering accuracy and hardware efficiency, making it highly suitable for resource-constrained, near-sensor deployment.

papers.title: Neuromorphic Control for 3D Navigation in Minecraft Using Genetic Algorithms
papers.summary: Neuromorphic Control for 3D Navigation in Minecraft Using Genetic Algorithms attacks the brittle problem of frame-precise Minecraft parkour control, where success depends on timing sprint, jump, strafe, and yaw inputs against real game physics rather than a simplified simulator. The paper challenges the usual assumption that evolutionary navigation can be trained on oracle-like voxel scans or off-engine clones and then transferred; instead, it pivots from full-state path planning and deterministic A*-style “get from A to B” navigation toward momentum-sensitive, line-of-sight control learned inside Mojang’s Java Edition on real server ticks. The core mechanical shift is replacing a thousand-neuron voxel grid with a compact raycast sensor suite plus goal-vector, velocity, on-ground, and clock features, then using a genetic algorithm to evolve the weights of a single-hidden-layer MLP that outputs three binary actions and one continuous yaw command. Training is not standard RL but per-network fitness evaluation over obstacle runs, with tournament selection, elite retention, per-gene Gaussian mutation, and adaptive mutation-rate boosts on plateaus. What distinguishes it is the explicit rejection of deterministic scripted pathing and the insistence on a live game engine with continual domain randomization via command blocks that regenerate gap patterns and force generalization. In fixed-course trials, networks quickly mastered specific layouts but overfit to motor sequences; with randomized curricula they generalized to unseen gap configurations and balance-beam variants, though at the cost of slower wall-clock training and sensitivity to network jitter. The work points toward hierarchical policies, human-steered curriculum design, and latency-aware embodied control research.
papers.abstract: The popular 2009 voxel based videogame, Minecraft, contains several distinct disciplines. One of which is "parkour," gameplay that focuses on traversing a world's environment with maximum efficiency. The Minecraft online community has turned the game's physics engine into dynamic puzzles, requiring players to masterfully manipulate motion mechanics through frame precise timing of keystrokes. Actions such as sprinting, sneaking, and mouse direction are all combined to clear specific difficult jumps. Through this project, we design a genetic algorithm to generate weights for a neural network to autonomously evaluate inputs for block distances, terrain, and obstacles to determine the most optimal pathing.

papers.title: Spoken Language Identification with Pre-trained Models and Margin Loss
papers.summary: Spoken Language Identification with Pre-trained Models and Margin Loss attacks the failure mode of speaker-controlled language ID where models latch onto speaker identity, recording conditions, or other shortcuts instead of language cues, especially when the same speaker appears across multiple languages and the system must also generalize to unseen-language verification. It pivots away from the baseline recipe of Wav2Vec2-Large plus ArcFace-style classification toward task-related pretraining and explicit margin shaping: a VoxLingua107-pretrained ECAPA-TDNN is fine-tuned as the utterance encoder, then paired with margin-based heads that enforce stronger inter-class separation in language space. The mechanical shift is not merely “better features,” but a tighter optimization target: AAM-Softmax injects an angular margin, while RAM-Softmax reweights only hard negatives by the real logit margin, suppressing easy classes and concentrating pressure on confusable languages. In the closed-condition TidyLang 2026 setting on Tidy-X (35 seen languages for classification, 40 unseen languages for verification, 4,474 speakers, 321k utterances), the system also uses MUSAN noise/music/speech and RIRS reverberation augmentation to reduce environmental dependence, and cosine similarity for open-set verification. Compared with the official baseline, the ECAPA-TDNN system reaches 85.95% macro accuracy, 90.96% micro accuracy, and 17.08% EER, with RAM-Softmax further improving micro accuracy to 91.73% and EER to 16.39%. This points toward SLID research that treats speaker disentanglement, hard-negative mining, and task-matched pretraining as the central levers for robust multilingual speech understanding.
papers.abstract: For the speaker-controlled spoken language identification task proposed in the TidyLang Challenge 2026, this paper proposes a language identification method based on pre-trained models and margin-based losses. The proposed method adopts a pre-trained ECAPA-TDNN as the feature encoder and incorporates margin-based losses to enhance the discriminative ability of language representations, thereby improving inter-class separability and reducing the interference of non-linguistic factors such as speaker characteristics. Experimental results on the Tidy-X dataset show that the proposed method achieves 85.95% macro accuracy and 90.96% micro accuracy on the language identification task and 17.08% equal error rate (EER) on the verification task. Compared with the official baseline, the macro accuracy improves by 45.7%, the micro accuracy improves by 15.2%, and the EER is reduced by approximately 50.8%, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method. The code will be released at https://github.com/PunkMale/TidyLang2026.

papers.title: Toward Fine-Grained Speech Inpainting Forensics:A Dataset, Method, and Metric for Multi-Region Tampering Localization
papers.summary: Toward Fine-Grained Speech Inpainting Forensics:A Dataset, Method, and Metric for Multi-Region Tampering Localization attacks the failure mode where modern voice cloning and TTS can swap just one to three words inside an otherwise authentic utterance, preserving speaker identity while changing meaning, yet existing deepfake benchmarks still assume utterance-level real/fake labels or at most one contiguous spoofed span. The paper pivots from detecting fully synthesized speech toward localizing sparse, word-level inpainting with unknown segment count, where the fake content is only 2–7% of the waveform and conventional detectors miss the attack almost entirely. Its core mechanical shift is threefold: MIST, a multilingual six-language dataset with precise word timestamps and 1–3 independently inpainted regions per utterance, built via LLM-guided semantic replacement plus speaker-conditioned neural voice cloning and artifact-minimized splicing; ISA, a backbone-agnostic inference loop that replaces a single utterance score with coarse sliding-window scanning, gap-tolerant region proposal, and fine boundary refinement to recover all tampered spans without knowing N in advance; and SF1@τ, a temporal-IoU segment F1 paired with Count Accuracy that penalizes both miscounting and fragmented localization. Unlike nearby partial-spoof work, it explicitly models multi-region tampering and count uncertainty. Zero-shot tests with a Wav2Vec2-AASIST backbone show utterance-level classifiers assign near-zero fake probability to MIST, while ISA still improves localization modestly; fine-tuning on MIST jumps SF1@0.5 sharply, suggesting the next research trajectory is task-trained, multilingual, fine-grained audio forensics rather than whole-clip classification.
papers.abstract: Recent advances in voice cloning and text-to-speech synthesis have made partial speech manipulation - where an adversary replaces a few words within an utterance to alter its meaning while preserving the speaker's identity - an increasingly realistic threat. Existing audio deepfake detection benchmarks focus on utterance-level binary classification or single-region tampering, leaving a critical gap in detecting and localizing multiple inpainted segments whose count is unknown a priori. We address this gap with three contributions. First, we introduce MIST (Multiregion Inpainting Speech Tampering), a large-scale multilingual dataset spanning 6 languages with 1-3 independently inpainted word-level segments per utterance, generated via LLM-guided semantic replacement and neural voice cloning, with fake content constituting only 2-7% of each utterance. Second, we propose ISA (Iterative Segment Analysis), a backbone-agnostic framework that performs coarse-to-fine sliding-window classification with gap-tolerant region proposal and boundary refinement to recover all tampered regions without prior knowledge of their count. Third, we define SF1@tau, a segment-level F1 metric based on temporal IoU matching that jointly evaluates region count accuracy and localization precision. Zero-shot evaluation reveals that partial inpainting at word granularity remains unsolved by existing deepfake detectors: utterance-level classifiers trained on fully synthesized speech assign near zero fake probability to MIST utterances where only 2-7% of content is manipulated. ISA consistently outperforms non-iterative baselines in this challenging setting, and the dataset, code, and evaluation toolkit are publicly released.

papers.title: Tibetan-TTS:Low-Resource Tibetan Speech Synthesis with Large Model Adaptation
papers.summary: Tibetan-TTS:Low-Resource Tibetan Speech Synthesis with Large Model Adaptation tackles the bottleneck that Tibetan TTS is blocked less by decoder capacity than by scarce, noisy, multi-source speech data, conservative orthography, and a non-bijective text-to-pronunciation mapping that breaks standard front-end and from-scratch neural TTS pipelines. The paper pivots from building Tibetan speech synthesis as a language-specific Tacotron/FastSpeech-style system toward adapting a pretrained two-stage large speech synthesis backbone from Xingchen AGI Lab, pretrained on about 200,000 hours of Chinese-English mixed speech and 5,000 hours of multi-dialect speech. The mechanical shift is not just fine-tuning, but a three-part control lever: a unified data-quality governance pipeline for audio cleaning, text normalization, and speech-text alignment; Tibetan-oriented text representation adaptation that replaces default tokenization with either syllable-level units or a Tibetan-trained BPE tokenizer; and cross-lingual adaptive training that updates only a lightweight subset of parameters to transfer general speech generation into Tibetan. Unusually, the work treats tokenizer choice as a core modeling variable for pronunciation stability rather than a preprocessing detail, and reports a tradeoff: BPE slightly improves naturalness while syllable-level units improve pronunciation accuracy. On native-speaker evaluation, the systems reach MOS 4.28 and 4.35 with pronunciation accuracies of 97.6% and 96.6%, outperforming an external commercial Tibetan TTS interface. The result points toward unified multi-dialect Tibetan speech synthesis via large-model adaptation plus linguistic representation design rather than bespoke low-resource training from scratch.
papers.abstract: Tibetan text-to-speech (TTS) has long been challenged by scarce speech resources, significant dialectal variation, and the complex mapping between written text and spoken pronunciation. To address these issues, this work presents, to the best of our knowledge, the first large-model-based Tibetan TTS system in the industry, built upon a large speech synthesis model developed by Xingchen AGI Lab. The proposed system integrates data quality enhancement, Tibetan-oriented text representation and tokenizer adaptation, and cross-lingual adaptive training for low-resource Tibetan speech synthesis. Experimental results show that the system can generate stable, natural, and intelligible Tibetan speech under low-resource conditions. In subjective evaluation, the MOS scores of the syllable-level and BPE-based systems reach 4.28 and 4.35, while their pronunciation accuracies reach 97.6% and 96.6%, respectively, outperforming an external commercial Tibetan TTS interface. These results demonstrate that combining a large-model backbone with Tibetan-oriented text representation adaptation and cross-lingual adaptive training enables highly usable low-resource Tibetan speech synthesis, and also provides a technical foundation for future unified multi-dialect Tibetan speech synthesis.

papers.title: A Parameter-Free First-Order Algorithm for Non-Convex Optimization with $\tilde{\mkern1mu O}(ε^{-5/3})$ Global Rate
papers.summary: A Parameter-Free First-Order Algorithm for Non-Convex Optimization with $\tilde{\mkern1mu O}(ε^{-5/3})$ Global Rate attacks a key bottleneck in smooth non-convex optimization: the best deterministic accelerated rate is known only when step size, momentum, and curvature constants $L_1,L_3$ are supplied a priori, making the theoretically optimal AGD-until-guilty style methods brittle and impractical. The paper pivots from “accelerate only after calibrating global smoothness” toward “recover acceleration by estimating curvature on the fly,” challenging the assumption that the optimal $\tilde O(ε^{-5/3})$ complexity inherently depends on known problem constants. The mechanical shift is PF-AGD: an inner accelerated loop with adaptive backtracking that infers local $L_1$ from Armijo violations, plus a gradient-based restart and witness-certificate mechanism that detects when the iterate path is only locally, not globally, convex-like; an outer loop then uses these certificates to decide whether to exploit negative curvature or raise the third-order curvature estimate $M_k$. Unusual relative to nearby work, it keeps the method deterministic, parameter-free, and faithful to theory while still using acceleration and third-order smoothness, rather than retreating to quasi-Newton, dimension-dependent finite-difference, or averaged/heavy-ball variants. On robust least squares, MNIST, ill-conditioned quadratics, and non-smooth test landscapes, PF-AGD beats practical AGD-Until-Guilty and other parameter-free baselines, often matching nonlinear CG and showing much less sensitivity to spectral conditioning. The main implication is that high-rate non-convex acceleration can be made self-tuning, pointing toward locally adaptive, certificate-driven optimizers and eventually stochastic line-search versions for modern ML.
papers.abstract: We introduce PF-AGD, the first parameter-free, deterministic, accelerated first-order method to achieve $O(ε^{-5/3}\log(1/ε))$ oracle complexity bound when minimizing sufficiently smooth, non-convex functions; this is the best-known bound for first-order methods on smooth non-convex objectives. Unlike existing methods possessing this rate that require a priori knowledge of smoothness constants, we use an adaptive backtracking scheme and a gradient-based restart mechanism to estimate local curvature. This yields a practical algorithm that matches best-known theoretical rates. Empirically, PF-AGD outperforms the practical variant of AGD-Until-Guilty (Carmon et al., 2017), as well as other parameter-free variants, and is a viable alternative to nonlinear conjugate gradient methods.

papers.title: Robust and Fast Training via Per-Sample Clipping
papers.summary: Robust and Fast Training via Per-Sample Clipping attacks the failure mode of stochastic optimization under heavy-tailed gradient noise, where vanilla SGD and even standard global clipping lose optimality, become hyperparameter-sensitive, and can be dominated by rare outlier gradients; it pivots from the usual “clip the averaged mini-batch gradient after aggregation” workflow toward clipping each sample before averaging, turning robustness into a property of the gradient estimator itself. The mechanical shift is PS-Clip-SGD, which forms a clipped mean of per-sample gradients with sample-dependent thresholds γ(k)t=min{1, αkβ/‖∇f(x,t,ξ(k)t)‖} and then updates with the averaged clipped estimator; the theory shows that with α=σ and β=p this estimator attains optimal in-expectation sample complexity for non-convex objectives under finite p-th moment noise, and also gives high-probability guarantees with only polylogarithmic dependence on failure probability, improving the usual heavy-tail clipping analyses and matching lower bounds up to logs. Unusually, the paper recasts per-example clipping—previously mostly a differential-privacy tool—as a robustness mechanism for heavy-tailed optimization, and it emphasizes a stronger squared-gradient convergence criterion rather than only gradient norms. On a quadratic toy problem with Pareto noise, AlexNet on CIFAR-100, and GPT-2 with gradient accumulation on OpenWebText, PS-Clip-SGD outperforms momentum SGD and standard clipping, while mini-batch-level clipping during accumulation can beat the common “clip only at the end” practice at nearly zero extra cost. The main tradeoff is extra per-sample compute and parameter knowledge; the future direction is parameter-free, constant-threshold, and more scalable robust training rules for large models.
papers.abstract: We propose a robust gradient estimator based on per-sample gradient clipping and analyze its properties both theoretically and empirically. We show that the resulting method, per-sample clipped SGD (PS-Clip-SGD), achieves optimal in-expectation convergence rates for non-convex optimization problems under heavy-tailed gradient noise. Moreover, we establish high-probability convergence guarantees that match the in-expectation rates up to polylogarithmic factors in the failure probability. We complement our theoretical results with multiple numerical experiments. In particular, we demonstrate that PS-Clip-SGD outperforms both vanilla SGD with momentum and standard gradient clipping when training AlexNet on the CIFAR-100 dataset, even after accounting for the additional computational time caused by per-sample clipping. We also empirically show that, in the presence of gradient accumulation, applying clipping at the mini-batch level can improve training performance while incurring virtually no additional computational cost. This finding is particularly interesting, as it contradicts the common practice of applying clipping only after all accumulation steps have been completed.

papers.title: A second-order method on the Stiefel manifold via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz
papers.summary: A second-order method on the Stiefel manifold via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz attacks the high-accuracy bottleneck in retraction-free orthogonality optimization: existing landing-style and penalty-free schemes avoid expensive retractions, but they are usually only first-order, so they stall when one needs tight feasibility and fast asymptotic convergence on problems like PCA, orthogonal Procrustes, and ICA. The paper pivots from “cheap but linear” infeasible updates toward a genuinely second-order landing framework that still lands on the Stiefel manifold without retractions. The mechanical shift is an explicit split of each step into a tangent objective-reduction component and a normal infeasibility-reduction component: the normal part is chosen as the order-1 Newton–Schulz orthogonalization step, shown geometrically to move along the normal space of the layered manifold and to contract orthogonality error quadratically, while the tangent part is not the naive Riemannian Newton direction but a modified Newton equation that compensates for the perturbation induced by the normal move. A further approximation removes projection and matrix inversion terms, yielding a projection-free, inverse-free solver that keeps only matrix multiplications and a nonsymmetric linear solve. This is unusual relative to nearby work because it makes Newton–Schulz a geometric normal flow rather than just an orthogonalizer, and because it proves local quadratic convergence for the exact method and superlinear convergence for the inexact Krylov-solved variant. The experiments show improved high-accuracy performance over penalty methods, inexact Riemannian Newton, and SQP, pointing toward globalized, stochastic, and generalized-Stiefel second-order landing methods.
papers.abstract: Retraction-free approaches offer attractive low-cost alternatives to Riemannian methods on the Stiefel manifold, but they are often first-order, which may limit the efficiency under high-accuracy requirements. To this end, we propose a second-order method landing on the Stiefel manifold without invoking retractions, which is proved to enjoy local quadratic (or superlinear for its inexact variant) convergence. The update consists of the sum of (i) a component tangent to the level set of the constraint-defining function that aims to reduce the objective and (ii) a component normal to the same level set that reduces the infeasibility. Specifically, we construct the normal component via Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz, a fixed-point iteration for orthogonalization. Moreover, we establish a geometric connection between the Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz iteration and Stiefel manifolds, in which Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz moves along the normal space. For the tangent component, we formulate a modified Newton equation that incorporates Newton$\unicode{x2013}$Schulz. Numerical experiments on the orthogonal Procrustes problem, principal component analysis, and real-data independent component analysis illustrate that the proposed method performs better than the existing methods.

papers.title: 2D Stability Selection: Design Jittering for Doubly Stable Feature Selection
papers.summary: 2D Stability Selection: Design Jittering for Doubly Stable Feature Selection attacks a concrete blind spot in high-dimensional regression: feature sets that look stable under resampling can still be brittle when the predictors themselves are noisy, as in measurement error or errors-in-variables regimes. The paper challenges the implicit assumption behind classical Stability Selection that only sampling variability matters, and pivots from subsampling-based robustness toward direct stress-testing of the design matrix. Its mechanical shift is a perturb-and-aggregate loop: for each noise level δ, add controlled Gaussian jitter W to X, refit a fixed base selector such as the Lasso, Elastic Net, or SCAD on the full sample, and compute selection frequencies across B randomized perturbations; then sweep δ over a grid to form a 2D stability path that exposes which variables persist as design noise increases. This is unusual relative to nearby stability work because the randomness is injected into the covariates rather than the sample split, so the method isolates measurement-error sensitivity while preserving n. Theoretical results show that irrepresentability for the Lasso survives sufficiently small perturbations and extend this with a high-probability Gaussian guarantee; support recovery follows by thresholding δ-averaged frequencies under a separation condition, with a data-driven largest-gap rule when the true model size is unknown. Experiments on synthetic p=1000 regression and the Rat Genome microarray data show improved robustness over Stability Selection and single fits, but also reveal failure when δ is too large or threshold estimation flattens. This points toward future feature selection methods that are explicitly noise-axis-aware and robustness-calibrated.
papers.abstract: We study feature selection in high-dimensional regression under two distinct sources of instability: sampling variability and measurement error in the design matrix. Stability Selection addresses the former through sub-sampling and aggregation, but does not explicitly stress-test robustness to noisy predictors. We introduce doubly stable feature selection, a perturb-and-aggregate framework that targets features whose inclusion is stable both across randomization and across increasing levels of design noise. The method injects controlled additive noise into the design matrix, fits a fixed base selector such as the Lasso on the perturbed data, and aggregates selection frequencies. Sweeping over a grid of noise levels yields a stability path that summarizes robustness to measurement error while using the full sample size and isolating the effect of design perturbations. On the theory side, we show that classical model-selection conditions are preserved under sufficiently small perturbations, with a high-probability extension for Gaussian noise. Empirically, experiments on synthetic and real datasets show improved robustness compared with Stability Selection and standard base selectors.

papers.title: Denoising data using convex relaxations
papers.summary: Denoising data using convex relaxations attacks the core failure mode of manifold denoising under large isotropic Gaussian corruption: the clean points live on a low-dimensional set in R^n, but the observed cloud is too noisy for direct tangent estimation or explicit manifold recovery to be stable. The paper pivots away from fitting the manifold itself toward a convex surrogate, replacing nonconvex geometry with projection onto K = conv(ΠM0) after a PCA dimension reduction step that removes orthogonal noise. The mechanical shift is especially distinctive: instead of assuming access to K, it builds a statistical distance oracle that estimates supporting hyperplanes from empirical Gaussian tail probabilities of the noisy sample, then uses those hyperplane distances to approximate Euclidean projection; in the reduced space, the authors even use exhaustive search over a sphere net rather than a standard convex solver because oracle calls are expensive. Under a lower-mass condition on the latent distribution, they prove finite-sample bounds that decompose error into PCA bias, statistical risk for least-squares projection under convex constraints, and oracle approximation error, while also quantifying the exponential-in-noise sample complexity expected for supersmooth deconvolution. Unlike nearby manifold-learning work, this framework does not require C2,1 regularity or R-exposedness, and it is verified for a Cryo-EM forward model by proving Lipschitz and covering-number control for the SO(k) action, X-ray transform, and pixel sampling. The direction it points toward is geometric inverse problems solved through convex relaxations plus data-driven oracles rather than direct manifold reconstruction.
papers.abstract: We study the problem of denoising observations \(Y_i=X_i+Z_i\), where the latent variables \(X_i\) are sampled from a low-dimensional manifold in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) and the noise variables \(Z_i\) are isotropic Gaussian. We propose a convex-relaxation estimator that first reduces dimension by principal component analysis and then projects the observations onto the convex hull of the projected latent manifold. We construct a statistical oracle that estimates its supporting hyperplanes from empirical Gaussian tail probabilities of the noisy sample. Under a lower-mass condition on the latent distribution, we prove finite-sample guarantees for the oracle and derive error bounds for the resulting denoiser. The analysis combines risk bounds for least-squares projection under convex constraints with entropy bounds for convex hulls. We also verify the assumptions of the framework for a Cryo-Electron Microscopy observation model by establishing suitable covering number and Lipschitz estimates for the associated group action and imaging operators.

papers.title: The Bayesian Reflex: Online Learning as the Autonomic Nervous System of Modern and Future AI
papers.summary: The Bayesian Reflex: Online Learning as the Autonomic Nervous System of Modern and Future AI reframes the central bottleneck in deployed AI as not better batch fitting but continuous adaptation under drift, stream arrival, and delayed feedback, challenging the default assumption that a model can be trained once and frozen. The paper pivots from static large-dataset scaling and ad hoc retraining toward online Bayesian control loops where yesterday’s posterior becomes today’s prior, and uncertainty itself drives action through exploration–exploitation balance.
