Its mechanical shift is twofold: first, the “look-up table principle” turns sequential inference in function space into a reusable computational template across dynamic emulation, nonparametric state-space models, circular time series, inverse regression for climate evaluation, and Recursive Gaussian Processes; second, the “ellipsoidal decomposition” framework aims at practically exact iid posterior sampling, replacing ordinary MCMC’s convergence ambiguity and particle filters’ degeneracy with perfect-sampling-style updates on compact ellipsoidal regions. What distinguishes the chapter from nearby online learning and Bayesian deep-learning work is its attempt to unify inference, decision-making, and exact sampling under a single reflex metaphor, spanning Kalman filtering, Thompson sampling, restless bandits, conformal sepsis prediction, stationarity testing, point processes, climate dynamics, and even prime-number modeling. The claimed implication is a foundational infrastructure for adaptive AI that does not merely update periodically but evolves continuously in situ; the main limitation is computational burden in high dimensions, motivating parallelization, federated Bayesian learning, and more scalable exact/near-exact posterior engines for future continual systems.
papers.abstract: This chapter introduces the Bayesian reflex -- an analogy with the autonomic nervous system -- as a unifying framework for online learning in AI. Bayesian online algorithms automatically maintain equilibrium in dynamic environments via three mechanisms: belief maintenance through probabilistic representations, sequential updating via Bayes' theorem, and uncertainty-driven action balancing exploration and exploitation. We survey online Bayesian methods, highlighting two computational principles: the look-up table principle for sequential inference in function space, and the ellipsoidal decomposition framework for nearly exact i.i.d. sampling from arbitrary posteriors. These principles are generalized across dynamic emulation, nonparametric state-space models, circular time series, inverse regression for climate model evaluation, and deep architectures via Recursive Gaussian Processes. Decision-making is explored via Thompson sampling and restless bandits. We extend the framework to assess infinite series convergence (applied to climate dynamics and the Riemann Hypothesis), model prime number distributions leading to the discovery of 184 strong Mersenne prime candidates, detect stationarity, and characterize point processes. The Bayesian reflex provides a foundational infrastructure for adaptive AI that continuously learns in a complex world.

papers.title: Generative Modeling with Orbit-Space Particle Flow Matching
papers.summary: Generative Modeling with Orbit-Space Particle Flow Matching attacks a specific bottleneck in particle generation: when particles are flattened into anonymous indices, permutation symmetry makes each index’s target wildly inconsistent, so flow-matching regressors average over incompatible roles and learn curved, unstable trajectories; meanwhile, standard linear paths waste the fact that a particle flow’s terminal velocity in physical space can carry geometric meaning. The paper pivots from Eulerian, grid-style or OT-heavy symmetry handling toward a particle-native, orbit-space formulation that reduces role ambiguity before learning. Its core mechanical shift is Orbit-Space Geometric Probability Paths (OGPP): canonicalize only the terminal endpoint X1 into a deterministic orbit representative, attach learnable particle-index embeddings so each slot specializes to a stable canonical role, keep X0 uncannonicalized to avoid cancellation-induced Lipschitz blowups, and replace straight interpolation with quadratic Hermite probability paths whose arc-length-aware terminal velocity is aligned with surface normals. That makes normals a byproduct of the same flow rather than a separate predictor, and joint canonicalization of (position, normal) further tightens the regression target. Unlike nearby point-cloud diffusion or equivariant flow work, OGPP explicitly analyzes conditional covariance and orbit-continuity, then evaluates on self-referential particle systems: minimal surfaces, blue-noise sampling, diffusion-limited aggregation, multilayer Thomson configurations, ShapeNet, and single-shape encoding. The claimed outcome is sharper geometry with far fewer steps—up to two orders of magnitude lower error on minimal surfaces in one step, 5× fewer inference steps on ShapeNet, and airplane EMD comparable to DiT-3D with 26× fewer parameters—pointing toward orbit-aware, attribute-encoded generative dynamics for physical particle worlds.
papers.abstract: We present Orbit-Space Geometric Probability Paths (OGPP), a particle-native flow-matching framework for generative modeling of particle systems. OGPP is motivated by two insights: (i) particles are defined up to permutation symmetries, so anonymous indexing inflates per-index target variance and yields curved, hard-to-learn flows; and (ii) particles live in physical space, so the flow terminal velocity has physical meaning and can encode geometric attributes, e.g., surface normals. OGPP instantiates three key components: (1) orbit-space canonicalization of the probability-path terminal endpoint, (2) particle index embeddings for role specialization, and (3) geometric probability paths with arc-length-aware terminal velocities that generate normals as a byproduct of the flow. We evaluate OGPP on minimal-surface benchmarks, where it reduces metric error by up to two orders of magnitude in a single inference step; on ShapeNet, where it matches the state of the art with 5x fewer steps and reaches airplane EMD comparable to DiT-3D with 26x fewer parameters and 5x fewer steps; and on single-shape encoding, where it produces normals and reconstructions competitive with 6D generators while operating entirely in 3D.

papers.title: Adaptive Interpolation-Synthesis for Motion In-Betweening on Keyframe-Based Animation
papers.summary: Adaptive Interpolation-Synthesis for Motion In-Betweening on Keyframe-Based Animation attacks the production bottleneck of 3D animation in-betweening, where animators must fill short gaps between semantically meaningful block keyposes while preserving timing, anticipation, held poses, and stylized snap rather than merely “completing” motion. It challenges the assumption embedded in recent Transformer/diffusion in-betweeners that dense mocap-like data, random missingness, and smooth physically plausible trajectories are the right formulation; instead it pivots from long-horizon, content-inference-heavy motion completion toward short-gap, style-preserving interpolation aligned with studio workflows. The mechanical shift is the Adaptive Interpolation-Synthesis (AIS) layer: a Bi-LSTM encodes masked keypose sequences, then per frame and per pose dimension the model predicts an explicit interpolation coefficient between previous and next keyposes, a direct synthesis branch for breakdown poses, and a learned gate that blends the two adaptively, replacing fixed interpolation formulas or pure residual offsets. A second shift is the Domain-Based Algorithm keypose schedule, which uses keypose extraction as a proxy for production block schedules and augments it to reduce the ambiguity caused by random masks. Evaluated on an 8.5-hour proprietary animation dataset plus LaFAN1 sparse-motion benchmarks, AIS-BiLSTM achieves state-of-the-art results on production-style keyframe data and remains competitive on mocap; in Autodesk Maya it cuts animator effort by 71% and yields a 3.5× speedup, though it remains sensitive to keypose schedule mismatch and short-horizon by design, pointing toward schedule-aware, tool-integrated, character-specific motion assistants.
papers.abstract: Motion in-betweening is one of the most artistically demanding and time consuming stages of 3D animation, where the expressivity and rhythm of motion are defined. The level of creative control it requires makes it a major production bottleneck, underscoring the need for intelligent tools that assist animators in this process. Although recent deep learning approaches have achieved strong results in motion synthesis and in-betweening, they assume data characteristics, motion styles, and problem formulations that diverge from professional animation workflows. To bridge this gap, we propose a method explicitly aligned with the constraints of motion in-betweening for keyframe-based animation in production environments. At its core, the Adaptive Interpolation-Synthesis (AIS) layer mirrors the animator's creative process by dynamically balancing learned interpolation and direct pose synthesis. In addition, a domain-based input keypose schedule reflects the distribution of production data, improving stylistic consistency and alignment between training and real-world usage. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on production data; when integrated into Autodesk Maya, it enables animators to complete in-betweening tasks with a 3.5x speedup.

papers.title: Spatial-Temporal Learning-Based Distributed Routing for Dynamic LEO Satellite Networks
papers.summary: Spatial-Temporal Learning-Based Distributed Routing for Dynamic LEO Satellite Networks attacks the bottleneck of routing under rapidly changing intersatellite topology and bursty, time-varying traffic, where shortest-path and static schemes break under route churn while centralized controllers do not scale. It also challenges the newer habit of treating LEO routing as either purely spatial graph learning or as a decoupled traffic-prediction-plus-routing pipeline; the paper pivots from topology-only, globally informed, or prediction-first workflows toward joint spatiotemporal, fully distributed decision-making from local observations. The mechanical shift is a POMDP formulation in which each satellite acts as an independent DQN agent, but its state encoder is upgraded with GAT for neighbor-weighted spatial aggregation and LSTM for temporal memory of congestion and link evolution. This is unusual in nearby work because it does not merely attach a graph network to RL: it explicitly fuses graph attention and sequence modeling inside the routing policy so the agent can anticipate queue buildup and choose less congested next hops, with reward shaped as negative delay plus queue penalty. In simulation on a 45-satellite LEO constellation with 120/180/240 Mbps traffic, the method beats Dijkstra, GraphPR, DQN-IR, and FDR-MARL on throughput, packet loss, queue length, and end-to-end delay, including up to 23.26% queue reduction, while keeping on-board compute and carbon footprint low. This points toward Green-AI-friendly, edge-deployed, QoS-aware multi-agent routing that jointly learns spatial structure, traffic periodicity, and congestion control.
papers.abstract: In this paper, we propose a spatial-temporal learning-based distributed routing framework for dynamic Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite networks, where graph attention networks (GAT) and long short-term memory (LSTM) are integrated within a deep Q-network (DQN)-based architecture to enable distributed and adaptive routing decisions based on local observations. The routing problem is formulated as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) to address partial observability under dynamic topology and time-varying traffic. Simulation results show that the proposed method significantly outperforms conventional and learning-based routing schemes in terms of throughput, packet loss, queue length, and end-to-end delay, while achieving proactive congestion avoidance with up to 23.26% queue reduction. In addition, the proposed approach maintains low computational overhead with negligible carbon emissions, demonstrating its efficiency from a Green AI perspective.

papers.title: Beyond State Machines: Executing Network Procedures with Agentic Tool-Calling Sequences
papers.summary: Beyond State Machines: Executing Network Procedures with Agentic Tool-Calling Sequences attacks a telecom-specific bottleneck: LLM agents can describe procedures, but sequential network workflows break when they must preserve exact tool order, branch on intermediate results, and avoid repeated reasoning across many turns. The paper challenges the old assumption that procedure execution should remain either a hard-coded state machine or a fully agent-side, step-by-step planning loop; instead, it pivots from “LLM reasons through every step” toward “procedure orchestration should be pushed into tools whenever possible.” The mechanical shift is to compare four delivery/execution placements for the same procedure, especially a tool-encapsulated mode where one high-level tool internally calls lower-level tools deterministically, shrinking the agent’s role to one trigger call plus final summarization. Using a UE IP allocation case study grounded in 3GPP-like logic over MCP servers, plus a separate stress test with 100 network-analytics tools and sequences up to length 50, the authors measure latency, exact sequence correctness, and failure modes. Their procedure-specific error taxonomy distinguishes wrong tool, duplicate tool, premature stop, wrong order, and no tool calls. The unusual result is that advanced tool-calling helps, but model size alone does not: Qwen-Coder-3B is the best tradeoff, A4 gives the lowest latency and highest correctness, and all models degrade as sequences lengthen, with premature stop becoming dominant and reliability falling sharply beyond about 30 steps. This points toward agent harnesses, reusable skills, and trace-labeled training for long-horizon procedural execution.
papers.abstract: Agentic AI will be an essential enabling technology for designing future mobile communication systems, which could provide flexible and customized services, automate complex network operations, and drive autonomous decision-making across the network. This work studies how Large Language Model (LLM)-based network AI agents can be utilized to execute network procedures expressed as sequences of tool invocations. We investigate four approaches, which differ in how the agent obtains the procedure and in how execution is distributed between the agent and the underlying tools. We evaluated the latency and execution correctness across these approaches using a User Equipment (UE) IP allocation procedure as a case study. Furthermore, we conduct a stress test to examine how many sequential procedural steps an LLM agent can reliably execute before failure. Our results show that approaches relying on iterative agent-side reasoning incur higher latency and are more prone to execution errors, while approaches where the procedure is encapsulated within a single tool, which internally orchestrates the required steps by invoking other tools, reduce latency by limiting repeated reasoning. The stress-test results further show that the model with advanced tool-calling capability maintains reliable execution over longer procedures than the other evaluated models; however, all models exhibit reliability degradation as procedure length increases, revealing clear execution limits in multi-step tool-based workflows. To systematically analyze failures in procedure execution, we introduce a procedure-specific error taxonomy that categorizes deviations in multi-step procedural execution.

papers.title: Benchmarking Wireless Representations: High-Dimensional vs. Compressed Embeddings for Efficiency and Robustness
papers.summary: Benchmarking Wireless Representations: High-Dimensional vs. Compressed Embeddings for Efficiency and Robustness attacks the practical bottleneck that wireless foundation-model features, although expressive, may be too costly and brittle for deployment once latency, memory, and CSI transmission overhead matter. The paper challenges the implicit assumption that higher-dimensional learned embeddings are automatically the best universal wireless representation, and pivots from “more semantic channels” toward an explicit cost-benefit regime where robustness and efficiency are co-equal objectives. The mechanical shift is a controlled benchmark over three representation families—LWM high-dimensional channel embeddings (including CLS summaries), compact autoencoder latents, and raw CSI—evaluated under the same downstream heads on DeepMIMO city_18_denver data for LoS/NLoS classification, beam selection, and a new multi-user power allocation task. Unlike nearby work that reports only downstream accuracy, this study systematically measures data efficiency, SNR robustness, parameters, FLOPs, and inference time, while making power allocation the stress test for system-level utility. The unusual result is that LWM embeddings can be strong in few-shot LoS/NLoS settings, yet they impose substantial transformer overhead and often degrade more under noise; compressed autoencoder representations are consistently smaller, more transmission-friendly, and frequently more stable across tasks, including near-optimal power allocation after a supervised warm-up with PGD labels followed by unsupervised spectral-efficiency optimization. The paper points toward wireless AI research that jointly learns representations and deployment constraints, rather than treating representation richness as the sole scaling axis.
papers.abstract: Building on recent advances in representation learning for wireless channels, this work investigates the cost-benefit trade-offs of high-dimensional channel embeddings in practical systems. We benchmark multiple wireless representations: high-dimensional learned embeddings from a wireless foundation model, compact autoencoder-based representations with significantly lower dimensionality, and raw data baselines, evaluating their performance across diverse downstream tasks. We then systematically analyze data efficiency, noise robustness, and computational complexity, explicitly characterizing the resource overhead associated with high-dimensional embeddings. Beyond standard tasks such as line-of-sight/non-line-of-sight (LoS/NLoS) classification and beam selection, we introduce power allocation as a new downstream task. Our results reveal clear trade-offs: while high-dimensional embeddings can perform well in few-shot regimes for certain tasks, they incur substantial latency and parameter overhead. In contrast, compressed latent representations learned by autoencoders demonstrate improved noise robustness and more stable performance across tasks, while significantly reducing computational and transmission costs.

papers.title: Context-Aware Wireless Token Communication via Joint Token Masking and Detection
papers.summary: Context-Aware Wireless Token Communication via Joint Token Masking and Detection attacks the inefficiency of sending every language token as if independent when token sequences are highly redundant and wireless channels corrupt some positions more than others. It challenges the conventional assumption behind ML detection and uniform power allocation: that each token deserves the same transmission effort and should be decoded only from its own received symbol. The pivot is from context-agnostic physical-layer symbol recovery and task-specific end-to-end codecs toward a shared-context communication loop in which a masked language model (BERT MLM) becomes a probabilistic prior used jointly by transmitter and receiver. The mechanical shift is two-sided: at the Rx, token decisions are made by iterative MAP-style detection that multiplies channel likelihoods with MLM contextual priors, while token-wise entropy control freezes confident positions early and concentrates refinement on uncertain ones; at the Tx, a greedy entropy-based masking policy omits tokens that the Rx can infer from context, and a masking-ratio controller stops when masking ceases to beat transmission, using cumulative detection-probability comparisons. Unlike nearby token masking or autoregressive detection work, this framework uses the same MLM as a shared Tx-Rx control lever rather than a one-sided decoder. On Europarl and WikiText-103 with 128-token packets, WordPiece tokens, 4-QAM, and Rayleigh block fading, it improves reconstruction SIM up to 1.77× and 1.63×, while reducing unnecessary Rx updates. The paper points toward adaptive semantic wireless systems where context, uncertainty, and resource allocation are co-optimized across MIMO/OFDM and eventually multimodal token streams.
papers.abstract: The increasing use of token-based representations in language-driven applications has motivated wireless token communication, where tokens are treated as fundamental units for transmission. However, conventional communication systems overlook dependencies among tokens and allocate transmission resources uniformly, leading to inefficient use of limited wireless resources under channel impairments. In this paper, we propose a context-aware token communication framework that leverages a masked language model (MLM) as a shared contextual model between the transmitter (Tx) and receiver (Rx). At the Rx, we develop a context-aware token detection method that integrates channel likelihoods with MLM-based contextual priors under a Bayesian formulation, enabling robust token inference over noisy channels. At the Tx, we propose a context-aware token masking strategy that selectively omits tokens that can be reliably inferred at the Rx, allowing the available power budget to be concentrated on more informative tokens. These components are jointly designed through a shared MLM, establishing a unified Tx-Rx framework for efficient token transmission and detection. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework significantly improves reconstruction performance compared to conventional and existing token communication schemes, achieving up to 1.77X and 1.63X performance gains on the Europarl corpus and WikiText-103 datasets, respectively.

papers.title: Observability Conditions and Filter Design for Visual Pose Estimation via Dual Quaternions
papers.summary: Observability Conditions and Filter Design for Visual Pose Estimation via Dual Quaternions attacks a concrete failure mode of classical perspective-n-point pipelines: they are memoryless, noise-sensitive, outlier-prone, and collapse during feature dropouts or occlusions because they cannot propagate pose through missing measurements. The paper explicitly pivots from one-shot geometric pose solving toward sequential SE(3) estimation with motion-aware state propagation, replacing deterministic PnP output with a dual quaternion Lie group unscented Kalman filter that carries both relative pose and dual velocity. The mechanical shift is twofold: first, a nonlinear observability study using Lie algebraic rank conditions on the observability codistribution for relative position-vector and unit-vector sensing; second, a left-invariant manifold filter whose retraction/lift, sigma-point propagation, and unit-quaternion normalization preserve rigid-body geometry without assuming cooperative target sensors, direct velocity sensing, or slowly varying motion. What is distinctive is that the paper recovers the classic P3P collinear-feature degeneracy from control-theoretic observability rather than purely geometric arguments, and shows that three noncollinear measurements suffice locally once a prior estimate exists. In simulation with a dynamically moving camera and target, five fiducial markers, pinhole-image measurements, and OpenCV PnP as baseline, the DQ-UKF maintains estimates through two-marker intervals where PnP fails, yields lower and smoother pose error, and exposes uncertainty growth during near-unobservable regimes. The work points toward observability-guided feature placement, trajectory planning, and broadly applicable dual-quaternion estimators for visual-inertial navigation and SLAM.
papers.abstract: This paper presents a dual quaternion framework for 6-DOF visual target tracking that addresses key limitations of perspective-n-point (P$n$P) solvers: sensitivity to noise and outliers, and inability to propagate estimates through measurement dropouts. A nonlinear observability analysis is performed using a Lie algebraic approach, deriving sufficient conditions for local observability under two sensing modalities: relative position vector and unit vector measurements. For the unit vector case, the classical collinear feature point degeneracy of the perspective-three-point problem is recovered through rank analysis of the observability codistribution matrix, providing a control-theoretic interpretation of a previously geometric result. A dual quaternion Lie group unscented Kalman filter is then developed, directly modeling relative dynamics without assumptions about cooperative measurements or slowly-varying motion. Simulations demonstrate improved pose estimation accuracy and robustness to occlusions compared to an off-the-shelf P$n$P solver. Results are broadly applicable to visual-inertial navigation, simultaneous localization and mapping, and P$n$P solver development.

papers.title: Set-Based Training of Neural Barrier Certificates for Safety Verification of Dynamical Systems
papers.summary: Set-Based Training of Neural Barrier Certificates for Safety Verification of Dynamical Systems attacks the bottleneck in formal safety synthesis for continuous dynamical systems: current neural barrier-certificate pipelines train on sampled states, then hand the candidate to an external SMT or sum-of-squares verifier, repeatedly cycling when counterexamples appear. That workflow assumes verification must stay outside learning and that sample fit is an acceptable proxy for certificate validity; this paper pivots from counterexample-guided retraining toward end-to-end certified training. The mechanical shift is a sound set-based loss built on conservative zonotope propagation through the network, initial-set, unsafe-set, and Lie-derivative constraints so that a zero loss formally proves the three barrier properties at once. To make the third property tractable, the method recursively encloses the network’s nonconvex zero-level set via branch-and-bound preimage splitting, then propagates gradient sets and bounds the Lie derivative over each enclosure; for nonlinear flows it uses interval-based range bounding or linearization. Unlike nearby neural-barrier work, it eliminates the verifier loop entirely and can handle multiple initial/unsafe sets plus linear, polynomial, and nonpolynomial dynamics. On 13 benchmarks including Peruffo, Darboux, Lyapunov, Exponential, Ratschan, Three Sets, and Two Barriers, implemented in CORA and tested over 10 seeds, it reports 100% success and is often faster than FOSSIL, while surpassing interval-only and polynomial-only methods on irregular geometries. The tradeoff is that deeper networks and tighter zero-level enclosures raise cost, pointing toward future AI systems where verification is embedded directly into training by structured set reasoning.
papers.abstract: Barrier certificates are scalar functions over the state space of dynamical systems that separate all unsafe states from all reachable states. The existence of a barrier certificate formally verifies the safety of the dynamical system. Recent approaches synthesize barrier certificates by iteratively training a neural network. In each iteration, the candidate is formally verified - if successful, the barrier certificate is found. Instead, we propose a set-based training approach that tightly integrates verification into training via a set-based loss function that soundly encodes all barrier certificate properties. A loss of zero formally proves the validity of the barrier certificate, collapsing the iterative training and verification into a single training procedure. Our experiments demonstrate that our set-based training approach scales well with the system dimension and naturally handles complex nonlinear dynamics.
